Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER having read
the King's Speech,
Sir WILLIAM COATES (in the uniform of a Deputy-Lieutenant):
I beg to move: That an humble Address
be presented to His Grace in the following
words:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament.
I esteem it a very great honour to be
permitted to move this Resolution on
the occasion of the opening of the New
Session of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland. I feel sure this House joins
in the spirit of restrained optimism which
pervades the Gracious Speech, and I am
sensible of the anxiety shown by His
Majesty's Government of Northern Ireland to ameliorate in every possible way
the difficulties under which both industry
and agriculture are suffering at the present time.
How clearly interwoven are these two
phases of activity can be seen in the concluding paragraph of His Majesty's
Gracious Speech, and I am sure this
House very cordially re-echoes the sentiments therein expressed. Largely owing
to conditions brought about by disappointing harvests and depressed markets
the present position of the Northern
Ireland farmer is not enviable.
We can
certainly, however, hope that after this
extremely wet winter next summer may
compensate them in full degree by giving
them weather and markets to their entire
satisfaction
I think the most satisfactory feature
is the view taken by the Government on
the unemployment position of to-day.
The favourable opinion expressed in the
Gracious Speech would appear to be
general throughout the United Kingdom,
and I was glad to note a few days ago
that in the opinion of one most capable
to judge, there are in fact more persons
in employment than of late years, for the
reduction in the numbers on the registers
of unemployed means that trade and industry have absorbed the annual increment of youth entering the labour
market. The "Times" calculates that
we have to-day 750,000 more employed
than ever before, and these figures suggest that had emigration proceeded on
the pre-war scale there would by this
time be hardly more labour available in
the home market than is required to meet
current needs. In addition, the present
success and future possibilities of the new
industries fostered by present policies is
apparent to all, as well as the enterprise
of our countrymen in developing them.
In this connection reference may
appropriately be made to the publicity
campaign carried out by the linen industry in the United States. No doubt
this will receive full consideration in
another place, but in view of its importance right hon. and hon. Members of
this House will feel that attention should
be drawn to it here. This great industry,
on which so largely depends the prosperity of Northern Ireland, is recognizing
the modern trend of events, and
endeavouring to focus public attention on
its products. Certainly such constructive
work should not be hampered in any way
through lack of funds, and while this
great key industry of ours is striving to
bring itself into line with modern competitive conditions, it is, I think, proving
itself worthy of the continued financial
support of the Government of Northern
Ireland. No reference to the unemployment question or to industry generally
would be complete unless the arrangements which have been made for the reopening of our second great shipbuilding
yard were mentioned. It is, indeed,
highly gratifying that this result has been
attained, and I am sure this House hopes
that under the new regime there may be
many years of success for the new firm,
such as were enjoyed in full degree by its
predecessors.
Many useful legislative Measures are
foreshadowed, but reference is made to
one enactment of past legislation, and
very deservedly So. The Widows',
Orphans' and Old Age Contributory
Pensions Act has brought a measure of
relief to many homes, even during the
short time it has been in force. Among
future legislation we are to have
Measure to extend the Loans Guarantee
a
"
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
6 MARCH 1928
Acts, following the success shown by
previous Acts in the furtherance of the
shipbuilding and other industries in
Northern Ireland, and many other enactments framed with the view to the safety,
advancement, and protection of those for
whom our Northern Government are re-
First among these
sponsible.
enactinments may be placed the Measure
tended to vary in some respects the financial provisions of the Constabulary Act,
1922.
Local authorities will welcome this
Measure, which will remove many difficulties and make the work of the original Act
much easier. A very important matter
will be the consideration of road improvements by local authorities. In the present
conditions of road traffic such a Measure
is urgently required for the safety and
protection of all road users, both vehicular
and pedestrian, and it is further to be
noted that conditions are to be made for
the regulation of street trading in our
Some such arrangement is also
towns.
very necessary.
The other Measures mentioned in the
King's Most Gracious Speech do not, I
think, require further comment at the
moment. They will, in due course, come
before this House for consideration, and
will, I am sure, receive every attention.
They will be framed, as I have before
mentioned, for the good of the Six-
County area. All this legislation, some
of which will be introduced in this House,
and some in another place, will mean
heavy work during the Session, but I am
quite sure that the Members of both
Houses will apply themselves diligently to
the work which will come before them in
the interests of their fellow citizens and
fellow countrymen, and I hope their
efforts will be productive of much good
for Northern Ireland. May I thank you,
Mr. Deputy Speaker, and the Members
of this House, for the patient hearing
they have given me, and for the consideration they have extended to the few
words I have been so kindly permitted to
say? I beg to move.
Mr. McDOWELL (in morning dress):
I beg to second the address of thanks for
the Gracious Speech from the Throne,
which has been moved in such a capable
manner by Senator Sir William Coates.
I much appreciate the honour which has
been conferred upon me, and as a workman representative I look upon it more as
a compliment to my fellow workmen,
whom I am so proud to represent in this
Chamber, than to me personally. It proves
that class distinction, which is so much
spoken of outside the House, is nonexistent here, and it is only in accordance
with the treatment which I have received
since I entered the Senate.
The major portion of the Bills foreshadowed in the Gracious Speech directly
concern the industrial workers, and I am
sure their progress through the different
stages will be watched with keen interest.
The gratification expressed by the knowledge that industrial peace continues to
prevail throughout Northern Ireland,
conveys to all the great interest which is
taken by his Grace in the industrial welfare of the people. The reference to trade
disputes I commend to the serious consideration of employers and employed.
Indeed I consider that it would be nothing
short of a calamity if anything should
occur to impede the revival in trade which
has been so long and anxiously awaited.
The payment of old-age pensions has
been a great benefit to both men and
women; it has removed the terrible fear
of want and privation which was continually threatening the many worn-out
workers. But neither pensions nor unemployment benefit are to be compared with
wages-
Mr. DORMAN: Hear hear.
Mr. McDOWELL- -for work honestly
performed, if the worker is physically fit.
Wages are free from any taint, which a
decent worker sometimes associates with
the other. Therefore, I am delighted to
know that the Government propose giving
facilities for the Loans Guarantee Acts to
be extended for a further period. We all
know the great benefit which this scheme
has rendered to the industries of Northern
Ireland, providing work and wages for a
large number of hands who would otherwise have been idle. I sincerely hope that
the further assistance promised will provide plenty of work and tide us over this
terrible trade depression.
The proposals regarding registration of
nursing and maternity homes, and street
trading, will, I am sure, receive the attention which they deserve. Every
person would be satisfied if they knew
that the supervision of these homes was
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell]
beyond question, and that the lives of
both mothers and infants were properly
cared for. We might reasonably expect
that the shortage of work for adults in
other occupations is sure to bring about
abuses in street trading, which I hope
will be satisfactorily remedied. The
other Measures also mentioned deal with
various subjects which are all ripe for
consideration. Great improvements can
be made in the roads of Northern Ireland
to ensure safety and comfort for those
driving motors, and, at the same time,
secure a great reduction in the number
of fatalities caused by dangerous bends
and corners.
Much has been done by the Government to make this small corner of the
world better to live in, and I am sure
the Bills now promised are all intended
for the good and welfare of our people.
The
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to congratulate
the mover and seconder of the Address
in reply to the Gracious Speech.
latter contains a number of proposed
Measures that are non-controversial, and
which will eventually receive the full support of all the Members of this House.
I desire to express my regret that the
Government has not considered it advisable to suggest some legislation with regard to the Rent Restrictions Act in
connection with the houses that will become de-controlled during the coming
summer. The present situation in
Belfast, so far as the housing of the
working classes is concerned, is a lamentable one, and we can anticipate with certainty that when the better and highervalued houses are de-controlled during the
coming summer there will inevitably be
intensified competition for the possession
of houses of a lower valuation. House
owners are at present utilizing every conceivable means for the purpose of taking
advantage of the occupiers of houses at
present controlled.
a
During last week a case came under
my observation of a widow against whom
decree had been issued some four
months
to
She undertook
ago.
pay
arrears of rent at the rate of ten shillings per week. She continued to pay
these arrears for twenty weeks, and on
missing one of these ten-shilling contributions a writ was served upon that
unfortunate woman to obtain possession
of the premises which she occupied, because when she vacates them they will
be de-controlled. The result was that
the learned judge administering the law
was compelled to give a decree for possession of the house, entailing an expense
of over £5 to that unfortunate widow.
The reason I mention this matter is the
on numerous occasions I have drawn the
attention of the House to what would
necessarily take place unless some steps
were taken to protect the working
classes from this keen competition and
unjust conduct on the part of house
owners. That poor
woman has now
been put to an expense of over £5. In
my reading of Holy Writ I have come
across a definition of true religion as
being to "visit the fatherless and the
widows, and to keep oneself unspotted
from the world." In this connection it
might be appropriately altered to read,
"to evict the widow and orphans with a
view to spotting a higher rent," all because of the non-payment of a small sum
of money.
There is another matter to which I would
like to refer. We all rejoice that the aged
have had some consideration extended to
them by a reduction in the qualifying age
for old-age pensions, but numerous cases
have come under my observation which
point to the fact that in the very near
future the Government will be compelled
to amend this contributory scheme of
pensions to enable justice to be done to
the poor when they reach the age of 65.
I take this opportunity of giving an illustration of a case that came under my
observation during the past few weeks.
It is the case of a man who was engaged
in clerical work in one of the shipyards
in Belfast. For eight years he was a
contributor under the National Health
Jnsurance scheme. His salary then rose
to a point when his employers ceased to
stamp his card. He remained in that
position for a few years and then his
wages were reduced. He continued to
stamp his card for a year and then he
became unemployed.
He
That man is now in this position.
has passed the age of 65, and he is asked
to pay 56 stamps. After he pays the
5 stamps he must patiently wait until
he is 69 before he becomes qualified for
an old-age pension under this particular
Act, and thereby an injustice is inflicted
upon him that would not have been inflicted upon him had it not been for this
contributory pensions scheme. I know
6 MARCH 1928
Debate on the Address

Debate on the Address
as Leader of the House to address you
on the opening of another Session. The
unfailing courtesy shown to me by this
House during the past two years has
made my duties here very pleasant.
of numerous other cases of widows whose
husbands have passed away and who, unfortunately, had not the necessary
number of stamps on their cards, with
the result that their widows are now deprived of a pension at 65 years of age
in consequence of the provisions of this
Act.
Another thing about which I desire to
express my regret is that the Government
has not recommended an acceleration in
the process of the reclamation of land in
Northern Ireland. I understand there
is a large portion of reclaimable land, and
I think the Government should shoulder
this obligation. We are driving the
children of the working classes of Northern Ireland to go out to clear the primeval
forests of various Colonies, and I have
several of my own sons engaged in that
particular occupation in two or three of
the Colonies, and they would much rather
be at home exercising their labour power
here than clearing the primeval forests
of our Colonies. I think that is another
question on which the Government ought
to concentrate its attention. It would
be far better to reclaim land at home and
open up means of earning a livelihood
here than to send our young people away
to exercise their labour for the enrichment
of other countries.
I would also like to say a word with
regard to unemployment. I take this
opportunity to express my disapproval of
the treatment that many unemployed
men are receiving in connection with the
Belfast Corporation's labour test scheme.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The Board
of Guardians.
Mr. DORMAN: Skilled artisans, who
are unemployed and have been turned off
the Unemployment Register, are subjected to the degradation of having to
work two or three days a week, and instead of handling the value of the labour
they give to the public bodies concerned
they are paid in chits, which they have
to take to shops. I cannot conceive anything more degrading to a respectable
working man than to be sent to a shop to
get provisions in payment for his labour.
It is reducing the skilled artisan and respectable working man to the position of
a chattel slave.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): It is again my privilege.
I
am sure that I voice the unanimous feeling of the House in expressing deep regret at the news of the death since we
last met of a distinguished Member,
Senator T. M. Greer. Senator Greer
was a notable figure in our assembly. His
ability, tact, and knowledge of public
affairs make it very difficult to fill his
place.
I feel that I have your hearty support
in offering my congratulations to the
mover and seconder of the Humble
Address on the speeches which they have
delivered here to-day. Sir William
Coates has long been a well-known personality among us. In his public as well
.
as in his business career he has won the
admiration of his fellow-citizens and
countrymen. He has filled with great
efficiency the offices of High Sheriff and
Lord Mayor of Belfast; he has been successively President of the Belfast Chamber of Commerce and Stock Exchange,
and has for many years acted as Chairman of the Finance Committee of the
Corporation. If he speaks on topics
which relate to our industrial or financial
affairs it is with no mean authority.
I should like no less to congratulate
the seconder of the Motion, who is an
equally honoured representative in our
community. Senator McDowell has been
a Member of our body since Parliament
first met in 1921, and has not spared
himself in the public service. We are
especially indebted to him to-day in that
he has taken upon himself the task of
seconding the Address at very short
notice indeed.
Turning to the proposed legislation
mentioned in the Speech, in the first
place I may mention the Roads Improvement Bill which was read for a first time
last Session in another place, but which
was postponed owing to pressure of other
business. In one sense this delay has
been beneficial, inasmuch as it has
afforded to the Minister of Home Affairs,
and to the various interests concerned, an
opportunity of considering more fully the
provisions which appeared in that Bill,
and of effecting considerable improvements. On a similar occasion last year
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
I ventured to point out what was its
object and I hope that it will not be trespassing on the indulgence of the House.
again to remind hon. Senators of the
necessity for this legislation. In the
1926 Session this Parliament passed an
important Measure dealing with road
traffic, the main object of which was to
exercise more effective control, more especially in regard to the heavier vehicles.
Recent experience, however, has shown
that something more remains to be done,
and that it is necessary not only to effect
an improvement in the conditions of
traffic, but also to make the roads more
suitable for the ever-increasing number
of vehicles. In many cases widening will
be necessary, and it is also apparent from
the number of accidents which occur at
cross roads that road corners must be improved. It will further be necessary to
ensure the removal of obstacles to view,
including hedges, walls and so on, and
hon. Members will accordingly find that
the Bill which will shortly come before
them is in the main designed to improve
what may be called the lay-out of our
roads.
I would also refer to another Measure
which affects the public health of the
community, namely, the Bill providing
for the registration of Nursing and
Maternity Homes. This question has for
some time been exercising the minds of
the administration and the Government,
not only in Northern Ireland, but also in
Great Britain, and hon. Senators may have
observed that the Imperial Parliament
have already taken up the matter and
have passed a Bill dealing with the registration of Nursing Homes, while a Bill
for the registration of Maternity Homes
is, I understand, going to be introduced.
It is proposed to deal with the two aspects
of the question in Northern Ireland
under one Bill.
For some time past there has been
considerable agitation in various urban
centres in the Province in regard to the
congestion and inconvenience caused by
hawkers and other persons who erect
stalls in the streets on market and fair
days for the sale of their goods. With a
few exceptions, no measure of control has
hitherto been vested in the urban
authorities, and the Government considers that the time has now come when
each urban authority, subject to certain.
limitations, should be empowered to direct
A
the activities of these street traders.
Bill with this object in view will shortly
be laid before this House.
I think it would not be out of place to
refer briefly to our housing problem.
Parliament decided last Session to
abandon the principle of paying subsidies
on the larger type of what might be described as middle-class houses, but, on
the other hand, to continue and increase
the subsidies on the smaller kitchen type,
available for workers at low rates of
wages. Hon. Senators will, no doubt, be
pleased to hear that this concession is
being eagerly availed of; the records of
the Ministry of Home Affairs show that
the number of new houses now being
erected each month under the subsidy
arrangement is nearly double of that recorded for similar periods last year.
With regard to the drainage of the
River Bann this House will, I am sure,
be interested if I make a reference to this
question, which has been receiving the
earnest consideration of the Government
during the past year. It will be remembered that the Drainage Advisory Committee in their interim report of March,
1927, intimated that the scheme as originally estimated at a total cost of about
£900,000 would be impracticable, if it
were assumed that the County Councils
would have to meet half the expense; but
that if the cost were reduced to about
£600,000-as is possible by the linking
up of the drainage scheme with the proposed hydro-electric scheme-it might be
practicable to carry out the scheme upon
terms which would be acceptable to the
local authorities.
Since the issue of that report work has
proceeded along two distinct lines. On
the one hand, the Loans Guarantee Advisory Committee have been engaged in
examining an application for a guarantee
submitted by the promoters of the hydroelectric scheme. In view of the large
issues at stake this examination has entailed a most searching investigation into
the whole of the financial and engineering details of the scheme. As a result of
expert independent advice which the
Committee were able to obtain, questions
were raised in regard to several features
of the proposal, and it is not possible at
the moment to forecast what the ultimate
decision
may be.
On the other hand,
while these investigations were proceeding, the Director of Works was authorized
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
6 MARCH 1928
of
the
are at
to continue his examination
drainage problem by making experimental borings in the Lower Bann
channel, and such results as
present available tend to show that the
amount of rock excavation which would
be necessary to enable the channel to discharge the waters of Lough Neagh is not
so great as had been expected. There is
reason to hope, therefore, that the
drainage scheme, apart altogether from
the hydro-electric scheme, might
carried out at a cost substantially lower
than was originally estimated.
The necessity of providing for the more
rapid discharge of the waters draining
into Lough Neagh and the Bann has, unfortunately, been very clearly demonstrated during the past few weeks, and
in view of the information now being
obtained by means of the further borings,
the Government has informed the local
authorities concerned of its decision to
proceed with the general scheme, provided only that the authorities are prepared to make the contribution recommended by the Drainage Advisory Committee Whether they will acquiesce is
a matter on which the Minister of
Finance is now awaiting the views of the
Councils.
As the hon. Member who seconded the
Motion has said, it is gratifying to know
that the Province has continued to be
free from strikes or industrial disputes of
any substantial importance. The latest
figures show that the number of persons
registered as unemployed is about twenty
thousand less than at the beginning of last
year. The arrangements for admitting
claims under the Contributory Pensions
Act
working smoothly.
are
Relief
schemes for unemployed are being put
into operation by local authorities as
vigorously as possible.
In regard to the proposed legislation
for the amendment of the law relating
to Bankruptcy, I may say that this Bill
will be the result of the labours of the
Commission which has recently reported
on the subject. Bankruptcy legislation
in Northern Ireland is now 70 years old,
whereas the law in Great Britain was revised and consolidated in 1914, and again
in 1926, and it is with the object of
bringing our legislation up to date that
this Measure is being introduced.
Hon. Members will have noted that
Measures directed to the further alleviation of conditions among those engaged
in agriculture are promised in the
Gracious Speech. The Ministry of Agriculture has been giving earnest consideration to the great necessity for the grading
of all classes of farm produce. This is
already being done by all our competitors
in the British markets. There is also a
great need for the organizing of farmers
into societies or associations. Last year
the Government granted a sum of £1,000
to the Farmers' Union and to the Ulster
Agricultural Organization Society for this
purpose, and in the coming year it is
proposed to double this amount. I am
glad to inform the House that the Minister of Finance finds himself in the posi
tion to be able to supplement the already
substantial grant in aid of Agricultural
rates to the figure of £128,000 per
annum, thus approximating the provision
made in Great Britain. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.)
In regard to the question of the reclamation of land as a means of relieving unemployment, which Senator
Dorman alluded to, hon. Members will
remember the discussions, in regard to
drainage, which took place in 1926, and
the resulting Drainage Act, which received the Royal Assent towards the close
of that year.
While local authorities
may not, up to the present, have taken
great advantage of this Act, I may say
that the Government is always prepared
to consider most sympathetically any relief schemes which may be put forward
by these authorities under the Act.
will be appreciated that other schemes of
reclamation which might be possible may
present grave difficulties; for example,
any proposal in regard to reclaiming foreshores would probably be outside the purview of this Parliament, as of course foreshores come under the category of Reserved Services.
It
With regard to my own Ministry,
the Ministry of Education, there is not
much to say, except that local
authorities are continuing to work
smoothly in their various activities which
were made possible under the Education
Act. It is, perhaps, not a very auspicious time for any large increase in local
expenditure, but within the limits of the
finances at their disposal, committees are
doing what they can to develop the possibilities of the Act.
It is proposed to introduce in another
place two Bills on minor matters which
appear to require legislation. They are of
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
such very minor importance that I think
if I were to give a detailed description of
them in this speech it would remove all
necessity of making a Second Reading
speech at a later stage. I need not refer
to them further.
In conclusion, I think it will be
noticed that although the legislative programme proposed for the forthcoming
Session does not perhaps contain many
new features of first class importance, at
the same time it will be seen that they
cover a wide range and contribute to the
improvement of our social and economic
welfare. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Mr. LESLIE: I should not like this
occasion to pass without voicing my own
humble tribute to the mover and
seconder of the Address in reply to the
Gracious Speech. We do not perhaps
very often have the advantage of hearing
the voice of the hon. Baronet, who
moved the Address, but we always think
of him as being the earliest Senator of
all, as being the Senator who first spoke
as a Senator of Northern Ireland, for it
will be remembered that when Sir
William Coates filled the office of Lord
Mayor of the city of Belfast he was constituted the original Senator on whom
depended the mechanical formation of
the Senate, that is, the swearing-in and
so on of the Senators who came into
office. As we know, Sir William is now
an elected Member, and we hope that he
will so continue for many years to come.
We are always pleased, I think, to hear
my hon. Friend on my left (Mr.
McDowell). He, too, is one of our early
Senators, and one of the first elected, as
has already been stated by the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House. I
am sure we all appreciate his counsel and
assistance.
Passing to the Speech itself, I think it
is plain that there is nothing in the way
of looking back indicated in the Speech
or the Measures it adumbrates. The work
which has been done comparatively recently by the Parliament of Northern
Ireland is not being in any way disturbed. I am afraid perhaps that that
want of disturbance will not altogether
appeal to the hon. Senator in front of me
(Mr. Dorman). He seemed to desire
some modification of the recent arrangements made as regards the release from
control of certain types of houses. I
confess I was not very clear as to what it
was he desired should be done in that
connection, nor was I clear altogether to
what his criticisms of the Widows' and
Orphans' Contributory Pensions Act
were directed. He appeared to suggest
rather that the effect of that Measure
had been to deprive certain persons of
65 years of age of something or other.
Well, I confess I was entirely unable to
follow him. My recollection of the Act
in question is that it conferred certain
pensions on people attaining the age of
65. I cannot charge my memory with
the fact that under any circumstances
whatever it took away any pension or
right from anybody who had attained
that age.
There is no unsettling of
recent work. The legislation indicated
in the Gracious Speech is entirely directed
to breaking fresh ground, and I think it
will be found that the Measures brought
before us will not be in any sense controversial. They will require, I think,
very careful discussion, for such matters
as road improvement, the adoption of
children, legitimacy, and the incidence
to be attached to Trust Funds, will all
present problems of their own which will
require discussion and a good deal of
thought.
I feel that there are Measures mentioned here in which we should have been
greatly helped by the assistance of the
hon. Senator already referred to who is
no longer with us. Some of those matters
which I have instanced were matters in
which Senator Greer had special knowledge, which he had acquired by long
years of study and by the constant examination of problems connected with them
which came before him either in his private profession or when he was the solicitor to the Antrim County Council. As
has been said, we shall miss him very
much, and I think we shall all remember
him with the most kindly of recollections.
I do not think there is anything I myself desire to regret in the Speech in the
way of omission. One point that had
occurred to me was that I should like to
have seen this year, as other years, some
Measure of consolidation, in order that
the clearing-up process of the Statute
Book which the advance of years has
rendered necessary might go on, but I
rather gathered from what was said by
the noble Viscount that the Bankruptcy
(Amendment) Bill will be in the nature
of a Consolidating Bill, and if so I am
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
6 MARCH 1928
inclined to think that consolidation will
be very nearly enough consolidation for
one Session. Other matters were mentioned by the noble Viscount which are
not mentioned in the Speech.
First of
all there is the case of the River Bann.
I live not very far from the River Bann,
and I have seen the River Bann in flood.
I cannot help reflecting, however, that the
River Bann is not the only river that
sometimes overflows its banks. The
River Thames in England has given
cause for anxiety of late. I believe it
habitually overflows its banks. The
river on which Paris stands also occasionally overflows. The Bann, whatever it
may be responsible for, certainly has not
a monoply of overflowing.
What decision the County Councils may
take on the communication which I think
the noble Viscount said was addressed to
them by the Ministry of Finance-not
through the more ordinary channel, the
Ministry of Home Affairs-I do not
know. I suppose they will consider very
carefully how they are to raise such proportion of the assessment as may come to
them. It will be remembered that the
early Report which was adopted, I think,
in the Report of the Advisory Committee
to which the noble Viscount referred,
suggested that the rates should be imposed on a graduated scale. The totally
inundated portion was to pay so much.
the hypothetically alleged water-logged
portion was to pay something less, and the
rest of the County was to pay a smaller
figure. I do not think that proposal was
received very enthusiastically, the complaint generally being, of course, that the
rateable value of the inundated portion
was so small that it would have to be an
excessive rate to make any effect on the
total burden.
The objection that was taken by the
County at large was that large portions.
of each of the Six Counties were not in
the least concerned, and the water-logged
part pointed out that the different ways
in which different people were affected
according to their distance from the
river, its tributaries, and so forth, were
so many as to make an equitable assessment practically impossible. However,
that problem would seem to be for the
County Councils.
To pass from that, I desire to express
my humble thanks to the Government for
intimating that they propose to come to
Parliament to increase the sum to be
allocated to farmers in relief of rates.
I think the noble Viscount mentioned the
sum of £128,000. I am sorry to think
1 forget the exact sum allotted last year.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: £98,000
or £95,000.
Mr. LESLIE: That is my recollection,
that it was something under £100,000,
and what is proposed now is an increase
of not less than 25 per cent. That will
be an appreciable alleviation of a burden
which has become intolerable. I think
if we get that this Session, and if we get
some, at all events, of the Measures
which are set forth in the Speech we shall
feel that our time has not been spent in
vain, and we may look forward to a Session which, as I say, will not give rise to
any acrimonious debate, but which is
likely to give rise to very considerable
study and a good deal of discussion.
Colonel SINCLAIR: Will you allow
me to join in the felicitations offered to
the mover and seconder of the Address
for the admirable speeches with which
they have favoured us. They have been
in every way worthy of the occasion and
of the diginity of this House. I rise
for the purpose of making one observation, and to ask the noble Viscount
for some information in regard to the
proposed Bill for the registration of
nursing and maternity homes, which is
foreshadowed in the Gracious Speech. I
understood from the speech of the noble
Viscount that nursing and maternity
homes were to be consolidated in the
Bill, and while it may be held to be desirable to institute registration and inspection for places of this kind, happily
it has been testified that in the Imperial
Province the urgency for this is not so
great as it is in Great Britain. I should
like to ask the Leader of the House
whether this Maternity and Nursing
Homes Bill is likely to be initiated in
this Chamber or in another place.
I desire further to remark that I hope
the mistake will not be made of imitating
the Imperial Parliament by introducing
an exact copy of its recent legislation on
this subject. A great deal of the value
of the Imperial Bill for the registration
and inspection of nursing homes was, I
think, cut away by an exemption being
introduced at the last moment in favour
of a particular religious sect. If this
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
inspection and registration is to be of any
value it must be impartially administered,
and I hope the framers of the Measure,
which will be introduced for our consideration and discussion at a later date,
will not in any way imitate that part of
the Imperial Measure which provides for
the omission from its provisions of a
religious sect or other body. If evasion
is permitted in one direction the value of
the Bill may be discounted, and I think
that in this part of the world it would be
invidious to make any distinction in
favour of any religious sect. Therefore,
I hope the Government will introduce
their Bill in a form in which it can be
impartially adminstered among all classes
of the community, and which will give
the community the guarantee outlined in
the speech of Senator McDowell, so that
the interests of patients and all concerned
will be amply secured and protected.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In answer
to Senator Colonel Sinclair, the Bill
in question will be initiated in this
House. I think I would prefer to postpone discussion of the details of the
Measure until it is so introduced. I do
not think it would be the correct procedure to go into details at the present
time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That a humble address be
presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave
to offer our humble thanks to Your
Grace for the Speech which Your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament.
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors of Northern Ireland.
Legitimacy Bill [S.].
Bill to amend the law relating to
children born out of wedlock was presented
by the Viscount Charlemont, read a
First time, and ordered to be printed,
and to be read a Second time to-morrow.
(Bill No. 1).
Adoption of Children Bill [S.].
Bill to make provision for the adoption.
of infants was presented by the Viscount
Charlemont, read a First time, and
ordered to be printed, and to be read a
Second time to-morrow. (Bill No. 2.)
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this House do now adjourn.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
should like to ask the noble Leader of
the House two questions. I suppose we
shall not meet again for some days?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: To-morrow.
Colonel
SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
As a member of a County Council I am
particularly interested in the Roads Improvement Bill, and I should like the
Leader of the House to tell me, if not
now at some convenient date, when that
Bill is likely to come before this House.
We have not seen the Bill yet, but I hope
it will be circulated as early as possible.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
endeavour to give the hon. Senator the
information to-morrow.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Fifteen Minutes after
Four o'clock.
Committees Appointed

(Deputy Speaker), in the Chair.

of the House): I beg to move, that the
following Senators be named Deputy-
Speakers of the Senate during the present Session of Parliament:-
Mr. J. A. Long.
The Viscount Bangor.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Joint Standing Orders Committee.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, that the Senators following be
named of the Joint Standing Orders
Committee:-
Sir G. S. Clark.
Mr. J. Cunningham.
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
I beg
to move, that the Senators following be
named of the Joint Standing Committee

Legitimacy Bill [S.].

propose to proceed with the Second Reading of this Bill to-day, as it has not yet
been printed.
Adoption of Children Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.

thing applies to the Second Reading of

to-day.

LONDON MIDLAND AND

The following Report was made from

was referred the Examiners Certificate
relating to the London, Midland, and

Which Report was agreed to by the
House. and ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Governor.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I have to report to the
House that a Privy Councillor of
Northern Ireland has waited upon His
Grace the Governor with the Address of
the Senate, pursuant to the Order of 6th
March, to which His Grace has been
pleased to return the following answer:
I have received with great satisfaction the expression of your thanks
for the Speech with which I have
opened the present Session of Parliament.
COURT OF CRIMINAL APPEAL
IN NORTHERN IRELAND.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he is aware that the Home Secretary stated recently in the Imperial

Į
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
15 MARCH 1928
Parliament that he would be prepared to
give sympathetic consideration to any
responsible representatives from Northern
Ireland in favour of a Measure designed
to constitute a Court of Criminal Appeal
in Northern Ireland; and, if so, whether
it is proposed to take any action in the
matter.
.
Mr. LESLIE I must begin by pointing out that owing conceivably to the
cryptic character of my calligraphy there
is a misprint in the question as it appears
on the Order Paper. The word should
he "representations," not "representatives." My question has its origin in a
question raised in the House of Commons
at Westminster, when an hon. Member,
an eminent counsel who had served in
the office of Solicitor-General, asked the
Home Secretary the question indicated on
the Paper. In reply, according to my
information, and according to the newspapers and I have checked the reply
and the newspaper reports in the official
Hansard of the Imperial Parliamentthe Home Secretary said he would be
prepared to give sympathetic consideration to any responsible representations
from Northern Ireland in favour of a
Measure for establishing a Court of
Criminal Appeal in Northern Ireland. I
do not know why the hon. Member to
whom I have referred raised this question
in the Imperial House of Commons. I
can only hope that it was not owing to
any want of confidence in His Majesty's
judges in Northern Ireland. Certainly I
myself have not that want of confidence,
nor, I imagine, has anybody else, but
that is entirely beside the point
It is rather interesting, I think, that
Sir William Joynson-Hicks said he
wanted representations from Northern
Ireland, not from the Government of
Northern Ireland, as one would have expected, and the question that occurs to
one is, where does he expect to get his
representations from? A matter of that
sort is not a matter on which you would
expect, let us say, Boards of Guardians
or Select Vestries to pass resolutions.
Resolutions could only come from some
body really competent to express
opinion on the point. Grand Juries, of
course, occur to cne at once, but it must
be remembered that a Grand Jury, once
it is discharged, has no corporate
capacity; also that a Grand Jury would
naturally be very diffident of making a
representation in open Court on a matter
an
such as this, unless they had received
some intimation that their opinion was
desired.
The English Act itself was, as the
House will remember, passed in the year
1907. Up to that time there was no
Court of Criminal Appeal in England. I
cannot help thinking that if that Act had
been passed fifteen or twenty years
earlier it would have applied to Ireland
as a matter of course, but it is well known
that there was a period in the early part
of this century when Ireland was expressly excluded from ameliorative
Measures.
The provisions of the English Act are,
briefly, that a convicted person may
appeal as of right on a point of law.
There is an appeal on a point of law in
Ireland to-day. It is obtained by
various methods that I need not deal
with in detail to-day. He may also
appeal with the leave of the Court of
Appeal, or by a certificate of the Judge
who tries him, on a question of fact, or
mixed law and fact, or
on any other
ground which the Court of Appeal thinks
is a proper ground, and finally, he may
appeal against his sentence if it is not
a sentence definitely fixed by law if the
Court of Appeal gives him leave to do so.
Those conditions prevail in England but
they do not prevail in Northern Ireland.
The Secretary of State in England has
the power of referring all points of doubt
to the Court of Criminal Appeal, and of
course the Royal prerogative of mercy is
entirely unaffected. For myself, as perhaps will have been gathered from the
fact that I put down the question, I
should be inclined to favour the establishment of such a Court here for the
sake of uniformity-
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member will not go too far beyond the scope
of the question. The question really
only asks whether it is proposed to make
representations.
Mr. LESLIE: I will leave the matter
there.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: It is
a fact that in reply to a question in the
Imperial House as to whether the Government would introduce a Bill to constitute
a Court of Criminal Appeal in Northern
Ireland, the Home Secretary made the
reply indicated in the hon. and learned
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Member's question. As the hon. and
learned Member implies, the question is
one which deals with a matter reserved
under the Government of Ireland Act,
and it is undoubtedly one which merits
fullest consideration. I may
the very
assure the hon. and learned Member that
if after full inquiry the Government is
satisfied that the establishment of such a
Court in Northern Ireland is desirable
in the interests of justice, the necessary
representations will be made.
PRESERVATION OF ANCIENT
MONUMENTS.
on
the
The following question stood
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he is in a position to state what
action has been taken under the Ancient
Monuments Act, 1926, and what places
have been taken over.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I ask
the question which stands in my name
for two principal reasons. One is that I
realize that in this damp season the vegetation is rapidly growing, and some of
these ancient monuments require to be
very carefully looked after. I hope that
is being done. In the second place it
has occurred to me that ancient monuments and historical sites tend to bring
a great many tourists to our shores, and
I think we require as many tourists to
visit this part of the country as we can
get. I therefore hope that the Committee which is responsible for the care
of ancient monuments will keep them in
good order, and will take steps to make
them well known so that our friends in
America and in the Colonies will know
what ancient monuments and historical
sites are to be seen in Ulster.
In considering this question a few days
ago I could not help thinking how the
centre of gravity has been changing in
certain directions. It is not so long ago
that eggs were scarcely considered as one
of the principal assets in connection with
agriculture, but now we find that the
production of eggs is almost as valuable.
to Northern Ireland as is the cattle
trade. Similarly it is not very long ago
since we thought very little of these
ancient monuments. They were allowed
to go to ruin and crumble to the earth,
but I think it is now realized that they
are of far greater value than was formerly thought.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
very pleased the right hon. Senator has
raised this question. My answer to it is
rather a long one, but I hope the House
will excuse me as it is of greater interest
than some other subjects I have heard
discussed here. I have received the
following information from my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, whose
Department, as successor to the Board of
Works, has assumed State charge of
Ancient Monuments.
At all the important monuments caretakers have been provided. Steps have
been taken to improve access and to
provide fencing where needed, also
direction posts have been placed on main
roads pointing the way to monuments of
interest in the vicinity. At Greyabbey,
County Down, the vegetation, which was
masking the refectory, has been removed,
The refectory is now clearly visible,
showing beautiful, pointed windows of the
same type as those in the chapel.
At Devenish Abbey, in County Fermanagh, foundations have been excavated
so as to show the original plan. The
very perfect Round Tower there has been
re-pointed, and a staircase built inside to
enable the public to ascend the tower
and examine its remarkable architecture.
In the same county an ancient Cross on
Inishmacsaint Island, which was out of
the perpendicular and threatening to fall,
has been straightened. Special attention
has also been given to the group of County
Londonderry churches at Maghera, Dungiven, Bovevagh and Banagher, which
contain very interesting and beautiful
pre-Norman work. With the co-operation
of the Royal Air Force, aerial photographs
are being taken of ancient sites in Ulster,
and it is expected that interesting discoveries will be made.
Some extremely interesting acquisitions
have to be recorded. The executors of
the late Mr. Francis Joseph Bigger, the
noted Ulster antiquary, presented to the
Ministry for preservation the castle in
the town of Ardglass, known as "Jordan's
Castle," together with its contents. Mr.
Bigger had bought this castle in order to
save it from destruction, and had stored
Legitimacy Bill-
Oral Answers

15 MARCH 1928
in it a number of objects of antiquity.
It is the suggestion of the executors,
readily accepted by the Ministry of
Finance, that this castle should preserve
the memory of Mr. Bigger, and be maintained as an example of ancient architecture and as a museum of antiquities,
particularly those of County Down.
Two other important acquisitions are
pending at the present time-firstly,
through the kind co-operation of the War
Department, that of Carrickfergus
Castle, the most impressive and bestpreserved ancient stronghold in Ireland.
The Ministry of Finance hopes to deal
with this in a manner befitting its age
and importance by clearing away some of
the modern structures which obscure the
view and prevent a realization of the
dignity and solidity of the structure.
Arrangements are also pending for the
transfer of Dunluce Castle through the
public spirit of the Earl of Antrim. As
Carrickfergus is the strongest, so Dunluce
is perhaps the most picturesque ard
romantic castle in Ireland. It is, however, in a state of considerable decay,
and some expenditure will be necessary if
it is to be preserved as it stands for the
pleasure and instruction of this and
succeeding generations.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of the answer to the right
hon. Senator's question, I suppose it is
the Advisory Committee that recommends
to the Ministry of Finance what ancient
monuments shall be preserved. I have a
list of members of the Advisory Committee and I think the Committee could be
considerably improved. If I could make
some suggestions to the responsible
authority to attain that object I should
be very glad to do so. We have been
told of many important things that have
been done to preserve ancient monuments,
but in my County of Down there are a
great many minor ancient monuments
that ought to be looked after but are not.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I
should be very pleased to receive any
representations the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman may care to make.
Legitimacy Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
I
Hou.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
Members are no doubt aware that a Bill
of a similar nature has been before the
Imperial Parliament for a considerable
number of years. It was placed on the
Statute Book in 1926. I need scarcely
say that it is the considered policy of
this Government to keep in line with the
Imperial social legislation. The present
Bill does not call for a detailed explanation-its provisions are, on the whole,
tolerably clear. There are, however,
some few points to which I think it
necessary to draw the attention of hon.
Members.
The first Clause in the Bill contains
the most important provision. It provides that where the parents of an illegitimate person marry, the marriage is
to render that person, if still alive,
legitimate. It will be noticed that Sub-
Clause (2) modifies this provision in a
most important manner. It states that
the Act is not to operate so as to legitimate a person whose father or mother was
married to a third person at the time
when the illegitimate person was born.
Clause 3 sets out in some detail the
rights of legitimate persons to take
interests in property. It will be observed
that the Act is not to affect the devolution of property which is limited in such
a way as to devolve with a dignity or title
of honour. Under the existing law, the
property of an illegitimate person who
dies intestate without legitimate issue
goes to the Crown, and while it is customary for the Crown to allow grants to
be made from the property to the relatives
of the deceased illegitimate, they have
no legal claim.
Clause 4 of the Bill provides that
where a legitimated person dies intestate,
his relations shall be entitled to take the
same interests in the property as they
Iwould have been entitled to take had the
deceased been born legitimate.
Clause 5 goes a step further, and
provides for the succession on intestacy
of illegitimate persons and their issue
dying before the marriage of their
parents.
Clause 6 makes it clear that a
legitimated person will be subject to the
same obligations in respect of maintenance and support of himself or his family
as he would have been had he been born
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
legitimate. and, further, by Sub-Clause
(2) it is laid down that the benefits of the
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Act, 1925, are applicable.
to a legitimated child who was alive at
the date of the passing of that Act,
subject to the marriage leading to such
legitimation having taken place before
the 4th January, 1926.
In Clause 8 the difficulties which
may arise from the parents of an illegitimate child being married outside Northern Ireland are met by providing that the
person may become legitimated if the
marriage has taken place while the father
was domiciled in a country by the law of
which the person became legitimated by
virtue of such marriage.
I have already referred to the fact that
the estate of an intestate illegitimate
goes to the Crown.
Clause 9 provides that an illegitimate
child, on its mother dying intestate, may,
if there are no legitimate children, succeed
to the property. The Clause also allows the
mother of an illegitimate person to inherit the property of her illegitimate
child, if it dies intestate.
In Clause 10 there is a somewhat
important reservation. Nothing in the
Act is to affect the succession to any
dignity or title of honour, or make it
possible for any person other than the
direct legitimate heir to succeed to any
such dignity or title. In conclusion, I
would like to say that I hope hon. Members will lend their support to a Bill
which will help to remove the stigma
attaching to illegitimacy, and to give such
persons, as far as possible, the same right
in law as they would have had if they
had been born legitimate. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am bound to say that
I am not at all an enthusiastic supporter
of the Measure, the Second Reading of
which has just been moved by the noble
Viscount. I am aware, of course, that
Bills of the same nature as this in one
form or another have been brought before
one or the other Chamber of the Imperial
Parliament on many occasions during
recent years, and I am also aware that
in the year 1926 a Bill, on which the Bill
before us this afternoon is modelled, got
satisfactorily through all its stages in
both Houses and received the Royal
Assent. But thinking of that I cannot
but carry my mind back over almost seven
centuries to that Wednesday, the morrow
of Saint Vincent, or in other words 23rd
January, 1236, when a question somewhat
similar to this was pressed or instanted—
I think that was the word that was used
-on the Earls and Barons of England
by the Bishops of that day. Your Lordship will remember, and the House will
remember, the reply of the Barons of
England. The reply was, "Nolumus
leges Angliae mutari"--"We are unwilling that the laws of England should be
changed." That was the attitude taken
up 700 years ago, and until 1926 in
England it still held the field. That a
Measure of this sort should have been
passed in England really represents a
very great victory for South Eastern
Europe.
Let us look for a few moments at the
Bill, and the pros and cons of it. I will
take the cons first. There will be a
distinct danger if this Bill is passed of
those who hitherto would be regarded as
the rightful heirs being ousted by the offspring of a clandestine marriage.
Possibilities of that sort were referred to
in the debates of both Houses across the
water. I do not suppose myself that the
danger is any greater in Northern Ireland
than in England, but there is such a
possibility.
The noble Viscount spoke of the removal
of the stigma of illegitimacy. This Bill
deals with only a very limited class of
illegitimate children, and that limitation
of class was perhaps the point round
which the greatest battle raged when the
proposals were under discussion in
England. The proposals before us omitand I personally agree they rightly omit
-the legitimation of illegitimate
child born during the marriage of one of
the parents. But the fact that that
child is excluded does limit the very
large class of children who will be
benefited.
an
As regards the removal of the stigma, I
think it is only fair to recollect that
people for this purpose may be divided
into two classes, namely, the people whose
marriages are taken note of and the
people whose marriages do not attract
much attention. In the case of persons
whose marriages are not much observed
the illegitimacy of the child is very likely
unknown altogether, but in the case of
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
15 MARCH 1928
those people whose marriages do attract
attention a subsequent legitimation will
not remove such reproach as there may be
from that child as being regarded as
having been born before the marriage of
the parents. However, those objections
are perhaps not so important as the one
7 am about to mention now, and which I
think is really the greatest objection that
can be urged against this Bill.
Is this Bill, if it becomes an Act, going
to promote morality or the reverse? We
all know the position to-day. If a man
and woman have intercourse, and it is
expected that that intercourse will be
fruitful it is possible for the man to
marry the woman, and if that is done
before the child is born the child is born
We all know-we meet it
legitimate.
perhaps more in our private lives where
the difficulties of our neighbours are
brought before us-that many a marriage
has been made because it had been represented to the man that he had got the
girl into troubie, as the saying goes, and
it is up to him to marry her for the sake
of the child. If we pass this Bill that
inducement will be done away with.
one
I know that all I am saying has already
been said across the water. As
speaker put it, the inducement that there
were but a few weeks in which to do what
was right for the sake of the coming
child is gone, and instead of there being
a few weeks available there will be an
indeterminable number of years, and I
am very much afraid that will not make
for morality. Whether it will ultimately
lead to the re-establishment of the old
Scottish system of hand fasting, which
is dealt with better than I can deal
with it this afternoon, and perhaps in
more detail than I need deal with it, in
Sir Walter Scott's well known novel,
"The Abbot," I do not know.
That is the practice which did actually
prevail in Scotland of a man and woman
living together first, and if the woman
produced a male child then the man felt
bound to marry her, and according to
Scottish law the offspring was legitimate.
I am afraid the Scottish practice came in
for a good deal of criticism, but there
was this much to be said in defence of it:
that at that time there was a great
scarcity of clergymen in Scotland and
there were difficulties about getting a
marriage performed in the proper
manner.
We do not want to see that
practice established in Northern Ireland,
or anything approaching it.
Passing from its disadvantages, let us
look at the merits the Bill appears to
have. The Bill does not go the full
length of Canon law in the way of establishing legitimacy. I am not an
authority on the old Canon law, and I
take my statement from what was said by
the
the hon. Member for Swindon in
House of Commons in England. He is
a King's Counsel and his religious profession I do not know, but his statement
definitely was that the old Canon law
would legitimate a child on the subsequent marriage of the parents whether
or not one of those parents had been
married at the time the child was born.
That is to say, he put it plainly that no
difference was made between the offspring
of fornication and the offspring of
adultery. This Bili does not make this differentiation. The offspring of fornication
may be legitimated but the offspring of
adultery will not. That the Bill does not
go so far as the Canon law is to my mind
an advantage.
Again, the Bill has been agitated for
for some years by those who are interested
in philanthropic work. The Women's
Councils of Northern Ireland have, I
think, more than once pressed the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home
Affairs, to introduce a Bill, and now they
have got it. Further, if we refer-and
I think the noble Viscount did refer-to
the second sub-clause of Clause 6 we see
that there is distinct benefit obtainable
under the Widows', Orphans' and Old
Age Contributory Pensions Act, and I
think that is a benefit for which I myself
pleaded ineffectually when that particular
Measure was before this House.
In Clause 7 a legitimated child will
receive consideration in the matter of
death duties, and that consideration is
always agreeable, I I think, to
to this
Chamber. Further on, I think in Clause
9, we see that arrangements are made for
mutual inheritance as between mother
and illegitimate child. Finally, in favour
of the Bill, it will assimilate us with the
law of England. It is very inconvenient
that we should have in different parts of
the British Islands different laws dealing
with this matter.
I do not think this Bill will result in
421776
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
complete uniformity. I am not sure that
its effect will be to bring England and
Northern Ireland into complete line with
Scotland. I have no knowledge of the
arrangements in the Isle of Man or in
the Channel Islands or in the Irish Free
State. I am afraid that all the component parts of the British Islands will not
be in agreement but still, if we must disagree with somebody, I think we should
all agree we should not select England as
the point of disagreement.
However, I think on the whole the
House will feel that public opinion will
support this Measure and that, therefore,
they will give it a Second Reading and
pass it to the Committee stage. When
we reach that stage, apart from two
minor matters of drafting, as to which
very possibly an explanation will be
forthcoming, there will, I think, be two
distinct points to which we ought to
direct our attention.
It will be observed that under the
Second Clause of the Bill we provide, as
has been done in England, that a petition
under the Legitimacy Declaration Act
(Ireland), 1868-the English Act was ten
years older-should be brought either in
the High Court, as at present, or in the
County Court. The County Court is a
new power. It was criticized very much
in the House of Commons, but the decision was taken in England to give such
power to the County Court, and it is
proposed to do the same here. I do not
object to that, but we have recently seen
in the newspapers the account of a case
brought in England under their Legitimacy Declaration Act, and I think I
say the public conscience was shocked by
the revelation there made.
may
It transpired that a lady, seventy years
of age, had had to come forward and
confess that many years ago she had lived
for SO many years before marriage
with so and so, and the result had been
certain children, some of them now married, whose names were given. I say the
public conscience was shocked by that.
It certainly attracted attention in the
newspapers, and I think there will be a
strong feeling that that should not occur
over here if any case of this sort should
come forward.
Where a case under the Legitimacy
Declaration Act is heard by a Judge of
the High Court-it is perfectly plain
from the statute whether English or
Irish-that Judge has power to hear
such a case in Chambers. I shall ask the
House in the Committee stage, if this
Bill goes through, to provide definitely
a similar power for the County Court
Judge. We shall be following a precedent we have set ourselves, and I do not
think there will be any great objection
to it.
The other question that I think we ought
to consider is, perhaps, rather more recondite. We provide towards the end of the
Bill that the succession to a dignity or
title of honour should not be affected.
We have said nothing in the Bill, and
they said nothing in England, as to the
question of the right to use armorial
bearings. Armorial bearings are not,
perhaps, a dignity, and are rather a
token of dignity, but they are an estate
of inheritance, and it is not, I think,
desirable that people who are not strictly
entitled to such a bearing as is borne by a
legitimate person should claim to get
recognition of the legality of their adoption of the same arms.
I mention the matter because, as the
House knows, the method of securing
arms in Ireland and
recognition of
England are not, and have never been,
quite the samne. The Ulster King-of-
Arms has power which the College of
Arms has not got, and therefore I propose, with great diffidence, to put down
words which will enable us to consider
the question of whether the provisions we
have made with regard to the dignity or
title of honour are sufficiently protective
of right to armorial bearings. On the
whole, though in a somewhat lukewarm
manner, I shall give my support to the
Second Reading, and I believe that on
the whole the Bill will be received with
general satisfaction, although I do not
suggest it will satisfy everybody.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. and learned
Senator has put a question that will undoubtedly be very widely asked in regard
to this Measure: Is this Bill calculated
to promote morality? It is an undoubted
fact that illegitimacy is largely confined
to the working classes, and I remember a
celebrated politician who recently held a
most eminent position under the Crown
declaring some thirty years ago that
illegitimacy in this country was connected
with a certain organization and a certain
religion.
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
15 MARCH 1928
Mr. McDOWELL: Give their names.
Mr. DORMAN: It was stated some
thirty or forty years ago that the proportion of illegitimacy in Ireland could
be predicted by the number of Orange
Lodges. At this particular period when
the politician was propagating this theory
as to the origin of illegitimacy a great
anti-Protestant Mission was proclaimed
throughout the land in which it was
alleged that illegitimacy was due to Protestantism.
Both of these conclusions
were absolutely false, because they were
based on wrong knowledge. The large
proportion of illegitimacy was not due to
the prevalence of Orange Lodges or to
the Protestant religion, but to the conditions under which the working classes
lived.
In this particular area of Ireland, the
Six Counties, a system of hiring prevailed
amongst agricultural labourers that did
not prevail in other parts of the country,
and which debarred those lusty young men
and women of the agricultural labour
class from the opportunity of setting up
a legitimate home. As the urge for reproduction becomes most clamant at a
certain age these people, under the impulse of that urge, and finding it impossible to set up legitimate homes,
illegitimately cohabited with one
other and produced a large percentage
of illegitimate children in this particular
part of Ireland.
an-
I hold that these circumstances have
been the cause of the large proportion of
illegitimacy, and even now I believe that
when the Registrar-General's returns
come to be made up for the period
through which we have just passed and
are passing, when unemployment is rife
and house rents are so high, it will be
discovered that there is a very large proportion of illegitimate children over and
above what would have been the case had
normal conditions ruled in our midst.
The old statement that was made 800
years ago can never be made again,
namely, "We
are unwilling that the
law of England shall be changed."
There is scarcely an iniquity that has
been perpetrated on the community that
has not been bolstered up by that statement. Immediately any reforms have
been determined upon by the people they
have been met by this 800 years' old cry
about interfering with the
laws of
England.
We here and all modern Parliaments
exist for the specific purpose of changing
ancient laws. The progress of society has
made it inevitable that Parliament must
necessarily devote a large portion of its
time to repealing or amending ancient
laws. The progress of civilization and of
justice demand that they should be
modified to suit present conditions.
I do not think this Measure is calculated to increase immorality. I believe
it will have a contrary effect. Only
yesterday 1 came in contact with a man
who is the father of an illegitimate child.
The mother is paying for its upkeep in
a working-class home, and she is engaged
in a factory earning her livelihood. The
man comes to the house weekly or fortnightly and gives the woman a certain
proportion of his wages for the upkeep of
that child, and yesterday, in the presence
of others, he expressed his regret that his
economic circumstances, the low wages he
is receiving in return for his labour, and
the high rents that prevail, prevent his
entering into legitimate marriage. I do
not believe this Bill will be calculated to
stimulate illicit intercourse. I believe it
will be calculated to arouse contrary
thoughts in the minds of young men.
would be an admirable thing if we could
attain the moral position when all our
young men would adopt the attitude of
Job by making a covenant with their eyes
that they will not look upon a maid till
they have entered into the bonds of holy
matrimony.
It
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
had not intended to speak on this Bill,
but I should like to say a word in reply
to the point made by Senator Leslie when
he used the Latin quotation of the reply
of the barons of England to the bishops.
That, of course, referred to the time of
Magna Charta. Many Acts that were
in force at that period have since been
repealed and new laws have been made
to take their place.
LESLIE: The date I mentioned
Mr.
was 1236.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
That was the time of Magna Charta and
many Acts that were in force then have
been repealed. I always thought that
Senator Leslie had a very tender heart,
but after his remarks to-day I have rather
changed my opinion. After all, this
Measure will do a great deal of good to
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
many innocent people who through no
fault of their own are suffering under
a severe handicap. The fact of their
being born illegitimate has made no difference to those who were born in wedlock, because as long as any legitimate
children or their descendants were alive
illegitimate children could not succeed to
an estate.
I agree with Senator Dorman when he
says this Bill will increase morality instead of having the reverse effect. With
regard to the case mentioned by Senator
Leslie in which an old lady seventy years
of age had to appear before a court in
England, I think she deserved the
greatest praise and sympathy for the fact
that her maternal feelings were so strong
that when she had the opportunity of
iegitimating her children she did not
hesitate, but rejoiced that she was able
to do so. There may be faults in the Bill,
but I think it contains more good than
evil, and I hope it will pass through this
House and become the law of the land.
It has struck me that, after all, we
are all illegitimate, because I never knew
that Adam and Eve were ever married.
Marriage was not necessary then, and it
is only a custom that has since been
adopted for the sake of the morality of
the world.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
a matter of this kind it is no use saying
that because a few centuries ago the law
was so and so we should not change it.
We must realize that those old barons of
England-and I think they deserve a
good deal of praise and admiration-had
slaves, and slavery has been done away
with in this country. Scotland has had
this law I think from Reformation times.
The law of Scotland is that if people
marry it legitimises children born before
the marriage, and as far as I can find out
the morality of Scotland is no lower than
that of England. We know that Stranraer and other parts of Scotland do get
a certain name, but I have seen the
figures and I believe the morality of
those parts of Scotland is just as good
as it is in our own country or in England.
I do not think this Bill will in any
way inflict hardship on anyone. On the
contrary, I am convinced it will confer
a great benefit on those who through no
fault of their own have been born in this
unfortunate position. I cannot help
thinking that if we went back 1,900 years
the Teacher of that day would have
worked on something like the lines of
this Bill. We know He treated things
as perhaps some of our Puritan people of
these days would not treat them, and we
would only be following His example if
we passed this Bill, as I trust we shall
do.
I
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
feel justified in speaking in favour of
the Second Reading of this Bill. I am
sure it will give the working classes some
little satisfaction to know that a Bill
similar to that which has been passed in
England has been introduced in Northern
Ireland. So far as the working classes
are concerned I do not agree that there
is any more immorality in this country
than there is in any other country.
think we might be proud of the fact
that Northern Ireland is more moral
than any other part of the United
Kingdom. As has been said by
previous speakers, this Measure will be an
act of charity to many poor innocent
individuals who are born in the unfortunate position which this Bill seeks to
remedy. It removes from them a stigma
which their fellow workers might hold
over their heads for the remainder of
their lives. It is unfair that a child who
was born a month before the marriage of
its parents should have to go through the
world as illegitimate, whereas the child.
born a month after the marriage is
legitimate. Therefore, I think this Bill
will remove an unfair distinction. For
the life of me I cannot understand what
Orange lodges have to do with this question. I have heard all sorts of accusations made against this unfortunate body,
but the latest and the worst is that
attributed to them by Senator Dorman.
Mr. DORMAN: I only quoted what an
eminent politician said. I have the
highest respect for the Orange Order.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
glad to hear that, because I understand
the hon. Senator was at one time a member of the Orange Order. So far as the
Orange Order is concerned I think it is
very likely to be more moral than any
other section of the community, because
morality is one of the first things that is
instilled into a member when he first
enters the portals of a lodge. I give the
Bill my entire support, particularly on
behalf of the working classes, because I
am satisfied the workers in this country
are as moral as they are in any other
part of the United Kingdom.
Legitimacy Bill-
Adjour ment
15 MARCH 1928
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill, and my
reason for so doing is that it offers protection to a small section of the community who are not in any way to blame
for their existence. I do not agree with
the statement made by Senator Dorman
that the working classes and the poor are
the greatest sinners in this respect. They
are not. The hon. Senator made reference to farm labourers and the young
women who are employed by farmers in
the country, and those who know no
better would gain the impression that
farm labourers and servant girls were
the most immoral people in the country.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. BARCLAY: They are not. As
for the city of Belfast, I roam about just
as much as the hon. Senator, and I do
not see much wrong in the city. There
may be an odd case here and there, but
you will find that anywhere you go. I
listened to a lecture given here some time
ago by a reverend gentleman who dressed
up in dongarees and went round what he
called the slums of the city of Belfast.
He told some wonderful tales. I have
been round those streets, and I have passed those public houses, although I was
never in them, and I have not seen anything that he saw. I must be nearsighted, and I think Senator Dorman
must also be able to see further than I
if he says Belfast is most immoral, and
that there is more illegitimacy amongst
the working classes than amongst those
who are better off.
To my mind the present law is very
anjust to the people this Bill seeks to
assist. We cannot deny the fact that
these people have been in the past, and
are at the present moment, very useful
and industrious citizens.
I am
very
pleased that this Bill will abolish a great
injustice, and will give these people a
proper place in society and an interest in
the inheritance of their parents. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
very grateful to the House for the reception it has given to this Bill. I think
the reasons adduced in its favour considerably outweigh those advanced against
it. Personally I do not think that any
increase in immorality will result from
the Bill becoming law for the reason that
the philanthropic societies who originally
urged its adoption in the Imperial House
of Commons did so from experience of
the conditions of the people who are most
likely to fall into difficulties of that kind.
If they had thought morality would be
endangered it is not likely they would
have advocated the Bill.
was
I would like to say that I did not
understand Senator Dorman to say anything about the Orange Order that could
be objected to. He merely quoted what
other people had said, and no doubt it
After all,
absolutely incorrect.
people talk a great deal of nonsense about
all organizations in the world, including
the Orange Order, and it is only right
that those people should be refuted,
Senator Dorman did his best to do.
Therefore, I think it is a pity that statements should be attributed to him which
he did not make at all. In reply to

before the Committee stage is reached.
