Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Measure, to which I have
now to ask the House to give a Second
Reading, is made up of a number of miscellaneous matters affecting certain Excise
and other duties, and, as it is a Money
Bill, it is only competent for the House
to consider the Measure as a whole and
not to amend any of its provisions.
It will be observed that the first three
Clauses deal with certain Excise duties.
The first Clause has for its object the fixing of the licence duty to be paid by
wholesale spirit dealers who may take out
a special dealer's licence. This licence,
which was authorized by the Intoxicating
Liquor Act of last year, applies to the case
of a wholesale dealer who wishes to sell to
retailers the small quantities of the
various kinds of spirits, as permitted in
Sub-section (1) of Section 5 of last year's
Act. It has been agreed after considerable discussion that the charge for this
new licence should be three guineas. Hon.
Members will notice that in the Bill on
the Table of the House this licence fee is
still shown as five guineas, but the amount
is now reduced to three guineas.
By the second Clause it is intended to
give an alternative for licence duty purposes in regard to the valuation of premises to the licensees of public-houses who
have been obliged to carry out structural
alterations in accordance with the Intoxicating Liquour Act of 1923. The
present position is that these holders have
two courses open to them in regard to
valuation, namely, either to pay licence
duty on the old valuation of the whole
premises, including that part which is no
longer used for the sale of liquor, or to
request that the portion set apart for the
sale of liquor should be revalued with
the attendant risk of having the poor-law
valuation of the premises increased. This
Clause of the present Bill gives the trader
the option of having a revaluation for
licence duty purposes of the restricted
portion only-but, according to the principles of the 1852 Valuation Act-without
affecting his poor-law valuation for
ordinary rating purposes, and if this
option should not prove to be to his
advantage he may continue to have his
licence duty assessed as hitherto.
Paragraph (a) of the Clause gives the
licensee this new option, while paragraph
(b) ensures that if the new alternative is
accepted the Commissioner of Valuation
shall proceed to revalue the licensed portion of the premises having regard to
present-day values, which is the method
prescribed in the Valuation (Ireland) Act
of 1852. The fee for such a re-valuation
will be on the same scale as that for
ordinary re-assessment. The trader's
safeguard against payment of a higher
rate of duty will be found in the proviso
to paragraph (b), which provides that in
no case shall the restricted portion of the
premises, if revalued, be assessed at a
greater amount than that which has
hitherto been assigned for the entire
premises.
Clause 3 of the Bill deals with the
question of gun licences and game certificates. The collection of these duties bas
hitherto been performed by Customs and
Excise officers acting as the agents of the
Ministry of Finance. It is felt, however,
that our own administration here is now
able directly to undertake this service,
and, in any event, as the number of Customs and Excise officers in our area is
being reduced, consequent upon the transfer to the Ministry of Labour of the Old-
Age Pensions service, it would be difficult
for them to continue the performance of
the service of issuing gun licences and
game certificates. It is proposed in the
Cause which we are considering to utilize
Finance Bill
Superanuation Bill
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
the services of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, who are, without doubt, most
familiar with the people concerned, and it
is felt that they are in the best position
to check the evasion of these duties.
In Clause 4 it is desired to give relief
in the matter of stamp duty to companies
which are being reconstructed or amalgamated. As the law stands at present, on
amalgamation or reconstruction of existing companies full stamp duty has to be
paid on the same scale as for new capital,
and this is considered to be burdensome.
A similar Clause to that now proposed was
contained in an
the
Act passed in
Imperial Parliament last year, and has
proved to be very satisfactory. I think
that hon. Members will agree that our
industries here should be in no worse
position than those in Great Britain in
this respect.
Clause 5 is intended to clear up a matter on which there is considerable doubt
at present. In a Letter or Power of
Attorney which contains in the instru
ment the names of more than one person,
there is at present a doubt whether the
stamp duty of 10s should be charged in
respect of each such person, or whether
one charge is sufficient to cover them all.
The present practice is that only one fee
should be charged, but it is thought desirable to put the question beyond dispute, and it is accordingly proposed in
this Clause to provide specifically that
one charge in these circumstances is
sufficient.
The next Clause--Clause 6--has special
relation to the Roads Improvement Bill,
which is at present under consideration
of this House. Hon. Members will recollect that under the Bill it is proposed
to register Vesting Orders of the Ministry
of Home Affairs prior to the settling of
the matter of compensation. The amount
of the stamp duty, however, on such a
Vesting Order cannot be settled until
the compensation is fixed, and so there
would be a difficulty in registering the
Order as, of course, the Order would not
be complete until the stamp duty is impressed on it. An escape from this difficulty is provided in Clause 6, by making
the Order exempt from stamp duty, and
providing instead a stamp duty on receipts issued at a later date in respect of
I beg to
the compensation payment.
move.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise for the
purpose of criticizing in any way the
details of the Measure which the noble
Viscount has just explained, but I do rise
to take exception, if I may, to his opening words. He referred to the Bill
before us as a Money Bill which it was
not within the province of this House to
amend. I think the noble Viscount went
too far. There are provisions in this
Measure which it seems to me it is perfectly competent for this House to
amend, if it so desires, although I do not
say it will wish to do so, and, therefore,
I hope the Bill will be put through Committee in the ordinary way. The Bill
does not become a Finance Bill in the
sense of being a Bill which this House
cannot touch in detail merely on account
of its being headed "Finance Bill," and
I, therefore, repeat the suggestion that
the Bill should be committed in the ordinary manner.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I have no objection to the Bill
being sent to the Committee in the
ordinary way.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.

CLAUSE 1 (Compulsory acquisition of
land for road purposes)—
Where a local authority desire, for
the purpose of improving or widening
an old road or street or making a new
road or street, to acquire any land
(otherwise than by agreement) they
may if they think fit, notwithstanding
anything in any enactment, apply to
the Ministry of Home Affairs for an
order vesting the land in the local
authority in accordance with, and
subject to, the provisions of
of the
Schedule to this Act, and the Ministry
of Home Affairs shall have power to
make such order.
The following Amendments stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:-
Page 1, lines 6 and 7, leave out "otherwise than by agreement" and insert by
agreement or otherwise."
Page 1, line 13, at end insert as a new
sub-section:-
("(2) In this section the expression.
'road' includes a footpath adjacent to a
road."
Mr. LESLIE: The first two Amendments standing in my name on the Paper
I do not propose to move. Inquiry which
has been made behind the Chair, as the
expression goes, shows that the first
Amendment would not help us, and that
the second can be more conveniently dealt
with on the definition Clause. I, therefore, begin by suggesting that at the
end of Clause 1, on page 1, we should
insert the following new Sub-section:-
(3) In this Section, and, where
not inconsistent with the context of
that Schedule, in the Schedule to this
Act, the expression 'land' includes any
easement or right over land.
It seems to me that words of that sort
are required. A suggestion to that end
was, I understand, made in another place,
but it did not materialize, if my information is correct, in the way of an absolute
Amendment on the Paper. Similar words
occur in the Local Government Act of
1898, and also in a Measure for which
I have less respect, the Development Act
of 1909. It seemed to me that the insertion of these words are necessary, but it
is not an Amendment I should wish to
press on the Minister of Home Affairs it
for any reason he does not desire to insert the words in the Bill. For the
moment I beg to move.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): May I again thank
this honourable House for the opportunity
they have afforded me of coming here this
afternoon. My hon. and learned Friend
(Mr. Leslie) has stated that he is not
very anxious about the fate of this
Amendment. I hope he is not, because
I can conceive many grounds on which, if
it was passed, it would very adversely
affect the Bill. He referred to the Act
of 1898, and said that a similar provision
was inserted in that Act. There is no
doubt that there is a reference to it in
that Act, but I suggest that those who
framed that Act put it in without fully
considering the matter, because, so far as
I can ascertain, it has never been acted
upon.
207 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Sir Dawson Bates]
But may I suggest one or two reasons
why it would be a mistake to accept this
Amendment. We are dealing here with
the compulsory acquisition of land for the
purpose of making and widening roads.
It is perfectly true that roads were originally tracks made over the land of the
owners, but as time went on a great deal
of confusion was caused, and finally, as
a result of a certain decision in the courts
to which I referred when last in this
House, and with which Senator Leslie is
familiar, modern legislators made up
their minds that in future the bed and
soil of the land would have to be
acquired May I quote one example of
the difficulty that would arise if such an
Amendment as that proposed was carried
into effect. Senator Leslie is aware that
under the Land Purchase Act when a
holding is vested in the tenant it is subject to the instalments payable in respect
of the purchase money, and one of the
conditions of that vesting is that it is
illegal-and I think this is the only restriction the holding-for the new
landlord to alienate a portion of that
land without the consent of the Ministry
of Finance, who represent the State.
on
The reason for that will be obvious to
hon. Members, because otherwise it might
mean that farms might be divided up
into small portions, and you would fall
back into the old days of congested districts, with no security to the State for
the money advanced. Curiously enough,
this does not apply to easements, and so
far as I can see there is nothing to prevent a tenant farmer who has acquired his
holding granting an easement. It has
not to be registered, and the State cannot object to it, and if we put this
Amendment into effect it might mean
that the local authority could acquire the
right to take an easement, and the purchase money would be paid to that tenant
farmer, and he would not be under any
obligation to pay any of that money to
the State in reduction of his annuity.
To that extent the security of the estate
would suffer. That is only one of the
very grave objections to the acceptance
of this Amendment.
But I would ask hon. Senators to
realize that this Bill is for the compulsory acquisition of land. There is nothing to prevent the local authority
entering into a voluntary agreement with
the landlord. There is nothing to prevent an adjoining owner dedicating of
his own accord a portion of his land for
the use of the public,, as many landlords
have done, but this is a compulsory system, and to introduce an entirely new
method of taking land by way of easement would render the operation of the
Act most unsatisfactory. I can see no
object in this Amendment. I think it
has been suggested that it would be
a cheaper method of dealing with the
question, but assuming for a moment
that this Amendment was put into operation, what would happen? The local
authority would go to the farmer and
say: Oh, we are not going to take
your land; we are going to have an easement over your land for all time, but
we are not going to pay you for the value
of the bed and soil of that land." Is it
going to satisfy the farmer or the landlord to know that he is not going to be
compensated for the public having this
right of way over his farm.

In theory the soil under the road would
still belong to the landowner, but what
is that to him. I fail to see that the
landowner will be in any better position
if this Amendment were passed than he
will be under the Bill as it stands. As
I said the other day-and I do not wish
to detain the Committee a moment longer
than is necessary--I have heard of landowners voluntarily dedicating land for
the use of the public for all time, because
hon. Members will realize that once land
is dedicated, it is dedicated for all time,
and cannot be taken away. Therefore,
it is most necessary that in the case of
compulsory acquisition all superior interests and everything should be acquired
by the local authority so that the local
authority may dedicate the right of passing and repassing to the public. Under
these circumstances, and after this explanation, I hope the hon. and learned
Member will not press his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: As I have already mdicated, I am prepared to ask the Committee to allow me to withdraw this
Amendment. I should perhaps explain
that the easement I was thinking of was
I was not
a purely ancillary easement.
thinking of a right of passage and
re-passage.
Amendment, bv leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie: :-
209 Roads Improvement Bill-
17 MAY 1928
Committee
Page 1, after line 13, insert as a new
Sub-section:

said to us himself when last he was here
that we were living in the year 1928,
and we ought not to be too hidebound by
the methods of the land legislation of past
times.
Nothing in this Section or in the
Schedule to this Act shall operate to
divest the owner of any land acquired
under this Section of an estate in feesimple in such land if such owner shall
have notified the Ministry of Home
Affairs that he is prepared, with or
without compensation as the case may
be, to dedicate such land to the use of
the public for the purposes of a public
road or street, and to be maintained
for such purposes by the
authority.
local
Mr. LESLIE: I now approach something in the nature of what the right
hon. Gentleman has just dealt with.
This Amendment does deal with the main
question. First of all, I am afraid I
must say there are two mistakes in the
Amendment on the Paper. The first one
is the printer's fault. The word "divert"
appears, whereas I mean, of course,
"divest.' The second mistake is in
the last line, and that is my fault, for
which I apologize. I have said "this
Schedule instead of "the Schedule to
this Act."
The CHAIRMAN: This is in the
next Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, in the consequential
Amendment. Those who are acquainted
with the first and second editions of the
Amendment Paper will see that I have
made several attempts to try to get a form
of words which would meet with my own
satisfaction at any rate. and I am not
sure whether I have yet succeeded in doing
so, as I am still uncertain whether what
is done here could not better be done in
the Schedule to the Bill, but the point I
want to get at is this. I want to make
it possible for the owner of land who is
to have his land taken, or who is
threatened with having his land taken for
the purpose of widening an existing road
or street, or making a new road or street,
to retain the ownership of the bed and
soil while dedicating to the public the
right of passage and re-passage. I have
no doubt I shall be told that I am trying
to turn a fee simple into a fee complex.
but I cannot help that. I may be told
that the idea is new, but if so I may remind the right hon. Gentleman that he
I do not think, as a matter of fact, that
there is anything new about the proposal.
When land has been dedicated in the past
for purposes of a public road the owner
always has retained the fee simple in that
road, subject only to the right of passage
and re-passage by the public, and to the
right of the local authority to do all such
things as were necessary for maintaining
that road for that passage and re-passage
of the public. I had hoped that we would
have had this afternoon the support of the
hon. Senator, His Majesty's Lieutenant
for the County of Armagh (Mr. Armstrong) who spoke on very much the same
lines as I am now trying to speak on the
occasion of the Second Reading of this
Bill, but I received a note from him this
morning in which he says that he regrets
that owing to a sharp attack of rheumatism
he is unable to be here this afternoon to
support this Amendment, with which he
entirely agrees both in substance and
words.
Let us see what advantages will remain
to an owner if he does get his bed and
soil. He gets the right to any minerals
there
may be under the land, and
minerals may be discovered later on if
they are not known at present. It may
be interesting to inquire what would happen if anybody suspected minerals under
a road, and asked for an inquiry as to
whether there were or were not minerals
there. Again, the owner, if he happened
to be possessed of the land on both sides
of the road, has some rights of tunnelling
from one side to the other provided he
does not disturb the surface of the road
or render it impracticable for the passage
of the public. He has the right of placing
things high over the road, and he has
the most valuable right of preventing the
misuse by the public of their right of
passage and re-passage; that is what may
be conveniently called trespassing. Senator Armstrong, if I remember rightly,
cited to the House on the Second Reading
the case of the Duke of Rutland against
a man named Harrison. He was a man
who interfered with grouse driving over
butts by passing and re-passing.
hon. Senator also referred to the Newmarket case of a tipster watching horses, but
he did not, I think, refer to the case in
The
Roads Improvement Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
which Rupert Brooke, the poet, was involved, generally called the moth-hunting
case. That was the case in which a party
of students took certain steps which had
the effect of interfering with pheasants
belonging to an irate landowner in
that neighbourhood. The subsequent
proceedings do not very much matter, but
damages were awarded to the adjacent
owner on the ground of disturbance.
If you take away the right of the adjoining owner to prosecute, or take summary steps to rid himself of trespassers,
you turn the county road practically into
a kind of no man's land, on which anybody can do anything he likes. It is
no use saying that if there is trouble
going on you can get the county council
to rush in, because the county council is
some way off, and action has to be taken
at once. Then again, in this case the
county councils do not want the bed and
soil of the road. They say quite definitely that it would be an embarrassment
to them, and if you insist upon taking
something from someone who does not
want to give it, in order to bestow it on
someone who does not want to receive it,
there will be trouble. If you take this
land, be it large or small, the first thing
that will happen will be that the owner
will require his rating assessment readjusted. Then the county council will
be left with an ownership which they do
not want, and I am afraid you will provoke opposition in a way you will not
provoke it if you will allow the man to
simply grant what I will call for a
moment to use a word that has just
been used in another place a quasi easement, the right of passage and repassage.
Now we come to the question of how
we can do it, and I thought we might
do it in this way. If a man tells the
Ministry of Home Affairs that he will,
possibly for nothing or possibly for compensation, dedicate land to the public,
he should be allowed to do so, and he
should not lose his compensation if he
wants it. There must be precedents in
Ireland for dedicating with compensation, because, as is known, there were
originally in this country, as the right
hon. Gentleman has just reminded us, a
good many tracks which developed into
roads, and such tracks were across somebody's land, and they were closed up and
new roads were made. The new road
was very often over the same owners property as the old track, and when the old
track was closed up there was compensation for the dedication of the new road.
It seems to me that this suggestion is of
very great importance, and I will go so
far as to say that if we cannot meet this
difficulty in some way or other, the whole
Clause, with the Schedule, ought to be
thrown out of the Bill.
I am not in the least wedded to the
particular form of the Amendment, but
I do insist on the point that you have
no justification for taking from a man a
greater estate than is necessary for your
purpose, and for doing that without any
security that there will be even a publie
inquiry before it is done. There is nothing in the Clause before us to require the
holding of a public inquiry before this
land is taken. That is entirely at the
option of the Ministry, and that is one
of the difficulties. If I am unable to succeed in getting some arrangement made
in this matter, I am afraid I shall have to
ask the Committee to throw out the entire Clause. If, on the other hand, we
can get something in it will be necessary
to consider the next Amendment on the
Paper, which is really the corollary of
this one. Whether it will be necessary
to do that now, or whether it will stand
over for Report is a question for you.
Words are on the Paper, and I hope the
Committee will look at them in order to
see that I have endeavoured to provide
against any injustice to the public of any
sort whatever. I have the support, as I
have said, of His Majesty's Lieutenant
for the County of Armagh, who is not
with us, in thinking that the words on
the Paper are sufficient, and accordingly
I beg to submit them to the Committee.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The remarks
which I made in regard to the earlier
Amendment, which I thought my hon.
and learned Friend moved for the same
purpose as this one, covered a good deal
of the ground which it is necessary to
cover in dealing with this Amendment.
Senator Leslie has not fully explained
why a landlord must wait until he is
served with a notice that his land is to
be acquired. I am certain that local
authorities, who are most anxious to
acquire land, or easements, or rights of
way, or whatever you like to call them,
in the cheapest possible way, would go
to the landlord and ask him if he would
dedicate that particular land. If the
landowner says "yes," he does it, and
213 Roads Improvement Bill—

17 MAY 1928
Committee
it is an accomplished fact without practically any expense to anyone.
I know
myself that in one county, the county
council of which my hon. and gallant
Friend who sits opposite (Col. Sharman
Crawford) is a member, it is frequently
done. Whether it is because they have
got such a tactful member I do not know,
but they have most extraordinary success in getting landowners to hand over
the necessary land for the purposes defined in this Bill.
This Bill is for the purpose of dealing
with frontage owners who have no regard
for the public interest, and I think the
last twenty or thirty years have shown
that the Act of 1899, to which Senator
Leslie has referred, has proved to be
utterly useless in this respect, because of
what I might describe as the dog-in-themanger attitude of the owners of a few
frontages who realized that under the old
Act the legal costs were so heavy that no
county council was prepared to embark on
the task of putting into operation the
compulsory powers contained in that. Act.
I know myself dozens of cases where
county councils have applied to frontage
owners for land. In some cases they have
met with the greatest possible generosity,
but in other cases those landowners,
knowing perfectly well that if they held
out there was a possibility of getting three
or four times the value of the fee simple
of that land owing to the fact that county
councils would not embark on a heavy bill
of costs that would be entailed, have refused to give up their land. This Bill
is meant to deal with those few cases.
It is perfectly obvious that no frontage
owner will have his land taken without
the county surveyor first going to him and
asking him if he would give the county
council the land required by them. It is
then open for him to say, "I do not want
you to take the fee simple of the land,
but I am prepared to dedicate it to the
public." The effect of this Amendment
would be that, first of all, the county
council would have to go to the expense
of putting an advertisement in the Press
applying to the Ministry of Home Affairs
for an inquiry as to a particular scheme,
and having incurred that expense they are
then to go to the landowner and
"You have got the option of dedicating
your land or selling under compulsory
powers."
say,
I do suggest to the Committee that if a
landowner, having been approached, as
he is bound to be,
be, by the county
council, has not got sufficient generosity
and sense of public duty either to arrive
at a reasonable figure for compensation or else to hand over his land
as I suggest, free of cost, to the
county council, he should take the consequences of having his fee simple acquired.
The county council do not want the fee
simple, but it is absolutely necessary, if
these compulsory powers are put into
operation, that the fee simple should be
acquired. The hon. and learned Senator
referred to minerals. If any landowner
has minerals under the road the way he
can protect himself is to dedicate only
whatever land is required for the use of
the public.
Mr. LESLIE: Without payment?
Sir DAWSON BATES: If he does not
wish to do that free of payment he can
get compensation at the hands of the ratepayers. Under these circumstances I hope
the Committee will not accept this Amendment. We have had the experience that
since 1898 the Act for the compulsory
acquisition of land has been a complete
failure owing to the expense involved, and
to accept this Amendment would only
mean a continuance of that evil state of
affairs.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman has made it perfectly plain that he
wants local authorities to get this land
without any payment at all
Sir DAWSON BATES: No.
Mr. LESLIE: What you say is that
if a man does not like to have his fee
simple taken let him give the land to
the public. The right hon. Gentleman
holds out a beautifully painted picture of
the county surveyor approaching the man,
but there is nothing whatever in the Bill
to the effect that before the axe descends
he is to be asked anything. Again I protest against treating this Clause as if it
was merely taking a little bit of land to
widen or improve a corner. You may make
a new road and take half a mile of frontage fifty feet in width. It may be a
very serious matter.
If it is intended that the man should
first be approached, and given an opportunity of dedicating his land on pay-
Roads Improvement Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
ment, why should we not provide for it in
the Bill? If we do not, what we are
doing-in order, the right hon. Gentleman says, to save expense and time-is
to deprive that man of his existing right
to go to the Judge of Assize. That Judge
of Assize, under the Act of 1898, is required to take into consideration whether
the public advantage is sufficient to outweigh the private wrong of compulsorily
taking his land. If you go round that
what you are doing is to disseize the
Dorman, Mr. R.
Bangor, Viscount
Barclay, William
Crawford, Col. Sharman
Cunningham, Samuel
man of his freehold without the judgment of his peers, and without the decision of the officers of the law. It does
not make it a bit better to say that you
have done it half-a-dozen times before.
I am quite unable to withdraw this
Amendment from the decision of the
Committee.
Question put, That those words be
there inserted.

Not Contents, 12.

The following Amendment stood on

Page 1, after line 13, insert as a new
Sub-section :
The provisions of the Schedule to
this Act shall apply in such a case as is
aforesaid, subject to such modifications
as may be prescribed. Such modifications shall provide in particular :-
(a) for the necessary modification of
the terms and effect of the vesting
order;
(b) that such vesting order shall be
conclusive evidence of the dedication
of the land;
(c) that the estate in the land retained
by the owner of such land shall be
taken into account in determining
the amount of the compensation, if
any, payable in respect of such land:
and
(d) that, so far as relates to an appeal
by the owner of the land, the provisions of paragraph 20 of this
Schedule shall not apply.

any restriction imposed thereby, he
may, within twenty-one days after

thereof, to the authority by whom the
notice was served, and thereupon the
question whether the notice shall be
withdrawn as respects any requirement
or restriction objected to shall be determined, if the parties so agree, by

or, in default of such agreement as

where the land is situate, and a county

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
2, page 2, line 22, after the word "and"
(to which the notice relates; and) to
insert :-
--
(ii) notice of the right of objection
shall be endorsed on the notice; and
Similar words will be found in Sub-
Clause 2 of Clause 3, and I think that
they are new words on which I congratulate the right hon. Gentleman. They
do not appear in the English Act, but if
they are wanted in Clause 3, which is a
minor Clause dealing with rather objectionable things, that is the lopping of
hedges, I think they are much more
wanted in what may be the more serious
Clause now before the Committee,
and accordingly I think they ought to go
in. All it means is that a person who
receives a notice will have the same notice
of his right of objection under Clause 2 as
he would have under Clause 3. I beg to
move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
in
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
Clause 2, page 3, line 10, to leave out
"Ministry of Home Affairs," and to insert thereof the words "county court
having jurisdiction where the land is
situate, and that that court shall have
jurisdiction to deal with any such
question."
This, I am afraid, is a more difficult
matter. Speaking generally, the Clause
before us is taken from the Imperial
Roads Improvement Act, and the Imperial Roads Improvement Act provides
that in the event of a disagreement between the parties, and in the event of
failure by the parties to agree upon an
arbitrator, the differences between thein
shall be determined by the County Court
Judge. We have followed the English
Section pretty closely, but those responsible for the Bill have substituted the
Ministry of Home Affairs to do the work
which in England is left to the County
Court Judge. For myself I cannot possibly agree to that. The position is fairly
simple. I think that legal questions of
various kinds may arise on this Clause,
and if a man cannot agree with whoever
serves notice upon him, and if he cannot
agree about an arbitrator, it must be
realized that the man
may have his
grievance and may have something to
say. If he has something to say in defence of his own property, the place for
him to say it is in one of His Majesty's
Courts of Law, where he would not only
have the advantage of having his case
heard by a trained judge, but he would
also have the opportunity of having his
case put forward by counsel, and examining witnesses on oath.
The right of resort to the Court is, I
need scarcely remind the Committee, an
inherent right in every subject of the
Crown, and to substitute a Ministry is,
to my mind, a very undesirable thing
and it is distinctly wrong.
It is not the
first time we have had to deal with
matters of that sort here. Even in England they have the same trouble. It will
be remembered that within the last three
weeks the House of Lords, the body across
the water which we endeavour to follow,
had occasion to object very strongly to
something of this nature, and the objection was successful, and there was an
alteration. Accordingly I am moving to
take out "the Ministry of Home
Affairs," and to substitute the words on
the Paper. If we do not, I can see no
reason to justify us in so far departing
from the English practice as to place this
matter in the hands of the Ministry.
Accordingly, I beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I fear that
my hon. and learned Friend has misunderstood the reason why this Clause
219 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Sir Dawson Bates]
I might
I
was framed in the way it is.
remind the Committee that the object of
this Clause is to have a rapid and a
cheap method of removing obstructions.
that may appear at the side of a road,
and which may perhaps involve loss of
life. Assuming for one moment that
there is an appeal to the County Court
Judge as hon. Senators are aware, the
County Court Judge only goes round
four times a year, and it is quite possible
that an appeal might be just too late
for one County Court. The result, would
be that six months might easily elapse
before the case was finally dealt with,
and even then the absence of a witness
might mean a further adjournment.
want to give the Committee a practical
example to show the reason the Government desire that this Clause should be retained as it is. Suppose there was a
farmer on one side of the road, and that
out of pure pig-headedness he put up two
large haystacks which obstructed the view
at a corner where there was a great deal
of very heavy traffic. Nothing could be
done with that individual for perhaps six
or nine months, and in the meantime he
would have used up his hay and the obstruction would have been removed before the hearing of the case came on.
There is no new principle in this. No
one would suggest that any Ministry is
better than a court of law-
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I would not
suggest that for one moment, and Senator
Leslie actually proposes to move, at a
later stage and I hope he does so-an
Amendment making the Ministry of
Home Affairs the final appeal tribunal in
regard to certain matters. Therefore,
I do not think my hon. and learned
Friend can seriously believe that the
Ministry have such a bad reputation as
he seems to suggest
Mr. LESLIE I do not suggest anything.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I can only
infer from what the hon. Member says.
But as I said, this is no new principle.
It is happening every day under the
Public Health Acts, because, after all,
when the Public Health Act, 1878, was
framed the whole idea of the framers there
was to have a rapid and cheap way of
dealing with the situation, and I suggest
that if a single case that had to be
decided had had to go before the
courts the Public Health Act of 1878
would have hopelessly broken down.
At the risk of wearying the Committee,
may I quote some of the many cases that
are dealt with in a similar manner to
that in which we propose to deal with this.
For instance, supposing the owner or occupier fails to make a drain after receiving
notice from the local authority, the appeal is to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
That is under the Act of 1878. Also where
the local authority fails to look after the
health of the people, or where the local
authority fails to provide a water supply,
and where a nusiance is not abated and so
on. I could quote innumerable cases, and
that is all done with a view to rapidity,
so that the health of the community will
not suffer by a day's longer delay than is
absolutely necessary. Here, I suggest,
in view of the terrible number of accidents
that we see taking place it is equally
necessary there should be a rapid method
of dealing with the situation. I quoted
the case of the haystack, because I think
it will bring it home to hon. Members
more than anything else. Apart, altogether, from that I do think the Senate
will be taking a very great responsibility in inserting a clause which would
mean the slightest delay in dealing with
the situation that may arise and may possibly involve risk of loss of life
Mr. LESLIE: It is very difficult to
He
follow the right hon. Gentleman.
makes a tremendous point of rapidity and
draws attention to the possibility of loss
of life. Then why did he hold this
Bill for a whole year? This Bill was
originally introduced in May of last year.
up
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am accused
of delay. I was most anxious, so far as
possible-I knew it would not be possible
to get a perfect Bill—at all events, I was
anxious to confer with the various interests concerned, the local authorities,
and the Roads Board. It was not because
we did not realize the necessity, but our
desire was to get a Measure which would
meet the interests of the people.
Mr. LESLIE: Let us hope that loss of
life did not result. I am not going to
give the right hon. Gentleman an assurance. Now, as to the haystacks, does he
suggest that farmers in Northern Ireland
are more pig-headed than in England?
221 Roads Improvement Bill-

17 MAY 1928
Committee
Again, lorries can cause just as much
obstruction as haystacks. I am not defending haystacks. If the Committee
accepts the suggestion of the right hon.
Gentleman it is simply putting time, and
possibly money, against justice. That
is what he suggests. In order to save
time, and perhaps save money, you are
not entitled to give a man a trial otherwise
than by a court.
I do not think the right hon. Gentleman's references, either to my other
Amendments or to the Public Health
Act, are in the least in point. I think
if the Committee have thoughtfully considered the cases in which I have suggested an appeal to the Ministry, they
will see they are distinctly different. For
instance, there is one in Clause 5 where
the Minister shall or shall not exercise
powers under the Roads Act. The
powers the Minister possesses under the
Roads Act, he possesses, as I imagine,
as a kind of trustee for all the interests
affected. He is a very natural person in
that case. Again, you will find in the
preceding Clause of the Bill a matter
which is left to the Ministry. You will
find in these provisions as regards parking vehicles. I understand the object
there. I left it as it was. I even refrained from pointing out that the
Ministry is substituted in that Clause
for the Court of Petty Sessions.
thought some city Member would like to
deal with that
I
The fact remains, this matter is left to
the court in England, and here it is left
to the Ministry. I cannot agree. It is
wrong in principle. It is for the court
to settle these matters. If there is occasional delay, it cannot be helped.
It
does not follow the decision of the court
would invariably be in favour of the
person who serves the notice. You must
wait for the decisions of your law courts.
Anybody knows that. It is generally
agreed that the difference between
civilized and a barbarian community is
the fact that the civilized community are
prepared to await events. I can see no
reason whatever for the substitution of
the Ministry for the court and I cannot
withdraw my Amendment.
a
Mr. LONG I desire to support the
Amendment which has been moved by
the hon. Senator. It is quite true that
this Clause as it appears here was before
the Roads Advisory Committee and approved by them. But I must confess
that I failed to notice this particular
provision, and I agree with Senator
Leslie in saying that the County
Court Judge would be a more impartial
tribunal to appeal to. The substitution
of the Ministry for the County Court
Judge would not commend itself to the
general public Every Senator knows
that the Ministry of Home Affairs is more
or less mixed up with the local authorities. In fact, in all the activities of the
local authorities the Ministry has part.
And I took the opportunity of consulting
three members of the community who are
interested in this thing, because they own
land at corners on which improvements
will undoubtedly be carried out at an
early opportunity. The local authority
will undoubtedly approach these three
men in the near future to acquire rights
over their property. And these three
men were unanimously of the opinion.
that the County Court Judge was a preferable authority to go to in case of an
appeal being necessary.
There is no doubt the only shadow of
excuse for substituting the Ministry is the
one the right hon. Gentleman has
laboured so much, that is, delay. It is
quite possible there would be delay. But
I suggest it is not likely it would operate
to the detriment of the public. It would
be a very rare case in which the next
sitting of County Court Judge would be
missed. With regard to the exaggerated
statement of the right hon. Gentleman
about haystacks, I just wish to suggest
that I know something about haystacks,
and I traverse the country by motor and
otherwise perhaps as much as anyone, and
I have never yet seen a haystack, not to
speak of two haystacks, built at the
corner of a road. And the hon. Senator
to my right (Mr. Hill Dickson) has also
had wide experience in travelling over
the country, and he says he has never
seen a haystack so placed. I suggest that
to obtain the confidence of the general
public, it would be well for the right hon.
Gentleman-I am sure he does not mind
very much having this accepted-to
agree. It would be more acceptable to
the general public.
Colonel SIIARMAN CRAWFORD: I
was rather, on first impression, inclined
to vote for the Amendment, but I have
changed my mind since I came in here,

CLAUSE 3 (Lopping of hedges overhanging roads).
(1) Where any hedge overhangs any
road, street or footpath so as to obstruct
or interfere with the light from any
public lamp, or to endanger or obstruct
the passage of vehicles or foot passengers or to obstruct the view of drivers
of vehicles, the local authority may
serve a notice on the owner of the hedge
or on the occupier of the premises on
which such hedge is growing, requiring
him to cut or lop the hedge within fourteen days, so as to prevent such obstruction or interference, and in default of
compliance the local authority may
themselves carry out the requisition of
their notice, doing no unnecessary damage, and may recover the cost in the
county court as a debt due by the owner
or occupier upon whom the notice was
served.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I beg to move
in Clause 3, line 1, after the word
"hedge" to insert "or tree." This is a
small amendment of which I have not
given notice. I do not see why a man
should be asked to cut his hedge while
another man should be allowed to let his
tree remain. A tree is far more likely to
blind the light than a hedge. I think
it is a simple amendment that might be
carried.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I do not object to the principle in the slightest, but
if the hon. Senator turns to Clause 20,
the definition Clause, he will find that a
hedge includes any tree or shrub.
Roads Improvement Bill—
Committee
17 MAY 1928
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Surely it would
be more simple to put it in here instead
of having to turn up this thing.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I suggest to the hon. Member
to withdraw the Amendment. If we put
in "or tree we would have also to put
in or shrub."

CLAUSE 4 (Prescription of building
lines)-
*
*
*
*
*
(3) Where a building line prescribed
under this section is in force it shall not
be lawful, except with the consent of
the authority by whom the building line
was prescribed, to erect or make nearer
to the middle of the road than the building line any new building, other than
a boundary wall or fence, or any permanent excavation below the level of
the road:
Provided that the consent of the local
authority for the purposes of this subsection may be given subject to such
conditions as the authority think fit to
impose, and any conditions so imposed
shall be binding upon any successor in
title to the owner, occupier or lessee of
any land to which they relate.
(4) If any person erects or makes, or
permits to be erected or made, any new
building or permanent excavation in
contravention of the provisions of this
section, he shall, without prejudice to
any other proceedings which may be
taken against him, be guilty of an
offence, and shall be liable on summary
conviction thereof to a penalty not exceeding five pounds, and any person so
convicted shall, within such time as the
court may allow, remove any building
erected, or fill in any excavation made
in contravention of this section, and if
he fails to do so he shall be deemed to
commit a continuing offence, and shall
be liable on summary conviction thereof
to a penalty not exceeding forty shillings
for each day upon which such failure
continues.
*
*
*
Provided that any consent required
for the purposes of this sub-section shall
not be unreasonably withheld, and any
question whether or not any consent so
required is unreasonably withheld shall
be determined by the Ministry of Home
Affairs.
(8) Where a local authority propose
to prescribe a building line extending
to the boundary of their area, notification of the proposal shall be sent to the
local authority of the area into which
the road, in relation to which the building line is proposed to be prescribed,
continues across the said boundary, and
if, within three months after the receipt
of such notification, the local authorities
concerned do not agree as to whether a
building line should be prescribed in
relation to any part of the said road
in continuation of the proposed building
line, or otherwise as to the exercise of
their respective powers under this
section in relation to the road, the
Ministry of Home Affairs shall have and
may exercise the powers of all or any of
those authorities under this section, or
may make such other order as it may
think fit; and no part of any building
line prescribed by the Ministry of Home
Affairs shall be altered except with the
consent of that Ministry, and any order
made by that Ministry as aforesaid shall
be binding upon the local authorities
concerned.

up. The Amendment, as it stands, is to
leave out on page 7, line 12, from "fence"
to the end of the line 13 ("level of the
road"), that is to leave out
(6 or any permeanent excavation-

-these words
or any permanent excavation.

THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: The difficulty was I
could not understand what the permanent
excavation was that the Clause was, so to
speak, getting at. I could understand it
in relation to a town. I was under the
impression that this Clause did not cover
towns. However, I understand that steps
are to be taken to make an Amendment
afterwards covering streets. Therefore I
think the Committee will probably be prepared to leave the words in on that explanation.
If you consider the words in connection
with a new building, there is something in
the nature of a kitchen area which is common enough in towns, but that sort of
thing does not happen in the country.
If the Clause were limited in its operation to the country, one had to look for
some explanation of permanent excavations. An excavation is simply a hollow.
For the moment, I simply move to omit
the words in order to get an explanation.
There are two consequential amendments
which, if necessary, we could have on
Report. I am sure that the right hon.
Gentleman will be able to explain what
is the intention as it stands.
some
There is, of course, also the question of "below the level of the
road." The level of the road, as
I understand it, is the surface; and
in Northern Ireland, and to
extent in England, very often the level
of the road is very much above the level
of the front gardens of houses surrounding
the road. That introduces another
factor. However, I think it would be
sufficient if I formally moved to omit
these words. For the moment I do not
suggest any in substitution.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The whole
object of this Clause is to have a frontage
line set out which will not prevent the
future widening of the road without
unnecessary expense to the local authority. We propose that the local authority
will fix that frontage line, and it is one
of many similar cases in the Bill where
the Ministry of Home Affairs leave it entirely to the local authority. Therefore,
hon. Members will see that once the
frontage line is prescribed along a road,
no building shall be placed outside that
line without permission from the local
authority. But it will be apparent, I
think, to hon. Members that it is not
likely to occur. It may be that while it
is not a building in the ordinary sense
of the word, some substructure may be
erected outside the frontage line which
might cause some embarrassment to the
local authorities. It might, first of all,
interfere with the foundations of the road
---if not at that time, at some future time
when the road was widened. It might
bring the edge of the road close to this
substructure I think the hon. Member
suggested a sunk garden. I can suggest
a pond for lillies, or something of that
sort. At all events, it would be an embarrassment to the local authorities if
any substructure should be near the
frontage line without the permission of
the local authority.
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the hon. and
learned Senator will withdraw the
Amendment, because among the roads in
the area are bog roads, and I think it
would be necessary, in the interests of the
community in future, in the case of excavations in the vicinity of a bog road.
that the occupier of the soil should not
be permitted to dig turf so close to the
border of the road as not only to prevent
the widening of the road in future, but
to undermine the foundations, especially in view of the heavier traffic that
might be put on.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the explanation
is pretty much what I expected. I see
the intention is to make it clearer, at a
later stage, that the Section applies in
the towns, and then the difficulty as it
appears to me will disappear. As regards the question raised by the hon.
Senator in front of me (Mr. Dorman)
about turf. Whatever the local authority do in prescribing building lines-if
they take so much land from a man, or
do something to prevent a man cutting
turf, they will have to pay for it.
course, I will withdraw my Amendment.
It really was to get an explanation I
moved it. The two following Amendments are consequential.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 9,
lines 10 and 11, to leave out from the
beginning of line 10 ("Provided that
any consent") to "shail" in line 11 ("Subsection shall not") and insert,
(8) Any consent required for the purpose of Sub-section (3) or Sub-section (7)
of this section "
It seemed to me that, having regard to
buildings-I was thinking of small matters such as summerhouses and things of
that sort there might be more in that
question of permanent excavations than
proved to be the case. But it seems to
me you ought to have some protection
here as we have in Sub-clause 1, I think,
of Clause 9. Clause 9 relates to the putting up of overhead rails, beams, pipes,
and so on, across a street; and it is provided there that the consent of the local
authority shall not be unreasonably withheld. It seems to me we ought to have
some protection under this section.
There is nothing much in it. It only involves altering a small number of words
as shown on the Paper. It makes the reasonableness of the consent applicable to
Sub-clause (3) about making the building

therefore move the Amendment.
I
Sir DAWSON BATES: I have no
objection to the Amendment which my
hon. and learned Friend has moved. We
deem it a great compliment to the Ministry
of Home Affairs.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
5, page 9, line 20, to leave out from the
word" boundary" ("across the said
boundary") to the end of paragraph (8).
I am afraid the right hon. Gentleman will
not take this as so complimentary to his.
Ministry as the last proposal. The point.
is, as it seems to me, that if two county
councils cannot agree as to a building line
the matter must be left in that state. Let
the Ministry by all means act the part of
mediators and try to bring the parties
together, but if they will not come together
each county council must be left master in
its own house. The county council are
responsible to their own ratepayers, and
any intervention of the Ministry can only
result in dictation to one or other of those
county councils if they cannot come to an
agreement. The effect of that would be
that expense would be incurred by one or
the other county council, and if they cannot agree, I say they ought to be left to
disagree.
I do not think the Ministry ought to
have the power to come down and say that
one or other of them shall do this or shall
not do the other. If they do incidentally
there will be a little trouble with Sub-
Clause 3 of this Clause, because there, I
presume, the authority by whom the building line is prescribed will be the Ministry
and not the county council which might
have prescribed it, and then the Ministry's
consent will be required. I am not very
sure about that, but the point is, I understand, although my information may not
be correct, that the county councils object
to these particular words as being an interference with their proper duty and their
proper responsibility to their own constituents. Accordingly I move the omission
of the words.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I may point
out to the Committee that it is only in
exceptional cases that it is proposed that
the Ministry should have any voice in
this matter. In the case of the fixing of
an ordinary frontage line each county
council is a law unto itself, and the
Ministry do not seek to have any power
to interfere. They only do so where a
road from one county runs into the adjoining county, and where one county
council cannot agree with the adjoining
county council as to the exact frontage.
I think the Committee will agree that
there ought to be something done in the
unfortunate event of the two county
councils differing as to the exact frontage
line. Personally, I cannot imagine such
a dispute occurring. My hon. and learned
Friend has suggested that we as a Ministry desire to dictate to the county councils. I am sure that Senator Long, who
has had a great deal of experience in regard to road matters, will not agree with
the hon. and learned Senator.
Mr. LONG: That is so.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Ministry
do distribute money from the Road Fund
and they have a voice in deciding how
that money is to be spent Things would
get into an absolutely chaotic condition if
Roads Improvement Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
every road authority could do as it liked.
Four or five years ago there were about
sixty different road authorities in Northern Ireland, and everybody agreed that
that was wrong.
We co-ordinated them,
and put first and second-class roads under
the county councils, and the rural
councils had even more right to say that
the county councils were dictating to
them than the county councils have to
say that the Ministry is dictating to them
in this matter. A difference of opinion
between county councils in regard to a
building line will only happen on extremely rare occasions, and I cannot
imagine that it will happen at all.
If it
does it can only be in three or four cases,
and, therefore, I hope the hon. and
learned Senator will not press his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I had rather expected
support from the county council representatives, but as they evidently do not think
the matter of any importance I shall ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Power of local authorities
to prohibit or restrict driving of vehicles
on roads)—
(1) A local authority may, by order,
prohibit or restrict, subject to such exceptions or conditions as to occasional
user or otherwise as may be specified in
the order, the driving of vehicles of any
specified class on any specified road
within the area of the local authority,
in any case in which it appears to the
local authority that the road is unsuitable in any respect for use by a vehicle
of that class, and would be damaged if
used by such vehicle:
Provided that a local authority shall
not make an order prohibiting or restricting the driving of any vehicle.
whose wheels are fitted with pneumatic
tyres, on a road classified under subsection (2) of section seventeen of the
Ministry of Transport Act, 1919, as a
first-class or second-class road

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Clause 5, page 9, line 38, after the first
"of" insert mechanically propelled."
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be better if the Amendment relating to
mechanically-propelled vehicles were allowed to stand over for the Report stage.
Amendment not moved.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
beg to move in Clause 5, page 10, line 5,
to leave out the words "or second-class.'
I regret that I have not been able, owing
to circumstances over which I had no control, to take any part in the previous
discussion of this Bill, in which I naturally
take a great interest. The necessity for
this Amendment is due to a small misunderstanding between the Minister of
Home Affairs and myself. A deputation
representing Antrim and Down waited
upon the Minister in regard to this particular Clause, which we looked upon as
probably the most important Clause in the
whole Bill, and we came away with the
impression that second-class roads would
be excluded from this Clause. The best
statement I can make in favour of this
Amendment is to read a portion of the
speech made by the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House, on the occasion of
the Second Reading of this Bill. It states
my case better than I could do so.
said:-
He
As I have already pointed out, local
authorities have had to incur greatly
increased expenditure to maintain the
roads as a result of the increase of traffic.
The burden on the rates has become so
great that it is essential to ensure that it
is not increased as a result of unreasonable use of the roads by heavy vehicles.
In Northern Ireland there is a great network of roads which fall within the category of third-class roads, and the maintenance of these roads is a great burden
on local rates. Many of these roads are
quite unsuitable for carrying heavy
traffic, and it would place an intolerable
burden upon the ratepayers to fit them
for the purpose; neither would any useful purpose be served by doing so.
Where a district is served by several
third-class roads, running more or less
parallel, some of which are fit to carry
heavy traffic and some of which are not,
it is reasonable that heavy traffic, or
at least heavy through traffic should be
confined to the suitable roads, and not
233 Roads Improvement Bill-

17 MAY 1928
Committee
allowed to use the unsuitable roads so
as to damage them to such an extent
as to put the ratepayers to great additional expense in repairing them.
-(Official Report, Vol. 9, No. 9, col. 128,
25th April, 1928).
That is the whole of my case.
In my
own county there is a second-class road
over which thirty-six buses run every
day, and that road is badly cut up.
I
think there are an unnecessarily large
number of buses using that road, but nobody has any control over them. The
county councils do not wish to interfere
with the service these buses are rendering to the public, but after they have
completely cut up this second-class road
they will probably leave it and go on to
a parallel road, which will eventually be
cut up in the same way. If it is going
to go on in this way, the ratepayers will
have to go to the great expense of repairing this road to enable it to continue to
carry that traffic. The ratepayers are
taxed to their utmost limit at the present
time, and the right hon. Gentleman informs me that the whole of the money
from the Road Fund has been allocated,
so that we cannot expect any additional
help from that source. Therefore, it
looks as if our roads, instead of being
improved, will go back to their former
condition, and will become less and less
fit to carry this heavy traffic. This year
every road authority has had to cut down
its estimates for roads.
At the present time the county council
has no right to interfere with this particular class of traffic on first and secondclass roads without first applying to the
Ministry of Home Affairs, and by the
time that is done the road may be cut up.
If the right hon. Gentleman accepts this
Amendment, and trusts the county councils not to act unfairly, no harm can be
done. With the surveyor we have in
County Down, we are in as good a position as anybody in the Ministry to judge
when a road is in a fit condition to carry
this traffic. I look upon this Bill as a
very good Bill. My only objection to it
is that it does not go far enough, particularly in regard to the matter with
which I am now dealing. The right hon.
Gentleman has referred to last year's
terrible death roll on the roads of Northern Ireland, and he expressed the opinion
that it will be increased this year. Yet
we have no power to deal adequately with
this appalling state of affairs.
If the Minister feels that he cannot.
accept this Amendment, I will not press
it, but it is a matter of great importance
to the ratepayers. These second-class
roads are not as a rule fit for this traffic,
and if this Amendment is not agreed to
the county councils are not sure what
position they are in as regards their power
to close certain roads. I hope we shall
not have to go back to the old method of
having to ask for an inquiry to be held.
before we can close a certain road, because
that is a very expensive method and
causes delay.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
hink time is of great importance in the
matter of closing a road that is being
tamaged by this traffic, and I would
suggest that if the Minister of Home
Affairs is not in a position to-day to give
a satisfactory answer to the case put forward by the hon. and gallant Senator, he
should consider the matter between now
and the Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I must say a word or two
in support of the Amendment moved by
the Chairman of the Association of County
Jouncils of Northern Ireland. It seems
to me that the position we ought to take
up on this particular question is one that
is likely to vary from time to time. The
view one takes to-day is by no means the
same as the view one took five years ago,
and still less likely is it that it will be the
view one will take five years hence.
I do not know whether the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman intends to press his
matter to a division. If he does, I think
I shall support him, because I feel that
at this time the county councils do imperatively require full power over these
second-class roads. The fact is that there
is not enough money for the roads, and the
County councils have to husband their
resources, and it is the best economy to
spend every penny they can get on the
first-class roads. They have to spend good
sums of money on second-class roads,
and I rather suggest that the money
spent on second-class roads is always
spent with a feeling of regret, that it could
not be put on first-class roads, SO as
to get these into first-class condition, and
then allow the funds to be released for
235 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
second-class roads. It seems to me that
anything that is done to help the county
councils to protect the second-class roads
is a good thing to do. I do not necessarily
say I will take the same view ten years
hence. Passing, for a minute. from the
direct support of the Amendment, which
I would have moved myself had the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman not been
here, I know, in fact, that this matter has
formed the subject of animated and frequent discussion between the Ministry and
the county councils, and I know, too, that
something has been done to meet the
difficulty. The earlier part of the Clause
does that.
I hope whatever may be the
decision this afternoon-whatever decision
the Committee comes to--I hope it will be
loyally accepted, and that by all parties
it will be recognized outside that this particular matter, as other matters that have
been raised with varying fortune during
the progress of the debate, has been carefully considered and debated inside Parliament as well as privately at the
Ministry. It is of the greatest importance that those who take a different view
-and there will be a difference of views;
whichever way the Committee decides they
will dissatisfy somebodv-I do hope that
everybody will recognize that Parliament
has made an honest endeavour to arrive
at the best possible decision. My own
view at the moment, I say, is that the
words "or second-class" should be left
out.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I would like
to say how much I appreciate the way in
which this Amendment has been moved,
because the Senators who spoke on this
matter realized some of the difficulties
that I had in regard to framing this Measure. I do not think any Clause gave
us greater difficulty. May I point out
this fact: Perhaps some of the hon. Senators may not fully realize that the roads
in Northern Ireland are divided into three
classes. Number 1 class, which is main
trunk roads, running from one place of
primary importance to another place of
primary importance. Number 2 class
roads run between places of lesser importance.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Not altogether.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Then the
third-class road is what is known as the
district road. I think I am right in saying this: That what we in Northern Ireland suffer from is having too many district roads. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
In many cases one finds three or four
district roads all leading in the same
direction and running parallel. Originally, it seems to me, they were built to
serve one or two farms in the neighbourhood. The law as it now stands is this:
That the local authority can apply at any
time to close any road, but as my hon.
Friend, the Senator sitting opposite
(Colonel Sharman Crawford), said, it had
to come to the Ministry. Application is
made to the Ministry, but he was slightly
incorrect when he stated it was necessary
to have an inquiry. I will agree that,
in a case of that kind, it is usual to have
an inquiry whether it should be closed.
But when we framed this Measure we
felt that delay might take place, and,
therefore, we tried to arrive at some
medium between the old system and
what has been suggested here. Accordingly, we have given absolute power
under this Bill to close either a first-class,
or a second-class, or a third-class road
against any traffic other than pneumatic
tyres. But as regards third-class roads,
we have given absolute power to the
county councils at once to close them
against buses and traffic of that kind,
because we realize that there are so many
of these third-class roads that frequently
what happens is the bus will go along one.
road one day, and then when the road is
cut up, it will proceed along another
road.
The policy that I have always thought
should be adopted by the county council
--and I hope it will be adopted as the
result of the passing of this Measure-is
that they will define which of these thirdclass roads should be used for bus traffic,
and prohibit the others. But when we
come to second-class roads greater difficulty arises. It is suggested here we
should give absolute power to the county
councils to close second-class roads against
all forms of traffic, including pneumatic
tvres. Hon. Members will appreciate
that as regards second-class roads they are
main roads, and it is the duty of the
county council to keep them open for
ordinary traffic, and I think hon. Senators will agree with me that nowadays
bus traffic is traffic that has come to stay,
and it has done very much to brighten
the life of the country people. But, at
the same time, we all realize it has come
237 Roads Improvement Bill—

17 MAY 1928
Committee
[Sir Dawson Bates]
cause
a
so quickly we have not been able to adjust
our finances to meet that particular case.
I think the hon. Member is, if I may say
So, going a little bit too far, bewe have as Government to
consider the interests of the ratepayers
and to consider the interests of the
road users, the bus owners, who have been
very indignant that we have gone so far
as we have done in giving this absolute
power-subject to appeal, of course-in
regard to third-class roads. Accordingly we have taken a more balanced view.
After all, I do not suggest that this Bill
is a perfect Measure. To a certain extent
it is experimental, because, as I have said,
this bus traffic and motor traffic has come
so rapidly upon the community we have
not been able to keep quite abreast of it.
Therefore, I would suggest to my hon.
Friend to withdraw the Amendment and
let us see how we get on with the Bill.
If we find it does not work satisfactorily
I would be only too happy to reconsider
the matter with the object of bringing in
another Bill to remedy this defect, if it
is a defect, and any other defects that may
develop afterwards.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Where a secondclass road is subject to extraordinary bus
traffic will the Minister give us any guarantee that he will classify it as a first-class
road.
That would meet the difficulty?
Sir DAWSON BATES: In reply to my
hon. Friend, I can give a practical illustration. In regard to a certain road in
his county where the bus traffic did develop
to an extraordinary degree, as the result
of representations I think it was made a
first-class road. It is all a question of
money, and we try to meet the county
councils where it does become a first-class
road.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am much obliged for the clear statement
the Minister of Home Affairs has given.
I only wish to point out that he says it
is the duty of the county council to keep
roads open for traffic. I quite agree with
him. But we cannot get the money to
keep the roads open. The effect of this
will be if we keep the road open and there
is no money to repair it, it will actually
have to be closed, because it will be so much
cut up.
What we would like to see until
better days is a little more money. I do
not think the heavy traffic is paying enough
for the damage it is doing. Will the
Minister of Home Affairs give us the right
to close the road while it is under repairs
to make it fit to carry that traffic? Under
the circumstances-it is not a perfect Bill
though it is a very good Bill; it does not
go far enough-I will withdraw the
Amendment for the present.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
NEW CLAUSE:
"6 (1) Where the local authority are
of opinion that any special and extraordinary traffic is causing, or may be expected to cause, damage to the surface
of any road, and that such damage is
or may be expected to be a source of
danger to persons lawfully using such
roads such authority may, without prejudice to any other power conferred by
any enactment, apply to any justice of
the peace having jurisdiction in their
area for a summary order prohibiting
such special and extraordinary traffic,
and such justice shall have power to
make such order.
(2) Any order so made shall forthwith be reported to the Minister of
Home Affairs, and shall remain in force
until that Minister shall exercise his
powers under sub-section four of section
seven of the Roads Act, 1920, or shall
revoke or modify the order of the
justice
(3) The provisions of the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts other than
those relating to appeal, and subject to
such modification as may be prescribed,
shall apply in the case of any application under this section
(4) The powers of the Minister of
Home Affairs in relation to an application under this section shall be deemed
to include a power when revoking or
modifying an order made by a justice.
under this section to require the deposit.
of such sum as the Minister may think
fit, and any sum so deposited shall be
available to meet any claim for damages
which the local authority may establish
under the provisions of the Public
Roads (Ireland) Act, 1911 "

ment. It is a desperate endeavour to do

imagine any court to which this matter
could be taken.

Sir DAWSON BATES: No.
county surveyor has a staff who move about

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :—
Page 14 line 19, at the end add "and a
power to provide directing lines on or
about the surface of a road or street at
or near any corner, crossing, bend or
junction."
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that in some
of the earlier Clauses I shall be considered
as not having paid enough regard to
the possibility of obstructions at corners,
and I am now appearing to support something in which I do believe. I do not
believe so much in hedging or ditching at
corners, but I do believe in white lines.
Sometimes in London the line is indicated by inserting strips of what appear
to be aluminium, or possibly it is steel,
in the road, and that must cost a certain
amount of money. It appeared to me
that it would be desirable that we should
give direct power to provide lines in the
same way that we give power to provide
sign posts and mirrors. Perhaps the
right hon. Gentleman has got that power
already, but even so I would like words
inserted in order to encourage the use
of these white lines, in which I greatly
believe, and in order to give white lines
some sort of Statutory recognition. I
hope the Minister will tell us that he approves of these white lines. I am not
sure that they are sufficiently used in this
country to enable a clear report to be
given as to their utility, but they are
used very much in England, and especially in London. After all this is one
thing you can do at that class of corner.
Therefore, I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
The county councils have been allotted a
certain amount of money every year for
the provision of these white lines. In
England they are doing away with the
painted lines, and if the Ministry have
money to spend on these aluminium or
steel lines they would be a great improvement in Northern Ireland.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think this
Amendment is unnecessary, because we
already distribute money out of the Road
Fund for this important purpose, and
we have power to distribute money for
that or any other like purpose that may
make for the safety of the public. With
regard to the substitution of aluminium
or steel lines for white painted lines, I
think we have an example of that in
Lisburn where, instead of white painted
line we have metal discs, and that is all
provided out of the Road Fund. We
do not want to limit ourselves to white
lines.
Mr. LESLIE: I avoided using the
words "white" and "paint" for that
reason but I think the Amendment has
served its purpose, because we have got
the approval of the Minister to those
white lines. and I therefore ask leave to
withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 12 (Projections against or in
front of houses or buildings)-
Any projection erected. or placed
against or in front of any house
or building, which, by reason of
being insecurely fixed or of defective
construction or otherwise, is a source of
danger to persons lawfully using a street
within an urban district or town shall,
for the purposes of sections sixty-nine
and seventy of the Towns Improvement
Clauses Act, 1847 (as incorporated with
the Towns Improvement (Ireland) Act,
1854, or with any other enactment), be
deemed to be an obstruction to the safe
or convenient passage along the street,

a road or street maintainable,

Committee
The
Mr. LESLIE: Still dealing with a
road, I beg to move, to add at the end of
that particular paragraph, in line 35, the
words "and includes a footpath."
fact is there is a certain amount of difficulty in saying that a road does or does
not include a footpath. There are decisions both ways in England and in Ireland. We do not use the expression
"highway" in Ireland. We have not
used it since 1765. It does appear in the
Summary Jurisdiction Act. I do not
know why. But we leave in doubt
whether a road does always include a
footpath, and we are increasing the doubt
here because we occasionally refer to
roads as "streets" and "footpaths." It
will clear up the whole difficulty if we say
it includes footpaths. I beg to move.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am very
much obliged to the hon. Senator for what
he has stated in regard to this Clause.
Personally, I think the Amendment improves the section, and, therefore, the
Government are prepared to accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

I understand it is one
out paragraph 8.
of the most useful paragraphs in the

The question is whether this paragraph
is within our powers, as defined by the
Order in Council of the 31st January,
1922, of which I hold a copy in my hand.
The point of that Order is to see that
powers under the Irish Land Act, 1903,
Section 54, and so on, for the sub-division
of holdings, are reserved to the extent
necessary to enable the Land Commission
to exercise those powers when they think
proper for the purpose of sales under the

ought to ask the right hon. Gentleman

shall be delighted to accept it.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I can safely
give my hon. and learned Friend the
assurance he asks for, and I have taken
the highest opinion in regard to this
matter. 1 would remind the Committee
that land purchase might be divided into
two categories. The first is what is
purely land purchase, that is, the process
under the Act before the land is actually
vested in the tenant. Then once the land
is vested in the tenant, subject to an
annuity-that is quite clearly within the
scope of this Parliament to deal with.
The annuities are actually payable to this
Government. It is for that reason we
have to protect this Government, who
are the trustees of the public purse in
dealing with the whole question.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that is quite
satisfactory.

Sir DAWSON BATES: The whole
cess of the Act is that the land is immediately vested in the local authority, and
then arbitration takes place with regard
