Roads Improvement Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
CLAUSE 1 (Compulsory acquisition of
land for road purposes).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 1, line 13, at end insert as a new
Sub-Section:-
"(2) Where any land vested as aforesaid has been dedicated by the local
authority to the public use, the person
from whom such land was acquired shall,
without prejudice to such public use and
without prejudice to the rights and
powers of the local authority, have, and
may exercise, the same remedy in respect of any Acts of trespass as such
person would have had if such person
had dedicated such land to the public
use for the purposes of a road or street."
Mr. LESLIE: This proposed new Subsection is, as the House will recognize,
another attempt, and possibly another unsuccessful attempt, to find some formula
which would render the Section, or rather
the Clause, as it is now, a little more acceptable and to give that Clause a little
better chance of being made workable and
a little less chance of being put on the
shelf in much the same way as its predecessors. The idea I had in my mind
was to see if we could in this way or in
some other way protect the landowner
from annoyance by giving him a chance
of protecting himself instead of having
to have recourse to the local authority
to do it for him. If by doing that we can
make it a little less unlikely that he will
object to the acquisition of his land, and
also from the point of view of the local
body we can make it a little more likely
that they will get their land at a somewhat cheaper rate we should do it. I
think all the cases cited by the Lieutenant
of Armagh (Mr. Armstrong) and by myself the other day might be considered as
dealing with invasions of a landowner's
privacy. The Committee has already decided that if there are minerals the landowner is to lose them and I do not say any
more about that.
But it is right, I think, that I should
say that it may be that the position of the
landowner under the new Clause will not
be materially worse than it might have
been under Sub-section (3) of Section 10
of the Local Government Act of which
so much has been said. From the point
of view of the local authority it will have
to pay the landowner not only for the
value of his land but for any other
damage that may be done, and I think I
am right in saying that the arrangements
made in the Schedule in the application of
paragraph 12 of the Working Classes Act
requires payment for severance, and I
think the provisions of the Lands Clauses.
Acts require compensation for any prejudicial effect, so to speak. If there
is any definite invasion of a man's privacy
the cost is likely to be very heavy, and
that is all the more so if you are taking
land for the purpose of making a new road
as distinct from an odd corner which very
often will be of no value whatever.
I do not know whether it will be possible
or practicable to introduce words of this
sort, but I think it will be recognized that
they are designed to meet a difficulty. I
would also point out that this House has
just accepted an Amendment made in
another place in relation to the School
Sites Bill (S.), in which it is provided.
that people may part with land and give
with it a right to use an avenue which
in itself is remaining private. I do not
want to take up any further time, but
I do suggest it is a matter we ought to
consider, and if it is impracticable to insert these words in the Bill the local
291 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage
[Mr. Leslie]
authority should exercise great care in
what they are doing, if they try to apply
this Section, so as to be able to make the
Section of use to them. I beg to move.
I
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am afraid
the hon. and learned Senator has not
helped the House to form any opinion as
to the reason why this Amendment should
be accepted. The only point he made, so
far as I could see, was that the Bill interfered with the privacy of a landowner.
cannot see how his privacy is affected in
any way, because if trespass takes place
on an adjoining owner he has his ordinary
remedy. It is perfectly true that in the
old days the bed and soil was in theory
vested in the landlord, who had the right
of bringing an action for trespass against
an individual who did not use the road in
the ordinary way. However, that was all
theory, and, as I have said before, we are
now living in 1928, and the whole tendency
of modern legislation is to vest in the local
authority the right of controlling roads and
to take away any remaining right that may
be vested in an adjoining owner in regard
to the road, though not in regard to his
lands. That is the rule regarding animals
straying on the road, and even in connection with motor traffic the local authority
has the right to control motor vehicles and
to say how the public using a particular
road are to use it.
Now, having acquired the fee simple of
land with one hand and having paid compensation with the same hand, the hon.
and learned Senator wants to give back
to the landowner with the other hand the
theoretical right to exercise some extraordinary power against the general public
in the use of that road. I believe there
were two cases during the last hundred
years where under the common law a
landowner did establish certain rights as
against the public, but this has all passed
away and the present system is to allow
the local authority, when once land has
been acquired, to exercise all the rights.
they have as owners of the road. I hope
that under these circumstances the House
will not accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
refrain from reminding the right hon.
Gentleman that the three cases-the case
of the Duke of Rutland, the race horse
case, and the moth-hunting case, in which
the poet, Rupert Brooke, was concerned,
are all within the last thirty or forty
years. It is an old notion undoubtedly
based on common law that the bed and
soil remains in the landlord, but it is
not an unduly ancient notion that a man
has a right of protection against trespassers. However, if the right hon.
Gentleman is very anxious that his local
authorities should have a power they do
not want, according to the informaton
sent to me, I do hope he will see that
when his local authorities acquire any of
these strips of land they will police them.
properly. I am afraid they will experience a good deal of embarrassment,
but I will not put the House to a Division
on this Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 2 (Prevention of obstruction
to view at corners).
*
*
*
*
*
If any person upon whom a notice
has been served under this section objects to comply with any requirement
of the notice, or objects to any restriction imposed thereby, he may, within
twenty-one days after receipt of the
notice, send his objection in writing,
stating the grounds thereof, to the
authority by whom the notice was
served, and thereupon the question
whether the notice shall be withdrawn
as respects any requirement or restriction objected to shall be determined, if
the parties so agree, by a single arbitrator appointed by them, or, in default of such agreement as aforesaid,
shall be determined by the Ministry of
Home Affairs.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 3, line 10, at end of Sub-section
(3) add:
Or by an arbitrator appointed by
that Ministry whose decision shall be
final as to whether the notice objected
to shall be withdrawn or modified, and
the cost of such reference shall be
borne as such arbitrator shall direct.
Mr. LESLIE: It has occurred to me,
on further consideration of what had
happened in Committee, that an Amendment of this sort might be a convenience
Business of the House 294
Adjournment--
22 MAY 1928
to us on Report. I thought at first that
the words on the Paper might be sufficient for the purpose, but it has since
been pointed out to me that a little more
is required, and hence the extension to
the Amendment, which you have heard
read. The suggested words of the extension, if I may use that expression, or
rather the extended words, are taken
from the Belfast Extension Act of 1896,
and I think it will be agreed that if a
dispute unhappily arises on this matter it
can very often be better dealt with by
an arbitrator than by any other authority. If in such a case as I have adumbrated the Ministry can get a local arbitrator in, say, County Fermanagh-I am
not thinking of the temptation which the
salmon of that county appear to offer
to local magistrates, but I had
better say in North Derry-if the question arises in North Derry, it might be a
convenience to have a local arbitrator to
settle the question. The expense would
be no greater than in the case of an ordinary arbitrator who will act if the parties
cannot come to terms.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am prepared
on behalf of the Government to accept this
Amendment. We could not accept it in
its original form, but the hon. Senator
very kindly drafted another form, which
will enable us to carry out the suggested
work. So far as the Ministry of Home
Affairs is concerned they have no desire
to do this work if it can possibly be
avoided, and I think the Amendment will
facilitate both the landowners and the
local authority acquiring land. Therefore I hope the House will accept the
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Adjournment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, That this House
do now adjourn.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
We are now approaching Whitsuntide,
and I suppose this House will soon be
adjourning for the Recess. There are
many reasons why Members would like to
know when the House will adjourn and
what business will be presented to the
House before the Adjournment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The programme I would submit
for the consideration of the House would
be that to-morrow we will take the Second
Reading of the Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Bill and the Third Reading of the
Finance and Roads Improvement Bill. On
Thursday I propose, with the approval of
the House, to take the Committee stages
of the Fisheries Bill, the National Health
Insurance Bill, and the Civil Authorities
(Special Powers) Bill, and to wind up
the business of the House on Friday, if
that will suit the convenience of hon.
Members. Otherwise we shall have to sit
in Whit week.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
do think it is rushing matters to suggest
that the House should sit on Friday, a
day which is recognized as the beginning
of the holidays. A great many people
who go away for Whit week are anxious
to get away on Friday afternoon, and I
think the House would prefer to sit on
Tuesday of next week rather than on
Friday next.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot help thinking
that the Members of this House must occasionally want a little time to think. It
is very hard to deal with so many Bills
in such a short time. Even if you want
to put an Amendment down-with the
almost certain knowledge that it is not
going to be accepted-but in order to get
the matter discussed, you do want to put
it down in time to give other Members a
chance to see what it is all about. Therefore, I do not think we should go so fast
as the noble Viscount suggests.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am entirely in the hands of
the House in this matter. If we do not
sit on Friday it will mean that the House
must sit on Tuesday, which will not be
very convenient for some Members, but
if it is the wish of the House that we
should not sit on Friday I shall not object.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am taking the attitude I do as a protest
against this House not being treated with
ordinary civility by the cther House who
send Bills to us in the manner of "take
them or leave them." I do not think it
is right.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes after
Five o'clock.
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 23rd May, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. J. A. LONG (Deputy-
Speaker) in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.

of the experiments on Living Animals
performed during the year 1927, under
licences granted in conformity with the
Act, 39 and 40 Vic., c. 77.

Census of Northern Ireland, 1926.
County of Down.
Presented (pursuant to Act), and
ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Administrative Provisions Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Administrative Provisions Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Civil Authorities (Special Powers) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The object and effect of this Bill
now before the House is to continue in
force for a period of five years the Civil
Authorities (Special Powers) Act of 1922.
As hon. Members are no doubt aware,
when this Act was passed in 1922 it was
provided by Section 12 of the Act that it
should continue in force for one year
only. However, as time went on and as
it became apparent that it would be
dangerous to relinquish any of the powers
conferred on the Minister of Home Affairs
by the Act, the Act has been continued
from time to time in the successive Expiring Laws Continuance Acts passed by
the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
Under existing circumstances the Act
has been continued up to the 31st Decemher, 1928, by the Expiring Laws Continuance Act of last year, and my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, who has been following with great
care, for years past, the activities of the
various disloyal and disruptive elements
in our midst, has satisfied himself and the
Government that as matters stand it will
be necessary to continue the Act for some
years to come.
Accordingly, as hon. Members will observe, the Government have decided, instead of renewing the Act as they have
been doing in the past, from year to year,
in the Expiring Laws Continuance Acts,
to make a clean sweep and keep the Act
as it stands on the Statute Book for a
period of five years from 1st January
This proposal is based on three
main considerations. In the first place,
as already pointed out and as will be explained in greater detail later on, the
Government are not yet satisfied that
those malcontents in our community have
yet learned the lesson that Ulster will not
stand for further outbreaks or disorders.
next.
In the second place, although peace prevails throughout the Province and law and
order have rapidly improved in recent
years, it is desirable to avoid as far as
possible the yearly debates which have
taken place on this particular Measure
during recent years, while, finally, it is
considered desirable to let all evil-doers
realize that for the next five years it will
be impossible for them to recommence
their evil and dangerous activities, and it
will at the same time give them an apportunity of turning their thoughts and
attentions to other and more profitable
pursuits.
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
23 MAY 1928
A
T
One great feature about this Act which
we now propose to continue is that while
it places great powers in the hands of the
Government in regard to dealing with
disorder and the disorderly elements in
our midst, it does not tend in any way to
infringe on the liberty of any law-abiding
subject. It has been obvious to everyone
in Ulster that conditions have rapidly
during the last few years become more
peaceful. This fortunate circumstance
is due in a very great measure to the fact
of our having on our Statute Book this
Act, whereby it is possible for my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs,
to nip in the bud immediately any attempt
at outbreak or disorder.
At the same time, from the reports which
have been furnished to him during the
last year or so, it appears obvious that the
danger, although scotched has not yet
been killed, and that the will if not the
power to overthrow the forces of law and
order and to plunge again into anarchy
and civil war is as strong to-day as ever
with a certain section of the community.
Hon. Members will understand that it is
not possible for my right hon. Friend to
disclose all the information at his disposal
in this respect, but it is possible at least
to refer to a few incidents to which publicity has been given recently and which
in themselves are symptomatic of the
strength and character of the forces which
are always working underground against
the State. If I may I should like to cite
a few examples to the House. I have
many, but I will only quote one or two,
as I do not wish to take up the time of
the House.
Hon. Members may remember that during the hearing of a charge in the Dublin
Courts against two men for being members of an illegal
association, certain
documents were produced by the Police
Authorities which went to show that the
Irish Republican Army organization is
being kept alive in Belfast and other
places in Northern Ireland. I might also
in this respect refer to the fact that in
December last the Royal Ulster Constagulary discovered in an empty house on
the outskirts of Belfast an arms and ammunition dump containing, amongst other
weapons, rifles, revolvers, a machine gun,
hand grenades, ammunition, detonators.
and explosives.
Similar activities of this character have
been apparent in Londonderry. In February last a poster was extensively displayed in this City purporting to be a
manifesto from the Republican Government. The gist of this poster was to urge
people to work for the setting up of a
Republican Parliament in Ireland, and
referred once more to the old slogan, "that
every difficulty in which England may
find herself must be used by the loyal
people of Ireland to restore the Independence of our country." The poster further
called upon the young men to train and
to be ready to take their places in defence of the Republic as their predecessors
did.
Hon. Members will also remember that
during the course of a recent Police Inquiry in London it was brought to light
that there was widespread smuggling of
arms and ammunition taking place into
Great Britain, and it is not unreasonable
to assume from the circumstances of the
case that the object of this smuggling was
to supply disloyal persons in Northern
Ireland and in the Irish Free State with
weapons for a future campaign against
one or both of the established Governments in Ireland. One of the men who
were prosecuted in England at that time
in this connection was a native of County
Fermanagh, and had been associated for
many years with the Republican movement.
As
Without going further into details, I
venture to express the opinion that the incidents I have related above will prove that
disloyal forces are still at work, and that
were it not for the curbing influence on
them of this Civil Authorities Act, their
activities might be much more pronounced
and their influence much stronger.
long as people of this character remain in
our midst and endeavour to corrupt our
youth and to lead them into wrong paths,
so long must the Government keep ample
powers to restrain their activities. It is
for these reasons that the Government
have brought in this Bill to which I now
ask the House to give a Second Reading.
I beg to move
Mr. DORMAN: I have never risen in
this House with as great a sense of personal responsibility as to any words that
might cross my lips as I have to-day. On
each occasion when this Measure was attempted to be renewed on the Statute
Book of Northern Ireland, I utilized my
powers in trying to place before the House
reasons why, in my opinion, the Bill
should not be allowed to continue in
Civil Authorities Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
force, in the interest of the peace and
harmony of the community of which we
are a part. It was stated at that great
historic event that took place last Saturday, namely, the laying of the foundation-stone of the future Parliament
buildings for this part of the great Commonwealth of Nations, that Ireland was
a country of paradoxes. It certainly is
a country of paradoxes. My memory
goes back for a period of sixty years, and
during the whole of that sixty years extra-constitutional Measures have been
placed upon the Statute Book designed for
the specific purpose of preventing crime,
but in many instances they deprived the
citizens of a free expression of opinions
that by no stretch of the imagination
could be said to be unconstitutional, although they were undoubtedly opposed
to those who were in power for the time
being. I was reared in the midst of this.
Fifty-one years ago I took an obligation
that many of the Members of the Government of Northern Ireland have taken.
and boast they have taken, an obligation
which made an influence on my life that
has never departed, and an obligation
that I have conscientiously and consistently endeavoured to carry out from then
until now.
The main part of the body of men in
Northern Ireland who constitute the
members of that organization are now
being appealed to by the Members of the
Government, and by supporters of the
Government to constitute themselves a
bulwark against the progress of this insidious philosophy that is commonly
termed Socialism, for which five and a
half million electors of Great Britain
voted at the last general election, and
for which no less than 50,000 people in
Belfast voted at the last general election
for this area. Every opportunity has
been taken by the Government and
their supporters to hold up to public disrepute the particular party in the State
which I happen to represent in this
honourable House. The most lying
statements have been made by members
of the party that supports the Government and by the Press. Indeed, the
Press has sunk so low as to refer to that
particular party and to its Sunday schools
in the most blasphemous terms, terms
which, as I told the editor of one of our
leading newspapers the other day, if they
had been inserted in any newspaper a
hundred years ago, would have meant
three years' imprisonment for the editor
or proprietor. I hope hon. Members will
bear with me, because we are living in
an extraordinary community, and I do
not wish to say anything here that is not
in perfect accord with the prayers we offer
up at the opening of our deliberations
here, that we should dwell together in
Christian love and charity.
But I do not think hon. Members, responsible statesmen in this area, give due
and proper consideration to the environment in which they live, and to the pos
sible consequences of an appeal to mass
psychology by continuous suggestion and
repetition, calculated, under certain circumstances, to bring about disastrous
social results in this community of which
we are a part I have, unfortunately,
had to go through the troubled times that
existed several years ago, and I am sufficiently interested in the question of
psychology to foresee disastrous results of
the violence and inhumanity which took
place in this area six or seven years ago.
I happened to be a victim on that occasion. I was working in the shipyard,
having had an opportunity for the first
time in my life of coming into contact with
the vast mass of individuals who are supporters of the Government, and it was
enough to shock the moral susceptibilities
of
any rational human being to hear the
language expressed towards other individuals before the disastrous results to
which I have referred came about.
I would take this opportunity to appeal
to statesmen in Northern Ireland to cease
this continuous repetition of unjust charges
that are always being made by Members
of another place and by Members of the
Government. They cannot attend a public meeting or unveil an Orange flag or
open an Orange Hall without making an
appeal to the multitude of the working
men of this Province to set themselves up
as a bulwark against the
against the insidious
philosophy that they call Socialism.
happen to have had the privilege, of which
I am proud, to have been the only Superintendent of the first Socialist Sunday
School ever held in Belfast. I profess to
be a Christian man, the father of a large
family, and I could not express a greater
or better wish for my fellow countrymen
than that future generations should be
brought up on the principles inculcated
by the teachings and tenets of that par-
I
301 Civil Authorities Bill-

23 MAY 1928
Second Reading
2 511 3

ticular organization, of which there is, unfortunately no branch in Belfast at present, although frequently Members of the
Government and their followers are inciting the people by telling them that there
are a number of these institutions and
that they are teaching blasphemy and
free love and other immoral subjects to
the youth of this community. I take it
that my duty as a Member of this House
is to review and amend any Measure passed
in another place that is calculated to
abridge or interfere with the constitutional rights and privileges of any party or
portion of citizens over which, as a
separate Parliament, we have jurisdiction. That is the reason why I would
appeal to the Members of this House not
to confer upon the Minister of Home
Affairs the extra-constitutional powers
that will undoubtedly be placed in his
hands if this Measure is left on the Statue Book of Northern Ireland for another
five years.
Further than that, I do not believe
there is any man in this, or in any other,
State, who should be trusted with the
powers that this Parliament is asked to
entrust to a Minister of the Crown, especially in view of the events I have already
referred to. I have been a loyal man all
my life.
No man has a greater respect
and veneration for the glories of the
British Constitution than I have. Under
that Constitution the people, when they
wake up to a realization of the power
with which they are invested, will be able
peacefully to bring about any reforms
that they think will be in the interest of
the peace, prosperity, and happiness of
the community as a whole. I believe in
neither bullets. bayonets, batons, or
bombs-but in the ballot. I believe that
great power is within the reach of the
people through the ballot, but I believe
that if we continue to place in the hands
of the Minister of Home Affairs the
powers which will be conferred upon him
by this Bill, we are placing in his hands
power that should not be placed in the
hands of any Minister, power that may
be used either consciously or unconsciously against the interests of the
minority and against the interests of a
party with which he does not agree, and
with which the Government do not agree,
judging by the statements made from time
to time by representatives of the Government.
I regret that the Minister of Home
Affairs is not present in this House today. It was my privilege recently to
preside over a meeting in a certain area
of this city, a constitutional meeting and
a peaceful meeting. We had not given
utterance to any unconstitutional sentiment, and we had endeavoured to impress
upon the people the necessity of using
the power of the vote to bring about
whatever reforms they thought
thought were
necessary. In the midst of the meeting a
few men, who, according to my information, were paid by a certain element in
the community, came out with drums,
and the authorities permitted them to
march up and down. They hustled me
off the chair I occupied as chairman of
that meeting. Had those who constituted
that audience been permitted to do so,
they could have overwhelmed the small
party that came against them, but when
they said, "Shall
we smash their
drums? I said "No." But I hold that
the Minister of Home Affairs had no
right to permit a peaceful meeting to
be broken up by a small party in the
community who came out with drums, and
who, I understand, were paid for that
night's work.

As the chairman of that meeting and a
lover of peace, I
to
suggested the
audience that we should move up to the
vicinity of the hospital, thinking that
this party would not have the audacity
to follow us to the neighbourhood of an
institution where the wives of the men
themselves were perhaps lying after childbirth, or where their children might be
undergoing an operation. But they followed us up to that hospital and
drummed us from there. The following
morning a Proclamation was out prohibiting all meetings of this constitutional
party. According to constitutional precedent the present Prime Minister of
Great Britain himself suggested to the
King the advisability of calling upon the
Leader of that very party to become the
Prime Minister of England. For these
reasons I think the Bill before the House
Is one that we should not allow to be
placed upon the Statute Book of Northern
Ireland. Even if we allowed it to be
placed upon the Statute Book, I think
we should place a limit on the time during which the Minister responsible should
be permitted to deprive citizens of the
liberty to which they are by right entitled.
I had intended to quote a speech I
made on this particular Act last year.
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
I then stated that I had known one man
for the last fifteen or sixteen years, and
that I was convinced he was as innocent
of any intention to commit a crime as I
was myself. Yet that man was incarcerated for eighteen months, his business
was destroyed, and he was compelled to
go and seek his livelihood in another part
of his native country. I mentioned the
case of another young man who had acted
as the hon. secretary of an organization,
the title of which would probably cause
a panic in the minds of officials of the
Government. I refer to the International Class War Prisoners' Aid Association, a perfectly constitutional association. The hon. secretary of that organization was arrested and deprived of his
liberty without the right of appeal to a
court of justice. For these reasons and
for others which I will not take up the
time of the House explaining, I feel it
my bounden duty as a lover of liberty to
use every endeavour I possibly can to prevent the placing upon the Statute Book
for any period of an extra-constitutional
Measure such as that now before this
House. It provides that
Any person authorized by the civil
authority, or any police constable, or
any member of any of His Majesty's
forces on duty may, where it is necessary for the purpose of effecting an
arrest in respect of any crime or any
offence against the regulations, exercise
the like powers as may at common law
be exercised by a police constable in
effecting arrest in a case where a felony
has been committed.
I have no hesitation in saying that if
the police authorities entered my home
and examined the literature I have there
they would get documents that they
would consider justified them in taking
action against me under this Act. I am
anxious to know the opinions of every
party in the State. I am anxious to
know the opinions of those to whom I am
diametrically opposed and I
and I do not
merely confine myself to reading the
literature of the party whose cause
espouse. Therefore, I have made myself
the proprietor of all the year books and
other literature of all the parties of which
I am not a member, including those to
which I am opposed. Just before leaving
home I opened Austin's "Student's Jurisprudence" and I found the following
passage which seems to me to be applicable to the present occasion :-
:-
I
Where persons in a state of subjection are free from legal duties their
liberties (generally speaking) would be
nearly useless to themselves unless
they were protected in the enjoyment
of their liberties by having legal rights
(importing legal duties on their
fellows) to those political liberties
which are left them by the sovereign
government.
I am legally free, for example, to
move from place to place, in so far as
I can move from place to place consistently with my legal obligations, but
this, my political liberty, would be a
sorry liberty unless my fellow-subjects
were restrained by a political duty from
assaulting and imprisoning my body.
This Rill proposes to take away this
political liberty, the right of freedom,
and to deprive individuals of the right of
appearing before a court of the land for
the purpose of proving their innocence.
We are all to be liable to the wishes of
the Minister, and who knows what information is brought to the Ministry? As I
have already said, my memory goes back
many years. It goes back to the great
strike of last century in another part of
this country when the gas workers were
out on strike. A lady who is now living
can substantiate what I am about to say.
Her husband was connected with the party
I am connected with. He was the
secretary of a trade union associated with
the men on strike, and he was approached
by a scoundrel who tried to get him to
receive the necessary formula to
manufacture a bomb to blow up a coal
schooner. The name of that man was
conveyed to the then authorities and he
was allowed to flee from the country.
Suppose my good comrade, innocent as
he was, had been foolish enough to accept
that formula and it had been found in
his house, nothing would have saved him
from a long term in gaol for a crime he
had never committed.
I regard this as a very serious thing
indeed. I would like to appeal to the
Minister of Home Affairs to cease these
continuous, insidious suggestions which are
calculated to bring about in the future
outbreaks such as occurred in the past.
When the trouble in Belfast broke out
I was working in the shipyard, and I had
been taking my dinner behind a machine
with a man whose religion I never inquired into. He was a sober, respectable
well educated young man who had a
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
23 MAY 1928
greater knowledge of science and history
than I had, and I was glad of the opportunity to sit at his feet and partake of
his knowledge. At that time men were
driven from their means of livelihood.
A man 5 ft. 10 ins. high and weighing
12 or 14 stones, a supporter of the present Government, beckoned me over at
dinner time. He said, "You had better
put your coat and hat on and go home."
That was said by a man of military age
who was capable of military service. I
asked why, and he replied, "They are
coming in from the brass shop to hit you
on the head with a spanner.'
"" I said,
"If I have injured any man in the brass
shop send him in here and I will make
amends. I am not going home.
I am
coming down to sell my labour power at
the gate in the morning, unless they kill
me in the meantime.”
That to a mau all of whose sons of
military age had done their duty in the
war, and who himself had spent four
years with the Young Men's Christian
Association tending to the wants and the
welfare of men on active service. That
is the kind of mentality that is calcu
lated to be brought about by these insinuations that are continually being made.
from platforms by statesmen and Government partisans in Northern Ireland, and
I appeal to them to cease this insidious
propaganda. It is calculated to poison
the minds of the people and a spark from
i may set a whole forest of humanity
ablaze. and bring about disaster to the
community. I speak strongly on this
question, because I feel strongly. I have
been the recipient of numerous letters
threatening to shoot me. When I was
elected to take my place in this House I
received more than one threatening letter
telling me that I would never
College Green, because they would shoot
me before I entered it. On the day that
I was elected a right hon. Gentleman had
the audacity to ask me in another part
of this building, "did I not know that
if they elected a Dublin man to the
Senate they would shoot him." I do not
believe the right hon. Gentleman would
think of shooting me or anyone else, but
such was his mentality that he thought
the rank and file of his party would shoot
me. Otherwise, why should he make use
of an expression of that kind
cross
Knowing the atmosphere in which I
live, knowing the political prejudices and
the religious bigotry that exists, I appeal
to the Government not to continue on
the Statute Book any Measure which is
calculated to break down the full and
complete confidence and trust of the
people, but on the contrary to make an
appeal to their humanitarianism and their
good-will to come in and to participate
in whatever constitutional reforms it is
thought necessary to bring about. For
the reasons I have given, and for many
others that I could give, I feel it my
duty to do all in my power to prevent
the Government placing this Measure on
the Statute Book.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
have listened with interest to the speech
made by the hon. Member, and during
the whole of that speech I did not hear
a single argument put forward why this
Bill should not be placed on the Statute
Book. What is the object of this Bill?
It is not to oppose Socialism or to prevent Socialists preaching at the street
corners. After the speech made by the
hon. Member I feel that we should congratulate ourselves on having a representative of the Socialist party of the calibre
of Senator Dorman. He has informed us
what a loyal, Christian, and God-fearing
individual he is, and, what is more, he
has told us that at one time he was a
member of the Orange Order, which enhances his value considerably. Unfortunately he has strayed from the path
and he has turned himself over to a
renegade organization that the Orange
Order would not touch. We have no objection to the hon. Senator going his way
and putting forward his views and teaching in Socialist Sunday schools.
Mr. DORMAN: Then why threaten
to shoot me?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
Someone has been pulling your leg.
have worked in the shipyard a great deal
longer than Senator Dorman. I was
born in Belfast and I know as much
about the Belfast working man as Senator
Dorman does.
If, as he has said, he has
resided in the South of Ireland for some
time he has not had as good an opportunity as I have had of studying the
good, loyal, sound men of the North of
Ireland.
Any loyalty that Senator
Dorman has he got during the time he
was in the Navy. He has evidently not
been successful in inducing his sons to
follow in his footsteps because his boys
are in the Navy and are good loyal
subjects.
367 Civil Authorities Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
Mr. DORMAN: So am I.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Why
does Senator Dorman talk about the
wrongs that can be inflicted on the community by this Bill when he knows very
well that the object of the Act which it
seeks to place on the Statute Book of
Northern Ireland for five years is to deal
with those rebels who intend, if they get
the chance, to ruin our homes and make
it impossible to maintain law and order
in this Province. He spoke of the time
when he received threatening letters.
That time has passed simply because the
Minister of Home Affairs had the very
powers that this Bill will continue to
place in his hands. As a result of those
powers he was able to restore peace by
the help of a loyal police force. The
Government and the people are determined to maintain that peace in the
future in spite of the efforts of those who
are determined when the opportunity
offers to upset the constitutional Government of this country. I am as anxious
as any person that the liberty of the subject should be preserved, and no lawabiding subject will suffer in the least as
a result of this Bill being placed on the
Statute Book for the next five years.
a
Senator Dorman has not been able to
point to a single case where, as the result
of the Minister of Home Affairs having
these powers, a law-abiding citizen has
been victimized or penalized. Such
thing is ridiculous. He referred to the
prayers offered in this House and he must
admit that he himself has been treated
in the most courteous manner since he
came here. Every one of us, although we
differ from him in his political views, have
treated him in the most courteous manner
and we have had to listen to some harrangues that it was very difficult to sit and
listen to. I intend to vote for the Second
Reading of this Bill. When one considers
the matters that have been brought to
light through the newspapers recently,
and the additional information that the
Minister of Home Affairs must possess as
to the activities of the disloyal elements
in our midst, the Members of the Government would deserve to be strung up on a
lamp post if they did not continue in
force the powers conferred upon them by
the Civil Authorities (Special Powers)
Act.
Senator Dorman spoke of Socialist
Sunday Schools. I would have no fear of
my children coming into contact with
Senator Dorman. I believe that he does
practice Christianity, but he is an exception in the Socialist party. I know a
considerable number of people who are
connected with the party to which he belongs and you could not even call them
Socialists. They are more like Communists or Bolsheviks. They cannot be
too extreme so
far as their political
opponents are concerned. I know how
they endeavoured to hound me down as
low as possible because they knew I held
Unionist views, and I have done more for
workmen in the Trade Union movement
than Senator Dorman has ever been able
to do from the street corner, and I am
proud of it. I welcome this Bill and I
am pleased that the Minister of Home
Affairs has taken the opportunity to prolong the operation of the Act with which
it deals for a period of five years instead of coming to the House to ask for
its renewal every year. I hope that at
the expiration of that period peace will
he thoroughly established. No matter
what any political or Republican organization may do we are determined to
establish and maintain peace and law and
order in Northern Ireland, and to carry
on our Government in a legitimate and
constitutional manner.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that in
dealing or endeavouring to deal with the
Bill before us this afternoon, we have two
main points which we ought to remember.
The first of these is that we are a Second
Chamber, and that as a Second Chamber
we have certain responsibilities and certain duties which we cannot and must
not shirk. We must, as it seems to me,
endeavour to discharge these responsibilities to the best of any ability we may
possess and without fear or favour,
affection or ill-will. We are not concerned here with the convenience of persons, with the convenience of Governments, and still less are we concerned with
our own convenience. If I had happened
to have consulted my own personal convenience I should not have risen this afternoon to have made the speech which I feel
I ought to endeavour to make.
One of our responsibilities, and perhaps
our greatest responsibility, is to see that
the principles of the Constitution are in
no way violated either inadvertently or
deliberately, and one of the principles
of the Constitution to which we
ought to devote our particular attention
309 Civil Authorities Bill-

23 MAY 1928
Second Reading

#
is the protection of the liberty of
each and all of His Majesty's subjects.
The second principle that we have to bear
constantly in mind is this: What is it
exactly we are discussing here this afternoon. Perhaps one could approach that
subject more conveniently by looking for
a moment or two at what it is we are not
discussing what it is that is not before us.
Now, we are not considering this afternoon whether we should or should not
pass a Bill for preserving peace and maintaining order in Northern Ireland. We
have not got to do that; the Bill is there
already. We all remember under what
circumstances the Bill was passed. We
all remember what provocation was given
for it. We know that we passed it and
why we passed it, and I think I may say
that should similar circumstances unhappily recur we should be perfectly
ready to pass a similar Bill again or, if it
was necessary, even a stronger Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: We know that the
powers given under the Bill have not been
harshly administered. We know they
have been administered with the greatest
possible moderation. We are not considering that to-day. We have not to
consider whether the Act, as it stands,
does or does not inflict any particular
injury on any law-abiding subject. The
Act, as I say, is there. We have not got
to consider this afternoon what would
happen if the Act lapses. The Act will
not lapse until the 31st of December of this
year in any circumstances unless, of
course, some special Act is passed repealing it, which is most improbable. We
have not got to consider what will happen
if the Act did lapse--at least not this
afternoon. What we are considering is
whether the Bill should be allowed to
continue for five years. We are asked
to consider that now, and not as in the
ordinary course, when we come to deal
with the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill
at the conclusion of the Session.
If this Measure came before us in the
ordinary way in the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill what would be necessary, I
imagine, in future would be, as it has
been in the past, for the Minister of
Home Affairs to come here personally, if
he chooses to exercise his, right, or for
the Minister in charge in this House-all
that Minister has to do is to come to
Parliament and say His Majesty's Government with a full sense of responsibility, and with the knowledge they
possess, feel it right to ask Parliament to
extend these powers for another year.
If they came to us like that I do not
think there would be any dissentient
voice. I am certain that no Member of
this House would ask the Minister making that statement to justify his statement. It would be recognized that there
were matters within the knowledge of the
Minister which he could not be urged to
disclose and which he ought not to disclose. In fact the Minister would not be
under the necessity of saying-if I may
say so anything like as much as the
noble Viscount said this afternoon. We
are not considering any of these things.
We are considering whether the Act of
1922 should be carried on to-day for five
years more. Now the Bill is a very
drastic one undoubtedly. I am not going
into the provisions. That is entirely beside the point. It is a drastic Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: It does touch the
liberty of the subject, although, undoubtedly, it only touches or threatens to
touch the liberty of one subject in order
that other subjects may be protected,
and the Act has at its very commencement a very satisfactory proviso.
The proviso is "that the ordinary course
of law and avocations of life and the enjoyment of property shall be interfered
with as little as may be permitted by the
exigencies of the steps required to be
taken under this Act." That proviso
has, as I have already said, been acted up
to to the last letter. However admirable
as has been the work the Act has done
the point still remains, should we release
such a drastic Act from the annual review and control of Parliament? Is it
not a constitutional principle that such
powers given for a limited period should
be subject to constant review by Parliament?
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: Let us see for a moment
what happens in England. As the House
knows, it is unlawful in Great Britain to
keep a standing army without the annual
consent of Parliament. That has been
so for many years, though it has but
little bearing perhaps on this particular
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
question. In England in 1920 they
found it necessary and advisable to pass
an Emergency Powers Act in order, as
will be remembered, to prevent the comransom and the
munity being held to
means and necessities of life interfered
with. Provision was made that if a state
His
of emergency appeared to
Majesty might issue a Proclamation say-
That Proclamation
ing it did exist.
would, of course, be laid before Parliament if sitting and if Parliament was not
sitting Parliament was to be called together within five days.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
exist
no
Mr. LESLIE: The Proclamation was
to remain in force one month and
longer, without prejudice, of course, to a
new Proclamation being issued to carry it
on.
Mr. DORMAN: Parallel lines!
Mr. LESLIE: We found it necessary
two years ago to pass a somewhat similar
Act. We did not reproduce the English
Act word for word. We did not make
any provision for calling Parliament together. We did not limit the Proclamation to one month, but perhaps it was
quite near enough. We know what was
said not only in this House but in another
place in 1922 under the circumstances to
which I have referred. The Bill, as it
We
then was, was brought before us.
were definitely told it was for one year and
not to be continued beyond a year unless
Parliament so decided. Parliament has so
decided on four or five occasions. But I
suggest to the House that no case has
been made for extending the powers for
five years, so to speak, in one piece.
It is true in fact that this present
Parliament is getting on in life; it is
approaching its end, and it might well be
that the Government might say to us
whether they say it to us now or at the
end of the year in the ordinary course
when the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill
comes before us-they might say that
under the circumstances it would be desirable to extend it for two years in order
that no inconvenience might be caused if
Parliament suddenly came to an end.
That would seem to me to be a not unreasonable thing to do. However, that
is not the proposal that is before the
House. The proposal is five years.
And
on the sole grounds that such a Measure
as the Civil Authorities (Special Powers)
Act of 1922 ought not to be removed
from the annual review of Parliament on
that ground and on that ground alone-
I am not attacking the Act in any way
whatever; I am not following either of
the two previous speakers.
not
attacking the Act as an Act. I am not
attacking the way it was administered.
Quite the contrary. But what I do find
difficulty in supporting is the proposal to
go on for five years instead of bringing
it annually before Parliament for review.
am
I
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support
the Second Reading of this Bill. I fully
agree that this Bill should be carried on
for another five years, although some,
perhaps, might object to carrying it on
for that length of time and might think
it advisable that this Bill should be reviewed and renewed every year. Well, I
think it is better to have a five years'
limit instead of having it brought up
every year and listening to speeches such
as we have been listening to to-day.
Since this Act came into existence I have
not yet met one single person, who has
kept the law, that this Bill has interfered
with. The fact of the matter is that it
has been of assistance to law-abiding
citizens to move about the city and the
country, and they do. with the
consciousness that they were safe and
secure while this Act was in existence.
SO
a
There has been a great deal said during
the past few days on this wonderful word
"liberty." It must have taken possession of this present age. Nearly every
place you go you will hear them speaking
of liberty. If you go to a temperance
meeting at the street corner they are talking about liberty there. If you go to
an anti-prohibition meeting they are
talking about liberty. If you go to
Socialist meeting at the Custom House
steps they are talking of liberty. They
want to take up a collection on Sunday
and on account of not being able to do so,
their liberties are interfered with. Here
we have people talking about liberty as
regards this Bill before the House to-day.
Now, my conception of liberty is like that
of Mill. He says a man has a right to
do what he likes so long as he does not
interfere with the liberties of other
people.
Civil Authorities Bill-
Second Reading
23 MAY 1928
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. BARCLAY: I say any party of
men or company of men have the right to
organize themselves into any kind of organization so long as that organization
has not for its object interference with or
the taking away of the rights or liberties
of any other people. And if it has for
1: its object the taking away of the rights
and liberties of other people, then I say
the Government that has been elected by
constitutional means is perfectly within
its right to be equipped with power to
look after the interests of the great
majority of the people, and to say that
their rights and liberties are not to be
tampered with or trampled under foot by
any disloyal organization.
As has been mentioned by the Leader
of the House, it is necessary that this Bill
should be continued. He mentioned
several things that have occurred even
while this Act is in force. Outside
the City of Derry a few months ago
there were a number of boys supposed
to be Boy Scouts, and these boys were
under a perfectly trained tutor who
was training them for the purpose of
throwing bombs. Now, we never hear
tell of Boy Scouts practising bomb throwing. Why? Because it is not necessary
that Boy Scouts should be so trained. In
the City of Derry or convenient to Derry
these boys practised bomb throwing.
When they were brought to court their
excuse was they were training as Boy
Scouts. Then a few months later what do
we find? We find during Easter week a
demonstration in the City of Derry. I
think the Minister of Home Affairs was a
little bit to blame. Why was this demonstration allowed to march through the
streets of Derry carrying colours and waving these colours in the face of loyal
subjects in the City of Derry? I would
like to know why they were permitted
to do so? Here we have had the same
thing taking place in Belfast. I am glad
to say they did not get going so far. The
same thing took place in Dungannon and
several other places in Northern Ireland.
When these people have gone so far with
an Act like this in force what would they
not do if this Act were removed from the
Statute Book.
Then it is mentioned to-day how
literature has been found encouraging this
disloyal organization which is in existence
at the present time, and which is ready
to rise at any time to try by all means
within its power to bring this Government of ours to naught if possible. As
for some other things that have been said
I am not going to say anything more only
this. Senator Dorman need not have felt
so strongly on getting a death warrant.
I got a death warrant one time. Then I
got a second. I had three in my pocket,
and the hon. Senator Sir William Coates,
who is here to-day, remembers one particular day that I appeared in the City
Hall. On that particular day I had three
death warrants in my coat pocket and I
am still alive. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). I say that Senator Dorman need
not be afraid of a death warrant. It will
never do him a bit of harm. Therefore,
I have great pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading of this Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
really did not intend to intervene this
afternoon, but I have listened during the
sitting to a number of speeches, very
eloquent no doubt, but a great deal of the
subject of them had very little to do with
the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
suggest that for a very few minutes we
should look at what is the cause why this
Bill should be renewed for a certain
period. I think even the hon. Senator
(Mr. Dorman) on my left admits that the
causes that were in existence when this
Bill was first passed are still alive to-day.
It broke out at that time above ground,
but we know ourselves what is going on
here and further on the other side of the
Border where the danger may come from,
these same spirits are still there and on
the slightest chance they might break out
easily to-morrow or the next day. This
also is not like a Bill that applies only
to ourselves. It applies from what I
understand to the Free State
across
the Border, where they have found it
necessary to introduce a Bill of the same
kind and they have taken it for seven
years, which shows they recognize the
difficulty, and this Bill will be heartily
welcomed by the Parliament which sits
in Dublin.
Senator Dorman made a very good
speech in some way-from the point of
oratory, but if I did not know anything
about this Bill I should have taken Mr.
Dorman's speech as a speech showing the
315 Civil Authorities Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Colonel Sharman Crawford]
necessity for the Bill being introduced.
I am glad the Minister of Home Affairs
is here now. We are asked to continue
this Bill for five years. The prospects of
permanent peace are not in sight.
what we do by passing this Bill is to stir
up angry spirits in the other House, which
we
And
think should be allowed to subside. I think these things must be
within the power of the Ministry. If
this Bill is passed-as I hope it will be
passed there is no reason why if the time
should come that Mr. Dorman prophesies the Government should not come
to the House and ask for its repeal. We
should allow it to pass to-day. For good
loyal men and even for disloyal men-Mr.
Dorman is a good loyal man-this Bill is
as much protection as it is for me. Therefore, we welcome this Bill to-day. It
does no injustice to anyone unless he is a
man who wishes to do evil.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): I am glad of the
opportunity which this honourable House
has given me to come here. I am only
sorry that business in another place prevented me from coming here to listen to
the whole debate. But there are one or
two points I wish to clear up. I would
have liked someone in this House to have
defined what liberty is. To my mind
everybody has a different idea of what
liberty is. I think when we find one
interfered with in the slightest degree
they always say their liberty is being
taken away. May I say this? I would
like to emphasize this for the benefit of
Mr. Dorman. This Act since it was first
placed upon the Statute Book has never
been used against political opponents of
the Government. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). Never on any occasion.
It was very interesting in another place
to hear the debate and to find that not
one single case was quoted where the
Government used the powers of that Act
against their political opponents.
The attitude the Government take up
is this: So long as any organization
exists and I think my hon. Friend has
already quoted cases-which has for its.
object the destruction of the constitution
by means of violence and bloodshed or
outrage, so long as this organization.
exists and so long as they openly state
that is what they intend to do, so long is
it necessary to have this Act upon the
Statute Book in order to meet the dangers
as they arise. I venture to say that if
we or our predecessors had equipped
themselves with these powers Northern
Ireland would have saved millions of
pounds in damage to property and, above
all, many valuable lives. We can all
realize that when I say that at one period
no fewer than 35 lives were lost in one
week in Northern Ireland.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: And
they were not all Socialists.
is.
Sir DAWSON BATES: No. So long as
any group of people have as their declared
intention if possible to have a repetition
of those terrible days, so long I suggest
the Government are justified in keeping
this Act on the Statute Book. Now
there was one case quoted. I only quote
it as one example of how useful this Act
It gives us power to exclude undesirable individuals from coming into certain
portions of Northern Ireland. A case
was quoted in the other House in which
a description was given of a poor school
teacher who had been shut up in prison
and had been interned and then he was
not allowed to reside in Northern Ireland. An order was served on him, and
he did not obey that order. The result
was, he was brought before the magistrates and charged with an offence under
the Act and received sentence of six
months' imprisonment, which he served.
That school teacher thought it desirable
to obey the law in future and returned to
the Free State. When that criticizm was
offered to me in the other House I was not
aware that on that very day when that
criticizm was being offered this innocent
school teacher had just been arrested,
charged with having a large number of
weapons and ammunition in his possession. As his case is sub judice I do not
propose to say anything further beyond
the fact that when the Free State thought
it necessary to deal with this poor school
teacher I think it is desirable that we
should try to keep him out of our territory. I only quote that one instance.
I want to emphasize again for the benefit particularly of my hon. Friend Senator
Dorman that in no case has this Act ever
been or will it ever be used against
political opponents of the Government.
With regard to the point of my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) that it is undesirable
to make it for a period of years it gives
stability to the magistrates. A good
many cases come before the magistrates.
Health Insurance Bill-
Committee
23 MAY 1928
and I think hon. Members will appreciate it is desirable for the resident magistrates to know that the Act is in force for
a certain period and not have any doubt
or think it is going to expire in one
month, as the penalty may be six months
imprisonment. I think hon. Members
will appreciate the desirability from that
point of view alone that there should be
a fixed period. Apart altogether from.
that, I do think it is very undesirable--
we all wish to let bygones be bygones if
we can to have to refer to cases for the
justification for this Act. It is very unpleasant, year after year, to have to get
Bangor, Viscount
Barclay, William
Coates, Sir William Frederick
Crawford, Colonel R. G. Sharman
up and have the same old discussion and
the same old reasons given in defence of
the Act. Therefore, the Government
came to the conclusion that they should
have one discussion for five years. I
hope in five years time whoever is
Minister of Home Affairs will be able to
say there is no further necessity for the
Act.

Mr. LESLIE: I should like to take
advantage of the presence of the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Labour,
to ask him to give us a little further information on the subject of Clause 2. I
do not think it is particularly clear as
it stands, and when we refer to Subsection (1) of Section 96 of the Act of
McDowell, William John
Pakenham, Colonel Hercules
1924 I am not sure that it gets very
much clearer. Sub-section (1) of Section
96 of the principal Act says, if for the
purpose of obtaining any benefit or payment or credit for reserved value under
the Act either for himself or for any
other person, any person knowingly makes
a false statement, false representation
and so on. I confess I am not very clear
what it is the Bill proposes to get at in
this new Clause. Is it for the purpose
of getting at the man who for the purpose
of evading paying for stamps makes some
false statement to somebody? If so, who
is the somebody? When is it suggested
that the false statement is going to be
made? I think the whole thing is anything but plain. It is more understandable, I think, in connection with
the obtaining of benefit. I hope the right
hon. Gentleman will be so kind as to
explain a matter which I am sure he
knows all about.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): This provision proposes to
enable the non-payment of arrears which
have been ordered by the court to be paid,
to be treated as an offence punishable by
fine. Hon Senators are aware of the fact
that we very frequently have to prosecute
persons in order to obtain payment of
arrears. There is very often difficulty in
collecting these arrears. There have been
special difficulties in the past, and this is
really to give the Department more power
Roads Improvement Bill
Health Insurance Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
in order to collect these arrears, and consequently to prevent non-compliance.
Hon. Senators are aware that it is absolutely necessary if we are to pay out large
sums for benefit that the contributions
shall be collected, and my Department
have had during the past few years great
difficulty in some instances in insisting
that the contributions are regularly paid.
It is most unfair not only to the insured
person but to the funds of the society.
Very frequently we find that an insured
person who would be entitled to benefit
if the contributions had been duly paid
is not able to claim benefit owing to the
laxity or wrong intention of the employer.
And the Department, as no doubt hon.
Senators know, have taken very strong
measures in many directions in order to
insist on the contributions being paid.
This will strengthen our hands, and I
think hon Members will agree to it.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not desire to object.
I only wanted information. The point
is, I now understand, principally in regard to arrears. Presumably the false
statement is going to be made to the inspector or somebody who has to make
inquiries.
Mr. ANDREWS: Quite.
Mr. DORMAN: I would like to express my approval of the action of the
Government in taking steps to see that
some punishment is inflicted on employers
for taking advantage of the workmen and
not paying the contributions. The right
hon. Gentleman stated there was a large
number of instances of the employee
being deprived of benefit owing to the
misconduct of the employers. Apparently his Department has not had sufficient authority to deal with the question.
Clause ordered to stand part of the

Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which the
noble Viscount has just invited us to read
the Third time is one which gave rise to
a good deal of discussion in this House,
and as a certain share of that discussion
fell upon my unworthy shoulders I
should not like to part with the Bill without giving some expression of good-will and
without expressing the hope that it may
prove satisfactory. I still regret it was
found impossible to meet the difficulty
about vesting the bed and soil of the
widened portion of the road, and I am
still somewhat doubtful as to whether the
Committee was wise in rejecting the proposal of the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman, the chairman of the Association of County Councils, as regards the
closing of second-class roads to certain
vehicles with pneumatic tyres. But, as
regards that, I venture to repeat the hope
I expressed in Committee that it will be
universally recognized that this question
has been very carefully considered, the
321 Roads Improvement Bill

23 MAY 1928
Adjournment
pros and cons were weighed, and, therefore, though some may think the decision
wrong, as I know they will think it
wrong, still I do hope the decision will
be universally accepted. I hope the
common sense and the sense of economy
which our local authorities have shown in
the past will remain in the future. I am
sure if that is so any difficulties in the
Bill will be found to disappear, and I
hope it will help us in meeting some of
the difficulties which were referred to
when the Bill was introduced by the noble
Viscount.

1919, and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce, under the Electricity (Supply)
Act, 1919, in respect of the Urban District of Ballycastle, in the County of
Antrim, be approved."
Perhaps I should briefly explain to the
House the object of this Motion. This
Special Order, granted by the Electricity
Commissioners for Northern Ireland and
confirmed by the Ministry of Commerce,
will confer upon the Ballycastle Electric
Light and Power Company, Limited,
statutory authority to supply electrical
energy for public and private purposes in
the Urban District of Ballycastle. The
Rules of Procedure have been complied
with and the Order follows the usual form.
Two applications were originally made
to the Electricity Commissioners in respect
of the same area; one from the Urban
District Council of Ballycastle, and one
from the Ballycastle Electric Light and
Power Company, Limited. After holding
a local inquiry into the matter, the Commissioners decided to grant the Company's
application and, in exercise of the powers
conferred upon them by Section 1 of the
Electric Lighting Act, 1888, they decided
to dispense with the consent of the Urban
District Council. The Commissioners
have made a report to the House in this
connection. I beg to move that the House
approve the Special Order.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Methodist College Bill.

the Third time.

Fisheries Bill-
24 MAY 1928
Committee
CLAUSE 4 (Licences for the sale of
salmon, trout and eels).
a
(2) An applicant for a licence to sell
salmon, trout, or eels shall make an application in writing in the prescribed
form to the clerk of the board of conservators of the district in which the
place where it is proposed to sell is
situated, and shall forward therewith
a certificate signed by a justice of the
peace that the said applicant is a fit
and proper person to receive such licence
and the said board may thereupon issue
through their clerk to such applicant a
licence in the prescribed form to sell
salmon, trout, and eels at such place,
and may in like manner from time to
time renew, in the prescribed form, any
such licence so issued by them, unless
the same has been terminated under this
Part of this Act.

*
(5) Every such licence shall be terminated by-
(a) the death of the holder; or
(b) revocation by the Ministry of
Commerce under this Part of this
Act; or
(c) surrender by the holder.
(6) Every such licence and every renewal thereof shall (unless the licence is
previously terminated) continue in force.
until the expiration of the calendar year
in which the licence was granted or last
renewed, as the case may be, and shall
then expire.

-*-
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I am really sorry
to trouble the Committee with such
small Amendment as the insertion
of the two words "grant and" not "grant
or,"
as it is accidentally misprinted
on the Paper-after the word "thereupon" in line 4, page 3. It seems to
me, much as I regret it, that we cannot
very well do without inserting those two
words, because all through the Clause we
refer to "grants," and, of course, it is
obvious that you must grant a licence
before you can issue it. Therefore, for the
sake of uniformity, I think we must insert
those words.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The effect of the Amendment
would be to make the sub-section read,
that when a dealer shall have made application for a dealer's licence, supported by
a certificate of the justice of the peace, "the
said Board may thereupon grant and issue
through their clerk to such applicant a
licence in the prescribed form." The
Government hold that the additional words
as proposed are unnecessary as the issue of
a licence is equivalent to the granting of
a licence and therefore the words "grant
and" are redundant. For that reason the
Amendment is not acceptable.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not entirely agree
with the noble Viscount. If the Ministry,
having started with the word "issue" had
stuck to the use of that word it would
have been different. What I object to is
that they chop and change. However,
I will not put the Committee to the
trouble of a division.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Ad-
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
4, line 33, sub-clause 5, to leave out paragraph (a). This is a more difficult matter.
The Clause before us reads that every such
licence shall be terminated by the death
of the holder. A provision of that sort
does not occur in the similar statute dealing with a licence to deal in game.
mittedly, such a licence is a personal
licence, and when the man dies he cannot
go on personally dealing in game or salmon, but the man must be presumed to be
a fishmonger or someone with a business
who has perishable goods in his shop, and
it seems to me rather unnecessary to put in
an express provision to the effect that his
licence should be terminated by his death.
Whether it will be necessary, if we take
this out, to insert other words, such as
I have on the Paper under Clause 7, to
make the expression "licence holder" to
include the personal representative, I do
not know. I should prefer to simply
leave out these words and leave the matter
exactly in the same position as a fishmonger who has a licence to deal in game.
In that case there is no provision for
cessation on death, and what happens is
that the shop carries on and whoever takes
over the business takes out a new licence.
That is the course I should have expected
to have been followed here. In any case,
whether the man is living or dead, the
licence will not last after the 31st Decem-
Fisheries Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie.]
ber, so that the interval is not great. It
seems unnecessary to put in a provision of
this sort, because it can only be an annoyance to whoever may succeed the man
who may have the misfortune to die.
think that on the ground of common
sense the most convenient plan is to leave
it out. I move accordingly.
I
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What I understand the hon. and
learned Senator desires is that a licence
should not terminate at the death of a
holder; in other words, that it should
apply to a set of premises. The Government desire that a fish dealer's licence
shall be personal, and for that reason they
have inserted in sub-section (2) a provision
that applicants for a licence are required
to furnish a certificate signed by a justice.
of the peace that they are fit and proper
persons to receive such a licence. I might
mention to the Committee that a game
dealer's licence in also a personal licence,
and if the game dealer who holds the
licence dies the person who remains in
charge of the shop cannot sell game.
Mr. LESLIE: He does.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This would not apply, as the
Committee will realize, to a firm that takes
out a licence in the name of the firm, but
would apply to those people who personally hold a licence. I do not think
there will be any real hardship inflicted
on anyone if this Amendment is not
accepted. In any case, it would only be
a question of a few days during which
anyone could not deal in salmon, trout,
or eels, and the Government are not prepared to accept the Amendment.
that
Mr. LESLIE: It really seems
whoever is responsible for this Bill is prepared, first of all, to assume that the representatve of a deceased man is not likely
to be a fit and proper person to receive a
licence. I should have thought they
would have taken the other view, that he
was so entitled, unless the contrary was
shown. In the second place those responsible for this Bill are indifferent to the
loss by destruction of any perishable.
goods that are in the shop, and in the
third place they are not particularly regardful of the convenience of the public
who are accustomed to buy fish from that
fishmonger. That is what it comes to.
However, I do not desire to press the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Line 41, at end of Sub-section (6) insert" Provided that any licence granted
under this section shall continue in force
until the expiration of the calendar year
1929, unless such licence is previously terminated by revocation or surrender."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment can be
disposed of by asking a question. I
should like to ask the noble Viscount if
he can tell us when it is proposed that
Part 2 of this Bill should come into operation. If I get an answer to that question
it will probably be unnecessary for me to
move this Amendment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I cannot at the moment tell the
hon. and learned Senator definitely, but
I will let him know on the Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: That puts us in rather
a difficulty. The point is that if this part
of the Bill is not to come into operation
until 1st Januray, 1929, there is no necessity to insert the words I have on the
Paper. Otherwise trouble arises.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am now able to inform the
hon. and learned Senator that Part 2 of
this Bill will come into force on 1st
January, 1929.
Mr. LESLIE: Then it is unnecessary
to move the Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Question put, That Clause 4 stand part
of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to take
this opportunity to inquire what view
is taken by the Government on the position of a man who sells, let us say, a
salmon steak in an ordinary refreshment
house. We say in Clause 4 that nobody,
after the commencement of this Section
shall sell, expose for sale, or keep for
sale, any salmon save as otherwise expressly provided unless he is duly
licensed. We see in the definition Clause
that for the purposes of this Act salmon
includes part of a salmon. I cannot find
in the whole of Part 2 any express provision that a man who keeps an eating-
Fisheries Bill-
Committee
24 MAY 1928
house may sell a piece of salmon to his
customers unless he has a licence under
this Act. I cannot believe that that is
the intention. As I have already said,
those responsible for this Bill have shown
a certain indifference to the public convenience, but I do not think they intend
It seems to me
to go as far as that.
that Clause 4, which is the first place it
occurs-although it could also be done on
Clause 8-is the most suitable time to ask
the Government what their intention is.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It certainly was not the intention of the Government that this Clause
should prohibit any restaurant from supplying the hon. and learned Member, or
any other Member of this House with a
salmon steak or whatever other fish he
might require.
I cannot, at the moment,
give the hon. and learned Member the assurance he asks for because I have only
just got notice of the question, but the
Government consulted their legal advisers
on this matter and I will give the hon.
Member an answer on the Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: And, if necessary, the
Government will bring up an Amendment?
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Yes.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Prohibition of sale of salmon, etc., without licence) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Publication of licence).
(1) Every holder of a licence for the
sale of salmon, trout, or eels shall cause
such licence to be displayed prominently
during business hours in the place to
which such licence relates.
(2) Lists of persons holding such
licences as aforesaid may be published at
such times and places, and in such manner and form, as the Ministry of Commerce may from time to time direct.
