*
*
*
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 4, line 30, leave out from
"licence" to "in" in line 31 (to be displayed prominently during business hours)
and insert "or the notice that he is the
holder of such licence, to be exhibited
during business hours at or."
Mr. LESLIE: Again I think I can
I want
save time by asking a question.
to know what the idea of the Government
is in requiring a man to display his licence
It
prominently during business hours.
occurs to me that a licence is not likely to
be a very large document, and that although you may stick it up on the shop
wall nobody can see it. It can scarcely be
displayed prominently, and I thought myself that the better plan would have been
to follow the Game Act and put a board
up outside a man's premises. It is a condition of a game licence that such a board
shall be put out, but perhaps the noble
Viscount can explain what the intention
is, and it may be unnecessary to press the
Amendment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The object the Government have
in view in requiring that a licence should
be prominently displayed is for the security of the public, so that they shall know
they are buying fish from an individual
who is authorized to sell it.
Mr. LESLIE: Do I understand the
noble Viscount to think that that end will
be attained better by the words in the Bill
than by the suggested alteration.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What notice does the hon. and
learned Member refer to? Who gives the
notice?
Mr. LESLIE: What was in my mind
was that there should be a notice displayed
on a board outside the shop, such as the
notice, "Licensed to sell tobacco." The
public would see that, but they would not
see a foolscap licence tucked away in the
corner of the shop.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: If such a notice is put up there is
no guarantee that it was issued by the
Ministry of Commerce.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

(2) Any person authorized in that
behalf by the Ministry of Commerce,
any officer authorized in that behalf by
a Board of Conservators, and any member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary
of a rank not lower than sergeant, may
inspect any register kept in pusuance
of this Section during such time as the
premises to which the register refers are
open for the carrying on of business; and
it shall be the duty of the licence-holder
and of every person keeping such register to produce for inspection by such
authorized person, officer, or member of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary such
register, and also all invoices, consigment notes, receipts and other documents (including copies thereof where
the originals are not available) which
may be required to verify any entry in
or explain any omission from such register, and to allow such authorized person, officer, or member of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary to take copies
thereof or extracts therefrom.
*

*
*

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 7, at the end of Sub-section (2) to
insert:-
:-
"Provided that any copy or extract
so taken shall not include any particulars as to price."
Here again is another small matter.
Perhaps I ought to say that these
Amendments were put on the Paper with
very great rapidity and without the opportunity of previous consultation. We
took the Second Reading of this Bill only
on Tuesday, and the debate on the
Second Reading is not in our hands yet.
Consequently, we have to put Amendments
down on the Paper very quickly and trust
to a conversational debate to clear up matters.
The difficulty here is that we have
provided earlier in the Clause that the
record to be kept by the man who is selling
fish shall not include any particulars as to
price. We have definitely provided that.
Then we go on to say that the Register is
to be produced for inspection by any
authorized person, and also all invoices,
consignment notes, receipts, and other
documents, including copies thereof where
the originals are not available, and permission is given for an authorized person
to take copies and extracts. If we require protection as to price in the earlier
part of the Clause so do we require it in
the second part, because if a policeman or
other authorized person is not to take details as to price from the Register-and
he cannot, because they are not thereneither ought he to be allowed to take
them from the invoices, if they give prices.
Accordingly, it seems to me that a short
proviso would meet the case, and I beg to
move the words on the Paper.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It is quite true that the register
itself will contain no particulars as to
price, and in so far as copies of or extracts from the register are concerned the
Amendment is therefore unnecessary. In
regard to copies of or extracts from invoices, consignment notes, receipts, or
other documents, it should be observed
that the production of any of these documents can only be required to verify an
entry or to explain an omission from the
register. In the opinion of the Government, the price at which the fish was purchased or sold might prove a material
point in the evidence that could be adduced in support of a charge of illegal capture. The Government is accordingly unable to accept the Amendment.
I am
Mr. LESLIE: I am very sorry.
trying not to be more obstructive than I
can help, but I am afraid the noble Viscount has failed to convince me. A man
is not to put particulars as regards price
in his register, where, I admit, they might
be of some use, but if an authorized
person wants furthur information he
may require the man to produce something which does give the information
as to price, and he can take a copy of
it. I do not think I ought to withdraw
this Amendment. Perhaps it could stand
over until the Report stage.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The point is that it might be
very necessary to know what price was paid
for certain fish. Sav that salmon was
sold for 2d per lb. The police would require to find out at what price it was purchased. It is only in cases where the
authorized person wants to verify an
entry that he is entitled to inspect invoices,
etc., to ascertain the price paid for the
fish.

CLAUSE 10 (Restrictions on capture
or possession of eels).
Any person who-
(a) lands, buys, sells, or has in his
possession eels any fourteen of
which weigh less than seven pounds
collectively; or
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
10, page 6, line 35, in paragraph (a) to
leave out the word 'lands." The Committee may remember that as the Clause
originally came before those who sit in another place it had relation to eels landed
less than 8 ozs. in weight. It was pointed
out that there was some difficulty in ascertaining the weight of an eel until you
got him on land, and an alteration was
made so as to make the offence relate to
having in possession eels any fourteen of
which weigh less than 7 lbs. collectively.
The word "lands" was left in and I think
the word has become rather out of place
now. If any word were wanted I think
it ought to be "takes" but I cannot see
that we want a word. At any rate I am
afraid the words "lands" is hopelessly
out of place and, therefore, I suggest we
should take it out.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I cannot agree with the hon. and
learned Senator that the word "lands"
makes the paragraph impracticable.
The insertion of the word "lands" is
quite distinct from the expression "having
possession" and it is put in intentionally.
The paragraph as it stands, in the opinion
of the Government, makes it perfectly
clear that the restriction imposed applies
equally to the fisherman as well as to the
dealer and the consumer. If the Amendment were accepted a question might well
arise as to whether the removal of undersized eels from the line to the boat
brought the fisherman within the application of the paragraph. The Government intends to make it an offence for a
fisherman to take into his boat any 14 eels
collectively weighing less than 7 lbs. In
other words, the act of removing from the
hooks and placing in the boat would in
itself constitute an offence. Otherwise a
fisherman might catch a lot of immature
eels and after landing them in his boat he
might discover that he had been observed
and consequently he might throw the eels
back into the water where they would die.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has
not explained how the man is to get the
eels off the hooks. He must get them off
some how. I think that perhaps the
point deserves further consideration.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The fisherman does not necessarily take a very long line into his
boat.
Mr. LESLIE: Not always.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: He takes the eels off the line
as it is being brought up. It is a line of a
thousand hooks, and perhaps more. If
these fish are immature he can throw
them back into the water, but if he takes
them into the boat they are dead and the
damage is done.

the Amendment.

CLAUSE 11 (Alteration of certain
licence duties).
*
*

(2) The licence duty for long lines for
eels shall be two pounds for a long line
with not more than one thousand hooks,
and three pounds for a long line with
more than one thousand hooks, and no
Board of Conservators shall have
authority to reduce or diminish the said
licence duties.
*
Colonel
*
*
PAKENHAM: I beg to
move, in Clause 11, page 7, line 29, to
leave out from the word "pounds" to the
word "and" in line 31 (for a long line
with not more than one thousand hooks,
and three pounds for a long line with
more than one thousand hooks). On the
Second Reading of this Bill I appealed to
the noble Viscount to ask the Government
to reconsider sub-section (2) of Clause 11,
which deals with the question of licences.
for long lines, and he replied to my request by saying that this sub-section had
been adapted from a by-law of the Enniskillen Conservators which was found to
work well. We know that the fishery
laws of Northern Ireland are not perhaps
on a par with all good fishery laws anywhere else; in fact they are so bad that it
has been considered necessary to bring in
this Bill. I do not consider that that
was a very fair answer to my request, and
I was therefore bound to put down an
Amendment with a view to getting the
Government to further consider the
matter. If my Amendment is accepted
the sub-section will read :-
The licence duty for long lines for
eels shall be two pounds, and no Board
of Conservators shall have authority to
reduce or diminish the said licence
duties.
I think this Amendment would be a
help and not a hindrance to the Government. We know how difficult it is to
am

detect some divergencies from the written
law. If you consider the fact that under
my Amendment the Government would
receive £2 for every long line-I
thinking particularly of Lough Neagh—
that would be a certain income for them.
The offices of Conservators would find it
extremely easy to issue licences, and they
would have no difficulty in knowing the
number of people on Lough Neagh who
used long lines; in fact, they know that
now. If you retain the present subsection and charge £2 for a long line with
a thousand hooks and £3 for a long line
with more than a thousand hooks you
will find that the extra £1 you charge
will be entirely wasted in trying to count
the number of hooks on a line and in the
number of police and water bailiffs you
will have to employ. They will have
several months' work and they will not
only have to count the number of hooks
on land but they will have to go out into
the Lough and count the hooks in the
boats because there is nothing to prevent
a fisherman adding several hundred more
hooks to his line when he is out in the
Lough. Unless the water bailiff has a
boat that goes faster than the fishing boat
he will not catch the fisherman, and that
will probably mean that water bailiffs will
have to be provided with boats that have
more power than sailing boats.
I do suggest to the Government that a
law which it is almost impossible to carry
out is a bad law. The Government will
get a far greater income if there is a uniform licence duty of £2 than by differentiating between a line of a thousand hooks
and a line with over a thousand hooks.
It would be very hard for a water bailiff
to be certain as to the number of hooks on
a line, and this Amendment would really
simplify their work and in the long run
would produce at least as good an income
as will be obtained under the sub-section
as it stands at present.
AND
Viscount MASSEREENE
FERRARD: I cannot agree with what
the hon. and gallant Member has said
in moving this Amendment. On the
Second Reading of this Bill I mentioned
the fact that these licence duties were
adapted from the licence duties payable
in the Enniskillen Conservancy district,
and the hon. and gallant Member seemed
to think that that was not a fair argument. I should like to point out that
these rates in the Enniskillen district were

fixed as recently as 1925, so that it cannot
be argued they were fixed so long ago
that they are not applicable to the present
times. The object of the Amendment is
to have a uniform duty of £2 for all long
lines, and I gather from what the hon.
and gallant Member said that he thinks
the difficulty of differentiating between
£2 lines and £3 lines will be so great as
to make the provision regarding the £3
duty a dead letter. This is not the view
of the Government. These long lines, as
we all know, are of great length and the
hooks are spaced several yards apart, and
any water bailiff could detect immediately
whether a line had a thousand hooks or
more even if the line were lying coiled up
in a boat.
As the hon. Member knows, lines in
Lough Neagh are put out in zigzag formation, and I am informed that a line that
takes a thousand hooks is about three miles
long. If a water bailiff saw that a fisherman was using a line that was more than
that length it would be quite obvious that
he was evading payment of a larger duty.
The Government do not consider there is
any great difficulty in determining whether
a fisherman is using a line of a thousand
hooks, or more than a thousand hooks, and
although there may be some attempts at
evasion if there were one or two prosecutions it would put a stop to it. I do
not think the hon. and gallant Member
has much ground for fearing that evasion
of the payment of the duty will result from
the adoption of this sub-section.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I do not think
the reply of the noble Viscount meets the
case at all, and I am afraid I shall have to
press the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 17 (Penalties, Constitution
of courts and legal proceedings).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(2) Notwithstanding anything in the
Fisheries Acts enacting that the sum
payable as a penalty by a person convicted of certain offences under those
Acts is to be not less than a certain sum,
a court of summary jurisdiction constituted as provided in sub-section (3) of
this section shall have power to impose
a penalty of less than such sum upon persons convicted of such offence, in any
case where the court considers that such
less penalty would meet the case.
(3) A court of summary jurisdiction,
when trying any offence under the
Fisheries Acts or under this Act, shall
be constituted of a resident magistrate,
sitting alone or with other resident
magistrates, and a court of quarter sessions, when hearing and determining an
appeal from such court of summary
jurisdiction, shall be constituted of the
county court judge or recorder sitting
alone.
(4) A person aggrieved by any conviction or order made by a court of summary jurisdiction on determining any
information or complaint under the
Fisheries Acts, or under this Act, may
appeal against the conviction or order in
manner provided by the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts.
(5) The provisions of section seventyfour of the County Officers and Courts
(Ireland) Act, 1877, shall apply to
orders of dismissal made by a court of
summary jurisdicton under this Act.
*
*
*
*
-*-
*
*
*
a
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
17, page 13, line 2, to leave out the words
"of summary jurisdiction." This is
very simple matter. I may say it is entirely unconnected with any Amendment
which follows it on the Order Paper.
The point is, as it seems to me, that in
the attempt made in the Bill to do away
with the minimum fine the job has only
been half done. They have given power
to a court of summary jurisdiction, no
matter how it is constituted, to impose a
penalty less than that which is prescribed
in certain Statutes, but I am inclined to
think that the Clause before us stops
there and does not give the same power
to a county court judge as that possessed
by a court of summary jurisdiction, and
I do not think that can be the intention.
As the matter stands it is possible for a
Fisheries Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie].
court of summary jurisdiction to dismiss
such a prosecution, in which case it comes
up to the quarter sessions and the dismiss
may be reversed and the man convicted.
If that happens, under this Bill the old
penalty would have to be imposed. The
power a court of quarter sessions possesses
to revise a decision of a court of summary
jurisdiction is given in Section 24 of the
Petty Sessions Act of 1851, a well-known
Section, and that is limited to confirm,
I am sure it was not devary or reverse.
liberately intended to deprive the county
court judge of the power possessed by a
court of summary jurisdiction, and I think
that whatever may happen to this subsection (3) the omission of the three words
"of summary jurisdiction" will do what is
necessary. I beg to move.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared
to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move, in
Clause 17, page 13, to leave out line 3
(constituted as provided in sub-section (3)
of this section). I think that by leave of
the Committee the Amendment which
follows it, in fact all the other Amendments which stand in my name on the
Paper, can be considered with this
Amendment, because they are all part of
the same thing. The point raised in the
first Amendment is whether in future this
jurisdiction should be confined to what are
called resident magistrates instead of
being left as at present to the ordinary
bench. Opinions differ, I believe, very
much on this matter. We heard the other
day from County Fermanagh of the
salmon-loving proclivities of the Fermanagh justices and that it was quite impossible to leave these matters with them,
but other Members protested and thought
their magistrates were quite capable of
dealing with the question of flax water
running into a ditch, and so on.
leave the matter to one resident magistrate you have one man deciding a question of fact, which is not in accordance
with our ordinary principle of law, and
if you insist on bringing two resident
magistrates and one favours a conviction
he will withdraw to agree with the other
man who is in favour of an acquittal.
If you
Then it is difficult to get two resident
magistrates together. The number of
resident magistrates in Northern Ireland
is only about twelve and the number of
petty sessions courts is twelve times that
would
number, and therefore there
be scarcely enough resident magistrates
to go round. It is inconvenient to
to hold combined courts, to hold a
court in Ballysmith and to hear there
cases coming from the surrounding
districts of Ballybrown, Bally jones,
and so on. That has been done under
the Civil Authorities Act and it used
to be done under the old Crimes Act,
but it is a distinct inconvenience and involves delay and expense. The ordinary
local magistrate says, "if I am not fit to
try such a simple case as whether water
is being improperly let off from a flax
dam, what am I competent to deal with ?"
That is what the ordinary magistrates
resent.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
No,
no.
Mr. LESLIE: The ordinary magistrate
whose sphere of office lies somewhere apart
from the right hon. and gallant Gentleman feels that his sphere of office is perpetually being encroached upon, and any
influence he may possess in the county and
any value that may attach to his decision
in other matters is very seriously impaired.
So far as I know there is no general dissatisfaction amongst the people as a whole
with the way in which magistrates have
dealt with offences under the Fisheries
Acts. I have, myself, been a magistrate
for rather more than a score of years, and
I have attended the benches to which I am
accredited with reasonable regularity, and
I have not found anything that could be
described as a determination not to convict in any case where the evidence points
to conviction. Speaking for my own part
of the country, I think the local benches
are entitled to respect, and I do not think
we shall serve the public interest by making the change suggested in this Bill.
On the other hand, I do agree that an
appeal from a Court of Summary Jurisdiction should be heard by a County Court
Judge or Recorder sitting alone. A
modern Court of Quarter Session is entirely different from the Court that existed
when those regulations were made. That
matter would form, if the first Amendment
is excepted, the subject of other Amendments which will have to be simply moved.
I beg to move.

posing a slight on the local magistrates of

Mr. LONG: I should like to support the

new
"Section three of the Salmon Fisheries
(Ireland) Act, 1863, is hereby repealed,

Mr. LONG: A number of my
neighbours who are engaged in salmon
fishing in the open sea and who are supporters of the Government have asked me
to endeavour to obtain the insertion of
this new Clause. I realize from information I have obtained in the Lobby that I
Committee


Fisheries Bill-
[Mr. Long]
THE SENATE
have not the shred of a shadow of a ghost
of a chance of succeeding, but I place the
Amendment before the Committee as it
was put before me. I understand that
prior to the year 1863 fishing with bag
nets was permitted, and in that year a law
was passed prohibiting the use of these
engines. A fixed draft net is a plain net
run out from the shore and it extends
from the surface of the water to the
bottom. It is tied to the shore and fixed
at the outer end by an anchor to hold it
in position. It circles round with the
tide and a man with a boat stands at the
outer end watching, and if he sees a salmon trying to get round the net he immediately throws a stone into the water
to cause the salmon to return and it is
This is a
pulled ashore by fishermen.
very precarious means of earning a livelihood and it is a mode of fishing that is
very primitive.
At the present time those engaged in
draft net fishing cannot obtain, or at least
have difficulty in obtaining, men to undertake the work. They find it is very unremunerative, and they ask for permission
to return to the old bag net which was
a more economical method of fishing and
required no attention. It was lifted once
a day, or oftener if they felt so inclined,
and it did not entail the same close attention as the present method demands. In
rough weather it is impossible for the men
to stand watching in a boat for the salmon
to endeavour to get round the net, and
when the rivers are muddy it is impossible
The result of the
to see into the water.
Act of 1863 has been a great increase in
river fishing and a consequent decrease in
sea fishing.
The major portion of the fisheries in
Northern Ireland are owned by the Bann
Fishery Company, which is an English
Company controlled by Sir Charles Petrie
and Sons, Liverpool. They have a Scotch
manager and the salmon is as a rule sent
to London, and even magistrates do not
I
get salmon under the London price.
must confess that the people in the

Therefore, I beg to move the Motion
I do not wish to
standing in my name.
detain the Committee at any length by

FERRARD: An Amendment in exactly

am right in saying it was practically the
The Government was unable to
accept the Amendment for the reason
that the restoration of facilities for the

not only in our rivers but also off our
coasts. The
has before
matter
formed the subject of very careful consideration by the Ministry of Commerce
in its capacity as the central fisheries

Government has been unable to insert a
provision in the Bill for the restoration
of the use of bag nets. The hon. Member,
I gather, seems to think that if this
Clause was not accepted it was in the interests of what, I think, he called the big
battalions to the detriment of the poorer
fishermen. The Government feel quite
frankly that if this Clause were inserted
it would be detrimental to the fishing, and
have a very serious effect on the fisheries
of Northern Ireland. For that reason
the Government cannot accept it.

propagation and scientific
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
NEW CLAUSE:-
In each fishing district, the season for
rod and line fishing shall be uniform
for all rivers within that district, and
such season, when fixed, shall not be extended or modified except with the
permission of the Ministry of Commerce.
Brought up and read a First time.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
think it is a matter of regret-I believe
it is unavoidable-that when a Bill of this
kind, where there are so many Amendments, is considered the Minister in
charge of the Bill is not represented here
to-day, so that the Government were defeated on one Amendment and there were
close divisions on two others. I think
that is unfortunate and, I say, it is a pity
the Minister was not present here to-day.
It puts the Leader of the House in a
rather false position.
I now come to the Amendment which,
I think, is really the most important
Amendment on the Paper. It really
affects fishing as a whole. I do not move
it for the purpose of opposing the Government in any way. The question of closing and opening is a matter on which
there is great diversity of opinion amongst
the supporters of fishing, and the only
reason I put the Amendment on the
Paper was that it was represented to me
by a great many people who are in favour
of the Amendment, and who have fishing
rights on rivers which open later than
other rivers in the same district to be
necessary. I understand the difference in
the period of opening of rivers in any area
is about three weeks, and what they complain of is that if a river-say the
Minister sitting opposite me has a river
which opens early and I myself have a
close river. His river opens three weeks
before mine. What is the result? The
result of that is it deprives the proprietors
of the fishing on my river from fishing,
and leaves it open to every poacher in the
district to come and take the fish in my
river and sell them as having been taken
in his river that opened early.
I understand there is a great deal to
be said on both sides. I do not expect
the Government to accept this Amendment at the present time, because it is a
matter that will require a tremendous
amount of inquiry. The original fixing
of closing times for different periods in
rivers in the whole of Ireland, which included Northern Ireland at that time,
was done over one hundred years ago.
There have been some variations since
then, but none of any great importance. I
think it would be better to bring the later
opening rivers up to the period of the
earlier opening rivers so as to give a
chance to the owners of the later opening
rivers to get their own fish. We will take
the kindred sport of grouse shooting.
There is a universal period for grouse
shooting. But, after all, between the
Yorkshire moors and the moors in the
North of Scotland there is nearly the
same period of difference, a fortnight or
three weeks, when grouse cannot be shot
because they are not fit to be shot.
has nevertheless proved useful in stopping
poaching in that district.
It
I am going to move my Amendment
but I am not going to press it to a division
to-day, because on this important question we all desire the same thing-to
protect the fisheries not only for the
owners but for strangers who come over
from the other side and bring money into
the country. After all, I have heard different accounts. I have never had the
pleasure of being a conservator but,
naturally, conservators are only human
beings. At the back of their mindsthough they may be thinking generally of

Fisheries Bill-

what is best for the salmon fishing-they
must consider what is best for their own
river and their own lines first. We know
the river salmon do run earlier in some
rivers, still I believe this difficulty can be
got over. I want the Government if they
are going to reject this Amendment of
mine to give me some hope as a representative wishing to encourage the sport of
fishing and to do the best thing for the
salmon fishing that in the near future, before the next season comes round, we can
do nothing this year, we may get a
better universal opinion-you can hardly
get a universal opinion as
you cannot
change the opinions of one body against
another-but something approaching it
might be done. I beg to move.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. and gallant Gentleman appealed to me that some steps
should be taken not at the moment but in
the immediate future to get some uniformity and to get a fixed time in each
fishing district. Of course the hon. and
gallant Gentleman knows that Boards of
Conservators have at the moment no
The hon.
power to fix the close season.
of
Member went through a great deal of
history, and I do not propose to follow
him in that. Of course, before there is
or can be an alteration in the seasons
there has to be a statutory inquiry, and
I may mention that since the passing of
the 1842 Act a large number of statutory
inquiries have been held, the result of
which was that different close seasons
were established in different places. If
we were to fix, as the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman desires, a uniform
close season either for the whole
Northern Ireland or, as he says, a large
section of such fishing district, it would
be tantamount to disregarding the experience of careful inquiries for something going on to a hundred years. The
Government do not feel justified in fixing
a close season for these fishing districts or
for Northern Ireland until inquiries have
been held. I have no doubt my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Commerce,
when there is a meeting of the Fisheries
Advisory Committee will go into the
whole subject again. I am told that even
they are not all of the same opinion on
the subject.

have got the answer I expected to get.
But I do not agree with the noble Leader

There is a great division of opinion and

349 Administrative Provisions Bill-
Second Reading
24 MAY 1928
I
Co

Title and Preamble agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
with Amendment to the House.
That the House do now
Ordered:
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.
Administrative Provisions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill contains reference
to four different matters which come under
the control of the Ministry of Finance,
and in which it is sought to improve the
machinery of Government administration.
I propose to deal with each Clause separately as the questions are unrelated to
each other, and I hope I may not have
to detain the House for more than a few
minutes in giving the necessary
planations.
ex-
Clause 1 deals with the question of the
appointment of official arbitrators in
cases of compulsory acquisition of land by
local authorities. Hon. Members will
realize that such cases arise under Acts
of Parliament such as, for example, the
Labourers Acts or those promoted by
local authorities for the acquisition of
land for public purposes, and so forth.
The existing machinery for dealing with
these cases was set up by an Act of our
own Parliament in 1922, which replaced.
that established by an Imperial Act of
1919. The existing Measure permits of
the establishment of a panel of competent
persons who may be also engaged in
private practice; and a number of people.
were, accordingly, nominated to the panel
and performed the work in turn as became necessary.
The serious difficulty in this existing
procedure has been that of expense. The
volume of business has not, of course, been
very great. As a number of persons have
been on the panel and as they only
occasionally had their turn, the fees which
they have charged have been proportionately heavy, and have been the subject
of considerable protest from many local
authorities.
To remedy this it is proposed in this
Clause of the Bill that instead of the
of
existing procedure the Minister
Finance should nominate one or more persons to act as arbitrators in such cases.
It is further proposed that this change
should only apply to cases in which local
authorities are concerned, and the result,
it is hoped, will be to satisfy their objections that the expense of the existing
system is out of proportion to the work
and the cost involved.
Clause 2 of the Bill deals with the question of a Government Chemist for Northern Ireland. It is proposed under this
Clause to make arrangements that the
statutory duties which are imposed on this
official should be undertaken by the
Government Chemist in London. Under
the existing Statute there is a doubt as to
the identity of the official who is designated for the purpose. In point of
fact, the services of the Government
Chemist in London have been frequently
utilized by our courts here, and this
Clause will put the matter beyond dispute.
In the third Clause of the Bill it is
proposed to transfer the duty of auditing
accounts under the Educational Endowments (Ireland) Act of 1885, from the
Ministry of Home Affairs to the Controller
and Auditor-General. This is a very
logical arrangement and will give a wel
come relief to the Audit Department of
the Ministry of Home Affairs, the main
duties of which are, of course, the auditing of the accounts of local authorities,
and not of educational institutions.
Clause 4 of the Bill has relation to the
question of officials who are authorized by
the Ministry of Finance to obtain administration in the case of deceased persons
whose estates do not exceed £500. Hon.
Members are probably aware that there
has been in existence an arrangement for
giving assistance in obtaining Grants of
Administration in such cases at a nominal
cost. The existing officers so authorized
outside the cities of Belfast and Londonderry have been the Customs officers who
collected and forwarded the necessary
documents to the Probate Registry and
Estate Duty Office. In our own two cities
similar assistance has been given by the
officers of the Probate Registry who are
stationed there, and in regard to them it
is not proposed to make any change.
Customs officials have, however, been
351 Administrative Provisions Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
drastically reduced in our area, mainly as
the result of the transfer of the administration of Old-Age Pensions from them to
our own Ministry of Labour, and if
assistance is to be continued in these probate cases it is necessary to find some other
officers to take their place. It is, accordingly, proposed to appoint managers of
Labour Exchanges to do this in places
where Customs officers have been withdrawn, and my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Finance, has no doubt that
the work will be done by them as
efficiently as it has been performed in the
past. Those Customs officers who may be
left and I think the number is being
reduced from about 30 to 5-will continue to perform the duties as hitherto.
I have explained very briefly the four
Clauses of the Bill. The right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, whom we
are pleased to see here, will be able to explain to hon. Members any point that may
arise in debate. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The House will observe
there is an Amendment standing in my
name on the Paper to leave out the word
"now," but not to proceed after that in
the more familiar manner and suggest
that the Bill be read a Second time this
day six months, but that the Bill be read
a Second time on the first sitting day after
the Summer recess. The object of the
Amendment, of course, is to get time to
consider this Bill. It is a four-clause Bill
which, as the noble Viscount has just
told us, is in effect four separate Bills, for
the four subjects are entirely unrelated.
It seems to me that this is a Bill which
this House does require time to consider,
and the clause of the Bill which I had
particularly in mind when putting down
my Motion or Amendment was the first
Clause. That Clause deals with new
arrangements in the matter of appointing
arbitrators in respect of land acquired
compulsorily.
The noble Viscount told us, I think,
that the present procedure is based on an
Act of our own Parliament, and he told
us that Act was based on and replaced the
Imperial Act of 1919. With that I cannot in the least agree.
I agree the Act of
1922 did make some alteration, but did
not touch the main feature of the Act of
1919. Now the Act of 1919, the full ttle
of which is "to amend the law in respect
of compensation in respect of land acquired compulsorily for public purposes
and the cost of proceedings thereon" is a
very important Measure indeed. I escertained this morning-I was not able to
begin considering the matter before this
morning as the Bill was not in our hands
until yesterday afternoon-I ascertained
this morning that the Second Reading
debate on that Bill in the Imperial House
of Commons occupied six hours. Judging
from the number of pages in the House of
Lords report I should say another six
hours were occupied by that assembly, and
the reports of the proceedings of the
House apart from the Committee proceedings, which were specially reported, were
commensurate with the importance of the
subject. The Act was moved in the Imperial House of Commons by the present
Lord Chief Justice of England. He was
followed in the debate by the Chairman of
the Commission which shortly before that
inquired into matters of this kind. One
of the cardinal features of the Act was
that arbitrators shall be on a panel and
that that panel shall be appointed-I am
speaking of the Act of 1919 as it came in
as regards Ireland by a committee consisting of the Lord Chancellor, the Master
of the Rolls, and the President of the
Surveyor's Institute.
A good many things have happened
since then, and by means of Adaptation
Orders and so on, the committee so far as
Northern Ireland is concerned now consists of the Lord Chief Justice and the
President of the Surveyors' Institute, or
rather a gentleman acting on his behalf.
That is provided for in the orginal Act.
Arbitrators were to be put on a panel by
these gentlemen. You have the judges
and you have the profession. The proposal now is not to repeal the whole Act
but to alter it in this respect that the
panel of arbitrators-I understand the
panel consists of either six or seven-in
place of that it is proposed that the Ministry of Finance shall appoint either one cr
two-I am not quite sure whicharbitrators and that the functions of the
Lord Chief Justice and of the President
of the Surveyors' Institute shall be suspended. Thus the Ministry which already
has control over the fees paid to these
gentlemen-they have that power as successors of the Treasury--has-
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): We have no control.
Mr. LESLIE: You have control over
the fees.
353 Administrative Provisions Bill-

24 MAY 1928
Second Reading
Mr. POLLOCK: We do the paying.
Mr. LESLIE : You did not appoint
the men. The men were appointed
judicially. That, of course, is where I
am inclined to join issue. I think however efficient the control of the Treasury
is—we know it is very efficient-still there
are matters which are outside the purview of the Ministry, and the deciding of
the professional qualifications of surveyors and arbitrators is, I venture to
suggest, outside. That is the main
point. It seems to me we ought to have
time to consider this matter in all its
bearings, and it would be better for that
purpose to postpone the Second Reading
of the Bill which is not perhaps peculiarly
urgent until after the Summer recess
rather than move somewhat hastily the
deletion of the Clause. I understand
complaints have been made as regards
fees charged by arbitrators. Well, the
fees are now at all events under the control of the Ministry of Finance.
Mr. POLLOCK: No.
Mr. LESLIE : To some extent.
Mr. POLLOCK : Not the fees.
Mr. LESLIE: Rules have been issued
as recently as 1925. I understand from
information given me by a gentleman of
that profession there was one very serious
cause of complaint, but that does not
seem to me a justification for upsetting
the whole arrangement. The great complaint was in the Northern end of the
Province about the expenses entailed in
an arbitration as regards labourers'
cottages. There were 48 cottages concerned and the arbitrators' fees amounted
to £72 9s.
Mr. POLLOCK: Sixteen cottages.
Mr. LESLIE: We are apparently not
dealing with the same case. In the case
I refer to there were 48 cottages and the
bill was £72 9s. The right hon. Gentleman answered a question on the subject
in another place and he said with his
accustomed lucidity and definition that
that was the amount which the arbitrator
collected and paid in, I think, to the Exchequer, and that the sums paid out by
the Ministry would be something less. I
am sorry that I did not bring the references with me. Evidently there is some
other case in the mind of the right hon.
Gentleman. The point which I do particularly want to direct attention to this
afternoon is, does this House want further
time to consider this Bill or does it not.
We could deal with the details later on.
I propose to suggest later on that if the
number of men on the panel must be reduced let them be reduced in numbers by
resignation or efflux of time. Let the
power of fixing the fees be under the
Ministry, and let the power of appointment remain with the Lord Chief Justice
and the President of the Surveyors'
Institute as originally by the Imperial
Act. The arbitrator we know is a man
of great importance.. He has to decideand there is no appeal-on the amount
that is to be paid as compensation when
land is taken over by a local authority,
whatever that may mean. We are not
told what is meant by a local authority,
or by a public authority. Public authority is defined by the Act of 1919. I
forget the definition of public authority
there. You have only to refer to the last
section, but it does not include Government Department.
As
The original Act of 1919 did include
Government Departments. The noble
Viscount has just pointed out that
Government Departments are omitted
from the provisions for a very proper
reason. I am not objecting to the Bill
as a whole at the moment. I am asking
for more time. As regards Clause 2,
which is I think a matter of a Government Chemist, I have nothing to say.
regards the question of educational endowments I am in the same position.
But coming to Clause 4, Clause 4 relates
to some method whereby those interested
in small estates can get out probate
rather more quickly than through the
poor solicitor and at less expense. Well,
of course, one does not know what their
expenses may be, but there is an old saying that the man who is his own lawyer
very often becomes somebody else's client.
At all events that is not the point I
have looked into. So far as I can I have
looked into Clause 1. I have seen representatives of the Surveyors' Institute and
have received letters from them about it.
Whether they have yet succeeded in obtaining an interview with the right hon.
Gentleman or not I do not know.
know that not only were they trying to
get an interview, but the right hon.
Gentleman was kind enough to inform me
yesterday afternoon that if he could man-
I
Administrative Provisions Bill--
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
age it he certainly would see them and see
them at once. Therefore this is the question I am leaving to the House: Do we
want more time or do we not? If I am
not supported in the demand for more
time I shall, of course, ask leave to withdraw my Amendment which does not, I
repeat, amount to the rejection of the
Bill.
Mr. POLLOCK: I had hoped that
following my interview with the hon.
Senator yesterday afternoon possibly his
difficulties in respect of the first Clause of
this Measure would have been removed.
I may state frankly at first that the
Government attach much importance to it
from many points of view. Not only this
Clause but also and especially the last
Clause of the Measure, number 4, to
which the hon. Senator has referred, are
regarded by the Government as of sufficient importance to warrant its prosecution, but I will touch upon that, if I
may, later. I refer now at the moment to
the topics to which the hon. Senator has
drawn attention, and it is desirable I
should give the House a short sketch
of the incidents which developed and
which rendered this Measure necessary
and especially this Clause. For some
considerable time past there have
been complaints as to the expenses
of adjudicating on small areas of ground
that were necessary for the building
of houses and such like, but without
dwelling much on that feature of it this
trouble culminated in a very striking
case which occurred not far from where
the hon. Senator lives, in Coleraine, where
the site for nine houses came under discussion and where an agreement could
not be reached between the buyer and the
seller. It was necessary the appointment
of an arbitrator should be made under the
circumstances outlined by the hon.
Senator. The cost of that particular
work amounted to some £80. I think it
was close upon £80, and the Urban
District Council at Coleraine were so
indignant at this charge that they immediately attacked the Ministry of
Finance who had nothing whatever to say,
I may remark, with regard to the appointment of an arbitrator or the assessment
of fees.
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask the right
hon. Gentleman for the date of that?
Mr. POLLOCK: I think possibly I
have the date here before me of this particular case. It happened 18 months ago.
The hon. Senator assumed that the
Minister of Finance had control of the
fees. Nominally, that is assumed to be
so, but in practice it is impossible that the
Ministry can force-it has no power of
forcing the arbitrator to reduce his fees.
As a matter of fact, in this case a serious
difference of opinion arose and the
Ministry of Finance was unable to assist
Coleraine Urban Council in the matter,
and I as Minister had to stand not only
very serious criticizm in my House,
but also criticizm from the Urban Council
of Coleraine, because of the excessive
charges that were made by the arbitrator.
There was another very serious case arose
in the City of Londonderry where a small
area of ground was required for the construction of the new bridge, and the fees
in this case amounted to some £800. The
Derry Corporation, of course, following
Coleraine experiment, attacked the
Ministry of Finance, and without
going into all the negotiations that
ensued, the case was ultimately settled for
£200. The Ministry then had protests
from almost every rural authority in the
Province indignantly protesting against
the methods that were adopted for the
carrying out of this work of determining
the value of these small areas of land here
and there over the country.
Perhaps it would be enlightening if I
gave just a short list of the authorities.
I have them here. I do not propose to
recapitulate the language in every case,
because it was very extreme in some
cases. I begin with Larne, "17th August,
1926. It is considered that the payment
of such fees would be detrimental to the
providing of labourers' cottages in future.
For this reason alone Larne Council have
indefinitely postponed considering the
applications for cottages already received." In this case the fees have
actually stopped the operation of building labourers' cottages.
Mr. DORMAN: It has been a scandal.
Mr. POLLOCK: Newtownards Rural
District Council a little more politely "request that the Ministry of Finance would
immediately reduce the fees scale"-
which we have no power to do. Londonderry considered "the scale fees most exorbitant and we request the reduction of
same at once." Coleraine Rural District
a resolution in very
Council passed
357 Administrative Provisions Bill--

24 MAY 1928
Second Reading
an

strong language. It is too long to read.
Ballymena Rural Council followed. They
had
important scheme for
labourers' cottages facing them, and they
were naturally very much concerned as to
the liability that might possibly arise
under that scheme. Then we have their
association, that is, the whole body of
public authorities throughout the Province protesting against this, and asking
for a change in the system-also the
Association of Rural District Councils in
addition to the general rural councils
throughout the whole Province.
In the face of that is the Government
to stand by and allow this condition of
things to obtain. It must be remembered
that where such heavy charges are imposed for perfectly simple matters it is
tending to retard the development of the
whole country. And if I may ask the hon.
Senator to think of the condition of things
that is facing us now, he will probably
revise his opinion in respect of the
urgency of the matter. It is well known
that cwing to the recent Road Bill there
will likely be very large measures of road
widening, road improvement, and the
cutting of corners and such like where
land has to be acquired. It is quite
possible that under circumstances of this
kind a great many differences of opinion
may arise with regard to value. I think
I have dealt sufficiently with that Clause
to impress hon. Senators with the knowledge that it is really a matter not only of
urgency, but there is something of abuse
that should be remedied. I think you
will agree it is an abuse that should be
remedied. It is the only method the
Government can think of for providing a
remedy so that the public authorities
throughout the country will be protected
from exorbitant charges.
In regard to the last Clause of the
Measure, I think the House will agree
that it is a Clause devised in the interests
of the poorer people of the Province.
Where an estate under £500 has to be
proved and the executors are, perhaps,
very illiterate persons we propose to
appoint the managers of the rural
changes to receive them and to give information and fill in printed forms for
them. These printed forms can be obtained at the Registry. If we do not
make provision for this it means that
these poorer people must go to solicitors
exand what the charges may be, of course,
they do not know. I am not declaiming
against that. But I would impress upon
the House that this is only the continuance of a practice which has existed for
fifty years. It exists in England to-day.
It has always existed in point of fact and,
therefore, we are not changing the practice. We had 400 of these small cases
The House will
during the last year.
realize from that how very necessary it
is for these people to have small estates
dealt with not only at moderate cost but
expeditiously. I think I have outlined
with as little prolixity as possible the
features of this Measure, and the Government would regard it as a very grave misfortune indeed if this little Measure I
have now presented to the House for
Second Reading were not expeditiously
passed.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to express the
hope that Senator Leslie will not press
his Amendment. I think this is a Measure that is required on the Statute Book
at the earliest possible moment. Hon.
Members may not be aware of the fact
that this Province has not had the same
facilities as other parts of Ireland to develop labourers' cottages and private
housing accommodation for agricultural
labourers. Those who are acquainted
with the condition of affairs in various
parts of the country will know that the
counties comprising Northern Ireland
have a very small percentage of agricultural labourers' cottages in comparison
with those in other parts of our native
land. In fact, there are single counties
in the South of Ireland that have more
labourers' cottages than there are in the
whole of Ulster, and one of the reasons for
that, I believe, has been the exorbitant
cost of the transfer of land in connection
with the erection of such cottages
Northern Ireland. I can state that I
know one county in which the average cost of transfer of land and
legal fees was only about £6 per cottage, and I believe that in the county
the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Colonel Sharman Crawford) belongs to
the cost came to £32 and sometimes £33
per cottage. In consequence of that exorbitant cost local district councils had
their hands tied. I therefore hope that
this Bill will result in stimulating the
various counties in Northern Ireland to
make further provision for labourers' cottages.
Civil Authorities Bill-
Health Insurance Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
A
As regards the latter part of the Bill,
I do not think it should be hung up.
large number of those poorer people who
live in the country have in the past had
the right to refer to the Revenue Officers
for the purpose of assisting them to take
out letters of administration under a
special Act of Parliament, especially with
regard to Industrial Assurance Policies.
I have personally come across cases where
persons who were ignorant of the rights
conferred upon them by law have been
charged very high legal costs in order to
obtain what was due to them. I know
of a case where a sum of £50 was due to
a person in consequence of an accident,
and the cost of taking out letters of administration for that small sum of £50
amounted to the sum of £15 17s 6d. I will
not mention the name of the firm of solicitors concerned. Many of the Imperial
officers who have been performing these
functions in our area are being removed,
and in my opinion it is necessary that at
the earliest possible moment the Government should be authorized to replace
these officers. I cannot conceive any
officer who will be more widely known
throughout all the districts of our Province than the managers of the various
Exchanges. The people will be able to
consult them and they will be able to
advise the people and protect them from
the exorbitant charges that have sometimes been made when they have had to
go elsewhere for advice.
Mr. LESLIE: We can, of course, go
into this matter more in detail at a later
stage, and I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 4 (Licences for the sale of
salmon, trout and eels).
(1) From and after the commencement of this section, and save as otherwise expressly provided in this Part of
this Act, no person shall sell, expose
for sale, or keep for sale at any place,
any salmon, trout, or eels (other than
salmon, trout, or eels dried, or
served in tins or other containers)
unless he is duly licensed under this
Part of this Act to sell salmon, trout,
or eels at such place.
pre-
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in Clause 4,
2 line 35, after the word "conpage
tainers" (or other containers) to insert
the words, "or cooked and prepared for
consumption at a meal.”
Hon. Senators will recollect that when
the Bill was last before this House the
hon. and learned Member (Senator
Leslie) expressed the view that, according to the strict wording of the Bill,
hotel keepers and inn keepers providing
salmon, trout or eels as portion of a meal
to their customers would be obliged to
procure a licence and to comply with the
obligations resting on licensees in the
matter of records, etc. Hon. Members
will also recollect that I gave an assurance to the House that it was not the
intention of the Government to bring
Fisheries Bill-
Report Stage
29 MAY 1928
F. GR.
1.
A
be
E
kai
1.
hotel keepers and inn keepers as a class
within the scope of the licensing scheme,
and that I promised that if, in the
opinion of the Government's advisers,
the Bill as it stood bore the construction
which the hon. Senator implied to it the
Government would put down an Amendment on Report stage with the object of
making the Government's intention on
the point unmistakably clear.
In order to remove any possibility of
doubt on the point, an Amendment has
been placed on the Order Paper, the
object of which is to exempt all sales and
the keeping for sale of salmon, trout or
eels cooked and prepared for consumption
at a meal. The wording of the Amendment does not, as will be noted, confine
this exemption from the licensing obligation to any particular class of persons,
but specifically excludes fish which has
been cooked and prepared from the
licensing scheme. Hon. Members will
also note that while the Amendment relieves the hotel keeper and such persons
from the necessity of procuring a licence,
it does not affect the responsibility placed.
upon all persons by paragraph (c) of
Clause 5 in the matter of purchase of
salmon, trout or eels, that is to say, the
purchase only from persons duly licensed
to sell such fish. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: As an occasional consumer of salmon at restaurants and places
of that kind I am very glad that the Government has introduced this Amendment.
I think, perhaps, if I may say so, the incident lends colour to a view which has of
late been more than once expressed in
this House, and that is that it is very
desirable that this House should be
allowed ample time for the consideration
of Measures which come to us from another place. I need say no more about
that, but I would like the noble Viscount
to tell us definitely, if he will, that
the Government is satisfied that 10
further difficulty is to be expected from
the words, "keeping for sale" which
occur in this Clause and in others, and
also that they are quite satisfied that no
part of Clause 5, either paragraph (a),
paragraph (b), or paragraph (c) requires
any consequential alteration. As
gards "keeping for sale," I may point
out that there is obviously a gap between
the time the person who intends to cook
the salmon for sale receives it from some
person, presumably licensed to sell, and
rethe time the man sells it in a somewhat
altered condition to members of the
public. I should like to be assured that
there is no possibility of a difficulty
occurring in that connection.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I have gone into this point
with the Ministry and they are quite
satisfied that what the hon. and learned
Member is afraid of cannot possibly take
place. I can give the hon. and learned
Member that assurance.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 7 (Register to be kept by
holder of licence).
*
*
*
*
(2) Any person authorized in that behalf by the Ministry of Commerce, any
officer authorized in that behalf by a
board of conservators, and any member
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary of a
rank not lower than sergeant, may inspect any register kept in pursuance of
this section during such time as the premises to which the register refers are
open for the carrying on of business;
and it shall be the duty of the licenceholder and of every person keeping such
register to produce for inspection by
such authorized person, officer, or member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary
such register, and also all invoices, consignment notes, receipts and other
documents (including copies thereof
where the originals are not available)
which may be required to verify any
entry in or explain any omission from
such register, and to allow such authorized person, officer, or member of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary to take
copies thereof or extracts therefrom.
Mr. LESLIE: It will be remembered
that on the Committee stage a small
Amendment of mine was negatived by
consent in order that the noble Viscount
might look further into the question. The
Amendment I then proposed, and which
I now repeat, is in Clause 7, page 5, line
27, at the end of Sub-section (2) to insert
the words, "Provided that any copy or
extract so taken shall not include any particulars as to price." The difficulty is, as
it seems to me, almost a mechanical one.
We have provided at the top of page 5 in
connection with the register that a man
has to keep that he is to enter the pre-
Administrative Provisions Bill 368
Fisheries Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Le lie]
scribed particulars which shall not include any particulars as to price about his
purchases, receipts and sales. We further
provide that the register is to be open for
inspection by any authorized person, and
further, that the person who keeps the
register is to produce for the inspection of
such authorized person not only the register but also invoice notes, receipts, and
other documents, including copies which
may be required to verify any entry or to
explain any omission from such register,
and allow copies or extracts to be taken.
It seems to me to follow from the first
provision that a similar provision is required as regards extracts or copies of
supporting documents. Otherwise it will
be possible for the authorized person who
is prohibited from getting details as to
price under the one to get them under the
other. That I am sure is not the intention. Why it was decided to exclude
particulars as to price I am not quite sure,
but I presume the intention was to avoid
creating any difficulties in connection
with the man's business, and that ought
to be continued to the end of the Subsection. Accordingly, I beg to move.
The MINISTER OF COMMERCE
(Mr. Barbour): The hon. and learned
Senator has, I think, almost explained the
reason for the difference in the two
Clauses to which he has drawn attention.
The object of the Bill generally is to
facilitate the honest trader, and to secure
him from any action of an inquisitorial
character or any undue interference with
his business, and I think it would be
reasonable to expect that such traders who
are carrying on their business and observing the law would not wish to have
people coming to them to inspect their
business books. Consequently, it is prescribed that the register should be kept to
show that the business is being carried on
in a legitimate manner, but should anything appear in it which would cause a
duly authorized person to suspect that'
fish had not been acquired in a proper way
he might wish to investigate the matter
further and, therefore, it might be a
matter of very material interest and
value in conducting any prosecution,
should such arise out of his investigations,
that he should know the price at which
the fish had been acquired. We wish not
only to punish anyone who is infringing.
the law, but we wish to ensure that the
trader who is carrying on his business
honestly and legitimately should be protected from anything in the nature of unfair competition from a less scrupulous
trader. It might happen that a less
honest trader might be selling fish at a
lower price than other traders, and an investigation might ensue which might disclose that the fish so sold had not been
lawfully acquired. The register itself, as
the hon. and learned Member has said, is
not required to contain particulars of this
price, and so we think in that case the
honest trader is protected in the ordinary
course of his business. It is only when
such further investigation is required that
it is deemed desirable that the investigator should have access to such things as
invoice notes from which to take the price
at which the fish was purchased, if he
should consider it necessary. I hope the
hon. and learned Member will accept this
explanation that there is a real reason for
this provision in the Bill, and that he will
not press his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry. It seems
to me that the production of documents
in verification are only required-not
when the trader is dishonest or when the
register is wrong-but when the authorized person for some reason or other thinks
they are wrong, so that if the authorized
person thinks he would like to know the
prices for any reason whatever he may do
so. I am sorry. I do not think the two
things stand together. I cannot withdraw. It seems to me an unreasonable
intervention. If the man thinks there is
something wrong he can examine; otherwise he cannot.
Amendment negatived.
Moved:
"That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Viscount Massereene
and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).

CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of 9 and 10
Geo. 5, c. 57, as to appointment of arbitrators in certain cases).
I
Mr. LESLIE: When the Bill was
before the House on the Second Reading
I suggested that more time should be
given for its consideration. I did not
want to reject the Bill. thought it
might very well have stood
over until
after the Summer recess. The reason I
wanted it to stand over was partly for
further consideration by the Members of
this House and partly for consideration
by people outside. However, there were
two Clauses which seemed to require consideration-the one now before us and
Clause 4, which affects small estates. No
other Member seemed to think that further
time should be given. Therefore, I did
not pursue that formal Amendment to
its logical conclusion. The House before
which I brought that proposal was very
differently constituted from the Committee as it sits this afternoon. Its numbers
were not so great. There were other
attractions. Therefore, I am afraid that
this afternoon I shall be obliged to some
extent to repeat what I said on a former
occasion. I am asking the Committee
this afternoon to leave out the whole of
this first Clause.
The first Clause, as will readily be
seen, introduces a very vital change in the
method of the appointment of the arbitrator who is to decide questions arising under
the Acquisition of Lands Act in so far
as these questions arise between a local
authority or a public authority, but not
in so far as they arise between a Government Department and a private owner.
There is a difference made there. The
result would be, of course, that we should
have two panels of arbitrators, one of
them the presently existing panel which
consists of some six or seven gentlemen
appointed by the Lord Chief Justice and
the President of the Surveyors' Institute
or the gentleman who acts for him. The
new panel consists of one or two people,
as to whose qualifications the Bill does
not give any detail, appointed by the
Ministry of Finance.
Before going on to what I may call the
general question I think I will ask the
House to look for a minute or two to the
actual detail of the wording of the
Clause. We say "any local authority."
The Bill does not define what a local
authority is. Presumably it is some
authority which has power to acquire land
compulsorily. I presume that is the intention, because, as a matter of fact, the
expression "local authority" is not defined
in the Acquisition of Land Act, 1919.
There is a difference between the Bill
before us and the Act of 1919 to the extent that the wording of the 1919 Act
introduces the words "any Government
Department or any local or public authority" and so on. It is important, I think,
to define local authority in every case
where we have occasion to use
the expression, if only to mark the difference
between a rate-making authority and an
authority which is not a rate-making
authority, or to put it quite shortly, for
example, the difference between a county
council and a rural district council. It is
left somewhat doubtful as to what this
local authority is.
It seems to follow from this Clause, the
second Sub-clause, that it is necessary to
inquire here and now whether the
Ministry of Finance has already appointed
someone under this Clause. Has anybody
been appointed in anticipation of the
passing of this Bill? If any such person
has been appointed one would like to know
who he is, on what recommendation he
was appointed, and what qualifications he
has got. If he has not been appointed—
I hope to be told he has not been-the
question arises as to what is to happen between the time this Bill becomes an Act
and the time the man is appointed. This
Bill apparently is to come into immediate operation. There is а
which would be seem to be left.
may be of no importance.
leave that for the moment.
too, that if the Bill goes on the man appointed may fall sick and so on. The
possibility of the failure of the arbitrator
was provided for in the earlier Acts. I
assume it will be carried on. Another
arbitrator can be appointed to finish the
work or to take on the work. I ask the
Committee to contemplate for a minute
gap
It
I will
It may be,
Administrative Provisions Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
what charge that arbitrator specially
selected is going to make, where he is to
come from-whether he is to come off the
old panel or not. The position will be if
it goes through that there will be two
panels, as I have said before.
Now, what is there that is wrong about
that? I hope to show presently there are
several things distinctly wrong. And
what is the present position? The present
position is based on the Act of 1919 which
was brought in deliberately in Great
Britain in order to render procedure under
the Land Clauses Act a little less cumbersome perhaps, and certainly less expensive.
The Bill was very carefully considered
on the other side. The House of Commons occupied some six hours, and such
well-known people as the present Lord
Chief Justice of Great Britain, and Sir
Leslie Scott took a rather leading part
in the debate. Now, that Act of 1919
was referred to in another place by the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Finance, and the right hon. Gentleman
was attacked or questioned-I may say I
do not think there is
necessity
to use the expression attacked," and
I apologize. After all no one attacks the
Minister. They attack the Clause.
""
any
The CHAIRMAN: I do not want to
interrupt the hon. Member, but I hope
he will stick strictly to the Clause and not
make a Second Reading speech.
Mr. LESLIE: I will try. The position
is we want to alter that Clause which
was defended by the right hon. Gentleman on the 16th March, 1926, where he
pointed out that the scale of fees charged
had recently been revised, and that since
1919 a considerable saving had been
effected. The definite object of this Bill
or of this Clause, as pointed out elsewhere,
was apparently that a saving will be
effected. The right hon. Gentleman
spoke of a slight saving. The present
scale of fees apparently is as recent as
November last year, and that scale of fees
was drawn up by the Ministry under the
powers they possess under the earlier Act.
The objection to the change seems to be
that it is proposed to take from a perfectly independent body, a body of only
two, consisting of the Lord Chief Justice
and the President of the Surveyors'
Institute the right of appointing the
arbitrator and to confer it on a Government Department. That seems to be
very objectionable, and one is entitled to
inquire what there is behind it, and by
what authority, so to speak, the sugges
tion was made.
I think the Committee is entitled to be
assured that the Lord Chief Justice has
definitely expressed approval, not merely
that he acquiesced or was tolerant but
definitely approved. The Committee
should also be told that the Surveyors'
Institute has been consulted. As a
matter of fact, I understand that the
Surveyors' Institute has not been consulted. Both in England and in Ireland.
the Surveyors' Institute rather resent not
being consulted. Again, have the county
councils or the Association of County
Councils been consulted? Has any ratemaking authority whatever been consulted? Is any such rate-making authority
behind this Bill? I understand the rural
district councils have approached the
right hon. Gentleman on the matter.
But it is, after all, the county councils
to whom one would look for guidance in
this matter, for, perhaps, it is the county
council that is more likely to appreciate
the importance of having a perfectly
qualified arbitrator-the importance of
knowing that a slight saving in expense
might be entirely out-weighed if the
arbitrator allowed too much.
As a matter of fact, we have not been
told there will be a saving. We are only
told it is hoped there will be. When the
Act of 1919 was originally brought forward the References Committee, as it was
called-the committee to appoint the arbitrator was constituted deliberately of the
Lord Chief Justice, the Master of the
Rolls and the President of the Surveyors'
Institute. I say that was done to secure
absolute impartiality, an impartiality
that would be recognized and respected by
everyone. I suggest that a proposal to
allow an arbitrator of that class to be
appointed by a Government Department
would stand no chance whatever of
acceptance by the British Parliament.
I
say also that it ought to stand no chance
of acceptance by the Parliament of
Northern Ireland. It is obvious that if
you can make a semi-judicial appointment
of that nature from which, speaking
generally, there is no appeal, you are opening a door to every kind of political
jobbery, and, I may say, other jobbery.
and you are doing what lies within you to
establish in Northern Ireland something
after the pattern of Dublin Castle, which
seems to me a very ill-judged suggestion.
Administrative Provisions Bill-
Committee
29 MAY 1928
Mr. BARBOUR: This Bill, generally
speaking, has been brought forth by considerations which have been gradually
acquired through the working of the
different provisions for some time back.
It was considered imperative to get these
conditions improved with the least possible delay. That was the reason for
bringing forward this Measure. I cannot
help thinking that hon. Members will
consider it as being not of a very drastic
character but really designed to do away
with many of the objections which have.
prompted the introduction of the Bill.
As to what has caused the introduction,
I can say that we have received representations of the strongest kind, most numerous representations, dealing with the particular matter of valuation which is the
subject of this Clause 1. The hon.
Senator (Mr. Leslie) has referred to the
fact that the procedure hitherto has been
based to a very large extent on what obtained in England. When modified it
was hoped our new regulations would suit
the conditions obtaining in Northern
Ireland, but, unfortunately, in actual
practice they have proved very far from
satisfactory.
One of the main conditions in England.
is that when the panel of arbitrators is
appointed it becomes a whole-time job
and these assessors have to give up any
private practice. When arrangements
were being made for Northern Ireland it
was considered that the volume of work
would hardly be sufficient to warrant the
panel of assessors giving up their private
practice, and so permission was given to
them to continue their private business.
It has proved that the volume of business
actually coming before them has not been
sufficient in any way to make this
position one of an attractive character,
and it was felt that very much better
service would be obtained if the panel
were done away with, and in lieu
thereof if either one or two gentlemen
were appointed they would be able to
undertake the whole of this class of work.
Undoubtedly it could be done easily as
the cases are not so very numerous.
As to the qualifications of these gentlemen, I think it is made perfectly clear in
the Clause that there would be one or
more person having special knowledge of
the valuation of land in Northern Ireland. In reply to the particular question asked by the hon. Senator, I may
say that the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Finance, did not in any way
prejudge the decision of this Chamber or
the decision elsewhere by taking any
action before the Bill had received the
approval of both Houses of Parliament.
Consequently no appointment has been
made in anticipation of their action on
the matter. So far from there being a
hiatus between the passing of this Bill
and the appointment of an assessor, I
do not think any difficulty or inconvenience is likely to arise in that way,
because these cases are very far from being continuous. They are dealt with as
they arise, and there will be ample time
for the making of an appointment in
order to prevent any lapse or interval in
the carrying out of the work.
It is quite true, as the hon. Senator
has said, that in future there will be two
panels, and I think this Committee will
bear in mind that this is not as sweeping
as possibly it might appear on first reading it over. The only portion of this
assessment that it is proposed to take
away from the existing panel is that dealing with the compulsory acquisition of
land by public bodies. I think the term
"public authority" is so generally used
that it is quite clear to the mind of everybody just what is intended by the expression, and so it is not necessary to incorporate in the Bill a special definition
of the words. Such valuations as compulsory acquisitions of lands for any
Government offices or even for companies,
or even for estate duty appeals, will remain in the hands of the panel as already
constituted.
We have found in the past that the
costs of these arbitrations have been so
excessive that they called forth a veritable
storm of protest from the public bodies
who were concerned in the charges. That
is not only a very objectionable thing,
but I am sure it will be appreciated that
should it have the effect of restricting or
retarding some of the beneficial legislation for the compulsory acquisition of
land it would be very deplorable. We
have had instances in connection with the
acquisition of land for labourers' cottages,
and in future when the right of appeal
comes into operation there will be the
acquisition of land for that purpose. It
would be a pity if a public authority were
to be deterred from making these improvements or if, in a spirit of despair,
375 Administrative Provisions Bill-

THE SENATE
Third Reading.
[Mr. Barbour]
they yielded to an exorbitant demand
rather than submit the matter to arbitration. Those are the real defects which
it is very desirable to overcome, and to
overcome with the least possible delay.
As to the appointment of an assessor for
this purpose the Ministry of Finance
already had the power of the appointment of assessors of land values in the
county courts, which, I think, is almost
as important a function as that which
will be entrusted to them under this Bill.
I hope the Clause will receive the approval
of this Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman did not deal with the question of the
scale of these fees, nor did he make out
any case whatever for urgency in this
matter, nor did he explain whether he
had the concurrence of any of these
bodies to which I referred. He did refer
to the appointment of an assessor to the
county court judge. There I would like
to disagree. To appoint an assessor to a
judge seems to me a very different thing
to appointing a man who is to act as a
judge himself, a judge from whom there
is no appeal. I am afraid the whole
proposal is to set the possibility of
saving against the chance of an unjust and inequitable decision. I am
sorry I cannot withdraw the Amendment.
a
Question put: That this Clause be left
out.

THE SENATE

Carriage of Eggs

Papers Presented
THE SENATE.

The Senate met at a Quarter after
Twelve of the Clock, Mr. J. A. LONG
(Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Commerce:
1. Newry Electricity Special Order,
1928.
2. Report of the Industrial Assurance

the year ended 31st December, 1927.
