Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time.”
Mr. DORMAN: As I was absent on
the Second Reading and Committee
stages of this Bill I should like to make a
few remarks before the Bill finally
passes. On the adjournment of the
House for the Summer Recess I ventured
to draw the attention of the Government
to the importance of the question of the
control of houses, and I desire to express
my gratitude to the Government for extending the period of the control of
houses of a low valuation. At the same
time I would like to draw the attention of
the Government to the fact that the
greatest injury is being inflicted upon the
tenants of houses of a combined business
and residential character. Unless the
Government take some steps to recontrol such houses they will be unwittingly and perhaps unconsciously inflicting serious injury on the working
classes. The class of business to which I
am drawing attention mainly caters for
the working classes in the various districts
of the city. Before control was taken off
they paid rents of from £40 to £90 per
annum. The houses having been decontrolled the landlords are, to my personal
knowledge, increasing the rents by 100
Rent Restrictions Bill
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
per cent. to 200 per cent., and in some
cases by 250 per cent. Most of these
business premises are doing what will be
considered a comparatively small trade
ranging from £600 to £2,000 a year, and
where the rent is being increased by 100
per cent. it will necessarily compel the
merchant to increase the price of the
commodities he sells by from 5 per cent.
to 12 per cent. otherwise he must necessarily reduce his own standard of
comfort.
un-
It was stated in another place that the
Government would not undertake the
control of prices, but if they will look at
the matter from the point of view I have
put before the House they will realise
that by decontrolling the class of house
to which I have referred they are
wittingly increasing prices inasmuch as a
merchant living in such premises has
either to increase the price of his goods or
reduce his own standard of comfort.
Therefore I suggest to the Government
that in the interests of a needy and helpless part of the community whose wages.
have not increased in proportion to the
increase in the cost of living they should
take into consideration the question of
either re-controlling such premises or setting up some form of fair rent court to
fix the rents of such houses.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will convey the suggestion of Senator Dorman to
the Minister of Home Affairs, but I
should like to say that the Government
are
at present considering the whole
question of control and decontrol.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read the Third time') agreed to.

-(Viscount Charlemont.)

Holywood Electricity Special Order.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, "That the Special
Order made by the Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland under the
Electricity (Supply) Acts, 1882 to 1919,
and confirmed by the Ministry of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply) Act,
1919, in respect of the Urban District of
Holywood and parts of the Rural District
of Castlereagh, in the County of Down,
be approved."
This Special Order was granted by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce. It will confer on the Urban
District Council of Holywood statutory
authority to supply electrical energy for
public and private purposes in the urban
district of Holywood and in certain parts
of the adjoining district of Castlereagh in
County Down. The rules of procedure
have been complied with, and I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Game Preservation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): As it name implies, the
object of this Bill is to provide more
Game Preservation Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
effective machinery for the preservafrom
tion of game, and thus to save
gradual destruction what should be, if
adequately looked after, a valuable
national asset. There is unfortunately
little doubt that poaching exists to a
considerable extent in Northern Ireland,
and is carried on not only by individuais
for their personal amusement, but, in
some cases, as a regular source of profit.
As has been pointed out in another place,
it is common knowledge, for example,
that grouse shot towards the end of the
close season are dispatched in quantities
for sale in English markets on the 12th
of August.
As a result of representations which
were made to my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, as to the
damage which was thus being caused to
this valuable national asset, and as to the
insufficiency of the existing powers for
dealing with the matter, the Minister
sought the advice of a representative
Committee, and it is mainly upon the
advice of this Committee that the present
Bill has been drafted. I have heard it
suggested that in bringing forward this
Bill the Government are legislating for
the benefit of a particular class-the
wealthy landlord-and not for the benefit
of the public generally. May I point out
very briefly that game rights are ราว
longer exclusively vested in the large
landlord-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
There are none left.
but,
Viscount CHARLEMONT
owing to the operation of the Land Purchase Acts, are being transferred in an
increasing degree to the purchasing
tenants. Improved
Improved machinery for the
preservation of game will, no doubt,
benefit to a limited extent those who
possess game rights as I have already
pointed out, now a large and increasing
class; it will also benefit the bona fide
sportsman, and, more important than
either of these considerations, it will also
prove of substantial assistance to the
country at large. There seems no reason
why Northern Ireland should not rival
Scotland in its attractiveness from
sportsman's point of view, and with the
improvement which it is hoped this Bill
will secure it will be better worth while
for persons who possess game rights to
employ gamekeepers, etc., and thus in
a
addition to attracting visitors with wellfilled pockets to this country, a certain
amount of additional employment may
also be hoped for.
I should like to make it quite clear that,
in providing more effective machinery for
the enforcement of the game laws, the
Government do not desire in any way to relieve owners of property of their own duty
of watching that property and of safeguarding the game. The police will 100
be used as gamekeepers, and in order that
this might be made absolutely clear, hon.
Members will find that the second paragraph of Clause 1 of the Bill leaves this
beyond doubt. I believe, however, that
the powers which the police are receiving
may have a deterring effect upon
poachers, and that owners of property
who have not heretofore thought it worth
their while to spend money on looking
after that property will now take the matter in hand and employ gamekeepers and
watchers for this purpose.
The Government have come to the conclusion that woodcock and snipe-which
have heretofore been classed as wild birds
and whose protection has been dealt with
by the Wild Birds Protection Actsshould, in future, have definite close
seasons fixed for them and should to all
intents and purposes be game; but, on the
other hand, it seems hardly equitable that
a farmer or other person shooting snipe
alone, perhaps in his own small piece of
bog, should have to take out a £3 game
licence, which under the existing law he
is required to do. Accordingly, in the
present Measure a Clause has been inserted with the result that snipe may in
future be shot on the ordinary 10/- gun
licence, and I am sure that in making
this change in the law the Government
will be supported by every sportsman.
I should like, if I may, to run shortly
through the Clauses, pointing out the intention of each, and in this manner perhaps I may clear up some doubts which
may be in the minds of hon. Members,
because I appreciate that a Bill of this
kind is somewhat difficult to understand,
containing as it does, many references to
previous statutes, unless a short explanation is given as to the general effect
of each Clause.
Clause 1 is perhaps the most important Clause in the Bill. The Constabulary Act of 1836 definitely provided that
取
Game Preservation Bill-
Second Reading
8 NOVEMBER 1928
E

the police should not in any way whatever be used for enforcing the game
rights. It is probable that the reasons
for this Clause were that it was the landlords in Ireland who at that time owned
the game rights and who might be expected to use their influence to obtain the
services of the police for gamekeeping
purposes, and also owing to the fact that
the opportunities for disposing of game,
which had been illegally obtained, were
considerably less than they are at the
present day. While we propose to reinove this limitation we have made it absolutely clear that under no circumstances
will the police be permitted to act as
gamekeepers, or to relieve the owners or
occupiers of land of their responsibility
to look after their own interests.
Regarding Clause 2, a police constable
has to-day no power to demand a game
licence, and he is only empowered to enter
private lands and to ask for the production of a gun licence if he sees a person
carrying a gun. Under this Clause the
police, before they can take any action
in connection with a game licence, must
have reasonable ground to suspect that
the person concerned is, or has been, unlawfully in pursuit of game, and then,
and then only, may they demand the
game certificate. Sub-sections 2 and 3 of
this Clause are really consequential on
this power. If it is to be brought home to
persons shooting game that they must
take out game licences, then it is essential
to give the police the powers enumerated
in these sub-sections.
Clause 3 provides that licensed game
dealers shall keep a register of their purchases, receipts, sales and disposals.
While I do not wish to cast any aspersions on honest game dealers, there is no
doubt in the minds of those responsible
for this Bill that if poachers could not
find persons willing to purchase game,
knowing it to have been poached or taken
during the close season, the type of poaching which is really the cause of the gradual
disappearance of game in Northern Ireland would cease. I do not think that
this Clause will impose any real burden on
the honest game dealer, and I can assure
hon. Members that in enforcing this
Clause the police will act in a discreet and
proper manner.
Coming to Clause 4, as I mentioned in
my opening remarks, woodcock and snipe
are not at the present time game for cer-
This
tain purposes, and one of these purposes
is in connection with dealers.
Clause has the effect of bringing woodcock and snipe under the law for the
purpose of dealers, and the particulars
which I mentioned in connection with
the last Clause will have to be kept
in the register referred to. Under this
Clause also, it is provided that a game
licence at a cost of £3 will not, in future,
be necessary for the purpose of shooting
snipe.
I believe that Clause 5 will meet with
unanimous approval. It is inserted in
the Bill with a view to trying to stop the
practice of marketing poached game, and
it is considered that the provisions in connection with labelling packages, set out
in this Clause, will facilitate the detection of such offences.
With regard to Clause 6, at the present
time the police have only power to search
persons on the public highway or street,
whom they believe to be coming from any
land where they have been unlawfully in
pursuit of game, and it is obvious that
these limited powers of search are inadequate. The effect of this Clause which,
on first sight, looks far-reaching, is really
to give the police power, if they have
reason to suspect that an offence has been
committed, to do certain things and to
enter certain places in the course of thei:
duty, and thus to satisfy themselves
whether or not an offence has, in fact,
been committed. There is, however, no
power to arrest under this Clause but
only to act by way of summons.
Clause also provides for bringing a person
believed to be guilty before the court,
and for obtaining an Order from the
Justice of the Peace for the disposal of
any game found on such person.
This
Clause 7 lays down a close season
for woodcock and snipe. As I mentioned
to hon. Members this matter has up to
the moment been dealt with by the Wild
Birds Protection Act, under which various
counties in Northern Ireland may have
different dates during which these birds
are protected. It is believed that it
would be much more satisfactory to everyone concerned to have a definite date
fixed for the whole of Northern Ireland
of which everyone may be aware. Certain
of the provisions of the Wild Birds Act
will continue to apply. These relate to
the catching of birds with traps, snares,
hooks, etc.
Game Preservation Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
are
Clause 8 lays down the penalties, and
I should point out that the sums
maximum sumis. They may appear
rather high, but it is the intention that
the court shall be able to deal not only
with the poor poacher but with the
wealthy dealer who carries on a substantial game business. The Clause also provides that the court shall be composed of
a resident magistrate. There are a great
many arguments in favour of this
course, and I may say that representations have been received from several
farmers, who are also justices of the peace,
to the effect that they heartily concur with
this proposal. I believe that local farmers that do not wish to be involved
themselves in cases which in all probability affect close neighbours, are only
too glad that the court is to be composed
of a resident magistrate and that they
will be relieved of a very unpleasant
duty.
Clause 9 provides that that part of
this Bill which will deal with the keeping
of registers by dealers, and also with
labelling consignments of game, shall not
come into operation on the passing of the
Act This will give time for the matter
of the registers to be gone into carefully,
and I can assure the House that as far
as possible in this connection we will do
away with what is commonly known as
"red tape."
I trust that the remarks which I have
made have assisted hon. members to
understand and appreciate this Measure
and to satisfy themselves that the question of game is a national one which is
well worth looking after and fostering,
and I feel sure that they will agree that
this Bill is much needed and should be
placed on the Statute Book without delay.
I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish in the first
place to congratulate the right hon.
Gentleman on the very clear exposition
he has given of this Measure. It is right
and proper that the Government should
take every step to preserve all national
assets. The noble Leader of the House
has declared that this Bill is necessary in
order to protect a national asset. For
many years I have been watching the introduction of legislation, not only in the
Parliament of Great Britain and the
Parliaments of Ireland but also in the
Parliaments of many of the Colonies, and
I have been struck with the fact that almost every interest has had legislation
passed for its protection, and that we never
see introduced into any of these Parliaments a distinct and definite Bill for the
preservation of the most valuable of the
national assets of this and every other
country, namely, human life. It is an
extraordinary thing that human life is
allowed to go to waste, and there has
probably been no time in the history of
civilised countries of the world when
human life ran to waste as it is doing today. One might say that in Northern
Ireland and in the Free State there has
never been a period when human life has
been exposed to such unfavourable conditions and environment as it is to-day. It
falls to my lot to move in and out of the
homes of the working classes in Belfast
and I can assure the House that there is
every necessity for legislation in the direction of the preservation of this greater
asset of human life. I find from personal
observation that the working classes are
living in such congested and over-crowded
conditions in the city that there must
necessarily in the future be an enormous
amount of unnecessary sickness and premature death.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think I must remind the hon. Member that he is speaking
on a Bill for the preservation of game and
not on a Bill for the preservation of
human life.
Mr. DORMAN: I understand that,
but I am using that argument as an illustration, although I may be out of order.
I do hope that one thing mentioned by
the noble Viscount will not enter the mind
of the Government of Northern Ireland in
the future. That is the suggestion he
made that we should emulate the example
of Scotland. Every hon. Member who
knows anything about the history of Scotland knows that thousands upon thousands
of what I call the greatest asset of Scotland, namely, human beings, have been
expropriated for the purpose of the preservation of game for sport. (Hon. Members: No, no.) I am not in a position
to quote the exact acreage, but I believe
there have been 200,000 of the populace of
Scotland driven off the land in the interests of the preservation of game and
the safeguarding of game rights in that
country. Many extremely productive
parts of Scotland have been set apart en-
501 Game Preservation Bili-

8 NOVEMBER 1928
Second Reading.

C
Y

.
tirely for the purpose of game preserves
and deer parks while untold suffering has
been imposed on the populace of that
country. Therefore I hope the Northern
Government will not emulate the example
of Scotland, but rather that they will encourage the development of human life
and thus increase the population. I know
it is very often thought we have too many
human beings, but that is not so.
Mr. LONG: On the whole I should
like to support this Bill although there
are some parts of it that I do not like. I
welcome the concession of the 10/- licence
for snipe shooting, but I dislike intensely
the provision that cases under this Bill
should be tried by a resident magistrate.
I am of opinion that the ordinary magistrates are fit and proper persons to deal
with offences of this nature, and I think
it is high time the Government paused
before adding any other insults to the
magistrates. It would be better if the
Government abolished them altogether as
they have done in the Free State.
He
At one period of my life I had a sneaking sympathy for poachers and poaching.
This was due, in a large measure, to the
fact that an old school fellow of mine
developed into a most marvellous shot.
He was also a fine runner, and a pastmaster in the art of disguise. He became
a most noted poacher in Ulster.
poached for adventure and not for what
he could make out of it in a commercial
sense. These escapades fired my youthful imagination and I installed him in
my mind as a sort of hero. After his
early death I learned something of the
operations of other poachers, and they
were quite in a different category. They
were simply out to obtain something to
which they had no legal or moral right.
I think poaching should be put down
with a strong hand because there are some
people who suffer by it.
Quite recently I was invited to estimate
the amount of damage a farmer had
suffered in a twenty acre field of oats.
This field adjoined a closely-preserved
estate, and my valuation as to the damage done to these oats was £85. On the
owner of the estate being approached in
the matter his reply was to the effect
that he regretted he could not control
the activities of the game and he disclaimed liability. I should like the very
competent legal officers of the Ministry
of Home Affairs to inform me
privately at a later stage whether
the ordinary law would deal with
a case of that nature. As a rule
the sportsman is a decent man, and
I understand the case I have quoted is
quite an exceptional one. With the limitations I have mentioned, I beg to support the Second Reading of this Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
entirely in favour of the main objects of
this Bill. I can readily understand that
it is necessary to promote the preservation of game in the country as much as
possible, and I also believe it is necessary
to place in the hands of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary the duty of carrying out the
provisions of this Bill in preference to
the Customs and Excise. There is, however, one Clause that I am very much
afraid of. I refer to Clause 2, Subclause (2), which states that a constable
shall have power to search any person
of whom he may be suspicious and also
the premises on which that person may
be. I do not know exactly whether that
reference to premises includes the house
where that man dwells. If it is the case
that a constable is to be empowered to
enter a man's home no matter how small a
hovel it may be, I do think it is time we
called a halt.
as
We have been continuously introducing
legislation which gives power to either the
police or civil servants to inquire into a
man's business affairs, but when it comes
to giving authority to a constable to
enter a man's home it is a very serious
matter, and it is a very dangerous power
to put into the hands of any ordinary
young constable. These men are subject
to the same whims and fancies
ordinary individuals. I can readily understand that in a country district there
might be some young policeman who
would have a spite against a person who
might be in the habit of going out to
shoot, and under the authority he is given
in this Bill if he saw one of these poor
men returning from a shoot he could make
it an excuse to enter his home and search
it without reasonable
any
cause. If that
is the intention of this Clause I think
something should be done to alter it.
It has always been accepted amongst
the working classes that a poor man in
his own home should be entitled to the
same privacy as the rich man in his castle.
I remember tremendous feeling being
aroused in Belfast a number of years ago
503 Game Preservation Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. J. Cunningham]
on account of the police following a
man into his own home after he had
been found guilty of some small breach
of the law. The police bludgeoned him
and arrested him, and I remember that
the whole of the workers contributed the
necessary funds to have an appeal lodged
in that case, and the appeal was upheld.
In that case the police had overstepped
their duty, and if such a thing would be
possible under this Clause it is a very
serious matter. It entirely sweeps away
the opinion hitherto neld by the working
man about the privacy of his own home.
A
I remember some years ago living in a
district outside Belfast which was a rather
bigoted place and one that held very
strong views on religious matters.
particular person had unfortunately been
compelled to listen to another man cursing
the King, and not being desirous of
running the risk of being fined the usual
40s he went into his own home and cursed
the Pope up the chimney. That person
must have been of the opinion that he
could do what he liked in his own home
and that there he would be free from the
risk of being charged with an offence and
probably fined 40s. I do hope the noble
Viscount will be able to satisfy us on
this point, because if the Clause enables
an ordinary constable to enter a home
and to put the man and his family to the
trouble of searching through the whole
house it is going to be a serious matter.
I do not understand Senator Dorman's
remarks with reference to the want of
legislation for the protection of human
life. We have to recognise-
Mr. SPEAKER: I have already ruled
Senator Dorman out of order on that
point.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I also
listened to his statement about the
Scottish moors. Some time ago I heard
politicians tell us about the way the poor
people were driven from Scotland to make
room for sportsmen shooting grouse and
deer. At that time I was just as
ignorant as I think Senator Dorman is
now on this question, but I began to read
about it and to see the kind of country
on which grouse and other game were preserved. It would be utterly impossible
to clear any human beings off that land
because they had never dwelt on it. It
is a pity that such statements should be
made, because people who have passed
through that part of Scotland know that
is is impossible for human beings to subsist on that kind of land. I support the
Bill, but I hope the noble Viscount will
be able to reassure me on the question of
the powers of policemen to search a person's home.
Colonel PAKENHAM: After the clear
explanation the noble Viscount has given
of this Bill I do not think the House will
have much hesitation in approving of it.
I would like to refer to a remark he made
that it had been stated in certain quarters
that this Bill was purely for the benefit of
the landlords. We all know that that
is an absolute fabrication of the truth.
There are very rew landlords left, and
those who are left are disappearing very
rapidly. The landlord has always preserved his own game. He has employed
gamekeepers, and he has been very strict
in the preservation of his game, rightly
or wrongly.
The cause of this Bill being introduced
is the fact that owing to the land salcs
that have taken place in Ulster during the
last twenty years or more a great deal
of the land which used to be preserved by
the landlords is now being preserved by
nobody, and the consequence is that the
poacher has found a great opportunit
for his talents over lands which the
farmers do not care to preserve, or are
too busy to preserve, or do not want to
preserve. The Government have been
appealed to by many people who believe
that the preservation of game is of some
advantage to the country, and the
Government have only done what other
Governments have done, namely, put into
action a law to support laws that have
already been passed for the protection of
game. Whatever blame you attach to
the old landlord in this respect you inust
attach this virtue to him that this Bill
would never have seen the light of day if
there had not been landlords to preserve
game in the past.
I should like to make a few remarks
regarding Clause 7. Snipe and woodcock
have been taken out of the Wild Birds
Protection Act and included in this Bill.
There is another bird I should have liked
to have seen included, and that is the
sporty and succulent bird called the wild
duck, which is now slaughtered by socalled sportsmen from the beginning to
the end of the year. I have seen wild
505 Game Preservation Bill-

8 NOVEMBER 1928
Second Reading.
duck shot on the 1st July.
It is a very
prevalent habit in some parts of County
Antrim to kill wild duck round about that
date. Anybody who knows anything
about wild duck knows that at that time
of the year it is barely capable of getting
on its legs, and yet it is slaughtered by a
large number of people for no other reason
than the pure delight of destruction, because at that period the bird is useless for
eating purposes.
Therefore, I should have been delighted
to have seen wild duck included in Clause
7. I live on the banks of Lough Neagh.
There were times in years past when that
lough was covered with wild duck which
provided good food for the people without
much trouble at the
time.
proper
Of late
years they have decreased in an extraordinary way. Where in the old days
you saw a couple of hundred you now
scarcely see twenty or thirty. There is no
doubt they are being exterminated all over
the country.
As regards the powers of the police, I
take it that this is the sort of thing that
will happen. The ordinary man has to
take out his game licence through the
police, and the police will be informed as
to those people in the district who have
game licences. Therefore, the police will
know the people in their own particular
district who have these licences, and if
a sergeant or a constable sees a man shooting he will know at once whether he has a
licence or not. If he knows the man has
a licence he will not bother him. He will
only go after what I call the motor bandit
poacher who is one of the worst types of
poacher we have got. He is not the
ordinary poor man who lives in the district
who goes out for a chance shot, but a man
who can well afford a motor car and does
not care where he goes.
He comes from the cities and the towns
and goes about the country shooting what
he can see. He gets on a farmer's land,
shoots anything on that land, and then
hops into his motor car and goes off. He
is the gentleman who is doing so much
damage and he is the gentleman we want
to get at. I hope this Bill will enable
the police to catch him. I should like to
support the noble Viscount's plea that
this Bill should go through this House
with as few Amendments as possible.
has been very carefully considered in
another place and has been amended in a
It
way that is satisfactory to nine-tenths of
the people who have asked for it. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading, and I congratulate the Government on the fact that they have at last
taken steps to do something to preserve a
very valuable national asset.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I should
like to support this Bill in its entirety,
and I hope the noble Leader of the House
will carry it through without any amendment. It is a peculiar thing to say, but
we must not forget that we are helping
these idlers, these ne'er-do-well fellows
otherwise called poachers, who do not own
an acre of land, to shoot game. Facts are
very hard to get over and are very stubborn, and it is a fact that wherever property has been sold by anyone who kept
game, in two years' time there was not
even a blackbird upon it. Therefore, if
this Bill is not passed the poachers will die
out because they will have nothing to do.
I have heard several hon. Senators make
slight objections to certain things in the
Bill, and I am sorry to say I think they
are only quibbles. They certainly have
not got any great weight and their objections can all be answered, as I am sure
they will be. The important thing is to
pass this Bill as it has been introduced into
this House, and I hope that the only
magistrates who will deal with the cases
under the Bill will be the resident magistrates.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should like to support the Second Reading of this Measure,
as I have been brought up in the country
and know something about this question.
I have had experience of sportsmen who
come from the cities and towns in motor
cars to shoot game in the country. They
come in season and out of season. I have
seen them coming at a season of the
year
when they should not have come and
killing a certain kind of
game which
should not have been killed at that time.
I am very glad to see that there is protection given to snipe and woodcock, and
that in future there will be a close season
for these birds. I remember some time
ago some of these sportsmen going
through a boggy kind of land in the summer season and shooting snipe that were
sitting, killing the birds and smashing
the eggs.
It is not one isolated case, as I
have heard of similar cases.
I welcome the Bill, and I hope it will
not be amended in any way. I do not
Game Preservation Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
Some
agree with the remarks made by Senator
Joseph Cunningham as to the powers of
the police to search a man's house.
of those people who live in hovels can
do a great deal of damage, and a policeman should have the same authority to
enter a hovel as he has to enter the premises of a business man to see if he has a
licence to sell game.
I cannot agree with Senator Long, who
would like cases brought under the Bill
to be tried by the ordinary magistrates.
From what I have heard in this House on
several occasions when other Bills have
been before us, I do not think it would be
advisable to alter the present form of the
Clause. When the Fisheries Bill was
before this House we were given to understand that ordinary magistrates could not
be trusted to deal with cases of poaching
under that Bill, and we were even told
that magistrates themselves went poaching at night.
Then when the Marketing of Potatoes
Bill was before the House we were again
told that magistrates could not be trusted
to deal with cases brought under that
Bill. A certain hon. Senator said that
he bought a bag of seed potatoes from a
magistrate. At the top of the bag the
potatoes were good but three parts f
them were rubbish. If the ordinary
magistrate cannot be trusted to deal with
cases under those two Acts they cannot
be trusted to deal with offences under
this Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Like most of the other
speakers I desire to support the Bill, but
I desire to go further and to thank the
Government for introducing it. I do
that, if I may say so, as one who has endeavoured in his small way to preserve
game for the last twenty-five years. During that time I have not found that any
harm has been done to my neighbours by
game preserving. On the contrary, my experience leads me to feel that this is a
Bill which can do nothing but good, for
after all game preservation must, as the
noble Viscount intimated, provide a certain amount of employment. It does
mean the production of a certain quantity of food which can be used for the
preservation of human life, amongst
other things, and game preservation does
attract people to live in the country, and
it does promote the social amenities of the
district. It promotes good-will between
a landlord, or what is left of him, and
his tenant farmers on whose good-will
alone depends the preservation of any
which is outside the actual congame
fines of a man's demesne.
I could wish we had seen the Report
of the Committee to which the noble Viscount referred. I think it might very
well have been laid before us one way or
the other, but I do not know that that
is of great importance. There is nothing
that I can see unduly drastic in the Bill
before us. It does nothing special as regards actual poaching, it does not increase the penalties for poaching; there
is nothing about that, but it does make
the way of the transgressor harder because it will make it harder for him to
dispose of his ill-gotten gains, and it will
make it difficult for anybody to act as a
dealer in game unlawfully acquired.
do not think the Bill contains anything
that can possibly do any harm to any
farmer, and as regards the £85 worth of
of damage mentioned by Senator Long, I
could wish that we had been told
whether the damage was attributed to
birds or to beasts, by which beasts I meant
originally hares and rabbits which the farmer has power to kill, but my hon. Friend
on my left reminds me that it may very
possibly have been due to rats.
As other hon. Senators' experiences have
been given I will detain the House for a
minute or two by relating my own experience. I was rearing pheasants one year,
and a three-acre field had been set for
oats. The weather was wet, the corn was
beaten down, and for some reason or other
a very large proportion of the pheasants
decided to go to that field day after day.
We could not keep them out. At the end
of the year I asked the farmer what he estimated the damage at to his three-acre
crop of oats. He said that if I gave him
a pound I should be doing all that was
I do not think my hon. Friend
on my left (Mr. Joseph Cunningham)
need be very frightened as regard this
power of search. My hon. and gallant
Friend (Colonel Pakenham) has just given
one indication of how it might work as
regards motor bandit poachers.
necessary.
I was thinking about the more common
cases. A man is seen apparently poaching
or trespassing, and chase is given. Any
search that might otherwise be necessary
Game Preservation Bill-
Second Reading.
8 NOVEMBER 1928
can be avoided at once by the man producing a game certificate or by admitting
that he has been unlawfully shooting
game. It may be that the man will run
away. Poachers very often do so and it
might be necessary to search the premises
immediately in the neighbourhood, such
as looking under the furze, and you might
have to chase him into a neighbouring
cottage. The whole thing has to be construed as being a case of whether a man
"is or has been." It is all part of one
transaction. You cannot go in to a man's
house on Monday next on the assumption
that he was pursuing game on Thursday
of the week before. That should disarm
criticism on that point.
I believe myself that this Bill is a necessary precursor of the consolidation of our
game laws. They need consolidation almost as much as any branch of our law.
They are very complicated because they
depend largely on legislation of the old
Irish Parliament, largely on legislation of
the Parliament of the United Kingdom of
Great Britain and Ireland, and so on, and,
of course, on the decisions of judges in
both countries. But the proposals contained in this Bill have undoubtedly
caused a very large amount of misunderstanding. I am referring to
the great amount of misrepresentation
both in the correspondence published
by the newspapers and in private conversation. I do not think one
entitled to suggest that anybody deliberately desired to misrepresent the Bill,
but the fact remains that it has been done
again and again. I think the cause of
that must be partly the interpretation of
our game laws--they are very intricate
and are almost contradictory in placesand partly the age of them. The
earliest Statute I can think of is one of
the time of Richard II, and I believe it is
still as much in force in Ireland as it ever
was, if it was ever in force at all. In
more recent times we still act on the
Statute of the time of William III, and
our principal Game Act in this country
is the one of the time of George III.
is
Then there is the fear based on the old
harshness of the game laws which dates
from the New Forest operations after
the Norman Conquest, and there has been
the fear that the ingenuity of lawyers has
succeeded in grafting a bastard slip on
the old tree of common law in relation
to property and the game laws which were
supposed to be themselves contrary to
nature which draws no distinction between
the partridge and the butterfly and the
hare and the squirrel. I suppose that is
partly the cause, but then there is the
fact that there is no general definition of
game. Acts of Parliament always have
to define game for the purpose of a particular Act. We have to do it ourselves
in the Bill before us. It is due to that
that it has not been generally appreciated
that really you have to deal with three
classes of game.
First of all there is the
game you require a certificate to kill;
secondly, the game you require a licence
to deal in; and there is a third which
includes the troublesome little animal
which is always six inches too short,
namely, the rabbit, which can best be
defined as a thing we can shoot with a
ten shilling licence but which we must
not trespass in pursuit of. It is also
forgotten very often that there is no
difference in the eye of the common law
between trespassing on land that is preserved and on land that is not preserved. I think that one of my hon.
Friends on my left had that in mind when
he spoke of the different causes for the
shortage of game at present.
It has been entirely overlooked that the
Bill before us is the logical outcome and
consequence of Measures introduced at no
very distant date either in the Parliament of the United Kingdom or in our
Own Parliament. It has not been
appreciated that the requirement that a
policeman may see a game certificate is
due to a provision in our recent
Finance Act under which we transfer
the issue of these licences from the
Excise to the police. I do not think
it has been generally appreciated-although the noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House, did lay stress on it-that the
Poaching Prevention Act of 1862 failed to
prove efficient, and is now going to be extended not only to the man who actually
shoots game, but also to the man who conveys it away. No special provision is
made as to the penalty for this man.
As we all like this Bill so much, as I
think we do, I think we have to remember that we are sitting here as trustees,
not for our private preferences, not only
in the interests of game preservation, but
for the public of Northern Ireland as
well, and therefore, if I may say so, I
think this is a case in which we should
be meticulously careful as to what we do.
We must be careful not to go too far, but
•
Game Preservation Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
we must not hesitate to go far enough.
Hence I think it happens that we must
inquire when we get to the Committee
stage and I am not going to dwell at
any length on the points I am about to
mention-whether we are not putting too
heavy a burden on the honest retailer.
It is desirable to sell game acquired
honestly, and it is not perhaps possible
to record every item of a day's transactions. We must be careful to consider
the new position which snipe are going
to take. Hitherto snipe have been included in what I call Class 1.
.
You required a £3 game certificate to shoot
snipe, but you did not require any licence
to deal in snipe. As the Bill stands at
present you will cease to require a £3
licence to shoot snipe, but you will require a £3 licence to deal in snipe. I
do not know if that is the real intention.
but I will direct attention to it on the
Committee stage.
We must also consider whether we are
sufficiently protecting snipe, but with that
point I will not deal now. There is the
difficulty of protecting one class of wild.
fowl after 15th February if you do not protect others. We have also to give a word
or two by way of discussion-if we go no
further to the possibility of re-establishing the partridge. Everybody regrets
very much that the partridge is no longer
obtainable in Nothern Ireland to the extent it used to be in the days of our
fathers and grandfathers.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: And quail.
en-
Mr. LESLIE: Quail are not touched
in the Bill. I understand that
in Scotland capercailzie became
tirely extinct and were reintroduced.
It would seem practicable to do something for what partridges are left
by extending the close season for them.
When this Bill becomes law there will be
more encouragement in the future than
there has been in the past to import
foreign birds of one sort and another and
try if we can rear them. They have
been tried in the past, but as a rule not
very successfully. The partridge is a
sufficiently sporting bird to be worth our
while to deal with it.
We have to think a little of the administration of our Bill. We have to
consider whether our penalties are duly
proportioned, and whether it will not be
necessary for us to apply the Firearms
Act-certainly not to amend the Act, because that would be wholly inappropriate,
but we might discuss whether anybody
offending with a gun should not be treated
in the way a naughty child was treated.
You take away the toy whip from the
naughty child who beats his little sister
with it. The question of the Court to
deal with these matters has already been
referred to. I put up a case against resident magistrates under the Fisheries
Act, and I am not going to do it again.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I had better do
it again, but I had not intended to. I
cannot leave the subject without saying
that I have myself been a magistrate for
a quarter of a century. I have sat on
many game cases. I have occasionally
reluctantly been obliged to bring game
prosecutions, and I have never yet known
a case in my own part of the country
where magistrates took an unreasonable
view of evidence or where in the case
of conviction they did not inflict a penalty
reasonably sufficient to prevent a
currence of the offence. I am not at all
sure that it is in the interests of game
preservation that this change as regards
magistrates should be made.
re-
Finally, the matter of administration
is one in which possibly it will be necessary
to make a change in the Bill. I refer to
the date of commencement. This Bill
was originally brought in elsewhere on 9th
May, and if it had been dealt with before
the adjournment for the Summer, before
the game season began, there would have
been no difficulty in bringing it into immediate operation. I doubt if it will be
possible for it to become law before the
end of the month, and it would seem
extraordinary to bring it into operation
earlier than next July, just before the
beginning of the game season.

are matters that can be fully discussed
when we come to deal with the Bill in

be of service to the Bill, perhaps not in
the letter but certainly in the spirit of
the administration when it rises phoenixlike as an Act. We should
cuss it thoroughly before

events of the public may appreciate
the fact that laws are not really imposed

upon them without very considerable consideration. I believe discussion later on
will be of service to the Bill. I believe
the Bill is a good Bill and one which
should receive more approval outside than
it appears to have received already.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
rise to support this Bill, but there is one
point I would like the noble Leader of
the House to clear up. In Clause 7 you
refer to the destruction of young woodcock and snipe, and you also refer to the
wilful destruction of the eggs of the woodcock but do not mention the eggs of the
snipe. With reference to the remarks
made by Senator Dorman as to the
preservation of game in Scotland, as one
who has read something of the history of
Scotland I understand that very few of
the population were removed for the
purpose of preserving game.
If you
read the history of Scotland you will find
that throughout the Highland and Lowland districts the population decreased
when sheep farming increased, and not
when game increased. I would like this
to be understood because I do not think
it is fair that it should go out to the
nublic that a large portion of the population of Scotland was driven off the land
for the purpose of preserving game for
amusement.
There is another point in these game
laws that we ought to consider. Reference has been made to the destruction
caused by game. I have taken an interest in this matter, and I have found
that throughout England thev estimate
that at least 10 per cent. of the corn crop
is destroyed by vermin and not by game.
If we preserve our game, and if the
smaller farmer finds it pays him to preserve game he will see that there are
fewer rats about his place as rats are the
curse of the agriculturist. The saving
that will be entailed by keeping down the
rat population would far more than make
up for the cost of the little corn that
may be picked up by pheasants. Therefore, I hope, not for the sake of sport but
for the sake of the poor man who has a
few acres of land, that this Bill will pass.
I think it will be one of the greatest blessings that can be conferred upon our
country, and it will give our country an
asset which, I am sorry to say, it does not
possess at present. I heartily support the
Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am one of those who support the Bill in
its entirety. The only fault I have to
find with it is that it does not go far
enough. I much prefer the Bill that was
introduced in the Free. State under which
game that was nearly exterminated is not
allowed to be killed for two years. I
want to refer particularly to the partridge. I remember as a boy my father
telling me about the quantity of partridge there were in County Down, which
is an ideal county for the breeding of
partridges. Although the landlords preserved their own game the farmers did
not know the value of partridges,
and not only were partridges unmercifully
slaughtered, but their eggs were taken
and eaten during the breeding season.
I had a most unfortunate experience
when I first began game preservation. I
thought I would try to breed partridges.
I got a large number of eggs from England and had a most successful issue from
them. Unfortunately patridges are birds
that will not stay near where they are
bred. I bred from 80 to 100 patridges
which came to maturity, but they then
went away and within a week of those
birds leaving there was not a single one
left alive. We ought to try to preserve
our partridges as they are the most valuable birds we have in this part of the
world.
Some people think the police are going
to be used as gamekeepers under this Bill.
That is not a fact. If this Bill becomes
law the police will not become gamekeepers, but if while on their ordinary
beats they come across something which
is against the law they will have power
to act. We all know there is a great deal
of poultry-rearing in this part of the
country, and in the past poultry dealers
have visited the poultry farms and bought
game that has been poached and they
have slipped it underneath the turkeys
or hens in the carts and taken it away.
It was well known who those men were
but they were never stopped.
As regards the question of the eggs of
snipe not being mentioned in this Bill,
I am told by Senator Leslie who knows
all about these laws that they are already
preserved under some Act of George III.
I am not going to detain the House with
other remarks which I intended to make,
but will defer them until the Committee
stage. The Bill was threatened with a
I
Game Preservation Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Col. Sharman Crawford]
great deal of opposition in another place
but its great value was always realized
and there was no serious amendment made
to it. If the Bill was seriously altered
in this House it would destroy its value.
I am glad to see that Senator Leslie has
been converted to my views about magistrates.
Mr. LESLIE: Not at all.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: In
my humble opinion if the ordinary magistrates dealt with cases under this Bill it
would not be worth the paper it is
written on, and if there is any attempt
made to amend the Bill in this respect
it will wreck it. I am going to support
the Bill in its entirety, and if there is any
useless or unnecessary Amendment
brought in I will oppose it.
Viscount BANGOR: I desire to support the Bill in its entirety. I am of
opinion that the owners of game would
not wish that the police should act as
gamekeepers. I think it is up to the
owners to look after their own game, but
I also think that the owners of game will
welcome the support they will get from
the police. Senator Joseph Cunningham
seemed to resent the police having power
to visit people in their houses if they
thought they might have been unlawfully
pursuing game. I would like to point
out that people who keep the law have
nothing to fear, and if a man can produce his game certificate or gun licence
when he is asked for it there is nothing
more done. The police will be in a position to know who has taken out a game
or gun licence, because in future licences
will have to be taken out through the
police. I do not think there is anything
unfair in the police going to a man's
house if they suspect that he has been in
pursuit of game, and that he has not got
a game licence.
With regard to woodcock and snipe, I
think the concession to allow a person to
shoot snipe with a 10/- licence is a good
one. I do not think the farmers shoot
snipe very much. I was arranging with
some of my former tenants about shooting
rights, and they said they would be very
glad to see me continue shooting. One man
said, "I suppose you will let me have an
occasional shot," I did not reply immediately and he said, "I see you are not
certain.
I never had a gun in my hand
in my life." I would have liked to have
seen wild duck included in this Bill, because a great quantity of them are shot
before the 1st August, and I would also
have liked to have seen teal included.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
prefer to reply to the majority of the
points raised at a later stage, but perhaps
I might be allowed to make a few remarks
in reference to some of the matters that
have been mentioned, especially where
one hon. Senator has not replied to another. I should like to give Senator
Dorman an assurance that no expropriation of people on a large scale, or even on
a small scale, is intended. I am not as
good at remembering dates as the hon.
and learned Senator (Senator Leslie)
opposite, but, I believe, the right of preserving game was originally given somewhere between 1066 and 1187, and the
man who was chiefly instrumental in
giving that right was afterwards shot by
an arrow in the preserved region he had
introduced. That would be a sad thing
to happen to any Member of the Government. With regard to the substitution of
resident magistrates for ordinary magistrates, it must be remembered that a
great many magistrates are in favour of
this substitution, and they say it would
be an invidious thing for them to have to
sit in judgment on friends of their own,
who had inadvertently been out with a
gun. No doubt we shall hear more about
this when the Clause itself comes before
the Committee.
With regard to Senator Joseph Cunningham's query about the police having
power to enter an innocent man's house
and search his floors and cupboards, I
think there is really no danger of this
happening. The Clause only gives power
to the police to visit a man's house if the
policeman has a reasonable belief that
the man has been unlawfully in pursuit
of game, or if he has failed to produce
his game certificate. I do not think it is
possible for a policeman to act in this way
out of spite.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
have known of some strange cases in
Belfast.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have
always lived in the country and, while the
police are subject to the same whims and
517 Queen's University Bill- 8 NOVEMBER 1928
Committee.

C
or 52
ht f

and
GOFF
it m
er
es b

A
Moved, "That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill,"—
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
fancies as an ordinary individual, I think
it is very unlikely that a policeman would
force his way into an innocent man's
house if there was no reason to assume
that he had been out with a gun.
policeman is not like an ordinary member
of the public. If I forced my way into
a man's house and if he did not kick me
out it would probably be a private matter
between himself and me, but the policeman who did the same thing would have
to give an account of his action. Therefore, I think it is most unlikely that such
a thing as Senator Cunningham fears
would ever happen.
Coming to the question of the protection
of wild duck, they are already protected
under the Wild Birds (Protection) Act
up to the 1st August. The real difficulty
is that the law is not sufficiently enforced.
I live on the opposite side of Lough Neagh
to Senator Colonel Pakenham, and I
know that any amount of immature wild
duck are killed long before they can fly.
It is a question of the enforcement of the
existing law and not that the law itself
is not sufficient to deal with the matter.
Then there was a question raised as to
the eggs of the snipe. They are already
safeguarded by statute, and it is not
necessary to duplicate the law in that
respect. Woodcocks' eggs, I understand,
have never been protected, but I think the
breeding of woodcock in this country is a
very small matter.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It
is growing tremendously.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Possibly if
they grow to any large numbers a Bill
may be brought in later to deal with
them. With reference to the question
raised by Senator Leslie that the report
of the Committee, to which I referred in
my speech, should have been laid on the
Table, it was a representative Committee
but more or less informal. They made
suggestions but did not issue a printed
report.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Queen's University of Belfast Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Additional grant for
general purposes of Queen's University.)
With your
COLONEL SINCLAIR :
permission I should like to allude to a
speech made by Senator Barclay on the
last occasion on which we met. I hope to
be able to disabuse his mind and the
minds of those for whom he spoke. I
want to say that no such unworthy motive
as he referred to has influenced the University, of which I have for many years
been a member. The University has no
desire to offer any obstacle to the entry
of worthy students coming from the working classes or from any class in the community. On the contrary, I think the
very best material we get is derived from
the source which the hon. Member referred to. Some changes have necessarily
been made owing to the undue financial
stringency under which the University
has been labouring for some years and
which has inspired us to ask for furthur
Government assistance. That has perhaps
been misinterpreted by the hon. Senator
and those who have not made themselves
fully aware of the circumstances.
The University last year spent £1,925
on scholarships. If we were in Great
Britain we would have spent nothing on
scholarships, because scholarships to universites in Great Britain, apart from private endowments, are entirely provided
by the local authorities. Therefore, it is
rather unfair that the Queen's University
has to provide scholarships out of its own
slender resources. Possibly the hon.
Senator referred to the reduction in the
number of scholarships. Let me explain
how that arose. A few years ago we
made a rearrangement to prevent a single
individual from holding several scholarships. We were also influenced by the
fact that various county councils throughout the Province have provided valuable
scholarships, and a large number of,
perhaps, struggling students, who were
worthy to enter the University and who
succeeded in obtaining these valuable
scholarships, are in that way enabled to
519 Queen's University Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Colonel Sinclair]
come to the University. The reduction
of scholarships from 12 to 8 at first view
looks as if some facilities had been withdrawn, but I think Senator Barclay will
see that it is entirely a matter of a rearrangement that has been forced upon
the University by financial stringency,
and that the reduction in the number of
scholarships is to some extent compensated
for by the grant of scholarships from the
county councils, so that there is really no
hardship involved.
Further, I should like the hon. Senator
to know that we have at Queen's
University a special fund under which a
grant can be made to scholars in case of
need. The late Vice-Chancellor founded
the students' fund and a number of us
subscribe to it. This fund is available
for any struggling student who may find
himself in difficulty at the beginning of
his university career, or for any student
who after entering the university under
favourable auspices may through family
troubles find himself unable to proceed
with his education there. An embarrassed
student of that kind has only to apply to
the Vice-Chancellor and the Secretary of
the University, and his case is inquired
into with the utmost courtesy, and an
amount sufficient to meet his fees or provide a supply of books, or any other form
of assistance that is desirable, is granted.
Thus, he may still continue at the
University and complete his course.
I should like to impress upon the
Senate the difference there is between the
financial position of Queen's University
and the universities across the water. We
have a rapidly growing University. Demands for further building, chairs and
lectureships, laboratories and equipment
are always coming before us, and whereas
in Great Britain the universities are
generously assisted by a special fund
known as the University Grants Commission, we do not enjoy any such assistance.
In managing the University we have to
have due regard to economy, and I am
sure that after the explanation I have
given as to how the apparent shrinkage
arose Senator Barclay will probably
realise that the deficiency has, to a great
extent, been made up by the action of
the county and city councils in granting
scholarships. Across the water in the
case of Parliamentary grants to universities or grants given from the University
Grant Commission no conditions are attached that they shall be used to provide
scholarships. It is entirely left to the
university to determine in what way these
grants can best be spent for the advancement of learning and the assistance of
students.
After this explanation I hope Members
of the Senate will clear their mind of any
idea that Queen's University policy has
been. guided by so unworthy a motive, as
Senator Barclay suggested, of preventing
the best class of student entering the
University from public elementary schools
and secondary schools through the facilities provided by the new Education Act
so ably administered by the noble Leader
of the House. This Act is designed to
provide facilities enabling students from
public elementary schools to gain the
highest honours that the University can
possibly afford, and I should not like
it to go out to the general public
that such discouragements as were
referred to by Senator Barclay existed.
On the contrary, the hon. Member
may go so far as to explain to any who
may have this idea that we welcome
these students who come from the
public elementary schools. I am sure
Senator Armstrong, who is a member of
the Senate of the University, will bear
me out in my statement that never has
any such narrow and disgraceful motive
actuated the Senate of the University.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am connected with the Down County
Council which was the first county council
to offer scholarships. We find that in
many instances when we have sent in the
names of students for scholarships they
were not able to pass the entrance examination. Whether that is due to their
elementary education or to the nature of
the entrance examination I do not know,
but there must be some reason for it. I
think it would be a good thing if the
Minister of Education and the representatives of Queen's University could find
some solution of this problem because it
is the cause of a great deal of discontent.
I have known children, who were recommended for scholarships and who were
apparently well qualified to enter the
University, unable to pass the entrance
examination.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: It gives me great
pleasure to say that having been over
seven years a member of the Senate of
Queen's University, I have on no occasion
noticed a suspicion of such a policy as
521 Queen's University Bill—

8 NOVEMBER 1928
Committee.

My B
Senator Barclay referred to. The authorities of the University bestow the greatest
care on the selection of those entitled to
scholarships. It is an essential qualification that entrance to the University
should be maintained at a high stanard,
or otherwise the University would become
a second-class university, and would lose
the high position it at present occupies.
With regard to the entrance examination
the standard must be maintained, and I
have no doubt that disappointments do
Occur.
However, it is not necessary that
everybody should have a university education. Many people have succeeded in
life without it, and many who have had
that advantage have failed. I do not
think that anything could excel the way in
which the University is managed at present, and it reflects the highest credit on
the authorities of the University.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should like to
thank the hon. and gallant Member (Col.
Sinclair) for the explanation he has given.
It is something like what I expected, and
I have not the least doubt that it will
allay a great deal of the suspicion that is
in the minds of a small section of the
community regarding Queen's University.
From the explanation which the hon.
and gallant Member has given it is apparent that the whole misunderstanding
has arisen owing to the reduction in the
number of scholarships from twelve to
eight. The way it was put to me was that
in 1926 Queen's University gave twelve
entrance scholarships and in 1928 the
number was reduced to eight. I do not
want this House to think that I am
hostile to the Queen's University.
fact of the matter is that I got into this
trouble by defending the University.
The
I was speaking in a park to a number
of men on the great advantages the children of to-day have as compared with the
time I was a boy, and I was pointing out
that it was possible to-day for a working
man's child to enter the Queen's University through the entrance scholarship and
become a classical scholar. This other
matter was cast up and as I had no information on the subject I had to keep quiet.
That was one of the reasons I raised the
question in the House and the explanation
which has been given has been satisfactory.
When the reduction in the number of
scholarships was put up to me I said it is
not in any way to try to obstruct the
poor man's child from entering. If there
was such a thing as a reduction it would
appear to me to be from a financial standpoint, and I am satisfied from the explanation given to-day that it is from
a financial standpoint.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Expenses of Agricultural
Faculty in teaching and research.)
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to know
whether the noble Viscount is in a position to give me an answer to the point I
raised on the Second Reading as to
whether there is anything of a restrictive
character in the use of the word "solely"
in this Clause.
I would
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to give the hon. Senator an assurance
that the word "solely" has no sinister
meaning. The object of the Government
in introducing the Bill is to make things
easier for the University. The University certainly will not be hampered in any
way by this Clause as it now stands. If
there is any furthur information necessary
as to the actual meaning of the word I
will be happy to give it later on.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that answer is
quite satisfactory.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Power of governing body
to borrow money) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.

without Amendment to the House.

resume.

received." (Viscount Charlemont.)

UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE
BENEFIT.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
McDowell:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the attention of the Government has been
drawn to the fact that a man who voluntarily gives up his job is now automatically
debarred from receiving unemployment
benefit; and whether all such cases should
not be considered on their merits.
Mr. McDOWELL: In asking the question that stands in my name I will be as
brief as I possibly can. While the question is a simple one there is underlying
it a considerable amount of misery, starvation, and, shall I say, the breaking up
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McDowell]
of homes. The matter dealt with has been
vividly brought to my notice during the
past fortnight, and I thought the umpire
had come to a most drastic and unreasonable finding in deciding that when once
a man or woman left his or her employment voluntarily they should be automatically debarred from receiving unemployment benefit. One particular case that
was brought to my notice was that of a
young lady who left her work on a Saturday to get married on the following
Monday or Tuesday. She had every intention of returning to her work the following week. Unfortunately this young
lady's mother was taken seriously ill and
she was ill for a considerable period, and
consequently her daughter could not go
back to her work. When she did back
go
there was no work for her, because there
is such depression in the linen trade that
when a worker leaves her work it is almost impossible for her to get her job
back again.
This particular case came before the
Court of Referees on different occasions.
Until recently the law was that if a person had committed a breach of the regulations her benefit was stopped for six
weeks and she then had the right to make
a further claim, and if she satisfied the
condition to be genuinely seeking work her
benefit was restored. In this case the
Court of Referees granted this young
woman benefit. However, about a week
afterwards, to my consternation, the case
was sent back to the Court of Referees
with an instruction to reconsider the
claim in the light of a decision given by
the umpire, namely, that once a worker
voluntarily leaves her work she is automatically disqualified from receiving benefit.
I consider that that is a most drastic decision and I do not think it is a decision
within the ambit of the Unemployment
Insurance Acts. It is certainly contrary
to the spirit of British law which lays it
down that after a person has served a
number of weeks disqualification from un.
employment benefit he has a right to
renew his application for benefit. But
according to this latest decision of the umpire in Northern Ireland a person has to
suffer disqualification until he is again.
able to get employment.
There are a variety of circumstances
under which a man might leave his employment voluntarily and thus be denied
unemployment benefit, irrespective of the
explanation he might be able to offer to
the Court of Referees. Take the case of
a man who has been continuously worrying the manager of a particular firm for
a job. The manager goes to the foreman
and tells him to start this man. Because
he thinks his position has been belittled
the foreman is continuously on this man's
back, and ultimately the position becomes
so unbearable that the man puts on his
coat and walks out. Under such circumstances this poor man would be debarred
from
receiving unemployment benefit
while he remained out of work, even if it
was for two years. Another case comes
to my mind, and it shows the contrast between an employer and the foreman alluded to in the previous case. The man
is working at his machine when the employer himself comes along and there are
words between the two of them. The employer turns round to the man and says,
"You are a liar.' The man, on the spur
of the moment, lifts his hand and hits his
employer. Of course, you will say that
that man deserves anything. He lifts his
coat and walks out of the works. If he
had applied for unemployment benefit
under those circumstances he would have
been hanged, but two days afterwards his
employer sent for him, held out his hand,
and said he had lost his temper and made a
mistake. The man said he, too, was sorry
that he lost his temper, and he was working for that employer yet.
I think it is a most drastic decision to
arrive at to say that when a man becomes
voluntarily idle he is debarred from receiving benefit. Suppose a man claims
benefit and the Court of Referees disallows
his claim. After six weeks he is entitled
to make a new claim. His claim is again
turned down and he is so aggravated that
he stops signing for four or five weeks and
then he again gets employment. This job
may last for six months and at the end
of that time, when he is again unemployed, he makes a further claim for
benefit. If my information is correct,
according to the decision of 'he umpire
he is bebarred from receiving benefit because of the fact that during those four
or five weeks when he was previously unemployed he failed to sign at the Employment Exchange. I do not think that is
fair, and I would ask the Government,
through the noble Leader of the House,
to instruct the umpire to decide every
case on its merits. If a man deserves to
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
15 NOVEMBER 1928
be punished let him be punished, but if he
can prove his claim to the satisfaction of
the Court of Referees the umpire should
abide by the decision of that Court.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In reply to the hon. Member, I am informed by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Labour, that
Section 8 of the Unemployment Insurance
Act, 1920, disqualifies for benefit an
applicant who voluntarily leaves his employment without just cause, and no
change such as has been suggested has
been made in this provision. An applicant who has left his employment voluntarily is provided with an opportunity of
proving to the insurance officer or to the
Court of Referees, who are the statutory
authorities for determining the matter,
whether he had just cause for leaving or
not. Each case was considered on its
merits.
CONTROL OF HEAVY TRAFFIC.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Sharman Crawford:
--
To ask the Leader of the House
whether, when the Government are considering the question of control of heavy
traffic on county roads, they will give due
consideration to the advisability of
placing in the hands of the county councils
a larger share of such control than they
have at present.
I
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
would like briefly to give the reasons why
I am asking the question which stands in
my name on the Paper. For some time
past my own and other county councils
have been asking for powers to obtain
more control over the roads in their respective counties than they have at the
present time. In certain small matters
they have got more control than they formerly had, but I noticed that in the course
of a debate in another place the other
day the Minister of Home Affairs stated
they were going to control the traffic on
the roads in the counties after they had
settled the question in Belfast. It seems
to me that the old saying that the people
who pay the piper have a right to call the
tune applies in this case. I think the
county councils are in a better position
to control the roads in their own counties
than is a Government department, and
therefore I hope that county councils and
other local authorities will be consulted
before legislation dealing with this subject is introduced in this Parliament.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply to
the question of the right hon. and gallant
Senator, I desire to assure him that in
connection with any proposals which the
Government may formulate for the control of traffic on our roads the fullest
possible consideration will be given to any
representations which the county councils
may offer on the subject. I should like
to add that my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, has given an
assurance in another place that he will
consult local authorities.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It
is better to make sure before the legislation
is introduced. Sometimes the legislation
is introduced and the consultation takes
place afterwards. I suggest that in this
case the consultation should take place before the legislation is introduced.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
bring that point before the Minister concerned.

Game Preservation Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
