Bill."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly,
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 15 of 6
& 7 Will. 4, c. 13) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Powers of Constabulary to
demand production of game certificates,
search for game, and enter upon lands,
23 & 24 Vict., c. 90).
(1) If any officer or constable has
reasonable ground to suspect that any
person is or has been unlawfully in pursuit of any game to which the provisions
of the Game Licences Act, 1860, relative
to certificates to kill game, as amended
by subsequent enactments (including
this Act) apply, such officer or constable
may demand from such person the production of a certificate to take or kill
game granted to such person under the
provisions aforesaid.
(2) If any such person fails to produce such certificate to take or kill game,
and permit such officer or constable to
read the same, it shall be lawful for such
officer or constable to require such person to declare to him immediately his
name and place of residence, and to
search such person and any game bag,
package, vehicle, or other thing, which
is capable of being used by such person
for the carrying of game, and to search
any premises on which such person
shall be found; and if such person refuses to declare his name and place of
residence as aforesaid, or gives a false
or fictitious name or place of residence,
or if such person refuses to allow such
officer or constable to make the aforesaid
search, he shall, in addition to any other
punishment to which he may be subject,
be guilty of an offence under this Act.
*
*
*
*
*
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
:-
Clause 1, page 1, lines 17 and 18, leave
out the word "unlawfully" (unlawfully
in the pursuit of any game.)
Mr. LESLIE: The first Amendment
standing in my name is, I really believe,
the only Amendment on the Paper which
is likely to cause us any serious difficulty.
I ought, I think, to say at once that I am
always in favour of amending a Clause
rather than deleting it, but I am rather
afraid that if we cannot deal with this
one rather unfortunate word it will be
necessary to suggest that the whole Clause
should come out of the Bill. The Clause,
as will be remembered, has been clarified
a good deal since it first made its appearance in another place. There was
never any doubt about the meaning of the
Clause. That is to say, a licence was not
to be asked for unless there was some
reason to suppose a game licence was required. That has been made plain, but the
word "unlawfully" was put in. I think
the word "unlawfully" was introduced
in order to add some further protection
of the liberty of the subject.
It was
alleged against the Clause that the power
of search given was undue interference,
and it will be remembered that that suggestion found some support when the Bill
was introduced here. We were not any of
us very seriously alarmed when the proposal to insert the word unlawfully was
made in another place by a learned
counsel for whose opinion I have the very
greatest possible respect, and the word
was adopted. It bears entirely on this
question of search, and, as regards that.
may I give a little example to the Committee of what happened to me the other
day. I was going, not fortuitously, but
as is my usual custom, and I was meditating on some trifles or other, I do not
remember what-
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Amendments.
533 Game Preservation Bill-

15 NOVEMBER 1928
Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I was totally absorbed
in those trifles when up came somebody,
not even known to me by name, a man
in uniform, not His Majesty's uniform.
He did not seize me by the hand or ask
me how I was or make any endearing remark at all. What he did say was
"Tickets please."
The road I was going
along is not the Sacred Way, it is the
railway, and the man was a ticket collector. Is not that the very position this
Clause was originally designed for; that
the constabulary, on whom it is desired to
cast the duty of supervising all matters
relating to game preservation, should be
able to ask any person for his game certificate without having any suspicion that
the man was doing anything unlawful.
It was pointed out the other day that
those of us who shoot in our own neighbourhoods were known to the police because we have taken out a licence, but
what is to happen when a stranger comes
into the district.
The police constable, as the Clause now
stands, has a double duty cast upon him.
He must have reasonable ground to suspect that a man is or has been in pursuit
of game, and he must have reasonable
ground to suspect that the man is
acting unlawfully.
That double duty
seems to me to put the constable in a
most impossible position, and I do not
think that that was what was intended
when the Clause was inserted in the Bill.
It is intended, I believe, that a constable
should act in the same way as the Excise
man used to act, but that he shall be
given some slightly further powers. It
is not necessary that he should actually
see the man carrying a gun as he has to
do in the case of an application for a gun
licence, and, therefore, I think it would
be in the interests of the administration
of the Bill that this word "unlawfully"
should come out.
But as I think that we ought,
if I may say so, to pay some deference to
the opinions expressed elsewhere, and
therefore if that word is taken out I suggest a second Amendment on the Paper
which, of course, apart from the excision
of the word "unlawfully," would be unnecessary and would not be moved.
Apart from this Clause, I think the noble
Viscount said in introducing the Bill that
the Constabulary have no authority whatever to enforce the provisions of the Game
Certificates Act. An arrangement was
made, as the Committee will remember, a
very short time ago in our Finance Act
of this year which only received the
Royal Assent on 4th June last, and in
that Act it was provided that,
The Ministry of Finance may, if it
thinks fit, by agreement in writing with
the Ministry of Home Affairs, arrange
for the exercise by officers and constables
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, either
concurrently with the Ministry of Finance or to the exclusion of that Ministry,
of any powers of that Ministry with
respect to the issue of, and the collection
of the excise duty chargeable on, game
certificates and licences and gun licences; and, so far as required for the purpose of giving effect to any such arrangement, the provisions of the enactments
with respect to game certificates and
licences and gun licences shall have effect
as if the officers and constables of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary to which
such arrangement applies were, in the
said enactments, mentioned in addition
to, or substituted for, the Ministry of
Finance and officers of that Ministry.
We
That Section gave no direct power to
the constabulary to ask for a game licence.
Parliament, I believe, has no knowledge
of the arrangements which have been
made between the two Ministries.
know in fact that game certificates are now
obtainable through the police and we presume arrangements have been made, but
I do not think that Parliament as such
has ever been told what those arrangements are. However, the fact remains
that there is nothing there to empower a
constable to demand a game certificate,
and unless he has that power it seems difficult to see how he is to carry out the intention of the Clause. Accordingly I now
suggest that this word should be left out,
and if that is accepted I will then move
words on the Paper which will provide a
kind of buffer between the demand for the
address and the exercise of such power for
search as may perhaps be necessary.
beg to move.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
Clause now stands the police must, before
demanding the production of a game certificate, have reasonable grounds for believing that the person was unlawfully in
pursuit of game. In thus slightly restricting the powers of the police in this respect
it will ensure that persons legitimately in
pursuit of game are not inconvenienced by

Mr. LESLIE: I will not press the

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 2, line 16, to leave out from
"shall" to "be" in line 18 (in addition to
any other punishment to which he may be
subject). The words I am moving to leave
out do not seem to me to be particularly
necessary, but if the noble Viscount wants
to retain them perhaps it might be unnecessary to trouble very much. The objection to them is this. What a man is
doing or is supposed to be going to do
under the Clause is in effect obstructing or
impeding an officer or police constable in
the execution of his duty. If we put these
words in there, that is to say that for that
particular impediment a man is to be subject, in addition to any other punishment
to which he may be subject, to such
punishment as may be regarded as being
expressed by the expression "being guilty
of an offence."
We are left a little doubtful as to what
is to happen in the later case under Sub-
Clause (4) of Clause 6, where a person who
obstructs or impedes an officer or constable
in the exercise of his powers will be guilty
of an offence under the Act. Is a man
guilty under Sub-Clause (4) of Clause 6
to get off a penalty for any offence he may
have committed other than impeding the
police, or is he not. I do not think he is,
and I do not think he would get off anyhow
if these words did not occur in Sub-Clause
(2) of Clause 2. There is something in
the nature of a precedent for these words
in the Gun Licences Act, but I venture to
suggest that they are not necessary and
should be taken out.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
These
words have been inserted in order to make
it clear that if in addition to the offences
enumerated in this Sub-Section some
other offence, such, for example, as being
in pursuit of game without a licence, has
also been committeed, that he will be
liable to punishment for the latter offence
as well as being guilty of the offence under
the Sub-Section. It is considered desirable that these words should remain as
they are, and, therefore, I cannot accept
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill.”
Mr. LESLIE: We have just dealt with
the question of the retention of the word
"unlawfully" and I am now going to suggest that it would be better that the
whole Clause should be left out rather
than that we should pass it with that
word left in. I do not want to repeat
what I have just said, but I must point
out that it is almost impracticable for
the Game Certificates Acts to be administered successfully by the constabulary
under that limitation. When looking
through the Bill as a whole I do not find
any power given to the constabulary to
require the production of a game dealers
licence under those circumstances where
game dealing may be suspected, and I
think it would be better if we left the
Clause out of the Bill and took one of
two courses-either there could be brought
up on Report a new Clause which would
not be open to any objection and which
would include also game dealers, or else
the whole matter dealt with in the Clause
should stand over until the next Revenue
Bill is introduced.
After all, the whole question of game
certificates or game dealers licences is
really more a revenue matter than a game
preservation matter. The issue of game
certificates is possibly indirectly a game
preservation matter, but the collection of
Excise duty is really a revenue affair. I
gather that the noble Viscount adheres to
what he said on the Second Reading of
this Bill-that apart from this Act the
constabulary have no powers. Having
gone as far as that I would like to suggest
537 Game Preservation Bill-

15 NOVEMBER 1928
Committee.
two little objections to this right of search
that have not been mentioned. I think
that if we deal with the matter in a
Revenue Bill, or if we bring up a different
Clause on Report we shall probably leave
out any reference to the power to search.
There are two objections that have not
so far been named. One is that terrible
powers such as these can be exercised just
as much against a man who has got a
game certificate but who happens to have
left it at home as against a man who has
not got a game certificate at all. What
it says is that if any person fails to produce, and in this case we can only take
that as meaning to produce it on the spot.
Therefore, a perfectly law-abiding person
with his licence in his desk is liable to be
dealt with in the same way as a man who
has no licence. In the second place, we
may take it that the Ulster constable will
be of the male sex. Suppose, which I
admit is unlikely, an alleged poacher is
a woman, or—which is much more likely
and is not unknown-is masquerading as
a woman, that introduces a difficulty, and
I honestly believe it would be in the best
intrests of the Bill to leave the Clause out
now and deal with it later as a revenue
matter when we next introduce a Finance
Bill. I do not believe it would seriously
delay the matter.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am afraid
I cannot agree to cut out the Clause entirely because it would leave the constable
with no powers at all.
Mr. LESLIE: Has he any already?
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: Perhaps
the hon. Member would be willing to
leave the matter over until the Report
stage when it can be considered again.
In suggesting that I can give no promise
as to what will happen when we do consider it again.
Mr. LESLIE: I am only anxious that
it should be further considered, and therefore, I will agree to the noble Viscount's
suggestion.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Register to be kept by
holder of licence to deal in game).
(1) From and after the commencement of this section every holder of a
licence to deal in game granted under
the Game Licences Act, 1860, and the
enactments amending the same, shall
keep or cause to be kept in the premises
specified therein a register, in the prescribed form, of all purchases, receipts,
sales, and disposals in any manner, of
game by him, and shall enter or cause
to be entered in such register forthwith
the prescribed particulars (which shall
not include any particulars as to price)
of such premises, receipts, sales, and
disposals.
*
*
*
*
*
For the purposes of this section---
(a) the expression " prescribed
means prescribed by the Ministry
of Home Affairs;
(b) a demand for inspection of a
register or other document shall be
deemed to have been duly made to
the licence-holder if such demand is
made verbally on the premises of
the licence-holder to any agent, employee, servant, or member of the
family of, the licence-holder there.
on.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
3, page 2, to leave out from the beginning
of line 41 to "every" in line 42 (rrom and
after the commencement of this section).
This Amendment, as I expect, will be appreciated, is one of a group of four, an!
I will discuss the whole four together,
because apart from the other three
Amendments this particular one will be
meaningless and rather useless. The
group I refer to is the Amendment immediately before us, a similar one on Clause
5, the one to leave out Clause 9, and the
one to substitute a new Clause for Clause
9. That gives the clue to the matter. I
suggest we should alter the date for the
commencement of the operation of the
Act. The Committee will remember that
this Bill was originally introduced in
another place on 9th May. If the Bill
had been passed before the House rose for
the Summer Recess there would have been
no difficulty and it could have come into
operation in time for the game season
which is now almost at its end. That did
not happen. We are now at the 15th
November, and I do not suppose it is possible for the Bill to receive the Royal
Assent before the beginning of next
month.

season.

think we must say it will be entirely contrary to precedents to bring such a Measure into operation in the middle of a
There have been other cases

grouse
Act from 1865. Those three Bills were

want to suggest that the Bill should not

of the

If the sugvisions may be in full force.
gestion which is embodied in this Amendment, that the Bill should not come into

Mr. LESLIE: I do not think sufficient

year
be until the end of February. You interfere very violently with that, and you are

do not know if that is the intention, but

Game Preservation Bill —

15 NOVEMBER 1928
It
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
3, page 3, line 3, after the word "price"
(particulars as to price) to insert, "or any
particulars of any sales or disposals incidental to an ordinary retail business.'
This is the difficulty of retail sale.
will be remembered that we had the same
trouble with the Fisheries Act. It was
originally proposed that a record would
have to be kept of every ounce of salmon
and every half inch of trout sold by the
retail salesman. That was altered and it
was provided that a record should not be
necessary for a sale of less than 10
pounds. Game are not sold by the
It
pound. They are sold by the brace.
seems to me entirely unreasonable that
we should put in a provision requiring
the perfectly honest game salesman to
record the sale of every individual brace
of pheasants. I feel it would not be of
much use if he did. I quite agree, of
course, that it is most important to put a
check on the illicit receipt of game, but
after the salesman has got the game it
does not seem to me to matter very much
what he does with it provided, of course,
he is not the illicit exporter. That is
the man we want to catch. We do not
want to put obstacles in the way of the
honest man.
If we did we might militate
against the sale of game. A man is
not going to buy game if he is going to
be worried about it.
I think we are entitled to some provision here to protect the honest salesman
from having to record every bird he sells,
every bird he gives away, and every
bird that goes bad. As regards the giving
away of game, I do not know if it is very
much done here, but we have all seen lines
of children waiting about six o'clock when
the fish which had not been sold was
given away. The same thing might occur
with the retailer who buys the game. He
does not sell it all; some is given away
or some is gone bad. At all events, if he
honestly sold it it does not matter whom
he sold it to. I think he is entitled to
some protection. I think if we do not
require these particulars of sales in the
retail business it will help that man without helping the dishonest exporter whom
we want to catch.
We must
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
have some efective check on the proposed
register to be kept by dealers, because a
dishonest dealer, in the case the hon.
Senator has referred to, may be disposing
of illicit game to a larger dealer. I do
not see very well how we can send out a
register to be filled up by the dishonest
dealer, because it might lead to misapprehension if someone got one by mistake.
(Laughter). I would like to give an assurance to the hon. Senator that the point
he has made will not be lost sight of, and
in prescribing the form the Ministry will
endeavour so far as possible to keep the
particulars required in the simpliest form.
so as to cause as little inconvenience as
possible to dealers, honest or otherwise.
Mr. LESLIE: We are now told that
some consideration is going to be shown to
the honest man, but I must join issue with
the noble Viscount on one point. I did
not suggest any alteration as regards the
purchase or acquisition of game. However, the case is not to be lost sight of and
if any complaint is made that any retailer
is unduly pressed it will be open to any
of us to bring the matter up in Parliament, and I certainly should not hesitate
to do so. I do not want to embarrass the
noble Viscount by pressing the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page 4,
line 5, to leave out "shall" and insert
"may" ("other document shall be
deemed'). We are dealing with paragraph (b) of Sub-clause (5) of Clause 3.
This is another difficulty. Again I am
anxious that the dealer should not be
troubled unnecessarily. I want to ensure
that the demand to inspect the register
shall be duly made to a responsible person
on the premises. As the paragraph stands
there is nothing to prevent the sergeant
of constabulary asking a woman in her
dotage, a grandmother, or asking a child
of seven or eight, or asking the office boy
to produce the register and, if he does not
get it, making a complaint that he has
been obstructed. There is no protection
there whatever.
What we want to get at is that the
demand shall be made to a responsible.
person. In the Fisheries Act the expression used is "person in the employment."
But I gather that although the Fisheries
Act has only been law since the 4th of
June and although the provisions dealing
with this matter have not come into operation the expression used appears already
to have given dissatisfaction. Therefore
I suppose the new words have been intro-
Game Preservation Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
duced, but this question of responsibility
has been left outstanding. It is again a
case of protecting a man against undue interference. When I saw the word "agent"
I hoped that, perhaps, I might be
able to get out of it on that, but the other

Amendment ought to be insisted on. I
thought, perhaps, the simple alteration.
from "shall' to "may" would be sufficient to give the court before whom the
case comes an opportunity of exercising

beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In this
matter, as in other matters, one of the chief
protections of the public is the belief in
the fact that the Royal Ulster Constabulary have as much brains as anybody
else. No one lower than a sergeant can
make this demand. He will, I presume,
have to explain to the court when he gives
evidence on whom he made the demand.
If he said he demanded it from the grandmother of 90 in her dotage, or if he went
up to the man's house and saw a perambulator at the door and asked the baby in
it the prosecution would fail immediately.
I think, therefore, he has to prove he asked
a sensible person and that it can be assumed that the powers will be exercised
with discretion. I think it impossible to
believe that there will be a prosecution
except in a case where there has been a
refusal on the part of someone who can be
held responsible. For that reason I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Extension of definition of
game for certain purposes, 1 & 2 Will. 4,
c. 32, 24 & 25 Vict., c. 91, 56 & 57 Vict.,
c. 7).
(1) The Game Act, 1831, shall have
effect as if in section two thereof the
word "game" included woodcock and
snipe, in addition to other species of
game mentioned in the said section; and
the provisions of the Game Licences Act,
1860 (as amended by section seventeen
of the Revenue (No. 2) Act, 1861, and
section two of the Customs and Inland
Revenue Act, 1893) relating to excise
licences to deal in game and the dealing
in and selling of game without an excise
licence, shall extend and apply to the
dealing in and selling of woodcock and
snipe.
(2) Section five of the Game Certificates (Ireland) Act, 1842, and Section
two of the Game Licences Act, 1860,
shall have effect as if snipe were omitted
from the several species of birds therein
enumerated.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
4, page 4, line 13, to leave out "snipe."
This is what might be described as an
amendment interrogative. I do not
really know what the Government will
do, and I am afraid I must add that I
do not particularly care. The point is
this: Hitherto snipe have been included
in what I called the other day the first
class of game.
You required a licence to
kill.
That is to be altered by Sub-clause
(2) and you will not require a game certificate to kill snipe in future. Hitherto,
snipe have never been in Class 2, that is,
the class of game you required a licence to
deal in. If it is intended that these two
words shall remain there what will happen will be
will not want a £3 licence
you
to shoot snipe, but you will want a £3
licence to sell it. I do not know whether
that is really the intention.
If we leave out the words "and snipe"
snipe will cease to be game for the purpose of this Act, and therefore anybody
will be able to send a hamper of snipe
about the country. I do not know
whether any hon. Senator has seen a hamper of snipe, but at all events snipe could
be sent about without being labelled as
game. It would be rather unfair to require a dealer to take out a £3 licence
just because you put snipe into that class
for no apparent reason. Taking it out of
one and putting it into another seems a
little curious, and, accordingly, for the
sake of finding out what the intention of
the Government is I put this Amendment
on the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It will be
remembered that we have withdrawn
snipe from the category of birds for the

is required, and it becomes obviously
Game Preservation Bill-

Committee.
we have used in Clause 2 is "reasonable
ground" and I think we ought to use the
same expression here. There is a difference between "reason" and "reasonable
ground" and it is rather well brought out
in the Firearms Act, and I will just intimate to the Committee the difference.
In Section 11 of the Firearms Act, a very
famous Section, we have it-If a justice
of the peace is satisfied by information on
oath that there is reasonable ground for
suspecting that an offence has been committed he may grant a search warrant, and
a constable in the exercise of the warrant may seize any weapon where he has
reasonable ground for suspecting that an
offence has been committed, and he may
further arrest a person whom he has
reason to believe to be guilty of an
offence.
more necessary than ever to secure that
indiscriminate shooting of these birds does
not take place. There will now be a
greater temptation to kill birds and to
sell them than there was formerly, and
the Government is satisfied that it is desirable that the restrictions in regard to
the sale of snipe should be put in force
as provided for in this Section. Under
the Bill as it stands the only person from
whom dealers will be entitled to buy snipe
will be persons who hold a three-guinea
licence, and now that we are allowing
individuals to shoot snipe on a ten-shilling
licence I am sure that everyone will agree
that we should make it is difficult as possible for unscrupulous persons to slaughter
these birds with a view to sale, and, therefore, it is considered that the word
"snipe" should be left in the Clause. For
that reason I am afraid I will be unable
to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Of course then I will
withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

"reasonable ground." This is really an
assimilating Amendment. The expression
I think that is the usual distinction, if
it is a case of suspicion we use "reasonable
ground," and if it is a case of belief
we use "reason." In any case we use the
expression in Clause 2 and it seems necessary to use it here. I, myself, would prefer "good cause." I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: An identical Amendment to Clause 2 was accepted
in another place, and it seems to me desirable to use the same form of words in both
cases. I should like to say, therefore, that
I have reasonable ground for accepting the
Amendment. (Laughter).
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Close season for woodcock
and snipe).
No person shall, on any pretence
whatsoever, kill or destroy any woodcock
or snipe, between the fifteenth day of
February and the twelfth day of August
in any year, or wilfully destroy the eggs
or nest of any woodcock; and any person offending against this section shall
be liable to the same penalty as is imposed by an Act passed by the Irish
Parliament in the session held in the
twenty-seventh year of the reign of His
Majesty King George the Third, chapter
thirty-five, intituled, An Act for the
Preservation of the Game, upon persons
offending against that Act. Such of the
provision of the Wild Birds Protection.
Game Preservation Bill
THE SENATE
Committee.
Acts, 1880 to 1908, as prohibit, or
authorise the making of orders prohibiting, the killing or taking of wild birds
during any specified time, and the
taking or destroying of wild birds' eggs,
shall cease to apply with respect to woodcock and snipe.
use
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page 6,
line 39, to leave out "on any pretence
whatsoever" and insert "wilfully take.
This is really a suggestion that instead of
using the words in the text we should go
back to Section 4 of the Act of George III,
which is really our general Game Act-it
was somewhere about 1787-and
exactly the same words as are used in that
Section. There is the particular reason
for doing so, that we recite the Act of
Parliament I have referred to later on and
impose the same penalty, and we really
want the word "take" which does not
appear
in the text. I am afraid if we use
the words "on any pretence whatever"
in the year 1928 it would have the effect
of making it illegal to kill a wounded
woodcock which was caught in a trap.
The same phrase was used about fifty years
ago, and it has not been used since. The
Hares Preservation Act carefully avoids it
and uses the expression "wilfully take."
I suggest that we should use it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page 6,
line 40, to leave out "woodcock or snipe"
and insert "snipe, teal, wild duck or woodcock." That is an Amendment of a somewhat different character. We are now
under this Clause specially protecting
woodcock and snipe. Between the
new close times we are going to stop the
shooting. We are going to provide that
the shooting of snipe and woodcock shall
stop on 15th February and we are allowing
it on 12th August. Hitherto the dates
were different in different counties. We
are, if I may say so, putting in a Clause
to legalize an illegality. Some people have
been guilty of shooting snipe on the mountains when the grouse season begins. The
main object is to give further protection.
There are other wild fowl beside snipe
and woodcock. Snipe are really wild fowl
though the Government has just decided
to make them game for one purpose. They
are generally classed as wild fowl although
}

they are game.
There are other birds
that want protecton even more than snipe.
Snipe are mostly foreign-bred birds. Wild
duck and teal are home-bred birds, and
they are becoming more and more rare
every year. It is impossible I suggest in
practice to protect snipe after the 15th
February if we allow wild duck and teal
to be shot up to the end of that month.
Even in my part of the country—and I
live in the very North of Antrim-wild
duck do, in fact, pair very considerably
before the end of February. I keep little
notes in my game book, especially before
the end of the season, and I have looked
it up and for the last six or seven years
again and again I have a note, "saw a
pair of wild duck" or "two pair." I do
suggest that we should be doing no violence
to anybody or anything if we made the
new date for snipe coincide with a new
date for teal and wild duck.
I must pause for a moment to point out
that we should do no violence to the Bill
if we do so. The Bill is a Bill for the
better preservation of game and the Title
of the Act of George III is a Bill for the
preservation of game, and we have further
protection in the definition Clause where
the word "game" has the same meaning
as in that Section of the Act of William IV
when not disagreeable to the text. I
think we should not be going outside the
Title of the Bill if we inserted this particular class of wild fowl. I hope those
Members of this Committee, whose opinion
on many subjects, particularly, perhaps,
as naturalists, is more valuable than mine,
will tell us if they think the better preservation of wild duck and teal would be in
the interests of the preservation of game.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should very
much like to back up this Amendment of
my hon. Friend, because I think wild duck
and teal do require a great deal more protection than they get under the Wild
Birds Protection Act. It may be answered
that they are not game. We have just
made snipe game under this Act, and I
think we might very well make wild duck
and teal game under this Act. There is
no question whatever that what the hon.
and learned Senator told us is true, that
wild duck and teal are becoming very
much more scarce, because the home-bred
birds are shot in large numbers early in
the season and have no chance of arriving
at maturity. It would not hurt this Bill,
and it would be a very great advantage to
a very interesting type of bird if we bring
549 Game Preservation Bill-

15 NOVEMBER 1928
Committee.
it under this Bill and give it further protection. It is now shot, as most people
know, quite early in July when it is no
good for food, and it is purely out of a
spirit of destruction that people go about
the country shooting flappers. I hope the
Government will accept this Amendment.
I am quite sure another place would accept
it willingly.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I should be very
glad indeed if the Government could see
their way to accept this Amendment.
am sure the House were, as I myself was,
very much struck with the observations of
the hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Pakenham) who sits opposite, and of the
Member who comes from Fermanagh (r.
Porter Porter) on the Second Reading.
One was speaking for Lough Neagh and
the other for Lough Erne, and they described what I think was a most lamentable state of things. These birds are being
slaughtered wantonly and wickedly, and
they are interesting and harmless birds
and nobody can object to their being preserved. I feel sure that in another place
-in fact in any place except where these
miscreants live-this Amendment would
be accepted readily. It may be said that
they are protected already, but the facts
show they are not protected. It may be
suggested also that it is worse in Scotland,
but that is all the greater reason why they
should be protected here. I should be
very glad, as I said, if the noble Viscount
will accept the Amendment.
Viscount BANGOR: I should like very
much to support the Amendment and I
hope the Government will see their way
to accept it. It mentions teal and wild
duck, and I presume when it mentions
wild duck it means all the varieties of
wild duck. There is a great number. I
suppose they are all included in that. I
hope the Government will include these
birds. A great number of them are shot
out of season, and I think if we gave them
the same protection as we do to snipe and
wild duck it would be a very good thing.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to point out that there is, at the
present moment, adequate machinery for
fixing the close season for wild duck and
teal if these laws were enforced. That
is the difficulty. I quite understand the
arguments of the Senators who have
spoken in favour of the Amendment, but
I regret I cannot accept it. I should say
there is, at the present moment, a commission sitting to consider the question.
I hope the Senators will be content with
that information.
Mr. LESLIE: Really, the Government
places us in a great difficulty. We are
all fairly acquainted with the method by
which an alteration in the close season
under the Wild Birds Protection Act is
effected. Some of us I remember distinctly twenty years ago went to quarter
sessions to settle the question of a close
time for snipe. We are waiting and
waiting. This would do no harm to anybody. I confess I feel very great difficulty about withdrawing an Amendment
which has been supported on all sides.
It cannot hurt anyone. I really hope it
will be reconsidered.
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. Member move it?
Mr. LESLIE: Yes; I move.
Amendment negatived.
Mr. LESLIE :
Perhaps I had better
move this one in case the Government
might accept it. It is at the end of line
42 to insert the words "teal or" before
woodcock. It is to give to teal the same
protection as regards destruction of their
eggs or nests as woodcock enjoy under the
statute.
MASSEREENE:
Viscount
Amendinent it that?
What
Mr. LESLIE: It is not on the Paper.
It is at the beginning of line 43 before
"woodcock" to insert "teal or." I want
to give the same protection to the nests
and eggs of teal that wild duck and snipe
have already, and that you are about to
give to woodcock. I did not put it on
the Paper at first because it occurred to
me the expression "wild duck," in that
particular Section of the Act of George
III, would be construed to include teal.
Perhaps it would. For the sake of finding out the intention of the Government
I suggest we do protect the nests and
eggs of teal.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have received no notice of this Amendment and
therefore I cannot answer it. I will consider the matter and give an answer on
the Report Stage.
Game Preservation Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment |
is in Clause 7, page 7, line 10, to leave out
the words "taking or." The difficulty
there is that the Wild Birds Acts have
drawn a distinction between taking a wild
bird's egg and destroying a wild bird's
egg, and the same protection is not given
under the Act of George III. Therefore,
it would appear necessary to leave the eggs
of the snipe and the woodcock to the protection of the Wild Birds Act in respect
of the taking of eggs, and I beg to move
the omission of the two words "taking or."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am willing to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
:-
Clause 7, page 7, line 11, leave out the
words "woodcock and snipe" and insert
instead thereof the words "snipe, teal,
wild duck, and woodcock."
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
Clause 7 to insert the following new
Clause:
(8. (1) If any person wilfully takes,
shoots, or attempts to take or shoot, any
partridge between the first day of
January and the first day of October in
any year after the commencement of this
Act, such person shall be guilty of an
offence against this Act.
(2) The provisions of this Section.
shall continue in force until the first day
of September, nineteen hundred and
thirty-two and no longer, unless Parliament shall otherwise determine."
This is the matter we spoke of the other
day when I suggested we should try to
give some protection to the partridge. At
present partridge shooting in Northern
Ireland lasts from 1st September to 31st
January, and it seemed to me that it
would be worth while as an experiment
to try limiting the shooting season from 1st
October to 31st December. Partridges
have had their close season varied more
than once. They are very nearly extinct
in Northern Ireland, but there are some
remaining. I understand that in the Free
State it is proposed to prohibit the shooting of partridges entirely for two years.
That is not a proposal I myself should
favour, because if the coveys are not
broken up there will be a great amount of
in-breeding. I do not think the health
of what birds we have got left is strong
enough to stand in-breeding, although
there are some who say it is the best thing
to do. It seems to me that my proposal
might be tried as an experimental measure, and I would like to hear what other
Members think from the naturalist point
of view.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
The effect
of this Amendment is, in short, to
lengthen the close season by two months,
but I am afraid this would not meet the
real difficulty which was suggested in the
previous discussion on this point. The
difficulty is that partridges, as soon as they
are able to fly, leave the place where they
have been bred and are liable to be shot
by people who are going about. I think
the preservation of partridges is a most
desirable thing. They have been extinct
in my neighbourhood for the last five
years, and I suggest that the real solution
of the difficulty would be a more rigorous
enforcement of the law as it at present
exists. For that reason I regret that at
this juncture I cannot accept the suggested new Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
. --
After Clause 7, insert as a new
Clause:
"10. If any person is convicted of
shooting or pursuing game in contravention of any provision of this Act. or
of any enactment enumerated in the
Schedule to this Act, any certificate to
kill game or any licence to use and carry
a gun which such person may hold shall
be null and void, and, for the purposes
of the Firearms Act, 1920, as amended
by the Firearms (Amendment) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926, and subject
to the provisions with respect to appeal
of Section two of the Statute last afore-

said, such person shall, for a period of
three years from the date of his conviction, be deemed to be a person unfitted to be entrusted with firearms."
I
Mr. LESLIE: This new Clause which
I am suggesting comes in two parts. First
of all, I suggest we should consider the
game licence or gun licence in cases where
there has been an infraction of the law as
regards shooting in pursuit of game.
need scarcely remind hon. Senators, all
of whom I am sure are acquainted with
the facts, that such a provision as the
cancellation of a game or gun licence for
an offence against the preservation of the
game laws already exists as regards
Great Britain. It will be found in
the Acts dealing with
game certificates and also in the Acts dealing with
gun licences. In either case in Great
Britain an offence against the Section
of the Act of William IV necessarily
implies that the licence shall be
rendered null and void. There is this
curious consequence of that, that as such
licences are available througout the
United Kingdom the licence of a man who
commits an offence in Berkshire and is
convicted in Berkshire becomes null and
void in Berkshire, and it also becomes
null and void in Northern Ireland, but
if a man is convicted in Northern Ireland
for an offence against the Act of George
III his licence would not be null and void
in Great Britain. On the other hand, the
penalties for poaching in Great Britain
are not so severe as they are in Northern
Ireland, but at present a game or gun
licence is not forfeited in Northern Ireland because a man is convicted of poaching, and it is so forfeited in Great
Britain. That is a curious anomaly
which this Bill seems to afford an opportunity of removing.
We come from that to the Firearms
question. As the Committee will remember, the Firearms Act as it applies in
Northern Ireland is entirely different to
the Firearms Act as it applies in Great
Britain. In Great Britain the Firearms
Act does not apply to shot-guns, and there
is no Firearms (Amendment) Act under
which you can be permitted to keep a
firearm in your house but not carry it
abroad. The Committee will remember
that although you can have forty certificates under the Firearms Act you cannot carry or use a gun without a gun
licence or kill game with it without a
game certificate. The question arises as
to whether the Firearms Act in Northern
Ireland is so bound up with the Firearms
and Gun Licence Acts that we are not
compelled to deal with it.
In Clause 1 we provide that the constabulary may enforce the Acts relating
to game.
There "may" means "shall."
The constabulary are to enforce the laws
for the preservation of game, but the
constabulary are the authority for issuing
certificates under the Firearms Act, and
are we going to leave it so that the police
can arrest a man with one hand for poaching with a gun and hand him out a firearm certificate with the other hand?
must look at what the provision of the
Firearms Act is. A man is not to have
a firearm without a certificate, but Sub-
Section (2) of Clause 1 says:-
We
A firearm certificate shall be granted
by the chief officer of police of the
district in which the applicant for the
certificate resides, if he is satisfied that
the applicant is a person who has a good
reason for requiring such a certificate
and can be permitted to have in his
possession, use, and carry a firearm or
ammunition without danger to the
public safety or to the peace, and on
payment of the prescribed fee.
Again, the certificate must be renewed
unless some of those things apply, but a
later Clause of the Act-I think Clause 2
--says that if a man is convicted of certain offences, the Court before whom he
is convicted may cancel his firearm certificate. We do not want to amend the
Firearms Act-it is not necessarybut the point is: Can we logically do
without specially applying the provisions
of the Firearms Act, the negative provisions, against a man who has been guilty
of poaching-of course, giving him the
power of appeal-or is the Government
satisfied that such powers as they have
got, if any, are sufficient to meet the case?
It seems a matter most proper to discuss on this Bill and it may very well be
that the noble Viscount can assure us
that it is considered that the police do
possess sufficient power to refuse a firearm certificate to a poacher or to take it
away from him on conviction. It may
be that the noble Viscount can refer me
to the statutory authority for that, and
if he says that he thinks he can carry on
Game Preservation Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
very well without this new Clause, and that
the proper course to be taken by the
police does not require statutory support, it will be an end of the matter.
In the meantime, I have put an Amendment on the Paper, and I move accordingly.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I should
like at the outset to point out that in this
Amendment game and gun licences are
regarded as being on a somewhat similar
footing to firearm certificates, but this
the Government cannot admit. They
are given for entirely different reasons
and the same coniderations do not apply.
As hon. Senators are aware, in Great
Britain the Firearms Act does not apply
to shot-guns, and in its application to
Ireland shot-guns were only included because they were, unfortunately, at that
time a very common weapon of offence.
I believe the suggestion is of too farreaching a character inasmuch as it would
apparently mean that a person who was
fully entitled to possess a weapon for purposes of his own personal security would,
if he were convicted of a poaching offence,
lose the right to retain his firearm certificate. On this ground alone the Government would be bound to oppose the
Amendment.
On the question of the cancellation of
game certificates and gun licences as
distinct from firearms certificates, I am
aware that provision exists in England
for the cancellation of game certificates
and gun licences in certain circumstances
such as those indicated, but this provision has not hitherto been extended to
Ireland. We have now in this Bill provided for very substantial penalties for
offences against the Act, and there seems
no cogent reason why the additional
penalty for this is in fact what the cancellation means-should be imposed.
therefore regret that I am unable to
accept the Amendment.
I
Mr. LESLIE: That is pretty much the
answer I expected, and almost anticipated in my own speech. I ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 8 (Penalties and legal proceedings).
(1) Any person guilty of an offence
under this Act shall be liable, on summary conviction thereof, in the case of
first offence to a fine not exceeding ten
pounds, and in the case of a second or
subsequent offence to a fine not exceeding twenty-five pounds, or at the discretion of the court to imprisonment for
any term not exceeding three months,
or to both such fine and imprisonment.
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
8, page 7, line 16, to leave out the words
"twenty-five," and to insert instead thereof the word "twenty." I do not know if
the Committee will be suprised to hear
that I attach a good deal of importance
to this Amendment. Its effect is to reduce
the maximum fine for a second offence
against this Act from £25 to £20. The
point is not that £25 is a greater sum than
£24 19s 11d, but that £25 is more than
double £10, which is the maximum for
a first offence. It is usual when Parliament introduces penal provisions to pay
some regard to precedent, and to have
some regard, if I may say so, to a sense of
proportion. The penalty is to be applicable to offences against this Act.
I cannot help wondering whether there is not
just a little misunderstanding knocking
about as to what it is this Act is going to
do. Let us see what the offences against
this Act are to which this fine may possibly
be applicable. The offences against the
Act are in Clause 2 the non-production of
game certificate and refusal to give name
and address or to submit to search. These
offences are scarcely likely to be committed
twice. Under Section 3 there is noncompliance by the holder of a dealer's
licence with the requirements of the Section. It will be remembered if that man
does that twice he will lose his dealer's
certificate and be incapable of getting
another one for five years. He is much
more severely treated than the poaching
who not having a game licence
commits an offence, although the penalties.
of this Act do not apply to a man convicted of poaching. There is nothing in
this Act which makes the punishment of
actual poaching anything more severe than
before, but it does make the penalties
more severe for dealing in game and for
not complying with the provisions with
regard to keeping records.
man
Then again under Clause 5, the nonlabelling of packages, for a second offence
557 Game Preservation Bill-

15 NOVEMBER 1928
Committee.
the penalty is £25. Under Section 6 a
second offence of obstructing or impeding
a policeman is punishable by a fine of £25.
And you have in addition the power of
giving a man three months imprisonment
with or without a fine of more than double
the maximum for a first offence. Let us
compare that with the penalties under the
Motor Vehicles Act. For reckless, wanton, or dangerous driving of a motor car
a person is liable to a fine of £1 up to £10
for a first offence, and in case of a second
conviction to £2 or any greater sum not
exceeding £20. That is exactly double.
When you get to a third offence then it is
the custom to put up the price. In the
following Section dealing with the more
serious offence of being drunk in charge
of a motor car-possibly quite as serious
an offence as not putting a label on a package of game-the penalty in the first case
is £5 with a maximum of £50 and for a
second conviction £10 minimum and £100
maximum, again exactly double.
But the most striking parallel in the
Motor Car Act is the general Section
which enacts that the penalty for an
offence against the Act for which no special
penalty is provided shall be £5 in the
case of a first offence, and £10 in the case
of a second offence. That seems to be considered sufficient. The £25 here is out of
proportion and it has not any precedent.
The penalties under this Bill have been
attacked elsewhere and I am sure that a
reduction in this case will be received with
acclamation.
There is another point to which I want
to draw attention. I spoke on the Second
Reading of the desirablity of consolidating.
our game laws at the earliest opportunity.
If we put in a new Bill, a new provision
making the penalty for a second offence
more than double the penalty for a first
offence, we shall find that very much in
the way when we come to consolidation.
I hope the Government on this occasion
will be able to accept the alteration of
twenty-five to twenty.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The reason
for a high maximum is, as pointed out in
my Second Reading speech, to enable the
court to inflict a proper penalty on persons breaking the law on a large scale.
If there were no receivers of stolen goods
in ordinary life there would be very little
theft, so if there were no dealers willing
to break the law on a large scale in this
matter there would be very little game
illegally shot. I think, therefore, this
maximum penalty should be retained.
But it is a maximum penalty. The actual
penalty is at the discretion of the court.
They can fine even a dishonest dealer a
very small sum for a purely technical
offence, or if they really think the man is
deserving of a heavy fine they can inflict
a heavy fine. I regret I should not say
I regret-but I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry I cannot
withdraw; it must go to the Committee.
Amendment negatived.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Commencement of Act).
The provisions of this Act with respect to the keeping of registers by the
holders of licences to deal in game, and
with respect to the marking of packages containing game, shall come into
operation on such day as may be appointed by the Minister of Home
Affairs, and before that day public
notice of those provisions shall be given
in such a manner as the said Minister
may direct.
Save as aforesaid, this Act shall come
into operation on the passing thereof.
The following amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Leave out the Clause and insert as a new
Clause:
"This Act shall come into operation
on the thirty-first day of July, nineteen
hundred and twenty-nine, and before
that day public notice of the provisions
of this Act, with respect to the keeping
of registers by the holders of licences to
deal in game, and with respect to the
marking of packages containing game,
shall be given in such manner as the
Minister of Home Affairs may direct."
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Short title and construction.)
Game Preservation Bill
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
(1) This Act may be cited as the
Game Preservation Act (Northern Ireland), 1928.
(2) References in this Act to enactments of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom shall be construed as references to those enactments as they apply in
Northern Ireland.
(3) In this Act the expression "game"
has, where the context does not otherwise require, the same meaning as the
expression "game" in Section two of the
Game Act, 1831, as amended by this
Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page
8, line 32, after "by" ("as amended by
this Act") to insert "any subsequent enactment and by." This is a small drafting Amendment and I think it is necessary. The fact is, Section 2 of the Game
Act of 1831 has been amended by other
enactments; the particular one I am thinking of is the Act of 1893. I rather think
it was also, as far as Northern Ireland is
concerned, amended by the application
of the Act of 1861, the taking of rabbits
out of a Section of the Act of 1860.
am afraid it is necessary to insert these
words.

20 NOVEMBER 1928
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 20th November, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Antrim Electricity Supply Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
And a message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the Bill as reported from
the Joint Committee, without Amendment.
MOTOR VEHICLES ACT.-
CONSTABULARY APPEALS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
To ask the Leader of the House whether,
with reference to the case of James
Henderson, recently dealt with at Ballymena Quarter Sessions, it can be arranged
that, before the hearing of Constabulary
appeal under the Motor Vehicles Act, the
Chairman of the Bench concerned shall
invariably be consulted and given the
opportunity of stating what did in fact
occur when the case was heard before him;
and whether anything more can be done to
ensure that the Constabulary shall understand correctly what evidence of a previous
conviction is required by Statute, and also
the limitations as regards questions that
can properly be put to a defendant who is
not a witness.
Mr. LESLIE: The matter to which the
question on the Paper relates has been
before this House more than once since
the Motor Vehicles Act of 1926 was
passed, and it is a matter that has troubled
more than one of those who have been
administering it. Therefore I make no
apology for referring to it again. I have
got to use as an illustration of my question
a particular case that was recently dealt
with. I desire to say at once that I do not
of course make any comment on the action
of the County Court Judge who ultimately
decided that case,
nor do I desire to
minimise in any way the difficulties to
which the Constabulary are put, nor to
deny that they are very often very
considerate towards offences against the
Motor Car Acts. I think it would be more
convenient first to tell the House what
happened in the case I have cited, and then
to show how I connect it with my question.
A motor driver was charged before the
Ballymoney magistrates with exceeding
the speed limit. It was submitted that he
had been going at twenty-nine miles an
hour. The man had his wits about him,
and asked various questions, but he was
unhesitatingly convicted. After he had
been convicted the Head-Constable, acting
for the District-Inspector of the locality,
tendered to the bench extracts showing
that a man of the same name had been
twice previously convicted, once at Portrush and once at Belfast, and one or other
of the offences dated back for some seven
or eight months. Somebody asked at once
whether the previous convictions were admitted, but as far as I recollect that question was not directly put to the defendant.
The chairman of the bench at once stated
that the man admitted nothing. Another
magistrate promptly inquired whether
there was any proof of the identity of the
man; that is of course the identity of the
then defendant with the man or men
previously convicted. The representatives of the constabulary then informed
the bench that such evidence was not
necessary.
I
That drew from myself, with the concurrence of others, a brief statement of the
effect of the Section of the Act of Parliament which deals with that matter.
imagine the Section is in the minds of all
magistrates present and I need not read
it, although I have it here. The Head-
Constable then asked the chairman to adjourn the bench in order to produce further evidence, but the chairman, as the
chairman of a Court of Summary Jurisdiction, declined to do it, and the bench
dealt with the man as a first offender, there
being no satisfactory evidence that he was
anything else. I may remind the House
that the consequences to the man were
likely to be serious if it was his third
offence, because he must of necessity be
fined £5, and he must of necessity lose his

Oral Answers

Oral Answers
[Mr. Leslie]
licence and his means of livelihood for a
That is the end
period of three months.
of the procedings at Ballymoney.
The constabulary, or the superior
authorities, were presumably satisfied that
was
done
within
justice
a
gross
the
had been
what
of
miscarriage
meaning of the phrase used by the
Minister of Home Affairs when inducing a
Committee of this House to accept the
Clause giving the right of appeal to the
At all events the
police in such cases.
constabulary did appeal, and the case was
re-heard by the County Court Judge
Before the County
according to law.
Court Judge witnesses were produced who
said they were in court when the man then
before the Court was convicted; that is,
witnesses were tendered to the County
Court Judge who had not been brought be-
There
was, I
fore the magistrates.
believe, some little difficulty even then in
connecting the man present at Ballymoney
with the man at Ballymena, but that was
got over by the evidence of one of the wit-
His Honour, of course, on the
evidence before him, varied the decision
of the Ballymoney bench and fined the
man in the minimum penalty and gave
nesses.
no costs.
The point that seems to have attracted
some attention is as to whether the magistrate ought to have questioned the man
at Ballymoney as to whether he was or
was not the man previously convicted.
The point is, of course, in a case of that
sort whether the evidence necessary to
establish previous convictions is or is not
in court. If the evidence is in court what
the man says is entirely beside the point,
but if, as in the case in my question, the
evidence is not in court it is plain that if a
magistrate does put a question as to
whether the convictions are admitted or
not to an undefended defendant, he will
probably tell him he is not obliged to
answer, and if he tells him that it is
obvious that it is a suggestion to the man
not to admit the thing, and that is not
perhaps very desirable. I am not going
into the question of decided cases.
body interested can turn them up, but
unless they refer to the actual case itself
they will probably not get much further.
Any-
But there is no statutory duty on magistrates to ask the question. The effect is, if
the man does not admit the previous convictions and the necessary evidence is not
there he simply calls the police bluff and
we are no further forward. If, however,
he does admit it and the evidence still is
not there the effect is that those pros
cuting get away with their bluff and succeed without proof of what they opined.
On the whole, the case was not a very
satisfactory one because the magistrates
were put in a false position in not having
before them the full evidence in the possession of the police. It cannot be stated
whether the defendant ultimately got his
proper amount of punishment, because we
cannot tell whether the suspension of his
licence two months later operated more
or less severely than if it had been
suspended at the time of his appearance
before the magistrates.
Extra expense
was incurred, and the constabulary were
placed in great difficulty. They had
either to acquiese in what was their mistake or they had to expose themselves to
vindictiveness in purthe accusation of
suing a man and bringing evidence against
him which they had not given to the
magistrates.
It seems to me that difficulties of this
sort would be a good deal avoided in the
future if it were made the practice to
consult the chairman of the bench before
an appeal was allowed to be brought.
Then the chairman of the bench could
state what did happen and perhaps give
his views as to whether it was a case in
which there should or should not be an
appeal. It will be remembered that in
ordinary cases where an appeal is by a
defendant against his sentence or against
the decision of the magistrates the decision of the magistrates is supported before the County Court Judge, but in this
case, where the appeal is by the police
against the dismiss, the magistrates' point
of view is not put up. The magistrates,
of course, are not exactly parties to the
case and there is that difference to be
observed. It will also be remembered
that it is the practice in England where
a convicted criminal goes to the Court of
Criminal Appeal, for the Judge's notes to
be submitted through the Registrar to
the higher court. It seems to me that the
nearest approximation we can get to those
two things is a report from the chairman
of the bench who is, of course, a
thoroughly impartial person who is not.
concerned either to support the prosecution or to assist the defendant.
As regards the second part of my question, as to whether more should be done
Orai Answers
Oral Answers
20 NOVEMBER 1928
to ensure that the constabulary shall
understand correctly what evidence of a
previous conviction is required by Statute.
The wording of the Statute is perfectly
plain, but it seems that a man of some
standing in the force was not acquainted
with it. It seems also that he was not perhaps acquainted with what the Leader of the
House said on 27th October last year when
discussing the general question of the endorsement of licences. He told us with
his accustomed accuracy, if I may say
so, that if a licence is endorsed it is
the duty of the police to inform the Court
at the proper time and to offer proof of
it. It cannot be contended that
similarity of name or address is proper
proof of identity.
mere
The third part of the question, as regards the questions that can properly be
put to a defendant who is not a witness,
is so obviously connected with what I have
already said that I need not say anything.
more about it. I offer no suggestion today, but I venture to refer to what I did
say in October last year, when in answer
to a question put by Senator Hill Dickson,
the House gave some minutes to considering the question of the endorsement of
motor licences. I beg to ask the somewhat complicated question standing in
my name.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): With reference to the first
part of this question, I have been advised
by the Attorney-General that upon an
appeal to the County Court Judge it is
open to the parties to state what occurred
before the Justices. If the facts
stated are in dispute the County Court
Judge may consult the Justices if he considers this necessary, but he cannot be
compelled to do so.
as
The second part of this question conveys the insinuation that the constabulary
do not understand the law of evidence, and
that in this case they tendered evidence
which was not admissible. The Attorney-General advises me that this insinuation is not based on fact, but that if
either party considers that evidence has
been rejected which should have been admitted or that evidence has been admitted
which should have been rejected it is open
to them to appeal to the County Court
Judge.
DUTIES OF A CROWN SOLICITOR.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
To ask the Leader of the House if he
can state whether it is any part of the
duty of a Crown Solicitor, or of any person acting for him, when appearing before a Court of Quarter Sessions on an
appeal from a decision of a Bench of
Magistrates, to express an opinion regarding the position or responsibility of
any individual Magistrate who has exercised judicial functions in the case.
Mr. LESLIE: The second question
standing in my name is due to something
that was said when the appeal which we
have just mentioned was before the Court
of Quarter Sessions. As I myself, though
not I alone, was the principal target
about whom the Crown Solicitor in question spoke in terms of what I can only
describe as a deliberate impertinence, I
will not make any further comment.
What was said was in the newspapers and
can be referred to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In an
appeal to the County Court Judge the
Crown Solicitor is entitled to state the
facts, and what occurred at the hearing
at Petty Sessions; and the Attorney-
General is satisfied that in the case of
James Henderson the Crown Solicitor
did so correctly.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
answer, may I point out that part of my
question asks whether it is any part of the
duty of a Crown Solicitor to express an
opinion regarding the position or responsibility of any individual magistrate who
has exercised judicial functions in a particular case. That has not been
answered.
I regret
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
that I have not got the answer to that,
but I rather think the circumstances complained of by the hon. and learned Senator happened after the actual conclusion
of the case. The Press report that I saw
did not say so, but I think the interchange of compliments-
Mr. LESLIE: It was not an interchange.
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
took

place after the actual case had been concluded, and that it was rather akin to a
