At any
rate, I put forward that submission.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read the Egan's
Divorce Bill the Third time.
Housing Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
one.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD The Bill now before the House is
a short one, and, I venture to say, a simple
Its object is merely to prolong for
another year the operation of the Housing
Act of 1927. Prior to the passing of the
1927 Act the Government had been
granting subsidies for houses up to a
maximum of 950 square feet superficial
area. It became apparent, however, that
under the earlier Acts builders had
concentrated on the largest possible houses
coming within the ambit of the Acts, and
that consequently the whole object of the
Government's housing policy, namely, to
provide houses for workers at low rates of
wages, was being negatived.
The Act of 1927 reduced the maximum
superficial area from 950 square feet to 650
square feet and the result of this change
has been the erection of a large number of
small houses. The Act of 1927 limited the
payment of subsidies to houses completed
before the 1st April, 1929, and in the
absence of further legislation housing
subsidies would cease as from that date.
The present Housing Bill extends the time
for the payment of subsidies for another
year, as it has been found that, while the
Act of last year was effective in encouraging
the erection of these workers' dwellings,
the shortage of this class of accommodation
has not yet been fully met.
In order to show the success which has
followed the change in policy introduced
by the Act of 1927 it is but necessary to
quote the number of houses-3,351approved for erection during the six
months ended 30th September
last
compared with the number approved for
the corresponding period last year-1,419.
In all, 14,708 houses have been approved
for erection since the inception of the
Housing Acts, and of these no less than
8,548 were completed up to the end of
October, 1928.
This question of the encouragement of
the erection of small houses by the
granting of subsidies is closely linked with
the question of rent restriction. As the
House is aware, an Act has been passed
during the present Session extending for a
further period the operation of the Rent
Restriction Acts in respect of the smaller
type of houses. The justification for that
Measure was that the supply of small
houses had not overtaken the demand, and
therefore it would have been within the
power of house owners to increase unduly
the rents of existing houses. That fact in
itself shows that the time has not yet come
for any relaxation of our efforts to increase
the supply of small houses.
As strong representations have been
made in another place and outside in
favour of allowing the area of the houses
to be increased by another 100 square feet,
it is desirable to make clear the Government's reasons for adhering to the limit at
present proposed. As already pointed out,
when houses of a larger type were
permitted it was found that the majority
of the houses being built approximated to
the maximum size and were too costly to
be available for the working classes, and
the area was reduced so that houses might
be built at a cost which would place them
within the reach of the poorer classes. The
addition of even 100 square feet, besides
increasing the cost, will bring into
competition for these houses a more
wealthy class of people and make it more
difficult for the poorer class to obtain
houses for themselves. Moreover, it is
found that houses are being so designed as
to be easily capable of having considerable
additions made to them, and the
additional 100 square feet would give
scope for the enlargement of the houses to
suit a class much better off than those for
whom the houses are primarily intended.
For these reasons it is considered advisable
to limit the size to 650 square feet. I beg
to move.
Mr. LONG: I think there is no doubt
that the provision of this subsidy has increased very considerably the number of
£69
20 NOVEMBER 1928
Housing Bill—

Second Reading
The
the subsidy has been paid on each of
them, but I have been told that houses
are being built in this way. When the
Housing Bill was going through this
House last year, I got the impression, I
do not know how, that the 650 superficial
feet was the ground floor measurement,
and I came to the conclusion that when
such a house was built with a second story
on such a ground floor it would be a very
substantial house for a working man. I
carried that impression for a considerable
time, and I was not the only one who had
it. Architects had the same impression
and so did some of the builders until it
was discovered that the 650 feet had to
be divided in two and that 325 feet was
to be allocated to the ground floor and
325 feet to the second floor.
houses available for the working classes,
and I am strongly in favour of the Measure being prolonged for a further period.
I should like, however, to draw the attentention of the Minister of Home Affairs
and the officials of that Ministry to a
practice that has grown very considerably
in some districts. Plans are prepared for
two semi-detached houses to comply with
the conditions for earning the subsidy on
a house of 650 superficial feet.
houses are duly built and are inspected
in the regular course by the Ministry's
engineering inspector. One would naturally think that two houses were being
provided for the people who required
them, but no such thing occurs.
sooner has the engineering inspector
passed the houses for the subsidy than
alterations are commenced and these two
houses which were originally intended for
two families are immediately converted
into one. They thus have a very trim
and neat little villa of about 1,300 superficial feet. I suggest that this practice
negatives in a large measure the object
the Government have in view in providing
houses for the working classes at a rent
they can reasonably be expected to pay.
No
I also suggest that this practice should
be combated in some way by the Ministry
of Home Affairs and its officials. I do
not know what steps they should take for
that purpose, but I have no doubt that
now the practice has been brought to
their notice it will be stopped in some
way. It has not yet grown to a very
great extent. I have seen several houses
which have been converted in this way
and with slight alterations they provide
very neat little villas. They are deliberately planned and built by these speculators, not with the object of providing
houses for the working people but for
providing a little villa which can be sold
for a good sum, and I suggest the object
of the Government in granting this subsidy is thereby defeated. I hope the
Ministry will take notice of my remarks
and will take steps to deal with this practice.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would like to support the Second Reading of this Bill. I
agree with Senator Long's remarks about
the evil to which he referred and I hope
the Government will take immediate steps
to put a stop to it. I have not myself
seen any of these semi-detached houses
that have been converted into villas after
When some of those houses had been
built I paid them a visit of inspection
and I had a conversation with a builder.
I asked him if there were 650 feet in the
floor space and he said, "No, it is only
half of that." I asked him where the
measurements were taken from and he
said from the inside of the walls and trom
gable to gable. I said "That looks a
very small space for 325 feet. There is
not 325 feet of living and sleeping room."
He said, "No, the fireplace comes off
that."
I said, "What do the fireplaces
measure," and he replied, "The four of
them measure 4 feet by 2 feet each." He
also told me that the inside walls, the
staircase and the well of the staircase also
came off the measurement of 650.
fact of the matter is that what with mortar,
plaster, lathes, and stairs, practically
one-third of the available space is wasted,
and to my mind the house is too small for
a working man. It does not suit the requirements of the working man with a
family of five or six. It gives only two
decent bedrooms, and as for the third
bedroom, a person would have to go outside it to undress, it is so small.
The
At the present time a large number of
builders are rushing up what are called
two-bedroomed houses on which they will
receive the subsidy. When these houses
are completed and the subsidy is paid the
builders require a person who comes along
to buy them to put down either £50 or
£100, and after that they have to pay
anything from 11s to 25s a week for practically the remainder of their lives.
may suit some young married couples who
are just starting housekeeping, but we
This
Second Reading
Housing Bill—

THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
must not forget that these young married
couples soon become old married couples
with perhaps four or five boys or girls. No
man or woman who has any regard for the
moral welfare of his or her family could
allow their grown-up children to sleep in
one room. They require at least a threebedroomed house so that these children
will be able to sleep by themselves.
The working man does not want a villa
type of house, nor does he want a garden
at the back of his house, because a great
many working men have not the time to
work in a garden. Neither does he want
a verandah at the front of his house. What
he really wants is a fair-sized house at a
reasonable rent, a plain house for a plain
working man, and for a plain working
man's wife and family. He wants a house
that will suit his requirements and that
will not involve much expense in later
years. I have been in the building trade
for a good number of years, and I know
that those fancy houses that are being
built are in years to come a source of considerable expense in keeping the rain and
damp out. The best type of house for the
working man is the house with a very
plain sidewall and roof.
There is another matter I would like to
mention. In 1927 there were a number
of houses built on which the subsidy was
paid. Some of those houses have been
lying vacant for the last twelve months.
They were built for sale, and the price the
builder put on them was £600, and he received the subsidy in addition. He cannot
get £600 for those houses, and he has held
them up since. I cannot for the life of
me see why any individual should be
allowed to hold up public money in this
way when houses are so urgently required
by the people. I think the Government
should have more supervision than they
have over houses on which they have paid
a subsidy. Both the Government and the
Corporation contribute a subsidy to these
houses, and I think that a person who
builds subsidy houses and then holds them
up to get his own price for them should be
compelled to pay rates on them from the
date on which the subsidy was paid in
respect of them.
I understand that in a street in North
Belfast a builder is erecting twenty subsidy
houses. They are supposed to be parlour
houses, and the parlour is taken off the
That leaves a very
325 feet of floor space.
small parlour and kitchen, and in addition
there is three feet taken off for the hall.
He is not building these houses for sale,
but for letting, and I am told that the
conditions on which he will let them are
£20 down to cover any damage that may
be done by the tenant while he occupies
the house, a rent of 16s a week, and a three
years' lease. I do not think any builder
who receives a subsidy should be in a position to make a bargain such as that. The
Government should see that such houses
are let at a reasonable rent and that no
other obligations should be imposed on a
person who takes such a house. He should
not be obliged to remain in it for three
years, but he should be at liberty to leave
if he finds a house that suits him better.
In supporting this Bill, I hope the Government will add another 50 feet to these
subsidy houses. Considering that SO
much has to be deducted from the present
measurement in the way I have indicated,
the addition of another 50 feet is necessary
to give the occupier reasonable room to
live and sleep in.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to approve of
and welcome this Measure. The home or
house of the working man is really the most
important part of his environment, and I
can assure the House that an extraordinary transformation takes place not
merely in a working man's cleanliness and
self respect but even in his appearance
when he is able to move into decent home
surroundings after being compelled,
through unfavourable economic circumstances, to live in a slum dwelling for
a long period of his life. This question of
the housing of the working classes is of
supreme importance to the community as
a whole, not merely from a physical but
from a moral and spiritual point of view.
I do not think the houses that are being
erected under the Government's housing
scheme are sufficiently large to justify
us looking forward to rearing in these
homes a physically strong future generation. If the medical officers of health in
the various urban and rural districts enforced the provisions of the Public Health
Acts they would have to decide that where
there were families living in them, these
houses were not sufficiently large to comply with the requirements of the Public
Health Acts so far as cubic space is concerned.
I understand that 300 feet is regarded
as the minimum requirement, and one can
Housing Bill—
Second Reading
20 NOVEMBER 1928
realise the injurious physical effect on
the future generation by two or three
children being put to sleep in a room
seven feet square. When one takes into
consideration the amount of air that passes
through the human lungs in an hour or
two the atmosphere of such a room must
almost immediately become contaminated
with a large amount of carbon di-oxide
that should not be in a bedroom at all.
I know the economic difficulties that confront those who are desirous of providing
proper homes for the working classes.
After what has been said by the hon.
Senators who have already spoken I do
think the Government should take into
consideration the advisability of exercising some control over the disposal of
houses on which the Government and the
local authority paid a subsidy on behalf
of the people. It is not fair that a man
should be permitted to receive public
money given to encourage the building of
houses to supply a crying public need and
in the interest of his own selfishness to
hold up that property for whatever period
he likes in order to obtain a fancy price
for it. I welcome the Measure and I wish
it every successs.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
also wish to support the Second Reading
of this Bill. I am extremely pleased to
find that the housing subsidy is to be extended for a further period. So far as
concerns the cubic space of the houses, I
think it should be borne in mind that
the particular object the Government
have in view is to endeavour to encourage
the building of houses that can be let at a
rent within the reach of the workers.
Unfortunately, the class of house that was
erected before the superficial area was
reduced and the subsidy increased was let
at a rent beyond the means of the ordinary
working man. The houses were very
suitable from the point of view of the
accommodation, but it was impossible for
the worker to pay the rent demanded for
them. After a great deal of trouble the
Government have been able to draw up a
specification which meets the requirements
of those people who were most in need of
a house. For the most part those people
were young people who were in many cases
residing with their fathers and mothers.
They could not find other accommodation
and they could not afford to pay the rent
required for the larger class of subsidy
house. Therefore I think the Government
has struck the happy medium and has
succeeded in providing the type of house
that the working people were most in want
of.
.
I know that during the past year or so
the Belfast Corporation has erected a
considerable number of this type of house
on a site known as Seaview, and the
demand for those houses is something
enormous. There is no demand whatever
for the class of house that was previously
built and let at a rent of from 14s to 22s
a week, but there is a great demand for
these houses that are let at about 8s a
week. That
goes to prove that the
Government have acted wisely in encouraging the erection of houses of the class
that will qualify to receive the subsidy
under this Bill. They can be let at a rent
within the reach of the worker's purse and
they will be suitable for the occupiers for a
number of years at least. They may then
want to look out for more accommodation,
but that is a matter for themselves. What
the Government have to do at the moment
is to cater for the people who are most in
need of houses, and that is being done
under the provisions of this Measure. For
this reason the Bill has my strong support.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
have pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading of this Bill. There are just two
points I would like to refer to. It has
been stated that
some builders who
erected houses under this housing scheme
received the subsidy and after spending
£400 or £500 on them they held up the
houses until they could get their own
price for them. If I build houses to
make
money I am not going to hold them
up unnecessarily, and I think that to
adopt the suggestion of Senators Barclay
and Dorman would be over-legislating for
a few odd cases. I know of cases where a
man has built houses on a bad site and has
regretted it, and I do not think it would
be wise to interfere with that class of tiedup house. Appeals have been made to
the Government to enable houses with
larger bedrooms to be erected. No
matter what the size of a bedroom is, if
it has not got proper ventilation and if
the occupiers close all the windows to
keep out the cold night air it will not be
healthy. No bedroom can provide for
twelve or even eight hours healthy sleep
with closed windows, and therefore I
think the sanitary authorities should insist that there should be proper ventilation in all bedrooms. With these few
remarks I support the Bill.

THE SENATE
Report Stage
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: After the speech of Senator
Joseph Cunningham there is not very
much left for me to say in reply to the
debate. The policy of the Government is
to put as few restrictions as possible on
the grant of the subsidy, because I think
it must be obvious to all hon. Senators
that the more restrictions you impose the
less likely you are to get builders to build
houses. As regards the price at which
houses are sold, there is this difficulty.
If a house was sold and changed hands
it would be extremely difficult for any
Government to tie the new owner as to
what price he was to sell his house for.
I foresee a great difficulty in that direction.
As
Senator Long referred to the question
of semi-detached houses-I presume he
meant single storey houses-which were
converted into villas after the builder had
received a subsidy in respect of each of
them, and then sold at a big profit.
far as I know this has only been done in
one case, but I should be very glad if the
hon. Senator would give me or the Home
Office a specific case such as he has described. This matter is receiving the attention of the Ministry of Home Affairs
and I understand the right hon. Gentleman is very shortly issuing a circular to
builders about building houses to a particular design. I will bring the question
Senator Long has raised to the notice of
the Minister of Home Affairs and I shall
be very glad if the hon. Member will give
me a specific case of this kind.
On Question. Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Game Preservation Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Moved, "That this Report be now received." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
CLAUSE 2 (Powers of constabulary
to demand production of game certifi
cates, search for game, and enter upon
lands, 23 & 24 Vict., c. 90).
Mr. LESLIE: There is an Amendment
standing in my name on the Order Paper
to leave out Clause 2. I do not want to
repeat the arguments I used at an
earlier stage, but I should like to say for
fear of any misinterpretation that I am
not afraid of the right of search introduced by the Clause. What I am afraid
of is embarrassing the administration of
the Bill when it becomes an Act, since if
it really is the case that the constabulary
have no other power than that to be given
them by this Bill no other power of demanding the production of a certificate
to kill game then the constabulary will
be actually restricted in carrying out the
obligations it is proposed to impose upon
them. This Clause, standing, as it will
stand, alone, must be held to restrict their
proceedings.
It seems to me that the Clause has
become a Revenue Clause, especially if we
remember that the word "sole" was taken
out of Clause 1 in the Bill as it came to
another place. The Clause originally
provided that no officer or constable
shall enter upon any lands for the
sole purpose of enforcing the game
rights of any owner
or occupier of
such lands, or of any person in whom the
game rights over such land are vested."
The word "sole" was taken out there after
the Clause in its present form had been
passed in another place. I consider then
that the Clause has now become a purely
revenue matter, and I am very anxious
that the administration of the Bill should
not fall between the two stools of administration for revenue purposes and administration as a game preservation
Measure.
It is, of course, really a point for the
Government to advise us on, but I think it
would be better to leave the Clause out
now and substitute something for it in the
next revenue Bill that is brought before
this House. There is certain to be a
Finance Bill sooner or later, and if that
method be taken it will presumably be
necessary to provide that the Clause should
operate whether the man was or had
recently been in pursuit of game. It will
also be necessary to retain the protection
given here that it should not be necessary
that a man should be seen actualy using
577 Game Preservation Bill-

20 NOVEMBER 1928
Report Stage
or carrying a gun. It is purely a matter
of administration, and I felt it right to
put these views before the House.
Accordingly I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
hon. and learned Senator is fully aware
that this particular Clause, which gives
the police considerable powers, is one of
the fundamental provisions of the Bill,
and that if his proposal were in fact
accepted this Bill would, to a very large.
extent at any rate, be rendered useless.
Doubtless the Clause as it stands does not
go as far as the hon. and learned Member
desires, but it is nevertheless one of the
principal provisions of the Bill. It gives
the police powers they have not hitherto
had and whicn, in the opinion of the
Government, are really essential if the
police are to be of real use in the enforcement of these game laws.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not desire to press
the Amendment after that reply, but I
am not attacking the essentiality of the
Clause.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 7 (Close season for woodcock
and snipe).
No person shall wilfully take, kill, or
destroy any woodcock or snipe between
the fifteenth day of February and the
twelfth day of August in any year, or
wilfully destroy the eggs or nest of any
woodcock.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
7, in the last line of page 6, to insert after
the word "any" the words "teal or." The
point I want to direct attention to here is
one that was put up on the Committee
stage, and it was decided it should be
looked into again at a later stage. If the
Amendment is accepted the Clause would
then read to the effect that no person
should wilfully destroy the eggs or nest of
any teal or woodcock. It was more or less
part of the general wild fowl question
which this House did not assent to, but it
has this advantage, that if we insert the
words it will clear up the question of what
the phrase "wild duck" really does mean
in the old Act of George III. Under that
Act the eggs of wild duck, widgeon, snipe
and plover are protected. Woodcock are
not, and that is why woodcock are specially
inserted in this Bill. The point does arise
as to what that particular Section of the
old Act means when it says "wild duck."
I thought it might clear things up if we
deliberately gave protection to the eggs or
nests of teal. I do not think it is a
matter of great importance, but it was left
over, and therefore should be disposed of.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think I will go into the procedure under
the Wild Birds Protection Act which the
hon. and learned Senator knows so well,
and which I dealt with at an earlier stage
of the Bill. The real question at issue is
what teal really means, and the difficulty
about that is that while everybody in this
House doubtless knows what teal looks
like when he sees it there is apparently a
great difference between how lawyers look
at teal and how ornithologists look at it.
That is the difficulty at the moment.
game Act of George III-I am happy to
be able to give the date, 1777-makes it an
offence to destroy the eggs of wild
birds, including wild duck, and it
is probable that this expression "wild
duck" includes teal. If it does not
there is ample machinery under the Wild
Birds Protection Act to secure that protection.
The
I think the Act included widgeon, and
if it includes widgeon, it is very difficult
to know what the original Act really did
mean, because if it did include widgeon
you might as well put in the pochard,
shoveller, pintail, etcetera. I could give
the House about twenty names of duck,
so there is really a great discrepancy. I
should like to say there is a Commission
sitting in the City on wild birds about
which I have given Senator Leslie information. That Commission is considering
questions of this kind, but as far as this
Bill is concerned I regret that I cannot
accept the Amendment, nor can I give
any definite information as to teal.
Mr. LESLIE: Before asking leave to
withdraw the Amendment I might perhaps contribute to the discussion the statement that Section 10 of the same Act of
George III speaks of "duck, teal, and
widgeon."
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 9 (Commencement of Act).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
:-
K
Report Stage
Game Preservation Bill-
THE SENATE

Leave out Clause 9, and insert as a new
Clause:
"This Act shall come into operation
on the first day of May, nineteen
hundred and twenty-nine, and before
that day public notice of the provisions.
of this Act, with respect to the keeping
of registers by the holders of licences
to deal in game, and with respect to the
marking of packages containing game,
shall be given in such manner as the
Minister of Home Affairs may direct.'
Mr. LESLIE: This is the question of
the date when the Bill should come into
operation. I pointed out before that
there was no precedent for curtailing an
existing season during the currency of that
season and that all precedents in respect .
of pheasants, partridges, and grouse were
brought into operation before the commencement of a season. I did not call
special attention to the case of sand grouse
which is very much to the point.
There
was a Bill passed in 1888 for the protection of sand grouse which had come
into this country and which it was hoped
would be acclimatised. Although the
Sand Grouse Protection Act was renewed
under the Expiring Laws Continuance
Act every year until two or three years
ago the sand grouse has never returned.
But the point is that the Bill, which
received the Royal Assent in 1888, did not
come into operation until 1st February,
1889. In any case it will be impossible to
bring into operation Clauses 3, 5, and 6,
before the present season is over, and it is
unfair perhaps to the existing tenants of
shootings to alter the date during the
season.
There is also the possibility of difficulty
as to what is to be done as regards pending
cases for a breach of any of the game laws.
There is a proposal in the Bill to alter
the constitution of the Court of Summary
Jurisdiction. I am afraid that if you
make a change suddenly there may be a
difficulty there. There are, as the House
knows, certain precedents for interference
in criminal matters by legislation, but you
do not want to seriously trouble the
Court to interpret your intention in a
matter of this kind. Previously I suggested 31st July for the commencement,
that being the day before the new game
licences would come in, but it was
pointed out that that was putting
it off rather too long because it
to be suggested that the
appears
slaughter of grouse takes place before the
proper time, 12th August, and that it
goes on even in the month of July. I do
not know what sort of young grouse you
would get before 31st July, but it seems
to depend a good deal on the climate and
the area where it was taken from.
In order to get over any difficulty of that
kind-it was not my intention to assist
the slaughter of immature birds-and
having regard also to the possibilities of
cold storage, I thought we had better put
the date back in the Bill. Accordingly I
am suggesting 1st May, and if that
date is not suitable another one can be
suggested. It is not material. The only
point is to get the date sufficiently far off
to allow everything necessary to be done
that should be done and not to interfere
with the existing arrangements for the
current season. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I understand the objections Senator Leslie has
raised to the immediate application of the
Bill, not including the Clauses relating to
dealers and the consignment of packages
of game are, in the first place, that
persons who have rented shootings in the
belief that they will be able to shoot woodcock and snipe until 1st March will
without warning now be deprived of the
last fortnight, and that in the second place
persons who have purchased game
certificates in the belief that it would
entitle them to shoot woodcock and snipe
until 1st March will also be disappointed
and will be deprived of the last fortnight
of the shooting season in respect of woodcock and snipe. This matter has been
carefully considered, and I cannot find
there is any ground for believing that
there is any danger of hardship arising
from the proposal in this Bill. While the
hon. and learned Senator mentioned on
the Committee stage that he believed
certain persons do rent shootings for the
specific purpose of shooting woodcock and
snipe, I cannot think their numbers are
very large.
Further, I am convinced that if they
are really true sportsmen they will be only
too glad to surrender the last fortnight in
February in the hope that the shooting
next year will be more prolific. On the
same ground I am sure that the holder of
a game certificate will feel no sense of
grievance at losing a fortnight, taking
into consideration the increased prospects
Game Preservation Bill
Adjournment
20 NOVEMBER 1928
for shooting in the following year if the
mating and breeding seasons are allowed
to follow their normal course without disturbance from the gun. In conclusion,
I might say that the sooner this Bill becomes operative the better. As its object
is the general enforcement of the game
laws sportsmen will obviously benefit and
I think, judging from various expressions.
of opinion, that the small disadvantages
the hon. and learned Senator has drawn
attention to as possibly arising from the
immediate operation of the Bill would.
be amply compensated for by the benefit.
the Government hope will result. Therefore, I am unable to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: What are you going to
do about pending cases?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Perhaps
you will raise that point on the Third
Reading.
Mr. LESLIE:
Very well. Then I
will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Adjournment.

Tuesday, 27th November, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in

Ministry of Commerce:

Business of the House.
move,
"That
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting in order to take the
Second Reading of the Representation of
the People Bill.”
I
Mr. DORMAN: I have never risen
in this House to speak under a deeper
sense of responsibility than I do to speak
on this Motion that has just been proposed by the noble Viscount. During my
whole lifetime I have been an advocate of
the rights of women to exercise the
suffrage on the same terms as men.
have also been an opponent of privilege
of any kind. If this Bill was a Bill solely
for onferring the vote upon women on
the same terms as it is conferred upon
men I should welcome the suspension of
the Standing Order and give the Bill my
whole-hearted support, as would my colleagues in another place have done. The
Government here makes a continuous profession of going on parallel lines and step
by step with Great Britain. The Government of Great Britain introduced a simple
Measure conferring on the women of
Great Britain the right of voting on the
same terms as men, and I believe that had
that Government attached to that simple
Measure a Clause to grant the privilege
that it is proposed to confer upon the
citizens of Northern Ireland the Prime
Minister of Great Britain might be said
to be signing his own political death
warrant.
I believe a wave of righteous indignation would arise amongst the democracy
of Great Britain that would sweep out of
office any Government that proposed such
a reactionary Measure as is the Bill now
before this House. This Government was
in the first instance set up under the electoral system of Proportional Representation, a system which applied to the elections both for this Parliament and for
local governing bodies. The Government,
in its wisdom or otherwise, has already
abolished Proportional Representation so
far as local governing bodies are concerned, and they have also-—
Mr. SPEAKER: I must ask the hon.
Member to postpone his speech on the
Bill because he cannot speak on the
merits of the Bill on the Motion now before the House.
Mr. DORMAN: That being so, I wish
to make my position clear not only to
hon. Members of this House but also to
the public outside. Having been a lifelong supporter of the right of women to
vote on the same terms as men, and an
opponent of such a privilege as this
Bill seeks to give, the Government is
placing me as, what I suppose I must regard myself, the Leader of the Opposition, in an extremely invidious position.
In the first place, if I support the suspension of the Standing Order, and thus
support this Measure, I will be supporting the principle of giving the franchise to money. In the second place, if
I vote against the Measure I will be much
misrepresented in the public press which
supports the Government as being opposed to the extension of the franchise
to women. Therefore, seeing that the
Government has already abolished Proportional Representation so far as it applied to local government elections, and
that it has made it obligatory for candidates seeking election to local governing
bodies to deposit £25 as a nomination
fee, which is beyond the reach of most
working men-—
Business of the House
27 NOVEMBER 1928
Mr. SPEAKER: I must again remind
the hon. Member that he can only speak
on the Motion before the House and not
on the Bill itself.
Mr. DORMAN: Under these circumstances I conceive it to be my duty to
those whom I represent in this House
and to myself to refuse to take part in
any deliberations on this particular
Measure, and therefore I withdraw from
the House.
The hon. Member then left the House.
Mr. LESLIE: As you have just reminded us, the question before the House
is whether or not the Standing Order
should be suspended for the purpose of
giving a Second Reading to the Representation of the People Bill. It seems to

me that the only ground on which such a
Motion made by the Leader of the House
could be objected to is on the ground
that it does not give Members sufficient
time to acquaint themselves with the
Measure they are asked to consider.
am, I think, as much in favour as anybody of giving ample time for the consideration of stages of Measures when
time for consideration is necessary.
But
we know perfectly well that the Bill
which it is proposed to read a Second
time this afternoon, the merits of which
I do not intend to discuss now, contains
only three major points, two of which are
for the Committee stage, and the third,
which for the sake of clearness I will call
the equalisation of the sexes, is based absolutely on an Imperial Statute which received the Royal Assent on the 2nd July
this year and has, therefore, been in our
minds and before us for a period of almost six months. I cannot for my own
part take any stand in opposition to the
proposal of the noble Viscount, and I
venture to commend it to the House.
Having regard to the time of year and
the machinery which such a Bill must
necessarily require, I would urge that
the remaining stages of the Bill be taken
with such celerity as may be possible.
On Question, Motion (That Standing
Order No. XXV be suspended for this
day's sitting for the purpose of taking
the Second Reading of the Representation of the People Bill) agreed to.
Representation of People Bill 586
Egan's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
And a Message was sent to the [ouse
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Bill as reported
from the Joint Committee without
Amendment.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Representation of the People Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
Representation of the People Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time." As appears from the Title
of the Bill, its principal object is to bring
into line with recent Imperial legislation
the arrangements regarding the franchise.
for men and women in respect of Parliamentary and Local Government Elections.
The Imperial Act, while it applies to the
elections of Members for Northern Ireland constituencies to the Parliament of
the United Kingdom, does not extend to
the elections for the Parliament of Northern Ireland or to our local government
elections, and, accordingly, once the principal of assimilating the franchises for
men and women for our local elections is
accepted, it becomes necessary to pass
special legislation in our Parliament giving
effect to the principle.
I presume that it is hardly necessary
to labour the point as to the desirability
of assimilating the franchise for men and
women, as the principle has been so generally welcomed not only in Great Britain
but also in Northern Ireland. I presume
also that the House would consider it
rather tiresome if I were to explain in
detail what the present situation is in regard to the Parliamentary and local
587 Representation of People Bill--

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
and
[Viscount Charlemont]
government franchises for men
women, respectively, and I will, therefore,
confine myself to the simple statement in
this connection that the present Bill will
make, in regard to the Parliamentary
franchise for the Parliament of Northern
Ireland, the conditions for women identical with those for men. In other words,
the new conditions will be:
(a) 21 years of age and upwards;
(b) Not subject to any legal incapacity;
(c) Either the appropriate residence
qualification or business premises
qualification, or,
(d) Being the wife or husband as the
case may be of a man or woman
entitled to be registered in respect
of a business premises qualification.
In this connection it may be a matter
of personal interest to you, Mr. Speaker,
and to Senator Lord Bangor, who is not
present, as it is to myself, that in regard
to the question of legal incapacity the Bill
introduces a new feature which in connection with the elections to the Parliament
of Northern Ireland removes the legal incapacity of peers to be registered and to
vote. It gives me great pleasure to
announce that fact.
Ac-
Now a word about the University franchise, and so far as this particular aspect
is concerned the question is somewhat
simple, as we have only one University
constituency and that University grants
degrees to both men and women.
cordingly, we are in the happy position of
being able to deal with the question of
University franchise by a simple Clause,
the effect of which will be that a man or
woman of 21 years of age and upwards
who has received a degree at the University other than an honorary degree, and
who is not subject to any legal incapacity,
will be entitled to be registered as a voter
for the constituency.
Turning to the question of the local
government franchise, a similar process
is being applied of assimilating the franchises for men and women, and under the
new conditions it is proposed that a man
and a woman will be placed in precisely
the same position, or that in other words
he or she shall be entitled to the local
government vote if:-
(a) of full age;
(b) not subject to legal incapacity;
(c) has occupied as owner or tenant for
at least six months a dwelling house
in the constituency or any land or
premises (not a dwelling house) of a
yearly value of £5 and upwards, or,
(d) is the husband or wife of a person
entitled to be so registered in respect
of premises in which both husband
and wife reside.
are
I have explained generally how the
principles of assimilation being
carried out and what, in general terms,
the results of this assimilation process will
be in so far as our Parliamentary and local
government elections are concerned, but
I should here explain that there is
another provision in the Bill which introduces an overriding qualification for
both Parliamentary and local government
franchises. I would refer hon. Members
of the House to Clause 4, which introduces the principle of three years' residence in the United Kingdom as being
a condition precedent to being registered
either as a Parliamentary or a Local
Government voter.
I should at the same time, however,
explain that this three years' residential
qualification will not apply in the case of
members of the Army, Navy and Air
Force, nor will it apply in the case of
persons born in Northern Ireland, or in
the case of those persons who are otherwise entitled to be registered by reason of
business premises qualification or as company representatives for local government elections-a matter to which I will
refer later on. It is well, perhaps, that
I should give some explanation as to the
reasons which have actuated the Government in introducing this principle into
the Bill.
Representations have been received for
many years past as to the practices which
have obtained in regard to the obtaining
of votes by persons who had no real interest
or standing in Northern Ireland. These
practices have been more apparent, perhaps, in the Border counties than elsewhere, but in these counties they have
been of very considerable proportions,
and it is obvious that owing to these practices the registers in these counties par-
589 Representation of People Bill- 27 NOVEMBER 1928
(90
Second Reading.
ticularly cannot be held to represent in
any way the ordinary residents and ratepayers in the areas concerned.
The Members of this House are aware
that there is a large floating population
of a transitory or migratory character
living from time to time in our Border
counties-persons with no real interest or
standing in the country and whose residence in Northern Ireland is entirely
based on temporary employment. In many
cases it has been stated that this so-called
temporary employment is secured merely
for the purpose of swelling the voters'
lists in one or other interests, and from
the information before the Government it
was apparent that drastic steps should
be taken to put a stop to these practices
and to ensure that our registers truly represent Northern Ireland feelings and interest. It will have been observed that
while the Government has introduced
this qualification in regard to residence and
occupation it has been careful to make
an exception in the case of persons
occupying business premises, such persons, undoubtedly, having substantial
interests in the country.
I will now turn to another new principle, which will be found in Clause 3 of
the Bill and which, in effect is designed
with the object of securing to our large
business and industrial concerns, not only
in our cities and towns but also throughout the country, an interest and voice in
local government administration. In
many of our cities and towns a considerable proportion of the rates is paid by the
companies occupying substantial premises
-whether factories, shops, stores, offices,
etc., and up to the present these organisations have had no voice whatsoever in the
expenditure of the rates paid by them.
This defect in our administration has
been brought under the notice of the
Government from time to time by various
trade and commercial organisations such
as the Chambers of Commerce and Chamber of Trade, and the Government is
satisfied that the representations are wellfounded and justifiable. Accordingly,
under Clause 3 of the Bill provision is
made for according to companies certain
voting powers at local government elections, and here I would remark that the
principle which has been adopted is on all
fours with the principle hitherto in operation of according votes to partners in
business concerns.
A company occupying premises with a
valuation of at least £10 will be entitled
to nominate one representative as a local
government elector in the particular area
in which the company is situated. Where
the valuation is much higher the company
will be entitled to nominate additional
representatives up to a maximum of six,
the number of representatives being determined by dividing the valuation by ten.
I feel sure that this new principle will
appeal generally to this House, and that
if passed into law it will have a steadying
and beneficial effect on our local government administration, and I would like to
reiterate once again in this connection
that the three years' residential qualification will not apply in the case of persons
nominated for the local government franchise by companies.
There is only one other feature in the
Bill to which I think attention should be
drawn, and that is in regard to the preparation and the use of the register. I am
glad to be able to say in this connection
that after consultation with the Imperial
Government authorities we have been able
to arrive at an arrangement under which
it will still be possible for us to use the
Imperial register for our local Parliamentary purposes, subject, of course, to such
slight changes and distinguishing features
as may be necessary to give effect to the
special provisions in this Bill to which I
have just referred. This is a fortunate
result for the Government and Parliament
as it is hoped that under it it will be possible for us to avoid having to incur any
additional expense in connection with the
preparation of the register.
Arrangements are being made in consultation with the appropriate officers concerned under which distinguishing marks
will be placed opposite the names of those
persons who though entitled to vote at
Imperial Parliamentary elections will not
be entitled to vote at our local Parliamentary elections owing to the operation of
the three years' residential qualification,
and similar arrangements will be made
for the case of peers who though entitled
to vote at our elections are still precluded
from voting at Imperial Parliamentary
elections. I hope with the above explanation of the various principles in the Bill,
and of the reasons underlying them, that
the House will see fit to accept them and
to accord to the Bill a Second Reading.
I beg to move.
591 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think that the
House will find itself in any difficulty in
acceding to the proposal which has just
been laid before it. The Bill is, if I may
say so, extremely clear. The subject,
perhaps, has its complications, but the Bill
is so drawn as to make it extremely easy,
assuming of course that anybody reading
it has some knowledge of the existing law
relating to representation of the people, or
that, if they have not that knowledge,
they do know where to find it, and if they
do not know where to find it they will find
in the Bill itself a kind of dictionary or
index of reference which will make it very
simple. I do not think it is necessary to
say anything about the assimilation of the
Parliamentary franchise of men and
women. I think we have all been agreed
for some years that it is what should be
done.
There are other points in the Bill which
have attracted more attention, perhaps,
and which have certainly given rise to
more discussion. One of them, perhaps,
I may call the Companies Franchise.
Clause, as to which the noble Viscount
has given an explanation which will appeal to almost everybody who has studied
that question. Complaint has been made.
by people interested in local government
that although they pay a very large proportion of rates they had no voice whatever when it came to the question of the
I do not
expenditure of those rates.
think that the new proposal can work any
injustice to anyone, and I will leave it at
that.
There remains this new question of the
three years' residence, and that is distinctly and admittedly, I think, a new
principle. Perhaps I may venture to give
an explanation which the noble Viscount
did not give. I am going to suggest that
the explanation for the introduction of
this proposal is that Northern Ireland is
not an island State. I think that is what
it comes to. I did consider very carefully
when I first got the Bill whether it was
reasonably possible that any injustice
could be worked to anyone by the new
proposal, and I did consider whether it
would not have been better to give United
Kingdom protection, so to speak, to domicile rather than to residence. However,
domicile is difficult of establishing, and it
has to be remembered that if you alter
residence to domicile you will probably
stand a chance of losing as many votes as
you will gain, since as it is possible to
have domicile without residence so also
is it possible to have residence without
domicile, and it is residence or occupation,
as the case may be, that has been accepted
in all modern legislation dealing with
representation. It is occupation or residence that has been taken to be the governing factor in deciding whether or not the
franchise should be given.
The reasons for the introduction of the
new principle have been explained by the
noble Viscount, and I need not repeat
them, but I will pass to something I
should have liked to have seen in the Bill
and which is not there. It might, I think,
be regarded as being within the compass
of the Bill as being in connection with the
representation of the people generally.
That is the case of the inhabitants of
Rathlin Island. We all know where
Rathlin Island is, and it appears from the
last census returns that the population of
that island is about 300. Of those 300
people some undoubtedly will be young
women who for the first time would be entitled to the franchise, but what are they
going to do with it? The problem of
Rathlin is that it is separated by some
miles of rather tempestuous water from the
mainland, and if an election takes place
in the winter, as it very frequently does,
it is not reasonably practicable for the
people of Rathlin to come ashore and
record their votes, which they ought to
do, in the town of Ballycastle.
Complaint was made on their behalf
some five or six years ago when I happened to be a member of the County
Council for the constituency of which
Rathlin Island formed part.
The question was what could be done in the way
of readjusting the polling stations, and
the case was at once put up that Rathlin
Island should have a polling station on
the island. That would meet their difficulty, but here at once objection was
made by the returning officers that if a
voter could not go to the mainland to
vote neither could the ballot boxes come
to the mainland to be counted, and it
would not be fair to hold up the election
for the receipt of the last ballot box or
possibly to invalidate the election by the
loss of that ballot box, so nothing was
done. The only way out would seem to
be to make the inhabitants of Rathlin
Island something in the nature of absent
voters, to give them, as might be possible, the same privileges as are given to
men afloat or absent for business reasons.
593 Representation of People Bill- 27 NOVEMBER 1928
Second Reading.

Imperial House of Parliament, the Secretary of State for the Home Department
said that that Measure would give the
people of Great Britain a greater and a
freer parliamentary representation than
any other democratic country in the
world had, and now that we are adopting
a Measure of a similar kind I say that we
here in Northern Ireland are giving the
people of the Province a greater
and freer parliamentary representation
than any other democratic country in
the world has with the exception
of Great Britain. I might say that
while I am whole-heartedly in favour
of political equality of the sexes
by the extension of the franchise to
women, which will remove any injustice
which women might suffer under the
existing law, I have at the same time a
feeling that 21 years of age is rather
young-
There is the possibility, as the House
knows, under the 1918 Act of dealing
with that matter, but it did seem to me
that this was a proper opportunity for
doing it, and I was under the limitation
of a definite promise which I gave to the
Chairman of the Ballycastle Rural District Council at the time I am speaking
of that I would draw attention to the
case of Rathlin on the first parliamentary opportunity. Having regard to the
time of year I do not think it would be
advisable to put any Amendment definitely on the Order Paper in regard to
this matter. I think it would be better
to leave it simply as a matter brought before the Government on the occasion of
the Second Reading, and to let it stand
over until time might better serve for
dealing with the subject. It is obvious
that the matter, although it might be
said to belong to this Bill, would belong
equally to any other Bill dealing with
constituencies or franchise.
I repeat that, in my judgment, the
whole Bill is one which will commend
itself to the people of Northern Ireland,
that is those people in Northern Ireland
who desire that our parliamentary system throughout should be representative,
up-to-date and convenient in management. I am sure the Measure will appeal
to such people, and, therefore, it ought
to be accepted by the House, and I would
venture to urge that having regard to the
fact that the existing register will be in
force on 15th December and that the
last day of the qualifying period for the
new register, which is to come into force
on 1st May next, is 1st December, next
week, we should, if possible, take the
Committee stage to-morrow. I do not believe there will be any Amendments put
down for the Committee stage. If it
would suit the programme of the Government, I think we should take the Committee stage of this Bill to-morrow and
get through the Third Reading as speedily
as possible.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to support
the Second Reading of this Bill as I have
been an advocate of votes for women for
a great number of years. Some years ago
when the agitation was going on I was
in Manchester and I supported this
policy, and so I am very pleased to-day
to be here to give my support to the
Second Reading of this Bill. When a
Bill similar to this Bill was before the
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Hear, hear.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would prefer the
age of 25 for both males and females, so
that they would have a little more experience of this work and would be in a
better position to choose the proper men to
represent them on the local boards or in
either the Northern Ireland Parliament
or the Imperial Parliament. While I do
not cast any reflection upon the young
people of 21-I know a great number of
those young people have a great deal of
thought and a great amount of intelligence. But there are others, both male
and female, who have as a rule very little
thought. They have been depending
upon their parents up to that time.
Their parents have found them in food
and clothing and have been responsible
for everything.
The young people, of course, are cut for
nothing but sport and pleasure, and so
on, but at the age of 25 a great number
of those people who were thoughtless at 21 will perhaps be married and
settled down in homes of their own. They
will then realise their responsibilities as
home keepers. Consequently I would
favour 25 years as the age more than 21,
but we have no alternative to granting
the young women the vote at 21 seeing
we have an Act upon the Statute Book
which gives votes to young men at 21.
Therefore, if we are going to give equality
it is necessary that we should pass this
Bill as it stands.
595 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Barclay]
There are several objections to women
having votes. Some people give as the
reason that the father or the head of the
house is responsible for the actions of the
family up to a certain age.
He is responsible for the happiness and the comfort of
the home. They also say that man has
got to do all the hard work. Another
argument is that in case of war the men
have to go and do all the fighting. I
suppose that some of the people who put
up an argument like that are afraid that
if women got the majority, as they will,
in case of war they might pass a Conscription Act and have all the men sent to the
front with the probability that they would
never come back. Whether this is the
case or not I do not know, but it is one
of the arguments advanced.
Then there is another argument put
forward, that is, that a husband is responsible for his wife's income tax although he has no right to her income or
to know what her income is. Whether
that is the case or not I do not know.
Fortunately, or unfortunately, I am not
in a position to say so, but perhaps there
are some hon. Members here this afternoon who are. If we let our minds go
back for a short time over the past few
years, especially to the days of the war,
what do we find? We find that the women
were just as patriotic and as intelligent
as the men, and that as far as the war was
concerned they took their place the same
as the men. When war broke out they
came forward in their thousands and went
out to the different battlefields to look
after the sick and the wounded and to
care for the dying. Others entered our
workshops to manufacture munitions,
while others organized themselves to provide comforts which were very acceptable
to the men in those dark, cold, winter
days.
When the Germans were putting our
ships loaded with foodstuffs to the bottom
of the sea with their submarines the
women came forward again and went upon
the land. They assisted the farmers to
produce food for the people, and they
were very successful in doing so. Those
girls are known to-day as land girls.
Therefore, when we look back over what
the women did during the war we have to
come to the conclusion that woman in the
past has taken her share in all the great
affairs of the nation and has proved as
useful in winning our battles as the man
and has made just as great a sacrifice.
Then others raised the objection that
if women get the vote they will be very
selfish and one-sided and will support
one another. History has proved that
during the past woman has always taken
her proper place. She has always allowed
man to take the lead. Therefore, I am
not the least bit afraid that when woman
does get the vote those people who are
fearing the things I have mentioned will
find out that woman will again take her
proper place. Someone said some time
ago that we might live to see the day
when this country of ours would be ruled
by short skirts and silk stockings. Whether
that will be the case or not I do not know.
Another gentleman in England has said
that we might live to see an ex-housemaid
becoming Prime Minister of England.
All I have got to say about the ex-housemaid is that if she had the capabilities
and the qualifications to be Prime Minister
of England good luck to her.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Hear, hear.
Mr. BARCLAY: There is another
thing I would like to mention and which
has been referred to by the noble Leader
of the House, that is the protection given
to the larger ratepayer. This is a thing
that has been upon my mind for a considerable time, and I have mentioned it
on one or two occasions. Sometimes I
got laughed at, and other times I was told
it was impossible. I was conscious that
the larger ratepayer was not getting a fair
crack of the whip. He is called upon to
pay a large amount every year for the
purpose of carrying on the business of the
nation, but has no voice as to how this
money is going to be spent. As was said
in this House the other day, the man who
pays the piper should have a voice in
choosing the tune. I think that a ratepayer who is called upon to pay hundreds,
and perhaps thousands, of pounds in the
year in rates should be in a position to
say something as to how this money is to
be spent. It is not right that we should
take money out of a man's pockets and
put it into the hands of other people who
never had money and leave it to them to
spend. No man can spend money as well
as a man who can earn money, and no man
is in a position to spend rightly but the
man who has made money through his own
energy and business ability. That man
is in a position to spend his own money as
well as that of other people.
597 Representation of People Bill 27 NOVEMBER 1928
Housing Bill

Message from the Commons.
The Commons have agreed to the
Boroughs Funds Bills (S) without Amendment. They have passed the Marketing
of Eggs Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
There is also the three years' residence
in Northern Ireland. I agree that it has
been fully explained here this afternoon.
Then there is another question which
arose a short time ago, that is the question
of personation. There seems to be a
great deal of trouble about this personating and a good deal has been said about
it. Some people have been asking for a
remedy. The only remedy I know is
compulsory voting. Australia has
adopted it and it has been a success
there. Every man and woman who is in
a position to vote and does not do so is
fined £10. If we had compulsory voting
here in Northern Ireland it would be a
great benefit. The Chancellor of the Exchequer might benefit by it, and, of
course, that would be a good thing. However, I support the Bill whole-heartedly,
and I am very pleased I have lived to
see the day women get votes.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am very
pleased with the reception the Bill has
received from Members of this House.
There is very little for me to reply to on
the points raised except that in reference
to the unhappy state of Rathlin Island.
I can only promise to bring this to the
notice of the Government. I cannot promise any remedy. I see very plainly the
possibility that the islanders cannot very
well come to the mainland, while if the
returning officer went to the island he
might very well become a non-returning
officer. (Laughter). I hope that something in the way of aeroplanes or some
other scheme will be brought into operation.
voters
In regard to what Senator Barclay
said about the age for women
being 25 years, it would obviously be impossible to make a distinction between
the age at which men and women qualified. I must say that, as a man, I view
with great approbation what he said.
about women-
-that when they came to
25 and married they would think less
about sport and care more about the
country and that their association with
their husbands would possibly make them
more sensible. I rather like that idea.
At the same time, as he will see, I cannot
do anything with regard to ages. For
the rest I thank the House for their reception of the Bill.

THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 28th November, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Representation of the People Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSES 1. (Assimilation of parliamentary franchise of men and women;
and 2 (Assimilation of local government
franchise of men and women), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Local Government franchise (companies)).
Mr. LESLIE: There has been so much
misrepresentation outside as regards the
effect of the Clause now before us-misrepresentation which it is hard to believe
can be based on an actual study of the
wording of the Bill and of this Section.
The Section is as plain as could be desired, but for the sake of those who will
never see the actual Act of Parliament
itself, and who may be misled by garbled
versions which they may read in the reports of speeches, I should like to draw
special attention to the fact that this
Clause 3 is definitely and expressly limited
to purposes of local government. It does
not in any way affect the Parliamentary
franchise. The whole object is to enfranchise, so to speak, companies in the
persons of their directors for the purposes
of local government, and for no
other purpose.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Amendment of ss 8 and
9 of principal Act), 5 (Consequential
amendments), 6 (Register and registration of electors), and 7 (Short title and
construction) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Schedule agreed to.

without Amendment to the House.

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 29th November, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
ANTRIM ELECTRICITY SPECIAL
ORDER.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, "That the Special
Order made by the Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland under the
Electricity (Supply) Acts, 1882 to 1919,
and confirmed by the Ministry of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply) Act,
1919, in respect of the Town of Antrim
and parts of the Rural District of Antrim,
in the County of Antrim, be approved."
This Order was granted by the Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce. It confers on the Antrim
Light and Power Company, Limited,
statutory authority to supply electrical
energy for public and private purposes in
the town of Antrim and parts of the rural
district of Antrim. The Rules of Procedure have been complied with, and the
Order, which follows the usual course, is
not opposed.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
TEXTILE TRADE AND THE LOANS
GUARANTEE FUND.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
McDowell:
To ask the Leader of the House how far
the textile trade has availed itself of the
Loans Guarantee Fund, and whether the
Government are making any efforts to help
the trade to recover its lost markets.
Mr. McDOWELL: In rising to ask
the question which stands in my name I

would like to crave the indulgence of the
House for a few moments. I am very
much interested, and have been all my
life, in the textile trade of Northern Ireland, and, as we all know, it is at the
moment in a very deplorable state. I do
not think that at any other time in our
history has the trade been in such a bad
state as it is in at the present time. Before the War the city of Belfast was blessed
with, perhaps, the four greatest industries
in the world, namely, shipbuilding, ropeworks, tobacco factories, and the textile
trade. I think we will all agree that those
industries were principally dependent on
the markets of the world. If we go still
furthur back, so far as the textile trade is
concerned, we will find that there was a
bigger home market than there was in
1914, because I remember the time in my
younger days when the very poor man, no
matter how poor he was, had a linen shirt
to put on to go to kirk on Sunday. Every
woman also had her linen aprons, and I
think all her underclothing was made of
linen. I admit that they do not wear
much now. I venture to say that even we
who are sitting in this House do not appreciate the extent to which the home market
of the linen trade has fallen away, and
probably very few of us
a linen
collar. They may be faced with a sort of
linen, but there are very few of us who
wear a linen shirt.
wear
In 1914 the textile trade was principally
dependent on the markets of the world
and then when the Great War came on
three of these four great industries were
absorbed in trying to help the Government to end the War as soon as possible.
The shipyards, the foundries, and the textile mills were practically taken over by
the Government. Consequently our markets were neglected and foreign countries
which for years previously had been buying our goods were able to manufacture
them for themselves and they thus took
away our foreign trade. During the War
we lost practically all these foreign markets, and during all this time these trades
had been under the control of the Government. A few months after the termination of the War the Government threw
these particular trades back on their own
resources and left them in a state of chaos
and disorder. Ever since that time they
have been fighting to retrieve their lost
position in the markets of the world.
When, subsequently, the Northern
Government was set up they were faced
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
29 NOVEMBER 1928
with two great problems. The first was
what practically amounted to a revolution
in the country, and the second was the
almost complete disorganisation of the
trade of the Province. At this time the
city of Belfast was probably in a worse
position than any other part of the United
Kingdom. The Northern Government
overcame the first difficulty in a comparatively short time, and they have since been
trying to the best of their ability to solve
the other one. The Loans Guarantee Act
was passed with the object of trying to
assist our industries to recover the position
they had lost, and I think it will be agreed
that had it not been for this Act the
Belfast shipyards would not be in the
flourishing position they are in to-day.
I have been wondering whether the
textile trade has availed itself of the
benefits of this Act to any extent. I
know the Government have done all they
could to assist the trade to advertise their
goods in the United States of America,
but I have been wondering whether it
would be possible for the Government to
come to the assistance of the textile trade
in the same way that they have assisted
the shipyards. The Government have
done all in their power to help the unemployed to obtain work by starting relief
schemes and by other means, and I am
very anxious to know whether the textile trade has taken advantage of the
Loans Guarantee Act, or whether it can
be enabled to do so. I do not wish to
detain the House at any great length,
but I should be very glad to know if the
Government could come to the assistance
of the textile trade through the medium
of the Loans Guarantee Act.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The answer to the first part
of the question is as follows: Two
guarantees in respect of loans aggregating
£50,000 have been issued in connection
with the textile trade, viz., one of
£40,000 to Sunsheen Ltd., and one of
£10,000 to Messrs. John Dinsmore & Co.,
of Kells, County Antrim. In the first case,
the Government guarantee, together with
a guarantee by the British Government
of £85,000, has enabled Sunsheen Ltd. to
be established. In the second case, the
guarantee of £10,000 enabled what had
formerly been a flourishing woollen
factory to be re-established. Generally,
it may be taken that the position of the
linen industry has been such as to render
the facilities provided by the Loans
Guarantee Acts of little avail under present circumstances. In this connection,
I would remind the hon. Member that
to qualify for a guarantee the loan must
be one which is to be applied towards a
capital undertaking, and it is obvious.
that the present is not a suitable time for
sinking large sums of money in the extension of premises or the replacement of
plant used in connection with the linen
industry.
In reply to the second part of the question, there are three ways in which the
linen industry is assisted by the Government. Firstly, in regard to publicity.
During the past two years the Northern
Government has contributed approximately £18,000 towards the campaign
organised by the linen industry for the
advertising of damasks in the United
States of America. The contribution was
made on the basis of a Government grant
of 10s for every £ contributed by traders
in Northern Ireland during each of the
years 1926 and 1927, subject to a maximum contribution of £10,000 in each
year.
The Government has now agreed to
make a further contribution towards an
extended advertising campaign. The
original campaign was confined to the
United States of America, the outstanding overseas market for linen goods, and,
moreover, was concerned only with the advertising of damasks. The new campaign will, however, cover all linens and
will be carried out not only in the United
States of America but in the home market and in the Dominions. The Government contribution this year is to be at the
rate of 15s for every £ raised by the
trade, subject to a maximum payment
of £17,500. The Irish Linen Guild has
been formed for the purpose of carrying
out this extended campaign and
couraging support to the new project has
been forthcoming from the trade.
en-
Secondly, in regard to research, the
Northern Government has made grants
to the Linen Industry Research Association amounting to £20,000 during the
past six years. Thirdly, in regard to
seed bulking, in connection with the
bulking of pedigree flax seed in Somerset,
the Northern Government has paid
£10,000 towards the financing of the
Flax Industry Development Society, Ltd.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
during the past three years. The object
of the Somerset scheme is to increase the
supply of seed giving a high yield of
fibre in order to promote the production
of flax within the Empire, including Northern Ireland itself. Up to the present,
appreciable quantities of pedigree seed
have been shipped to Canada and New
Zealand for further bulking, while experimental quantities have been sent to
several other parts of the Empire.
Mr. DORMAN: I should like to
see that this
say that I regret to
staple industry of Northern Ireland has
suffered so much during the post-war
period, but at the same time it is not surprising to me that the industries the hon.
Member (Senator McDowell) has referred
to have suffered as they have done since
the end of the War. I remember when
the War broke out speaking to some of
my fellow-workers in the shipyard and
pointing out to them that the inevitable
economic result of this War, as of all wars,
and especially wars on such a large scale
as the late War, would be that Northern
Ireland would probably be hit harder than
any other part of the Commonwealth of
Nations because our principle industries
were dependent on a world market and
not on the demand of the home market.
It is a fact that during the post-war period
the capitalists of Great Britain and Northern Ireland pursued a peculiar economic
policy in regard to the industries of the
country. The competing capitalists in the
United States were pursuing a similar
policy, and at that time, in 1922, America
was in the same hazardous position as regarded her industries and workers as Great
Britain and Northern Ireland were in.
But a few public-spirited men in the
United States, led mainly by Mr. Henry
Ford, pointed out to their fellow capitalists
that the policy they were initiating and
were about to pursue would be disastrous
to their then declining industries, inasmuch as that policy was calculated to reduce the purchasing capacity of the mass
of the customers in the vicinity of the industry concerned. Mr. Henry Ford, on
account of the eminent industrial position
he occupied, and of his vast wealth, got
the ear of the Press of his country. Everyone knows that without the aid of the
Press it is futile for an individual capitalist
or for a small body of capitalists, no matter
how public spirited they may be, to attempt to do anything to revive languishing industries of the area in which they
live and in which they may take a deep
and intelligent interest, though they may
not be able to command the position that
men of eminence and property are able to
do. What was the result? In the United
States of America-
