Marketing of Eggs Bill, 1928.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill now before the House
is presented in fulfilment of an undertaking given by the Government to promote
legislation parallel to certain provisions
relating to the marking of preserved eggs,
embodied in the Agricultural Produce
(Grading and Marking) Act, 1928, which
was passed in the Imperial House and received the Royal Assent in August last.
The provisions in question require that
all preserved eggs of whatever origin and
all home produced cold stored and chemically stored eggs must be marked on the
shell with an indication of the process to
which they have been subjected. In the
case of cold stored and chemically stored
eggs the requirement as to marking will
continue in force so long as an Order in
Council under the Merchandise Marks
Act, 1926, prescribing the marking with
an indication of origin of imported eggs,
is in force.
To appreciate the importance of this Bill
it is necessary to examine briefly the relation it bears to the Imperial Act and
to the Order in Council under the Merchandise Marks Act, to which reference
has already been made. As hon. Members
are fully aware, the British market is
supplied with eggs from many countries,
but, as is natural, the highest prices are
returned for the best classes of home produced eggs.
Such being the case it is to be
expected that, while no legal obstacle supervenes, traders in Great Britain will endeavour to sell as many eggs as possible
under this description with not too great
a regard to the truth as to the origin of
the commodity.
The agricultural authorities in Great
Britain could not but view this state of
affairs with concern, particularly while the
organisation of the home trade was so ineffective as to result in the substantial part
of the home supplies being inferior in
quality to much of the imported com-
611 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
modity. Remedial measures, of which
the present Bills forms part, have been inaugurated. To ensure that the home product shall not fraudulently be deprived of
the market to which, by its natural advantages, it is entitled, the aid of the Merchandise Marks Act of 1926 has been
invoked. From early next year all eggs,
other than eggs produced in Great Britain
and Northern Ireland, will be marked on
the shell with an indication of origin.
For the improvement of the industry in
Great Britain a grading and branding
scheme has been launched under the Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking)
Act. That scheme is incomplete without
provision that preserved eggs, whether of
British or Northern Ireland origin, shall
not be sold as fresh eggs.
The Bill now
before the Senate is, therefore, an essential
unit in the structure which is being raised
to foster the growth of the poultry industry
in Great Britain and Northern Ireland.
In 1927 a Committee set up in Great
Britain under the Merchandise Marks
Act, 1926, made some comments regarding
preserved eggs, including the following:-
"Attention might be given also to the
problem presented by preserved and
cold-stored eggs. It has been suggested
to us, in the course of our inquiry, that
the need of distinguishing between preserved and fresh eggs is not less than
that of differentiating imported from
home-produced eggs and that if a
Marking Order were made there would,
in the absence of such a precaution, be a
danger of English preserved eggs being
sold as 'new-laid' and so damaging the
home egg trade. This matter is, we
think, not strictly within our province,
but we can well believe that the home
egg trade would be prejudiced by preserved eggs, whether of home or foreign
origin, being passed off as 'English new
laid.'
The Committee reported, accordingly,
Meanwhile
on the 14th October, 1927.
the English Ministry of Agriculture and
Fisheries had been investigating the practicability of improving the poultry industry at home, and subsequent to the
issue of the Report already mentioned a
Bill was introduced in the Imperial Parliament making provision for the grading and
marking of agricultural produce generally, and dealing specially with the marking of eggs other than home-produced
fresh eggs.
This Bill is now the Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act,
1928. In the Report of the Standing
Committee it is stated: :-
"It is observed that the Agricultural
Produce (Grading and Marking) Bill
does not extend to Northern Ireland.
We understand, however, that arrangements are being made to secure that
eggs produced in Northern Ireland and
cold stored in that country may not be
sold in Great Britain unless suitably
marked."
This Bill now before the House undertakes that those arrangements shall be
carried out. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill. If there is
any section of the community that is injured by the false description of foodstuffs and the adulteration of foodstuffs
it is the masses of the working classes of
this and every other community. I have
a wide knowledge of the distribution of
products all over Ireland and for nearly
half a century I have taken a considerable
interest in the distribution of these particular products and in the prices that
have been paid for them. I can assure
the House that in every part of Ireland
inferior goods of foreign manufacture
have been distributed greatly to the detriment of the superior products of our
own manufactories. I discovered as long
as thirty years ago that in this Northern
area inferior American bacon was offered
for sale in provision merchants' shops and
was sold as Limerick bacon, a bacon that
has the highest reputation in the world.
I know that similar misrepresentation with
regard to that product has taken place in
Munster, Leinster and Connaught, and
that working people in those large centres
of distribution very often have palmed off
on them goods of foreign manufacture
which are described as of Irish manufacture. They take them home and consume them under the false impression
that they are the products of our own
country. Every step the Government
will take for the purpose of developing
the resources of our own country will
now and at all times have my sincere
and whole-hearted support. I think this
Measure will be calculated to safeguard
many consumers in Belfast and other
parts of Northern Ireland, and I, therefore, give it my cordial support.
€13 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

29 NOVEMBER 1928
Second Reading
certain
Mr. LONG: There was a
amount of dissatisfaction amongst those
engaged in the egg industry with the
Acts already passed in regard to the marketing of eggs. The feeling prevailed
that these Acts were uncalled for and
were unnecessary and that compliance
with their provisions would be irksome.
In practice, however, it has been found
that no great difficulty was experienced
and that the great majority of the former
objectors have now realised that their
fears were groundless and that the proud
position now held by Irish eggs in the
British market is due, in a large
measure, to the operation of the Acts to
which I have referred. That is a fact
which cannot be gainsaid, and I am sure it
will be a great gratification to the Ministry of Agriculture and those associated
with them to know that this is so.
present Bill is a necessary corollary to
the present Acts, and I am assured by
those who know that this is a fair and
reasonable way of dealing with a matter
that required legislative attention.
therefore cordially support the Second
reading of the Bill.
The
I
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am one of those who have reason to know
the great advantage the former Marketing
of Eggs Act has been to farmers of Northern Ireland. I think we all agree that the
egg trade is one of the principal ways in
which the farmer's wife can help him in
these difficult times. I have not the
original Act before me, but I notice that
in the Bill now before the House no penalties are provided. I suppose the penalties
intended to apply are those in the preceding Act.
A rather extraordinary thing came
under my own observation the other day.
When a preserved egg was opened it was
found to contain not only the preserved
egg but also an embryo chicken. I would
like to know what I ought to have done
under those circumstances, because that is
the kind of thing we ought to stop as it
spoils our egg market in England. I do
not know where the egg came from, but in
addition to a preserved egg it contained a
preserved chicken.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to give my
support to the Second Reading of this Bill.
The Bills previously passed in this House
have been of great benefit, not only to the
producer but to the consumer. I am not
a producer of eggs myself, but I am a consumer, and I have noticed that since the
Government took steps to look after the
bad
egg industry I have not seen so many
eggs coming into our house. Previously a
great number of eggs came into the house
that were not fit for human consumption.
I am very glad to see that under this Bill
eggs will in future have to bear the mark
of their country of origin.
I understand from those who know something about the ways of the egg merchants
and the methods they have of packing or
repacking eggs that it is difficult for the
Government to exercise supervision over
all merchants. I have been told by a person who has been engaged in this business
in Glasgow how Irish eggs have been sold
out of boxes and afterwards the same boxes
were refilled with foreign eggs of a cheaper
class and sold as Irish eggs.
I would like
to know how the Government is going to
take steps to prevent that practice. Such
a practice can be carried out in the back
store where there are not many people
going about and then the eggs can be
brought into the shop or set down on the
footpath outside the shop marked "Irish
eggs." How can we distinguish an Irish
egg from a foreign egg? I think it is almost impossible to do so.
With regard to what the hon. and
gallant Senator (Colonel Sharman-Crawford) said about finding a chicken in a preserved egg, I can understand how that
happened. It is only in certain seasons of
the
year that such a thing can take place.
I do not know whether the farmer or
We
farmer's wife is altogether to blame.
know that at a certain season of the year
hens wander away and make a nest in a
hedge or ditch where they lay their eggs
and sit on them for a time. The result is
that life begins in those eggs, and instead
of the farmer destroying them he may put
them on the market-perhaps because he
is in need of money. I would like to know
how an inspector is to detect such an egg.
I think this Bill will further improve the
position of the egg trade, and I expect that
as a result of it we who are consumers in
the City of Belfast will get even better
value for our money in the future than we
have got in the past.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: There is very little for me to
say in reply to the debate. I am glad
to know that the House approves of this
Adjournment.
Business of the House
THE SENATE
up
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Measure. The hon. and gallant Senator
(Colonel Sharman Crawford) raised the
question of penalties. I should like to
point out to him that Clause 4 of this Bill
connects with the previous Act of 1926
so far as concerns penalties. As regards
the conundrum about the preserved
chicken in the preserved egg, I cannot
answer him at the moment and I
am not sure that I will be able to supply
him with the answer, but I will endeavour
to do so. With regard to Senator Barclay's point about the placing of eggs that
are not Irish into Irish boxes, I would
point out that in future when this Bill
becomes an Act all imported eggs will be
individually marked on the shell so that
such a practice as he referred to cannot be
carried out in future. I am very glad the
House has given such a cordial reception
to this Bill.
On Question, Motion (That this Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
We know that the Session is nearing its
end, in fact, I have been told that so far
as the other House is concerned the Session will end on Tuesday next. There is
a very important Measure that I believe
is to come to this House before the end
of the present Session. I refer to the Derating Bill. I would like to ask the
noble Viscount whether it is his intention
to ask for the suspension of Standing
Orders in order to take the First and
Second Readings of that Bill on the same
day. If that is the intention I am afraid
I shall have to oppose it, because it puts
Members like myself who may have
Amendments to move in a peculiar position. If the First and Second Readings
are taken on the same day without previous notice being given it makes it difficult for hon. Senators to put down
Amendments.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have not
got the Bill yet so that I cannot say what
I will do. However, if a question arose
as to the advantage of suspending Standing Orders I would have to give my
reasons for such a Motion, and it would
be for the House to approve or disapprove. I cannot suspend
suspend Standing
Orders of my own volition.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
do not suppose there is anyone in this
House, or in any other House, who knows
exactly how the Derating Bill will work
out, and if the Second Reading is taken
on the same day as the First Reading it
may debar some of us from moving
Amendments in Committee.
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I understand the Bill that is coming forward is
a Bill of definition and not a Bill that
will suddenly bring into operation the
Derating scheme. It is a Bill that defines such matters as what "hereditament, "tenure," and "fee farm" really
mean so as to have such things properly
defined when the principal Derating Bill
comes along. Therefore, as the Bill does
not propose to do anything of importance
the Second Reading speech will be merely
an introductory one. The question of
the suspension of the Standing Order is
a matter for the convenience of the
Senate. I do not wish to rush hon.
Senators.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: If
it is the fact that the First and Second
Readings will amount to nothing then I
will be quite satisfied that they should be
taken on the same day. All I want to be

and gallant Member from moving any

Slaughtered Animals (Compensation)
Bill [S.].
Order for consideration of Commons'
Amendments read.
Moved, "That the House do now take
into consideration the Amendments made
by the Commons to this Bill."—(Viscount
Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Commons Amendment:
Clause 2, page 2, line 7, leave out from
the beginning of the line to the end of
line 9, and insert "such organisation, consisting of representatives of the bodies.
now known as "The North of Ireland
Cattle Traders' and Stock Owners' Association' and 'The Londonderry and North-
West Cattle Dealers' and Shippers' Ass
SSOciation' as may be formed by those bodies
and recognised by the Ministry of Agriculture for the purposes of this Act; and
such organisation as aforesaid is in this
Act referred to as 'The Association'.”
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving,
"That this House do agree with the Commons in the said Amendment," I should
like to explain the reason for it. When
this Bill was discussed in draft with the
North of Ireland Cattle Traders' Association and the Londonderry and North-
West Cattle Dealers' Association both
bodies agreed to form for the purpose of
working the Measure a joint Association
to be known as "The Northern Ireland
Live Stock Exporters' Association." The
intention was that this joint Association
would have been set up immediately after
the discussion. This joint body has, however, not yet been formed because one of
the Associations has been pressing to have
the Bill remodelled on a wider basis. The
other Association has notified that it is
ready to come in and has appointed the
necessary representatives. The object of
the Amendment is to make clear that the
joint body may still be formed by the two
Associations concerned.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Commons Amendment:
Clause 11, page 8, line 26, after "section" insert "and subject to any directions
which may be given by the British
Authorities."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do agree with
the Commons in the said Amendment,"
The reason for this Amendment is that
it is in accordance with a suggestion
which was made by the British Ministry
of Agriculture and Fisheries. They
point out that the disposal of carcases of
animals slaughtered on account of foot
and mouth disease in British lairages
must be a matter for direction by the
British Ministry, and that there may be
instances in which the salvage of carcases could not be permitted by that
Ministry on account of the risk involved
of spreading infection. I think it is a
perfectly reasonable view and correctly
represents the existing position.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, "That this House do agree with

This Amendment is really consequential
on the first. It is to provide that if a
joint Association is not constituted as
contemplated arrangements may still be
made, if considered desirable, for working
the Measure. As I have already explained, the original intention was that
the joint Association would have been
formed before now.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House

Finance (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill falls into two parts,
the first consisting of Clauses 1, 2 and
3 and dealing with the question of
bankers' licences and stamp duties, while
the second part, consisting of Clauses 4,
5 and 6, concerns the entertainments
duty.
In the first part it is proposed to make
a change in the existing arrangements.
In place of the system at present
whereby banks pay a licence duty for the
privilege of issuing unstamped notes, bills
of exchange, etc., or in the case of those
banks which have no note issue, a special
duty in lieu of this licence duty in return
for the privilege of issuing other unstamped paper, there will now be a
general bankers' licence duty payable by
all institutions which carry on the business of banking in Northern Ireland.
The actual amount of the duty which will
fall on the bank has not, in effect, been
altered; the annual stamp duty payable
on a single banker's licence under this
Bill will be £30.
Clause 2 gives the conditions for the
issue of this licence and it will be noticed
that under Sub-Section 3 of this Clause
a maximum of four such licences, that is,
a total payment of £120, will cover all the
branches of any of the banks in Northern Ireland. This arrangement is also
similar to that already in force. It will
also be seen under Sub-Section (5) of
Clause 2 that the position of the Bank of
Ireland is being regularized. This bank
will now fall into line with the others in
the matter of payment of licence duty.
Hitherto, by virtue of an old Act, this
Bank was exempt from all licence duty,
but there is now no reason why it should
be different to the other banking institutions. I may say in this connection
that the Bank of Ireland has concurred
in this change; and, indeed, all the provisions of this part of the Bill have the
consent of the banks concerned.
Sub-section (6) of Clause 2 gives the
Ministry of Finance power to dispense
with the giving of bonds by banks as a
security for the proper performance of
the conditions imposed on them. It is
now not thought necessary to insist on
bonds in the case of banks of such standing as we have in Northern Ireland and,
accordingly, it is thought right to have
power to dispense with this requirement.
Clause 3 of the Bill clarifies the position
in regard to the payment by banks of a
composition for stamp duties on bills of
exchange and bank post bills. There is no
real change proposed in the existing law;
it is only re-enacted in simpler form in
this Bill.
The second part of this Bill, which deals
with the entertainments duty, will, I
think, be fairly clear to hon. Members.
Clause 4 exempts horse races from the
duty in accordance with the promise given
some short time ago by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, in
another place. The reason for the concession is that it is believed that this sport
is doing, and can do, a good deal to help
farmers who are concerned with the horsebreeding industry. Exemption from entertainments duty will do something to
help this sport and, accordingly, the
farmers will eventually receive some benefit. The loss to the revenue will not be
very important.
Clause 5 disposes of two matters.
Firstly, Sub-clause (a) definitely includes
in the list of entertainments which are
exempt from tax any gymnastic or ath-
Finance Bill-
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
letic displays. Such displays as those
promoted by boy scouts and other similar
organizations will in future be clearly
exempt. Sub-clause (b) definitely relegates boxing to the class of sport "ordinarily contested indoors," which will mean
that exemption will only be granted in
this case where there is no prize money or
remuneration involved.
One further matter remains, namely, an
extension of the conditions for repayment
of entertainments duty in cases of entertainments which are promoted for philanthropic or charitable purposes. Clause 6
of the Bill gives additional exemption in
such cases where the expenses of promoting the entertainment are under £10, and
I think that hon. Members will agree that
this is a reasonable proposal. Perhaps
I might give the House an example to explain how this proposal will work. Assume there is a fete organised by some
Church or charitable institution and that
it is on a small scale. Those who have
promoted it expect to realise £60 or £100,
but for some reason or other, probably on
account of unpropituous weather, the receipts amount to between only £20 or £30.
The expenses of the entertainment do not
come to more than £10. Under the old
law, as £10 is more than one-third of the
total receipts, the promoters would have
to pay entertainment duty, but under
this Bill they will be exempt. I hope that
this explanation of the various matters in
the Bill will prove sufficient for hon.
Members to understand a Measure that is
somewhat complicated.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Royal Assent.
I am

directed by the Prime Minister on his
behalf to acquaint the House that his
Grace the Governor, in the name of, and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bill:-
Representation of the People.

and
CLAUSE 1 (Preserving, cold
chemical storage and pickling of eggs.)
of
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think that perhaps this would
be a convenient opportunity for me to
reply to a question raised by the hon.
and gallant Senator opposite (Colonel
Sharman Crawford) in connection with a
somewhat extraordinary occurrence
which he acquainted the House, namely,
the finding of a preserved chicken in a
preserved egg. The sale of preserved
eggs like those referred to would, I understand, render the vendor liable to prosecution under the Sale of Food and
Drugs Act for delivering an article not
of the nature and substance or quality
demanded.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not know
if I am in order-I have not put down
any Amendment-but I should like to
ask the noble Viscount if those preserved eggs in bottles that are imported
into this country from China are included
in this Bill. I understand that hundreds
of tons of bottled yolks of eggs from China
come into this country and go principally
to the bakeries where they are used in
the making of pastry and other similar
things. I would like to know if there is
anything in this Bill to prevent those
eggs being sold to the public either as
fresh or preserved eggs, and, if not,
whether it is possible to have them
included in the Bill. I see that by line
25 of this Clause the Bill refers only to
eggs with the shell on, but surely there
is a tremendous quantity of eggs imported into this country without the
shells on. They may be useful for making pastry or for chemical purposes, such
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committ
4 DECEMBER 1928
as making Beecham's Pills and things of
that kind, but they are useless for eating
and I would like to know whether it
would be possible to have them included
in this Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
had intended to raise that question myself. I am aware that there are tons of
preserved Chinese eggs imported into this
country. I was in Siberia for some little
time and one of the things the Russians
used to give one was an egg
laid six
months before.
Mr. LESLIE: On the general question
of the importation of Chinese eggs, this
Bill is for the marketing of eggs with the
special intention of improving the mar
keting of eggs produced in Northern Ireland and protecting those eggs from competition in the way of chemically stored
eggs and eggs of that class.
I do not
think it was ever intended that this
Measure should extend to eggs imported
in the manner indicated by hon.
Senators.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It
is a great pity it was not.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it would
be appropriate under the Title.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am aware
that the Merchandise Marks Act may
cover this and that the Public Health
Acts also cover it, but this Bill deals
may
with preserved eggs, and surely these
Chinese eggs are preserved eggs.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a matter that has been
very carefully considered by the Advisory
Committee. As the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) has said, this is a
Bill primarily for the marketing of eggs,
and I do not know how it would be possible to insert an Amendment to carry out
what the hon. Senator (Mr. Hill Dickson)
desires. Parliament cannot legislate so
that a man who buys pastry in which
Chinese eggs have been used can be informed that such eggs have been so used
by the bakery. I appreciate the hon.
Senator's point, but I do not see how we
can meet it.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: The purchaser
knows what he is buying, but the consumer does not know what he is eating.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Removal of cold-stored
and chemically-stored British eggs).
Mr. LESLIE :
Before this Clause
passes I should like to be assured that the
Government is satisfied that the reference
to the Ministry of Agriculture will not be
misunderstood in any quarter. It is, of
course, intended to refer to our Own
Ministry of Agriculture, but there are
repeated references to the United Kingdom which seem to suggest the possibility
of a misunderstanding. As the Committee
knows, the expression, "Ministry of Agriculture," has been defined as meaning the
Ministry of Agriculture for Northern
Ireland unless a contrary intention appears, and what I should like to be reassured on is that the Government is
satisfied there is no such contrary intention here. If the Government is not so
satisfied an Amendment could easily be
introduced at a later stage.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I can assure the hon. and
learned Senator that the Government is
perfectly satisfied that the Ministry of
Agriculture is the Ministry of Agriculture
for Northern Ireland.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 3 (Marketing of preserved
eggs), and 4 (Short title, construction,
and citation) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Preamble and title agreed to.

without Amendment to the House.

Rating and Valuation Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Rating and Valuation (Apportionment)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill,
to which I would ask the House to give a
Second Reading, is the first of the
Measures necessary to bring into operation
the relief from rates which it is proposed
to grant in Northern Ireland under what
has come to be known as the Derating
Scheme. Hon. Members will appreciate
that the Measure now before us is a preparatory one, but it is necessary, in order
that it may be understood, that I should
give some details of the general provisions
of the Scheme which, in its application to
Northern Ireland, has formed the subject
of an exhaustive report by an inter-Departmental Committee. I think that hon.
Members have already had the opportunity
of seeing this report, which has recently
been published.
The relief afforded by the Derating
Scheme falls into three distinct compartments. Firstly, there is the removal of
the burden of rates generally from agricultural land and farm buildings; secondly,
the removal of the burden of rates to the
extent of 75 per cent, from hereditaments
used for productive industry; and thirdly,
a similar relief, that is, to the extent of 75
per cent. on what are termed freighttransport hereditaments. A condition of
the relief afforded to this latter category
is that it must be utilised by the companies
concerned for the purpose of making reductions in their freight charges on certain
classes of traffic.
The first purpose, namely, that of agricultural relief, is the most important part
of the whole scheme; in short, the proposal
is that rates on agricultural land and on
farm buildings should entirely cease
from 1st of October, 1929.
as
This exemption will apply to all charges, whether
county-at-large, union or district rates,
but will be exclusive of malicious injury
and drainage rates, and also water rates
in so far as the latter represent a distinct
payment for water used on farms.
The relief given to farmers by these
proposals is probably the greatest assistance
that the Government has ever been able to
grant them. It will probably be found to
be more helpful to them than even the
I think that when
Land Purchase Acts.
it is generally realised throughout our
country districts what this Measure really
means, the farmers will be satisfied that
something really appreciable is being done
for them.
Relief from agricultural rates is, of
course, not a new departure, as a considerable grant is already being made by Parliament in relief of agricultural rates in
Northern Ireland. There has been a
special grant given to local authorities for
this purpose which in the current year
amounts to over a quarter of a million.
But in spite of this existing relief the farmer in our area has been left with a rate
burden of two-thirds of the total rates;
that is, he has only secured relief to the
extent of one-third of the total for both
land and buildings. When this new
Scheme is applied he will be exempted entirely from all rates with the exception of
a charge on his residence, and also of the
minor charges which I have mentioned
and which are not included in the Scheme.
In regard to the actual details of this
part of the Scheme, I would refer hon.
Members to Clauses 2 and 3 of our present
Bill. An important feature is that contained in Sub-clause (b) of Clause 3, which
says that the valuation of agricultural
buildings shall be estimated at one-third
of the total valuation assigned to the
farmer's dwelling-house and farm offices.
The necessity for this division of one-third
and two-thirds arises owing to the fact that
hitherto there has been no distinction in
the valuation lists as between farm offices
and residences, and the figure of one-third
for farm offices and two-thirds for residences has, after careful investigation,
been finally fixed as representing a very
fair average.
If any farmer is not satisfied with this
proportion of one-third and two-thirds
for the buildings on his holding it will be
open to him to appeal for a revaluation
when the farm offices and the residence
will be separately valued and relief will
then be given on the basis of the actual
valuation assigned to the farm buildings.
The additional assistance to agriculturists
under these arrangements, over and above
the relief of about £256,000 already
being given, is estimated at £475,000 for
land and at £55,000 for farm buildings.
The sum of all these reliefs will be about
£768,000.
633 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
How much?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: £768,000.
I think that hon. Members will agree
that this is a not inconsiderable
sum. I now come to the second
part of the Derating Scheme, that
is, the relief which will be given to
industrial hereditaments. As I have
already said, the proposals in this connection are that such hereditaments
should receive relief to the extent of 75
per cent. of the burden of the rates.
Clauses 4 and 5 of our present Bill give
particulars of the hereditaments to which
these provisions will apply. It will be
noticed that private dwelling-houses,
shops, distributing warehouses for wholesale business, storage warehouses, and so
forth are excluded, in accordance with the
list given in sub-clause (1) of Clause 4,
from receiving relief. The rates from
which industries will be relieved to the
extent of 75 per cent. will be similar to
those I have already enumerated in connection with agriculture, namely, countyat-large, union or district rates, and also
water rates unless they are on the basis
of meter charges or of a special rate for
a definite consumption of water. The
total relief thus given to industry will
amount to about £120,000 a year.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: In
addition? We have £768,000 a year and
now another sum. Is that in addition to
the first sum you have mentioned? The
total sum
we shall have to find will be
more than £768,000?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes. This
is admittedly not a very great sum,
but
it is hoped that it may help, in some cases
at all events, to make just the difference
between failure and success.
one.
It is also worth noticing that the proposal that productive industry only should
benefit is fundamentally a sound
The mills and factories are the basis of
our prosperity. There is no fear that any
concern will suffer by reason of the new
discrimination in their valuation which
may require to be made. Existing total
valuations are to be taken and apportioned in accordance with the provisions
laid down in the Schedule
In regard to the third part of the
Scheme, dealing with freight-transport
relief, it is here also proposed to follow
the British Scheme and to relieve railways, docks and canals of 75 per cent. of
their rates in the same way as relief is
being given to productive industries.
There is an important difference, however,
between the relief given to these carrying
companies and to productive industries,
and this is that the carrying companies
will not be allowed to retain the amount
so given, but will have to pass it on to
their customers in the form of rebates on
freight charges. Moreover, these rebates
on freight charges are to be confined to
certain commodities. The selection of
these commodities has received a great
deal of consideration and it has finally
been decided that the relief would be
applied so far as railways and canals are
concerned to the following:-
Industrial coal terminating in Northern Ireland, but excluding coal to be
used by public utility undertakings;
Linen and cotton yarn and cloth, together with flax and tow yarn, originating or terminating in Northern
Ireland;
Potatoes and live stock originating
in Northern Ireland;
Artificial manures and feeding stuffs
terminating in Northern Ireland.
It will be seen from this list that both
the agricultural and the industrial interests will receive benefit. The transport undertakings themselves, that is,
the railways and canals, should receive.
an indirect benefit by reason of the additional trade which will be attracted to
them on account of the lower rates for
carriage. The remaining matter in this
part of the Bill relating to freight transport hereditaments is that of the items
which will be affected in the relief given
to docks and harbours. These items are
as follow:
Launching and other charges in connection with ships which are imposed
by harbour authorities;
Cattle, pigs and sheep;
Potatoes;
Linen and cotton yarn and cloth;
Flax.
635 Rating and Valuation Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Charlemont]
The details of this are set out more
fully in a White Paper which has been
issued on the subject.
Clauses 6 and 7 of our present Bill
provide for the distinction in the valuation lists of the freight transport hereditaments which will receive relief in
accordance with the arrangements I have
outlined. I would, however, refer hon.
Members to sub-section (1) of Clause 6
where the only railways which will have
relief are those with at least two stations
in Northern Ireland. Light railways
are also included in the category of railways for the purpose of the Bill.
In regard to harbours, certain of these
will come under the provisions of the Bill
as not being entitled to relief. These
harbours or docks which will be exempted
will include those belonging to local
authorities; those of a valuation under
£20; and those private harbours which
are used mainly for the business of their
owners.
Before concluding, I would only wish
to add that I do not propose, at this
stage, to enter into the other provisions
incidental to the whole Derating Scheme,
such as, for example, the arrangements
by which local authorities will be recouped for the loss of rates occasioned
by the working of the scheme and other
matters such as road control, and so
forth. These questions are at present
under investigation by a further Inter-
Departmental Committee which will consult the local authorities, and it would
not be right to anticipate any recommendations that may be made; and in any
case such matters do not come within the
scope of the present Bill, but will be
dealt with by further legislation in the
Spring. I hope that hon. Members will
give a Second Reading to the Measure
which is now before us and that the
whole scheme will mean a brighter outlook for our productive industries, in
which agriculture takes a leading place.
I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
suppose this is one of the most complicated
and intricate Bills that has ever been laid
before Parliament. It deals with a new
subject and breaks fresh ground.
The
relief that has been referred to is most
desirable, but I do not think there is any
Member of this or any other House, or
even the Minister of Finance himself,
whom we are so pleased to see here to-day,
who can foresee how this Bill will actually
work out in practice. I am not going into
the details of the Measure, because all the
matters the noble Viscount has mentioned
are most desirable, but he has only shown
us the things on one side of the curtain
and he has not revealed even a glimmer of
the other side. I do not believe that any
Government has a secret pot of gold, and
that all they have to do is to delve into it
and find the money that may be required
for any project. The financial aspect of
this Bill is a very serious matter. The
figures the noble Viscount has given the
House show that the proposals contained
in this Bill will mean that practically a
million pounds will have to be found every
year.
There are two classes of people who find
the money for Government services. One
class is the ratepayers and the other is the
taxpayers. I hope it is not the intention
to pay this money into one pocket and take
it out of the other. In some ways I am
suspicious as to how this Scheme is going
to be financed. Even Sir Neville Chamberlain in another place gave very little
information on that subject, and it does not
appear as if we here are going to get much
information; in fact, I do not think any of
us will leave this House to-day much wiser
on this Bill than when we came into it.
I have a shrewd suspicion as to the source
from which the Government propose to
find this million of money a year. I am
not at liberty to say what it is, but if it is
true that it is going to be found from an
outside source that is all right. At the
same time, I do not think we should be
asked to take a pig in a poke and simply
be satisfied with the statement that the
County Councils are not going to be any
worse off by reason of their losing these
rates.
The majority of the County Councils of
Ulster and I refer particularly to the
Down County Council-are chiefly agricultural counties, and depend on the rates
that are raised from the farmers. Personally I hope that the farmer will get every
possible benefit from this Scheme, but I
think it should be made clear that those
other ratepayers who are not farmers are
not going to have to pay any extra rates
to make up for those the farmers and
others will cease to pay. Speaking as a
637 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading.
member of a County Council, I should like
to point out that the agricultural ratepayers are the people who up to the present
time have acted as a brake on the expenditure on roads. I do not know, because it
is not made clear in the Bill, whether the
Education Rate is included in those rates
from the payment of which farmers will be
exempt under this Bill.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): All rates except drainage.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am glad to hear that it is not. Let us take
another County Council service that forms
a principal and increasing source of expenditure. That is the upkeep of the
roads. Unless this Bill imposes some
restriction on those who up to now
have acted as a safeguard against excessive expenditure on the roads, there is
a possibility of their becoming spenders
in this respect in the future, and there is a
danger that the farmers will want money
spent on almost every road. It is only
with the financial aspect of the Bill that I
am concerned, and, after all, that is the
most important part of the Measure. The
noble Leader of the House said it was no
inconsiderable relief that farmers and
others were going to get under this Bill,
but on the other hand it will be no inconsiderable amount of money that will have
to be found to finance this Scheme.
I hope I am not wearying the House,
but there are other matters in connection
with this Bill that ought to receive consideration. I want to refer again to the
County of Down, where we have a number of small harbours which are a source
of perpetual expenditure to us. It is not
a case of an average annual expenditure,
because very often something suddenly
happens to a particular harbour and we
have to spend a good deal of money on
it. I suppose the Government will base
their repayments for loss of rates on the
average annual income and expenditure
of local bodies, but that system would not
work out fairly in the case of county
councils, because it would not be equitable to take, for instance, the average
annual expenditure on harbours. If
there is going to be a hard and fast rule
in this respect it will make it very difficult for county councils to finance their
services. Up to now we have been able to
go to the Government and say "We want
£30,000 or £40,000 that has to be spent
on this harbour," and the Government
would say "We have got no money for
this job." At present when we have to
raise money for any special object we
simply put it on next year's rates so that
the expense may be met.
I am in thorough accord with the Bill,
but as one who has been accustomed to
deal with finance I entertain a certain
amount of suspicion in regard to it. The
question must have been considered where
the money is coming from to provide for
the loss of rates. I do not think it is
fair of the Government at this period,
certainly not to an assemblage like this,
not to take us into their confidence. It
cannot be that there is anything that they
are ashamed of doing. I do hope that
the Minister of Finance will to-day, as
I know he did in the other House, give
us an assurance that we are not going to
lose anything and will not be expected
to find any more money. It may be that
the Imperial Government is blocking it,
but I do think that before the Bill appears
in its next stage the Government should
take us into their confidence and let us
know how the Bill is going to be paid for.
Mr. LONG: The Measure before the
House to-day will be regarded by the
agriculturists of the Province as one of
the two greatest boons which have been
granted to them in this generation. The
other was the Land Act. I am not going
to say which is the greater boon, but I
am convinced that the Measure at present
being considered will bring a ray of hope
and sunshine into drab and dreary lives
in many homesteads on the hillsides of
Ulster. I know for a fact that the agriculturists of the Six Counties are
already the envy of those engaged in
similar occupations in the Free State.
They only wish that a similar state of
affairs was impending in their case.
Farmers in the past have been accused
of grumbling and grousing without necessary cause. There is a very well-known
old saying that a contented farmer and
sober lord are never met. Mark Twain
has stated that the second part of that
saying is a libel because the noble persons
referred to are frequently sober, but we
from our experience know that this part
of the saying is not only offensive but untrue. I must admit that there was some
ground for the first part of the statement.
Farmers were certainly discontented and
639 Rating and Valuation Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Long]
I think that they had cause for discontentment. Hon. Senators can have no couception of the misery and dispair that
hard-working farmers suffered from.
They have no idea of how hard they had
to work. They had long hours, very
often beginning at four o'clock in the
morning and ending at nine o'clock at
night. Notwithstanding that hard work.
in some cases they practically get no return for their arduous labours.
very glad to repeat the words used by
Senator Barclay when he was speaking
on the Representation of the People Bill.
I am pleased to have lived to see the day
when the rates will be entirely abolished
on agricultural land and buildings.
Mr. BARCLAY: Hear, Hear.
I am
Mr. LONG: With regard to the segregation of agricultural buildings and the
houses of the farmers, I entirely approve
of the principle. If that principle had
not been adopted it would have entailed
an expensive valuation of the entire farm
This
buildings in Northern Ireland.
would certainly not be desirable, and it
is very questionable if it would be worth
the cost. Personally, I would prefer a
larger fraction. Instead of one-third I
should have preferred one-half. I know
from my own experience that the farm
buildings are counted equal in value to
the farmer's dwelling-house. My own
small house is insured against fire
for the same
as the farm
buildings are insured. I consulted
દી number of insurance agents who
deal in this sort of risk, and they
assured me that it is the general custom
throughout the country for the farmer's
house to be insured for a sum similar to
what the farm buildings are insured for.
sum
There is a matter which has been causing concern to me as a member of a local
authority. It is one of the matters to
which the right hon. Gentleman, Senator
Colonel Sharman Crawford, who preceeded me, referred to. I am very
pleased to see the Chancellor of the Exchequer (Mr. Pollock) here as he could
perhaps throw some light on the matter.
As hon. Senators have gathered, land in
future will be entirely free from rates,
and the local authorities are faced with
the situation that the right hon. Senator
has pictured. Suppose a local authority
decide to embark on some scheme of considerable magnitude. Let me take an
illustration. Suppose in County Londonderry we receive £100,000 in relief of
rates. We may want in the future to
build a new lunatic asylum or at least a
villa to accommodate the increased number of patients we have. I should think
as regards a work like that or any other
work of public utility, of course an approved work of utility—
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Mr. LONG-
-there should be reasonable room for extension; there should be
a measure of elasticity. I should have
misgivings in adopting a scheme of the
kind I have indicated if the entire sum
were to be met with expenditure on buildings. I think that would be entirely unfair and inequitable. I, therefore, suggest to the Government that to provide
for this elasticity I have mentioned there
should be, say, a rate of 1s in the £ fixed
for the next five years, as the amount
which the County Councils and other
rating authorities should not be allowed
to exceed.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Mr. LONG: In all cases it would be
absolutely necessary that the sanction of
the Ministry should be obtained to any of
these works. With regard to the whole
Bill I have nothing but praise, and I most
heartily welcome it on behalf of the agriculturists of the Six Counties.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount, in
introducing this Bill this afternoon, took,
if I may say so, a somewhat ecstatic flight.
He took us direct to the promised land,
and he did not perhaps bestow any undue
amount of attention to the oiling and lubricating of the aeroplane to which he had
entrusted himself for carriage. Since he
has taken us to the promised land we may
spend a few minutes in looking at what
that land has to give us, what particular
kinds of milk and honey are to be offered
to Northern Ireland in the future. I need
not say very much about it, because much
that I would have said has already been
said. There is to be a relief to the farmers
of an agricultural rate in respect of everything I understood the Minister of
Finance to say except malicious injury
rates and drainage rates, and perhaps I
think in some small cases some of those
very local rates.
61 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading.
Mr. POLLOCK: All rates.
Mr. LESLIE: I need not spend further
time on that. It is a very great boon.
Our principal industries are to get assistance also, though not perhaps to the same
extent 75 per cent. is the figure suggested
although, of course, that figure is not in
the Bill. Finally, the ancillary service of
freight transport is to get a reduction
whether that transport service be by railway or by canal, or by such canals as there
are in Northern Ireland. It is not perhaps our fault that we have not more, but
at all events whether by railway, or canal
or dock, which includes harbour, there is
to be a relief there, and that is to react
again on the producer, because it is to
come back again in the form of a rebate.
But we
are not yet in the promised
land. We are not even on the top of
Pisgah. Before we get there we have to
wrestle with the scree at the foot of the
mountain, and remove any boulders lying
on our passage to the summit. I do not
think we are going to find any great difficulty in our journey, and that journey is
the passage of the Bill through this House,
because the Bill before us is, if I may say
so, very plain indeed. It is, I think, as
explicit as a Bill on such a complicated
subject could possibly be. I do not say
there are not little matters here and there
requiring explanation. Of course there
are, but the Minister of Finance, speaking
in another place, referred some inquiring
friend to the dictionary which he would
find in the Bill itself. I do not know that
a more happy phrase could have been
selected. Nevertheless, there will be
things that one wants to know.
I am going to suggest as an example
something to which I am quite sure an
answer will be available. There must be
an answer, because of the words adopted
in connection with what I am going to
raise. I think that whoever takes this
Bill in the Committee stage may expect
to be asked for further information as regards the "vessel" which comes into matters relating to docks. Some will want to
be assured that the expression "vessel"
includes every form of barge or lighter
however it may be propelled, so long as it
is used in tidal waters or in a harbour. I
think that is a fair sample of the questions
which will, perhaps, be put, and which, I
am certain, can be answered.
One looks naturally enough to what is
not in the Bill before us. I think what is
not in this Bill is particularly interesting,
having regard to what we have been shown
as regards the future. Under this Bill
rateable value is not, if I understand the ⚫
matter, affected at all. What is to happen
the rateable value? The assessment or
apportionment of rates upon it is dealt
with, but the rateable value itself is not
dealt with. Neither are the incomes of
local authorities in any way to be affected
by this Measure, nor, as I understand it,
by the Measure to come. It is not in a
way perhaps correct to speak of this as a
relief of rates. It is the farmer who pays
who is going to be relieved his rates and
those who receive the rates the local
authorities are going to be compensated.
That is how I understand it. It is relief
to the one and compensation to the other.
The question has now been asked as to
where the money is to come from to effect
this.
we
We are not told in the Bill where
the money is to come from--and I think
perhaps for very good reasons-but
are given to understand that the money
is not to come from an extra rate put
upon that part of the rateable value which
does not get relief. I think the Minister
of Finance agrees.
Mr. POLLOCK indicated assent.
Mr. LESLIE: That is a great boon.
It seems to me that we are going to owe
this great benefit, as we Owe so many
others, to our Imperial connection, to the
fact that we are part of the United Kingdom consisting of Great Britain and
Northern Ireland. But the fact that we
are to get so much and that we are to owe
SO much to our Imperial connection
renders it even more incumbent upon us
than it would have been before not to
attempt to interfere seriously with the
structure of the Measure. It would be
quite easy to open our mouths even more
widely and to ask for more. I do not
know that we should get it, but I think it
would be very unwise to try. We must,
if we can, follow the Imperial Act and
follow the wording of it, and the wording
of the Northern Ireland Act-as I am
sure the right hon. and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) who
opened the debate has noticed-follows
the wording of the Imperial Statute.
Our difficulties will not be of that class
at all. Any difficulties we may experience will be difficulties for the futuredifficulties of apportionment and difficul-
•
L
643 Rating and Valuation Bill-
C44
THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
ties, perhaps, of administration. As regards the difficulties of apportionment one
may, perhaps, say that in this Bill
industrial and freight-transport hereditaments are not entirely mutually exclusive.
It does not seem to me to matter very
much whether they are or are not from
one point of view, because the percentage
of relief is to be the same although the
ultimate destination of that relief is different. The question seems to come up
rather in connection with a matter which
affects us, although I do not think it
affects our friends in Great Britain, and
that is in the case of those motor services
which we have here as ancillary to our
railway companies, the motor services
which are used by the railway companies
under our Act of 1927, and will be used
more perhaps in the future for the conveyance of passengers with or without
their luggage either directly to their
destinations or partly as feeders for the
railway systems.
Now railway motor services of that sort
are distinctly different from the ordinary
omnibus motor service. The ordinary
omnibus motor service is not rated in any
way in respect of the roads over which it
runs and, therefore, such omnibus service
would not have any claim that I can see
to share in the relief which it is proposed
to give to railways, which do run along
the railways they have made and in
respect of which they are rated directly.
Whether it is intended or not that such
hereditaments as are directly attributable
to what I call auxiliary railway motor
services-whether it is intended that they
should share in this relief is a matter
which, perhaps, the Minister of Finance
(Mr. Pollock) will be able to tell us, if
not to-day at all events on some future
date. I must confess that I myself am
not particularly hopeful that the scope of
the Measure does extend to such hereditaments as I have mentioned, but I
would, however, like to know, because if
it is intended that the Measure should so
extend it would be necessary to make
some very trivial alterations in the text of
the Bill. I believe it would be necessary
to move three Amendments involving, I
think, the omission of three words and
the substitution of four others. That
would be the aggregate verbal alteration
that would be necessary, but the material
difference would be very great. I hope
we will be told that to-day one way or the
other.
I
Passing to the question of administration, most of it will be done by the county
councils, and they will work principally
on the directions given in the Schedule.
The only point I should like to make
about that is that it seems to me that
the time allowed to the county councils is insufficient for the purpose.
rather doubt whether all our county
councils, having regard to the slowness of
the rural posts, can perfect their final
lists within fourteen days, but I am assured by a really competent authority
that if fourteen days is too little it is not
much too little. The question of Belfast,
where they have no county council but
where they have a substitute for a county
council which is called a Corporation, and
its very large number of industrial hereditaments will, I have no doubt, be dealt
with by someone having more knowledge
of the city of Belfast than I have.
all events, I think we may take it that
this period of fourteen days and the other
periods mentioned in the Bill will be understood as being directory and not mandatory, and, therefore, it is not necessary
for us to make any suggestion for extension here. If, in fact, any alteration of
that sort is necessary I think it could best
be achieved by allowing a little time to
elapse. There must necessarily be some
time between this Bill and its successor,
and during that time I imagine full information can be obtained on a matter of
that sort, and if it should be necessary
to make any definite alteration it could
I suggest, be very easily done.
At
Now, I have spoken for a moment of
the future. There is one word I want
to say about the Bill which is to follow
this one, more or less a technical point
perhaps. I do hope that when we get
the final Bill, that is, the second chapter,
the old expression "Irish Valuation Act"
will disappear. It is quite time we had
a little valuation code of our own, and
I should like the new Bill to be so worded
that it will end with the words "This
Act shall be construed and may be cited"
-if that is the appropriate expression-
"with the Northern Ireland Valuation
Acts, 1852 to 1929." I shall hope to see,
at the same time, Section 24 of the Intrepretation Act repealed, and then we
shall get rid of the expression "Irish
Valuation" and start right afresh in Northern Ireland.
Taking the matter as a whole I am
certain, as my hon. Friend (Mr. Long)
645 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading
opposite said, that this Bill will be
accepted in the Province as an instalment,
perhaps, of an old promise, not made by
our own Government, that when opportunity served something should be done
for realty at the expense, perhaps, of
personalty, but even more that it will be
accepted as a necessary instalment of the
really great thing that is going to be done
for the Province by one of the Parliaments of the United Kingdom under the
leadership of a Government which has
done so much to keep the Province which
is to benefit within the United Kingdom.
It is a benefit that Northern Ireland
would not have got, as I think my hon.
Friend (Mr. Long) said, had she joined up
with the Free State. It is a benefit that
the farmers and industrialists of Northern Ireland will get by virtue of their
continued connection with the United
Kingdom. For that reason alone the
Bill will be received with gratification,
and I hope it will also be received with
some gratitude to those who are more immediately responsible for bringing the
matter before this House. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
a
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not wish
to detain the House, for nearly all that
has been said is what I should have liked
to have said myself in favour of the Bill.
But I would not like the Bill to furgo
ther without, as a representative of
agriculture in this community, thanking
the Government for introducing
Measure that will be one of the greatest
boons to the farmers of Northern Ireland that they have ever received. I do
not know that the reduction in freighttransport charges will benefit the smaller
farmers to any great extent. It will, of
course, benefit the larger dealers and the
bigger men, but at my station to-day
there was a small farmer who had got
delivery of 6 cwt. of Indian meal, and
when the stationmaster got him to sign
the form the ten per cent. reduction on
the carriage of that amounted to 2d. He
asked where this came from; the stationmaster said it was a refund from the
Government, and his reply was that he
thought it would not go to his head.
As an agriculturist I certainly welcome.
the Bill and thank the Government for
its introduction. As a member of the
county council and other boards, our
difficulty, as the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) !
mentioned, is to know where the money
is going to come from for schemes connected with the county. I am very much
afraid that the borrowing powers of the
councils for the building of new schools,
hospitals, or asylums, as my hon. Friend
(Mr. Long) has said, will be considerably
curtailed. We will be very much obliged
if the Minister of Finance, who is so welcome here to-day, will let us know if the
borrowing powers of the councils will not.
be curtailed when the rateable value of
all land in the county is taken out of their
hands.
Mr. DORMAN: I find it difficult to
speak on a Measure such as this.
As a
Socialist I look upon this as an acknowledgment on the part of the Government
of the failure of our present method of
producing and distributing wealth.
Practically every stage of the production
of wealth consumed in the community
will be covered by this Derating Measure.
I would very much like to see the duty of
passing on the advantage to the community imposed not merely upon those
who are engaged in the freight end of the
industries dealt with in this particular
Bill. We have no guarantee that the
freedom from rates given to the agriculturists will be passed on to the vast masses
of the consumers of the agricultural produce of our area,
nor have we any
guarantee that it will be passed on from
the productive end of industry to the consumers.
What I understand will take place will
be this: Immediately the saleable value
of farms will go up, the market price of
productive shares will go up, and the advantage of this Derating Bill will not be
enjoyed by the masses of the consumers.
It is not the first time that a scheme of
derating has been found necessary. After
the long Napoleonic wars a derating
scheme was tried in Great Britain at the
lower end of the social scale, and it was
found that derating did not ultimately
effect the object the Government had in
view. At that particular period hand
labour was largely being displaced by
machine production; great misery was
prevalent amongst the community, and it
was found necessary because of the shortage of house accommodation and the
poverty of the lower classes of the community to derate working-class houses.
Now the working classes did not obtain
the advantage of that derating scheme,
647 Rating and Valuation Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
and, if my memory serves me right, a
Parliamentary Committee set up across
the water investigated the results and
found that practically in all cases the derating did not result in any advantage
to the people it was intended to benefit.
If my memory is correct, people launched
into the excessive erection of dwellinghouses with a view to obtaining higher
rentals than they otherwise would have
had if the de-rating scheme had not been
in existence. I do not know how the
Government will arrange that the advantages of this de-rating scheme will be
obtained by the masses of the community.
My own opinion is that it is a proof of
the failure of private enterprise and of
competition to supply the necessary needs.
of the community. I believe that this
de-rating scheme has originated in similar
causes to those that led to the de-rating
scheme in the early part of last century,
and that ultimately it will prove as ineffectual to remedy the evils that at
present exist in society.
I am sure the agriculturists will all be
thankful for this Measure. Roughly
speaking, if the burden had to be borne
locally, it would amount to 15s. a head
of the population. If the Imperial Parliament is giving to Northern Ireland a
grant of 15s. a head per annum for derating, when we ought to be thankful to
the Imperial Government.
Mr. BARCLAY: I might say, with
others, that I welcome this Bill which is
before the House to-day, and which has
as its object the assistance of our industries, especially our competitive industries;
but at the same time I am not satisfied
that the relief that is going to be given
to the rates will lower the cost of production to such an extent that it will
improve trade. We have heard a great
Ideal this afternoon of the benefits that
are to be derived from this de-rating
scheme, so far as the agricultural community are concerned, but I did not hear
very much said about the benefits to be
derived from it by other industries, such
as the linen industry, the shipbuilding
industry, and all the other industries that
we have in the city of Belfast, upon which
hundreds and thousands of people depend
for their daily bread.
The reason I make the statement that I
am afraid it will not improve trade to
such an extent is that the Government
have not half enough money at their disposal for the purpose of financing all the
industries to such an extent that they will
be able to reduce the cost of the products
which they put upon the competitive
market. I do not know the amount that
the Chancellor of the Exchequer has past
him for this purpose, but from what I
saw in the paper the other evening it
would not go very far. A million of
money has been mentioned to-day. What
would a million of money do in the North
of Ireland to assist all our competitive
industries? It would not go very far.
With your permission, Mr. Speaker,
and with the permission of the House—
if I am in order I would like to say that
personally I believe an extension of the
Safeguarding of Industries Act would
have served the purpose in view much
better than the relief proposed to be given
to the rates under this Bill. I do not
know whether I am in order in making
that statement at the present time, but
I am fully convinced in my own mind
that it would do so, because our industries
are calling for safeguarding, and if we
had a safeguarding scheme we would not
be taking the money out of Peter's pocket
and putting it into Paul's. We would be
taking it out of the foreigner's pocket,
and putting it into the pockets of our own
people in this country. If we look back
over the industries that have been safeguarded the results go to prove that we
are quite able to hold our own in the
competitive markets, provided that we
get a fair chance. I am sorry to say it
is not within the jurisdiction of our
Government to safeguard our industries.
We depend upon the Imperial Government in that connection, and I would
have been very pleased if the Imperial
Government, instead of bringing in a derating scheme such as this, had brought
in an extension of the Safeguarding of
Industries Act instead.
We have heard a great deal this afternoon as to the great benefit that is going
to accrue to the agricultural community
from this Measure, and I agree that it
is going to be of great benefit to the
agricultural industry. I was brought up
on a farm myself. I know all the sorrows
and sufferings, the weeping, the wailing
and gnashing of teeth, of the farmers
especially in a bad year, and I know from
personal experience the difficulties that
649 Rating and Valuation Bill-
5 DECEMBER 1928

Second Reading
they have to face. Of course my experience was gained a good number of years
ducts.
I agree, however, with Senator
ago.
Long, that sometimes a farmer works the
whole year round, and, when he pays all
his just debts-that is, if he is a just man
-he has scarcely as much left as buys
himself a pair of boots or a pair of
corduroy trousers.
I remember the time when we used to
sell flax in the market for something like
5/6 to 7/6. We used to get 30s to 40s per
cwt. for our pork, and I have taken two
loads of potatoes to market with my
father, and got 13d a cwt. Things are
not as bad now as they were then, but so
far as I understand things are very bad
at the present time. The price of cattle
is coming down; the price of pork is
coming down; the prices of other farm
produce are coming down, and so farmers
are very badly hit at the present time. I
was very pleased to hear it said to-day
that they are going to benefit by this
relief to the rates, but what strikes me is
this is this relief to the rates going to
make the farmer produce anything more
than he is producing at the present time?
I dare say there are a number of hardworking farmers, men of business
capacity, who will take advantage of this
relief, and who will use this
money
the purpose of buying seed, buying extra
manure or paying wages for the breaking
up of land that is lying at the present
time under grass. I agree that a certain
number of farmers will do that, but, on
the other hand, if I know anything about
some of them, they will take a deep sigh
of relief, and perhaps they will say,
"Thank God for that; that is a tremendous burden removed from my shoulders."
They may be tempted to say, in the words
of the Old Book:
for
Take thine ease, eat, drink and be
merryfor the great burden of rates that was
upon you at one time is now removed,
and hangs about the neck of the Chancellor of the Exchequer.
I would like to know how pressure could
be brought to bear on those men to cause
them to produce something extra for this
relief. But from what I know of the
agricultural community, especially of those
who want to do well, they really do not
want relief of rates or subsidies, or any
!
kind of charitable gift, so much as they
want a market and prices for their pro-
If the farmers have a market and
prices for their products, they will produce the goods; the only way we can get
the farmer to produce the goods is to give
him a price for them. We have one of
the best markets within our reach, within
a few hours' journey of our own shores.
I refer to the British market. We have
in Great Britain a great industrial
population that needs to be fed, and that
requires the products that Northern Ireland is quite capable of producing; and
if we could obtain better prices I have
not the least doubt Great Britain would
take from us all that we could possibly
give them. There is put upon the British
market annually about twenty-two million
pounds' worth of beef. That is one thing
we could produce a great deal more of in
Northern Ireland than we are doing at
the present time, and I understand that
cattle are coming down in price. There
is also put upon the market in Great
Britain about ninety million pounds.
worth of mutton. We could produce a
are
great deal more mutton than we
doing. There is also put upon that market a million pounds worth of poultry.
There are three articles of food, three
commodities, that we in Northern Ireland
are quite capable of putting upon the
British market.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon.

I have allowed him to go on for some

are not in order.

not finished, I think.

However, I was just going to say that we

is going to take advantage of this market.
now that he is going to receive this relief

doubt this Measure will be of great benefit
