UNIVERSITY of CALIFORNIA
AT
ICS ANGELES
LIBRARY
UC SOUTHERN REGIONAL LIBRARY FACILITY
A 000 175 240 1

Wednesday, 7th March, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. J. A. LONG
(Deputy Speaker), in the Chair.
Deputy Speakers.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I beg to move, that the
following Senators be named Deputy-
Speakers of the Senate during the present Session of Parliament:-
Mr. J. A. Long.
The Viscount Bangor.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Joint Standing Orders Committee.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, that the Senators following be
named of the Joint Standing Orders
Committee:-
Sir G. S. Clark.
Mr. J. Cunningham.
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, that the Senators following be
named of the Joint Standing Committee
on Divorce Bills:-
Mr. R. Dorman.
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
Mr. W. J. McDowell.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Legitimacy Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
propose to proceed with the Second Reading of this Bill to-day, as it has not yet
been printed.
Adoption of Children Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The same
thing applies to the Second Reading of
this Bill, which I do not propose to take
to-day.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes after
Three o'clock until such
date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint.
Local Bill
Papers Presented
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 15th March, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the
Chair.
Ministry of Commerce.
Report of the Departmental Advisory
Committee on the Development of
Fishery Harbours in Northern Ireland.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Insurance Fund
National Health
and Unemployment Insurance
Insurance Fund
(Northern Ireland):-
1. Account showing the nature of the
Securities held by the Commissioners for
the Reduction of the National Debt at
31st December, 1927, on behalf of the
Ministry of Finance for Northern Ireland,
as Investments for Moneys, forming part
of the National Health Insurance Fund
for Northern Ireland, paid over to them
under Section 70 (1) of the National
Health Insurance Act, 1924, as made
applicable to Northern Ireland by
Section 129 (13) of the said Act.
11. Account showing the nature and
Amount of the Securities held by the
Ministry of Finance for Northern Ireland,
at 31st March, 1927, as Investments for
Moneys, forming part of the Unemployment Insurance Fund, Northern Ireland,
paid over to them by the Ministry of
Labour (N.I.), under Section 14 (3) and
46 (2) of the Unemployment Insurance
Act, 1920, as made applicable to
Northern Ireland by Paragraph 6 (B) of
the Government of Ireland (Adaptation
of Unemployment Insurance Acts) Order,
1922.
Return made by the Official Assignee
of the Belfast Local Bankruptcy Court
for the year ended 31st December, 1927.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and
ordered to lie on the Table
LOCAL BILL.
LONDON MIDLAND AND
SCOTTISH RAILWAY BILL.
The following Report was made from
the Standing Orders Committee to whom
was referred the Examiners Certificate
relating to the London, Midland, and
Scottish Railway (Petition for Bill):-
That the Committee had met and had
resolved, that the Standing Orders not
complied with in respect of the said
Petition for a Bill ought to be dispensed
with, and leave given to introduce the
Bill.
Which Report was agreed to by the
House. and ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Governor.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I have to report to the
House that a Privy Councillor of
Northern Ireland has waited upon His
Grace the Governor with the Address of
the Senate, pursuant to the Order of 6th
March, to which His Grace has been
pleased to return the following answer:—
I have received with great satisfaction the expression of your thanks
for the Speech with which I have
opened the present Session of Parliament.
COURT OF CRIMINAL APPEAL
IN NORTHERN IRELAND.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he is aware that the Home Secretary stated recently in the Imperial
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
15 MARCH 1928
Parliament that he would be prepared to
give sympathetic consideration
to any
responsible representatives from Northern
Ireland in favour of a Measure designed
to constitute a Court of Criminal Appeal
in Northern Ireland; and, if so, whether
it is proposed to take any action in the
matter.
•
Mr. LESLIE I must begin by pointing out that owing conceivably to the
cryptic character of my calligraphy there
is a misprint in the question as it appears
on the Order Paper. The word should
he "representations," not "representatives." My question has its origin in a
question raised in the House of Commons
at Westminster, when an hon. Member,
an eminent counsel who had served in
the office of Solicitor-General, asked the
Home Secretary the question indicated on
the Paper. In reply, according to my
information, and according to the newspapers and I have checked the reply
and the newspaper reports in the official
Hansard of the Imperial Parliamentthe Home Secretary said he would be
prepared to give sympathetic consideration to any responsible representations
from Northern Ireland in favour of a
Measure for establishing a Court of
Criminal Appeal in Northern Ireland.
do not know why the hon. Member to
whom I have referred raised this question
in the Imperial House of Commons. I
can only hope that it was not owing to
any want of confidence in His Majesty's
judges in Northern Ireland. Certainly I
myself have not that want of confidence,
nor, I imagine, has anybody else, but
that is entirely beside the point
It is rather interesting, I think, that
Sir William Joynson-Hicks said. he
wanted representations from Northern
Ireland, not from the Government of
Northern Ireland, as one would have expected, and the question that occurs to
one is, where does he expect to get his
representations from? A matter of that
sort is not a matter on which you would
expect, let us say, Boards of Guardians
or Select Vestries to pass resolutions.
Resolutions could only come from some
body really competent to express an
opinion on the point. Grand Juries, of
course, occur to cne at once, but it must
be remembered that a Grand Jury, once
it is discharged, has no corporate
capacity; also that a Grand Jury would
naturally be very diffident of making a
representation in open Court on a matter
such as this, unless they had received
some intimation that their opinion was
desired.
The English Act itself was, as the
House will remember, passed in the year
1907. Up to that time there was no
Court of Criminal Appeal in England. I
cannot help thinking that if that Act had
been passed fifteen or twenty years
earlier it would have applied to Ireland
as a matter of course, but it is well known
that there was a period in the early part
of this century when Ireland was expressly excluded from ameliorative
Measures.
The provisions of the English Act are,
briefly, that a convicted person may
appeal as of right on a point of law.
There is an appeal on a point of law in
Ireland to-day. It is obtained by
various methods that I need not deal
with in detail to-day. He may also
appeal with the leave of the Court of
Appeal, or by a certificate of the Judge
who tries him, on a question of fact, or
mixed law and fact, or
on any other
ground which the Court of Appeal thinks
is a proper ground, and finally, he may
appeal against his sentence if it is not
a sentence definitely fixed by law if the
Court of Appeal gives him leave to do so.
Those conditions prevail in England but
they do not prevail in Northern Ireland.
The Secretary of State in England has
the power of referring all points of doubt
to the Court of Criminal Appeal, and of
course the Royal prerogative of mercy is
entirely unaffected. For myself, as perhaps will have been gathered from the
fact that I put down the question, I
should be inclined to favour the establishment of such a Court here for the
sake of uniformity-
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member will not go too far beyond the scope
of the question. The question really
only asks whether it is proposed to make
representations.
Mr. LESLIE: I will leave the matter
there.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: It is
a fact that in reply to a question in the
Imperial House as to whether the Government would introduce a Bill to constitute
a Court of Criminal Appeal in Northern
Ireland, the Home Secretary made the
reply indicated in the hon. and learned
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Member's question. As the hon. and
learned Member implies, the question is
one which deals with a matter reserved
under the Government of Ireland Act,
and it is undoubtedly one which merits
the very fullest consideration. I may
assure the hon. and learned Member that
if after full inquiry the Government is
satisfied that the establishment of such a
Court in Northern Ireland is desirable
in the interests of justice, the necessary
representations will be made.
PRESERVATION OF ANCIENT
MONUMENTS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Samuel
Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether he is in a position to state what
action has been taken under the Ancient
Monuments Act, 1926, and what places
have been taken over.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I ask
the question which stands in my name
for two principal reasons. One is that I
realize that in this damp season the vegetation is rapidly growing, and some of
these ancient monuments require to be
very carefully looked after. I hope that
is being done. In the second place it
has occurred to me that ancient monuments and historical sites tend to bring
a great many tourists to our shores, and
I think we require as many tourists to
visit this part of the country as we can
get. I therefore hope that the Committee which is responsible for the care
of ancient monuments will keep them in
good order, and will take steps to make
them well known so that our friends in
America and in the Colonies will know
what ancient monuments and historical
sites are to be seen in Ulster.
In considering this question a few days.
ago I could not help thinking how the
centre of gravity has been changing in
certain directions. It is not so long ago
that eggs were scarcely considered as one
of the principal assets in connection with
agriculture, but now we find that the
production of eggs is almost as valuable
to Northern Ireland as is the cattle
trade. Similarly it is not very long ago
since we thought very little of these
ancient monuments. They were allowed
to go to ruin and crumble to the earth,
but I think it is now realized that they
are of far greater value than was formerly thought.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
very pleased the right hon. Senator has
raised this question. My answer to it is
rather a long one, but I hope the House
will excuse me as it is of greater interest
than some other subjects I have heard
discussed here. I have received the
following information from my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, whose
Department, as successor to the Board of
Works, has assumed State charge of
Ancient Monuments.
At all the important monuments caretakers have been provided. Steps have
been taken to improve access and to
provide fencing where needed, also
direction posts have been placed on main
roads pointing the way to monuments of
interest in the vicinity. At Greyabbey,
County Down, the vegetation, which was
masking the refectory, has been removed,
The refectory is now clearly visible,
showing beautiful, pointed windows of the
same type as those in the chapel.
At Devenish Abbey, in County Fermanagh, foundations have been excavated
SO as to show the original plan. The
very perfect Round Tower there has been
re-pointed, and a staircase built inside to
enable the public to ascend the tower
and examine its remarkable architecture.
In the same county an ancient Cross on
Inishmacsaint Island, which was out of
the perpendicular and threatening to fall,
has been straightened. Special attention
has also been given to the group of County
Londonderry churches at Maghera, Dungiven, Bovevagh and Banagher, which
contain very interesting and beautiful
pre-Norman work. With the co-operation
of the Royal Air Force, aerial photographs
are being taken of ancient sites in Ulster,
and it is expected that interesting discoveries will be made.
Some extremely interesting acquisitions
have to be recorded. The executors of
the late Mr. Francis Joseph Bigger, the
noted Ulster antiquary, presented to the
Ministry for preservation the castle in
the town of Ardglass, known as "Jordan's
Castle," together with its contents. Mr.
Bigger had bought this castle in order to
save it from destruction, and had stored
Oral Answers
Legitimacy Bill-
15 MARCH 1928
in it a number of objects of antiquity.
It is the suggestion of the executors,
readily accepted by the Ministry of
Finance, that this castle should preserve
the memory of Mr. Bigger, and be maintained as an example of ancient architecture and as a museum of antiquities,
particularly those of County Down.
Two other important acquisitions are
pending at the present time-firstly,
through the kind co-operation of the War
Department, that of Carrickfergus
Castle, the most impressive and bestpreserved ancient stronghold in Ireland.
The Ministry of Finance hopes to deal
with this in a manner befitting its age
and importance by clearing away some of
the modern structures which obscure the
view and prevent a realization of the
dignity and solidity of the structure.
Arrangements are also pending for the
transfer of Dunluce Castle through the
public spirit of the Earl of Antrim. As
Carrickfergus is the strongest, so Dunluce
is perhaps the most picturesque ard
romantic castle in Ireland. It is, however, in a state of considerable decay,
and some expenditure will be necessary if
it is to be preserved as it stands for the
pleasure and instruction of this and
succeeding generations.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of the answer to the right
hon. Senator's question, I suppose it is
the Advisory Committee that recommends
to the Ministry of Finance what ancient
monuments shall be preserved. I have a
list of members of the Advisory Committee and I think the Committee could be
considerably improved. If I could make
gome suggestions to the responsible
authority to attain that object I should
be very glad to do so. We have been
told of many important things that have
been done to preserve ancient monuments,
but in my County of Down there are a
great many minor ancient monuments
that ought to be looked after but are not.
I
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
should be very pleased to receive any
representations the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman may care to make.
Legitimacy Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
Hon.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
Members are no doubt aware that a Bill
of a similar nature has been before the
Imperial Parliament for a considerable
number of years. It was placed on the
Statute Book in 1926. I need scarcely
say that it is the considered policy of
this Government to keep in line with the
Imperial social legislation. The present
Bill does not call for a detailed explanation-its provisions are, on the whole,
There are, however,
tolerably clear.
some few points to which I think it
necessary to draw the attention of hon.
Members.
The first Clause in the Bill contains
the most important provision. It provides that where the parents of an illegitimate person marry, the marriage is
to render that person, if still alive,
legitimate. It will be noticed that Sub-
Clause (2) modifies this provision in a
most important manner. It states that
the Act is not to operate so as to legitimate a person whose father or mother was
married to a third person at the time
when the illegitimate person was born.
Clause 3 sets out in some detail the
rights of legitimate persons to take
interests in property. It will be observed
that the Act is not to affect the devolution of property which is limited in such
a way as to devoive with a dignity or title
of honour. Under the existing law, the
property of an illegitimate person who
dies intestate without legitimate issue
goes to the Crown, and while it is customary for the Crown to allow grants to
be made from the property to the relatives
of the deceased illegitimate, they have
no legal claim.
Clause 4 of the Bill provides that
where a legitimated person dies intestate,
his relations shall be entitled to take the
same interests in the property as they
would have been entitled to take had the
deceased been born legitimate.
Clause 5 goes a step further, and
provides for the succession on intestacy
of illegitimate persons and their issue
dying before the marriage of
of their
parents.
Clause 6 makes it clear that a
legitimated person will be subject to the
same obligations in respect of maintenance and support of himself or his family
as he would have been had he been born
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
legitimate. and, further, by Sub-Clause
(2) it is laid down that the benefits of the
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Act, 1925, are applicable
to a legitimated child who was alive at
the date of the passing of that Act,
subject to the marriage leading to such
legitimation having taken place before.
the 4th January, 1926.
In Clause 8 the difficulties which
may arise from the parents of an illegitimate child being married outside Northern Ireland are met by providing that the
person may become legitimated if the
marriage has taken place while the father
was domiciled in a country by the law of
which the person became legitimated by
virtue of such marriage.
I have already referred to the fact that
the estate of an intestate illegitimate
goes to the Crown.
Clause 9 provides that an illegitimate
child, on its mother dying intestate, may,
if there are no legitimate children, succeed
to the property. The Clause also allows the
mother of an illegitimate person to inherit the property of her illegitimate
child, if it dies intestate.
In Clause 10 there is a somewhat
important reservation. Nothing in the
Act is to affect the succession to any
dignity or title of honour, or make it
possible for any person other than the
direct legitimate heir to succeed to any
such dignity or title. In conclusion, I
would like to say that I hope hon. Members will lend their support to a Bill
which will help to remove the stigma
attaching to illegitimacy, and to give such
persons, as far as possible, the same right
in law as they would have had if they
had been born legitimate. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am bound to say that
I am not at all an enthusiastic supporter
of the Measure, the Second Reading of
which has just been moved by the noble
Viscount. I am aware, of course, that
Bills of the same nature as this in one
form or another have been brought before
one or the other Chamber of the Imperial
Parliament on many occasions during
recent years, and I am also aware that
in the year 1926 a Bill, on which the Bill
before us this afternoon is modelled, got
satisfactorily through all its stages in
both Houses and received the Royal
Assent. But thinking of that I cannot
but carry my mind back over almost seven
centuries to that Wednesday, the morrow
of Saint Vincent, or in other words 23rd
January, 1236, when a question somewhat
similar to this was pressed or instanted-
I think that was the word that was used
-on the Earls and Barons of England
by the Bishops of that day. Your Lordship will remember, and the House will
remember, the reply of the Barons of
England. The reply was, "Nolumus
leges Angliae mutari"--"We are unwilling that the laws of England should be
changed." That was the attitude taken
up 700 years ago, and until 1926 in
England it still held the field. That a
Measure of this sort should have been
passed in England really represents a
very great victory for South Eastern
Europe.
Let us look for a few moments at the
Bill, and the pros and cons of it. I will
take the cons first. There will be a
distinct danger if this Bill is passed of
those who hitherto would be regarded as
the rightful heirs being ousted by the offspring of a clandestine marriage.
Possibilities of that sort were referred to
in the debates of both Houses across the
water. I do not suppose myself that the
danger is any greater in Northern Ireland
than in England, but there is such a
possibility.
The noble Viscount spoke of the removal
of the stigma of illegitimacy. This Bill
deals with only a very limited class of
illegitimate children, and that limitation.
of class was perhaps the point round
which the greatest battle raged when the
proposals were under discussion in
England. The proposals before us omitand I personally agree they rightly omit
-the legitimation of an illegitimate
child born during the marriage of one of
the parents. But the fact that that
child is excluded does limit the very
large class of children who will be
benefited.
As regards the removal of the stigma, I
think it is only fair to recollect that
people for this purpose may be divided
into two classes, namely, the people whose
marriages are taken note of and the
people whose marriages do not attract
much attention. In the case of persons
whose marriages are not much observed
the illegitimacy of the child is very likely
unknown altogether, but in the case of
15 MARCH 1928
Legitimacy Bill-

Second Reading
manner. We do not want to see that
practice established in Northern Ireland,
or anything approaching it.
those people whose marriages do attract
attention a subsequent legitimation will
not remove such reproach as there may be
from that child as being regarded as
having been born before the marriage of
the parents. However, those objections
are perhaps not so important as the one
I am about to mention now, and which I
think is really the greatest objection that
can be urged against this Bill.
Is this Bill, if it becomes an Act, going
to promote morality or the reverse? We
all know the position to-day. If a man
and woman have intercourse, and it is
expected that that intercourse will be
fruitful it is possible for the man to
marry the woman, and if that is done
before the child is born the child is born
We all know-we meet it
legitimate.
perhaps more in our private lives where
the difficulties of our neighbours are
brought before us-that many a marriage
has been made because it had been represented to the man that he had got the
girl into troubie, as the saying goes, and
it is up to him to marry her for the sake
of the child. If we pass this Bill that
inducement will be done away with.
one
I know that all I am saying has already
been said across the water. As
speaker put it, the inducement that there
were but a few weeks in which to do what
was right for the sake of the coming
child is gone, and instead of there being
a few weeks available there will be an
indeterminable number of years, and I
am very much afraid that will not make
for morality. Whether it will ultimately
lead to the re-establishment of the old
Scottish system of hand fasting, which
is dealt with better than I can deal
with it this afternoon, and perhaps in
more detail than I need deal with it, in
Sir Walter Scott's well known novel,
"The Abbot," I do not know.
That is the practice which did actually
prevail in Scotland of a man and woman
living together first, and if the woman
produced a male child then the man felt
bound to marry her, and according to
Scottish law the offspring was legitimate.
I am afraid the Scottish practice came in
for a good deal of criticism, but there
was this much to be said in defence of it:
that at that time there was a great
scarcity of clergymen in Scotland and
there were difficulties about getting
marriage performed in the proper
a
Passing from its disadvantages, let us
look at the merits the Bill appears to
have. The Bill does not go the full
length of Canon law in the way of establishing legitimacy. I am not an
authority on the old Canon law, and I
take my statement from what was said by
the hon. Member for Swindon in the
House of Commons in England. He is
a King's Counsel and his religious profession I do not know, but his statement
definitely was that the old Canon law
would legitimate a child on the subsequent marriage of the parents whether
or not one of those parents had been
married at the time the child was born.
That is to say, he put it plainly that no
difference was made between the offspring
and
of fornication
the offspring of
adultery. This Bili does not make this differentiation. The offspring of fornication
may be legitimated but the offspring of
adultery will not. That the Bill does not
go so far as the Canon law is to my mind
an advantage.
Again, the Bill has been agitated for
for some years by those who are interested
in philanthropic work. The Women's
Councils of Northern Ireland have, I
think, more than once pressed the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Home
Affairs, to introduce a Bill, and now they
have got it. Further, if we refer-and
I think the noble Viscount did refer-to
the second sub-clause of Clause 6 we see
that there is distinct benefit obtainable
under the Widows', Orphans' and Old
Age Contributory Pensions Act, and I
think that is a benefit for which I myself
pleaded ineffectually when that particular
Measure was before this House.
In Clause 7 a legitimated child will
receive consideration in the matter of
death duties, and that consideration is
always agreeable, I think, to
to this
Chamber. Further on, I think in Clause
9, we see that arrangements are made for
mutual inheritance as between mother
and illegitimate child. Finally, in favour
of the Bill, it will assimilate us with the
law of England. It is very inconvenient
that we should have in different parts of
the British Islands different laws dealing
with this matter.
I do not think this Bill will result in
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
complete uniformity. I am not sure that
its effect will be to bring England and
Northern Ireland into complete line with
Scotland. I have no knowledge of the
arrangements in the Isle of Man or in
the Channel Islands or in the Irish Free
State. I am afraid that all the component parts of the British Islands will not
be in agreement but still, if we must disagree with somebody, I think we should
all agree we should not select England as
the point of disagreement.
However, I think on the whole the
House will feel that public opinion will
support this Measure and that, therefore,
they will give it a Second Reading and
pass it to the Committee stage. When
we reach that stage, apart from two
minor matters of drafting, as to which
very possibly an explanation will be
forthcoming, there will, I think, be two
distinct points to which we ought to
direct our attention.
It will be observed that under the
Second Clause of the Bill we provide, as
has been done in England, that a petition
under the Legitimacy Declaration Act
(Ireland), 1868-the English Act was ten
years older should be brought either in
the High Court, as at present, or in the
County Court. The County Court is a
new power. It was criticized very much
in the House of Commons, but the decision was taken in England to give such
power to the County Court, and it is
proposed to do the same here. I do not
object to that, but we have recently seen
in the newspapers the account of a case
brought in England under their Legitimacy Declaration Act, and I think I may
say the public conscience was shocked by
the revelation there made.
It transpired that a lady, seventy years
of age, had had to come forward and
confess that many years ago she had lived
for so many years before marriage
with so and so, and the result had been
certain children, some of them now married, whose names were given. I the
public conscience was shocked by that.
It certainly attracted attention in the
newspapers, and I think there will be a
strong feeling that that should not occur
over here if any case of this sort should
come forward.
say
Where a case under the Legitimacy
Declaration Act is heard by a Judge of
the High Court-it is perfectly plain
from the statute whether English or
Irish-that Judge has power to hear
such a case in Chambers. I shall ask the
House in the Committee stage, if this
Bill goes through, to provide definitely
a similar power for the County Court
Judge. We shall be following a precedent we have set ourselves, and I do not
think there will be any great objection
to it.
The other question that I think we ought
to consider is, perhaps, rather more recondite. We provide towards the end of the
Bill that the succession to a dignity or
title of honour should not be affected.
We have said nothing in the Bill, and
they said nothing in England, as to the
question of the right to use armorial
bearings. Armorial bearings are not,
perhaps, a dignity, and are rather a
token of dignity, but they are an estate
of inheritance, and it is not, I think,
desirable that people who are not strictly
entitled to such a bearing as is borne by a
legitimate person should claim to get
recognition of the legality of their adoption of the same arms.
I mention the matter because, as the
House knows, the method of securing
arms in Ireland and
recognition of
England are not, and have never been,
quite the same. The Ulster King-of-
Arms has power which the College of
Arms has not got, and therefore I propose, with great diffidence, to put down
words which will enable us to consider
the question of whether the provisions we
have made with regard to the dignity or
title of honour are sufficiently protective
of right to armorial bearings. On the
whole, though in a somewhat lukewarm
manner, I shall give my support to the
Second Reading, and I believe that on
the whole the Bill will be received with
general satisfaction, although I do not
suggest it will satisfy everybody.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. and learned
Senator has put a question that will undoubtedly be very widely asked in regard
to this Measure: Is this Bill calculated
to promote morality? It is an undoubted
fact that illegitimacy is largely confined
to the working classes, and I remember a
celebrated politician who recently held a
most eminent position under the Crown
declaring some thirty years ago that
illegitimacy in this country was connected
with a certain organization and a certain
religion.
Legitimacy Bill-
Second Reading
15 MARCH 1928
Mr. McDOWELL: Give their names.
Mr. DORMAN: It was stated some
thirty or forty years ago that the proportion of illegitimacy in Ireland could
be predicted by the number of Orange
Lodges. At this particular period when
the politician was propagating this theory
as to the origin of illegitimacy a great
anti. Protestant Mission was proclaimed
throughout the land in which it was
alleged that illegitimacy was due to Protestantism.
Both of these conclusions
were absolutely false, because they were
based on wrong knowledge. The large
proportion of illegitimacy was not due to
the prevalence of Orange Lodges or to
the Protestant religion, but to the conditions under which the working classes
lived.
In this particular area of Ireland, the
Six Counties, a system of hiring prevailed
amongst agricultural labourers that did
not prevail in other parts of the country,
and which debarred those lusty young men
and women of the agricultural labour
class from the opportunity of setting up
As the urge for rea legitimate home.
production becomes most clamant at a
certain age these people, under the impulse of that urge, and finding it impossible to set up legitimate homes,
illegitimately cohabited with one
other and produced a large percentage
of illegitimate children in this particular
part of Ireland.
an-
I hold that these circumstances have
been the cause of the large proportion of
illegitimacy, and even now I believe that
when the Registrar-General's returns.
come to be made up for the period
through which we have just passed and
are passing, when unemployment is rife
and house rents are so high, it will be
discovered that there is a very large proportion of illegitimate children over and
above what would have been the case had
normal conditions ruled in our midst.
The old statement that was made 800
years ago can never be made again,
namely, "We
are unwilling that the
law of England shall be changed."
There is scarcely an iniquity that has
been perpetrated on the community that
has not been bolstered up by that statement. Immediately any reforms have
been determined upon by the people they
have been met by this 800 years' old cry
about interfering with the laws
laws of
England.
We here and all modern Parliaments
exist for the specific purpose of changing
ancient laws. The progress of society has
made it inevitable that Parliament must
necessarily devote a large portion of its
time to repealing or amending ancient
laws.
The progress
of civilization and of
justice demand that they should be
modified to suit present conditions.
I do not think this Measure is calculated to increase immorality. I believe
it will have a contrary effect. Only
yesterday 1 came in contact with a man
who is the father of an illegitimate child.
The mother is paying for its upkeep in
a working-class home, and she is engaged
in a factory earning her livelihood. The
man comes to the house weekly or fortnightly and gives the woman a certain
proportion of his wages for the upkeep of
that child, and yesterday, in the presence
of others, he expressed his regret that his
economic circumstances, the low wages he
is receiving in return for his labour, and
the high rents that prevail, prevent his
entering into legitimate marriage. I do
not believe this Bill will be calculated to
stimulate illicit intercourse. I believe it
will be calculated to arouse contrary
thoughts in the minds of young men.
would be an admirable thing if we could
attain the moral position when all our
young men would adopt the attitude of
Job by making a covenant with their eyes
that they will not look upon a maid till
they have entered into the bonds of holy
matrimony.
It
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
had not intended to speak on this Bill,
but I should like to say a word in reply

he used the Latin quotation of the reply

That, of course, referred to the time of
Magna Charta. Many Acts that were
in force at that period have since been
repealed and new laws have been made
to take their place.

was 1236.

[Colonel Sharman Crawford)
many innocent people who through no
fault of their own are suffering under
a severe handicap. The fact of their
being born illegitimate has made no difference to those who were born in wedlock, because as long as any legitimate
children or their descendants were alive
illegitimate children could not succeed to
an estate.
I agree with Senator Dorman when he
says this Bill will increase morality instead of having the reverse effect. With
regard to the case mentioned by Senator
Leslie in which an old lady seventy years
of age had to appear before a court in
England, I think she deserved the
greatest praise and sympathy for the fact
that her maternal feelings were so strong
that when she had the opportunity of
iegitimating her children she did not
hesitate, but rejoiced that she was able
to do so. There may be faults in the Bill,
but I think it contains more good than
evil, and I hope it will pass through this
House and become the law of the land.
It has struck me that, after all, we
are all illegitimate, because I never knew
that Adam and Eve were ever married.
Marriage was not necessary then, and it
is only a custom that has since been
adopted for the sake of the morality of
the world.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
a matter of this kind it is no use saying
that because a few centuries ago the law
was so and so we should not change it.
We must realize that those old barons of
England and I think they deserve a
good deal of praise and admiration-had
slaves, and slavery has been done away
with in this country. Scotland has had
this law I think from Reformation times.
The law of Scotland is that if people
marry it legitimises children born before
the marriage, and as far as I can find out
the morality of Scotland is no lower than
that of England. We know that Stranraer and other parts of Scotland do get
a certain name, but I have seen the
figures and I believe the morality of
those parts of Scotland is just as good
as it is in our own country or in England.
I do not think this Bill will in any
way inflict hardship on anyone. On the
contrary, I am convinced it will confer
a great benefit on those who through no
fault of their own have been born in this
unfortunate position. I cannot help
thinking that if we went back 1,900 years
the Teacher of that day would
have
worked on something like the lines of
this Bill. We know He treated things
as perhaps some of our Puritan people of
these days would not treat them, and we
would only be following His example if
we passed this Bill, as I trust we shall
do.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
feel justified in speaking in favour of
the Second Reading of this Bill. I am
sure it will give the working classes some
little satisfaction to know that a Bill
similar to that which has been passed in
England has been introduced in Northern
Ireland. So far as the working classes
are concerned I do not agree that there
is any more immorality in this country
than there is in any other country. I
think we might be proud of the fact
that Northern Ireland is more moral
than any other part of the United
Kingdom. As has been said by
previous speakers, this Measure will be an
act of charity to many poor innocent
individuals who are born in the unfortunate position which this Bill seeks to
remedy. It removes from them a stigma
which their fellow workers might hold
over their heads for the remainder of
their lives. It is unfair that a child who
was born a month before the marriage of
its parents should have to go through the
world as illegitimate, whereas the child
born a month after the marriage is
legitimate. Therefore, I think this Bill
will remove an unfair distinction. For
the life of me I cannot understand what
Orange lodges have to do with this question. I have heard all sorts of accusations made against this unfortunate body,
but the latest and the worst is that
attributed to them by Senator Dorman.
Mr. DORMAN: I only quoted what an
eminent politician said. I have the
highest respect for the Orange Order.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
glad to hear that, because I understand
the hon. Senator was at one time a member of the Orange Order. So far as the
Orange Order is concerned I think it is
very likely to be more moral than any
other section of the community, because
morality is one of the first things that is
instilled into a member when he first
enters the portals of a lodge. I give the
Bill my entire support, particularly on
behalf of the working classes, because I
am satisfied the workers in this country
are as moral as they are in any other
part of the United Kingdom.
Adjour ment
Legitimacy Bill-

15 MARCH 1928
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill, and my
reason for so doing is that it offers protection to a small section of the community who are not in any way to blame
for their existence. I do not agree with
the statement made by Senator Dorman
that the working classes and the poor are
the greatest sinners in this respect. They
are not. The hon. Senator made reference to farm labourers and the young
women who are employed by farmers in
the country, and those who know no
better would gain the impression that
farm labourers and servant girls were
the most immoral people in the country.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. BARCLAY: They are not. As
for the city of Belfast, I roam about just
as much as the hon. Senator, and I do
not see much wrong in the city. There
may be an odd case here and there, but
you will find that anywhere you go. I
listened to a lecture given here some time
ago by a reverend gentleman who dressed
up in dongarees and went round what he
called the slums of the city of Belfast.
He told some wonderful tales. I have
been round those streets, and I have passed those public houses, although I was
never in them, and I have not seen anything that he saw. I must be nearsighted, and I think Senator Dorman
must also be able to see further than I
if he says Belfast is most immoral, and
that there is more illegitimacy amongst
the working classes than amongst those
who are better off.
To my mind the present law is very
unjust to the people this Bill seeks to
assist. We cannot deny the fact that
these people have been in the past, and
are at the present moment,
useful
very
and industrious citizens. I am very
pleased that this Bill will abolish a great
injustice, and will give these people a
proper place in society and an interest in
the inheritance of their parents. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
very grateful to the House for the reception it has given to this Bill. I think
the reasons adduced in its favour considerably outweigh those advanced against
it. Personally I do not think that any
increase in immorality will result from
the Bill becoming law for the reason that
the philanthropic societies who originally
urged its adoption in the Imperial House
of Commons did so from experience of
the conditions of the people who are most
likely to fall into difficulties of that kind.
If they had thought morality would be
endangered it is not likely they would
have advocated the Bill.
I would like to say that I did not
understand Senator Dorman to say anything about the Orange Order that could
be objected to. He merely quoted what
other people had said, and no doubt it
was absolutely incorrect. After all,
people talk a great deal of nonsense about
all organizations in the world, including
the Orange Order, and it is only right
that those people should be refuted, as
Senator Dorman did his best to do.
Therefore, I think it is a pity that statements should be attributed to him which
he did not make at all. In reply to
Senator Leslie, I would like to inquire
into the matter of armorial bearings
before the Committee stage is reached.
On Question, Motion: "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for the next sitting day.
Adoption of Children Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I do
not intend to proceed with the Second
Reading of this Bill to-day. As a matter
of fact it has not yet been printed.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker may
appoint.
Mr. LESLIE: May I take this opportunity of asking the noble Viscount when
he proposes to take the Committee stage
of the Bill to which the House has just
given a Second Reading?
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I do
not know the exact date, but will acquaint
the hon. Member definitely later.

Fifteen Minutes after

date as

may appoint.
Business of the House-
Ancient Monuments
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 27th March, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. J. A. LONG
(Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Charter of the Borough of Bangor.
Ministry of Finance:
Register of Temporary Laws for the
4th Session 2nd Parliament of Northern
Ireland.
Presented (pursuant to Act) and
ordered to lie on the Table,
Belfast Corporation Bill (Petition
for Bill.)
The Deputy-Speaker acquainted the
House, That the Clerk of the Senate had
laid upon the Table the Certificate from
the Examiner that, having regard to the
Report of the Standing Orders Committee of 15th March, Standing Orders,
so far as applicable, have been complied
with in respect of the Belfast Corporation.
(Petition for Bill).
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, That Standing Order No. XXV be dispensed with
for the purpose of passing the Consolidated Fund Bill and the Loans Guarantee Bill.
On Question. Motion agreed to.
Pictures of Ancient Monuments.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:
To ask the Leader of the House whether, in view of the interest that is taken
in Ancient Monuments, steps could be
taken to keep a record for Northern Ireland, by pictures and photograps, of
those interesting landmarks of the past,
which are passing away unnoticed.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to ask the noble
Leader of the House the question which
stands in my name on the Order Paper.
The reason I do so is because there has
been a great interest taken in ancient
monuments in Northern Ireland, and a
certain amount of protection has been
given to them, as well as to historical
buildings in Northern Ireland. I thought
that perhaps something on similar lines.
might be done as regards the many valuable pictures and photographs that we
have in this Province. I have not the
least doubt that we have in this part of
the country a great number of oil paintings, water-colour drawings, and photographs of distinguished men and women.
who have done a great deal to build up
this Province and the British Empire
during their lifetime, and I would like
an assurance that the same interest that
is being manifested in ancient monuments will be taken in these pictures and
photographs so that they may be preserved for the education of future generations. I hope the noble Viscount will be
able to give me a satisfactory reply to my
question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
that this question has been asked, as it
will give me an opportunity, with the
permission of the House, to make a statement regarding the interesting and important subject to which the hon Member
has directed attention.
Since the passing of the Ancient Monuments Act (Northern Ireland), 1926 the
question of preserving a record of all
ancient monuments has been carefully
considered. The magnitude of the problem may be judged from the fact that
structures, which can be described by
this term, are much more numerous than
is generally known. There are probably
at least 2,000 within the Six Counties
of Northern Ireland.
Consolidated Fund Bill 50
Ancient Monuments--
27 MARCH 1928
The Branch of the Ministry of Finance
to which has been assigned the duty of
administering the Act at an early date
turned its attention to the collection of
written descriptions, measured drawings,
photographs, etc., of all monuments in
Northern Ireland, whether in charge of
the Ministry or not, and has already collected a considerable amount of material
of this kind. It is hoped, also, that with
the co-operation of the Ancient Monuments Advsory Committee a systematic
survey of the monuments of Northern
Ireland may be set on foot. In the course
of this survey much descriptive and illustrative matter will be collected, and it
may be possible, if an adequate amount
of support from the book-buying public
can be secured, to publish the results of
this survey in the same manner as that
adopted for the antiquities of various
counties in Great Britain; but in any
case I can assure the hon. Member that all
inatter collected will be retained, and
the Ancient Monuments Branch will
always endeavour to secure a record of
any monument in danger of immediate
destruction. The Branch would also welcome the co-operation of local antiquaries in drawing its attention to endangered monuments or contributing
descriptive matter relating to ancient
structures.
I should like before passing from the
subject, to direct the hon. Member's attention to the very valuable work of the
kind contemplated by his question which
has already been done by the Belfast
Art Gallery and Museum, and particularly to the unique set of drawings of
old Belfast collected by Mr. Thomas
McGowan and generously presented by
him to the Museum. The Ancient Monuments Branch is working in close cooperation with the Museum, and I am
pleased to say that there have already
been many opportunities of mutual assistance.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
reply, may I ask the noble Viscount what
amount of money is now at the disposal
of the Advisory Committee, or perhaps
the question might be raised a little later
on this afternoon.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think I
must have notice of that question, and I
would prefer that the hon. and learned
Senator would raise it at the next meeting of the Senate, as the information can
hardly be at my disposal this afternoon.
Message from the Commons.

the

Hampton House School Bill,
Dromore Urban District Council Bill,
Antrim Electricity Supply Bill,
London, Midland and Scottish Railway
Bill.
They have passed the
Consolidated Fund Bill,
Loans Guarantee Bill,
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
Consolidated Fund Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this Bill be now read a
Second time. As hon. Members are
aware, this is purely a Money Bill which
it is not competent for this House to
amend. I do not think that its various
provisions need any detailed explanation,
as they are quite clear and explicit.
Accordingly, I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Out of curiosity I would like to ask why,
if this House is not competent to deal
with this Bill, it is necessary to bring it
before us at all.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask the
noble Viscount whether, in this Consoli
dated Bill which we cannot amend, but
which, of course, if so disposed, we can
reject, provision is made for any sum of
Consolidated Fund Bill
Loans Guarantee Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
money to be placed at the disposal of the
Advisory Committee on Ancient Mounments. It seems to me that if we do not
get an answer to that now, we are a little
in the dark. The matter is not of very
great importance, and if it would be
better to raise the point, as it has just
been suggested, at a later date, that
can be done. In the meantime I presume I am correct in assuming that such
sum of money as has been voted in the
appropriate Chamber for use during the
financial year with which the Bill deals,
covers the money I have referred to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Col. Sharman Crawford), I may say that
while the Senate cannot amend the Bill
before the House they can reject it if
they think fit to do so. That is really
the reason for bringing the Bill before
this House. With regard to Senator
Leslie's question about the funds of the
Ancient Monuments Advisory Com
mittee, while I should have preferred to
have had notice of the question, I think
it is safe to assume that the money mentioned in the Bill before the House will
be available for Ancient Monuments as
for other matters.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now Committed. (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion negatived.

Third time. (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.—
(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill without
Amendment.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, That this Bill be now read a
Second time. As hon. Members are
aware, this is a Money Bill, and it is not
within the power of this House to amend
any of its provisions. I do not propose
go into its details at any great length.
Its object, like that of the original Measure which it continues, is to create employment.
to
The maximum limit on the aggregate
capital amount of the loans, which may
be guaranteed, was increased by the Act
of 1927 from £9,000,000 to £10,500,000,
and the position at the present time is
that guarantees have been issued, or are
about to be issued, aggregating a sum of
£8,770,657, leaving a margin of approximately £1,700,000 for further undertakings. The Government feel that this
existing margin is sufficient to cover any
new application which may be granted,
more especially as the bigger shipyard in
Belfast is likely to be fully occupied during this year with the work already in
hand.
The power of the Ministry of Finance
to guarantee loans expires, under existing
legislation, on the 31st of the present
month. The hope was expressed on a
former occasion that it would be possible
to withdraw these facilities at the end of
this current financial year, and in expressing that hope the Government had
particularly in mind the fact that the
British powers in this respect have been
allowed to lapse. The Government are
of opinion, however, that our circumstances here, with the limited number of
major occupations, are not wholly
analogous to the wider basis of British
industry, and accordingly propose that
the time should be further extended to
31st March, 1929.
I am sure that hon. Members generally are in sympathy with the object of
this Bill, which is to continue for a limited
period the provisions of the original Act
which has been so useful in assisting our
industries over the exceptional period of
depression which they have passed
through.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion negatived.

the Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill without any
Amendment.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.

a First time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Legitimacy Bill [S.].
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly,
[Mr. LONG in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Legitimation by subsequent marriage of parents).
*
*
*
*
(2) Nothing in this Act shall operate
to legitimate a person whose father or
mother was married to a third person
when the illegitimate person was born.
*
*
* *
Mr. GLENDINNING: I beg to move
the deletion of Sub-clause (2) of Clause 1.
This is one of the very few Measures
which came to us in a virgin state, and I
am glad to think that, thanks mainly to
the hon. Senator from Ballymoney
(Senator Leslie), it will not pass to another place without criticism. Other hon.
Members may hold opinions concerning it
which they may hesitate to express from
feelings of delicacy, but I believe it to be
a duty to point out what I consider to
be faults in the Measure, although the
Government in their anxiety to follow
exactly the lines of the English law may
not be prepared to accept my Amendment.
I have read this Bill with considerable
interest. It is a human document,
and I have also listened carefully to the
noble Lord the Leader of the House in
his exposition of its Clauses. There are,
however, two enactments in the Bill
which appear to me to fall short of the
object intended, and this Sub-section (2)
of Clause 1 is one of them.
This Measure, broadly, is brought in to
legitimate the issue resulting from an
immoral act, when the parents subsequently marry. I may
be defective
in logic or understanding,, but I
really fail to see why the fact that one
of the parents was married when the misconduct took place should exclude the
issue from the benefits of the Bill. Indeed I will go further and say that it is
perfectly conceivable that an immoral relationship should occur and be, if not
justified, certainly palliated by the very
fact that one of the guilty parties was
married.
Take the case of a man accustomed to
marriage, whose wife becomes insane,
while the man remains youthful and
virile. He would be a stern purist who
would not find several excuses for a
breach of morality by a man so condemned morally speaking to a life of
rigid chastity. I hope I shall not be
accused of condoning open immorality,
but we must take human nature as it is.
Indeed, an orator might find inspiration.
for a high flight of eloquence in painting
the cruelty of this sub-section towards the
result of an unhappy marriage. He might
take as an example the case of a man of
a sensitive and highly-strung temperament, married to a woman who turned
out to be a drunken virago, a man,
who in his misery turned to another
woman for sympathy. There are plenty
of virtuous women who, loving such a
man and knowing the unhappy circumstances of his life, would freely sacrifice
all she possessed, even her honour, to
give him any happiness in her power.
Human nature is like that.
Legitimacy Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
Then if the drunken spouse died and
the widower gave his friend the only
reparation in his power, surely their offspring of such a connection is as much
entitled to be legitimated as any other illegitimate issue.
I am happily married myself, I am glad
to say, nor have I come much in contact
with ill-assorted couples, but judging
from what we read in novels, in the daily
news, and in the reports of divorce cases,
there must be innumerable cases where
illegitimate birth occurs as the result of
unhappy marriages, and why such issue
is explicitly barred and excluded from
the mercy of this Bill I fail to comprehend.
The poet Burns has truly said:
"Who made the heart, 'tis He alone,
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring its various bias.
Then at the balance, let's be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What's done we partly may compute,
But know not what's resisted.
For these reasons I beg to move the
deletion of the sub-section.
Mr. LESLIE: I appreciate very much.
the kindly reference which the hon.
Member who has just sat down was good
enough to make to my endeavours to
enliven the debate, but much as I appreciate that I am afraid I must ask the
Committee most definitely to reject the
Amendment which has just been moved.
If this Committee were to excise Subsection (2) of Clause 1 the effect would be
to strike at the very root of the institution
of Christian marriage. It is one of the
basic principles of marriage undertaken
in accordance with the formulas of almost
any church-certainly of any Catholic
church, whether it traces itself back to
Armagh, Canterbury, Constantinople, or
Rome-they all agree that the basic
principle of Christian marriage is the
exclusive union of one man with one
woman, and some hold that that marriage
is absolutely indissoluble by an earthly
court. They say marriage must endure
till death. and when hard cases such as
my hon. Friend has drawn attention to
are pointed out, they say in reply: "After
all, the man and woman took each other
They
for worse as well as for better.
entered into a relationship which they
ought not to break."
The question was raised, as the Committee is aware, in the House of Commons
in England when the corresponding
Measure was under debate there, and I
have just referred to the Speech which
the Home Secretary, Sir William Joynson
Hicks delivered on the occasion. I see
that he referred specially to the inclusion
of this particular Sub-clause as a Subclause which might give rise to differences
of opinion, and he invited the British
House of Commons to pass the Bill as it
was on the ground that the excision of
this Clause would stir the religious feeling of the country, and he pointed out
the duty of Parliament and the Government to pay attention to general religious feeling.
A great deal was said in the debate in
the House of Commons, and something
was said here on the Second Reading of
the Bill, as regards old Canon law on this
subject. I have endeavoured, since the
House passed the Second Reading, to
ascertain exactly what the old Canon law
was as regards the legitimation of the
offspring of adultery under a subsequent
marriage, but I regret to say I have not
succeeded, and I am in some doubt as to
whether the hon. Member for Swindon's
reference in the House of Commons debate
was not rather a reference to the date
of conception as opposed to the date of
birth, and I am not sure that he intended
to go so far as I thought he did. However, those are matters on which hcn.
Senators can form an opinion for themselves by reference to the Hansard
debates.
I think that apart from the question of
religious opinions there is another matter
to be considered, and that is that a child,
legitimate or illegitimate, is the product
of two parties. You have to look at this
matter on the assumption that the child
it is sought to legitimate is the child of
a particular woman as well as of a particular man. Let the Committee ponder
on the appalling legal complications which
might ensue if a married woman had an
illegitimate child and then subsequently
married the adulterer. It would really
be an extremely difficult problem to
unravel. If a child is born in wedlock
during the union of its parents-it is pre-
Legitimacy Bill—
Committee
27 MARCH 1928
And
sumably legitimate to start with.
suppose you did allow this possibility of
legitimation per subsequens matrimonium,
look at the inducement you are holding
out to crime. You have a man married
and he commits adultery with another
woman. There is a child. What arrangements he may have made with the woman
you do not know. He may have told her
that if possible he would marry her, but the
fact remains that a child is born illegitimately while the man is married. There is
most direct inducement to the adulteress
to commit murder to get rid of the man's
lawful wife in order that he can marry
the other woman. It seems to me that
there is nothing to be said in favour of
the proposed Amendment so far as regards this Bill.
a
The question which I think Senator
Glendinning really raised is the question
whether you ought to alter the law relating to divorce so as to allow, so far
as an earthly court can allow, of the dissolution by statutory authority of a
marriage on the ground of breach.
contract other than the one breach of
contract which you do at present admit.
At present, of course, in this country
divorce can only be in the nature of a
judicial separation divorce a
mensa et
thoro-the old Canon law-but such a
divorce can subsequently be made a complete divorce by Parliament in this
country and by the High Court in England. Such a separation is only given if
adultery has been committed. It is quite
a modern invention to give a woman relief
simply on the ground of the adultery of
her husband. Formerly a husband got
relief on account of the adultery of his
wife, but the wife did not get relief on
account of the adultery of her husband
unless it was coupled with cruelty or
personal violence.
That has been altered and at the
present moment the two parties are on an
equal footing; that is, relief can be obtained when the marriage contract has
been broken in one particular, but
Senator Glendinning suggests other examples such as madness, drunkenness, and
so on, where in effect the marriage contract of companionship has been broken.
For that breach the law does not give
relief. It seems to me that if it is
desired to make a change there it is the
law relating to divorce that should be
considered, not the law relating to the
legitimation of children. I hope the
Committee will not accept this Amendment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think
the Amendment proposed by Senator
Glendinning cuts at the root of all ideas
of Christian marriage. We all know
perfectly well, as Senator Leslie has said,
that a person marries "for better or
worse, for richer or poorer," and I do not
think it would be in the least advisable
to accept this Amendment. For one
thing it would introduce a matter of
extreme controversy into the Bill, and it
would set the Churches, which obviously
would take one side, against the people
who would take another side. For that
reason I must resist the Amendment.
In view of
Mr. GLENDINNING:
the number of objections named by
Senator Leslie and the noble Leader of
the House-objections which I may confess I had not sufficiently carefully considered-I will ask permission to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the

of legitimated persons, 31 & 32 Vict.,

*

Provided that, where a petition is
presented to the county court, the
county court, if it considers that the
case is one which owing to the value
of the property involved or otherwise
ought to be dealt with by the High
Court, may, and if so ordered by the
High Court shall, transfer the matter
to the High Court, and on such transfer
the proceeding shall be continued in
the High Court as if it had been
originally commenced therein.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 2, line 23, at the end of
the Clause to add the following new subsection: :-
"(3) The jurisdiction by this Section
conferred upon the county court shall
be deemed to include power to hear
Legitimacy Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
and determine a petition otherwise
than in open court.'
I mentioned on the Second Reading
that I proposed to move an Amendment
such as this. The object of the Amendment is to give a county court hearing a
petition for declaration of legitimacy the
same power of hearing the application in
private as a judge of the High Court
possesses when a similar case comes before
that court. I think I pointed out on the
Second Reading that a judge of the High
Court had direct power to hear such an
application in Chambers owing to
reference in the Legitimacy Act of 1868
to earlier legislation. We are now giving
the county court a new power to hear
petitions, and I am asking the Committee to give a county court judge power
to hear a petition in private if he sees
fit to do so. All the provisions of the
Act of 1868 will remain; that is the
notification of the Attorney-General and
the citation of all the parties concerned.
a
All that is asked is that the county
court judge shall have power similar to
that which we gave in the Affiliation
Orders Act to resident magistrates to
make arrangements for illegitimate
children in private. Power similar to
that has been given in England in the
Adoption of Children Act, an Act which
I understand in the perhaps distant future
we propose to follow. Whether the court
be the High Court, the county court, or
a Court of Summary Jurisdiction power
is given to hear and determine the matter
otherwise than in public, but in the
Adoption of Children Act the power is to
be regulated by Rules of Court.
It will be seen on reference to the Bill
before us that something is said in the
first sub-clause of Clause 2 about Rules
of Court, and, therefore, it might perhaps
be thought that this might be dealt with
under Rules of Court. But I think that
if it is desirable that this power should
be given at all it should be directly
given by Parliament and inserted in the
Bill, because Rules of Court in England
are subject to the authority of the Parliament of Great Britain, but Rules of Court
in Northern Ireland are not subject to
the authority of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland. They form part of a
reserved service which we cannot touch.
Therefore, I venture to ask the Committee
to accept the Amendment and agree to
insert the words on the Paper.
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the Committee will accept this Amendment. The
disturbance of domestic amenities is very
often due to the action of third parties,
and if the Bill is left as it is at present
1 can forsee that those who have been
morally weak enough to conceive children
outside wedlock would be open to an exposure, if the petition was heard in a
public court, that would be calculated to
destroy the peace of the homes of those
parents who had agreed to bring a petition
to legitimize their offspring born before
the parents had entered into the bonds
of holy matrimony. I think that if the
Government look at the Amendment in
that light they will realize it is one that it
would be advisable to accept in the interests of the peace of the homes of those
who may wish to take advantage of this
Bill when it becomes an Act.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: While
there are undoubtedly cases which it is
desirable should not be heard in open
court, it is agreed that it is not desirable
to encourage the hearing of cases in
camera by increasing the power of judges
to use their discretion in this matter. It
has been laid down by Lord Halsbury
that as a general principle all courts must
be open to the public, although the judge
may for good and sufficient reason order
that the public or a certain section of the
public shall be temporarily excluded.
This is further established in a Chancery
judgment in 1894 in re Martindale. I
trust that now that it has been explained
to the hon. and learned Senator that
there is power at the present time for the
judge, if he thinks fit, to carry out what
is in effect the intention of the Amendment, he will agree to withdraw it.
Mr, LESLIE: Do I understand the
noble Viscount to say that a county court
judge has such power? I quite agree it
is so in the case of the High Court, but
my difficulty is that the county court
judge is being given a new power he did
not previously possess, and I cannot find
that he has any power to hear these cases
in camera unless we give it to him.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
county court judge already has the
power.
Mr. LESLIE: If that is the case,
will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave withdrawn.

Clause 3, page 2, lines 25 and 26, to leave

trivial matter. There is an apparent
inconsistency in Clause 3 as it stands. It
is an inconsistency which we did not
create, but which we took over from
England. If the Committee will refer
to Sub-clause 3 of Clause 1 they will see
that the expression used is "children or
remoter issue." The words in the Subclause before us are "children or more
remote issue," and passing to Clauses 4,
5, and others, the expression again is
remoter." It makes no possible difference to the sense, but it is always
desirable that we should be consistent in
our phraseology, and therefore I suggest
this Amendment.
""
I believe this inconsistency arose owing
to the fact that during the passage of the
Bill in England various Amendments
were introduced, and that is possibly
how the matter occurred. If there is any
intention that the two words should mean
something different we shall no doubt be
told, but so far as I can see that is not
So.

The hon. and learned Senator's legal

I can understand what "more remote"

(3) For the purposes of this section,
the expression "country" includes any
part of His Majesty's Dominions, as
well as a foreign country.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 8, page 5, line 14, in Sub-section
(3), to leave out the word "any" and
insert "England and Wales, Scotland and
any other."
This Amendment raises a
problem which is not a large one, and
which I think we have not dealt with
before. That is why I propose to
direct attention to it this afternoon.
The words used in sub-section (3) of
Clause 8 are "any part of His Majesty's
Dominions." So far as I can form an
opinion I rather expect the noble Viscount will confute me with a whole sheaf
of precedents-but I rather think it is not
usual to lump Great Britain and the
Dominions together; it is usual to give
Great Britain a little place to itself. The
point is, of course, that although we all
understand well enough what we mean
by His Majesty's Dominions, the expression "Dominions" has usually to be translated according to the context. In the subclause before us there is no context, and
I propose to give one. The words in the
corresponding English Act are "includes
Great Britain, Scotland, and any other
part of
His Majesty's Dominions."
Scotland no doubt was specially mentioned owing to the difference in law in
this matter prevailing between England
and Wales and Scotland. Therefore, I
thought that although it is much more
usual to say Great Britain rather than
England, Wales, and Scotland, perhaps
it would be better to put the whole thing
on the Paper.
The difference between the Dominions
outside and England I have generally
taken to be this: That His Majesty's
power over the Dominions is usually obtained either directly or indirectly by force
(4
Legitimacy Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
of arms, either by direct conquest or by
treaty as the result of a successful
campaign. I do not know what can be
said about Wales, but Scotland was not
conquered by England nor was England
or Scotland obtained by force of arms.
England and Scotland adopted the
Crown because the King of England was
originally elected. The present King
of course holds by statutory title, as we
know, but the original kingship came
differently to England and to the
Dominions. I do not know that we have
done this before, and I direct attention
to it by this Amendment. On the other
hand, I cannot but admit that
Northern Ireland is not separately mentioned in the English Act, Northern
Ireland can only come into the benefits
of the English Act as a Dominion. Therefore, there is perhaps something of an
element of tit-for-tat about the words
used in the Bill before us. I beg to
move.
as
I am
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
glad to say for my own sake that the
Government have not seen fit to go into
the matter of whether Wales and Scotland were ever properly defeated by
England, except in certain places at
certain specified times. I am prepared
to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 10, page 5, line 38, at end of
sub-clause (1) insert the following new
sub-section :-
"(2) Nothing in this Act shall prejudice or affect the authority of Ulster
King-of-Arms."
Mr. LESLIE: This is another matter
so
of some slight interest which was mentioned on the Second Reading. I think
I may go so far as to tell the Committee
that the noble Viscount has been
kind as to look into the matter since the
House gave the Bill a Second Reading,
and I may add that with his unfailing
kindness he has let me know something
of the inquiries he has made. The point
is whether these matters over which the
Ulster King-of-Arms has authority are
or can properly be described as dignities.
I confess that I am not very certain. I
cannot make up my mind definitely
whether they are dignities.
If they are this Parliament cannot
touch them, and if they are not I am not
quite sure whether this Parliament can
touch them either, because the authority
in matters of this kind is part of the
Crown prerogative, and it has been committed in Ireland to the Ulster King-of-
Arms. We are prohibited, as the Committee knows, from legislation touching
the Crown. It seems to me that as we
are putting down a Saving Clause in
respect of Dignities and Titles of Honour
we ought perhaps to put down some
Saving Clause for the Ulster King-of-
Arms. At the same time I must confess
that I am not at all sure that it makes
any difference whatever whether we put
anything in or whether we do not.
The
In the corresponding English Act there
is nothing said about the authority of the
Heralds' College. The arrangements for
the decision of heraldic matters are not
the same in England and Ireland. Both
countries are miles behind Scotland, but
the mere fact that there was a Scottish
Act of Parliament passed in 1672 did
rather lead me to wonder whether we
ought not to put this Saving Clause in
this Bill. That is all it comes to.
intention of the Amendment is to do nothing but put in a Saving Clause, but
the noble Viscount will be able to advise
us whether we ought to do anything or
not. For the moment I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
view of the Government of Northern
Ireland is that the granting of the right
to armorial bearings is a question which
arises between the Crown and the subject,
the Crown having deputed its powers in
the matter to the Ulster King-of-Arms,
and this Department is not affected by
the present Bill at all. In the time that
has elapsed since the Second Reading I
took the opportunity to consult the

Mr. LESLIE : I ask leave to withdraw my Amendment, and I thank the

ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Before
actually moving the adjournment I should
like to have the opinion of hon. Members
as to the dates on which the House should
meet in the near future. I would like to
consult their convenience. It is desirable
to get the Unemployment Insurance Bill
through all its stages before Easter, and
it is a matter for hon. Members whether
they will take the Second Reading and
the Committee and other stages by suspending Standing Orders on Thursday,
or whether they would like to have a
longer interval between the Second
Reading and the Committee stage, in
which case we will take the Second Reading on Thursday and the other stages on
Tuesday of next week. I would like to
consult the convenience of hon. Senators
in the matter.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the Unemploy
ment Insurance Bill is a Measure of considerable importance to the great mass of
the working classes of Northern Ireland.
As we only got possession of the Bill today, I would ask the noble Viscount to
postpone the Second Reading for a few
days to give Members time to examine the
Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: If it is
the opinion of hon. Members that it is
desirable to have the Second Reading on
Thursday and the Committee and remaining stages on Tuesday of next week, I will
move, That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday.

Mr. LESLIE: I think I might say in
asking the question that stands in my
name, that when the Ancient Monuments
Bill was under discussion in this House
the opinion was expressed that the sum of
£600 per annum, which at that time was
all that was provided for the preservation
of ancient monuments, was a scanty
sum for the purpose for which it was
intended. Since that time we have had
more than one question asked in this
House, indicating an interest, amongst
some members of the general public at all
events, in the general question, and it occurred to me to ask for information as to
the amount of money at present being
applied for the purpose indicated by my
question.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: As the
hon. and learned Member stated, a sum
of £600 was voted by Parliament for the
preservation of ancient monuments during
the current financial year. With regard
to the proposals for 1928-29, Parliament
will be asked to vote a sum of £1,025 for
the maintainance of the various monuments. This sum is allotted as follows:-
Special Works, all Monuments, £400.
Special Works, Dunluce Castle, £400.
Special Works, Jordan's Castle, Ardglass, £100.
General maintenance, including caretaker's wages, £125.
