Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
Bill which I introduce to you to-day is
designed to bring the Unemployment
Insurance system in Northern Ireland
into line with that in Great Britain.
Hon. Members will remember that
there is in existence an agreement between Great Britain and Northern
Ireland, known as the Reinsurance Agreement, which provides that with varying
rates of unemployment in the two areas
losses out of one fund will be shared by
both countries. Under this agreement
very considerable sums have been paid
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
29 MARCH 1928
by Great Britain to Northern Ireland,
and it is essential in order that this valuable arrangement may be continued that
the Unemployment Insurance Scheme in
Northern Ireland should be maintained
on the same principles as those which
obtain in the remainder of the United
Kingdom.
In Great Britain a Measure amending
the Unemployment Insurance Acts was
recently passed into law following the
lines of the report of the Blanesburgh
Committee. That Committee, which
consisted of representatives of all classes,
including employers and workers, considered the whole question of Unemployment Insurance and made comprehensive
recommendations as to the steps which
should be taken towards simplifying the
present system, removing anomalies, and
making it more suited to the needs of the
future.
The scheme established under the Unemployment Insurance Act of 1920, which
was the first wide Measure of insurance
against unemployment, was designed in
a period of comparatively good employment. It provided benefits under certain
conditions which limited strictly the sums
which might be drawn. The most important of these was the "one-in-si"
rule, and the provisions which define the
amount which might be received in any
one year. This scheme might have proved
adequate if employment had continued
at a high level. In the trade depression
which unfortunately followed, the system
was found to be quite unequal to the
needs of the situation, and modifications
had to be made from time to time.
The development of the scheme took
place during difficult periods. With
work exceedingly scarce and workers out
of employment for prolonged periods it
became necessary to ease the conditions
for the receipt of benefit, and the system
now known as that of extended benefit
was introduced. Under this system
benefit became payable in certain circumstances where workers were not entitled
to benefit under the conditions of the
original statutes, now called standard
benefit, or where such benefit was
hausted. In order to prevent an undue
strain on the fund through claims where
the public interest did not demand that
payments should be made the principle
of Ministerial discretion was established.
ex-
In the determination of the cases in
which the discretion of the Ministry
should be exercised the voluntary services of the Local Employment Committees were used as advisory bodies. Unemployed workers could not claim extended benefit as of right and there was
no appeal against the decision of the
Ministry. This arrangement was never
intended to be more than an emergency
one, and hopes were held from the commencement that its life would not be
unduly prolonged. These hopes have
been deferred, however, and the system
which I have mentioned still continues.
The provisions of the scheme as it at
present exists are in many respects unsatisfactory. The conditions are unequal
in working and difficult to administer,
and could not be regarded as satisfactory
in normal circumstances. There are two
kinds of benefit-standard and extended.
There are two cases of title to benefit, one
which has reference almost entirely to
contributions paid, and the other which
requires close investigation, not only of
a claimant's past history, but also of his
future prospects, and personal financial
circumstances. There are two methods
of administration, one form of benefit
being claimable as of right with an appeal
against the insurance officer's decision to
the Court of Referees and the Umpire,
and the other being subject to powers discretionary and absolute on the part of
the Ministry, acting on the advice of the
Local Employment Committees.
The main lines of reconstruction are
designed towards removing these anomalies and simplifying the scheme and its
administration. The Ministerial discretion will be abolished and benefits will
in all cases be claimable as of right. The
conditions which regulate the amount of
benefit to be drawn to the number of
contributions paid will disappear, as will
also those which limit the amount of
benefit payable in any one year. Benefit
will become payable during periods of
unemployment without restriction as to
amount other than such as is required.
to ensure that the claimant can still be
regarded as in the insurance field.
The test for this is that 30 contributions must have been paid during the
previous two years. For disabled ex-
Servicemen whose disability prevents
them from obtaining employment the
71 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
number of qualifying contributions will
be 10. In case of illness during the two
years the period will be lengthened by
the duration of the illness up to a maximum of four years in all.
In future the distinction between
standard and extended benefit will cease,
and there will be only one kind of benefit
payable to all claimants. There will,
moreover, be only two main conditions
for the receipt of benefit-that the unemployed worker has had 30 contributions
in the last two years, and is genuinely
seeking work. If these conditions are
fulfilled the claimant will receive benefit
without regard to the amount which he
has obtained in any past periods.
The
The procedure for the determination
of claims will be that which exists at the
moment in connection with standard
benefit. Decisions on claims will be
given in the first instance by insurance
officers, and there will be the right of
appeal to local Courts of Referees.
Umpire's office will be continued, and a
further appeal will lie in certain cases to
him. Rota Committees will cease to act,
and local Employment Committees will
resume what should be their normal
functions of advising the Ministry in
connection with problems concerning
employment and trade conditions.
In referring to the passing of the Rota
Committees, the Government would take
the opportunity of expressing their appreciation of the arduous labours which
have been performed by the Committee
members, and of the good services which
they have rendered in the interests of
the insured classes and of the Unemployment Insurance scheme generally during
the difficult years through which that
scheme has passed.
The Bill deals with certain other matters, amongst the most important of
which are the rates of contributions and
benefits for young persons. At present
there are flat rates of contributions and
benefits for persons over 18 years of age,
separate rates being, of course, in existence for male and female workers.
Experience has shown that having regard
to the earning powers of persons between
18 and 21 it is not desirable that the
adult rates of benefits should be paid in
these cases. It is recognized that during
these years earning capacity in a large
proportion of cases increases steeply, and
it has been found desirable to introduce
a sliding scale of benefits which raises
step by step the rates payable to boys
and girls of 16 and 17 years to those
payable at the age of 21. The new rates
of benefits are set out in the 3rd Schedule
to the Bill.
A variation is also proposed in the
rates of benefits for male adults and for
adult dependants. The present rates are
18s and 5s per week respectively, and
these it is proposed to alter to 17s and 7s.
The change is one of distribution only,
and is based on the view that the differing responsibilities of married men and
single men respectively are more nearly
represented by the difference of 7s than
by the present difference of 5s. It is
considered more desirable to pay a single
man 17s and a married man 24s than to
give 18s to the single man and only 23s
to the married.
In the course of a short speech it is not
possible to refer to every point dealt with
in the Bill, and I select the following for
special mention. Hon. Members will
have the opportunity themselves of asking questions in connection with any
points on which they desire information.
A residence test is being introduced which
will require that claimants for benefit will
have been resident in the United Kingdom for three years prior to the date of
claim. Regulations to be made in connection with this test will secure that insured persons leaving the United Kingdom for temporary periods in the course
of their employment, to seek employment,
or for health or other reasons, will not be
prejudiced.
The provisions in regard to the persons
in respect of whom dependants benefit
may be drawn are being altered, and, on
the whole, the revisions are in the nature
of extensions in favour of the insured
classes. The conditions governing the
disqualification of unemployed workers
for benefits during trade disputes will be
revised to remove the disqualification in
cases where the claimants are not themselves interested in a dispute, and where
there are no members of their class at
the same premises who are so interested.
The Continuity Rule is being extended
to increase from six to ten weeks the
periods of employment which may occur
73 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 29 MARCH 1928

Second Reading
without the unemployed persons being
required to undergo further waiting
periods before receipt of further benefit.
The right to "contract out" under special
schemes is being abolished, but the position of the existing scheme for the insurance industry is secured.
Transitional provisions have been inserted to effect snooth transfer from the
old conditions to the new and to prevent,
during a considerable period, insured
persons from being deprived of benefit
solely as the result of the new condition
as to numbers of contributions. The new
first statutory condition which requires
30 contributions during the previous two
years will not be enforced for persons
over 18 years of age during the first 12
months after the commencement of the
Act nor for the remainder of benefit
year which has commenced for any claimant during those 12 months.
any
Claimants will, therefore, have a period
varying from one to two years before the
new conditions are brought fully into
cperation. During that period benefit
will be claimable as of right on conditions
which approximate closely to the existing
conditions for extended benefit, but without regard to the financial circumstances
of the claimant or his family.
Before I sit down I would invite the
attention of Members to the manner in
which the Bill has been drawn. In the
case of a number of matters such as the
statutory conditions for receipt of benefit,
the person in respect of whom dependants.
benefit may be paid, etc., in carrying out
certain Amendments the provisions of the
original Statutes have been consolidated.
In this way the intention of the new
Measure is more clearly indicated and
references to previous Acts have been
largely avoided.
In considering the propriety of introducing at the present moment the provisions which are embodied in this Bill,
Members will no doubt bear in mind the
observations which I made at the beginning of my speech in regard to the
Reinsurance Agreement with Great
Britain. If Northern Ireland were to
fail to maintain that similarity with
Great Britain in Unemployment Insurance matters which is essential to the
continuation of the agreement in question
the financial loss would be so great that
the stability of the Northern Ireland
Unemployment Fund could not be maintained except by the raising of contributions or the reduction of benefits. This
is a situation which the Government
could not contemplate. Their policy has
always been equality of social standards
with Great Britain. The Bill which is
now before this House will secure to
Northern Ireland the British rates of
benefit and will enable this to be done
without the imposition of additional
contributions. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: In the first place I
wish to congratulate the right hon.
Gentleman on the very clear exposition
he has given of this Measure. On the
eccasion when the last Unemployment
Insurance Bill was introduced in this
House I found it necessary
to vote
against its provisions because of certain
disabilities that it imposed upon those
who were at that time unemployed. While
I welcome many of the advantages that
are contained in this new Unemployment
Insurance Bill there are certain things
within its purview that I cannot give my
consent to, and on which I shall feel
bound to divide the House this afternoon.
The removal of the family-income test is
an improvement that every right-thinking
individual must approve of. In the past
very great injustice and wrong was inficted upon the unemployed and on those
who had to seek outdoor relief by the
application of the family-income test.
I hold in my hand a letter which is a
striking illustration of the evil of this
system, both as it applied to the unemployed and those who are destitute and
are necessarily seeking support from the
Guardians. I received this letter some
eighteen weeks ago from a
very selfrespecting, sober, and 'industrious man
who during the war volunteered with
three brothers to go and fight for the
protection of the Empire. His letter will
convey better than any words of mine
could do the advantage that will accrue
to the unemployed by the removal of this
particular test. This gentleman wrote
me in the following terms:
Sir,
Being out of work and getting no
bureau, I applied to the Guardians for
a little relief. I faced the Board and
answered all questions correctly. I live
in the house with my sister-in-law.
She is a widow with two children and
75 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
an old mother to keep out of her pension
of £2 4s a week. They took this
into account when speaking to me.
In
1914 I left good employment along with
three more brothers and joined up,
done three years in France and
Belgium, discharged in 1918, unable to
work till 1920. Now I am able to work
I cannot get it. I have been employed
lately on tramway construction as a
general labourer. At the Board they
asked me what pension I had. I told
them the truth, 10s, but out of that
I have to pay 6s for room and fire, never
speaking about societies, which leaves
me about 2s to keep a wife and child.
Surely they do not expect my sisterin-law to keep me and mine. I want
no help of them if they will provide
work. Failing to provide work, a little
assistance was all I asked, for my wife
is unable to work through ill-health.
A lady Guardian suggested for my wife
to come into the house to gather
strength. God knows when it comes
to my wife or child or myself having
to go into the poor-house through no
fault of my own when I am able to
work I will seek another way out of
the difficulty. This is a very poor return for men that done their best in
time of need. Sir, this is a true statement. My wife is against my name
being spoken of. If you, Sir, can see
me anytime at my address and hear for
yourself. To you alone I give my
address.
I
He gives his address. The other way
out of the difficulty is going to be the
man's head in the gas oven. That will be
removed. That dark cloud of mental
instability which very often results from
investigation into the family means will
be removed by this amending Bill, and
in that particular I give it my wholehearted support and encouragement.
am very glad that the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Labour, is here this
afternoon, because in carefully reading
over the debate in another place I find
the right hon. Gentleman fell into an
error with regard to the genesis of this
question that is being considered in this
House. He stated, I think, the genesis
of unemployment insurance arose from
the action of trade unionists in 1905 or
the succeeding years. That is not so.
The genesis of the demand for unemployment benefit was when the late Mr.
James Kier Hardie tabled in the British
House of Commons the Right to Work
Bill, in the year 1893.
For many years before that the party
of which Mr. Kier Hardie was the
founder had been proclaiming at the
street corners the necessity for some provision being made for those unemployed,
and who could not get an opportunity to
work in order that they might live. In
my reading I have always been convinced
of the fact that the great right of every
citizen of the great British Commonwealth
of Nations was the right of life. Now,
of what use is the abstract right of life to
those people in the community who have.
no right of access to the means of life?
It seems to me to be a mere fiction to be
given life and to be shut out from the
right of access to the means of obtaining
that which is necessary for the continuance of life. That is one of the reasons
why the party of which Mr. Kier Hardie
was the founder disseminated through
Great Britain and through some parts of
Ireland the importance of the State
guaranteeing the individual the abstract
right to life, and seeing to it that if
those privileged constitutionally to the
right of ownership of the means of life
should shut the people out from access to
those means of life, then the State should
in justice provide sustenance and support
for every one of its citizens.
May I remind the House that one of
the greatest British Jurists, in the early
part of last century, in that work of his
on legislation, said the first and most
important duty of every legislature was
to provide sustenance for the citizens, and
the second duty was to provide abundance sustenance and abundance for the
citizens of every community. I do not
think this Bill has set out with that
specific object in view. It is going to inflict a serious hardship upon the most
helpless portion of the community at a
period in their age which all physiologists recognize as being the period at
which they require of necessity, because
of the structure of their organism, the
greatest possible proportion of food, because of the strain that is placed upon
the organism in development from about
16 to 21 years of age.
I have another reason for objecting to
it, and that is this. I believe that very
much of the immorality of the society in
which we live, and very much of the
prostitution which unfortunately afflicts
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
29 MARCH 1928
some of the big centres of population, is
owing to the pressure, the economic
screw, grinding down the wages of these
people at a vital period of their lives,
when there are certain urges in their
nature, and when they require all the
assistance, moral and physical, they can
get, to keep them on the straight line.
In reading his speech I find the right
hon. Gentleman gave as a reason why this
reduction of the unemployment payment
at these younger ages has been found
necessary is that certain parents aproached him, pointing out to him the
impossibility they found of keeping their
children inside the paths of rectitude and
parental obedience at this particular age.
I find the Parliamentary Secretary of the
Ministry of Labour brought forward
another instance. Perhaps I would be permitted to quote his words. He states that
the father-I do not wish unnecessarily to
occupy the time of the House-the father
said a certain boy refused work, where he
was only in receipt of 9s or 10s a week,
having facilities to obtain 19s at the
Labour Exchange. The father talked
the matter over with the boy, but he ha
absolutely no control over him.
Does the right hon. Gentleman and his
Parliamentary Secretary realize what a
reflection that is upon the churches of
Belfast, upon the educational institutions
of our country, upon the Sunday schools
of our country, upon the control of
parents of the country over their offspring? Is it because there are a few
who lack the necessary moral fibre, and
have not had the advantage of the training in their youth that should be given
every boy-is it because a few take unjust advantage of facilities that are held
out by the unemployment insurance to
their age, their situation in life, this reduction is made, while from week to week
we see and are led to believe that probably
for everyone who thus malingers and
obtains unjustly unemployment benefit at
these there are hundreds leaving our
shores to seek employment in the Colonies
or some other part of the Empire? The
right hon. Gentleman has endeavoured
to justify this reduction on economic
grounds. Well, I have been a diligent
student of economics for many years, and
I just looked at one of the volumes on
that subject which it was my privilege to
study 50 years ago. I hope the House
will bear patiently with me while I give
this quotation which I think bears
directly upon the subject we are considerages,
ing this afternoon. The author is not a
Socialist, but an orthodox economist. He
might be called a Marxian before Marx.
I am quoting from M'Culloch's Political
Economy. He stated in that work:-
Let a population once become contented with a lower description of food,
and an inferior standard of comfort,
and they may bid a long adieu to anything better, and every reduction in
wages, which is not of a transient description, contributes to bring about
this undesirable result.
Then, again, in another part of the same
volume he said: .-
The best interests of Society require
that the rate of wages should be elevated
as high as possible, that a taste for
comforts and enjoyments should be
widely diffused, and, if possible, engrafted into the national character.
The age at which we are about to reduce the unemployment income is the
most critical age in their lives. It is the
age when habits are formed that go to
constitute the permanent character of the
future citizens, and the period at which
we should safeguard their right to build
up a desire for these things which this
celebrated economist said 100 years ago
were necessary for the State and the welfare of society.
The labourers are the thews and
sinews of industry. Their numbers are
not estimated, like those of other
classes, by hundreds, by thousands, or
even by hundreds of thousands, but by
millions. It is by them that our
machinery is constructed and kept in
motion, that our agriculture, manufactures, and commerce are successfully carried on, . . . Everything,
then, that may have the slightest
tendency to depress their condition-
And I hold that this reduction of the
unemployment allowance to citizens of
that particuar age has a direct tendency
to depress their conditions.
Everything then that may have the
slightest tendency to depress their condition or sink them into the social scale
should be particularly guarded against.
Those who feed and clothe all the rest
should be themselves well fed and well
clothed.
Now, it was my lot very early in life,
some 34 years ago, to have allotted to me
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
with another member of the political
party with which I had the honour to be
connected for the last 34 years
the task of making investigations in
the capital of this country as to
what were the causes, as I have
already mentioned, underlying the life.
many of our sisters must lead, and I
found from extensive inquiry with that
gentleman-who, by the way, was an
Englishman and an inspector of the
multiple shops in the City of Dublin, in
one of which I happened to be manager
at the time. We carried our investigations through very carefuly, and we
were driven to the conclusion that the
low standard of subsistence and remuneration was the cause of some nine-tenths
of the shame that manifested itself in
our public thoroughfares in capitals of
this country.
This is also true of the City of Belfast.
It has been my unwilling duty to draw
attention in public to names of firms in
Belfast itself, whose terms for young
women of the ages for which we are legislating the rates of unemployment benefit
for, were such that they had to sell their
virtue upon the streets at a time when
59 was equivalent to 8s or 9s now. Taking
into consideration the inflated
housing and the inflated cost of
lodging, and the increased cost of
living and all the circumstances surrounding the young unemployed of this particular area I must feel it my duty to
divide the House at the close of this
debate on this particular Measure.
cost of
Only this morning I happened to take
down an old book in which I opened a
page and found an extract which I think
exactly fits in with the situation we are
discussing here this afternoon. It was
a book written by a member of the Society
of Friends who were engaged in the
emancipation movement for the chattel
slaves of the United States of America
in the thirties of the last century. The
writer says:
For a
man habitually to stint his
dependants in their food is the extremes of meanness and cruelty, and
the greatest evidence he can give of
utter indifference to their comfort.
The father who stints his children or
domestics, or the master his apprentices, or the employer his labourers, or
the officer his soldiers, or the captain
his crew, when able to furnish them
with sufficient food, is everywhere
a
looked on as unfeeling and cruel. All
mankind agree to call such a character
inhuman. If anything can move
hard heart it is the appeal of hunger.
The Arab robber, whose whole life
is a prowl for plunder, will freely
divide his camel's milk with the hungry
stranger who halts at his tent door,
though he may have just waylaid him,
and stripped him of his money. Even
savages take pity on hunger. Who
ever went famishing from an Indian's
wigwam? As much as hunger craves
is the Indian's free gift even to an
enemy. The necessity for food is such
a universal want, so constant, manifest,
and imperative, that the heart is more
touched with pity by the plea of hunger,
and more ready to supply that want
than any other. He who can habitually
inflict on others the pain of hunger by
giving them insufficient food can inflict
on them any other pain.
As I have already stated, I feel it my
duty from this point of view, from the
Christian point of view, from the point
of view of morals and sound ethics, to
record this afternoon my disapproval of
the reduction of the standard of unemployment remuneration to people at this
critical age of their life.
Mr.
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: 1
have always felt that it is a great advantage to be one who has been reared
and brought up among the workingclasses. I am, therefore, quite well
acquainted with the conditions under
which they live. I never felt more
pleased than I feel to-day that I
have had that experience, because it is
impossible for Senator Dornan, while
he can gull others, to think he can possibly gull one who has been reared
amongst the workers, and knows exactly
the conditions under which they live. If
Mr. Dorman were sincere in the statements he has made here to-day, I would
consider him so innocent that he should
not be allowed out without someone to
take care of him, but I know that he is
speaking with his tongue in his cheek.
Mr. DORMAN: Thank you.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
idea of Senator Dorman making all
these statements to-day about wages and
conditions of the workers and how
some women are driven to prostitution
and such like! All that has no bearing
whatever on
the Act of Parliament
81 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 29 MARCH 1928
Second Reading

cause, so long as he was getting that
money, he could not get him to rise in
the morning or go to bed at night.
before the House. The wages, and the
benefit paid for the period of unemployment to tide them over their
difficulties are two different things. It
is a mistake for any people, I do not care
what views they may hold, to think they
should pay the same to a person unemployed as to a person employed.
Mr. DORMAN: Give them employment.
The
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
person who is employed requires some inducement, some incentive to do his work.
When he becomes unemployed and on the
streets, either through his own fault, or
on account of conditions over which he
has had no control, I think it is the duty
of the State to make some provision for
the man, his wife and family, to tide them
over the difficulty. I do not know why
Senator Dornan stated that the originator
of the Right to Work Bill was Kier
Hardie
Mr. DORMAN: I would remind the
hon. Senator that my name is Dorman,
and not Dornan.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
very sorry. I will try to remember that
in future. Mr. Derman claims that it was
the action of Mr. Kier Hardie in introducing the Right to Work Bill. If I remember aright, that Bill was introduced
by Mr. George N. Barnes, the general
secretary of the Engineers' Society, a man
I knew very well, a level-headed man and
a wise man, so wise, indeed, that the
Socialists have driven him out of the
Socialist party, howling him out of the
seat he sat for in Glasgow.
Senator Dorman can hardly credit the
statement made by the Minister of Labour
(Mr. Andrews) regarding the conduct of
such a boy receiving benefit, and he says
how injurious it is to the churches and
such like. But everyone knows that there
are cases of that kind-hundreds of cases,
not one isolated case. Only the cther
day I met a gentleman.
I need not say
what position he is in, but he had a boy
in the printing trade. His business got
slack and the foreman or employer sent
the boy out for a short time until things
would get a little better. That boy, who
was serving his time to the printing trade,
had only a few shillings a week. When
he went to the Exchange to claim his
benefit he got his full 18s, and the father
told me it was injurious to the boy be-
Mr. DORMAN: It is all in the way
you train them.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There
is the inducement. If a boy is receiving
18s a week for doing nothing that boy
has no intention of looking for work of
a nature such as will require him to rise
in the morning to attend to his business.
I will give Senator Dorman the name
and address of the man if he doubts me,
and there are hundreds of cases like that.
That is one of the good points in the Bill.
I do not know whether Senator Dorman
ever sat on a Rota Committee. I have
often sat on Rota Committees. Indeed,
I was one of the first to sit on a Referees
Court, and my experience is that you get
a young man before you who tells you
that he certainly is looking for work. He
may be doing so, but I always thought
that 18s a week for a young boy of
eighteen years of age was a tremendous inducement to malinger and to avoid work.
A boy of that age would probably be in
receipt of 12s a week if he were serving
his time to any occupation or trade, or
indeed a much lower wage, and you could
hardly expect such a boy to be in a hurry
looking for work when he is receiving such
an amount in benefit. Indeed, I think
I spoke to the Minister of Labour before
this Bill was introduced and pointed out
how beneficial a reduction would be to
those serving on Rota Committees, because we would then be able to grant a
boy an extension of benefit at the smaller
rate, and it would not be an inducement
to malinger or to avoid work.
1 have a very good recollection of the
Insurance Bill being introduced in 1912,
and of the small number of trades that
were included in that Bill. I believe the
engineering and shipbuilding trades were
the two first to be included, and they were
followed by the building trade.
I remember well how, when I was attending
one of our delegate meetings in Manchester, a most remarkable thing was that
the whole of the delegates of that trade
union were strongly opposed to the introduction of this insurance scheme, and
they demanded that an insurance officer
should be sent up from London to explain
the scheme to them before they started
to draw up rules for the administration of
benefit.
83 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
When we look at the very small number
of workers included in that scheme at
first, the great changes that have taken
place in the meantime, the improvement
made in 1920, and the experience that has
been obtained during years of good trade
and bad trade, we can only come to the
conclusion that such a Bill as this is
necessary at the present time to put the
benefit on something like a proper scale.
The regulations contained in this Bill are
probably an improvement on any of the
previous Bills. I do not really know how
this thirty stamps qualification will turn
out, so far as the workers are concerned,
but I understand that the Minister of
Labour has made arrangements that it is
not to be insisted on for the first year.
That is a concession which I am very glad
to see, and for the first year we will be
able to see how it will affect the workers.
We all must recognize that the workers
have a difficult time to tide over these
unemployed periods, especially if a man
has a wife and family. It involves
a little sacrifice to tide cver such a time
until a man obtains employment, and at
a time such as the present when trade
Is so very bad we have to make a little
extra allowance for these workers.
to
It was also necessary that something
should be done in connection with standard benefit and extended benefit. As
far as extended benefit was concerned,
there was no rule laid down as
how much a man would be entitled to.
On one occasion a man might come before
the court, find a very sympathetic chairman who was inclined to extend his benefit and give him a longer period. The
next time he would come before the Rota
Committee, and the chairman probably
would not be sympathetic and would cut
him off. Up to the present it has been
left just to sentiment really. In the future
we have a definite line laid down, so that
it will not depend upon the chairman of
any particular court being sympathetic.
or otherwise. It is to be carried out by
regulations which will be more satisfactory.
Mr. DORMAN: fear, hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
was glad to hear the noble Viscount
making reference to the Rota Committees.
Undoubtedly it was a very unpleasant duty
one had to perform, endeavouring to do
what we could for the benefit of the
workers, listening to their cases, sometimes going away very sad, and on other
occasions going away with the impression
that we were being imposed upon, but
could not avoid it. The Rota Committees
have done their best, and have acted very
honestly and fairly in trying to carry out
the insurance scheme. We all must recognize that any legislation that is introduced to regulate the payment of benefit is
bound to give dissatisfaction somewhere.
There are people who would like benefit
for nothing, or money for nothing, all
the time, and if they got all the payments
necessary to afford them a decent livelihood I do not think they would trouble
about work. Therefore, legislation for
the regulation of benefit is bound to give
some dissatisfaction. From my own experience of trade unions-and this insurance scheme was drawn up more or
less on trade union lines--and of secretaries who were carrying out the rules
and making out the sheets for members,
I am perfectly satisfied that some men
were very much annoyed and very disappointed to find benefit being reduced on a
certain week. Senator Dorman, of
course, has made it appear that this is
going to cause more injury to the workers
than any other Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: To the young people,
not to all.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: If it
is only the young people with whom Senator Dorman is concerned, I do not think
he need trouble so much. My concern has
always been for the poor man with a wife
and family to keep. I did not worry myself so very seriously about the young man
living with his parents or friends, because
he could always get a decent living, and
if he could not get it here there were
other places. My sympathy always went
out to the poor man with a wife and
family, and I generally found that that
man made a strong effort to get employment if it was to be had. I certainly
support the Second reading of this Bill
with the greatest pleasure, and I do hope
it will work out satisfactorily. As I have
said already, I am sure it will if it is
watched over by the Minister of Labour.
The right hon. Gentleman always exhibits considerable sympathy with the working classes, and I am sure that if there
is any hardship inflicted, he will be prepared to amend it as early as possible.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second reading of this Bill, and I might
say that perhaps there is no one in this
85 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

29 MARCH 1928
Second Reading
House to-day-or indeed in the other
House-whom this Bill affects more than
it does myself as an insured person and a
representative of the working classes.
The Bill, on the whole, will be of more
benefit to the worker in the long run
than the Act which is passing out of existence. Some men reaped a good harvest
out of the old Act owing to the extended
benefit, while men like myself, who have
been in constant employment, have had
to pay the piper. When the increase was
made, we had to pay it, and others
reaped the benefit, and now perhaps when
we would require a little bit ourselves
there is not very much for us.
I understand very well that we have to
go on parallel lines with Great Britain
seeing that a large contribution comes
to Northern Ireland from the Imperial
Exchequer. That is the one important
reason 1 have for supporting this Bill
to-day. If it were not that we were
getting this assistance from the Imperial
Exchequer, 1 am afraid there would be
a great deal of complaint amongst the
working classes about having to pay this
extra money. I am very pleased to know
that the Chancellor of the Imperial Exchequer is to continue to contribute towards this fund in Northern Ireland.
Now, Senator Dorman-I think that is
the right way to pronounce the word
while he says a great many good things-
Mr. DORMAN: Thank you.
Mr. BARCLAY- says a great many
other things with which I do not altogether
agree. On the last day on which we had
a short debate in this House he and I
did not altogether agree about the
working-class people, although they were
poor, being very immoral. That seems to
grate upon my conscience.
Mr. DORMAN: I never said that.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not think that
unemployment or the want of money
would cause any young man, or any young
woman, to go and lead an immoral life.
Senator Dorman made that statement in
the House to-day-and he has also made
the statement at the Custom House Steps,
for I have heard it more than once or
twice, to the effect that some young
women in the city of Belfast have been
paid so low wages that they had to dress
themselves up, powder their faces, put
red stuff on their lips, and go out in
the evening to try and increase their
salary. I never saw this, and I never
heard of any person making the statement
but the hon. Senator, and I have knocked
about Belfast a good deal, and am well
acquainted with a great many people connected with missionary work in the city.
These ladies and gentlemen have given
their lives and their time to that kind of
work, and I never heard one of them
yet make a statement like that.
Now, there is one question some of the
workers have been asking me, and I would
like to put it to the Minister of Labour.
This question seems to be troubling a
great many men at the present time
who are unemployed and on the streets,
that is, that you must have thirty stamps
before you are entitled to the benefit.
What is troubling them is this: They
say, "During the last two years the two
years in which they are to qualify for the
six months' benefit in the future-we have
not wrought more than nine, or, perhaps,
ten or twelve months. Each year we
have drawn what we were entitled to
draw according to the Act of Parliament.
How do we stand?" Some of these men
have almost come to the end of their
last six months, and their whole dread is
that if this Bill comes into force shortly
they would have to go two years without
receiving any benefit.
Then there is another question that a
seafaring man put to me. Of course it
has no bearing on this, but it deals with
sickness and disablement benefit. I
would like the Press to take a particular
note of the answer the Minister of
Labour will give to the question. He is a
seafaring man. His ship is built in Belfast,
and this ship is to trade between foreign
ports, say North and South America. When
he is signing on in Belfast he signs under
British rule, that is, the person employing
him is entitled to stamp his cards.
When
he gets to America he has to sign again
under the American rules, which enable
that same employer to do without the
stamping of cards. This leaves him practically on his beam ends. He spends a
number of years there, say one or two, or,
perhaps, more. He is trading between.
those two ports, and he is receiving no
stamps on his card. At the same time
he has a wife and family here in the
city of Belfast, and weekly, or fortnightly, or monthly, he sends the money
he is earning there for the support of
his wife and family.
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
This man says to me, "Supposing I met
with an accident, and something would
happen that I could not prove that it was
caused by the neglect of the person employing me, and I had to come over here,
how do I stand? Or supposing I contracted some disease and died, how would
my wife and children fare as far as pension is concerned, seeing that I have subscribed to this insurance, but have had
no stamps put on my card during the
last two years?" That is a question that
is troubling a great number of seafaring
men in the city of Belfast. I would like
Mr. Andrews to give me an answer to
this question. Perhaps it would ease
their minds.
I should like to say as regards the three
years' residence in Northern Ireland that
that is a very wise provision. We find
people coming from across the border
and working for a few hours in Northern
Ireland. They get their cards stamped
and live on the other side of the border.
When they are unemployed they draw the
Exchange money, and then go back and
spend it in the Free State. While they
are working in Northern Ireland, although living in the Free State, they
are taking employment out of the hands
of the men and women here.
I agree
with that part of the Clause. Three
years is a very short time. As a matter of
fact, if I had had a conversation with the
Minister of Labour I would have said
four or five years.
Mr. DORMAN: Or ten.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not think I have
much more to say. I have great pleasure
in supporting the Second reading of the
Bill.
Mr. McDOWELL: Seeing that the
Minister is here, I would like to ask two
or three questions on this Bill. Before
I do so I should like to comment on the
statements made by Senator Dorman.
He is here representing a certain party,
and that party, so far as I know, agreed
with all the provisions of this Bill behind
closed doors.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. McDOWELL: Now, when the
Bill becomes public property, they come
into the open and try to belittle some of
the Clauses.
Mr. DORMAN: Some.
Mr. McDOWELL: I said "some."
Why? The reasons are obvious. I am glad
that the matter of the family income has
been done away with. Senator Dorman
recited a very pathetic incident. I could
also recite very many, and one in particular. This was a man who went away
when he was 16 years of age. He joined
the British Army and was a soldier for
practically seventeen years. He went
through the Great War, but unfortunately he did not marry. He was discharged in England and remained there
for a short period, but could get no employment. His parents, who were still
residing in Belfast, seeing that he was in
bad circumstances across the water, told
him to come over to Belfast. He was
thirty years of age, and he came over,
but because his father happened to have
a few shillings above the family income
he was turned down. I think that was
a great hardship. I am glad that is done
away with.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. McDOWELL: Am I to understand that the seasonal worker is done
away with? I think under the old Act
he had to have 35 or 36 stamps for three
consecutive years before he was entitled
to receive benefit. Am I to assume now
that if he had 30 stamps inside the last
two years he will be entitled to benefit?
It would set a number of minds at ease if
that was only known. I would also like
the Minister to define for me what he
means by genuinely seeking employment." I think it is a most difficult task.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. J. CUNNINGHAM: It is not
done away with.
Mr. McDOWELL: More is the pity.
When a man makes an effort to get employment, I do not think "genuinely"
means that he must go round half of the
town seeking employment. I think it
really means that he can concentrate his
efforts on perhaps three or four places.
where he is likely to obtain employment.
For instance, a case was brought to my
notice the other day where a man concentrated his efforts on four or five firms.
He went round these firms every day.
Unemployment Insurance Bill- 29 MARCH 1928
Second Reading
After going to one of them a number of
mornings the manager said to him,
""
What is the use of you coming here?
You were here yesterday morning." He
said, Yes, I was, and I was here the
morning before that, and the morning before that again, but you did not want any
man, and you may want one this morning." The manager said, "You give me
the impression that you are anxious for
work, come and I will start you." Supposing he had gone round a dozen or two
dozen firms, the probability is that he
would have had to go round again and
nobody would have taken the slightest
notice of him. I think that is a question
that should be very carefully looked into
so far as the Ministry is concerned.
I think this Bill will commend itself
to the working men. Another point
comes to my mind. Say a man ceased
employment at the latter end of 1927.
We know so far as his contributions are
concerned he has plenty, He has worked
continuously since 1912, and he becomes
idle. I understand that it is the intention of the Minister to give a year
or two years' grace under the present system, but up to 1930 he has had no work.
We will assume he has been receiving
benefit all that time. Yet at the end of
that time, although he had not 30 contributions to his credit in that two years,
he has a surplus to his credit. In what
way are you going to deal with that man?
That may apply to a number of cases.
would like a reply to these two or three
questions. I commend the Bill as a whole
for adoption by the House.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to detain the House for more than a few
moments. We have already heard that
this Bill is based on the English Act, and
it follows from that, that unless we are not
prevented by our commonsense from even
desiring to upset the advantageous arrangement made with Great Britain, we
have no choice but to pass the Bill without any serious structural alteration.
Quite apart from that, as it seems to me,
the Bill possesses some very distinct
merits of its own. It will ultimately
make for simplification in the text itself.
The method adopted seems, if I may say
so, to be more or less an additional laurel
in the wreath with which our Parliamentary Draftsman is rapidly surrounding
himself. It is much more easy to understand than some of its predecessors.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill will, when
adopted, make for simplification in practical methods, which perhaps the right
hon. Gentleman (Mr. Andrews) will
elaborate for us later on, and which I
need not speak on at the moment. Again,
the Bill has negative merits. It makes
no pretence of interfering with the
functions of Boards of Poor-law Guardians,
nor does it make any pretence of readjusting pensions in respect of war service, nor
does it in any way interfere with legislation as regards the Workmen's Compensation Acts or the Widows' and Orphans'
Contributory Pensions Act. It does none
of these things, and none of these things,
it seems to me. are in any way in point
in considering the Bill.
But there is another very great merit,
and it is the educational provision which
is made. I think I am right in saying that
that is a new departure in this Measure
and in the coresponding English Measure, or if it is not an entirely new departure, the sanction, perhaps, to be given to
it is a new departure, and it seems to me
very highly commendable. I do not in
the least follow the complaint as to the
reduction of what I may describe as the
youthful dependant. I am very much
afraid that at present both the cinema
and the bookmaker take too high a toll
of the money paid out in unemployment
benefit, particularly to youthful members
of the community. If additional provision for attendance at technical classes
will in any way tend to prevent that, it
would be very much to the good. On the
contrary, so far from objecting to such
reduction as is made as regards youths,
I am afraid I am somewhat doubtful as
to whether it will be found practicable
ultimately to maintain the present standard of benefit. That remark, of course,
applies to Great Britain just as much as
to ourselves. It may very well be, as it
seems to me, that a change of economic
conditions may render an alteration
necessary.
Now, I pass to add my own humble
contribution to the bombardment of interrogatories which has been hurled at the
right hon. Gentleman. First of all, I
want to refer to this question of the three
years' residence in Northern Ireland. We
have heard a great deal about it. One of
my Friends on my left (Mr. Barclay)
mentioned it this afternoon, and we heard
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the answer. I cannot find in the Bill
that three years' residence in Northern
Ireland is required. What I do find is
three years' residence in the United Kingdom. I think I am correct in saying
there is no special provision as regards
residence in Northern Ireland.
United Kingdom that is the test.
It is the
Mr. ANDREWS indicated assent.
Mr. LESLIE: That is a point of very
great importance. The United Kingdom
means Great Britain and Northern Ireland. Our workers move backwards
and forwards between Great Britain
and Northern Ireland, and are subject to Unemployment Insurance, and
stamps, and books all the time. If they
inove in the other direction, which my
Friend on my left mentioned, and are
not so subject, the position is entirely
different. The point is a good one, and
I am glad to have confirmation from the
right hon. Gentleman that there is no
special reference to Northern Ireland in
that connection.
The other small matter has reference to
the adopted child. I am a little afraid
there may be some possibility of the
adopted child falling out owing to the
fact that in England there is provision.
made for adopted children by a recent
statute which we have not yet followed,
but which we intend to follow. What I
am afraid of is that it might be said later
on that the adopted child in the meaning
of this Act can only mean a child adopted
in accordance with the law of Great
Britain, a law which exists there at the
moment, but not in Northern Ireland.
On the other hand, it may very well be
said that as soon as we pass a Measure of
that sort the adopted child will come in.
The phrase "adopted child," as used in
this Bill, is merely descriptive, and there
is no danger to any properly accredited
child-and the child must be properly
accredited; we must be sure the child is
a bona-fide adopted child, not a dummy
put up for the purpose of the Act.
It might be said there is no real danger.
I will ask the right hon. Gentleman at
a later stage-the point can definitely be
raised on the question that Clause 7
stand part of the Bill-to say there is no
danger. It is plain we must have a reference in this Bill to the adopted child,
because the adopted child might be imported from Great Britain. The same
expression "adopted child" was used, it
may be remembered, in the English Act
of 1922; that was the Act passed before
the recent legislation dealing with child
adoption. That leads me to think there
is no danger lurking behind the matters
to which I am drawing attention, but I
should like the Minister to confirm this
definitely at a later stage. I have nothing more to say except that I cordially
support the Bill, and believe it will be a
great satisfaction to everybody when it
is passed.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: This Measure has
been criticized in the other House favourably and unfavourably, but, if I may say
so, I think the greatest tribute that has yet
been paid to it has been paid by Senator
Dorman, to-day, because he found only
two of its provisions to criticize. One is
a provision which does away with what he
felt was a serious blemish in the previous
Acts, namely, the abolition of the familyincome test. The provision which he did
criticize somewhat severely, but not unfairly, was that which reduces the benefit
to a young man and a young woman.
quite admit the matter can be approached
from two standpoints, but it is not for
me to apportion the blame for that condition of affairs which he thinks exists,
but which I think he exaggerates. Undoubtedly, a serious state of affairs was
created, particularly among our young
men, by the amount of benefit they received when unemployed being at a higher
rate than the wages they received when
in employment. That applied particularly to young men serving their apprenticeships.
on.
They entered an apprenticeship and for
the first year received 9s or 10s a week,
the next year 11s or 12s a week, and so
It was a temptation to those young
men to feel that if they became unemployed they would very probably get
18s a week for a year or two years if they
could make a pretence of genuinely seeking work during that period. I am
speaking about what I know because, in
addition to the case to which the hon.
Senator referred, and to which I referred
in the other House, many cases have been
brought to my notice, both by employers
and by parents of these young men, which
convinced me and, what was more important still, also convinced the Imperial
Minister of Labour, that really a wrong
was being done to those young men by
putting this temptation in their way.
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
29 MARCH 1928
It has been said that if they left their
employers of their own accord, or if they
got dismissed, they did not get benefit.
That was quite true for six weeks, but at
the end of six weeks they were entitled to
claim benefit, and in some instances they
were prepared to take the risk in order
to gain probably a very great advantage.
Therefore, I think on consideration the
hon. Senator, who takes a very reasonable
view of these things in some respects, will
feel that although this provision may
cause hardship in
certain directions-I
regret to say that-yet, in the main, it
will really be of advantage to our young
trade unionists. I do not know a greater
Injustice that we could do to these young
people than to teach them in their youth
that they can live without work.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. ANDREWS: It is part of the
creation plan that we should all work,
and it is only by the work of all her sons
that Ulster can become the prosperous
place which we hope eventually to see
her. A considerable number of questions
have been asked which I shall endeavour
to answer. The first that I need refer
to was in reference to the thirty stamps
in the two years, which, of course, is the
first statutory condition for the receipt of
benefit, and, therefore, the most important provision in the Bill
It is quite
right that what is intended should be
thoroughly understood. One thing I
know that made many of our unemployed
men very anxious was this. They felt
they had accumulated during the last few
years considerable overdrafts due to the
fact that having drawn all their standard
benefit which they received as a statutory
right they had what is called extended
benefit, which was really benefit granted
in consideration of contributions, which it
was expected would later on be paid.
can relieve the mind of the hon. Senator
at once by telling him that by this Measure all the overdrafts are wiped off.
Mr. BARCLAY: Hear, hear.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: Every insured worker
will get a clean sheet and start afresh on
an equal footing. That is one of the
great benefits our workpeople will receive
by this Measure when it becomes the law
of the land. Then, again, as regards the
bringing in of this provision of thirty
stamps, if it had been brought in hurriedly it would undoubtedly have created

a great deal of hardship, because some of
our people have been out of employment
continuously for a year or two, and, in
some instances, perhaps for a longer period.
Obviously they would have had no stamps
to their credit at all. That being recognized, it was suggested by the Blanesburgh Committee, which very thoroughly
investigated this whole problem of Unemployment Insurance, that it was right to
have what is called a transitional period,
a period in which a claimant could receive
benefit either under the old conditions or
under the new. The old conditions will be
applied in the case of those who cannot
obtain benefit under the new conditions
for a period of one or two years.
will give those who may have been continuously out of employment for one or
two years a year or two's grace in order
to get thirty contributions upon their
cards and thereby qualify for benefit if
at a later date they should become unemployed again. Hon. Senators will appreciate that this is a very wise and very
reasonable provision. It will save hardship in many directions where otherwise
hardship would have been caused.
That
I think it was Senator McDowell who
raised the question about seasonal workers.
for
The special conditions
seasonal
workers will be wiped out by the uni-
66 G.S.W."
versal application of the
and 30
contributions." The first
statutory condition deals with the qualification for benefit of thirty contributions for the purpose of showing if a
claimant is really an insured person, and
the second condition is that he should
show he was genuinely seeking employment. I know that in the administration of this condition a good deal of dissatisfaction has, I am afraid inevitably,
been created, because-speaking with all
modesty in the presence of such a distinguished educational authority as my right
hon. Friend (Viscount Charlemont)-
there are certain words in the English
language that cannot be done without,
but that at the same time do not convey
exactly the same meaning to every human
mind. Some people may say that a certain thing is reasonable, while other persons may say that it is not reasonable;
but, on the law of averages, those words,
although necessarily a little flexible m
their meaning, do create the same impression on different people, and, thank
goodness, by this Measure the Ministerial
discretion is ended, and it will not be left
to me to define what those particular
95 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

[Mr. Andrews]
words
mean.
They will be left to
Courts of Referees
May I say, in that connection, that 1
think the Courts of Referees have been
unfairly and ungenerously criticized? I
think we should all remember that these
Courts are set up as perfectly impartial
bodies, not for the purpose of giving
benefit to those who are not entitled to
it, not for the purpose of disqualifying
those who are entitled to it, but for judging each case absolutely fairly when it is
brought before them. We should remember also the personnel of those Courts.
First of all, there is the workers' representative. I do not think the workers'
representative would be very likely to be
prejudiced against the interests of his
fellow working people. Then there is the
employers' representative. He is only a
third of the whole, and I think I may say,
in a matter of this kind, that employers
would not wish to do a wrong to workers
to whom all reasonable employers feel
they are much indebted. The third
member of the Court is a distinguished
lawyer, who is chosen because he is
neither an employee nor an employer.
He is independent of both, and has
been brought up to weigh evidence, and,
therefore, to come to a proper decision.
That is how the matter is going to be
settled in the future.
so.
There will be a statutory right to all
benefit. In the past that has not been
A good deal of loose thinking has
been directed towards this change. I have
heard it stated, on many occasions, by
people who ought to have been better informed that this Bill intended to wipe out
entirely extended benefit. That is only
half a truth, and half a truth is a very
dangerous thing. You might as well say
its intention was to wipe out standard
benefit. It really does wipe out both
standard benefit and extended benefit, as
we have known them, and it substitutes
for those forms of benefit a new form of
benefit altogether, under conditions which
I think are not unreasonable or unfair.
There is another thing that I think we
should remember. We hear a great deal
about the amount of benefit to be paid.
and the length of time it should be naid
for. but we never hear a word said about
contributions. That is a strange fact.
and yet the contributions must bear a
distinct relation to the benefit paid. If
we are going to raise the benefits in all
directions, then we must be prepared to
raise the contributions, which would put
a heavier burden still upon industry and
upon the employers who, remember, have
got to pay contributions for all their
workers, and upon the employees, who
are already under a considerable strain in
naving to pay for this social service alone
-apart from other social services-sevenpence a week.
I should have stated, in dealing with
the benefit payable to young persons--
young women and young men--that there
again a reasonable course is being pursued, because in consideration of the
benefits being reduced, the contributions
are also being reduced to some extent.
The hon. Senator who sits opposite me
(Mr. Dorman) dealt with many matters
beyond those, if I may say so, with which
this Bill deals. He spoke about the right
to work, and matters of that kind which
have been referred to frequently on public
platforms. But, when you talk about the
right to work, you set up a principle
which is very hard to defend, because up
to the present in this world it has always
been laid down that the duty lay upon
the worker to seek employment; but, if
you set up the right to maintenancefull maintenance-
-or the right to work,
you really place upon the State the responsibility of obtaining work for everyone. However right that may be in
theory, it would not work in practice-
Mr. DORMAN: Why?
Mr. ANDREWS-obviously, but I
do not wish to continue that argument.
I think I have said sufficient to show the
hon. Senator that this matter can be approached from a different standpoint from
that which he takes.
Senator Barclay gave me a very difficult
question to answer; in fact, it was never
put to me before, and but for a certain
amount of help which I have received
from the officials' gallery I would not
have been in a position to answer it. He
asked what about persons who joined up
as crewз on a ship in Belfast, then went
off in that ship, went to foreign ports,
and came back after a few years. How
would they stand? I am glad to be able
to tell him that in connection with all
these social services as long as the ship
flies the Union Jack-the flag of the
United Kingdom-and as long as it is
British-owned, the insured persons are in
no way prejudiced. If they find that
Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
29 MARCH 1928
[Mr. Andrews]
the ship is being transferred to another
owner, or to another country, then 1
would suggest to them that the sooner
they get on to a British ship the better
for many reasons.
That is the answer to
the question.
Regarding the adopted child, I am glad
to be able to tell the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) that it is cared for
in this Measure. It is true that the law
here is different from that of Great
Britain, but if an adopted child comes
here from Great Britain the provision in
this Measure will keep that child all
right. Dependant's benefit will be paid.
As to the position here at the moment,
we intend to pass very shortly a Measure
which will bring the law into step with
that of Great Britain, and, therefore, the
matter will be absolutely straightened
out, and in no way will the position of
the adopted child be prejudiced.
I think I have answered most of the
points that have been raised, but if I
have omitted anything I will be quite
pleased, if I am reminded of it, to endeavour to meet the point. May I, in
conclusion, thank this honourable House
for the patient way in which they have
listened to my few remarks?
Mr. BARCLAY: I neglected to ask all
of the question that was put to me-
Mr. DEPUTY SPEAKER: The hon.
Senator can only speak by leave of the
House. I take it that you agree?
The House indicated assent.
Mr. BARCLAY: Part of the question
escaped my memory, and it has occurred
to me now. The gentleman who was
speaking to me said: "Suppose I joined a
foreign company, an American company,
under the Stars and Stripes." When he
goes under the Stars and Stripes he is
not looked upon as an insured person.
What this gentleman wanted to know
was this: if he is earning wages under the
Stars and Stripes and sends those wages
home to support his wife and family in
the city of Belfast, will his wife be allowed to become a contributor on behalf
of her husband?
Mr. ANDREWS: I think you mean
this could his wife, acting on his behalf,
make him a voluntary contributor?
Mr. BARCLAY: Yes.
Mr. ANDREWS: Speaking on the spur
of the moment, I think probably he
could but if the hon. Senator will raise
the question again on the Committee
stage either the Leader of the House or
I will give him an answer which will be
beyond all doubt.
While I am on my feet, may I say that
there was one matter raised by the hon.
and learned Senator opposite (Mr. Leslie)
that I should like to refer to, that was
in reference to the educational training
efforts which are made under the provisions of this Bill. Up to the present
any training that was given to young
persons or adults for domestic service, or
for other purposes, had to be paid for
by the Ministry of Education, to the
extent that they had means to pay for,
and a good deal of help was given by
our technical schools and by charitable
organizations in certain instances; but we
were not in a position to devote from the
Unemployment Fund any grant of money
towards an educational effort of the kind.
This Bill will permit a portion of the
expense incurred in the education of unemployed insured persons to be defrayed
from the Unemployment Fund

a
Mr. LESLIE: There are two matters
which were referred to at an earlier stage
of this Measure to which I would like to
direct attention for two or three minutes.
The one I will take first relates to an
Amendment which was moved and withdrawn, dealing with the possible legitimation of the proceeds of illicit union
between married man
or a married
woman and another. I think it is perhaps right to remind the House that there
is nothing in the Bill before us which
would prevent relief being given in such
a case, as for want of a better example I
will call the Enoch Arden case, where
a woman contracted a second marriage,
and the original husband ultimately reappeared. There is nothing in this Bill
which would prevent application being
mrade to Parliament in such a case for a
special Act of legitimation.
There are
instances, about two or three a century,
where that has been done. It is possible
for a child, which is the result of innocent
bigamy, to become legitimated by the
direct action of Parliament, and there
is nothing in this Bill which interferes
with that.
The other point is the more difficult one
of the question of giving the County Court
Judge power to hear applications under
this Bill in private; that is to say, in
chambers. What was in my mind when
I mentioned the matter on the Second
Reading, and again when I brought forward an Amendment in Committee, was
that the County Court Judge in this matter should be placed in exactly the same
position as a High Court Judge. I think
the discussion went off a little, it being
thought that the County Court Judge and
the High Court Judge had already identical powers, and an instance was given
where a Judge had power to clear the
Court when any evidence was about to be
given which it was contrary to public decency to hear in public. That was not
quite what I meant.
What I think is really wanted is power
to hear in chambers. That power, as I
understand it, a Judge of the High Court
does possess.
He gets it by virtue of the
Legitimacy Declaration (Ireland) Act,
1868, which refers to powers given to the
Judges in connection with probate matters, and I think the Probate Act in
question bears date 1859, although I am
speaking subject to correction. In that
Act it is clearly stated that a Judge shall
have power to hear cases in chambers, but
that power to hear in chambers is quite a
different power to their power to clear
the Court in the ordinary way.
that power to hear in chambers has been
given to County Court Judges in special
Statutes.
I find
It was given in the Irish Land
Act, 1870, and again in the Land Law
Act of 1881. I therefore hope-although
I am not putting any Amendment on the
Paper to-day-that the matter may receive further attention in another place.
In this matter this House is a chamber of
first instance, and it is perhaps not inappropriate that a proposal such as that
which I have just made should emanate
from the more directly representative
Chamber, because if it is accepted it would
undoubtedly be somewhat contrary to our
general policy to which the noble Viscount
alluded at an earlier stage.
The general policy is the hearing of
cases in open Court. That is a very important point with which, speaking generally, I am myself in accord. I think it
will be remembered that the Parliament
of Northern Ireland has made distinctions in this way not only in the Affiliation Orders Act, but also when we were
dealing with the Criminal Law Amend-
Legitimacy Bill
Adjournment
3 APRIL 1928
ment Bill in, I think, the year 1923. We
directly refused--or rather those sitting
elsewhere refused-to follow England in
requiring a certain class of case to be
heard in open Court instead of, as was
then and still is the usage in Northern
Ireland, hearing it in secret. I do not
think it would be right to delay the passage of this Bill in respect of a matter of
this kind. I do hope it will not be lost
sight of. If the opinion I hold is shared
attention will be directed to the matter,
as I say, in another place.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
hon. and learned Senator has said he
would not press for an answer to the last
point he has mentioned in his speech, I
would prefer not to answer it beyond saying the Government's attention will be
directed to the point he has mentioned.
With regard to his first point, the case of
a possible Enoch Arden in Ulster, I think
nothing in the Bill will affect that matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Mesage was sent to the Commons
to carry down the said Bill and desire
their concurrence thereto.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am authorised by the Prime
Minister, on his behalf, to inform the
Senate that His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, in the name and on
behalf of His Majesty the King, has been
