CARE OF MENTALLY DEFICIENT
CHILDREN.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:
To ask the Leader of the House if it is
the intention of the Government to bring
in a Bill to make provision for the care
and education of mentally deficient children, so that they may be trained for
occupations which will contribute to their
support in life.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to ask the
Deputy-Leader of the House the Question
which stands in my name on the Order
Paper. My reason for doing so is to find
out if anything has been done, or if anything can be done, through the medium
255 Mentally Deficient Children
[Mr. Barclay]
THE SENATE
of the Education Act for the benefit of
those children who are mentally defective.
They are in no way to blame for the
position they find themselves in to-day,
but up to the present time so far as Northern Ireland is concerned this class of
children has been greatly neglected both
by Church and State. A short time ago
the local authorities in the County of
Tyrone had before them a child of this
description. They were practically at
their wit's end to know what to do with it.
There was no home or institution in
Northern Ireland to which they could send
it; in fact there is no institution in all
Ireland to which a Protestant child who
is mentally defective can be sent. I understand there is only one institution in the
whole of Ireland, and that is connected
with one of the convents in the Irish Free
State. A short time afterwards I read in
the Press that one of our public men referred to this and other similar cases, and
he said it was a scandal and a shame that
Northern Ireland should make no provision for this class of child.
The same thing has come under my own
observation during the past few months.
I have occasion to visit the Purdysburn
Mental Hospital, and during one of my
visits there I found a child under ten years
of age in one of the villas along with men.
At first I thought the child was probably
a friend of one of the female attendants,
and I asked no questions, but seeing it
again on a second visit I made it my
business to inquire if the child belonged
to any of the attendants, and I was informed that it was a little patient. I was
surprised to find a child of that age in
that institution along with men, and after
making inquiries I discovered that we had
no suitable place in Northern Ireland to
which to send such children.
I agree with the gentleman who said
that such a state of affairs is a scandal
and a shame, and I think it is high time
that something was done in this respect,
so that these mentally defective children
can if possible be educated and trained to
some useful occupation whereby later on in
life they might be able to contribute in some
little way to their own livelihood. Since
the Northern Government came into existence they have done a great deal for the
benefit of all sections of the community.
They have passed an Education Act and
made it possible for the poor man's child
to ascend the educational ladder and re-
Mentally Deficient Children 256
ceive a college education and become a
classical scholar. We have also made provision for widows', orphans' and old-age
pensions, and for the unemployed, the
sick, and the disabled, and I think it is
the duty of the State to take up this question of the care and education of mentally
defective children and to see that something is done for these children, who are
in no way to blame for the position they
find themselves in to-day. If we go round
the market towns to-day we can see standing at some gate, entry or some street
corner some poor fellow who has been
neglected in this respect early in life. He
is probably useful in some respects. He
can run errands and do a little gardening
for neighbours, but he gets no wage or
anything except a few coppers. If men
who are able to do a little work like this
had been properly trained when young
they could probably have done a great deal
in later life for their own support.
Then we have a large number of young
women, some even in the City of Belfast,
who are more or less mentally deficient.
Only yesterdey I was talking to a gentleman in York Street, and he pointed out to
me a young girl, turned fourteen, who is
quite simple in some respects while in other
respects she is as wise as any other person.
He said it was a shame that we had no institution in Northern Ireland to which
such girls could be sent and educated and
trained. We are all aware that in some
cases advantage is taken of those poor girls
and as a result a new generation of mental deficients is brought into existence.
It should be the duty of the Government
to look after such girls as these, and I ask
this question in the hope that in the near
future something will be done, and 'that
if local authorities have no power to deal
with them under the present Education
Act the Government will bring in a short
Bill giving them power to make provision
for this class of person.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the absence of my noble
Friend, the Leader of the House (Viscount
Charlemont), who, I regret to say, is
absent owing to indisposition, it falls to
me to reply to this question. The Education Act of 1923 contains powers for dealing with the education of the mentally
deficient children such as the hon. Member
refers to in his question, and the question
of giving full effect to this provision is
at the present moment engaging the
257 Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928

attention of the Ministry of Education.
In fact it is proposed to have a conference
with the Association of Education Authorities on this subject this very week. This
question is bound up also with that of
the provision required for the care and
maintenance of mentally deficient persons
other than children up to the age of sixteen years. It cannot be dealt with apart
from the recommendations on this subject
contained in the Report of the Departmental Commission on Local Government
Administration, which report is at the
present moment under consideration.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The purpose of this Bill is to
maintain uniformity in the National
Health Insurance system in the United
Kingdom. When the Health Insurance
Service was transferred to the Government
of Northern Ireland in 1922 numbers of
insured persons in this area were members
of Approved Societies operating both in
this area and in Great Britain. A wider
basis of insurance for our Northern Ireland
workers was thus afforded, and the
Government has been unwilling to disturb
this arrangement, which was to the undoubted advantage of the insured working
people of Northern Ireland. The whole
system of National Health Insurance has,
therefore, continued to be organized, both
administratively and financially, on a
common basis for the whole of the United
Kingdom.
It will be within the recollection of hon.
Members that about three years ago a
Royal Commission was set up by the Imperial Government to inquire into the
working of the Health Insurance Scheme.
This Commission, under the able chairmanship of the late Lord Lawrence, of
Kingsgate, considered evidence in great
detail and eventually presented a long and
valuable report. The Imperial Government, after considering this report, have
introduced in the Imperial Parliament a
Bill which embodies those of the recommendations of the majority of the Commission which they have decided should
be presented to Parliament for inclusion
in the National Health Insurance Code.
That Bill has already passed through
Committee stage in the Imperial House
of Commons.
I have already indicated to hon.
Members the advantages of a common
system of insurance throughout the Kingdoin, and the Government here have, therefore, decided that Health Insurance law
in Northern Ireland should be amended to
conform to the changes proposed in Great
Britain. Some of the provisions of the
Imperial Bill relate to matters such as
the Mercantile Marine, which, under the
Constitution, are outside the legislative
competence of the Parliament of Northern Ireland. Othere relate to matters
such as the Central Fund and the
affairs of International Approved Societies, which can best be dealt with by
an Act of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom; while its remaining provisions
propose to make changes in the law which,
if they are to form part of the National
Health Insurance Code in Northern Ireland, can properly be dealt with only by
legislation of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland itself.
The Government here had, therefore, to
determine the best manner in which the
changes under contemplation could be
effected in Northern Ireland. As regards
those changes which relate to matters reserved under the Government of Ireland
Act, they will apply automatically in
Northern Ireland when enacted by the
Imperial Parliament. As regards those
matters which I have mentioned as being
most conveniently dealt with by an Act
of the Parliament of the United Kingdom,
the Government has arranged for a Člause
to be included in the Imperial Bill providing that those provisions shall come
into effect in Northern Ireland if and
when the Parliament of Northern Ireland
expresses its consent to this arrangement.
The primary purpose of the Bill to
which I now ask the House to give a
Second Reading, is the expression of the
consent of Parliament to the application
of the provisions of the Imperial Bill to
Northern Ireland. The procedure which
is proposed is, that His Grace the Governor
should be given power to apply the provisions of the Imperial Bill to Northern
Ireland by Order in Council from a date
to be specified in the Order. The date
proposed for the commencement of the
Imperial Bill is 2nd July, 1928, and, if
this is not altered in the subsequent stages
E
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
of the Bill, a similar date will be chosen
for its operation in Northern Ireland. If
the provisions of the Imperial Bill are
applied to Northern Ireland, an additional
charge will fall on our Exchequer in consequence of the entry into insurance of
classes of persons previously excluded, and
through the bringing into full insurance
benefits of certain sections of the Deposit
Contributors class, whose benefits are, at
the moment, limited in extent.
Sub-section (3) of Clause 1 of the
Northern Ireland Bill makes provision for
this additional Exchequer grant. The
provisions of the Imperial Bill which affect
Northern Ireland are set out in an
Appendix to a White Paper which has
been circulated to hon. Members, and I
feel that it is unnecessary for me to take
up the time of the House by explaining
them in any great detail. I should, however, like to draw the attention of hon.
Members to the proposed new provisions.
relating to arrears, which they will find
on page 10 of the Appendix, and to the
continuance in insurance of persons who
cease to be employed. Under the existing
law an insured person is entitled to a free
year of insurance from the date on which
he pays his last contribution. His insurance may, however, be further continued
from year to year if he satisfies the tests
laid down for the prolongation of insurance.
Again, the rate of benefit to which an
insured person is normally entitled depends on the number of contributions
which he has paid during the previous contribution year. Consequently,
when a man loses his employment and subquently becomes sick he finds himself
subjected to a substantial reduction in the
rate of sickness or disablement benefit to
which he is entitled. For a number of
years past a partial mitigation of the effect
of this provision has been secured to the
insured person by granting to societies free
credits of contributions in respect of unemployed insured persons who satisfy their
societies that they have been genuinely
seeking work. This free credit has had
the result of entitling the sick person to
some cash benefit at all events so long as
he continues in insurance.
Furthermore, hon. Members are aware
that the Contributory Pension Scheme is
interlocked closely with the Health Insurance Scheme. Under the existing law,
however, the date of cessation of insurance
for Health Insurance purposes differs from
the date of cessation for Pension purposes.
This is found to be a source of considerable embarrassment in the course of the
administration of the two schemes. From
all points of view, therefore, it has become
evident of recent years that the existing
provisions relating to arrears and to the
continuance in insurance of insured persons who fall out of employment are not
entirely satisfactory. The new legislation
proposes to alter them.
Under the new provisions a person who
ceases employment will be continued in
insurance for an
average period of 21
months from the date on which he was last
employed, and during that period he will
be entitled to full benefits without regard
to arrears, if his failure to pay contributions has been due to sickness or to
genuine unemployinent. Moreover, if,
during the whole of that 21 months, he
has been genuinely looking for work, and
if he has paid the prescribed number of
contributions since he entered insurance,
he will be entitled to have his insurance
extended for another year, but during this
year's extension he will be entitled to
benefits at half rates only.
Approved Societies will be recouped the
cost of remitting arrears from the same
source as now meets the cost of granting
free credits. namely, the unallocated
balances in the Stamp Sales Account.
Furthermore, it is proposed to make the
date of cessation of insurance for pension
purposes the same as that for Health
Insurance; but I might perhaps mention
one important modification of this principle, the object of which is to prevent
elderly persons who lose employment
late in life forfeiting entirely their rights
to an old-age pension. Hon. Members
will find this provision explained in detail
in paragraph E of page 10 of the White
Paper. I shall refer to but one other
provision of the Imperial Bill. Under
the existing law, persons who are denied
admission to Approved Societies on account of the state of their health, or who
for reasons best known to themselves decline or neglect to join a Society, are
known as Deposit Contributors. They
pay the same contributions as the ordinary
insured contributor but are entitled to
benefits much less favourable than those
of the Society member.
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
It is proposed that those Deposit Contributors who are unable to obtain admission to Societies on health grounds
should be formed into an Insurance Section, whose affairs will be administered
by the Ministry of Labour. Cash benefits
will be granted to them on at least as
favourable a scale as the minimum
granted to Approved Society members. A
deserving class of employed persons will
thus be provided for during illness more
generously than at present. The other
provisions of the Imperial Bill to be applied to Northern Ireland are of subsidiary importance. They relate in the
main to technical matters of administrative control, and hon. Members will find
them explained in the Appendix to the
White Paper. I have now dealt with the
primary purpose of the Northern Ireland
Bill and have mentioned the principal
provisions of the Imperial Bill which will
affect Northern Ireland.
With regard to Clause 2 of the Northern
Ireland Bill, the opportunity has been
taken to propose to Parliament certain
amendments of the law in Northern Ireland relating to offences under the Insurance Acts. The general effect of these
amendments is to make the way of the
transgressor just a little bit harder.
Clause 3 of the Northern Ireland Bill
Ideals with two matters. Sub-Section (1)
relates to the cost of medical certification,
and Sub-Section (2) proposes to amend the
Northern Ireland Economy Act. With
regard to medical certification, a medical
referee service was established early last
year in order that Approved Societies
might have the benefit of a second medical
opinion in cases of doubtful incapacity for
work. Societies have made such an extensive use of this service that it has become necessary to ask for an increase in
the amount which may be appropriated
from societies' funds to meet the cost.
With regard to the amendment of the
Economy Act, the Clause proposes to
establish in Northern Ireland the same
order of preference on claims on unallocated balances in the Stamp Sales Account
as prevails with regard to balances in that
account in Great Britain. This Bill deals
with what is possibly the most intricate
and technical of the many great schemes
of social legislation which, from time to
time, are the subject of our deliberations
here; and to those hon. Members who are
not in close daily touch with the actual
administration of Health Insurance my
exposition of the subject probably leaves
much to be desired. I may, however, inform the House that the provisions of the
Measure have been discussed with and
agreed to by representatives of Approved
Society opinion both in Great Britain and
in Northern Ireland, and I can commend
the Bill to hon. Members as another addition to the Statute Book for the advantage
of our community generally and of our
insured workpeople in particular. I beg
to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
sure this Bill will be heartily welcomed
by all workers in Northern Ireland.
There are considerable improvements in
the Bill compared with the old insurance
scheme. For instance, it is possible now
to recover from the employer benefits
which a man may have been deprived of
owing to the neglect of his employer to
stamp his cards. There have been many
cases where men have suffered injury on
that account, and I am glad to see that
that is one improvement suggested in this
Bill. With reference to pensions, it was
quite evident that it was necessary for
considerable improvements and alterations to be made in the National Health
Insurance scheme, to make it applicable
to our new pensions scheme. There are
quite a number of people who would have
found it very difficult to have continued
to be entitled to pensions under the
National Health Insurance scheme. This
new Bill will continue a man in benefit until
the time arrives when he is entitled to an
old-age pension. The Government have
taken into consideration, for the first time,
a man who may be unemployed for a considerable time. When the original Act
was first introduced, the trade unions
considered it necessary for them to stamp
the National Health Insurance cards of
their members who were receiving unemployment benefit for a lengthened
period. A man who did not belong to a
trade union, and was unemployed for a
long period, was very liable to run so much
in arrears that it became difficult for him
to pay them, and he, therefore, suffered
when he made a claim for sickness benefit. This Bill makes a great improvement
in that respect.
The deposit contributor was another
unfortunate individual who required some
little attention and deserved more bene-
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. J. Cunningham]
fit that he was in receipt of. In a great
many cases deposit contributors were
women. The Insurance Societies were
very reluctant to admit married women,
and after they left their insurance
society they were forced to become ordinary deposit contributors.
The benefits
they received were not in proportion to
the contributions they paid, and in respect of this class of insured person the
Bill makes a welcome change.
There is one matter about which I am
not quite clear, and that is in regard to
doctors' certificates. When the National
Health Insurance Act was first introduced
there was a panel of doctors set up, and
they were supposed to provide free certificates for a man when he was on sick
benefit. The few panel doctors who were
then in existence, and who are probably
in existence to-day, are not known to all
the workers, and if a worker was attending his own family doctor he was often
very dilatory about leaving him and going
to a strange doctor, the panel doctor, for
his certificate for sick benefit. I think
some arrangement might be made to enable a worker on sick benefit, who did
not require a doctor's attention continuously, to get a weekly certificate to
send to his organization to enable him to
obtain benefit.
for
The practice at present is that a man
goes to a doctor, and allows the doctor
to examine him. He is probably suffering
ing from an acute form of rheumatism,
for which he is not likely to require much
medicine. If he requires a certificate
weekly he has to go to that doctor weekly,
and he has either to pay for the certificate direct or he has
to pay
medicine, which appears to me to be an
excuse to make a charge for the certificate.
I would like the Minister of Labour to
make it clear to the workers that it is
possible for them to get a certificate free
of cost, and that the address of panel
doctors should be made known to all
organizations, so that workers can either
attend those panel doctor or get a certificate free from other doctors. I heartily
support this Bill, because I think it is a
considerable improvement on the scheme
we have been accustomed to during the
past two years. It has made provision for
men who would otherwise have suffered
on account of unemployment and long
periods of sickness when they were about
to become entitled to an old-age pension.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we all listened
with great attention and considerable
interest to the speech of the Noble Viscount who introduced this somewhat complicated Measure to us in terms of the
greatest possible lucidity. I am sure we
shall greatly appreciate, in the sense of
giving due weight to what he said, the
fact that the terms of this Measure have
been discussed and approved by the representatives of the Approved Societies, and
we shall also give great weight to what
has just been said by the hon. Senator on
my left (Mr. Joseph Cunningham). It
seems clear that my hon. Friend and those
he represents will welcome this Bill, and
that is a great point in its favour. I did
not, I must confess, quite follow him
when he suggested there was a new provision introduced to enable a worker
whose card had not been stamped to recover his possible loss from his employer.
If I read the Bill correctly-and as to that
the Minister of Labour will no doubt tell
us there is no change in principle; it is
only the method of recovery that has been
altered and extended. I think the power
to recover from a defaulting employer was
always there, although the method of enforcement was somewhat different.
Passing from detail to the more main
question, it seems to me that the point
that is likely to attract attention is the
course taken in asking this Parliament to
allow an Imperial Act dealing with matters which are within the law-making
powers of this Parliament to become law
in Northern Ireland without the direct
sanction of the Northern Parliament, but
by means of an Order in Council by his
Grace the Governor. On the face of
things a proposal of that sort is unconstitutional, but in this case, if I may say
so, the unconstitutionalism is so flagrant
and so glaring as almost to disarm criticism. The point is, of course, that the
matter has been discussed in the Imperial
Parliament and our own representatives
are part of that Parliament. Also, looking to the relations as legislative bodies of
the Parliament at Westminster and this
Parliament it seems to me obvious that
you must not endeavour to apply too
rigidly to the minor Parliament the same
conditions as those which obtain in the
larger Parliament. I think we might
now and agan find it advisable to take a
course such as this, a course which it is
difficult to defend in theory but which we
know is going to work extremely well
in practice.
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
We had, as the House will remember,
a somewhat similar case four years ago,
when in order to secure that some of our
unemployed should not lose benefit it was
necessary for us to legislate here in
advance, and we did so. The conditions
then were somewhat different. We legislated for a maximum period of nine
months, or until we ourselves made provision in the matter. We did make provision before six of the nine months had
gone, and the Bill came to us endorsed
with the unanimous approval of the other
House. I do not know what course the
discussion on this Bill took elsewhere, and
I am not sure that it is of very great importance. I do think we shall find there
will be no complaint made against us by
those we indirectly represent on
the
ground that, in order to secure improved
benefits for some of our people, which
apparently are not going to cost anything,
we have proceeded with more than the
usual rapidity of Parliament. Therefore for myself, subject to some little discussion of details at a later stage, I shall
support the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
approval of this Bill, as it is calculated
to remove anomalies and injustices that
a large number of insured persons felt they
were suffering under. Under the system
of giving a free grant to those in arrears
of contributions the various societies have
not acted in anything like a uniform
manner, and large numbers of insured
persons who unfortunately have been unemployed for a number of months prior
to the close of the insurance year have
found after six or nine months that they
have been deprived of the income they
were expecting to receive when they were
laid aside by illness. I welcome this
Measure from the point of view that it
will put an end to that grievance. Many
men and women have had a feeling of
indignation against the former Act because
of the inequality of treatment in various
societies. It is well known to all those
who know anything about the administration of the Act that some societies have
had a larger volume of sickness amongst
their members than other societies have
had, and hitherto they have not been able
to deal with their members when incapacitated in the same generous manner that
other societies have dealt with their
members under similar circumstances.
This Bill will remove that feeling of
discontent amongst many insured persons.
Recently a man approached me on this
question. He had been five months unemployed, and it was the end of the insurance year. He continued unemployed
until such time as by the old Act it was
necessary for the society to issue to him
an arrears card, which he had to stamp
within a certain period, otherwise his sickness benefit would be reduced for the
following insurance year. This Bill will
also remove that anomaly, and it will thus
I
take away from many insured persons that
feeling of discontent and injustice.
would also like to express the hope that not
merely will we move on parallel lines with
Great Britain in the matter of National
Health Insurance, but that in the near
future such a situation will be brought
about that will make it possible for Irishmen to go from Northern Ireland to the
Irish Free State and from the Irish Free
State to Northern Ireland, and have an
exchange of benefits on an equal footing.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): I am very gratified that this
Bill has been so sympathetically received,
both in this House and in another place.
I think that reception is due to the fact
that we all recognize it is an improvement
on the law as it stands at the moment, and
that it will better the position of our insured contributors. It will get over one
serious hardship, which many of our
people have been suffering under in the
past, namely, insufficient provision for
those persons who were willing to
join Approved Societies and who were
rejected as bad lives! Those were
the people who really required an
insurance scheme, and yet, through no
fault of their own, they were unable to
get the full benefits which the scheme gave
to their more fortunate fellow-workers.
As the noble Viscount has said, it brings
in certain other workers as insured persons who previously were prevented from
becoming associated with the scheme, and
it does give greater powers to my Department to see that those employers who do
not act fairly towards their workers in
the matter of stamping their cards are
brought to justice.
As regards the important matter of
certification, which was raised by Senator
Joseph Cunningham, I think he is under
a wrong impression, or else some doctors
are acting in a very wrong way.
We
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
have 700 certifiers in Northern Ireland.
Practically all the doctors in our country
districts are certifiers, and almost all the
doctors in our industrial centres, and
none of these doctors is entitled to
charge insured persons for giving certificates. I want to make that perfectly
clear, and I want to say that no complaint
has ever reached my Department, so far
as I know, that doctors were acting in
the way suggested by the hon. Senator,
a way that I certainly would not be prepared to defend.
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) has raised a most important and
interesting point as regards the unconstitutional procedure which he thinks has
been adopted in connection with this
Bill. My own view is that the great
point is that the right thing should be
done at the right time, and that procedure is a secondary consideration.
Further, I would point out that if this
Parliament was absolutely separate and
distinct from the British Parliament, a
stronger case could be made out as regards
unconstitutional action, but we all know
--and most of us are very glad that it is
so that this Parliament is so associated
with the Imperial Parliament that it is
very hard to distinguish where the duties
of the one commence and the duties of
the other cease. However, as a matter of
fact the procedure that has been adopted
is not, in my opinion, what can be called
irregular. It simply amounts to this:
That the Imperial Parliament must deal
with certain matters in this Bill, because
that Parliament alone has power to do
so, such as matters associated with the
Mercantile Marine and what are termed
International Societies.
The questions of the prolongation of
insurance and arrears either Parliament
can deal with, but for obvious reasons it
is desirable in this instance that they
should be dealt with by the British Parliament always subject to this and this is
provided for in the Bill in the clearest
possible way that any action taken by
the British Parliament in these matters
does not become law here until this Parliament decides. You are asked to-day to
give your sanction to the application of
these features of the British Measure to
Northern Ireland by Order in Council on
the same date as those provisions are
brought into force in Great Britain. Then
there are other matters dealing with our
own courts here which we alone can deal
with and which this Bill provides. I
think I have covered the various points
raised, and I just wish, in conclusion,
to thank this House, and particularly
those hon. Members who have spoken, for
the reception they have given to this
Measure.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to take this
opportunity of thanking the Minister of
Labour for the information he has given
to this House on the question of panel
doctors. I did not know we had so many
panel doctors in Northern Ireland. I
always thought panel doctors were few.
and far between in the city of Belfast.
I was very glad to hear the right hon.
Gentleman say that those doctors had no
right to receive a fee from an insured
contributor who went to them for a certificate. I had the unfortunate experience
last year and during part of this year of
having to go to a doctor to get a weekly
certificate, and he charged me a shilling.
I paid a shilling for every certificate I
got until the insurance was stopped and I
ceased to go to him. I might say that
the insurance was stopped long before I
was better; in fact I am not altogether
better yet, but the insurance was stopped
on 16th or 18th January last. The next
time I go to that doctor for a certificate I
shall be able to talk to him.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think the correct
way to talk to him would be to tell him
that he was receiving payment twice for
the same thing.
Mr. McDOWELL: Might I say also
that in the case of a man who is attending
one of these doctors and who prefers not
to take his medicine, although he wishes
to have his certificate, the doctor, although he may not charge the man for
the certificate, lets him know that if he
does not take the medicine he will not
get the certificate. Consequently a man
is between the devil and the deep sea,
and he takes the medicine and pays for
it whether he requires it or not.
Mr. ANDREWS: If the hon. Senator
will bring cases like that to my notice I
will see that the doctor is removed from
the panel.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
could send a crowd along to you in the
course of a week.
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second Time) agreed to.
Fisheries Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE and
FERRARD: I am afraid I shall be some
time in explaining the provisions of this
Bill, and I crave the indulgence of the
House for the length of time I may take.
The Fisheries Bill, which was presented
to and read a first time by this House on
Thursday last, represents a still further
step in the efforts made over the greater
part of a century to regulate our fisheries
in the manner best calculated not only to
conserve existing stocks but to allow of
their further development. Legislation
in respect of fisheries is a matter which,
from its very nature, is always likely to
give rise to considerable divergence of
opinion. On the one hand there are those
who oppose any step which is calculated
to restrict the activities of the considerable
community either mainly or totally dependent on fishing for their livelihood.
On the other, there are those who,
either by reason of direct financial interest
or from their solicitude for the preservation and development of our fisheries, desire to impose a check upon those activities. As between these two classes of persons the Government is expected to hold
the balance evenly, that is to say, to
recognize the right of the fishermen to a
reasonable livelihood from their calling
without, at the same time, endangering
the position for those to come after them.
No Government would be justified, however, in placing any restriction on the use
or exploitation of our fisheries which had
not been shown to be essential to the conservation of our fisheries or to their development to the utmost of their natural
capacity.
The present Measure has been framed
with that limitation constantly in view,
and it is the sincere belief of the Government that each and everyone of the provisions embodied in the Bill will, if not
immediately, in the long run, operate to
the advantage of all the interests concerned. As an instance of the provisions
designed to attain this end I might mention the proposed limitation which the Bill
proposes to place upon the size of eel that
may lawfully be taken in Northern Ireland. The House will readily conceive
that in fixing a limit of this kind the
Government has not been actuated by
any intention of restricting the quantity
or number of eels that may ultimately
On
be captured within our territory.
the contrary the limitation is proposed as
a result of a statutory Inquiry_held last
winter, at the instance of two Boards of
Conservators, into the present times and
methods of ell-fishing in Northern Ireland,
and is designed to allow of our valuable
eel fisheries attaining to something like
their natural capacities. At the present
time the quantity of mature eels taken
at the weirs by the owners of the Lough
Neagh Eel Fishery amounts only to a
little more than 30 tons per annum.
In addition to this catch must be added,
of course, the eels taken by the fishermen
either by long lines or by nets in Lough
Neagh itself. Included in these latter
catches, however, is a very considerable
proportion of small immature eels weighing in some instances, I am informed, as
little as 2 ozs. each. Taking both the
weir and the lough fishings together the
total capture amounts only to some 170
tons. Expert opinion has placed the full
natural capacity of the Lough Neagh Eel
Fishery as high as 700 tons per annum.
Even at a conservative estimate an output
of 500 tons per annum might reasonably
be expected. The statutory Inquiry
showed, in the opinion of the Government,
that the present unsatisfactory state of
affairs in regard to this particular fishery
was mainly due to the extensive captures
of small eels by use of long lines and that,
unless the present methods of capture
were regulated both by way of enforcing
a close season and of limiting the size of
eels that might be taken, the output from
the fishery was in grave danger of being
reduced to almost negligible dimensions.
In the Bill, therefore, provision has been
made to apply a statutory close season to
all instruments and devices used for the
capture of eels and for the limitation of
the weight of the eels that may be captured. I have chosen the instance of eel
fishing as a forcible example of the particular circumstances in which the Government has found it necessary to impose
restrictions which, while perhaps immediately unacceptable to certain interests, will
be found ultimately to operate to the advantage of all.
Fisheries Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
The First Part of the Bill is devoted
to amendment of the existing statutes
which relate to those bodies, the Boards
of Conservators, to which the executive
administration of great part of the fishery
laws has been entrusted. These Boards
.comprise members elected by licence
holders together with ex-officio members
who serve by virtue of their direct interest in the ownership of the fishery
rights. The development of fisheries is
essentially a matter for far-sighted policy,
in the execution of which the present
period of three years during which each
Board holds office is unquestionably too
limited. In order that Boards may be
able to conceive and execute a policy with
regard to fishery matters in their district
more effectively, the Bill extends their life
from three years to five years.
The remainder of Part I provides for
the resignation of an elected member of
a Board who, for any reason, finds himself
unable to continue his duties and for the
filling of the vacancy so created by the
co-option of a successor by the remaining
members of the Board. Apparently by
some oversight provision for the resignation of a conservator was omitted from
the earlier Acts, and, althought vacancies
caused by death or incapacity were foreseen, the earlier Acts stipulated that such
vacancies should be filled by election, a
costly procedure which diverts substantial
amounts of the Board's limited financial
resources from the more legitimate uses
which could be found for such sums.
Part II of the Bill is designed, by regulating and controlling the purchase
and sale of salmon, trout and eels, to
facilitate the detection of persons injuring
the stocks of these fish by fishing without
permission, by the use of illegal methods
or by the capture of immature fish.
Clause 7 of the Bill, which requires dealers to record in a register all purchases,
receipts, and sales of salmon, trout, and
eels, was the subject of much discussion
in another place. As originally drafted.
it called for the registration of all sales,
including retail sales of small amounts.
In the light of representations that the
inclusion of these retail sales, in the particulars called for in the register, would
impose hardship upon dealers, without
corresponding advantage to the administration, it was arranged to modify this
Clause, and, as it now stands for the
an
It
consideration of this House, sales to any
person in any day aggregating less than
ten pounds weight are exempt from registration. It is thought that this fully
meets the objections raised to the original
provisions, without materially impairing
the utility of the registers. In Clause 8
hon. Members will notice that hawkers
have been excluded from the provisions
of this part of the Bill. Considerable
criticism has been made of this omission,
and the Government has given very careful thought to the matter. Hawkers are
itinerant class without fixed premises. They deal in comparatively small
quantities of fish, and in many cases
would be incapable of keeping a register
of their dealings in the up-to-date and
accurate form which alone would make
such a register of practical value.
would, accordingly, be very difficult to
apply to them the same regulations as
those provided in the Bill for fish dealers
operating from fixed premises. It is
doubtless true that hawkers may from
time to time provide an outlet for
poached fish, but it is questionable
whether this outlet is nearly so great as
that found amongst consumers by the
poachers themselves surreptitiously offering their illicit catches for sale, a practice which could not be prevented by the
licensing provisions of the present Bill,
and with which water-bailiffs and other
authorized officers could, when they detect it, deal fairly adequately under
existing legislation. On the balance,
therefore, the Government does not at
present feel that any great advantage
would be gained by including hawkers in
the provisions of this part of the Bill, and
has preferred to exempt them from the
licensing obligations until experience has
shown their inclusion both desirable and
practicable.
Clause 10 refers particularly to eels and
its provisions are based upon the results
of very exhaustive inquiry last autumn
into the eel fishings of Northern Ireland
to which I have already alluded. The
weight limitation in sub-clause (a) originally fixed an individual minimum weight
of 8 ounces, but, following representations
made to the Government regarding the
difficulty of estimating accurately the
weight of an eel when it is brought into
the fishing boat, amendment to the present wording was accepted by the Government. Similarly with regard to
the daily close season the Govern-
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
ment, following representations made
by fishermen regarding the difficulty of
setting long lines after sunset and lifting
them before sunrise, agreed to exclude
long lines from the operation of the daily
close season. As in the case of hawkers,
however, this is a matter on which careful
watch will be kept, so that if it is found
by experience that the exemption of long
lines from the daily close season is leading
to abuse and continued deterioration of
the eel stocks steps may be taken at a
future date to prevent such abuse.
no
The Third Part of the Bill deals with
licence duties. Hon. Senators are,
doubt, aware that the funds out of which
the Boards of Conservators meet the expenses of the protection of fisheries are
largely provided by those exploiting the
fisheries within the Board's area, in the
form of licence duties paid in respect of
the different engines used by them for
the capture of fish. For many years
past the cost of preserving the fisheries
in Northern Ireland has exceeded the revenue derived from licence duties, and it
has been necessary to augment the funds
of the Boards of Conservators by grantsin-aid from the Exchequer. Whilst the
payment of such grants-in-aid can undoubtedly be defended in view of the advantages derived by the State as a whole
from the existence of a healthy fishing
industry, it is obviously undesirable that
those exploiting the fisheries should be
relieved from paying their full share of
the cost of preserving the fisheries and,
at the same time, it is essential that each
section of the fishery interests should pay
an equitable share as compared with the
other sections.
The scale of licence duties at present
operative in Northern Ireland was fixed
many years ago and the Government
decided, accordingly, to include in the
present Bill certain modifications of the
existing duties. Section 11, which fixes
the new scale of duties, was the subject of
prolonged discussion in another place, and
the provisions of the section now before
this House represent those arrived at as
the result of much discussion. Hon. Members will notice that the duty on single
salmon rods has been raised from £1 to £1
10s per annum. The additional 10s per
annum is unlikely to cause any serious
hardship to anglers and will provide substantial additional revenue to the Boards
of Conservators. The maximum rate of
duty on trammel nets, a type of net the
use of which is permitted by by-law, for
the capture of pollen in Lough Neagh, is
reduced from £1 10s 0d to an absolute rate
of £1. This will not involve any serious
reduction in revenue to the Boards since
the majority of trammel net users fish in
the Coleraine conservancy district, where
the Board, exercising its power to reduce
duties below the statutory maximum, has
been accustomed to charge £1 per annum.
At present draft nets for pollen are subject to a duty of £1 10s Od per annum,
whilst the same net if used for eels would
be subject to a duty of £3 per annum.
These nets are, in fact, used for both
pollen and eels, and it has been thought
desirable, accordingly, to fix a new duty
of £2 per annum on all kinds of draft nets
irrespective of the use to which they are
put. By this amendment fishermen
accustomed to use nets for the capture of
pollen only suffer to the extent of 10s per
annum, whilst on the other hand all other
kinds of draft net fishermen benefit to the
extent of £1 per annum by the reduction
of the duty from £3 to £2. Sub-Section
(2) of the Clause in question imposes, in
all districts in Northern Ireland, a duty
on long lines for eels. For a reason, to
which I shall refer in connection with
Clause 12, no licence duty has hitherto
been payable in the Coleraine district on
long lines, although such a duty is payable
in the Enniskillen district. The rate of
duty specified in Sub-Section (2) is the
same as that already operative in the
Enniskillen district.
Clause 12 removes an anomaly which
was created by the wording of an earlier
Act. Whilst it was enacted that all
engines for the capture of salmon, trout
and eels must be licensed and whilst provision was made for the fixing of a licence
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
duty on a new type of engine by a Board of
Conservators, the wording of the enactment
provided that an application must be made
by the person proposing to use the new
type of engine, for the fixing of a licence
duty before the Board concerned was entitled to fix such duty. In the case of
long lines for eels in the Coleraine district
no such application was ever made, and
the users of the lines were thereby enabled
to escape payment of duty since no licence
duty could be fixed by the Board.
Clause now in question so amends the
earlier enactment as to make the Ministry
of Commerce responsible for the fixing of
new licence duties and authorizes the
Ministry to do so without waiting for an
application from an intending user.
The
Part IV of the Bill deals with administration and legal proceedings. In the
original 1842 Act, and in a number of
the subsequent amending Acts, extensive
powers of search and seizure were conferred upon water-bailiffs and members
of the Constabulary force, in order that
they might be in a position to enforce the
provisions regulating fishery matters.
The provisions of Part II of the present
Bill necessitates some extension of these
powers, and it has been found desirable,
accordingly, to bring together and to
bring up to date the various provisions
in respect of search and seizure scattered
through the earlier Acts. This is the
purpose of Clause 13 of the Bill which,
in the main, extends rather in detail than
in principle the powers in question.
There are, however, two matters dealt
Iwith in the Clause which were not dealt
with in any of the earlier Acts, and to
which, in view of criticizms directed
against them in the other House, I may
with advantage devote a few moments.
I refer to the provisions of Sub-sections.
(1) (g) and (6), and Sub-sections (3) and
(4).
Hon. Senators will notice that in Subsection (1) (g) authorized officers are entitled to demand from the custodian of
any fish or other article examined who
may not be the actual owner of such fish
or article the name and address of the
owner, and in Sub-section (6) the custodian of the article becomes guilty of an
offence under the Bill should, he refuse
to supply the name and address of the
owner where known to him. As originally drafted, Sub-section (6) created
some apprehension in the minds of members of the other House lest it could be
interpreted as requiring a person suspected of having committed an offence to
act as an informer in respect of his associates, an interpretation which the Government had no intention of applying to
the sub-section. The sub-section was,
accordingly, re-worded, and in its present
form removes all ground for misapprehension on this point.
With regard to Sub-sections (3) and (4),
hon. Senators will doubtless be able to
conceive circumstances following the
seizure of fish in which such fish would
deteriorate before action in respect of
them could be completed. In these circumstances it is essential that means
should be found of disposing of the fish
without destroying the value of the
evidence obtained through their seizure.
No provision to this effect appears in any
of the earlier Acts, and the omission is
corrected by Sub-sections (3) and (4) of
the present clause. With respect
Clause 14 I need say no more than that
it is desirable that the central authority,
which is contributing at least a proportion
of the funds administered by Boards of
Conservators, should have some remedy
if it finds that the duties of the Board
are not being carried out with proper
efficency by the Board's staff.
to
Hon. Senators will doubtless recollect
that extensive changes were made a few
years ago, following the establishment of
the Irish Free State and the final settlement of the Boundary between the Irish
Free State and Northern Ireland in the
division of Northern Ireland amongst the
different Boards of Conservators. The
present conservancy districts appear to be
satisfactory, but circumstances may arise
in which the Government may find it
necessary to make alterations in the fishery
districts or in their constituent electoral
divisions. Advantage has, accordingly,
been taken of the present Bill to consolidate and simplify in Clause 15 the procedure involved in making these changes.
Similarly in Section 16 certain minor
changes have been made in the procedure
preliminary to the holding of inquiries
into fishery matters.
The first Sub-Clause of Clause 17 fixes
the upper limit of the penalties which may
be imposed in respect of offences against
the provisions of the Bill, whilst the second
Sub-clause repeals all the earlier provisions.
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
which fixed minimum penalties in respect
of fishery offences. The repeal of these minimum penalty Clauses in the earlier Acts
has been made possible by, and should be
read in conjunction with, the provisions
of Sub-Section (3), which provides for the
trial of all fishery cases in the first instance by a resident magistrate and, in
the case of an appeal, by a county court
judge or Recorder sitting alone. Hitherto
fishery cases have been tried by benches
of local magistrates, and two very serious
difficulties have arisen in respect of which
repeated representations have been made
to the Government. On the one hand the
local benches have been obliged in certain
cases, by the fixing of statutory minimum
penalties, to impose penalties which in
their opinion greatly exceeded the necessities of the case, and they have felt it
their duty to support petitions sent forward to the Governor by convicted persons
in almost every case where a heavy penalty
has thus been imposed.
Hon. Senators will appreciate that this
is a most undesirable position both for the
magistrates and for the Government. On
the other hand instances have frequently
occurred in which local magistrates have
regarded fishery offences as of a venial
nature and have imposed penalties so
small as to be quite useless as a deterrent,
and there has been practically no uniformity in the manner in which fishery cases
have been dealt with in courts of summary
jurisdiction. Resident magistrates are
trained officers with very wide experience
in the trial and punishment of the
different kinds of offence dealt with by
courts of summary jurisdiction.
By removing all the lower limits from
the penalties to be imposed in respect of
fishery cases and by leaving the fixing of
such penalties to the discretion of resident
magistrates the Government is confident
that the utilization of these officers' wide
experience will result in a much more
equitable and uniform administraton of
the law. The remaining sub-sections in
Clause 17 introduce no new principles and
merely extend to the provisions of this
Bill provisions already enacted in earlier
statutes with regard to appeals from
courts of summary jurisdiction.
Part V of the Bill includes two clauses
which were inserted in the course of the
consideration of the Bill in another place.
Section 66 of the 1842 Fisheries Act prohibited the use in inland waters of any
net for the capture of coarse fish during
the salmon close season. This prohibition is in many cases entirely justified
since in many waters the use of nets during the salmon close season would seriously endanger the salmon and trout
stocks. On the other hand there are undoubtedly cases, for example in portions.
of Lough Erne, where the use of such
nets could safely be permitted and where,
in fact, the reduction of coarse fish stocks
during the winter would be of definite
advantage to the salmon and trout
fisheries. Clause 18 of the Bill recognizes
this fact and empowers the Ministry of
Commerce to authorize the use of nets in
this manner under certain circumstances.
Clause 19 is necessitated by the provisions of Section 73 of the 1842 Act,
which prohibits any person from taking,
selling, purchasing, or having in his possession for any purpose the fry of salmon,
trout, or eels. A Clause in a later Act
provides for the possession of salmon fry
for scientific or restocking purposes, but
no similar provision has ever been made
with regard to eels. Consequently,
owners of eel fisheries who capture elvers
in Northern Irish waters, or who purchase
elvers from places outside Northern Ireland for the purpose of restocking and
developing their own fisheries, commit a
technical breach of the law in so doing.
It has been thought well, accordingly, to
regularize this matter by providing in
Clause 19 for the capture, etc., of salmon,
trout and eel fry for scientific and restocking purposes. The remainder of
Part V of the Bill calls for no comment,
dealing as it does with definitions and
with the dates on which the different
provisions of the Bill shall come into
operation. I apologize to the House for
the length of time I have taken in trying
to explain some of the principles and
Clauses of the Bill. I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
The Bill is an excellent one in many respects, but in regard to one particular
matter dealt with it is the general wish in
the country that there should be an
amendment made. The salmon fishing
rivers of Northern Ireland are, of course,
comprised within a comparatively small
area, but in spite of that fact there is a
considerable difference in the time of
opening and closing the various rivers for
fishing, and this is a great encouragement
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Colonel Crawford]
to poaching. The hon. Senator opposite
(Mr. Porter Porter) may have a salmon
river that opens early, and I may have a
river that opens a few weeks later. As
soon as Senator Porter Porter's river is
opened for fishing poaching immediately
starts in my river, and the salmon are
sold as having been caught in his river.
If his river opens later than mine poaching commences in his river when my river
opens, and his salmon are sold as my
salmon. In my opinion the salmon
fisheries should get the same protection as
the eel fisheries receive in this Bill. Eels
are the only fish that are to be protected,
and it would be a great benefit to Conservators and the owners of salmon rivers
if similar protection was afforded to
salmon fisheries. It is my intention to
move an amendment in Committee with
this object in view.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I hope that in
rising for the first time in this House
hon. Members will grant me the indulgence usually granted to a
newcomer
until he has proved himself unworthy.
I am very glad to be able to speak on
this Bill because, living as I do among the
fishermen of Lough Neagh, I know the
feeling they have, especially in regard to
our friend the eel. The sound fisherman
has recognized for some years that immature eels were being taken out of
Lough Neagh by more short-sighted
brethren of his who did not realize that
if they persisted in catching immature
fish they would never get fish growing into
maturity and the stock would suffer.
There is no doubt that the Lough Neagh
stock of eels has deteriorated very severely
during past years. This Bill will do away
with a great deal of the cause of that
trouble, because however short-sighted a
man is if he attempts to do what he has
been doing in the past he will be punished
and heavily fined. Therefore, I welcome
this Bill on behalf of my friends of Lough
Neagh. All the sound fishermen will
welcome it, and the unsound man will
have to do what he is told.
I will now pass to one of the Regulations
dealing with licences. Clause 11, Subsection (2) reads as follows:
The licence duty for long lines for eels
shall be two pounds for a long line with
not more than one thousand hooks, and
three pounds for a long line with more
than one thousand hooks, and no Board
of Conservators shall have authority to
reduce or diminish the said licence
duties.
I want, if I can, to demonstrate to this
House the extraordinary difficulty the
Government and conservators will have in
discovering whether a line is of 1,000 hooks
or 1,010 hooks. They have not got on
Lough Neagh sufficient inspectors or water
bailiffs to count a line of 1,000 hooks, and
when it comes to distinguishing whether
a line is of 1,000 hooks or 1,050 hooks it
will be impossible to do it. Even if an
inspector was satisfied that a certain line
was of 1,000 hooks what is to prevent a
fisherman adding 500 more hooks when he
gets out into the Lough. Therefore, I
feel that this is a provision that will not
be carried out, and when you make a law
the terms of which are not carried out by
anybody it becomes a dead letter.
know that in America a law which prohibits certain things is being continuously
evaded by the people, who do not believe
in it. In the same way your long line
man, who thinks that a licence duty of £3
for 1,050 hooks is too much will always
do the bailiffs in the eye if he possibly can.
We
Therefore, I ask the Government to
consider the advisability of having one
uniform licence for the long line, and I
think it is only fair to the fishermen to
make it £2, which is the Enniskillen rate,
and not to charge £3 for a line that is a
little longer than the one for which you
propose to charge £2. It will be impossible to carry out the Sub-clause as it at
present stands. It will be a dead letter
law, and a dead letter law is no law at all.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill before us is,
as I think the noble Viscount told us,
about the fiftieth Bill dealing with fishery
matters in Ireland as a whole. I think
that, taking it all round, it will be found
in clear up a good many outstanding
difficulties. I have not the knowledge
of my hon. Friend, who has just sat
down (Colonel Pakenham)--whose views
we are so glad to hear in this Chamber-—-
on matters relating particularly to eels,
and I do not propose to inflict any observations on that subject, either on the
House or on the Committee at a later
date, but there are matters more of detail
which will require attention. I think,
perhaps, that the fishmonger, that is the
perfectly honest man who sells fish, has
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
not even yet received quite as much consideration as he should, but to that we
can refer later. I have particularly in
mind the provision that a licence is to
terminate on a man's death. There is
no such provision in the case of a man
who has a licence to deal in game, or a
man who has a licence to kill game. In
the latter case it is quite unimportant,
because a dead man does not kill game,
but in the case of the other man who has
a licence it seems to me an unnecessary
provision, and we ought to deal with it
later on
The other point is the proposal to transfer the jurisdiction from the ordinary
magistrates, who are mostly men of experience, to Resident Magistrates.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
No, no.
Mr. LESLIE: There really are magistrates who have been for some years in
Commission, and who go year by year and
month by month and sit on the Benches
to which they are accredited. Some of
those magistrates have found, as I mvself have found, that a Bench, taken as
a whole, is just as fair as any other tribunal in dealing with a case as to
whether flax-water has or has not been
let into a fishing river. The proposal to
take away one by one the powers of local
justices is very much resented by the
majority of magistrates.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
No.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I am not
able to agree with the right hon. and
gallant Member. At all events, it is a
mistake to provoke disagreement unless it
is absolutely essential, and perhaps I had
better say
it is resented by some
magistrates.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD.
Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: It is not as a rule desired by the people as a whole that magistrates should be deprived of the powers
which they possess.
It does not add to
the weight of a magisterial decision on
some matter which is left to the ordinary
Bench if it is known that there are some
matters, trivial ones perhaps, which such
magistrates are not considered competent
to deal with. With that I will endeavour
to deal at a later date. The noble Viccount spoke of the removal of the minimum fine. The Fishery Acts were, of
course, punctuated with minimum fines
of one sort or another, and curiously
enough, although I may claim to have
opposed minimum fines in this House as
often as anyone else, I never attacked the
minimum fine in fishery cases. I have
pointed out that an offence under the
Fishery Act is not only an offence against
the law. because it is a breach of the law,
but it is a definite offence, or rather a
definite wrong to whoever has the rights
of fishing. The thing partakes more,
perhaps, of the nature of a civil wrong
than a criminal offence, but it is
both, and as it is impossible in cases of
this sort to act adequately and fairly,
minimum fines in those cases have always
been defended, particularly on the ground.
that a proportion of them went indirectly
to the person aggrieved, because they
were generally given to the water-bailiffs
to help them in the cost of protection.
Those minimum fines have now gone,
but I cannot see that that makes it in
any way necessary to remove the ordinary
magistrates from having jurisdiction in
fishery cases. I do agree, as I have again
and again agreed, that it is better that
appeals in matters of this sort should go
to the county court judge sitting alone
than to a mixed bench of Quarter Sessions.
There is no use appealing from, let us
say, the bench at Ballysmith to the
Quarter Sessions sitting at Ballyjones, because if the case had arisen in the Petty
Sessions' district of Ballysmith you would
get at the Quarter Sessions at Ballyjones.
such magistrates as did not happen to have
sat on the bench at Ballysmith. I think
the proposal that appeals should be heard
by the County Court Judge or Recorder
sitting alone is far the better one. As I
have said, there are various points of detail that will have to be considered in
Committee, but I do not think we are
likely to be occupied very long with them.
I do think the Bill will fill a long-felt want
in various directions, and it has my
support.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I should like
to support the remarks of the hon. and
Gallant Member (Colonel Pakenham) with
regard to the differentiation in the licence
duty on long lines of 1,000 hooks and long
lines of over 1,000 hooks. There is no use
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Porter Porter]
our making a law that we cannot enforce,
and that particular provision cannot be
enforced. It would take the available
police half their time to count the number
of hooks on a line. Considering that a
man with a long line can catch up to 80
fish in a night the question of whether the
licence costs £1 or £2 does not matter.
Therefore I think the least we can do is
to exercise common sense in this matter and
pass a Bill that we can enforce. With
regard to the controversy between the hon.
and learned Member (Mr. Leslie) and the
hon. and Gallant Member (Colonel Sharman Crawford) in respect of local magistrates, I must take sides with the hon. and
gallant Member. A resident magistrate
is the only magistrate who can give an
unbiassed judgment. Local magistrates
in my county have actually taken salmon
from the river themselves, as I am sure
Members of this House are aware, and it
is a thing we cannot help. I understand
they do not require a licence at all to fish
in Lough Erne. I believe that if a heavier
licence duty was put on nets it would be
much more effective in protecting and developing the stock of fish. They are
responsible for a great deal of the harm
that is done in destroying immature fish,
and it is very hard to catch people netting,
and when they are caught they generally
get off with a trivial fine.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the most remarkable thing about this debate was the
figures given by the noble Viscount as to
the potential food value of the eel fisheries
of Northern Ireland. The figures supplied
by the specialist called in to advise the
Government tell us that the potential eel
production of our fisheries is 700 tons per
annum in Lough Neagh alone, and yet
the maximum that has been taken is only
170 tons, and of that quantity 140 tons
were immature fish, that is, fish that, if
they had been left to develop, would have
brought to the people of Northern Ireland
700 tons of fish, or an increase of 2,200
per cent. per annum on the present supply
from that source. I am pleased to support this Measure with a view to preventing this wanton destruction of human
food. I hope the attention of fishermen
will be drawn to these amazing figures and
that the wrong they are inflicting not only
on the fisheries but on the community in
general by insisting on catching and destroying immature fish will be realized by
them.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am glad to know that on the
whole the House gives its approval to
this Bill. I will endeavour, as far as I
can, to answer the various points raised
by hon. Members during the course of
this interesting debate. The hon. and
gallant Senator (Colonel Sharman Crawford), as well as the hon. Member (Mr.
Porter Porter), desires a uniform close
season throughout Northern Ireland. I
do not think it would be possible to do
that under this Bill, because it can be
done at the present time under the existing law. If it is desired to alter a close
season in any district there has to be a
statutory inquiry held, and after that
has taken place the period of a close season can be altered.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Each Board of Conservators are jealous
of their own rivers, and they do not consider other rivers.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I should like to congratulate
the hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Pakenham) on his maiden speech, and I
trust that we may often have the pleasure of hearing his voice in this House.
He lives on the same Lough as I do, and
he is very much concerned about the
difference in the licence duty for a long
line for eels that has a thousand hooks,
and a long line that has more than a
thousand hooks. As a matter of fact,
the rates of licence duty as laid down in
this Bill were actually taken from the
rates ruling in the Enniskillen district,
where I am told they work satisfactorily.
Of course, there is always the danger that
some fisherman who has got a licence for
a long line with a thousand hooks may
try to add a few more hooks to it, but I
am told that the system has worked fairly
satisfactorily in the Enniskillen district,
and we propose to apply it to the rest of
Northern Ireland. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) disliked the idea of
a licence lapsing on the death of the
owner. The reason for that is that in
this Bill the Government do not desire
to attach a licence to a particular place.
The licence is granted to an individual,
and the hon. and learned Member probably knows that each applicant for a
licence has to obtain a certificate from a
justice of the peace. It does not in the
least follow that if Mr. A., who has a
licence, dies, his son, whoever he may
School Sites Bill-
Second Reading
22 MAY 1928
be, will be a desirable person to retain
that licence, and, therefore, under the
Bill a licence will lapse on the death of
the particular individual who first
obtained it.
I am not going to enter into the controversy of local magistrates against
Resident Magistrates, nor shall I attempt
to hold the balance between the hon, and
learned Member who comes
from my
county and the hon. and gallant Member
who comes from another county. The
hon. and gallant Member seems to suggest that local magistrates are not competent to deal with fishery cases, but as
the Lieutenant of my county I cannot possibly agree with him. Senator Dorman referred to the supply of fish and the loss
to the community owing to the decreased
output as compared with what might be
produced. It is perfectly true that the
fishing industry of Lough Neagh has
deteriorated, and if we can do anything
to increase the food supply of Northern
Ireland, so far as fish is concerned, we
ought to do so, because the development
of that industry will mean not only more
money for the fishermen, but an increased
supply of food for the people.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
School Sites Bill [S.].
Order for consideration ol Commons'
Amendments read.
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of 44 and 45
Vict., c. 65, as to leases for schools).
The power of making leases which is
conferred by the Leases for Schools
(Ireland) Act, 1881, upon the persons
therein described shall extend to the
making (subject to the limitations contained in section four of the said Act
as to quantity, term and rent) of leases
to an education authority of land together with a school or schools and playground, teacher's residence, or other
school accommodation, erected or provided on such land.
Cormons' Amendment:
Page 1, line 10, leave out from
"authority to the end of the Clause,
and insert:
(a) of land for the purpose of providing a playground or place of recreation or other school accommodation,
in connection with a school or schools;
or
(b) of land together with a school or
schools and playground, teacher's residence, or other school accommodation,
erected or provided on such land.
((
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, That this
House do agree with the Commons in the
said Amendment. This Amendment and
the one following were described in
another place as largely
as largely conveyancing
lawyers Amendments. This Amendment amplifies the original clause. The
original draft as it left this House provided that the Leases for Schools (Ireland) Act of 1881 should be extended to
the making of leases to an Education
Authority of land together with a school
or schools and playground, teacher's residence or other school accommodation
erected or provided on such land." The
Commons' Amendment extends this further
so that the old Act of 1881 can also be applied to taking additional adjoining land
for playground purposes, etc. in connection with an existing school.
Amendment agreed to.
Commons' Amendment:-
Page 1, line 12, at end add as a new
Sub-section :-
The persons described in the Leases
for Schools (Ireland) Act, 1881, shall
(in addition to the power of making
leases conferred by that Act or by that
Act as amended by this Act) have power
to make leases to an education authority
for any term or in perpetuity, of any
quantity of land for any of the purposes
mentioned in the said Act or in this
Act.
Provided that every lease under the
provisions of this sub-section shall ensure the best rents that can reasonably
be obtained, regard being had to any
money laid out or to be laid out on the
premises leased and generally to the
circumstances of the case, and the provisions of Sections five, six, and seven
of the Leases for Schools (Ireland) Act,
1881, shall apply to every such lease.
THE SENATE
School Sites Bill

Finance Bill
Finance Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill. (Viscount Massereene and
rard.)
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Amendment is on account
of certain technical legal difficulties in
the way of limited owners making the
leases of lands for schools and playgrounds. If limited owners want to
make a long lease, 99 years, for the purpose of the erection of a school, it is
necessary, unless there is special power
in the settlement, to apply to the Court
of Chancery. Further, if the lease is
wanted, not for building purposes, but
for a playground, it cannot be done under
the existing law unless there is special
power in the settlement. Consequently
this Amendment is necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to ask
whether the word "rents" in the plural
is correct. It struck me as a possible
misprint for rent."
((
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I will make inquiries.
Mr. LESLIE: I presume the House
will pass the Amendment in the form in
which it came from the Commons.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 2 (Amendment of 31 Geo.
3, c. 46 (Irish) ).
Commons' Amendment:-
Clause 2, page 2, line 13, at end insert
"any such sale, letting or transfer, may
include such easement, quasi-easement,
or right, in or over any other lands retained by the said governors or guardians,
and may be made subject to such restrictions, exceptions or reservations as may
be agreed upon."
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Amendment is on account
of a difficulty that was perceived in the
proposed arrangement for taking over a
portion of the grounds of the Armagh
Observatory for the projected new school.
It is intended that portion of the present
avenue leading to the Observatory should
he used by the school staff and children
as a means of entry to the school premises, and this Amendment ensures that
the trustees of the Observatory, when
letting or transferring the school site,
will also give the necessary facilities for
entry to it. As a matter of fact the school
children desired to use a portion of the
main avenue, and this Amendment enables them to do so.
Amendment agreed to.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

CLAUSE 3 (Arrangements for issue

Mr. LESLIE: It seems rather unnecessary to say that one welcomes the
changes in the matter of dealing with
game certificates, and it leads one to hope
that there may be some return given to
a person who has taken out a licence to
kill game for years past, and who is well
aware that a large number of people are
shooting game without paying their
duties. One hopes that putting the
matter into the hands of the police will
result in some benefit being given to the
game preserver in exchange for his certificate. On this Clause I might point out
that it is slightly unfair to the people of
Northern Ireland that, although they
take out their £3 licence to kill game or
their 10s licence to shoot other birds, they
still have to take out a certificate for a
shot-gun licence, which they have not to
do in Great Britain. I am sure the noble
Viscount will draw the attention of the
Minister of Finance to that incongruity
and perhaps some change may be made.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
