PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Egan's Divorce Bill
Resolved: That this Bill be now read
a Second time.
moment legislation on the lines of the
Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1928,
recently passed by the Imperial Parliament. As to when this Bill will be introduced I am afraid I cannot say, but I
can give the hon. Senator the assurance
that it will in any event be introduced as
one of the first Measures of the forthcoming Spring Session.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read a Second time
McMullan's Divorce Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
They have passed the Marketing of
Potatoes Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
CRIMINAL LAW AMENDMENT
АСТ.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether it is the intention of the Governmen during the present Session to introduce a Bill to amend, on the lines of the
Act passed by the Imperial Parliament
in August last, the law in Northern Ireland relating to the time limit for prosecuting certain offences against females;
and, if not, whether facilities would be
given to such a Bill if introduced by a
Private Member.
Mr. LESLIE: In asking the unstarred
question which stands in my name I
think it is only necessary to explain that
the Imperial Act to which I refer will
come into operation on 1st January next
year, and that the time limit to which it
relates is in respect of the offence of unlawful carnal knowledge of a girl under
sixteen. The period for prosecuting such
an offence at the moment in Great Britain
and Northern Ireland is nine months.
After 1st January next year it will be
twelve months in Great Britain. I need
not, I think, say more than that.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the question of the
hon. Senator, I should like to say that
this question has been receiving the attention of the Ministry of Home Affairs
for some time past. It has been decided
to introduce at the earliest possible
McMullan's Divorce Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.-(Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-

enactments given in the Schedule, and
there is no change from those which were
continued in the corresponding Bill of last
year. I beg to move.

quoted in the Schedule, and I rise only

(4) Where a Bill, or a part or parts
or clause or clauses of a Bill, is or are
withdrawn, no further expense shall be
incurred by the governing body or
mayor or chairman or about the promotion of the Bill, or part or parts or
clause or clauses, so withdrawn; but,
subject as aforesaid, all costs, charges
and expenses incurred by the governing body or mayor or chairman in, or
as incidental to, the preparation and
promotion of the Bill up to and inclusive of its deposit in Parliament and
withdrawal (when withdrawn), and in,
or as incidental to, the holding of a
meeting or the taking of a poll under
the principal Act as amended by this
Act, shall, when taxed by the taxing
officer of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland and allowed under the principal Act, be charged on and payable
out of such one or more of the public
funds or rates under the control of the
governing body (and, if more than one,
then in such proportions) as the governing body, having regard to the nature
and objects of the Bill, may determine
to be just and proper.

Sir WILLIAM COATES: I have a
number of Amendments on the Order
Paper to-day in reference to this Bill.
The first refers to the Bill itself, and the
others refer to the Schedule to the Bill.
The Amendments are largely matters of
detail which the experience of the Belfast
Corporation has shown to be necessary in
view of the antiquated working of the
old Act of 1888. I may say that the
Bill as a whole is warmly welcomed and
approved by the Corporation of Belfast
and the new system of putting a proposed
Bill to the electors is very desirable. The
first Amendment which I have to propose is one in connection with the text
of the Bill. It is in Clause 1, page 2,
line 25, to leave out from "when" to the
end of sub-section (4), and insert:
"allowed under the principal Act, be
chargeable upon the funds or rates upon
which the expenses of promoting such
Bills are made chargeable under the
principal Act."
This Amendment proposes to do away
with the necessity of having the expenses
of an abortive Bill taxed by the taxing
officer of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland. I would point out that such
a provision is not necessary and is not in
conformity with the old Borough Funds
Act of 1888, under which the sole authority appointed to tax the expenses of the
Bill was the auditor of the Local Government Board. All authorities who will be
affected by the Bill are subject to this
audit, and as a matter of fact it is the
custom in connection with Bills promoted
by the Belfast Corporation not to provide for the taxation of the costs of the
Bill by the taxing officer of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, but to allow
the matter to be dealt with in the ordinary way by the public auditor. With
move the
this explanation I beg to
Amendment which stands in my name.
Mr. LESLIE: Before we conclude the
discussion on that Amendment I should
Borough Funds Bill-
Report Stage.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
like to ask whether authorities other than
the Belfast Corporation have been consulted in this matter. The Bill will
apply not solely to the Belfast Corporation but to other public bodies throughout the Six Counties, and one would like
to be assured that this and the various
other Amendments have received their
approval. I welcome the presentation
to this House of a series of Amendments
by any hon. Member who has gone into a
particular Bill, but the time we have had
for considering these Amendments has been
very short indeed. I think none of us
saw them until we arrived in the House
this afternoon, and it does seem necessary that we should lay stress on the
point that other bodies besides the Belfast Corporation are entitled to be heard
on them.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I would
like to answer Senator Leslie by saying
that of course every authority in Northern Ireland has its accounts audited,
and from that point of view the procedure here is rather different to the procedure in England. There they have no
public auditor as we have here, and the
question of the costs of a Bill would in
Northern Ireland be entirely subject to
the public auditor's purview.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I understand that no other
authorities have been consulted with
regard to the costs of a local Bill being
taxed, but I have no reason to anticipate
that they would not welcome the Amendment. Therefore the Government do not
propose to offer any objection to it.
Amendment agreed to.
SCHEDULE, Part I. (Regulations for
Meeting of Electors.)
*
*
*
*
*
*
(d) that copies of the Bill may be
inspected and purchased at a place
within the district specified in the
notice, between the hours of ten in the
forenoon and five in the afternoon, on
every week day for twenty-one days
after the date of the first advertisment,
and that extracts may be taken free of
charge; and
*
*
*
*
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move in the Schedule, Part I, page 4,
Regulation 1 (d) to leave out "five" (five
in the afternoon) and insert "half-past
four." It is desired to limit the time
for the purchase and inspection of a Bill.
Instead of it being, as in the Bill at present, from ten to five on weekdays and
on Saturdays, we desire to limit it to from
ten to four-thirty on weekdays and from
ten to 12-30 on Saturdays. It is a small
matter, but I trust the Government will
see their way to agree to it.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared to
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: There is a
further Amendment in the same paragraph (d), after the word "day" (every
weekday) to insert "except Saturday, and
between the hours of ten in the forenoon
and half-past twelve in the afternoon on
every Saturday." I beg to move.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government have no objection to this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
9. A requisition for a poll by electors
must be in writing signed by the persons
making it, and must be delivered to the
mayor or chairman within seven days
after the date of the meeting or any
adjournment thereof.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment I have to move is in Regulation 9. It is to leave out the words "in
writing," and insert "in the form set out
in Part III of this Schedule, and be." It
is proposed in the Bill that a requisition
for a poll must be in writing, but it is
desired to have notice of a poll given in
the form set out in Part III of the Schedule. The reason for this is that it is
considered necessary for the purpose of
checking signatures with the register of
electors that the necessary information
should be given by those who demand a
poll. I think the Amendment is very
desirable, and I trust it will be accepted.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not like to keep
rising to bring up what is the same point,
but I do think this is a matter for which
there should be some little time given for
consideration. To present on the Report
stage a very important proposal of this
kind requiring that notice shall be given
Borough Funds Bill-
Report Stage.
25 OCTOBER 1928
in the form set out in the Schedule when
both the notice itself and the form appear
for the first time is really, I think, somewhat unfair to the House. There would
have been an opportunity of bringing
these matters forward both on the Second
Reading and on the Committee stage, and
those opportunities were not availed of. I
do not think the House ought to be asked
to accept this proposal this afternoon.
The Amendment might perhaps be moved.
again after the Third Reading.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: So far as the Government are
concerned they are prepared to accept this
Amendment, but the hon. Member who
has just spoken desires some further time
to consider the proposal. Therefore, I
suggest it should be brought up again on
the Third Reading.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I certainly feel very much inclined to support
the view expressed by Senator Leslie.
Other public bodies may have reason to
complain of these Amendments if they are
rushed through without any notice. They
may injuriously affect other bodies although they are satisfactory to the Belfast
Corporation, and the statement may be
made that this Parliament is only out to
satisfy one particular body and is unconcerned about others.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I am quite
agreeable to the suggestion of the noble
Viscount that this Amendment should be
left over for the Third Reading. I agree
that the time for its consideration has been
short. As a matter of fact I was only put
in possession of these facts and figures
yesterday, and if it is the desire of the
House that this and other Amendments
should be left over to the Third Reading
I will agree to that course.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Part II (Regulations for taking a poll.)
4. The presiding officer shall, within
three days after the expiration of the
time within which a poll may be required under Part I of this Schedule,
but not less than seven days before the
day appointed for the collection of the
voting papers, publish a notice signed
by him and specifying the date and
place of the said meeting, the resolution or resolutions proposed thereat,
the facts that a poll was required and
will be taken upon any such resolution,
the day or days on which the voting
papers will be delivered, and the day
on which they will be collected, and
the place for the examination and
casting up of the votes, and shall also
cause copies of such notice to be affixed
on the usual place or places for affixing
notices in the district.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move, in Part II, Regulation 4, after
"the" (before the day) to insert "first."
In this instance the point is that three
days are given for the distribution of
voting papers in connection with any poll
it may be necessary to take on a Bill promoted by any local authority. On the
other hand, the papers have to be collected on one day. In the first place that
is almost a physical impossibility, and in
the second place in the case of houses
that are closed the papers are not collected at all in many cases. It is therefore suggested that it would be well to
allow three days for collection as well as
three days for distribution, and that is
the object of this Amendment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared
to accept this Amendment, and I do not
think any local authority could possibly
object to it.
Amendment agreed to.
""
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment I have to move is in line 6
of Regulation 4. It is to leave out the
words "proposed thereat" and to insert
"with respect to which a poll has been
required. Under the new arrangements
some parts of a Bill might be voted on
favourably and some might be defeated.
It is not desired therefore to put every
one to the electors, but only those in respect of which a poll has been required.
I think that will make the Amendment
clear, and I beg to move.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared to
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

""
""
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move, in line 8 of Regulation 4, after
the word day" to insert or days.'
That is consequential on a previous
Amendment.

(5). (b) The presiding officer shall
cause voting papers in the said form to
be prepared and shall insert therein
the resolution or resolutions upon
which a poll is to be taken, and the
date and place of the meeting at which
each such resolution was proposed, and
the day and time when the voting
papers are to be collected.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment I beg to move is in Regulation 5 (b) line 4, after the word “day,'
insert or days." This is an Amendment to the same effect as the previous
one.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government will also agree
to that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment standing in my name is, at
the end of Regulation 5 (b) to insert
"The presiding officer shall also insert
on each voting paper the name and number on the register and polling district
letter of the elector, and the address at
which the voting paper of such elector is
to be delivered."
This Amendment deals with a case
where there are four voters in one house.
When four voting papers are left at that
house it is almost impossible for the occupiers to say which paper belongs to
each person. Consequently it is thought
desirable that the name of the elector,
his number, his polling district letter,
and his address should form part of the
voting paper. I think this is a very wise
thing, and I hope it will be accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: I would ask that this
proposal also should stand over for Third
Reading. It necessitates reference to the
principal Act.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: So far as I
am concerned I am agreeable that it
should stand over.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
6. The presiding officer, three days
at least before the day of collection of
the voting papers, shall cause one such
voting paper to be delivered by persons
appointed by him for that purpose at
the address stated in the Register of
Electors for the time being of each
elector.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment is in Regulation 6, on page 7.
I beg to move, after the second "the"
(the day of) to insert "first." It is a
consequential Amendment as a result of
the decision of the House to give three
days for the collection of the papers.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This is a consequential Amendment and is acceptable to the Government.
Amendment agreed to.
9. The presiding officer shall cause
the voting papers to be collected on the
day of collection by such persons as he
may appoint.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move, in Regulation 9, on page 7, after
the word "day" (on the day) insert "or
days." This is another consequential
Amendment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I will agree to that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
10. No voting paper shall be received
or admitted unless the same has been
delivered at the address of the voter as
aforesaid, nor unless the same is collected by the persons appointed for that
purpose:
Provided that--
(i) if any person entitled to receive
a voting paper has not received a voting paper as aforesaid, he shall, on personal application before the day of
collection to the presiding officer, be
entitled to receive a voting paper from
the presiding officer, and to fill up the
same in his presence and then and
there to deliver the same to him:
(ii) if any voting paper duly de-

II. (a) The presiding officer shall,
on the day immediately following the
day of the collection of the voting
papers, and on as many days immediately succeeding as may be necessary,
attend at the place appointed for the
examination and casting up of the
votes, and ascertain the validity of the
votes by an examination of the Register of Electors for the time being, and
by examining such persons as he may
see fit. He shall cast up such of the
votes as he finds to be valid and to have
been duly given, collected or received,
and shall ascertain the number of such
votes respectively in favour of and
against the adoption of a resolution.
*
*
*
*
*
(c) Any three of the electors who have
required a poll may themselves attend
or appoint an agent to attend the examination and casting up of the votes
given thereat; and any person so attending who obstructs or in any way
interferes with the examination and
casting up of the votes may, by order
of the presiding officer, be forthwith
removed from the place appointed for
that purpose, and if so removed shall
not be permitted to return.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: In Regulation 11 (a) on Page 7, I beg to move,
after the first "the" (following the day)
to insert "last." As there are now
three days given for the collection of the
voting papers, I think it is necessary to
put in the word "last."
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Amendment is accepted.
There is a misprint on the Order Paper.
The word "day" should read "last.'
Sir WILLIAM COATES: My next
Amendment is in Regulation 11 (c), after
the word "poll" (required a poll) to insert "and any three persons appointed
by the governing body." At the present.
moment it is not quite clear in the Bill
whether the governing body would have
any power to appoint anyone to attend
the examination of the votes, and it is
desired they should have that power.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Governemnt are prepared to
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

THE SENATE
*

14. shall be liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding
twenty pounds, or, in the discretion of
the court, to imprisonment for any
period not exceeding three months.
An attempt to commit any offence
specified in this regulation shall be
punishable in the manner in which the
offence is punishable.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Page 8, Regulation 14, after "twenty
pounds or" insert "in default of payment of such penalty, or, alternatively."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment relates to a matter to which I referred when
the Bill was in Committee. I pointed
out then that it was possible that
although Parliament was providing a
maximum period of imprisonment for
a possible offence yet it was possible
for a man to be sentenced or to
have to serve a longer term of imprisonment because he could not pay the
fine which is provided by Parliament as an
alternative, and presumably a preferable
alternative, to imprisonment.
I suggested that in order to bring the two
points into line we should raise the period
of imprisonment from three to four
months, but that suggestion did not find
favour, and I must say I am glad it did
not. Another alternative would be to reduce the amount of the fine so as to
make that correspond, but I think a
better way still is to use the words I have
on the Paper, or some other words if a
better form of words can be found. I
think that if time were given to consult
the older Statutes a better form of words
might be found.
I suggest that imprisonment in default.
of payment should be limited by Statute
to three months. It is, as was pointed
out to me, quite possible under the Petty
Sessions Act for magistrates who issue
warrants of distress to limit the period of
imprisonment to some period less than the
maximum, but the fact remains that it is
possible for a man to serve more imprisonment because he cannot pay a fine than he
would have to serve if he had been
sentenced to imprisonment in the first instance. That cannot possibly be the intention of Parliament, nor do I think it is
in itself desirable. Accordingly I have put
down this form of words, but if a more
satisfactory form of words can be suggested I shall be only too happy to agree
to it.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The object of this Amendment is,
as the hon. Senator has said, similar to
the Amendment he proposed on the Committee stage of this Bill, increasing the
period of imprisonment frem three to
four months. That is to say, the hon.
Member wishes to secure uniformity be
tween the penalty for default in payment of a fine of £20 and the penalty of
imprisonment without the option of a
fine. The provision for penalties contained in the Bill follows that contained
in the Borough Funds (Ireland) Act,
1888. This provision has therefore been
in force for forty years and there is
nothing to show that any difficulty has
ever resulted from it. In fact, I may
say that no difficulty can possibly arise if
it is administered with common sense, as
the period of imprisonment under the
Petty Sessions Act for default of payment of a fine of £20 is a term not exceeding four months. In other words,
the term is not a fixed term and allows
discretion as to the length of imprisonment up to four months. No hard and
fast rule has been followed in legislation
so as to make the period of imprisonment
the same as might be imposed in default
of payment of a fine. For example, the
penalty for a third offence under Section
1 of the Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act, 1926, is a fine not exceed
ing £50 or imprisonment not exceeding
three months. Under these circumstances the Government do not consider
the Amendment necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I could have wished the
noble Viscount had told us how many
prosecutions there had been under the Act
of 1888. I do not think there have been
any at all. It is an academic subject,
and it seemed to me that an academic
Bill provided a very good opportunity
to deal with the point. It must receive
attention when we consider our Summary Jurisdiction Act. However, I do
not think it is worth while putting the
House to a division, and I will withdraw
the Amendment.

o'clock until Tuesday next.

TELEPHONE FACILITIES IN
CRUMLIN DISTRICT.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the rame of Colonel
Pakenham: :-
To ask the Leader of the House
would
whether representations
be
made to the Post Office Authorities to
afford additional facilities to telephone
subscribers in the Crumlin district, as
call
the average time for getting a
through to Belfast and other places is
half-an-hour to one hour.
Colonel PAKENHAM: In rising to
ask the noble Viscount the question which
stands in my name on the Order Paper I
would like only to say that the complaints of delay on this subject are very
general and that any alleviation of these
corditions will be very much appreciated
by the subscribers to the service.
encom-
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): My right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Commerce, has made
quiries and learns that additional telephone circuits have recently been provided between Crumlin and Antrim and
between Antrim and Belfast, the installation of the latter having been
pleted on 2nd August last. The postal
authorities state that a total of 238 trunk
calls were made from Crumlin during the
period 1st October to 24th October, both
dates inclusive, and that the average
delay in the case of all those calls was
5 minutes, 15 seconds. Only in the case
of 14 calls did the delay exceed 15
minutes, the maximum delay being 45
minutes, which was occasioned in one case
only. Of the 34 trunk calls made by the
hon. and gallant Member during the
same period 27 were treated on demand,
that is to say, connection was established
within an interval of one or two minutes.
The average delay in respect of the entire 34 calls was 4 minutes, 42 seconds,
the maximum delay having been 32
minutes.
The post office authorities state that
these figures represent a very marked imthe conditions which
provement in
existed prior to the installation of the
two additional circuits and that, while
the delay which occurred on a few occasions during the period to which I have
referred appears to warrant investigation, the average delay in the case of the
remaining calls does not appear to be excessive. In this connection it may be well
to remind the hon. and gallant Member
that the demand made on the circuits in
question during the recent visit of Her
Royal Highness Princess Mary was exceptionally heavy and that the average
delay would be necessarily greater during
a time of such pressure than in normal
circumstances. The postal authorities.
have undertaken to investigate the instances in which the delay appears to
have been excessive and to do all that is
possible to bring the service to the highest.
degree of efficiency.
Marketing of Potatoes Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Marketing of Potatoes Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
deals with the marketing of the most important item of our agricultural produce.
The potato crop not only forms the basis
for an exceedingly valuable export trade
but is also the keystone of the system of
crop rotation in the North of Ireland.
While the quality of our potatoes has
always been such as would recommend
them in any market it has, nevertheless,
been a fact in recent years that the
methods of placing them on the export
market have been such as seriously to
jeopardise our export trade.
With the permission of the House, I
will read an extract from the report of
the Agricultural Produce Committee,
which fully investigated this question.
That report says:
"We received conclusive evidence that
Northern Ireland at present stands in
the gravest danger of losing its chief
outlet for its important potato industry.
In many of the large British consuming
areas, buyers are evincing the strongest
disinclination to take Northern Ireland
potatoes. These buyers complain, and
the justice of their complaint is beyond
question, of inferior selection and dressing; of the mixing of varieties of dissimilar keeping, cooking and table
qualities; of the incorrect description of
consignments; of waste through the use
of improper bags; of shortage of weight;
and for other undesirable features. Unfortunately, these shortcomings on the
part of our own consignments are
accentuated by the condition in which
certain of our foreign competitors place
their inspected potatoes on the British
market. In foreign markets with which
Northern Ireland formerly conducted
an important export trade in seed potatoes, the same unsatisfactory position is
disclosed, and certain of those markets
are now being increasingly supplied
from other sources with seed of guaranteed purity and quality.
"We are emphatically of the opinion.
that the taking of legislative action to
restore confidence in our potato exports
is a matter of urgency."
I should say further that all progressive
agricultural opinion in Northern Ireland
is now firmly convinced of the necessity for
official inspection of all potatoes offered
for export. My right hon. Friend the
Minister of Agriculture has, for several
years, been convinced on this point and
has wished to take the necessary legislative steps to secure the better grading,
packing, etc., of Northern Ireland potatoes. Difficulties, however, arising out
of the wording of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, prevented the problem
being dealt with in an adequate manner.
The doubts thrown by that Act on the
powers of the Government of Northern
Ireland to legislate in certain trade matters have now been removed by the Northern Ireland (Miscellaneous Provisions)
Act recently passed by the Imperial Parliament and it has at last been found praċticable to introduce the Bill which is now
before the House.
I do not propose to enter into all the
details of the Bill. It will, I hope, be suffi
cient at present to summarise its general
provisions. These are briefly, firstly, all
merchants exporting potatoes to Great
Britain must be licensed, and in the first
instance every person applying for a
licence will obtain one-its continuance
depending on compliance with the Regu
lations. The fee of £1 will be charged
for each licence. Secondly, potatoes exported to Great Britain must, with certain
minor exceptions, be accompanied by a
certificate of an inspector that they are of
the prescribed standard of quality and
have been graded and packed in a proper
manner. To help to cover the cost of administering the Act a fee of 6d. per ton
will be charged for all potatoes inspected.
Thirdly, it is also proposed to grant
permits for potatoes coming within certain categories, such as lots grown by
the exporters, lots sent for experimental
demonstration, or similar purposes, and
lots of less than, say, one ton. Licerces
will not be required in these cases but
consignments may be inspected.
Fourthly, where, for the purpose of
issuing a permit, inspection of a consignment is necessary, fees at the rate of 6d
per ton will be charged but for the permit
itself no fee will be charged. The Bill
provides that at present its provisions
shall not apply to potatoes sent to the
Irish Free State, but later on an arrangement can be made to have such exports
inspected if considered desirable. Lastly,
the intention is that the Act shall come
437 Marketing of Potatoes Bill- 30 OCTOBER 1928

Second Reading.
into force on the first day of February
next-the period intervening between
the passing of the Measure and its coming
into operation being devoted to the training of the necessary staff and the education of the farmer and the merchant in
the provisions of the Act. I hope that
these general principles commend themselves to the House. I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
am entirely in support of this Bill. I
notice there is no reference in it to Black
Scab, which is one of the most serious
curses of the potato trade in this Province and I hope the Black Scab Order
will not be affected by anything contained
in this Measure now before the House.
We all know what a success the Marketing
of Eggs Act has been, and I am sure the
same amount of success will attend this
Bill, which I am sure every agriculturist in Ulster will welcome.
It
cases
Mr. LONG: Like the last speaker, I
rise to support this Bill. It is a pity
that such a Bill should be necessary.
might reasonably be considered that it is
a reflection on the probity of the agricultural community, but I think everyone
who understands the circumstances will
acknowledge that a farmer will be met
with who either inadvertently or through
negligence, or perhaps in some
fraudulently, will put a few bags of bad
potatoes in a lot. There is no doubt that
even one bag of bad potatoes in a lot has
a detrimental effect on the trade generally. There has been a demand for the
Bill, I understand, from a number of
agricultural committees, from a number
of exporters, and from some farmers as
well. The only objection I can see to the
Bill is that it will create an additional
horde of inspectors, and the general feeling in the country is that we are overstaffed with officials; that we have far too
many officials for the size of our territory.
However, I very much fear that this cannot be avoided. Regarding the charge
of 6d per ton, in my view that will amount
to about £7,000 a year and I think a
very much less sum would be sufficient to
defray the cost of inspection. I would
suggest that if in the course of time the
Ministry of Agriculture find that a lesser
sum will suffice they might see their way
to reduce the fee or to amend the Bill oy
inserting the words "an amount not
exceeding 6d per ton."
As I have said, there is no doubt that
this Bill is required. I will give the
House one example to prove its necessity.
I purchased some seed potatoes in
Londonderry market from a man who
should have been doubly reliable inasmuch as he was not only a magistrate but
a county councillor. On taking them to
my place I found that on top of the bags
were some very nice seed indeed, ut twothirds of the contents of the bags was
rubbish. A good deal of the success or
otherwise of this Bill will depend on the
regulations that the Ministry of Agricul
ture make for carrying it out. If the
inspectors are competent men and if the
inspections are carried out at convenient
centres at reasonable times and in a
reasonable way I think it should be a
success. I had some experience of the
last inspection of potatoes. I was a witness to a lorry load of potatoes being sent
for inspection and they were rejected.
The exporter said, "Loose out that bay
horse and put the grey mare in the
lorry." The grey mare was immediately
put into the lorry and it was taken back
and passed all right.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Was this in Londonderry too?
Mr. LONG: Yes. The extract read
by the noble Leader of the House from
the report of the Agricultural Produce
Committee should convince every Member
of this House that it is the bounden duty
of everyone of us to see that the produce
that is exported from this country is as
good as it can possibly be made. If we
are to hold our markets we must market
our produce in the best possible condition. I therefore heartily support the
Measure now before the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise only to ask the
noble Viscount if he can give us any idea
of the number of potato exporters who
are likely to be affected by the Bill. The
number of potato suppliers, farmers, is
very great, but I imagine it is not the
farmer who will be directly affected by
the provisions of the Bill but the dealer,
and more particularly the exporter, and
I think it would be of some assistance to
us if the noble Viscount could give us
some information on that head. As regards the Bill itself, I have no doubt
there is some demand for it. It is
only to-day attaining its twenty-first
birthday, reckoning by days and not
4:9

Expiring Laws Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie].
years, and I would like to be assured that
there is a solid public demand for the
Bill and particularly a demand from the
class to whom I have referred.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Like
the other hon. Senators who have spoken
I met a very
I gladly support this Bill.
large buyer of potatoes in England and he
pointed out to me the disadvantages that
are associated with our potatoes that are
exported to England. He said they were
of all sizes and of all species, and that
they could not wash them by machinery
half as efficiently as they would be able
to do if they were graded. He pointed out
that foreign growers graded their potatoes and that when they were put into a
machine they could be washed and peeled
properly. Another thing he mentioned
was this: that he was of Irish descent and
had a very kindly feeling towards Ireland.
He said, "I dearly love Ireland, but I do
not want to buy a shovelful of earth,
even though it is Irish earth, especially
when it weighs almost as much as the potatoes." I think this Bill will help to remove such objections to Northern Ireland
potatoes as those. Senator Long referred
to the charge of 6d. per ton for inspection.
I see the Bill provides that the fee is not
to exceed 6d per ton, so that if it is not
necessary to charge 6d. the Ministry need
not impose the full fee. I welcome this
Bill and I think it will prove to be a most
useful Measure.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am very
glad that this Bill has met with such a
favourable reception. I will give the information asked for by Senator Leslie at
a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday next.

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
beg to move, in the Schedule, Part 1, page
4, to leave out paragraph 7. On the
Order Paper the paragraph I am moving
to leave out appears as on page 5, but it
is on page 4. It may be within the recollection of some hon. Members that this
time twelve months ago I moved a similar
Amendment to the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. At that time I had an interview at the Ministry of Home Affairs
and I believe I received an assurance that
if I did not pursue the Amendment this
particular Act would be withdrawn from
the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill this
year. However, I see it is again included
in the Bill. I do not think the officials
of the Ministry of Home Affairs understand the class of dwelling that I want to
have excluded from this Bill. They cannot be described as houses. They are
cabins or huts and they do not come under
the Rent Restrictions Acts. They were
built at a time when there was a great
scarcity of houses at the end of the war
and many of them are not fit to put even
beasts into. These miserable places
sprang up all over the country, and
especially in my own district. They were
subject to no laws or regulations made by
the urban or rural district council and
there was no question of rent. Sites were
let by farmers in their fields and on them
these shelters were erected. I myself sold
a place to a farmer for £3,000, and in the
first year he made £3,000 by letting the
field for this purpose and his income ever
since has been £2,000 or £3,000 a year.
These huts or cabins or shelters were built
30 OCTOBER 1928
Expiring Laws Bill--

Committee.
a
and they use them for occupation during
the summer ard at week-ends. It would
be a very bad thing if we hastily interfered with these dwellings. They may be
small and some of them may be in
dilapidated state but there are quite a
number that are very substantial and
beautiful burgalows and I would be very
sorry to take any hasty action that would
interfere with them. I could not vote for
the Amendment without knowing exactly
what effect it would have on those huts
that have been erected by the workers of
Belfast and other towns.
without any sanitation, without any water
supply, and without any roads, and they
are not fit for a human being to live in.
The necessity for the Act under which
these huts were allowed to be built has
now disappeared and the time has come
when such a nuisance should be put an end
to. These places are dens of insanitation
and I have read that in many instances
they are dens of immorality. It would be
no hardship on anyone to bring them
under the laws which govern the building
of other houses so that the local authorities
of Bangor and Newtownards and other
to decide
towns should have the right
whether these places are fit to live in or
not. A good many of them are tumbling
to pieces, but in the case of others, if water
was laid on and proper sanitary arrangements made they might be allowed to remain. As it is they are the greatest
curse we have in this part of the country
at the present time. Bangor and other
towns are most anxious to get control of
them and it is with this object that I
have put down this Amenment.
Mr. LONG: I quite agree with the
hon, and gallant Gentleman who has just
spoken regarding the nuisance of these
buildings, but I understood it was competent for a rural district council within
whose area these buildings were erected to
adopt building by-laws, a model copy of
which is to be had from the Ministry of
Home Affairs. In the event of their
adopting these by-laws they have full
control over every building erected in their
district. If that is so, and I understand it
is, it might meet the views of the right
hon. and gallant Gentleman.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
dare say Senator Long is correct in his
statement, but I am not referring to houses
that are being built at the present time.
I am only dealing with huts that were
erected under this special Act of Parliament and which are not now fit to exist.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
rather afraid this is a very far-reaching
proposal if it applies to huts that have
been erected during the last few years.
They may not be in a satisfactory sanitary condition and for all I know immorality may take place in some of them,
but that does not apply to all. There
are a very large number of workers in
Belfast who have built huts in these
country districts and close to the shore,
Mr. GLENDINNING: Would it not
answer the right hon. and gallant gentleman's purpose
if these huts, instead of
being removed by legislation, were put
under the proper authority and rated and
made fit for human beings to live in.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
That is what the Amendment means.
Viscourt MASSEREENE AND FER-

Borough Funds Bill (S).

Moved: That this Bill be now read the

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, That this
Bill be re-committed generally. I think
the circumstances leading up to this proposal are known to most of the hon. Senators here to-day but some of those who
are now present were not here last Thursday. On last Thursday we took the Report stage of the Bill and when we entered
the House we saw for the first time a page
and a half of Amendments all to be moved
on Report. The Amendments, speaking
generally, dealt with matters which had
not been raised either on the Second Reading or in Committee. They were in fact
entirely new matters and I believe I am
correct in saying it is not in accordance
with Parliamentary practice to introduce
on the Report stage something which has
not been before the House or the Committee at an earlier stage.
I do not know whether you, Mr.
Speaker, will think it in order for me to
go through the various matters in the Bill
to which I should wish to draw attention if the House agrees with me in my
Motion for re-committal. It might perhaps save time if your Lordship allowed
me do so, because I imagine it will be
accepted that on a re-committal one should
not bring up any matter other than a matter which arises directly or indirectly from
what happend at the last stage-in this
case the Report stage. I should therefore
if this Bill is re-committed limit myself
strictly indeed I have no desire to go
outside it-to matters which arose on the
Report stage.
The matters to which I wish to direct
attention are first of all, the alteration in
sub-clause (4) of the first Clause. The
effect of that alteration was to remove the
Parliamentary taxing officer. I do not
know whether any Member appreciated
the purport of the Amendment moved on
Report. I must confess that at the
moment I did not; I had not the preceding
legislation sufficiently in my mind. But
it will be remembered that when the
noble Viscount introduced the Bill to us
and when the Bill was received with something resembling acclamation from all
quarters of the House, one of the points
on which the noble Viscount laid stress was
the fact that we were assimilating ourselves to the English Act of 1903. The
English Act of 1903 introduced the Parliamentary taxing officer. Before that,
under the English Act of 1872 there was
no provision as regards a Parliamentary
taxing officer. It seems to me that
Borough Funds Bill.
Poor Relief Bill.
30 OCTOBER 1928
before Parliament consents altogether
to abandon control of its Parliamentary
taxing officer Parliament ought to have
very clear notice of the proposal and ample
time to make up its mind whether or not
it desires to accept that provision. For
myself, I prefer the wording of the
original Act both on account of the Parliamentary officer and also on account of
the wording of the particular enactment.
It
The original Bill took us straight back to
what I will call the fountain head.
took us straight back to Section (3) of
our Act of 1888. That is to say, it prescribes that a local authority may spread
a charge which is to be incurred over such
funds under its control as seem to be desirable and in such proportions as seem
right. All that was, as I thought, most
suitably reproduced in the original Bill.
The Amendment takes us back, not
directly to the fountain head, but indirectly by the somewhat circuitous route
provided under Section 7-the Section
which we are in effect repealing by substitution.
an
Passing from that to the Schedule itself, I think that perhaps the House will

somewhat hasty in consenting to
alteration in the hours during which a
I think it

may be thought that it is a little unfair
that a working class elector, whether in
Belfast or in any urban district in the
Six Counties-for the House will remember that this Bill applies to every urban
district and every town under the orders
of town commissioners or other sanitary
authority-I think it will be thought that
it is a little hard on the working-class
elector who has the privilege of making a
requisition against a proposal of his
governing body to require him to buy at
copy of the Bill before 12-30 on Saturday
which is the one day he may have free
time to get a copy of the Bill. On weekdays the office is apparently to be closed
at half-past four. The English proposal
is five o'clock throughout the week. The
next Amendment that would normally
have been moved on Third Reading is one
of the most important and one which I
support. Passing over a small matter
of a re-arrangement which seems necessary we come to the question of a deputy
appointed by the presiding officer.

Mr. LESLIE: I can only say that I

about the deputy to be appointed by the

Poor Relief Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: In this Bill which is
before the House it is proposed
to amend the existing legislation in
regard to poor relief for exceptional
distress. Prior to the Local Government
(Ireland) Act of 1898 outdoor relief
could only be granted to destitute persons.
who were disabled by reason of old age
or some defect of mind or body, or to
destitute widows with two or more
children; and no person in occupation of
more than one-quarter of an acre of land
was eligible to receive such relief.
Section 13 of the 1898 Act extended
outdoor relief in cases of exceptional distress. The Local Government Board
were enabled under its provisions, if they
thought fit, to authorize the Guardians
by an Order to grant relief for a period
not exceeding two months where the
latter had satisfied the county council
that exceptional distress existed. The
financial arrangements were that one-half
of any expenditure so incurred should be
levied off the county at large, the other
4 8
Poor Relief Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
half being borne by the union concerned.
It will be easily understood that this provision in the 1898 Act was a distinct
It
advance on previous legislation.
recognized for the first time that under
exceptional industrial conditions it would
be proper to make some temporary provision to relieve a hard-working man who
was quite able to take up employment but
who, through no fault of his own, found
himself in peril of destitution.
In recent years, as hon. Members of
course know, there has been a serious situation here in Northern Ireland. In 1923
the Belfast Board of Guardians did in fact
apply to the Ministry of Home Affairs
and secured an order under Section 13 of
the 1898 Act authorising them to grant
outdoor relief under that section in view
of the prevailing exceptional distress.
It will be realised, however, that the
existing procedure in regard to the application for such an order has very definite
drawbacks. In the first place, it is necessary for the Guardians to apply through
the county or county borough council.
The object of this provision was, of course,
to ensure that the rating authorities were
consulted and that they approved. If
this necessary approval is given by the
rating authority and if an Order is made,
one-half of the cost of the relief has to
be borne by the union concerned and the
other half is a general charge on the
county or county borough. Then again,
the period during which the Order is in
force is limited to two months as a maximum. This means that at the end of
that time, if relief is to be continued, a
fresh Order has to be applied for, and
has again to go through the
channeis.
same
In the Bill which is before the House
it is proposed to simplify the procedure.
The Ministry of Home Affairs is to take
the initiative where it is satisfied that exceptional distress prevails. In arriving at
a decision it will be guided by representations which it may receive from the
Boards of Guardians or from any other
reliable source, such as it own inspectors
or the Ministry of Labour. The Order,
if made, will be for such period as may
be prescribed, and, accordingly, the old
limitation of the two months' maximum
will disappear.
It is proposed in this Bill now to repeal
the provision in the Poor Relief Act of
1862, so far as exceptional distress is concerned, which excludes an occupier of
more than one-quarter of an acre of land
from outdoor relief. A similar repealing
clause was contained in Section 13 of the
1898 Act. The financial arrangements
now proposed are that the expense will be
borne by the union or division concerned;
provided, however, that the county or
county borough council may, if they so
desire, raise one-half of the amount as a
county-at-large charge.
It is the considered opinion of the
Government that this amending Bill is
very necessary under the conditions which
obtain at present and it is hoped that the
existing difficulties which have arisen in
administration of this exceptional relief
will now as far as possible be removed. I
beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that everybody who at any time has served as a
poor-law guardian in a country district
will welcome this Bill, and particularly
of course the Sub-clause which repeals the
exclusion of the occupier of more than a
quarter of an acre of land. Almost all
of us who have filled the office I have
mentioned must have met time and again
very sad cases where relief ought to have
been given but where it could not be on
account of this provision. I cordially
support the Bill, but I should like to be
assured that it is intended to come into
immediate operation.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
wish to say that I certainly welcome and
give my support to the Bill. I am very
glad indeed that the Government has
thought it advisable to introduce this
amending Bill. There has been a little
confusion and a little hesitancy on the
part of some boards of guardians to help
those who were in the unfortunate position of not being able to contribute properly to the support of their wives and
families. This I believe will remove any
difficulty. One has to admit at a time
like this when trade has been so very bad
for such a long period there is bound to
be a good number of even able-bodied men
who are unable to find work to obtain the
proper means of supporting their wives
and families. This will make it much
easier for the guardians to deal with
cases like that.
There are none of us who desire any
public board to grant an unemployed man
Rent Restrictions Bill. 450
Poor Relief Bill.
30 OCTOBER 1928
as much money as he would have if he
were working. Every wise person is opposed to a course of that description. It
would be an inducement to a man continuously to shirk work and remain unemployed. But if they are prepared only
to give what will keep a man, his wife,
and family from starvation I am sure it
will be a satisfaction to all concerned. I
am very glad to see the Government introduce this Measure, especially at the
present time, to tide these people over
the difficult position in which they find
themselves, especially those connected
with the linen trade. There are very
hard cases and this may help to induce the
guardians to give them some assistance.
Mr. BARCLAY: I also would like to
support the Second Reading of this Bill.
My reason for doing so is that as I move
about through the City of Belfast I find
there is a great amount of distress and
poverty at the present time, and I am
afraid as winter comes on it will not be
improved anything, but it will perhaps
grow worse. The Bill that we passed
through the House here a short time ago,
the Unemployment Insurance Bill, did
away with the extended benefit to those
who were unemployed persons, and a
number of such persons have come to the
end of their resources in this respect, and
to-day they have no source of revenue
whatever. I am very pleased to see that
through this Bill such people will be
helped in some little way.
But in reading the Bill through I could
not see that there was very much protection given to the ratepayers. If there is
I cannot see it. What struck me was this:
that we should have a specified time of
residence in Northern Ireland before any
person would be entitled to relief, because
there is a probability of some people
coming in if they thought we had lost our
heads and found our hearts. We do not
want to do that. We might have some
coming from the Free State, and we
might have some coming from England
and Scotland to get on to the rates here if
they thought it was possible to do so. I
think if there is no protection in the
original Bill to the ratepayer then this
question ought to be taken into consideration. I have great pleasure in supporting the Second Reading.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I would like
also to support the Second Reading of this
Bill, especially the Clause to which the
hon. Senator opposite (Mr. Leslie) has referred, the Clause which gives the
guardians power to give outdoor relief to
a person who has a quarter of an acre of
land or upwards. But I would like to
point out to the House that thirty years
ago when the right hon. Gentleman,
Colonel Sharman Crawford, was chairman
of Newtownards Board of Guardians, the
outdoor relief given there was £33 per
week, and to-day with old-age pensions,
unemployment benefit, widows' pensions,
and every other benefit that has been
granted to that class of people who are
always more or less in distress-the more
so the more they get, I think-the outdoor relief is still £33 a week. After all
these grants that have been given we still
seem to have to fall back on the ratepayers for their support. And while it
may be necessary to extend relief in time
of distress such as is in existence at
present I think there should be some encouragement for people to seek work at a
wage that would benefit trade generally
instead of looking for relief from every
possible source.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not think after the debate
we have listened to that I need say much.
Hon. Senator Barclay raised the question
of a specified time. I think that would
come into a Bill dealing with the poor-law
generally. But under this Bill the
guardians would have to use their discretion in these matters.
On question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday next.

This is a
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
very short Bill and I will not occupy the

period of control on houses of a rateable
value not exceeding £26 by three years,
namely, from 1st May, 1930, as is prescribed in the existing Act of 1925, to 1st
May, 1933.
When the Act of 1925 was passed it
was hoped that the acute shortage in this

the Government are not however satisfied
that this desirable state of affairs will prevail next year, and it is accordingly proposed to extend the period of control until
1933. It is satisfactory to know that at
the moment very considerable progress is
being made in building houses of the category mentioned in the Bill, the average
number of new subsidy houses of this
smaller class submitted for approval being
now between 400 and 500 a month.
I should like only to repeat that it

falsified in the meantime it will be for the

CLAUSE 1 (Establishment of Slaughtered Animals (Compensation) Trustees
for Northern Ireland.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: By leave
of the Committee I should like to make a
statement now about questions that were
left over from the last time. It will be
recalled that when the House adjourned
a promise was given on behalf of the
Government that a statement would be
made in regard to the demand made by
the North of Ireland Cattle Traders' Association that the scope of this Bill should
be widened so as to include losses due to
detention in British lairages.
It is satisfactory to note that the Bill
has had a generally favourable reception
and the only complaint is, apparently,
that it does not go far enough. At the
outset it seems desirable to remind the
House of the present position in regard to
the slaughter of animals in Ireland and
in Great Britain, so that the problem to
be dealt with may be clearly discerned.
At present when animals are slaughtered in Ireland by official order on
account of foot-and-mouth disease the
appropriate Irish Government is responsible for compensation. Similarly when
animals are slaughtered in Great Britain,
otherwise than in a landing place, the
British Government is liable. Prior to
the passing of the Importation of Animals
Act, 1922, by the Imperial Parliament
the British Government was liable, even
when animals were slaughtered in landing
places. By that Act, however, the
British Government relieved itself of this
liability and it is this landing place gap
This
that now remains to be bridged.
unsatisfactory position has been frequently
stressed by the cattle trade.
A number of animals have already been
slaughtered in British lairages and the
owners have received no compensation but
this Bill gives the power to pay, even in
these retrospective cases. This retrospective clause must not be lost sight of
when considering the proposal to extend
the scope of the Measure. Losses due to
slaughter are definitely known and can be
checked. Clearly, however, it would,
after a lapse of time, be difficult, if not
impossible to verify claims in respect of
vague losses due to detention. It is therefore. clear that even if the demand for an
extension of the Measure as proposed
were admitted it could not, for the reasons
stated, be made retrospective.
However, our fundamental objection to
widening the scope of the Bill is that such
a course must involve higher insurance
453 Slaughtered Animals Bill- 30 OCTOBER 1928

Committee.
fees at Northern Ireland ports than at
Free State ports. In this connection it
may be remarked that a Bill similar to
the present one and with corresponding
contributions has already been passed by
the Free State Parliament and will, it is
understood, be shortly put in force in
Free State ports.
The House will appreciate the anxiety
of the Government that nothing should be
done which might have the effect of prejudicing the trade of our ports and it is
at least arguable that higher insurance
contributions would have such an effect.
The Government is not, therefore, prepared, on the information at present before it, to sanction any scheme involving
higher contributions than those embodied
in this Bill. They suggest, however, that
since the Bill is otherwise acceptable it
should now be proceeded with and put
into operation. Experience of the actual
working of the Measure will, doubtless,
suggest directions in which it can be improved and if a reasonable case can be
made out to have risks other than
slaughter included without at the same
time jeopardizing trade through Northern
Ireland ports the question of extension can
be reconsidered at a future date.
On the Second Reading stage the question was asked as to whether the trustees
in borrowing would have behind them a
Government guarantee. The answer is in
the negative but a reference to Section 4,
Sub-section (2) (b) shows that repayments
of moneys borrowed are a direct charge on
the Compensation Fund. This is regarded as adequate security. Another
question was as to whether there would be
any right of appeal in regard to compensation. The reply is that the verdict of
the Committee of assessors appointed
under Clause 11 (6) is final.
The CHAIRMAN: I do not think I
can allow discussion on that now. It is
the privilege of the Leader of the House
to give an explanation.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to appointment and constitution of Trustees) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Qualification of Trustees)
(1) Every member of the Trustees
shall be a person who is ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland and is engaged
in the business of exporting live stock
from Northern Ireland.
(2) At least four members of the
Trustees shall be persons engaged in the
business of exporting cattle and sheep,
or either of them, from Northern Ireland, and at least one member of the
Trustees shall be a person engaged in
the business of exporting pigs from
Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: For the sake of uniformity with another Clause of the Bill I beg
to move in Sub-section (2), page 2, line 35
to leave out "and sheep, or either of
them" and to insert" or sheep."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.

arrangements as the said Ministry

I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move at the end of the Clause to add:

made the assets and liabilities of the
Compensation Fund shall, if the said
Ministry so direct, be transferred to,
and vest in, the said Ministry by virtue
of this Act, for the purposes for which
the said fund has been established."
The Clause as drafted makes no provision for the transfer of funds in the
event of the Ministry having to make arrangements for the administration of the
Measure through the failure of the association or the Trustees to function. The
object of the Amendment is to remedy
this.
Amendment agreed to.

(4) Where a Bill, or a part or parts
or clause or clauses of a Bill, is or are
withdrawn, no further expense shall be

mayor or chairman in or about the

*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 2, line 25, to leave out from the
word "when" (shall, when) to the end of
sub-section (4) and insert:-
-
"taxed by the taxing officer of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, and
allowed under the principal Act, be
charged on and payable out of such one
or more of the public funds or rates
under the control of the governing body,
(and, if more than one, then in such
proportions) as the governing body,
having regard to the nature and objects
of the Bill, may determine to be just
and proper."
The proposal on the Paper is in effect
to re-introduce the wording of the original
Bill. When the noble Viscount introduced
the Bill to us and asked the House to give
the Bill a Second Reading, which the
House did very promptly, the noble Viscount pointed out that one of the advantages claimed for the new Measure was
that it would assimilate the law in
Northern Ireland with respect to the particular matter with which the Bill dealt
to that in England, and he quoted to us
the Imperial Statute of 1903. The particular point to which this Amendment
relates which connects it with the Act of
1903 is of course the requirement that
certain expenses should be taxed by the
taxing officer of Parliament. As regards
that I think it is necessary to go back a
little into the history of the matter. The
first Boroughs Funds Bill was the English
Act of 1872, which did not apply to Ireland. In that Act provision was made
for the taxation of costs of abortive Bills,
and it is to be remembered, I think, that
these questions can only arise in connection
with an abortive Bill; that is a Bill promoted or sought to be promoted by some
governing body which is contrary to the
feelings of the constituents of that
governing body. The Bill is then objected
to by the electors and if a majority of them
are against the Bill the promotion of the
Bill does not go on and the question of the
taxation of costs then arises.
Borough Funds Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Under the 1872 Act it is provided that
the taxation should be by a person appointed by a Secretary of State. In 1888
a somewhat similar Measure was passed
for Ireland, and in that Measure the reference to the Secretary of State of course
did not appear.
Matters continued in
that way until 1903 when the Bill of
which we have already spoken was introduced, and I may remark when the
Bill was before the Imperial Parliament
Mr. T. M. Healy, who was then a Member of the House, inquired as to whether
it could be extended to Ireland.
seemed to favour its extension to Ireland.
The reply he received from the late Mr.
Wyndham was that he had not been in
close touch with the Bill, but he would
see what could be done. Nothing was
He
done, and it was left to the Parliament of
Northern Ireland in 1928 to act on the
suggestion made by Mr. Healy in 1903.
The object of introducing the parliamentary taxing officer seems to me to be that
the voters, being the wronged party and
the successful party-that is to say, they
have succeeded in their contention that
the governing body ought not to promote
the Bill, but they have necessarily ultimately to bear the cost, and I think it
must have been in the mind of Parliament
that in that unfortunate result it was
only right that some independent person
should be interposed as auditor. As it
is a parliamentary matter Parliament, I
thought the parliamentary
taxing officer would be a good substitute
for the person appointed by the Secretary of State.
presume,
The proposal of the Government originally was that we should enact the
words which now appear in the Amendment on the Paper and if the Government has changed its mind on that subject I think it is only due to this Committee that they should be told exactly
what has moved the Government to
change its mind, and why it has done so.
There is an old couplet which will be remembered by many of us dealing with the
unfortunate death of an infant-
If I must so soon be done for,
I wonder what I was begun for.
It may very well be that the House,
having heard the explanation of the
Government, may think it is not necessary to insist on the taxation officer originally suggested to them by the Government. That is of course for the Committee but I suggest we are entitled to a
full explanation of a somewhat sudden
change of front. Accordingly I put
these words on the Paper, and I beg to
move.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I must be
taken as objecting to the Amendment
which has been moved by Senator Leslie.
The original wording that was put into
the Bill was inserted before it was seen
by those who have great experience in
this kind of work and as soon as the undesirability of bringing these costs before
a
Parliamentary taxing officer was
realised it was suggested that it would be
simpler to allow the auditor of all the
local governing bodies in Northern Ireland to audit the accounts in connection
with the promotion of a local Bill,
as is done in connection with all
other Bills which have from time to time
been put forward by any local authority
or governing body in Northern Ireland.
Senator Leslie has suggested that we
should follow the English rule. That
may be all right in its way, but the hon.
Senator does not seem to realise that
there is no such thing as a public auditor
in England. There the taxing master is
the appropriate officer. In Northern
Ireland all the expenditure of local
governing bodies is audited by a local
government auditor under the Ministry of
Home Affairs. This alteration in the
Bill before the Committee was proposed
by me in the first instance and was
accepted on the Committee stage.
Mr. LESLIE: Not on the Committee
stage.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: On the Report stage, and I hope it will be retained
in the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The explanation given by Senator Sir William
Coates is the one which induced the
Government to make the change. All
good Governments should be willing to
make any change at any time if they see
a good reason for it, and so I am not
going to apologize for the change of front
on this particular occasion. On the whole
the Government were inclined to think
that Sir William Coates's Amendment
was an improvement, in view of the fact
that proceedings in Parliament in the
case of a rejected Bill would be of such a
Committee.

465 Borough Funds Bill-
1 NOVEMBER 1928
trivial character it would be unnecessary
for the governing body to be put to the
trouble and expense of taxation by the
taxing officer, especially as the ratepayers
were safeguarded by the Local Government auditor. I trust that this explanation will be sufficient. I ought to say at
the end that I am afraid I am unable to
accept the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite prepared to
withdraw the amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title, construction
and citation) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
SCHEDULE.
PART I.
Regulations for Meeting of Electors.
1. Where the governing body of a district have resolved, in accordance with
the provisions of this Act, to promote a
Bill in Parliament, and the Bill has been
deposited, notice shall be given by
placards and by advertisement in
local newspaper circulating in the district
in two successive weeks stating-
(a) the title of the Bill; and
(b)
some
the brief statement of the objects of the Bill; and
(c) that the Bill has been deposited,
and the date on which it was first deposited, in Parliament; and
(d) that copies of the Bill may be
inspected and purchased at a place
within the district specified in the
notice, between the hours of ten in the
forenoon and half-past four in the
afternoon, on every week day except
Saturday, and between the hours of
ten in the forenoon and half-past twelve
in the afternoon on every Saturday, for
twenty-one days after the date of the
first advertisement, and that extracts
may be

4. The presiding officer shall, within
three days after the expiration of the time
within which a poll may be required under
Part I. of this Schedule, but not less than
seven days before the first day appointed
for the collection of the voting papers,
publish a notice signed by him and specifying the date and place of the said meeting, the resolution or resolutions with
respect to which a poll has been required,
the facts that a poll was required and will
be taken upon any such resolution, the

will be delivered, and the day or days on

notice to be affixed on the usual place or
places for affixing notices in the district.

of this Schedule, and the polls upon any

to be taken, and the date and place of

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Regulation 1 (d), line 4, to leave out from "weekday" ("every week-day except Saturday")
to "for" in line 6 ("Saturday, for
twenty-one"). The difficulty in connection with the first paragraph of the Schedule arises in Clause (d). In the Bill as
it came before us the Schedule originally
provided that copies of the Bill must be
available for inspection and purchase between the hours of 10 and 5 on every weekday during the period of 21 days after the
date of the first advertisement. On the
suggestion of Senator Sir Wm. Coates,
which was made on the Report Stage,
the hours of 10 to 5 o'clock on an ordinary day were cut down to from ten to
half-past four. That, perhaps, is a matter of importance, but I do not propose
to dilate upon it this afternoon.
on
The hon. Baronet moved that
Saturday the hours should be from
ten in the forenoon to half-past
twelve in the afternoon. The effect
of that is plainly to cut out the
workingman elector from any reasonable
chance of exercising his privilege of going
to inspect the Bill and making copies free
of charge. The hours in England are
from 10 to 5 on all the six days of the
week. I cannot see, although I do not
mind about the half hour, how it would be
possible for this House to defend putting
in a provision that deliberately curtails
the opportunities of the elector to study
a Measure in which he is intimately concerned, nor can I see that such proposal
agrees in any way with what was said by
the noble Viscount in introducing the Bill
when he said that the Bill---
does not in any way weaken the reasonable safeguards which at present exist
to prevent a local authority acting in
this connection in opposition to the
majority of the electors.
I think that cutting out Saturday afternoon from the working man does distinctly
weaken his power of opposition to the Bill.
There is no justification for it at all.
Anybody who wishes can exercise the
statutory privilege of objecting to a Bill
which the local authority desires. There
is no authority for treating that man as a
kind of criminal or putting anything in
his way. It is obviously necessary that it
should be possible for him to obtain a copy
of the Bill or to make extracts. It is only
reasonable in the case of a working man
that he should have the opportunity at
some time when he would possibly be
disengaged. I beg to move.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: For my part
I am quite willing to leave this entirely
to the judgment of this Committee. It
is a matter of comparatively small importance only it would suit the convenience
of certain officials. I think that was the
reason it was put in.
Borough Funds Bill-
Committee.
1 NOVEMBER 1928
Mr. McDOWELL: I think the Amendment is a reasonable one and I ask the
Committee to support it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In these
circumstances I think I can accept the
amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move on page 5, Regulation 9, line 1, to
leave out in writing" ("be in writing
signed') and to insert "in the form set
out in Part III of this Schedule, and be."
The object of the amendment is that
there shall be some information given
as to who brings forward the requisition
for a poll. It is very desirable indeed
that whoever does bring forward a requisition for a poll should give his name
and address and his number on the polling district because otherwise it is almost
impossible to find out in a large constituency in a county or town who the particular person is who may be sending a
requisition form. I think this was largely
considered at the first Committee Stage
and I may quite safely leave it to this
Committee to see the reasons why it should
be in.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am willing to accept the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I had intended to rise
at once to say that I was reluctantly
obliged to ask the Committee to disagree
with the proposal, but the noble Viscount
was before me. I was about to give
exactly the reasons why this Committee
should reject the proposal. I imagine it
is a matter on which this Committee will
exercise its own judgment whether the hon.
Baronet does or does not leave it to the
Committee. The objection is fairly obvious. The moment we introduce a form
-and we have it in a later amendment
-we lay ourselves under the necessity of
providing by regulation that the form
shall be available to the electors. There
is nothing here as to where the man who
wishes to make an objection is to obtain
the form to make it on. There is nothing about it at all. Nor is the form itself
altogether properly drawn. We are left in
doubt as to whether the whole body of
objectors in a recent case which is before the minds of all of us I think there
were several hundreds--we are not
told whether they are to sign the identical form or whether one man alone is
to sign and have a hundred separate
forms. You would want "we" or "I"
and so on.
What is done in the English Act is in
There
the Schedule of the Act of 1903.
is a provision that the Local Government
Board may make regulations and may
prescribe forms and the regulation goes
on to say that if that is done those forms,
or forms to the like effect, shall be used.
That is a very different proposal from
that now before us. The proposal before
the Committee is that the requisition
must be on the stereotyped form and we
are not told where the man is to get the
form. It is not hard to imagine that if
the man applies to the office of the local
authority--it is not Belfast Corporation
alone; it may be town commissioners-he
is not likely to get great assistance. I do
not think it can be expected that every
man who may desire to object to a proposal of the governing body will be
acquainted with his registration number.
Registers giving the numbers and so on
are not kept at every street corner, and
they are sometimes out of date.
People have only got seven days in
which to send in a requisition and I do
not think it is reasonable or in accordance
with what was said that they should be
confined by these restrictions. As regards
the difficulties to which the local
authority may be put, they have got the
man's name. They can look up the register for these things. They have nothing else to do with their time. In the
case I have before my mind only five
signatures were required and I cannot believe that amongst hundreds there could
be any difficulty in finding five just men
to object. In future there would have
to be one hundred in the case of Belfast
and a lesser number in an urban district.
I cannot believe there will be any difficulty. If we pass this and if there is any
informality in the form of requisition the
requisition for all I can see to the contrary is dead and the governing body goes
on its sinful course unrestrained by any
opposition. There is no provision either
in the Bill before us or in the Act of 1888
which says an informality in the form of
requisition shall not vitiate the requisition. There is such a provision as regards costs. I do not think it would be
fair to the electors to pass this proposal
and therefore I am obliged to ask the
Committee to reject the amendment.
Committee.

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
first sight I thought the officer was required to fill in the number and particulars on this form, but when I learn to

have all through the Bill made it very
difficult for the few people to show their
objections to Bills that may be promoted.
There is a very considerable handicap
against them and we want to avoid making it impossible. If they required all
these regulations to be carried out it
would make it impossible for the genuine
elector to raise his objections to the Bill
he really was opposed to. I think it

easy for the officer to obtain the number
and all these particulars required.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to support the two last speakers and I
think there are quite enough provisions in
the Bill to safeguard all interests. The
elector has to give his name, address, and
other particulars and surely there are
enough officials to check those particulars.

Sir WILLIAM COATES: I will withdraw the Amendment.

Mr. LESLIE: We now come to page 6,

What I am suggesting is a slight verbal

the facts that a poll was required." It
seems to me to be an unnecessary repetition both in the text and on the paper.
I think it is a mere matter of verbal

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will accept that Amendment.
