Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, That Standing Order No. XXV be dispensed with
for the purpose of passing the Consolidated Fund Bill and the Loans Guarantee Bill.
On Question. Motion agreed to.
Pictures of Ancient Monuments.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Barclay:
To ask the Leader of the House whether, in view of the interest that is taken
in Ancient Monuments, steps could be
taken to keep a record for Northern Ireland, by pictures and photograps, of
those interesting landmarks of the past,
which are passing away unnoticed.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to ask the noble
Leader of the House the question which
stands in my name on the Order Paper.
The reason I do so is because there has
been a great interest taken in ancient
monuments in Northern Ireland, and a
certain amount of protection has been
given to them, as well as to historical
buildings in Northern Ireland. I thought
that perhaps something on similar lines
might be done as regards the many valuable pictures and photographs that we
have in this Province. I have not the
least doubt that we have in this part of
the country a great number of oil paintings, water-colour drawings, and photographs of distinguished men and women
who have done a great deal to build up
this Province and the British Empire
during their lifetime, and I would like
an assurance that the same interest that
is being manifested in ancient monuments will be taken in these pictures and
photographs so that they may be preserved for the education of future generations. I hope the noble Viscount will be
able to give me a satisfactory reply to my
question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
that this question has been asked, as it
will give me an opportunity, with the
permission of the House, to make a statement regarding the interesting and important subject to which the hon Member
has directed attention.
Since the passing of the Ancient Monuments Act (Northern Ireland), 1926 the
question of preserving a record of all
ancient monuments has been carefully
considered. The magnitude of the problem may be judged from the fact that
structures which can be described by
this term, are much more numerous than
is generally known. There are probably
at least 2,000 within the Six Counties
of Northern Ireland.
,
Consolidated Fund Bill 50
Ancient Monuments-
27 MARCH 1928
e
The Branch of the Ministry of Finance
to which has been assigned the duty of
administering the Act at an early date
turned its attention to the collection of
written descriptions, measured drawings,
photographs, etc., of all monuments in
Northern Ireland, whether in charge of
the Ministry or not, and has already collected a considerable amount of material
of this kind. It is hoped, also, that with
the co-operation of the Ancient Monuments Advsory Committee a systematic
survey of the monuments of Northern
Ireland may be set on foot. In the course
of this survey much descriptive and illustrative matter will be collected, and it
may be possible, if an adequate amount
of support from the book-buying public
can be secured, to publish the results of
this survey in the same manner as that
adopted for the antiquities of various
counties in Great Britain; but in any
case I can assure the hon. Member that all
matter collected will be retained, and
the Ancient Monuments Branch will
always endeavour to secure a record of
any monument in danger of immediate
destruction. The Branch would also welcome
the co-operation of local antiquaries in drawing its attention to endangered monuments or contributing
descriptive matter relating to ancient
structures.
I should like before passing from the
subject, to direct the hon. Member's attention to the very valuable work of the
kind contemplated by his question which
bas already been done by the Belfast
Art Gallery and Museum, and particularly to the unique set of drawings of
old Belfast collected by Mr. Thomas
McGowan and generously presented by
him to the Museum. The Ancient Monuments Branch is working in close cooperation with the Museum, and I am
pleased to say that there have already
been many opportunities of mutual assistance.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that
reply, may I ask the noble Viscount what
amount of money is now at the disposal
of the Advisory Committee, or perhaps
the question might be raised a little later
on this afternoon.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think I
must have notice of that question, and I
would prefer that the hon. and learned
Senator would raise it at the next meeting of the Senate, as the information can
hardly be at my disposal this afternoon.

Consolidated Fund Bill.

a
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this Bill be now read
Second time. As hon. Members are
aware, this is purely a Money Bill which
it is not competent for this House to
amend. I do not think that its various
provisions need any detailed explanation,
as they are quite clear and explicit.
Accordingly, I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Out of curiosity I would like to ask why,
if this House is not competent to deal
with this Bill, it is necessary to bring it
before us at all.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask the
noble Viscount whether, in this Consoli
dated Bill which we cannot amend, but
which, of course, if so disposed, we can
reject, provision is made for any sum of
Loans Guarantee Bill 52
Consolidated Fund Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
money to be placed at the disposal of the
Advisory Committee on Ancient Mounments. It seems to me that if we do not
get an answer to that now, we are a little
in the dark. The matter is not of very
great importance, and if it would be
better to raise the point, as it has just
been suggested, at a Jater date, that
can be done. In the meantime I presume I am correct in assuming that such
sum of money as has been voted in the
appropriate Chamber for use during the
financial year with which the Bill deals,
covers the money I have referred to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Col. Sharman Crawford), I may say that
while the Senate cannot amend the Bill
before the House they can reject it if
they think fit to do so. That is really
the reason for bringing the Bill before
this House. With regard to Senator
Leslie's question about the funds of the
Ancient Monuments Advisory Com
mittee, while I should have preferred to
have had notice of the question, I think
it is safe to assume that the money mentioned in the Bill before the House will
be available for Ancient Monuments as
for other matters.

Loans Guarantee Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.--(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this Bill be now read a
Second time. As hon. Members are
aware, this is a Money Bill, and it is not
within the power of this House to amend
any of its provisions. I do not propose
to go into its details at any great length.
Its object, like that of the original Measure which it continues, is to create employment.
The maximum limit on the aggregate
capital amount of the loans, which may
be guaranteed, was increased by the Act
of 1927 from £9,000,000 to £10,500,000,
and the position at the present time is
that guarantees have been issued, or are
about to be issued, aggregating a sum of
£8,770,657, leaving a margin of approximately £1,700,000 for further undertakings. The Government feel that this
existing margin is sufficient to cover any
new application which may be granted,
more especially as the bigger shipyard in
Belfast is likely to be fully occupied during this year with the work already in
hand.
The power of the Ministry of Finance
to guarantee loans expires, under existing
legislation, on the 31st of the present
month. The hope was expressed on a
former occasion that it would be possible
to withdraw these facilities at the end of
this current financial year, and in expressing that hope the Government had
particularly in mind the fact that the
British powers in this respect have been
allowed to lapse. The Government are
of opinion, however, that our circumstances here, with the limited number of
major occupations, are not wholly
analogous to the wider basis of British
industry, and accordingly propose that
the time should be further extended to
31st March, 1929.
I am sure that hon. Members generally are in sympathy with the object of
this Bill, which is to continue for a limited
period the provisions of the original Act
which has been so useful in assisting our
industries over the exceptional period of
depression which they have passed
through.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion negatived.

In Committee accordingly,

CLAUSE 1 (Legitimation by subsequent marriage of parents).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(2) Nothing in this Act shall operate
to legitimate a person whose father or
mother was married to a third person
when the illegitimate person was born.
*
*
**
*
*
*
*
Mr. GLENDINNING: I beg to move
the deletion of Sub-clause (2) of Clause 1.
This is one of the very few Measures
which came to us in a virgin state, and I
am glad to think that, thanks mainly to
the hon. Senator from Ballymoney
(Senator Leslie), it will not pass to another place without criticism. Other hon.
Members may hold opinions concerning it
which they may hesitate to express from
feelings of delicacy, but I believe it to be
a duty to point out what I consider to
be faults in the Measure, although the
Government in their anxiety to follow
exactly the lines of the English law may
not be prepared to accept my Amendment.
a
I have read this Bill with considerable
interest. It is
human document,
and I have also listened carefully to the
noble Lord the Leader of the House in
his exposition of its Clauses. There are,
however, two enactments in the Bill
which appear to me to fall short of the
object intended, and this Sub-section (2)
of Clause 1 is one of them.
This Measure, broadly, is brought in to
legitimate the issue resulting from an
immoral act, when the parents subsequently marry. I may be defective
in logic or
understanding, but I
really fail to see why the fact that one
of the parents was married when the misconduct took place should exclude the
issue from the benefits of the Bill. Indeed I will go further and say that it is
perfectly conceivable that an immoral relationship should occur and be, if not
justified, certainly palliated by the very
fact that one of the guilty parties was
married.
a
Take the case of a man accustomed to
marriage, whose wife becomes insane,
while the man remains youthful and
virile. He would be a stern purist who
would not find several excuses for
breach of morality by a man so condemned morally speaking to a life of
rigid chastity. I hope I shall not be
accused of condoning open immorality,
but we must take human nature as it is.
Indeed, an orator might find inspiration
for a high flight of eloquence in painting
the cruelty of this sub-section towards the
result of an unhappy marriage. He might
take as an example the case of a man of
a
sensitive and highly-strung temperament, married to a woman who turned
out to be a drunken virago, a man,
who in his misery turned to another
woman for sympathy. There are plenty
of virtuous women who, loving such
man and knowing the unhappy circumstances of his life, would freely sacrifice
all she possessed, even her honour, to
give him any happiness in her power.
Human nature is like that.
a
Legitimacy Bill-
Committee
THE SENA ΓΕ
[Mr. Glendinning]
Then if the drunken spouse died and
the widower gave his friend the only
reparation in his power, surely their offspring of such a connection is as much
entitled to be legitimated as any other illegitimate issue.
I am happily married myself, I am glad
to say, nor have I come much in contact
with ill-assorted couples, but judging
from what we read in novels, in the daily
news, and in the reports of divorce cases,
there must be innumerable cases where
illegitimate birth occurs as the result of
unhappy marriages, and why such issue
is explicitly barred and excluded from
the mercy of this Bill I fail to comprehend.
The poet Burns has truly said :
"Who made the heart, 'tis He alone,
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring its various bias.
Then at the balance, let's be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What's done we partly may compute,
But know not what's resisted.
For these reasons I beg to move the
deletion of the sub-section.
Mr. LESLIE: I appreciate very much
the kindly reference which the hon.
Member who has just sat down was good
enough to make to my endeavours to
enliven the debate, but much as I appreciate that I am afraid I must ask the
Committee most definitely to reject the
Amendment which has just been moved.
If this Committee were to excise Subsection (2) of Clause 1 the effect would be
to strike at the very root of the institution
of Christian marriage. It is one of the
basic principles of marriage undertaken
in accordance with the formulas of almost
any church certainly of any Catholic
church, whether it traces itself back to
Armagh, Canterbury, Constantinople, or
Rome-they all agree that the basic
principle of Christian marriage is the
exclusive union of one man with one
woman, and some hold that that marriage
is absolutely indissoluble by an earthly
court. They say marriage must endure
till death, and when hard cases such as
my hon. Friend has drawn attention to
are pointed out, they say in reply: "After
all, the man and woman took each other
They
for worse as well as for better.
entered into a relationship which they
ought not to break."
The question was raised, as the Committee is aware, in the House of Commons
in England when the corresponding
Measure was under debate there, and I
have just referred to the Speech which
the Home Secretary, Sir William Joynson
Hicks delivered on the occasion. I see
that he referred specially to the inclusion
of this particular Sub-clause as a Subclause which might give rise to differences
of opinion, and he invited the British
House of Commons to pass the Bill as it
was on the ground that the excision of
this Clause would stir the religious feeling of the country, and he pointed out
the duty of Parliament and the Government to pay attention to general religious feeling.
A great deal was said in the debate in
the House of Commons, and something
was said here on the Second Reading of
the Bill, as regards old Canon law on this
subject. I have endeavoured, since the
House passed the Second Reading, to
ascertain exactly what the old Canon law
was as regards the legitimation of the
offspring of adultery under a subsequent
marriage, but I regret to say I have not
succeeded, and I am in some doubt as to
whether the hon. Member for Swindon's
reference in the House of Commons debate
was not rather a reference to the date
of conception as opposed to the date of
birth, and I am not sure that he intended
to go so far as I thought he did. However, those are matters on which hon.
Senators can form an opinion for themselves by reference to the Hansard
debates.
I think that apart from the question of
religious opinions there is another matter
to be considered, and that is that a child,
legitimate or illegitimate, is the product
of two parties. You have to look at this
matter on the assumption that the child
it is sought to legitimate is the child of
a particular woman as well as of a particular man. Let the Committee ponder
on the appalling legal complications which
might ensue if a married woman had an
illegitimate child and then subsequently
married the adulterer. It would really
be an extremely difficult problem to
unravel. If a child is born in wedlock
during the union of its parents it is pre-
Legitimacy Bill-
Committee
27 MARCH 1928
sumably legitimate to start with. And
suppose you did allow this possibility of
legitimation per subsequens matrimonium,
look at the inducement you are holding
out to crime. You have a man married
and he commits adultery with another
woman. There is a child. What arrangements he may have made with the woman
you do not know. He may have told her
that if possible he would marry her, but the
fact remains that a child is born illegitimately while the man is married. There is a
most direct inducement to the adulteress
to commit murder to get rid of the man's
lawful wife in order that he can marry
the other woman. It seems to me that
there is nothing to be said in favour of
the proposed Amendment so far as regards this Bill.
a
of
a
The question which I think Senator
Glendinning really raised is the question
whether you ought to alter the law relating to divorce so as to allow, so far
as an earthly court can allow, of the dissolution by statutory authority of
marriage on the ground of breach of
contract other than the one breach
contract which you do at present admit.
At present, of course, in this country
divorce can only be in the nature of a
judicial separation divorce a mensa et
thoro--the old Canon law-but such
divorce can subsequently be made a complete divorce by Parliament in this
country and by the High Court in England. Such a separation is only given if
adultery has been committed. It is quite
a modern invention to give a woman relief
simply on the ground of the adultery of
her husband. Formerly a husband got
relief on account of the adultery of his
wife, but the wife did not get relief on
account of the adultery of her husband
unless it was coupled with cruelty
personal violence.
or
That has been altered and at the
present moment the two parties are on an
equal footing; that is, relief can be obtained when the marriage contract has
been broken in one particular, but
Senator Glendinning suggests other examples such as madness, drunkenness, and
so on, where in effect the marriage contract of companionship has been broken.
For that breach the law does not give
relief. It seems to me that if it is
desired to make a change there it is the
law relating to divorce that should be
considered, not the law relating to the
legitimation of children. I hope the
Committee will not accept this Amendment.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I think
the Amendment proposed by Senator
Glendinning cuts at the root of all ideas
of Christian marriage. We all know
perfectly well, as Senator Leslie has said,
that a person marries for better or
worse, for richer or poorer," and I do not
think it would be in the least advisable
to accept this Amendment. For one
thing it would introduce
matter of
extreme controversy into the Bill, and it
would set the Churches, which obviously
would take one side, against the people
who would take another side. For that
reason I must resist the Amendment.
a
Mr. GLENDINNING: In view of
the number of objections named by
Senator Leslie and the noble Leader of
the House-objections which I may confess I had not sufficiently carefully considered-I will ask permission to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Declarations of legitimacy
of legitimated persons, 31 & 32 Vict.,
c. 20).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Provided that, where a petition is
presented to the county court, the
county court, if it considers that the
case is one which owing to the value
of the property involved or otherwise
ought to be dealt with by the High
Court, may, and if so ordered by the
High Court shall, transfer the matter
to the High Court, and on such transfer
the proceeding shall be continued in
the High Court as if it had been
originally commenced therein.
Mr. LESLIE:
I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 2, line 23, at the end of
the Clause to add the following new subsection:-
"(3) The jurisdiction by this Section
conferred upon the county court shall
be deemed to include power to hear
Legitimacy Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(Mr. Leslie]
and determine a petition otherwise
than in open court."
I mentioned on the Second Reading
that I proposed to move an Amendment
such as this. The object of the Amendment is to give a county court hearing a
petition for declaration of legitimacy the
same power of hearing the application in
private as a judge of the High Court
possesses when a similar case comes before
that court. I think I pointed out on the
Second Reading that a judge of the High
Court had direct power to hear such an
application in Chambers owing to
reference in the Legitimacy Act of 1868
to earlier legislation. We are now giving
the county court a new power to hear
petitions, and I am asking the Committee to give a county court judge power
to hear a petition in private if he sees
fit to do so. All the provisions of the
Act of 1868 will remain; that is the
notification of the Attorney-General and
the citation of all the parties concerned.
a
All that is asked is that the county
court judge shall have power similar to
that which we gave in the Affiliation
Orders Act to resident magistrates to
make arrangements for illegitimate
children en in private. Power similar to
that has been given in England in the
Adoption of Children Act, an Act which
I understand in the perhaps distant future
we propose to follow. Whether the court
be the High Court, the county court, or
a Court of Summary Jurisdiction power
is given to hear and determine the matter
otherwise than in public, but in the
Adoption of Children Act the power is to
be regulated by Rules of Court.
It will be seen on reference to the Bill
before us that something is said in the
first sub-clause of Clause 2 about Rules
of Court, and, therefore, it might perhaps
be thought that this might be dealt with
under Rules of Court. But I think that
if it is desirable that this power should
be given at all it should be directly
given by Parliament and inserted in the
Bill, because Rules of Court in England
are subiect to the authority of the Parliament of Great Britain, but Rules of Court
in Northern Ireland are not subject to
the authority of the Parliament
Northern Ireland. They form part of a
reserved service which we cannot touch.
Therefore, I venture to ask the Committee
to accept the Amendment and agree to
insert the words on the Paper.
of
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the Committee will accept this Amendment. The
disturbance of domestic amenities is very
often due to the action of third parties,
and if the Bill is left as it is at present
1 can forsee that those who have been
morally weak enough to conceive children
outside wedlock would be open to an exposure, if the petition was heard in a
public court, that would be calculated to
destroy the peace of the homes of those
parents who had agreed to bring a petition
to legitimize their offspring born before
the parents had entered into the bonds
of holy matrimony. I think that if the
Government look at the Amendment in
that light they will realize it is one that it
would be advisable to accept in the interests of the peace of the homes of those
who may wish to take advantage of this
Bill when it becomes an Act.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: While
there are undoubtedly cases which it is
desirable should not be heard in open
court, it is agreed that it is not desirable
to encourage the hearing of cases in
camera by increasing the power of judges
to use their discretion in this matter. It
has been laid down by Lord Halsbury
that as a general principle all courts must
be open to the public, although the judge
may for good and sufficient reason order
that the public or a certain section of the
public shall be temporarily excluded.
This is further established in a Chancery
judgment in 1894 in re Martindale.
trust that now that it has been explained
to the hon. and learned Senator that
there is power at the present time for the
judge, if he thinks fit, to carry out what
is in effect the intention of the Amendment, he will agree to withdraw it.
I
Mr, LESLIE: Do I understand the
noble Viscount to say that a county court
judge has such power? I quite agree it
is so in the case of the High Court, but
my difficulty is that the county court
judge is being given a new power he did
not previously possess, and I cannot find
that he has any power to hear these cases
in camera unless we give it to him.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
county court judge already has the
power.
Mr. LESLIE: If that is the case, I
will withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave withdrawn.

CLAUSE 3 (Rights of legitimated

(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, a legitimated person and his spouse,
children or more remote issue shall be
entitled to take any interest-
*

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 3, page 2, lines 25 and 26, to leave
out the words "more remote" and
insert the word "remoter." This is a
trivial matter. There is an apparent
inconsistency in Clause 3 as it stands. It
is an inconsistency which we did not
create, but which we took over from
England. If the Committee will refer
to Sub-clause 3 of Clause 1 they will see
that the expression used is "children or
remoter issue." The words in the Subclause before us are "children or more
remote issue," and passing to Clauses 4,
5, and others, the expression again is
"remoter." It makes no possible difference
to the sense, but it is always
desirable that we should be consistent in
our phraseology, and therefore I suggest
this Amendment.
I believe this inconsistency arose owing
to the fact that during the passage of the
Bill in England various Amendments
were introduced, and that is possibly
how the matter occurred. If there is any
intention that the two words should mean
something different we shall no doubt be
told, but so far as I can see that is not
SO.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
The hon. and learned Senator's legal
mind may be able to perceive some inconsistency in the two expressions. I know
I can understand what "more remote"
but I cannot understand the
meaning of "remoter" because I have
never heard of the word..
means,
،،
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
word remoter," while it is novel to
some extent, is, as a matter of fact, rather
more consistent than
more remote,"
and for the sake of consistency I am
willing to accept the Amendment.
،،
Amendment agreed to.

the expression "country" includes any

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 8, page 5, line 14, in Sub-section
(3), to leave out the word "any" and
insert "England and Wales, Scotland and
any other." This Amendment raises a
problem which is not a large one, and
which I think we have not dealt with
before. That is why I propose to
direct attention to it this afternoon.
The words used in sub-section (3) of
Clause 8 are "any part of His Majesty's
Dominions." So far as I can form an
opinion-I rather expect the noble Viscount will confute me with a whole sheaf
of precedents-but I rather think it is not
usual to lump Great Britain and the
Dominions together; it is usual to give
Great Britain a little place to itself. The
point is, of course, that although we all
understand well enough what we mean
by His Majesty's Dominions, the expression "Dominions' has usually to be translated according to the context. In the subclause before us there is no context, and
I propose to give one. The words in the
corresponding English Act are "includes
Great Britain, Scotland, and any other
part of His Majesty's Dominions."
Scotland no doubt was specially mentioned owing to the difference in law in
this matter prevailing between England
and Wales and Scotland. Therefore, I
thought that although it is much more
usual to say Great Britain rather than
England, Wales, and Scotland, perhaps
it would be better to put the whole thing
on the Paper.
The difference between the Dominions
outside and England I have generally
taken to be this: That His Majesty's
power over the Dominions is usually obtained either directly or indirectly by force
(4
Legitimacy Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
as
a
of arms, either by direct conquest or by
treaty
the result of successful
I do not know what can be
campaign.
said about Wales, but Scotland was not
conquered by England nor was England
or Scotland obtained by force of arms.
England and Scotland adopted the
Crown because the King of England was
originally elected. The present King
of course holds by statutory title, as we
know, but the original kingship came
differently to England and to the
Dominions. I do not know that we have
done this before, and I direct attention
to it by this Amendment. On the other
hand, I cannot but admit that
Northern Ireland is not separately mentioned in the English Act, Northern
Ireland can only come into the benefits
of the English Act as a Dominion. Therefore, there is perhaps something of an
element of tit-for-tat about the words
used in the Bill before us. I beg to
move.
as
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
glad to say for my own sake that the
Government have not seen fit to go into
the matter of whether Wales and Scotland were ever properly defeated by
England, except in certain places at
certain specified times. I am prepared
to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

construed as affecting the succession to
any dignity or title of honour or as

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie: -
Clause 10, page 5, line 38, at end of
sub-clause (1) insert the following new
sub-section:
"(2) Nothing in this Act shall prejudice or affect the authority of Ulster
King-of-Arms."
Mr. LESLIE: This is another matter
of some slight interest which was mentioned on the Second Reading. I think
I may go so far as to tell the Committee
that the noble Viscount has been SO
kind as to look into the matter since the
House gave the Bill a Second Reading,
and I may add that with his unfailing
kindness he has let me know something
of the inquiries he has made. The point
is whether these matters over which the
Ulster King-of-Arms has authority are
or can properly be described as dignities.
I confess that I am not very certain. I
cannot make up my mind definitely
whether they are dignities.
If they are this Parliament cannot
touch them, and if they are not I am not
quite sure whether this Parliament can
touch them either, because the authority
in matters of this kind is part of the
Crown prerogative, and it has been committed in Ireland to the Ulster King-of-
Arms. We are prohibited, as the Committee knows, from legislation touching
the Crown. It seems to me that as we
are putting down a Saving Clause in
respect of Dignities and Titles of Honour
we ought perhaps to put down some
Saving Clause for the Ulster King-of-
Arms. At the same time I must confess
that I am not at all sure that it makes
any difference whatever whether we put
anything in or whether we do not.
In the corresponding English Act there
is nothing said about the authority of the
Heralds' College. The arrangements for
the decision of heraldic matters are not
the same in England and Ireland. Both
countries are miles behind Scotland, but
the mere fact that there was a Scottish
Act of Parliament passed in 1672 did
rather lead me to wonder whether we
ought not to put this Saving Clause in
this Bill. That is all it comes to. The
intention of the Amendment is to do nothing but put in a Saving Clause, but
the noble Viscount will be able to advise
us whether we ought to do anything or
not. For the moment I beg to move.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
view of the Government of Northern
Ireland is that the granting of the right
to armorial bearings is a question which
arises between the Crown and the subject,
the Crown having deputed its powers in
the matter to the Ulster King-of-Arms,
and this Department is not affected by
the present Bill at all. In the time that
has elapsed since the Second Reading I
took the opportunity to consult the

VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
I
regret to say that I am still not in a

ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: Before
actually moving the adjournment I should
like to have the opinion of hon. Members
as to the dates on which the House should
meet in the near future. I would like to
consult their convenience. It is desirable
to get the Unemployment Insurance Bill
through all its stages before Easter, and
it is a matter for hon. Members whether
they will take the Second Reading and
the Committee and other stages by suspending Standing Orders on Thursday,
or whether they would like to have a
longer interval between the Second
Reading and the Committee stage, in
which case we will take the Second Reading on Thursday and the other stages on
Tuesday of next week. I would like to
consult the convenience of hon. Senators
in the matter.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the Unemployment Insurance Bill is a Measure of considerable importance to the great mass of
the working classes of Northern Ireland.
As we only got possession of the Bill today, I would ask the noble Viscount to
postpone the Second Reading for a few
days to give Members time to examine the
Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: If it is
the opinion of hon. Members that it is
desirable to have the Second Reading on
Thursday and the Committee and remaining stages on Tuesday of next week, I will
move, That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Can the noble Leader of the House give
us any information as to when the House
will adjourn for Easter?
VISCOUNT
CHARLEMONT:
We
should adjourn for Easter on this day
week, although I think I remember the
hon. Member saying that never again
would he support the suspension of the
Standing Orders to pass a Bill through
its remaining stages.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
No, no.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: If there
is no objection raised to the suspension of
Standing Orders there should be no difficulty in adjourning for Easter on this
day week.
Mr. BARCLAY: When can we get a
copy of the Unemployment Insurance
Bill? It is not on the Table to-day.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I will
find out for the hon. Member.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Fifteen Minutes after
Four o'clock,
Thursday next.

Thursday, 29th March, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. J. A. LONG
(Deputy Speaker) in the Chair.
Royal Assent.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I am authorized by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his assent to the
following Bills:-
“
Consolidated Fund;
Loans Guarantee.
Bill Presented.
SCHOOL SITES BILL
to amend the Leases for Schools (Ireland) Act, 1881, and otherwise to make
provision for enabling certain leases and
grants to be made for School purposes,

read a First time, and ordered to be

PRESERVATION OF ANCIENT
MONUMENTS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
he can state the amount provided by
Parliament for the purposes of the
preservation of ancient monuments and
of the Advisory Committee during the
current financial year.
Mr. LESLIE: I think I might say in
asking the question that stands in my
name, that when the Ancient Monuments
Bill was under discussion in this House
the opinion was expressed that the sum of
£600 per annum, which at that time was
all that was provided for the preservation
of ancient monuments, was a scanty
sum for the purpose for which it was
intended. Since that time we have had
more than one question asked in this
House, indicating an interest, amongst
some members of the general public at all
events, in the general question, and it occurred to me to ask for information as to
the amount of money at present being
applied for the purpose indicated by my
question.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: As the
hon. and learned Member stated, a sum
of £600 was voted by Parliament for the
preservation of ancient monuments during
the current financial year. With regard
to the proposals for 1928-29, Parliament
will be asked to vote a sum of £1,025 for
the maintainance of the various monuments. This sum is allotted as follows:-
Special Works, all Monuments, £400.
Special Works, Dunluce Castle, £400.
Special Works, Jordan's Castle, Ardglass, £100.
General maintenance, including caretaker's wages, £125.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for second Reading read.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: The
Bill which I introduce to you to-day is
designed to bring the Unemployment
Insurance system in Northern Ireland
into line with that in Great Britain.
Hon. Members will remember that
there is in existence an agreement between Great Britain and Northern
Ireland, known as the Reinsurance Agreement, which provides that with varying
rates of unemployment in the two areas
losses out of one fund will be shared by
both countries. Under this agreement
very considerable sums have been paid
B

69 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
29 MARCH 1928

Tas
_ad
his

ור
--
-
D.
-dby Great Britain to Northern Ireland,
and it is essential in order that this valuable arrangement may be continued that
the Unemployment Insurance Scheme in
Northern Ireland should be maintained
on the same principles as those which
obtain in the remainder of the United
Kingdom.
In Great Britain a Measure amending
the Unemployment Insurance Acts was
recently passed into law following the
lines of the report of the Blanesburgh
Committee.
That Committee, which
consisted of representatives of all classes,
including employers and workers, considered the whole question of Unemployment Insurance and made comprehensive
recommendations as to the steps which
should be taken towards simplifying the
present system, removing anomalies, and
making it more suited to the needs of the
future.
The scheme established under the Unemployment Insurance Act of 1920, which
was the first wide Measure of insurance
against unemployment, was designed in
a period of comparatively good employment. It provided benefits under certain
conditions which limited strictly the sums
which might be drawn. The most important of these was the "one-in-sia"
rule, and the provisions which define the
amount which might be received in any
one year. This scheme might have proved
adequate if employment had continued
at a high level. In the trade depression
which unfortunately followed, the system
was found to be quite unequal to the
needs of the situation, and modifications
had to be made from time to time.
was
The development of the scheme took
place during difficult periods. With
work exceedingly scarce and workers out
of employment for prolonged periods it
became necessary to ease the conditions
for the receipt of benefit, and the system
now known as that of extended benefit
introduced.
Under this system
benefit became payable in certain circumstances where workers were not entitled
to benefit under the conditions of the
original statutes, now called standard
benefit, or where such benefit was exhausted. In order to prevent an undue
strain on the fund through claims where
the public interest did not demand that
payments should be made the principle
of Ministerial discretion was established.
In the determination of the cases in
which the discretion of the Ministry
should be exercised the voluntary services of the Local Employment Committees were used as advisory bodies. Unemployed workers could not claim extended benefit as of right and there was
no appeal against the decision of the
Ministry. This arrangement was never
intended to be more than an emergency
one, and hopes were held from the commencement that its life would not be
unduly prolonged. These hopes have
been deferred, however, and the system
which I have mentioned still continues.
The provisions of the scheme as it at
present exists are in many respects unsatisfactory. The conditions are unequal
in working and difficult to administer,
and could not be regarded as satisfactory
in normal circumstances. There are two
kinds of benefit-standard and extended.
There are two cases of title to benefit, one
which has reference almost entirely to
contributions paid, and the other which
requires close investigation, not only of
a claimant's past history, but also of his
future prospects, and personal financial
circumstances. There are two methods
of administration, one form of benefit
being claimable as of right with an appeal
against the insurance officer's decision to
the Court of Referees and the Umpire,
and the other being subject to powers discretionary and absolute on the part of
the Ministry, acting on the advice of the
Local Employment Committees.
The main lines of reconstruction are
designed towards removing these anomalies and simplifying the scheme and its
administration. The Ministerial discretion will be abolished and benefits will
in all cases be claimable as of right. The
conditions which regulate the amount of
benefit to be drawn to the number of
contributions paid will disappear, as will
also those which limit the amount of
benefit payable in any one year. Benefit
will become payable during periods of
unemployment without restriction as to
amount other than such as is required
to ensure that the claimant can still be
regarded as in the insurance field.
The test for this is that 30 contributions must have been paid during the
previous two years. For disabled ex-
Servicemen whose disability prevents
them from obtaining employment the
71 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
number of qualifying contributions will
be 10. In case of illness during the two
years the period will be lengthened by
the duration of the illness up to a maximum of four years in all.
In future the distinction between
standard and extended benefit will cease,
and there will be only one kind of benefit
payable to all claimants. There will,
moreover, be only two main conditions
for the receipt of benefit that the unemployed worker has had 30 contributions
in the last two years, and is genuinely
seeking work. If these conditions are
fulfilled the claimant will receive benefit
without regard to the amount which he
has obtained in any past periods.
The procedure for the determination
of claims will be that which exists at the
moment in connection with standard
benefit. Decisions on claims will be
given in the first instance by insurance
officers, and there will be the right of
appeal to local Courts of Referees. The
Umpire's office will be continued, and a
further appeal will lie in certain cases to
him. Rota Committees will cease to act,
and local Employment Committees will
resume what should be their normal
functions of advising the Ministry in
connection with problems concerning
employment and trade conditions.
In referring to the passing of the Rota
Committees, the Government would take
the opportunity of expressing their appreciation of the arduous labours which
have been performed by the Committee
members, and of the good services which
they have rendered in the interests of
the insured classes and of the Unemployment Insurance scheme generally during
the difficult years through which that
scheme has passed.
The Bill deals with certain other matters, amongst the most important of
which are the rates of contributions and
benefits for young persons. At present
there are flat rates of contributions and
benefits for persons over 18 years of age,
separate rates being, of course, in existence for male and female workers.
Experience has shown that having regard
to the earning powers of persons between
18 and 21 it is not desirable that the
adult rates of benefits should be paid in
these cases. It is recognized that during
these years earning capacity in a large
proportion of cases increases steeply, and
it has been found desirable to introduce
a sliding scale of benefits which raises
step by step the rates payable to boys
and girls of 16 and 17 years to those
payable at the age of 21. The new rates
of benefits are set out in the 3rd Schedule
to the Bill.
A variation is also proposed in the
rates of benefits for male adults and for
adult dependants. The present rates are
18s and 5s per week respectively, and
these it is proposed to alter to 17s and 7s.
The change is one of distribution only,
and is based on the view that the differing responsibilities of married men and
single men respectively are more nearly
represented by the difference of 7s than
by the present difference of 5s. It is
considered more desirable to pay a single
man 17s and a married man 24s than to
give 18s to the single man and only 23s
to the married.
In the course of a short speech it is not
possible to refer to every point dealt with
in the Bill, and I select the following for
special mention. Hon. Members will
have the opportunity themselves of asking questions in connection with any
points on which they desire information.
A residence test is being introduced which
will require that claimants for benefit will
have been resident in the United Kingdom for three years prior to the date of
claim. Regulations to be made in connection with this test will secure that insured persons leaving the United Kingdom for temporary periods in the course
of their employment, to seek employment,
or for health or other reasons, will not be
prejudiced.
The provisions in regard to the persons
in respect of whom dependants benefit
may be drawn are being altered, and, on
the whole, the revisions are in the nature
of extensions in favour of the insured
classes. The conditions governing the
disqualification of unemployed workers
for benefits during trade disputes will be
revised to remove the disqualification in
cases where the claimants are not themselves interested in a dispute, and where
there are no members of their class at
the same premises who are so interested.
The Continuity Ruie is being extended
to increase from six to ten weeks the
periods of employment which may occur
73 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 29 MARCH 1928

Second Reading
d

e

1.
h

without the unemployed persons being
required to undergo further waiting
periods before receipt of further benefit.
The right to "contract out" under special
schemes is being abolished, but the position of the existing scheme for the insurance industry is secured.
Transitional provisions have been inserted to effect smooth transfer from the
old conditions to the new and to prevent,
during a considerable period, insured
persons from being deprived of benefit
solely as the result of the new condition
as to numbers of contributions. The new
first statutory condition which requires
30 contributions during the previous two
years will not be enforced for persons
over 18 years of age during the first 12
months after the commencement of the
Act nor for the remainder of any benefit
year which has commenced for any claimant during those 12 months.
Claimants will, therefore, have a period
varying from one to two years before the
new conditions are brought fully into
cperation. During that period benefit
will be claimable as of right on conditions
which approximate closely to the existing
conditions for extended benefit, but without regard to the financial circumstances
of the claimant or his family.
Before I sit down I would invite the
attention of Members to the manner in
which the Bill has been drawn. In the
case of a number of matters such as the
statutory conditions for receipt of benefit,
the person in respect of whom dependants
benefit may be paid, etc., in carrying out
certain Amendments the provisions of the
original Statutes have been consolidated.
In this way the intention of the new
Measure is more clearly indicated and
references to previous Acts have been
largely avoided.
In considering the propriety of introducing at the present moment the provisions which are embodied in this Bill,
Members will no doubt bear in mind the
observations which I made at the beginning of my speech in regard to the
Reinsurance Agreement with Great
Britain. If Northern Ireland were to
fail to maintain that similarity with
Great Britain in Unemployment Insurance matters which is essential to the
continuation of the agreement in question
the financial loss would be so great that
the stability of the Northern Ireland
Unemployment Fund could not be maintained except by the raising of contributions or the reduction of benefits. This
is a situation which the Government
could not contemplate. Their policy has
always been equality of social standards
with Great Britain. The Bill which is
now before this House will secure
Northern Ireland the British rates of
benefit and will enable this to be done
without the imposition of additional
contributions. I beg to move.
to
Mr. DORMAN: In the first place I
wish to congratulate the right hon.
Gentleman on the very clear exposition
he has given of this Measure. On the
occasion when the last Unemployment
Insurance Bill was introduced in this
House I found it necessary to vote
against its provisions because of certain
disabilities that it imposed upon those
who were at that time unemployed. While
I welcome many of the advantages that
are contained in this new Unemployment
Insurance Bill there are certain things
within its purview that I cannot give my
consent to, and on which I shall feel
bound to divide the House this afternoon.
The removal of the family-income test is
an improvement that every right-thinking
individual must approve of. In the past
very great injustice and wrong was inflicted upon the unemployed and on those
who had to seek outdoor relief by the
application of the family-income test.
a
I hold in my hand a letter which is a
striking illustration of the evil of this
system, both as it applied to the unemployed and those who are destitute and
are necessarily seeking support from the
Guardians. I received this letter some
eighteen weeks ago from
very selfrespecting, sober, and 'industrious man
who during the war volunteered with
three brothers to go and fight for the
protection of the Empire. His letter will
convey better than any words of mine
could do the advantage that will accrue
to the unemployed by the removal of this
particular test. This gentleman wrote
me in the following terms: -
Sir,
Being out of work and getting no
bureau, I applied to the Guardians for
a little relief. I faced the Board and
answered all questions correctly. I live
in the house with my sister-in-law.
She is a widow with two children and
75 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
an old mother to keep out of her pension
of £2 4s a week. They took this
into account when speaking to me. In
1914 I left good employment along with
three more brothers and joined up,
done three years in France and
Belgium, discharged in 1918, unable to
work till 1920. Now I am able to work
I cannot get it. I have been employed
lately on tramway construction as a
general labourer. At the Board they
asked me what pension I had. I told
them the truth, 10s, but out of that
I have to pay 6s for room and fire, never
speaking about societies, which leaves
me about 2s to keep a wife and child.
Surely they do not expect my sisterin-law to keep me and mine. I want
no help of them if they will provide
work. Failing to provide work, a little
assistance was all I asked, for my wife
is unable to work through ill-health.
A lady Guardian suggested for my wife
to come into the house to gather
strength. God knows when it comes
to my wife or child or myself having
to go into the poor-house through no
fault of my own when I am able to
work I will seek another way out of
the difficulty. This is a very poor return for men that done their best in
time of need. Sir, this is a true statement. My wife is against my name
being spoken of. If you, Sir, can see
me anytime at my address and hear for
yourself. To you alone I give my
address.
He gives his address. The other way
out of the difficulty is going to be the
man's head in the gas oven. That will be
removed. That dark cloud of mental
instability which very often results from
investigation into the family means will
be removed by this amending Bill, and
in that particular I give it my wholehearted support and encouragement. I
am very glad that the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Labour, is here this
afternoon, because in carefully reading
over the debate in another place I find
the right hon. Gentleman fell into an
error with regard to the genesis of this
question that is being considered in this
House. He stated, I think, the genesis
of unemployment insurance arose from
the action of trade unionists in 1905 or
the succeeding years. That is not so.
The genesis of the demand for unemployment benefit was when the late Mr.
James Kier Hardie tabled in the British
House of Commons the Right to Work
Bill, in the year 1893.
For many years before that the party
of which Mr. Kier Hardie was the
founder had been proclaiming at the
street corners the necessity for some provision being made for those unemployed,
and who could not get an opportunity to
work in order that they might live. In
my reading I have always been convinced
of the fact that the great right of every
citizen of the great British Commonwealth
of Nations was the right of life. Now,
of what use is the abstract right of life to
those people in the community who have
no right of access to the means of life?
It seems to me to be a mere fiction to be
given life and to be shut out from the
right of access to the means of obtaining
that which is necessary for the continuance of life. That is one of the reasons
why the party of which Mr. Kier Hardie
was the founder disseminated through
Great Britain and through some parts of
Ireland the importance of the State
guaranteeing the individual the abstract
right to life, and seeing to it that if
those privileged constitutionally to the
right of ownership of the means of life
should shut the people out from access to
those means of life, then the State should
in justice provide sustenance and support
for every one of its citizens.
May I remind the House that one of
the greatest British Jurists, in the early
part of last century, in that work of his
on legislation, said the first and most
important duty of every legislature was
to provide sustenance for the citizens, and
the second duty was to provide abundance-sustenance and abundance for the
citizens of every community. I do not
think this Bill has set out with that
specific object in view. It is going to inflict a serious hardship upon the most
helpless portion of the community at a
period in their age which all physiologists recognize as being the period at
which they require of necessity, because
of the structure of their organism, the
greatest possible proportion of food, because of the strain that is placed upon
the organism in development from about
16 to 21 years of age.
I have another reason for objecting to
it, and that is this. I believe that very
much of the immorality of the society in
which we live, and very much of the
prostitution which unfortunately afflicts

S

some of the big centres of population, is
owing to the pressure, the economic
screw, grinding down the wages of these
people at a vital period of their lives,
when there are certain urges in their
nature, and when they require all the
assistance, moral and physical, they can
get, to keep them on the straight line.
In reading his speech I find the right
hon. Gentleman gave as a reason why this
reduction of the unemployment payment
at these younger ages has been found
necessary is that certain parents aproached him, pointing out to him the
impossibility they found of keeping their
children inside the paths of rectitude and
parental obedience at this particular age.
I find the Parliamentary Secretary of the
Ministry of Labour brought forward
another instance. Perhaps I would be permitted to quote his words. He states that
the father-I do not wish unnecessarily to
occupy the time of the House-the father
said a certain boy refused work, where he
was only in receipt of 9s or 10s a week,
having facilities to obtain 19s at the
The father talked
Labour Exchange.
the matter over with the boy, but he had
absolutely no control over him.
Does the right hon. Gentleman and his
Parliamentary Secretary realize what a
reflection that is upon the churches o
Belfast, upon the educational institutions
of our country, upon the Sunday schools
of our country, upon the control of
parents of the country over their offspring? Is it because there are a few
who lack the necessary moral fibre, and
have not had the advantage of the training in their youth that should be given
every boy is it because a few take unjust advantage of facilities that are held
out by the unemployment insurance to
their age, their situation in life, this reduction is made, while from week to week
we see and are led to believe that probably
for everyone who thus malingers and
obtains unjustly unemployment benefit at
these ages, there are hundreds leaving our
shores to seek employment in the Colonies
or some other part of the Empire? The
right hon. Gentleman has endeavoured
to justify this reduction on economic
grounds. Well, I have been a diligent
student of economics for many years, and
I just looked at one of the volumes on
that subject which it was my privilege to
study 50 years ago. I hope the House
will bear patiently with me while I give
this quotation which I think bears
directly upon the subject we are considering this afternoon. The author is not a
Socialist, but an orthodox economist. He
might be called a Marxian before Marx.
I am quoting from M'Culloch's Political
Economy. He stated in that work:-
Let a population once become contented with a lower description of food,
and an inferior standard of comfort,
and they may bid a long adieu to anything better, and every reduction in
wages, which is not of a transient description, contributes to bring about
this undesirable result.
Then, again, in another part of the same
volume he said :-
The best interests of Society require
that the rate of wages should be elevated
as high as possible, that a taste for
comforts and enjoyments should be
widely diffused, and, if possible, engrafted into the national character.
The age at which we are about to reduce the unemployment income is the
most critical age in their lives. It is the
age when habits are formed that go to
constitute the permanent character of the
future citizens, and the period at which
we should safeguard their right to build
up a desire for these things which this
celebrated economist said 100 years ago
were necessary for the State and the welfare of society.
our
The labourers are the thews and
sinews of industry. Their numbers are
not estimated, like those of other
classes, by hundreds, by thousands, or
even by hundreds of thousands, but by
millions. It is by them that
machinery is constructed and kept in
motion, that our agriculture, manufactures, and commerce are successfully carried on,
Everything,
then, that may have the slightest
tendency to depress their condition
And I hold that this reduction of the
unemployment allowance to citizens of
that particuar age has a direct tendency
to depress their conditions.
Everything then that may have the
slightest tendency to depress their condition or sink them into the social scale
should be particularly guarded against.
Those who feed and clothe all the rest
should be themselves well fed and well
clothed.
Now, it was my lot very early in life,
some 34 years ago, to have allotted to me
79 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
to
with another member of the political
party with which I had the honour to be
connected for the last 34 years
the task of making investigations in
the capital of this country as
what were the causes, as I have
already mentioned, underlying the life
many of our sisters must lead, and I
found from extensive inquiry with that
gentleman-who, by the way, was an
Englishman and an inspector of the
multiple shops in the City of Dublin, in
one of which I happened to be manager
at the time. We carried our investigations through very carefuly, and we
were driven to the conclusion that the
low standard of subsistence and remuneration was the cause of some nine-tenths
of the shame that manifested itself in
our public thoroughfares in capitals of
this country.
This is also true of the City of Belfast.
It has been my unwilling duty to draw
attention in public to names of firms in
Belfast itself, whose terms for young
women of the ages for which we are legislating the rates of unemployment benefit
for, were such that they had to sell their
virtue upon the streets at a time when
59 was equivalent to 8s or 9s now. Taking
into consideration the inflated cost of
housing and the inflated
cost of
lodging, and the increased cost of
living and all the circumstances surrounding the young unemployed of this particular area I must feel it my duty to
divide the House at the close of this
debate on this particular Measure.
Only this morning I happened to take
down an old book in which I opened a
page and found an extract which I think
exactly fits in with the situation we are
discussing here this afternoon. It was
a book written by a member of the Society
of Friends who were engaged in the
emancipation movement for the chattel
slaves of the United States of America
in the thirties of the last century. The
writer says:
For a man habitually to stint his
dependants in their food is the extremes of meanness and cruelty, and
the greatest evidence he can give of
utter indifference to their comfort.
The father who stints his children or
domestics, or the master his apprentices, or the employer his labourers, or
the officer his soldiers, or the captain
his crew, when able to furnish them
with sufficient food, is everywhere
looked on as unfeeling and cruel. All
mankind agree to call such a character
inhuman. If anything can move a
hard heart it is the appeal of hunger.
The Arab robber, whose whole life
is a prowl for plunder, will freely
divide his camel's milk with the hungry
stranger who halts at his tent door,
though he may have just waylaid him,
and stripped him of his money. Even
savages take pity on hunger. Who
ever went famishing from an Indian's
wigwam? As much as hunger craves
is the Indian's free gift even to an
enemy. The necessity for food is such
a universal want, so constant, manifest,
and imperative, that the heart is more
touched with pity by the plea of hunger,
and more ready to supply that want
than any other. He who can habitually
inflict on others the pain of hunger by
giving them insufficient food can inflict
on them any other pain.
As I have already stated, I feel it my
duty from this point of view, from the
Christian point of view, from the point
of view of morals and sound ethics, to
record this afternoon my disapproval of
the reduction of the standard of unemployment remuneration to people at this
critical age of their life.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: 1
have always felt that it is a great advantage to be one who has been reared
and brought up among the workingclasses. I am, therefore, quite well
acquainted with the conditions under
which they live. I never felt more
pleased than I feel to-day that I
have had that experience, because it is
impossible for Senator Dornan, while
he can gull others, to think he can possibly gull one who has been reared
amongst the workers, and knows exactly
the conditions under which they live. If
Mr. Dorman were sincere in the statements he has made here to-day, I would
consider him so innocent that he should
not be allowed out without someone to
take care of him, but I know that he is
speaking with his tongue in his cheek.
Mr. DORMAN: Thank you.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
idea of Senator Dorman making all
these statements to-day about wages and
conditions of the workers and how
some women are driven to prostitution
and such like! All that has no bearing
whatever on the Act of Parliament

Mr. DORMAN: Give them employment.
The
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
person who is employed requires some inducement, some incentive to do his work.
When he becomes unemployed and on the
streets, either through his own fault, or
on account of conditions over which he
has had no control, I think it is the duty
of the State to make some provision for
the man, his wife and family, to tide them
over the difficulty. I do not know why
Senator Dornan stated that the originator
of the Right to Work Bill was Kier
Hardie
Mr. DORMAN: I would remind the
hon. Senator that my name is Dorman,
and not Dornan.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
very sorry. I will try to remember that
in future. Mr. Dorman claims that it was
the action of Mr. Kier Hardie in introducing the Right to Work Bill. If I remember aright, that Bill was introduced
by Mr. George N. Barnes, the general
secretary of the Engineers' Society, a man
I knew very well, a level-headed man and
a
wise man, so wise, indeed, that the
Socialists have driven him out of the
Socialist party, howling him out of the
seat he sat for in Glasgow.
Senator Dorman can hardly credit the
statement made by the Minister of Labour
(Mr. Andrews) regarding the conduct of
such a boy receiving benefit, and he says
how injurious it is to the churches and
such like. But everyone knows that there
are cases of that kind-hundreds of cases,
not one isolated case. Only the other
day I met a gentleman. I need not say
what position he is in, but he had a boy
in the printing trade. His business got
slack and the foreman or employer sent
the boy out for a short time until things
would get a little better. That boy, who
was serving his time to the printing trade,
had only a few shillings a week. When
he went to the Exchange to claim his
benefit he got his full 18s, and the father
told me it was injurious to the boy because, so long as he was getting that
money, he could not get him to rise in
the morning or go to bed at night.
Mr. DORMAN: It is all in the way
you train them.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There
is the inducement. If a boy is receiving
18s a week for doing nothing that boy
has no intention of looking for work of
a nature such as will require him to rise
In the morning to attend to his business.
i will give Senator Dorman the name
and address of the man if he doubts me,
and there are hundreds of cases like that.
That is one of the good points in the Bill.
I do not know whether Senator Dorman
ever sat on a Rota Committee. I have
often sat on Rota Committees.
Indeed,
I was one of the first to sit on a Referees
Court, and my experience is that you get
a young man before you who tells you
that he certainly is looking for work. He
may be doing so, but I always thought
that 18s a week for a young boy of
eighteen years of age was a tremendous inducement to malinger and to avoid work.
A. boy of that age would probably be in
receipt of 12s a week if he were serving
his time to any occupation or trade, or
indeed a much lower wage, and you could
hardly expect such a boy to be in a hurry
looking for work when he is receiving such
un amount in benefit. Indeed, I think
I spoke to the Minister of Labour before
this Bill was introduced and pointed out
how beneficial a reduction would be to
those serving on Rota Committees, because we would then be able to grant a
boy an extension of benefit at the smaller
rate, and it would not be an inducement
to malinger or to avoid work.
I have a very good recollection of the
Insurance Bill being introduced in 1912,
and of the small number of trades that
were included in that Bill. I believe the
engineering and shipbuilding trades were
the two first to be included, and they were
followed by the building trade. I remember well how, when I was attending
one of our delegate meetings in Manchester, a most remarkable thing was that
the whole of the delegates of that trade
union were strongly opposed to the introduction of this insurance scheme, and
they demanded that an insurance officer
should be sent up from London to explain
the scheme to them before they started
to draw up rules for the administration of
benefit.
83 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading

[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
When we look at the very small number
of workers included in that scheme at
first, the great changes that have taken
place in the meantime, the improvement
made in 1920, and the experience that has
been obtained during years of good trade
and bad trade, we can only come to the
conclusion that such a Bill as this is
necessary at the present time to put the
benefit on something like a proper scale.
The regulations contained in this Bill are
probably an improvement on any of the
previous Bills. I do not really know how
this thirty stamps qualification will turn
out, so far as the workers are concerned,
but I understand that the Minister of
Labour has made arrangements that it is
not to be insisted on for the first year.
That is a concession which I am very glad
to see, and for the first year we will be
able to see how it will affect the workers.
We all must recognize that the workers
have a difficult time to tide over these
unemployed periods, especially if a man
has
wife and family. It involves
a little sacrifice to tide over such a time
until a man obtains employment, and at
a time such as the present when trade
Is so very bad we have to make a little
extra allowance for these workers.
a
a
times going away very sad, and on other
occasions going away with the impression
that we were being imposed upon, but
could not avoid it. The Rota Committees
have done their best, and have acted very
honestly and fairly in trying to carry out
the insurance scheme. We all must recognize that any legislation that is introduced to regulate the payment of benefit is
bound to give dissatisfaction somewhere.
There are people who would like benefit
for nothing, or money for nothing, all
the time, and if they got all the payments
necessary to afford them
decent livelihood I do not think they would trouble
about work. Therefore, legislation for
the regulation of benefit is bound to give
some dissatisfaction. From my own experience of trade unions and this insurance scheme was drawn up more or
less on trade union lines--and of secretaries who were carrying out the rules
and making out the sheets for members,
I am perfectly satisfied that some men
were very much annoyed and very disappointed to find benefit being reduced on a
certain week. Senator Dorman,
of
course, has made it appear that this is
going to cause more injury to the workers
than any other Bill.
It was also necessary that something
should be done in connection with standard benefit and extended benefit. As
far as extended benefit was concerned,
there was no rule laid down as to
how much a man would be entitled to.
On one occasion a man might come before
the court, find a very sympathetic chairman who was inclined to extend his benefit and give him a longer period.
The
next time he would come before the Rota
Committee, and the chairman probably
would not be sympathetic and would cut
him off. Up to the present it has been
left just to sentiment really. In the future
we have a definite line laid down, so that
it will not depend upon the chairman of
any particular court being sympathetic
or otherwise. It is to be carried out by
regulations which will be more satisfactory.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
was glad to hear the noble Viscount
making reference to the Rota Committees.
Undoubtedly it was a very unpleasant duty
one had to perform, endeavouring to do
what we could for the benefit of the
workers, listening to their cases, some-
Mr. DORMAN: To the young people,
not to all.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: If it
is only the young people with whom Senator Dorman is concerned, I do not think
he need trouble so much. My concern has
always been for the poor man with a wife
and family to keep. I did not worry myself so very seriously about the young man
living with his parents or friends, because
he could always get a decent living, and
if he could not get it here there were
other places. My sympathy always went
out to the poor man with a wife and
family, and I generally found that that
man made a strong effort to get employment if it was to be had. I certainly
support the Second reading of this Bill
with the greatest pleasure, and I do hope
it will work out satisfactorily. As I have
said already, I am sure it will if it is
watched over by the Minister of Labour.
The right hon. Gentleman always exhibits considerable sympathy with the working classes, and I am sure that if there
is any hardship inflicted, he will be prepared to amend it as early as possible.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second reading of this Bill, and I might
say that perhaps there is no one in this
