House to-day-or indeed in the other
House-whom this Bill affects more than
it does myself as an insured person and a
representative of the working classes.
The Bill, on the whole, will be of more
benefit to the worker in the long run
than the Act which is passing out of existence. Some men reaped a good harvest
out of the old Act owing to the extended
benefit, while men like myself, who have
been in constant employment, have had
to pay the piper. When the increase was
made, we had to pay it, and others
reaped the benefit, and now perhaps when
we would require a little bit ourselves
there is not very much for us.
I understand very well that we have to
go on parallel lines with Great Britain
seeing that a large contribution comes
to Northern Ireland from the Imperial
Exchequer. That is the one important
reason 1 have for supporting this Bill
to-day. If it were not that we were
getting this assistance from the Imperial
Exchequer, I am afraid there would be
a great deal of complaint amongst the
working classes about having to pay this
extra money. I am very pleased to know
that the Chancellor of the Imperial Exchequer is to continue to contribute towards this fund in Northern Ireland.
Now, Senator Dorman-I think that is
the right way to pronounce the word--
while he says a great many good things-
Mr. DORMAN: Thank you.
Mr. BARCLAY-----says a great many
other things with which I do not altogether
egree. On the last day on which we had
a short debate in this House he and I
did not altogether agree about the
working-class people, although they were
poor, being very immoral. That seems to
grate upon my conscience.
Mr. DORMAN: I never said that.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not think that
unemployment or the want of money
would cause any young man, or any young
woman, to go and lead an immoral life.
Senator Dorman made that statement in
the House to-day and he has also made
the statement at the Custom House Steps,
for I have heard it more than once or
twice, to the effect that some young
women in the city of Belfast have been
paid so low wages that they had to dress
themselves up, powder their faces, put
red stuff on their lips, and go out in
the evening to try and increase their
I never saw this, and I never
salary.
heard of any person making the statement
but the hon. Senator, and I have knocked
about Belfast a good deal, and am well
acquainted with a great many people connected with missionary work in the city.
These ladies and gentlemen have given
their lives and their time to that kind of
work, and I never heard one of them
yet make a statement like that.
Now, there is one question some of the
workers have been asking me, and I would
like to put it to the Minister of Labour.
This question seems to be troubling a
great many men at the present time
who are unemployed and on the streets,
that is, that you must have thirty stamps
before you are entitled to the benefit.
What is troubling them is this: They
say, "During the last two years the two
years in which they are to qualify for the
six months' benefit in the future-we have
not wrought more than nine, or, perhaps,
ten or twelve months. Each year we
have drawn what we were entitled to
draw according to the Act of Parliament.
How do we stand?" Some of these men
have almost come to the end of their
last six months, and their whole dread is
that if this Bill comes into force shortly
they would have to go two years without
receiving any benefit.
I
Then there is another question that a
seafaring man put to me. Of course it
has no bearing on this, but it deals with
sickness and disablement benefit.
would like the Press to take a particular
note of the answer the Minister of
Labour will give to the question. He is a
seafaring man. His ship is built in Belfast,
and this ship is to trade between foreign
ports, say North and South America. When
he is signing on in Belfast he signs under
British rule, that is, the person employing
him is entitled to stamp his cards. When
he gets to America he has to sign again
under the American rules, which enable
that same employer to do without the
stamping of cards. This leaves him practically on his beam ends. He spends a
number of years there, say one or two, or,
perhaps, more. He is trading between
those two ports, and he is receiving no
stamps on his card. At the same time
he has a wife and family here in the
city of Belfast, and weekly, or
nightly, or monthly, he sends the money
he is earning there for the support of
his wife and family.
fort-
87 Unemployment Insurance Bill- THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Mr. Barclay]
This man says to me, "Supposing I met
with an accident, and something would
happen that I could not prove that it was
caused by the neglect of the person employing me, and I had to come over here,
how do I stand? Or supposing I contracted some disease and died, how would
my wife and children fare as far as pension is concerned, seeing that I have subscribed to this insurance, but have had
no stamps put on my card during the
last two years?" That is a question that
is troubling a great number of seafaring
men in the city of Belfast. I would like
Mr. Andrews to give me an answer to
this question. Perhaps it would ease
their minds.
I should like to say as regards the three
years' residence in Northern Ireland that
that is a very wise provision. We find
people coming from across the border
and working for a few hours in Northern
Ireland. They get their cards stamped
and live on the other side of the border.
When they are unemployed they draw the
Exchange money, and then go back and
spend it in the Free State. While they
are working in Northern Ireland, although living in the Free State, they
are taking employment out of the hands
of the men and women here. I agree
with that part of the Clause. Three
years is a very short time. As a matter of
fact, if I had had a conversation with the
Minister of Labour I would have said
four or five years.
Mr. DORMAN: Or ten.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not think I have
much more to say. I have great pleasure
in supporting the Second reading of the
Bill.
Mr. MCDOWELL: Seeing that the
Minister is here, I would like to ask two
or three questions on this Bill. Before
I do so I should like to comment on the
statements made by Senator Dorman.
He is here representing a certain party,
and that party, so far as I know, agreed
with all the provisions of this Bill behind
closed doors.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. McDOWELL: Now, when the
Bill becomes public property, they come
into the open and try to belittle some of
the Clauses.
Mr. DORMAN: Some.
Mr. McDOWELL: I said "some."
Why? The reasons are obvious. I am glad
that the matter of the family income has
been done away with. Senator Dorman
recited a very pathetic incident. I could
also recite very many, and one in particular. This was a man who went away
when he was 16 years of age. He joined
the British Army and was a soldier for
practically seventeen years.
He went
through the Great War, but unfortunately he did not marry. He was discharged in England and remained there
for a short period, but could get no employment. His parents, who were still
residing in Belfast, seeing that he was in
bad circumstances across the water, told
him to come over to Belfast. He was
thirty years of age, and he came over,
but because his father happened to have
a few shillings above the family income
he was turned down. I think that was
a great hardship. I am glad that is done
away with.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. McDOWELL: Am I to understand that the seasonal worker is done
away with? I think under the old Act
he had to have 35 or 36 stamps for three
consecutive years before he was entitled
to receive benefit. Am I to assume now
that if he had 30 stamps inside the last
two years he will be entitled to benefit?
It would set a number of minds at ease if
that was only known. I would also like
the Minister to define for me what he
means by "genuinely seeking employment." I think it is a most difficult task.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. J. CUNNINGHAM: It is not
done away with.
Mr. McDOWELL: More is the pity.
When a man makes an effort to get employment, I do not think "genuinely"
means that he must go round half of the
town seeking employment.
I think it
really means that he can concentrate his
efforts on perhaps three or four places
where he is likely to obtain employment.
For instance, a case was brought to my
notice the other day where a man concentrated his efforts on four or five firms.
He went round these firms every day.

After going to one of them a number of
mornings the manager said to him,
"What is the use of you coming here?
You were here yesterday morning." He
said, "Yes, I was, and I was here the
morning before that, and the morning before that again, but you did not want any
man, and you may want one this morning." The manager said, You give me
the impression that you are anxious for
work, come and I will start you." Supposing he had gone round a dozen or two
dozen firms, the probability is that he
would have had to go round again and
nobody would have taken the slightest
notice of him. I think that is a question
that should be very carefully looked into
so far as the Ministry is concerned.
I think this Bill will commend itself
to the working men. Another point
comes to my mind. Say a man ceased
employment at the latter end of 1927.
We know so far as his contributions are
concerned he has plenty, He has worked
continuously since 1912, and he becomes
idle. I understand that it is the intention of the Minister to give a year
or two years' grace under the present system, but up to 1930 he has had no work.
We will assume he has been receiving
benefit all that time. Yet at the end of
that time, although he had not 30 contributions to his credit in that two years,
he has a surplus to his credit. In what
way are you going to deal with that man?
That may apply to a number of cases.
would like a reply to these two or three
questions. I commend the Bill as a whole
for adoption by the House.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to detain the House for more than a few
moments. We have already heard that
this Bill is based on the English Act, and
it follows from that, that unless we are not
prevented by our commonsense from even
desiring to upset the advantageous arrangement made with Great Britain, we
have no choice but to pass the Bill without any serious structural alteration.
Quite apart from that, as it seems to me,
the Bill possesses some very distinct
merits of its own. It will ultimately
make for simplification in the text itself.
The method adopted seems, if I may say
so, to be more or less an additional laurel
in the wreath with which our Parliamentary Draftsman is rapidly surrounding
himself. It is much more easy to understand than some of its predecessors.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill will, when
adopted, make for simplification in practical methods, which perhaps the right
hon. Gentleman (Mr. Andrews) will
elaborate for us later on, and which I
need not speak on at the moment. Again,
the Bill has negative merits. It makes
no pretence of interfering with the
functions of Boards of Poor-law Guardians,
nor does it make any pretence of readjusting pensions in respect of war service, nor
does it in any way interfere with legislation as regards the Workmen's Compensation Acts or the Widows' and Orphans'
Contributory Pensions Act. It does none
of these things, and none of these things,
it seems to me, are in any way in point
in considering the Bill.
But there is another very great merit,
and it is the educational provision which
is made. I think I am right in saying that
that is a new departure in this Measure
and in the coresponding English Measure, or if it is not an entirely new departure, the sanction, perhaps, to be given to
it is a new departure, and it seems to me
very highly commendable. I do not in
the least follow the complaint as to the
reduction of what I may describe as the
youthful dependant. I am very much
afraid that at present both the cinema
and the bookmaker take too high a toll
of the money paid out in unemployment
benefit, particularly to youthful members
of the community. If additional provision for attendance at technical classes
will in any way tend to prevent that, it
would be very much to the good. On the
contrary, so far from objecting to such
reduction as is made as regards youths,
I am afraid I am somewhat doubtful as
to whether it will be found practicable
ultimately to maintain the present standard of benefit. That remark, of course,
applies to Great Britain just as much as
to ourselves. It may very well be, as it
seems to me, that a change of economic
conditions may render an alteration
necessary.
Now, I pass to add my own humble
contribution to the bombardment of interrogatories which has been hurled at the
right hon. Gentleman. First of all, I
want to refer to this question of the three
years' residence in Northern Ireland. We
have heard a great deal about it. One of
my Friends on my left (Mr. Barclay)
mentioned it this afternoon, and we heard
91 Unemployment Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

[Mr. Leslie]
the answer. I cannot find in the Bill
that three years' residence in Northern
Ireland is required. What I do find is
three years' residence in the United Kingdom. I think I am correct in saying
there is no special provision as regards
residence in Northern Ireland. It is the
United Kingdom that is the test.
Mr. ANDREWS indicated assent.
move
Mr. LESLIE: That is a point of very
great importance. The United Kingdom
means Great Britain and Northern Ireland. Our workers
backwards
and forwards between Great Britain
and Northern Ireland, and are subject to Unemployment Insurance, and
stamps, and books all the time. If they
move in the other direction, which my
Friend on my left mentioned, and are
not so subject, the position is entirely
different. The point is a good one, and
I am glad to have confirmation from the
right hon. Gentleman that there is no
special reference to Northern Ireland in
that connection.
The other small matter has reference to
the adopted child. I am a little afraid
there may be some possibility of the
adopted child falling out owing to the
fact that in England there is provision
made for adopted children by a recent
statute which we have not yet followed,
but which we intend to follow. What I
am afraid of is that it might be said later
on that the adopted child in the meaning
of this Act can only mean a child adopted
in accordance with the law of Great
Britain, a law which exists there at the
moment, but not in Northern Ireland.
On the other hand, it may very well be
said that as soon as we pass a Measure of
that sort the adopted child will come in.
The phrase "adopted child," as used in
this Bill, is merely descriptive, and there
is no danger to any properly accredited
child-and the child must be properly
accredited; we must be sure the child is
a bona-fide adopted child, not a dummy
put up for the purpose of the Act.
It might be said there is no real danger.
I will ask the right hon. Gentleman at
a later stage the point can definitely be
raised on the question that Clause 7
stand part of the Bill-to say there is no
danger. It is plain we must have a reference in this Bill to the adopted child,
because the adopted child might be imported from Great Britain. The same
expression "adopted child" was used, it
may be remembered, in the English Act
of 1922; that was the Act passed before
the recent legislation dealing with child
adoption. That leads me to think there
is no danger lurking behind the matters
to which I am drawing attention, but I
should like the Minister to confirm this
definitely at a later stage. I have nothing more to say except that I cordially
support the Bill, and believe it will be a
great satisfaction to everybody when it
is passed.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: This Measure has
been criticized in the other House favourably and unfavourably, but, if I may say
so, I think the greatest tribute that has yet
been paid to it has been paid by Senator
Dorman, to-day, because he found only
two of its provisions to criticize. One is
a provision which does away with what he
felt was a serious blemish in the previous
Acts, namely, the abolition of the family.
income test. The provision which he did
criticize somewhat severely, but not unfairly, was that which reduces the benefit
to a young man and a young woman.
quite admit the matter can be approached
from two standpoints, but it is not for
me to apportion the blame for that condition of affairs which he thinks exists,
but which I think he exaggerates. Undoubtedly,
serious state of affairs was
created, particularly among our young
men, by the amount of benefit they received when unemployed being at a higher
rate than the wages they received when
in employment. That applied particularly to young men serving their apprenticeships.
on.
a
They entered an apprenticeship and for
the first year received 9s or 10s a week,
the next year 11s or 12s a week, and so
It was a temptation to those young
men to feel that if they became unemployed they would very probably get
18s a week for a year or two years if they
could make a pretence of genuinely seeking work during that period. I am
speaking about what I know because, in
addition to the case to which the hon.
Senator referred, and to which I referred
in the other House, many cases have been
brought to my notice, both by employers
and by parents of these young men, which
convinced me and, what was more important still, also convinced the Imperial
Minister of Labour, that really a wrong
was being done to those young men by
putting this temptation in their way.

It has been said that if they left their
employers of their own accord, or if they
got dismissed, they did not get benefit.
That was quite true for six weeks, but at
the end of six weeks they were entitled to
claim benefit, and in some instances they
were prepared to take the risk in order
to gain probably a very great advantage.
Therefore, I think on consideration the
hon. Senator, who takes a very reasonable
view of these things in some respects, will
feel that although this provision may
cause hardship in certain directions-I
regret to say that yet, in the main, it
will really be of advantage to our young
trade unionists. I do not know a greater
Injustice that we could do to these young
people than to teach them in their youth
that they can live without work.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. ANDREWS: It is part of the
creation plan that we should all work,
and it is only by the work of all her sons
that Ulster can become the prosperous
place which we hope eventually to see
her. A considerable number of questions
have been asked which I shall endeavour
to answer. The first that I need refer
to was in reference to the thirty stamps
in the two years, which, of course, is the
first statutory condition for the receipt of
benefit, and, therefore, the most important provision in the Bill. It is quite
right that what is intended should be
thoroughly understood. One thing I
know that made many of our unemployed
men very anxious was this. They felt
they had accumulated during the last few
years considerable overdrafts due to the
fact that having drawn all their standard
benefit which they received as a statutory
right they had what is called extended
benefit, which was really benefit granted
in consideration of contributions, which it
was expected would later on be paid.
can relieve the mind of the hon. Senator
at once by telling him that by this Measure all the overdrafts are wiped off.
Mr. BARCLAY: Hear, hear.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: Every insured worker
will get a clean sheet and start afresh on
an equal footing. That is one of the
great benefits our workpeople will receive
by this Measure when it becomes the law
of the land. Then, again, as regards the
bringing in of this provision of thirty
stamps, if it had been brought in hurriedly it would undoubtedly have created
a great deal of hardship, because some of
our people have been out of employment
continuously for a year or two, and, in
some instances, perhaps for a longer period.
Obviously they would have had no stamps
to their credit at all. That being recognized, it was suggested by the Blanesburgh Committee, which very thoroughly
investigated this whole problem of Unemployment Insurance, that it was right to
have what is called a transitional period,
a period in which a claimant could receive
benefit either under the old conditions or
under the new. The old conditions will be
applied in the case of those who cannot
obtain benefit under the new conditions
for a period of one or two years. That
will give those who may have been continuously out of employment for one or
two years a year or two's grace in order
to get thirty contributions upon their
cards and thereby qualify for benefit if
at a later date they should become unemployed again. Hon. Senators will appreciate that this is a very wise and very
reasonable provision. It will save hardship in many directions where otherwise
hardship would have been caused.
،،
I think it was Senator McDowell who
raised the question about seasonal workers.
The special conditions for seasonal
workers will be wiped out by the universal application of the "G.S.W."
and 30 contributions." The first
statutory condition deals with the qualification for benefit of thirty contributions for the purpose of showing if a
claimant is really an insured person, and
the second condition is that he should
show he was genuinely seeking employment. I know that in the administration of this condition a good deal of dissatisfaction has, I am afraid inevitably,
been created, because-speaking with all
modesty in the presence of such a distinguished educational authority as my right
hon. Friend (Viscount Charlemont) -
there are certain words in the English
language that cannot be done without,
but that at the same time do not convey
exactly the same meaning to every human
mind. Some people may say that a certain thing is reasonable, while other persons may say that it is not reasonable;
but, on the law of averages, those words,
although necessarily a little flexible in
their meaning, do create the same impression on different people, and, thank
goodness, by this Measure the Ministerial
discretion is ended, and it will not be left
to me to define what those particular
95 Unemployment Insurance Bill--

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Andrews]
mean.
They will be left to
words
Courts of Referees
May I say, in that connection. that 1
think the Courts of Referees have been
unfairly and ungenerously criticized? I
think we should all remember that these
Courts are set up as perfectly impartial
bodies, not for the purpose of giving
benefit to those who are not entitled to
it, not for the purpose of disqualifying
those who are entitled to it, but for judging each case absolutely fairly when it is
brought before them. We should remember also the personnel of those Courts.
First of all, there is the workers' representative. I do not think the workers'
representative would be very likely to be
prejudiced against the interests of his
fellow working people. Then there is the
employers' representative. He is only a
third of the whole, and I think I may say,
in a matter of this kind, that employers
would not wish to do a wrong to workers
to whom all reasonable employers feel
they are much indebted. The third
member of the Court is a distinguished
lawyer, who is chosen because he is
neither an employee nor an employer.
He is independent of both, and has
been brought up to weigh evidence, and,
therefore, to come to a proper decision.
That is how the matter is going to be
settled in the future.
There will be a statutory right to all
benefit. In the past that has not been
SO. A good deal of loose thinking has
been directed towards this change. I have
heard it stated, on many occasions, by
people who ought to have been better informed that this Bill intended to wipe out
entirely extended benefit. That is only
half a truth, and half a truth is a very
dangerous thing. You might as well say
its intention was to wipe out standard
benefit. It really does wipe out both
standard benefit and extended benefit, as
we have known them, and it substitutes
for those forms of benefit a new form of
benefit altogether, under conditions which
I think are not unreasonable or unfair.
There is another thing that I think we
should remember. We hear a great deal
about the amount of benefit to be paid.
and the length of time it should be paid
for. but we never hear a word said about
contributions. That is a strange fact.
and yet the contributions must bear a
distinct relation to the benefit paid.
we are going to raise the benefits in all
If
directions, then we must be prepared to
raise the contributions, which would put
a heavier burden still upon industry and
upon the employers who, remember, have
got to pay contributions for all their
workers, and upon the employees, who
are already under a considerable strain in
naving to pay for this social service alone
-apart from other social services-sevenpence a week.
I should have stated, in dealing with
the benefit payable to young personsyoung women and young men--that there
again a reasonable course is being pursued, because in consideration of the
benefits being reduced, the contributions
are also being reduced to some extent.
The hon. Senator who sits opposite me
(Mr. Dorman) dealt with many matters
beyond those, if I may say so, with which
this Bill deals. He spoke about the right
to work, and matters of that kind which
have been referred to frequently on public
platforms. But, when you talk about the
right to work, you set up a principle
which is very hard to defend, because up
to the present in this world it has always
been laid down that the duty lay upon
the worker to seek employment; but, if
you set up the right to maintenancefull maintenance or the right to work,
you really place upon the State the responsibility of obtaining work for every-
However right that may be in
theory, it would not work in practiceone.
Mr. DORMAN: Why?
Mr. ANDREWS-obviously, but I
do not wish to continue that argument.
I think I have said sufficient to show the
hon. Senator that this matter can be approached from a different standpoint from
that which he takes.
Senator Barclay gave me a very difficult
question to answer; in fact, it was never
put to me before, and but for a certain
amount of help which I have received
from the officials' gallery I would not
have been in a position to answer it. He
asked what about persons who joined up
as crews on a ship in Belfast, then went
off in that ship, went to foreign ports,
and came back after a few years. How
would they stand? I am glad to be able
to tell him that in connection with all
these social services as long as the ship
flies the Union Jack-the flag of the
United Kingdom and as long as it is
British-owned, the insured persons are in
no way prejudiced. If they find that
97 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

29 MARCH 1928

[Mr. Andrews]
the ship is being transferred to another
owner, or to another country, then I
would suggest to them that the sooner
they get on to a British ship the better
for many reasons. That is the answer to
the question.
Regarding the adopted child, I am glad
to be able to tell the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) that it is cared for
in this Measure. It is true that the law
here is different from that of Great
Britain, but if an adopted child comes
here from Great Britain the provision in
this Measure will keep that child all
right. Dependant's benefit will be paid.
As to the position here at the moment,
we intend to pass very shortly a Measure
which will bring the law into step with
that of Great Britain, and, therefore, the
matter will be absolutely straightened
out, and in no way will the position of
the adopted child be prejudiced.
I think I have answered most of the
points that have been raised, but if I
have omitted anything I will be quite
pleased, if I am reminded of it, to endeavour to meet the point. May I, in
conclusion, thank this honourable House
for the patient way in which they have
listened to my few remarks?
Mr. BARCLAY: I neglected to ask all
of the question that was put to me
Mr. DEPUTY SPEAKER: The hon.
Senator can only speak by leave of the
House. I take it that you agree?
The House indicated assent.
Mr. BARCLAY: Part of the question
escaped my memory, and it has occurred
to me now. The gentleman who was
speaking to me said: "Suppose I joined a
foreign company, an American company,
under the Stars and Stripes." When he
goes under the Stars and Stripes he is
not looked upon as an insured person.
What this gentleman wanted to know
was this: if he is earning wages under the
Stars and Stripes and sends those wages
home to support his wife and family in
the city of Belfast, will his wife be allowed to become a contributor on behalf
of her husband?
Mr. ANDREWS: I think you mean
this: could his wife, acting on his behalf,
make him a voluntary contributor?
Mr. BARCLAY: Yes.
Mr. ANDREWS: Speaking on the spur
of the moment, I think probably he
could: but if the hon. Senator will raise
the question again on the Committee
stage either the Leader of the House or
I will give him an answer which will be
beyond all doubt.
While I am on my feet, may I say that
there was one matter raised by the hon.
and learned Senator opposite (Mr. Leslie)
that I should like to refer to, that was
in reference to the educational training
efforts which are made under the provisions of this Bill. Up to the present
any training that was given to young
persons or adults for domestic service, or
for other purposes, had to be paid for
by the Ministry of Education, to the
extent that they had means to pay for,
and a good deal of help was given by
our technical schools and by charitable
organizations in certain instances; but we
were not in a position to devote from the
Unemployment Fund any grant of money
towards an educational effort of the kind.
This Bill will permit a portion of the
expense incurred in the education of unemployed insured persons to be defrayed
from the Unemployment Fund

Legitimacy Bill [S.].
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, That this
Bill be now read the Third time.
a
Mr. LESLIE: There are two matters
which were referred to at an earlier stage
of this Measure to which I would like to
direct attention for two or three minutes.
The one I will take first relates to an
Amendment which was moved and withdrawn, dealing with the possible legitimation of the proceeds of illicit union
between
married man or a married
woman and another. I think it is perhaps right to remind the House that there
is nothing in the Bill before us which
would prevent relief being given in such
a case, as for want of a better example I
will call the Enoch Arden case, where
a woman contracted a second marriage,
and the original husband ultimately reappeared. There is nothing in this Bill
which would prevent application being
made to Parliament in such a case for a
special Act of legitimation. There are
instances, about two or three a century,
where that has been done. It is possible
for a child, which is the result of innocent
bigamy, to become legitimated by the
direct action of Parliament, and there
is nothing in this Bill which interferes
with that.
The other point is the more difficult one
of the question of giving the County Court
Judge power to hear applications under
this Bill in private; that is to say, in
chambers. What was in my mind when
I mentioned the matter on the Second
Reading, and again when I brought forward an Amendment in Committee, was
that the County Court Judge in this matter should be placed in exactly the same
position as a High Court Judge. I think
the discussion went off a little, it being
thought that the County Court Judge and
the High Court Judge had already identical powers, and an instance was given
where a Judge had power to clear the
Court when any evidence was about to be
given which it was contrary to public decency to hear in public. That was not
quite what I meant.
What I think is really wanted is power
to hear in chambers. That power, as I
understand it, a Judge of the High Court
does possess. He gets it by virtue of the
Legitimacy Declaration (Ireland) Act,
1868, which refers to powers given to the
Judges in connection with probate matters, and I think the Probate Act in
question bears date 1859, although I am
speaking subject to correction. In that
Act it is clearly stated that a Judge shall
have power to hear cases in chambers, but
that power to hear in chambers is quite a
different power to their power to clear
the Court in the ordinary way. I find
that power to hear in chambers has been
given to County Court Judges in special
Statutes. It was given in the Irish Land
Act, 1870, and again in the Land Law
Act of 1881. I therefore hope-although
I am not putting any Amendment on the
Paper to-day-that the matter may
ceive further attention in another, place.
In this matter this House is a chamber of
first instance, and it is perhaps not inappropriate that a proposal such as that
which I have just made should emanate
from the more directly representative
Chamber, because if it is accepted it would
undoubtedly be somewhat contrary to our
general policy to which the noble Viscount
alluded at an earlier stage.
re-
The general policy is the hearing of
cases in open Court. That is a very important point with which, speaking generally, I am myself in accord. I think it
will be remembered that the Parliament
of Northern Ireland has made distinctions in this way not only in the Affiliation Orders Act, but also when we were
dealing with the Criminal Law Amend-
Legitimacy Bill
Adjournment
3 APRIL 1928

atne
ng

enthe
be
e
I
ment Bill in, I think, the year 1923. We
directly refused--or rather those sitting
elsewhere refused to follow England in
requiring a certain class of case to be
heard in open Court instead of, as was
then and still is the usage in Northern
Ireland, hearing it in secret. I do not
think it would be right to delay the passage of this Bill in respect of a matter of
this kind. I do hope it will not be lost
sight of. If the opinion I hold is shared
attention will be directed to the matter,
as I say, in another place.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
hon. and learned Senator has said he
would not press for an answer to the last
point he has mentioned in his speech, I
would prefer not to answer it beyond saying the Government's attention will be
directed to the point he has mentioned.
With regard to his first point, the case of
a possible Enoch Arden in Ulster, I think
nothing in the Bill will affect that matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill do now pass.-
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Mesage was sent to the Commons
to carry down the said Bill and desire
their concurrence thereto.

that he had received a certificate from
the Clerk of the Crown, that Colonel
Hercules Arthur Pakenham, of Langford
Lodge, Crumlin, Co. Antrim, was on the
4th day of April, 1928, duly elected a
Senator to serve in the Parliament of Northern Ireland, in the room of Mr. Thomas
MacGregor Greer, deceased.
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the House
will allow me to apologise for being late
in taking the Chair. I was in a rather
difficult position, because I was down to
propose a vote of thanks at a luncheon
this afternoon, and I should have been
discourteous if I had not waited to do so.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).

Royal Assent.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am authorised by the Prime
Minister, on his behalf, to inform the
Senate that His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, in the name and on
behalf of His Majesty the King, has been
pleased to give his assent to the following
Bill:
Uuemployment Insurance.
School Sites Bill (S).
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the absence of my noble
Friend, the Leader of the House, (Viscount Charlemont), who is away on an
important matter connected with the Department of which he is the head, I propose to move the Second Reading of this
Bill. The purpose of this small Bill is
two-fold; firstly, to facilitate Education
Authorities in accepting the transfer of
public elementary schools, and secondly,
to make it possible for the Local Education Authority to obtain a suitable site
for a new school in the town of Armagh.
I hope these two objects will meet with
the approval of hon. Members, as the sole
aim is to further the interests of public
education.
The first Clause of the Bill is for the
purpose of clarifying certain statutory
provisions contained in the Leases for
Schools (Ireland) Act of 1881. Under
the provisions of that Act some classes of
trustees and limited owners," such as
tenants for life under family and similar
settlements, were empowered to grant a
lease for school purposes of not more than
،،
one acre of their estate for a term not
exceeding 900 years and at a nominal
rent. It appears, however, according to
the legal advice given to the Ministry of
Education and to its predecessors in office,
the Commissioners of National Education
in Ireland, that that Act did not give
power to such "limited owners" to grant
a similar lease of a site which had a school
already erected on it. The need for such
a requirement was not, of course, greatly
felt prior to the passing of the Education
Act of 1923; but transfers of existing
school buildings to Education Authorities are, however, made possible by this
latter Act, and the difficulty is accordingly now more acute. The Ministry
of Education has also been legally advised
that the provision contained in the 1923
Act, in sub-section (2) of Section 14 which
enables managers of a school to make an
arrangement for its transfer to an Education Authority "notwithstanding any
provision contained in any instrument
regulating the trusts or management of
such schools," does not remedy this defect
in regard to these special cases which we
are considering, namely, of "limited
owners."
There are a number of estates in Northern Ireland held under settlements such
as those mentioned, on which schools were
provided by the generosity of life tenants
and which have since been similarly maintained by their successors. The life
tenants or trustees in several such cases
are now desirous of transferring these
schools to the Local Education Authority
on a long term lease, free of rent or at a
nominal rent. As I have explained, we
are advised that a tenant for life has no
power to do this; he could only grant a
lease for life at an nominal rent, though
he would have power to grant the site on
a long term lease if there were no school
erected on it. The object of the clause is,
therefore, to remove this anomaly. The
longer lease is required by Education
Authorities in the case of all schools where
it may be necessary to effect considerable
extensions of the buildings, or to make
certain improvements, so as to render
them suitable for modern school requirements.
In regard to the second clause of the
Bill, the position is that the Local Education Authority has decided that a new
provided school is urgently required in the
town of Armagh, to replace several existing schools. A great deal of trouble has
School Sites Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
been taken in endeavouring to obtain a
suitable site, and the Authority has come
to the conclusion that the most convenient
and suitable place would be on a portion
of the grounds of the Observatory. This
property is, however, held by Trustees,
under an old Act of the Irish Parliament in the reign of George III, and this
clause of our present Bill is intended to
give them greater powers than those which
they already possess. The original Act
gives no powers to the Governors to grant
leases to any person, and this present
clause is designed to give power to them
to sell or make a grant of a limited portion
of the grounds to the Local Education
Authority. Any proceeds received by the
Governors in respect of the transaction
will be subject to the same trusts and
purposes as were declared in the original
Act and thus the interests of the Observatory will be fully safeguarded. I beg
to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are all
convinced that if the condition of things in
1881, when the Leases for Schools (Ireland) Act was passed, had been as they
are now, we should not have been under
the necessity of passing what is in effect
an Amending Act. I am certain the
Imperial Parliament would have given
the same power to "limited owners" to
transfer a small parcel of land with a
school upon it, as it did give to transfer a
small parcel of land in order that a school
might be put upon it. There cannot, I
imagine, be the smallest objection, but
curiously enough this Bill seems to raise
a small constitutional point which
have not had to consider before. That
1s, our power to legislate in regard to the
rights of the Crown.
It
we
so happens that the very first
"limited owner" mentioned in the Act of
1881 was her late Majesty Queen Victoria.
I imagine the noble Viscount will be able
to tell us at a later stage-I do not want
him to trouble to answer this afternoon---
that in the opinion of the advisers of the
Government, the limitation imposed on
this Parliament as regards the property
of the Crown cannot be construed to
allow the Crown to be put in a position
less favourable than any of its subjects.
That would seem to be a perfect answer,
but I think we ought to have it definitely
from the Government. It may be that
there is no property of the Crown in
Northern Ireland that can possibly be
affected. That I do not know.
As regards the other half of the Bill,
the proposal to take some of the land at
the Observatory, I cannot help thinking
that if the late Most Reverend Richard
Robinson, Primate of All Ireland, were
aware of the present proposal, he would
regard it as fully carrying out the views
which he entertained. I hope it is intended to provide a playground in connection with this school. If not, it may
be that we ought to insert words to provide for that at a later date. I think,
perhaps, it may be well for us to define
carefully the amount of land which may
be taken, because the terms of the old
Act of 1791 are very explicit, and it
might be thought advisable--I do not say
necessary that we should say it is three
statute acres we are giving them power to
take. That, of course, we can consider
later, and it may be we shall hear from
the Senator, who I hope is going to speak
after me (Senator Armstrong), that the
twenty acres provided in the Irish Act are
of the statute character, and not of the
larger kind. That is really a point of
detail, and one only mentions it because
the Committee stage of the Bill is likely to
be taken rather quickly. The Bill, as a
whole, will make for convenience, and I
am sure will be supported
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I think the three
acres provided in this Measure to be taken
by the Education authority are statute
acres, and therefore the quantity of land,
whether it is Irish or English, does not
really very much matter. The site has
been chosen with very great care, and it
is the most admirable site that could be
conceived. The authorities of the Observatory are quite satisfied with the terms.
We had a joint meeting of the two bodies,
and everybody is pleased with the arrangement come to, because the Observatory
will gain as regards its financial position,
which is not very satisfactory, and the
Education Authority will get an excellent
site for its school.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With regard to the question
raised by Senator Leslie, I am not in a
position to answer it at the moment.
However, I will look into the matter and
at a later stage of the Bill I will fully
answer it.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday next.

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Clause 2, page 2, line 8, after "acres"
insert "statute measure."
Mr. LESLIE: The proposal on the
Paper is a very small matter, but I think
it is advisable to insert the two words
"statute measure" after "three acres" in
rder not perhaps so much to define the
kind of acre, but for the sake of conforming with the English Act of 1881, which
is really the principal Act on which this
Bill is based and which is referred to in
the First Clause we have just passed.
Section 4 of that Act when referring to
the acre, defines it as an acre statute
measure. That Act was passed in 1881.
As a matter of fact the acre as we know
it, the acre of 4,840 square yards, depends on the Weights and Measures Act
of 1878, and it so happens that in Ireland,
earlier in the same year, the question
actually came before the Courts as to
whether, under a will, certain lands
could be held to be expressed in Irish
acres. The Court held, on the authority
of the Act of George the Fourth, that it
meant statute acres, and decided that in
any voluntary instrument or in a contract
an acre should be the acre as we know it.
Nevertheless, with that decision in front
of it, the Imperial Parliament, in 1881
thought it necessary to insert these words
“
were.
statute measure." I therefore think it
might be as well to follow their example,
especially as the Act of the Irish Parliament of 1791, which deals with twenty
acres, does not say what sort of acres those
At first sight it might seem rather
doubtful whether they were the modern
acre or the old Irish acre, but I understand from information given to
privately that they were more likely to
be the statute acre than the other, though
the present assessment does not seem to
correspond with either. I think as a
matter of precaution these words should
be inserted.
me
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I am prepared to accept
this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, in Clause 2, page 2, after the
word 'erection" in line 10, to delete the
words to the end of the paragraph (and
maintenance on such land by the said
education authority of a school and other
buildings connected therewith) and to
insert instead thereof the following: -
Provision and maintenance on such
land by such education authority in
accordance with its powers and duties
of
school or schools, and other
buildings and facilities connected
therewith.
a
This Amendment gives a wider definition than that which is on the Order
Paper in the name of Senator Leslie, and
I am much obliged to him for raising the
matter on the Second Reading. I think
it is conceivable that at some future time
another school, such as a Junior Technical
School, might be erected on the same
site, and that at some still more distant
future time it might be thought desirable
to go in for poultry keeping or something
of that kind. In suggesting that I am
not drawing on my imagination, because
I have had an opportunity of looking up
the Rules and Regulations of the Commissioners of National Education of
thirty years ago and I find that grants
were paid for pigs, poultry and other livestock, and what has been may be again.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I ought perhaps to say
that I entirely support the proposal of
the noble Viscount which embodies what
I originally put on the Paper. The intention was to avoid the new power which
was to be given being unduly restricted.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Roads Improvement Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): When the Motor Traffic and
Vehicles Act was before Parliament in
1926, the Minister of Home Affairs stated
that a Roads Improvement Bill would be
a necessary corollary to that Act. That
Act, as the House is aware, dealt with
the regulation of traffic, and particularly
with the regulation of Public Service
Vehicle traffic, but something more than
a mere regulation of traffic was necessary
to deal with the tremendous increase in the
number of vehicles using the roads. and
to render the roads reasonably safe for
traffic.
The
I need not trouble the House with statistics to emphasize the great volume of
traffic which has now to be carried on
roads which were never designed for anything like the number of vehicles now in
use, nor for vehicles of the weight of the
modern motor lorry or omnibus.
expenditure on roads has grown tremendously. In 1913 the expenditure on
roads was £361,093, in 1921 the expenditure had risen to £843,000, while in
1926-27 it amounted to no less than a
sum of £1,411,723, of which £578,726
was contributed by the Government from
the Road Fund, and the remainder found
by local authorities.
In order to fit the roads to carry
modern traffic, it is necessary that the
construction of the roads should be improved, and that in may cases the roads
should be widened and dangerous corners
removed, and further, that every effort
should be made to allow the drivers of
vehicles to have an unobstructed view.
The present Bill aims at facilitating the
attainment of these objects by local
authorities, who are responsible for the
maintenance and construction of the
roads.
The Bill may be divided into two main
divisions. The first Clause and the Schedule are designed to assist local authorities in the work of widening roads and
removing dangerous corners. The remainder of the Bill gives powers to the
local authorities, subject in some cases to
a right of appeal, to enable them to prevent obstructions to view on the roads,
the prescription of building lines and matters of that kind, and also for certain
measures which have for their object the
relief of congestion of traffic.
The first Clause and the Schedule to
the Bill deal with the compulsory acquisition of land for improving or widening
roads and streets, or the construction of
123 Roads Improvement Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
new roads or streets. A very considerable
amount of work in this direction has
already been done by local authorities.
They have acquired, by agreement, land
for widening roads and cutting off dangerous corners. I am glad to say that in
the majority of cases land-owners have
been reasonable, and local authorities
have been able to acquire the necessary
Jand at a reasonable price, or in some instances gratuitously, or at much below
its real value.
In Belfast County Borough considerable schemes of widening have been undertaken under the authority of a local Act
of Parliament. There are compulsory
powers for the acquisition of land for road
widening purposes already in existence.
Section 10 of the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898, provides a procedure,
and a procedure was also provided by the
Development and Roads Improvement
Funds Act of 1909, and it may now be
asked why it is necessary to provide a
further procedure. The answer is that
the existing procedures are too cumbersome and expensive, and the cost of acquiring the land under them and of
making title to it, is out of proportion to
the value of the land to be acquired,
especially in the case where only small
pieces are required.
I think it will not be disputed that before there is expropriation of private
rights two things are necessary; first, that
it should be shown that it is essential in
the public interest that the rights of the
individual should be transferred to the
local authority, and that it is reasonable
that they should be so transferred. For
instance, if a local authority wish to take
a piece of land from a man for a public
purpose he may be able to show that the
damage to him, apart from any reasonable
money compensation, may be so great as
to be out of proportion to any benefit that
would accrue to the public, or that there
is other land which could be taken with
less injustice, and which would be equally
suitable for the purpose. There is, therefore, the question of whether the land
should be taken at all or not, and, in the
second place, if it should be taken, what
compensation should be awarded. Keeping these two matters in view, the provisions of this Bill aim at providing a cheap
and speedy method of acquiring land for
public purposes, whilst, at the same time,
safeguarding the legitimate rights of individuals.
It has never been found to be an easy
matter to devise a method for the compulsory acquisition of land which will
satisfy these conditions. Various schemes
for the purpose have been embodied in
Acts of Parliament, and the present
scheme is an attempt to embody in one
scheme the most suitable features of those
previously devised. It has been criticized
as being unnecessarily cumbersome and
expensive. A good deal of the criticizm
has been based on a misapprehension of
the proposal and of the practical conditions under which it has to be worked,
and no alternative scheme which would
be cheaper or more expeditious, and
would be, at the same time, practicable,
has been suggested. It has been suggested that if a local authority wishes to
take a small piece of land from the corner
of a field in order to remove a dangerous
corner in
road and has to put this
machinery into operation, the cost of
acquiring this particular piece of land
would be out of proportion to the probable
value, which might not be more than
£20 or £30, and it may be admitted at
once that this is so, but it may be asserted
that it would be equally so in the case of
any compulsory procedure which gave any
safeguards to the individual concerned.
a
No reasonable local authority would
proceed on these lines any more than a
rural district council would formulate a
scheme for the compulsory acquisition of
a single plot on which to build a
labourer's cottage. Surely what a local
authority would do would be to look
ahead for say three years, and make up
their minds what widenings or cutting of
corners they proposed to do in that time,
and make their scheme accordingly. You
would then have a number of cases which
could be dealt with in one scheme, one
set of advertisements, one inquiry if an
inquiry proves to be necessary and one
Order. If this is done, and it is the
reasonable course, what may be called the
overhead charges of the scheme would
not, when spread over the total number
of the separate items of the scheme, be
out of proportion. There is just one
other general observation which I wish
to make upon this scheme, and it is that
no part of the scheme or procedure is
without precedent. Perhaps I might
now give a sketch of the procedure for
the information of the House. When
the local authority makes the scheme,
showing what lands they wish to acquire,
| they give notice by public advertisement,
