One of the difficulties in connection
with the existing schemes for compulsory
acquisition is the great delay which takes
place between the time of the decision
that the land shall be compulsory
acquired and the time that the local
authority gets possession, and in order to
get over this difficulty it is provided that
the Order of the Ministry confirming the
scheme shall vest the land in the local
authority from the date of the Order, or
such other date as may be specified.
effect of this will be to give to the local
authority a statutory title in the land
itself, and to transfer to the compensation
payable in respect of the land all the interests which previously were in the land.
This will enable the local authority to proceed expeditiously with their works without waiting for proof of title to the various
interests in the land, which is often a
somewhat lengthy business.
The
Having taken the land it is necessary
that the owner should be compensated, and
the Bill provides that compensation is to
be fixed by an arbitrator in the absence
of an agreement. Until the money is
actually paid over it is to bear interest
at the rate of five per cent. The Schedule
also provides that in determining the
amount of any compensation the arbitrator
is to have regard to the extent to which
other lands of the owner may be benefited
by the proposed work or road, and it also
makes provision that compensation is not
to be awarded in respect of any alteration
or improvement made in the land after
the service of the notice by the local
authority.
The provisions as to compensation incorporate the provisions of the Second Schedule of the Housing of the Working
Classes Act, 1890—with the exception
of certain articles as amended by the
Acquisition of Land (Assessment of
Compensation) Act, 1919, and this includes one provision to which I would
like to draw attention. If the local
authority, after the making of the Compulsory Order, makes an offer of compensation, which is refused, and the arbitrator
does not award a greater sum than was
offered he may direct that the costs of the
arbitration be borne by those who refused
the offer. This is a reasonable protection
against local authorities being put to unnecessary expense by an unreasonable
attitude on the part of the owner of the
land.
The compensation having been fixed, it
will be necessary for the owners to show
their title to receive the compensation in
the same way as if they were selling the
land, but I am sure the House is aware
that the making of title to land is often
an expensive matter, and following the
precedent laid down in the Labourers Acts
special provisions are proposed in order to
obviate expense where the money involved
is small. This special procedure will apply
to cases under £100, and it authorizes the
payment of the compensation where the
claimant makes a statutory declaration
stating whether he claims as absolute or
limited owner, and gives prima facie evidence which satisfies the local authority
that for not less than six years immediately
preceding he or his immediate predecessor
in title has been in receipt of the rents or
profits of the land in respect of which the
compensation is payable or in actual
operation thereof.
This is a very simple procedure and saves
a vast amount of expense. It is perfectly
true that it involves a certain amount of
risk, because if someone else comes along
within six years and proves that he and
not the person who was paid, was entitled
to the compensation, the local authority
will have to pay over again, but an experience extending over more than twenty
years of the working of the Labourers Acts
has shown that the risk is negligible and
that the saving is great.
Provision is also made for payment into
Court where no one can make satisfactory
title to the money, and for this purpose
the jurisdiction of the County Court is
extended to a sum of £1,000. Amounts
over that sum will have to be paid into
the High Court. Provision is made for
an appeal against the arbitrator's award
if the amount exceeds £100.
127 Roads Improvement Bill—

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
Coming now to the other part of the
Bill, I do not think it will be necessary
for me to dwell upon it at any great
length. Clause 2 deals with obstructions
to view at corners, and enables the local
authority to require an owner or occupier
of the land to alter the height or character
of any wall, fence or hedge, which includes,
by virtue of the definition Section, a hoarding or paling, and also a tree or shrub,
even although they do not form part of
the hedge. If the owner considers that
the requirements of the notice are unreasonable he may appeal either to an
arbitrator appointed jointly by the owner
and the local authority or, if they cannot
agree upon an arbitrator, he may appeal
to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
The
person may either do the work himself
and recover the cost from the local authority or he may require the local authority
to do it themselves.
The Clause also enables a local authority to serve a notice on an owner,
occupier, or lessee of land, restraining
them either absolutely or subject to such
conditions as may be specified in the
notice, from permitting any building,
wall, fence or hedge to be erected or
planted on the land where it would cause
danger by obstructing the view of persons
using the road. Similar rights of appeal
are given as in the first case.
The next Clause empowers local authorities to require any hedge or tree which
overhangs the road so as to obstruct or
interfere with the light from any public
lamp, or to endanger or obstruct the
passage of vehicles or foot passengers, or
to obstruct the view of drivers of vehicles,
to be cut or lopped by the owner within
21 days, or in case of default they may
go in and do the work themselves, recovering the cost in the County Court
from the owner or occupier on whom the
notice was served. A right of appeal
against the requirements of the notice is
given to a Court of Summary Jurisdiction,
Clause 4 enables local authorities to
prescribe building lines on any road maintainable by them, after giving due notice
to the persons interested. It enables any
party whose property has been injuriously
affected to claim compensation, to be fixed
by arbitration, but it further provides
that in fixing the compensation, there
shall be taken into account any benefits
accruing to the person to whom compensation is payable by reason of any improvement made, or about to be made, in
the road. The case of the fixing of a
building line on a road running into the
areas of two adjoining local authorities is
also dealt with. It is obviously desirable
that a common building line should be
prescribed, and if the two authorities are
unable to agree, the matter is to be settled
by the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Clause 5 deals with a matter which has
given rise to some controversy.
Under
Section 7 of the Roads Act of 1920, a
local authority may apply to the Ministry
of Home Affairs for an Order closing any
road against particular types of traffic.
The grounds on which the Ministry may
exercise this power are that a particular
highway cannot be used by that type of
vehicle without endangering the safety
of the vehicle or the persons therein, or
of other traffic using the highway, or that
the highway is unsuitable for use by a
vehicle of that class.
As I have already pointed out, local
authorities have had to incur greatly increased expenditure to maintain the
roads as a result of the increase of traffic.
The burden on the rates has become so
great that it is essential to ensure that it
is not increased as a result of unreasonable
use of the roads by heavy vehicles. In
Northern Ireland there is a great network of roads which fall within the category of third-class roads, and the maintenance of these roads is a great burden
on local rates. Many of these roads are
quite unsuitable for carrying heavy traffic,
and it would place an intolerable burden
upon the ratepayers to fit them for the
purpose; neither would any useful purpose be served by doing so.
Where a
district is served by several third-class
roads, running more or less parallel, some
of which are fit to carry heavy traffic and
some of which are not, it is reasonable
that heavy traffic, or at least heavy
through traffic, should be confined to the
suitable roads, and not allowed to use
the unsuitable roads so as to damage them
to such an extent as to put the ratepayers to great additional expense in repairing them.
Under the existing law a local inquiry
is necessary before any road can be closed.
This must cause a certain amount of delay
129 Roads Improvement Bill-

25 APRIL 1928
Second Reading

h

is

D
Del
t
م


and in the meantime the road may have
heen irreparably damaged. The Ministry
of Home Affairs have been urged by
jocal authorities to empower them to
close any road against any class of traffic,
but the Minister has not felt himself able
to submit to Parliament proposals of such
a drastic nature.
He is, however, of
opinion that local authorities have made
a reasonable case for some alteration in
the present procedure, and, accordingly,
it is proposed to empower local authorities to close any third-class road to any
particular class of vehicle, if it appears
to them that the road is unsuitable for
use by a vehicle of that class, and would
be damaged if used by such vehicle. It
will be observed that the power thus
given is narrower than that given to the
Ministry by the Roads Act of 1920. It
will be a condition precedent to the
closing of any road by the local authority that undue damage would be caused
to the road.
As regards first- and second-class roads,
it is proposed to enable local authorities
to close any specified road of these classes
against vehicles not entirely fitted with
pneumatic tyres if undue damage would
be done to the road. The reason for this
distinction is that, with regard to roads
of these classes, vehicles fitted with pneumatic tyres will not damage them so
quickly as to render it essential that
immediate action should be taken. On
the other hand, vehicles fitted with solid
tyres may do so. In all cases where the
local authorities exercise these powers a
right of appeal on the part of any person aggrieved to the Ministry is given.
The Ministry's powers under the
Roads Acts of 1920 remain unimpaired. Great damage has been
done to the roads by the unreasonable
use of them by owners of vehicles, and
great expense has been caused to ratepayers. An attempt was made to deal
with this matter by the Public Roads Act
of 1911, but unfortunately this Act has
proved ineffective for the purpose.
Fears have been expressed that local
authorities will use the powers given to
them by this Clause unreasonably, particularly with regard to motor omnibuses,
but the Government is of opinion that
these fears are groundless.
The local
authorities are particularly susceptible to
local opinion, and it is extremely unlikely
that they would use such powers in a way
not in accordance with local opinion. If
an omnibus service is serving the needs
of a district and the closing of a particular
road against such vehicles would interfere
in such a way as to prevent that district
being served it is in the highest degree
unlikely that a local authority would interfere. On the other hand, it may be that
the service could be quite well carried on
by vehicles of a lighter type without unduly damaging the roads, and it is but
reasonable that the local authority who
have to maintain the road should be enabled to require the use of the lighter type
of vehicle. In any event the right of
appeal protects the legitimate interests of
vehicle owners.
Clause 6 enables local authorities to provide parking places, where it is necessary
for the purpose of relieving or preventing
congestion of traffic within a county
borough or other urban district, and, if
necessary, to acquire land for the purpose,
or they may appropriate any part of a
street within the district for use as a parking place provided that they do not interfere unreasonably with the right of access
to any premises adjoining the street or the
use of the street by any person entitled
to use it, or so as to be a nuisance.
In
case any person is aggrieved by the proposal of the local authority they may
appeal to the Ministry of Home Affairs.
In
There can hardly be any dispute as to
the necessity for some provision of this
kind.
Hon. Members will, I am sure,
have noticed the great inconvenience and
congestion caused by cars standing in the
streets not only in large towns but in the
smaller towns on market days, fair days
and other special occasions. In certain places the local authority has
assigned places for parking in the
streets, and although this is a convenient arrangement it has been done
without any statutory authority.
many of our towns the streets are narrow,
and there is no suitable place on the streets
where a parking place can be assigned,
and therefore it is necessary to give local
authorities the power to acquire land
specially for the purpose. Some fears have
been expressed lest the powers given by
this Clause should enable local authorities
to develop into garage owners, but these
fears are groundless. The Clause merely
gives power to provide a place where
vehicles may stand. It does not enable
the local authority to provide and sell
motor requisites or petrol or anything of
C
131 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
that kind, and a local authority cannot
carry on such a business without express
statutory authority.
were
In-
On
The next Clause deals with a matter on
which there has been a very considerable
difference of opinion, namely, the question
whether petrol pumps should be allowed
to be placed on the public streets.
quiries which
instituted by the
Ministry of Home Affairs amongst the
various local authorities have shown that
on this matter there is a great diversity
of opinion. Some local authorities are in
favour of it and have actually allowed the
erection of petrol pumps although there is
no statutory authority for doing so.
the other hand, other local authorities are
strongly opposed to any such permission
being given. The Government feel that
this is a matter which should be decided
by each local authority for itself, and
therefore it is proposed under this Clause
that the local authority may, if they think
fit, grant licences for the erection of pumps
within their own area. In coming to a
decision the local authority will, no doubt,
consider whether the special conditions of
their area make this desirable, whether it
will convenience the motoring public or
not, and in fact take all the relevant considerations into account. The Clause enables the local authority to charge a fee
for these licences by means of by-laws and
to impose penalties for the erection of
pumps without
licence under this
Section.
a
Clause & removes the limit of £1 imposed by the Grand Jury Act of 1836, as
adapted, on expenditure by county councils on such things as milestones, direction posts and so forth, and extends to
urban districts the powers of county councils in this matter. It also gives power
to the local authorities to set up mirrors
at dangerous corners, crossings or other
places on roads, and enables the Ministry
of Home Affairs to contribute to the cost
of the erection of signposts, mirrors and
so on. in the same way that they can contribute to the cost of the maintenance of
the road. These mirrors have been
found to lessen, to a very considerable degree, the danger to traffic at crossroads,
as they enable the drivers of vehicles approaching a crossroad to see the traffic
approaching on the other roads, and it is
hoped that local authorities will make
use of these mirrors at suitable places.
Clause 9 requires the consent of the
local authority before anyone places any
overhead rail, beam, pipe, wire or other
similar apparatus over, along or across
any road or street, and enables the local
authority, in giving such consent, to im.
pose conditions. The consent must not
be unreasonably withheld, and any question as to whether the consent has been
unreasonably withheld, or the conditions
unreasonable, is to be determined by the
Ministry of Home Affairs.
I need not refer in detail to the next
two Clauses, as they speak for themselves.
They impose on the owner of property
the duty of preventing surface water from
their premises flowing on footpaths, and
preventing soil, etc., from being washed
into streets.
Clause 12 provides that projections in
front of houses or buildings which are insecurely fixed or of defective construction, or otherwise a source of danger to
persons lawfully using the street, are to
be deemed to be obstructions.
One of the most pressing problems in
connection with road construction is the
testing of new materials designed to stand
up to modern traffic conditions. Such
methods of construction as reinforced
concrete, asphalt, and so on, are very expensive, but naturally local authorities
are reluctant to go to the expense of experimenting with untried materials, and
prefer to stick to the present methods,
which have been found to be durable,
even although they may be expensive.
If, however, the progress in the reconstruction of roads is to be accelerated
within the financial resources available,
it is necessary that less expensive methods
of construction should be found, especially for roads which are not subjected to
the heaviest forms of traffic, and, accordingly, by Clause 13, it is proposed that the
Ministry of Home Affairs may, either by
itself, or through any authority or
organization approved by the Ministry,
conduct experiments or trials for the improvement of the construction of roads, or
for testing the effect of various classes of
vehicles on various types of roads, and
may incur such expenditure as may be
necessary for the purpose, subject to the
approval of the Ministry of Finance. I
would like to emphasize the part of the
Clause which provides that no experiment
133 Roads Improvement Bill-

25 APRIL 1928
Second Reading


or trial under this Section shall be conducted on any road, except with the consent of the local authority responsible for
the maintenance of the road.
It has been suggested that such experiments should only be conducted through
local authorities, but this suggestion cannot be acceded to. Very valuable experiments are carried out through such
organizations as the Royal Automobile
Club, and the Ministry might well desire
to use the services of organizations of this
kind; or, again, a particular firm may
have a process which the Ministry desire
to test, and it would obviously be desirable to have the work of laying the piece
of road for testing purposes done by the
firm who are experts in the matter. As
I have already pointed out, local authorities are fully protected by the necessity
for obtaining their consent to the use of
the roads under their charge for the purpose of these experiments. The remaining Clauses of the Bill are of a consequential and formal character, and I need
not trouble the House by going through
them in detail.
In conclusion, I would like to take this
opportunity of expressing, on behalf of
the Government, their appreciation of the
assistance which we have received in the
preparation of this Bill from the local
authorities who have been consulted by
the Ministry of Home Affairs from time
to time on the provisions of the Bill.
Many suggestions from local sources have
been embodied in the Bill. Other suggestions have been made which the Minister has not found himself able to agree
to, but these were mainly of a minor
character. In the main, the Bill meets
the wishes and requirements of the local
authorities, and is an earnest attempt to
deal with various matters in connection
with the roads, which experience has
shown to be of pressing importance.
beg to move.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I desire as much as anybody else the improvement and the safety
of our thoroughfares, and I appreciate the
eloquence of the speech to which we have
just listened, although perhaps I was not
able always to follow the statistics given
to us or appreciate the arguments which
were used. The Bill which was the subject
of the speech of the noble Viscount is a
very complicated one. It appears to me to
super-impose itself on a variety of existing
codes. There are matters in this Bill
which are dealt with in our Public Health
Acts, in our Towns Improvement Acts, in
our Local Government Act, and in our
Summary Jurisdiction Act. I could have
wished for a simpler document, or even for
a set of separate Bills. However, we must
take the Bill or leave the Bill as it comes
before us. It is undoubtedly a very important and a very far-reaching Bill.
It
contains some novel features, or at all
events features which are novel in Northern Ireland.
I do not say that that is
necessarily an objection.
The Bill is, as I say, a far-reaching Bill.
Sometimes I think it goes too far.
Sometimes, perhaps, it does not go far enough.
I recognize that it is an enabling Bill, and
that we shall have in the future as in the
past the protection of our county councils,
who will probably be able to administer
this Bill, if it becomes an Act, with
economy and with commonsense.
in spite of that protection I am not
satisfied that this Bill is what the
public interest in Northern Ireland really
requires at the present time. Therefore, for the convenience of the debate at
all events, I beg to move, "that the word
But
now' in the proposal of the noble Viscount be left out," and that at the end of
his Motion be added the words, "upon this
day six months." In moving that Amendment I do not desire that any misapprehension should arise. I do not desire that
the Amendment should be taken as implying an uncompromising hostility and a
permanent hostility against every proposal in the Measure.
I
That is not what I intend, but I do
think that perhaps that Amendment will
absolve me from dilating on the merits
that I have observed in the Bill, and although I do propose to subject myself to
that self-denying ordinance I cannot do so
so far as to refrain from congratulating
the Government and the Ministry of Home
Affairs on the change of front which
appears to be exhibited in Clause 7, permitting the erection of petrol pumps.
seem to remember not so very long ago
(See Debates, 1923, col. 378) bringing
that matter forward in this House,
and I seem to remember that it was
somewhat summarily turned down. But
the other merits of the Bill, parking
places and so on, I propose to leave, with
confidence, to other speakers, and I will
endeavour to address myself to what seem
to me to be the demerits of the Measure.
I am afraid I must make some demand
on the time of the House, but I will endeavour, so far as I can, to avoid points
135 Roads Improvement Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

[Mr. Leslie].
of detail. If I should inadvertently transgress I must plead in extenuation that the
Title is extremely wide--unusually wide,
perhaps that the Bill contains 21 Clauses
as well as a Schedule, and that there are
at least ten or a dozen of those Clauses
which might have formed the subject of
separate Measures.
Before I go on to deal with the new
features and the shortcomings and so on
of the Bill I should like to say a few words
as regards the problem presented to-day
in Northern Ireland, and how the problem
in Northern Ireland, as it seems to me,
differs somewhat from the problem
exhibited in Great Britain. The root of
the problem is, as we have heard, and as
we all know, the growth of motor traffic
on our roads. That is the root of the
problem. There are two branches I want
to speak of. The first is the number of
accidents that occur in connection with
motor traffic or that are caused by motor
traffic, and the second is the liability which
is cast directly on our County Councils
and indirectly on our ratepayers of
constructing, repairing and mantaining
roads suitable to carry and I think
the noble Viscount referred to thatthat modern traffic, although they
originally designed for traffic
of a wholly different character. These
motors are, of course, mechanicallypropelled vehicles, and the moment we use
that phrase we imply both speed and
weight. I think we need not go further
into that.
were
There is a point to which I should like
to draw attention, because I am afraid it is
not always appreciated. In the old days
of horse-driven traffic, when horses did
perhaps nine miles an hour, very often
less, rarely more, the driver was usually
perched on a box. He was high up above
the road, and he had opportunities of
vision entirely denied to the driver of a
motor car or to the driver of a motor omnibus, although not always to the driver of a
motor lorry. We have not only increased
the speed and weight of vehicles using our
country roads, but the drivers are now
put in a position where they have not the
same opportunities of seeing what is before them, or what may come before them,
as the driver of older times. I think that
is an important matter which is not always
remembered.
Now, as to these accidents. An enterprising London newsaper a few days ago
published some percentages, which it had
caused to be worked out, dealing with
617 cases of fatalities. According to
that figure 36 per cent. of the fatalities
were due, in some way or other, to the
driver, but only 4.3 per cent. of those
fatalities were due to want of caution at
road junctions. That is, I think, a
rather remarkable figure which we ought
to bear in mind. The carelessness of
pedestrians seems to have caused 27 per
cent. of the fatalities, but that is not before us to-day, nor need we deal with the
question of road damage, although it may
have to be referred to in a few moments.
I come now to the difference in the problem in Great Britain and Northern Ireland. First of all, in Northern Ireland
many of our roads are based on bog
foundations. In that respect we have a
difficulty which they have not got in
Great Britain. We cannot give such
roads a good foundation. Secondly, we
have at present considerably less traffic
than they have in Great Britain.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What about the Scotch roads?
Mr. LESLIE: I am comparing our
roads with the English roads, because I
know the English roads better than the
Scottish roads. We have less traffic, and
we have no speed limit at present for our
private cars. I think, both in England
and Scotland, the twenty miles speed
limit still continues. In Northern Ireland we allow our roads to be used for
motor races. Of course, I do not say
that motor races are allowed to take
place except under very proper restrictions, and I do not make any complaint of
the fact that we are able to have races on
our roads, nor do I suggest that the driver
of a motor car, merely because the speed
limit of twenty miles has been removed,
is entitled to go at any pace he likes
That is not the case. He has not got
that power, and he never had. But I
do suggest that, when we are considering
whether we should adopt here sections of
Acts of Parliament passed at Westminster
for Great Britain, we ought to take these
matters into our consideration.
Now, as regards novel features in the
Bill, the first of them, as it seems to me,
is the suggestion that there is a duty upon
the owners of the lands adjacent to the
roads so to use their property as to allow

people to use that road for vehicles driven
pace which was never contemplated when these adjacent owners, or
their predecessors, gave the land. That
seems to me quite a new idea.
always has been some duty to the road.
We have always been required to do
various things in the way of scouring
the ditches, but this is something, I think,
beyond anything that has gone before.
As the second novel feature, there appears to be a lack of appreciation of the
primary purpose for which fences exist.
Fences, after all, exist mainly for four
purposes. First, the owner of the land
enclosed by a fence or fences desires to
retain his own cattle or beasts on his
land. Secondly, he desires to exclude
trespassers human, canine and otherwise.
Thirdly, he wants the fences to serve as
shelter for his cattle; and fourthly, to
keep the dust of the roads from his fields
These purposes are served in different
localities in different ways. One man
requires fences higher than another, but
taking people all round, they do not desire to maintain unduly high fences, for
the simple reason that the ordinary high
hedge, as soon as it gets too high, fails at
the bottom and has to be cut down, in
order that a close fence may be obtained.
The fact remains that the man wants proper fences.
The underlying idea-I have spoken
particularly of hedges the underlying
suggestion seems to me that it is possible,
by the operation of orders issued under
this Act, to render every corner safe.
That is to say, by cutting down fences,
lowering walls, banks, and so on, irrespective of what sort of fences the owner
of the land requires. When you have
done all that, you can open up the roads
and let everybody go as fast as they like
down one road and across another. That,
I think, is a mistake. I very gravely
doubt whether it is desirable to go too
far. There is a happy medium.
doubt if it is desirable to go too far in
the way of removing every inducement
to caution. There are corners, and I
think there will always be corners, due
to the configuration of the land, quite
irrespective of the hedge or wall, which
will prevent getting a clear view of cross
traffic. In these cases it is often very
doubtful whether you ought to try to do
something which will produce a false
sense of security.
I

And again there seems to me to be a
suggestion that is perhaps far fetched, and
it is best that it is. Some of us remember
when the Budget which first imposed taxes
on motorists was introduced it was suggested that the proceeds of these taxes
would be used for producing new roads
of indeterminate width and of special construction, and of all these roads motorists
would come into possession without any
speed limit. I forgot what was to happen
at cross-roads. But there seems to be a suggestion here that as that promise or
suggestion has never been implemented by
the Government it is to be implemented
in Northern Ireland by a combination of
the local authorities and the adjacent landowners.
There is yet another novel feature which
I do not like, and that is that the Ministry
of Home Affairs is to settle certain matters
that hitherto have been left to courts of
law
It appears to be thought that it is
for the Ministry to decide as to the reasonableness or unreasonableness of the attitude
of this or that person or local body. I shall
refer to this matter again later on. I pass
for the moment to what seems to me to be
certain shortcomings in the matter. The
first one of all arises from the first Clause
of the Bill. It is the question of footpaths. I know what a street is. When I
am in England I have heard of a highway.
But what exactly is a road? Does it or
does it not include a footpath? I cannot
satisfy myself that in the Clause of this
Bill which deals with the widening of
roads, there is any provision for a footpath on which we can rely, and I cannot
see myself any greater safeguard for the
pedestrian than the provision of
footpath.
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
a
Mr. LESLIE: And following as a second
difficulty in this same first Clause-really
if there is any one Clause in the Bill more
than another that is difficult it is this
first Clause-I cannot find in this-the
widening Clause, where further provision
is to be made in respect of the compulsory
acquisition of land either for roads or
streets-I cannot find in the ambit of the
new vesting order which it is proposed to
institute, any provision for obtaining simply that right to use land for the purposes
of roads which is of the nature of an easement. And I cannot find any power to
apply such vesting order to those privileges which are undoubtedly easements and
which are very often required in connec-
139 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie].
tion with roads. The new plan so far as
it includes the vesting order does not much
matter. As far as that vesting order will
be cheaper or quicker than the old process
there can, I think, be no objection to it.
I do not think that the whole method
suggested by the Clause will be found
materially cheaper or materially quicker.
I have consulted one or two friends of
mine who have personal knowledge of
county council work, and the information
I have got is that they have no great hope
of this method either accelerating or
cheapening procedure.
It has often been said, and I am afraid
it is too true, that when a few inches of
land are required for widening a road,
an undue price is asked. Well, if it was
asked, it was not paid. There were powers
which could be put into force that this
Clause does not take away. They got
that land compulsorily and at a fair price.
But that will not be altered. You will
not get your land ny cheaper this
way
than the other, that is, if the arbitrator
does his duty. And as the arbitrator is,
I am glad to see, to be one of a panel of
arbitrators he is not to be an official of
the Ministry-I think there is no doubt
a fair price will be paid. There is no
saving there. There is very little saving,
it seems to me, in the case of conveyance.
The man has got to make title. You can
take the land and get on with the work.
The money has to be paid, and the man
whose land it was has to make title to
it. The cost of making title to that fund
is likely to be just as great as the cost of
making title to the land of which it was
part. This cost has got to be borne by
the local authority.
I cannot see that there is any great
saving either in time or in money. And
there is this objection that this new plan
of taking compulsorily is without appeal
to a Court. Under the Section which the
noble Viscount quoted-I think it is
Section 10, Sub-section 3 of the Local
Government Act-appeal lay-in fact
it still lies to the Court and not
to the Ministry.
a matter
which,
satisrule, gives some
But the new plan will, as I
faction.
think, antagonize the landowner. A prudent landowner would be bound to oppose
a proposal to take from him the bed and
soil of the land required for making a
new road, or widening of an old road, beas
a
That is
cause the effect of that would undoubtedly
be to give an island to extraneous hands
between one part of the man's property
and another, because of the fact that a man
dedicated portion of his land to the use
of the public to pass and repass along,
whether it be an easement or whether it
be not--one judge, in 1795, said definitely
it was, and another learned judge in, I
think, 1868 said definitely it was notat all events, everybody agrees that the
right of passage is something in the
nature of an easement, and that easement, if I may use the word, does not
take away the property of the man in the
bed and soil of his road.
Now, taking into account that the property in the road is still of great value to
the landowner, if you try to take land and
make an island, you arouse antagonism,
and do great injustice. Also I do not
know if this has been pointed out before
----if you do that with a view to increasing
the safety of the public, you are in fact
doing something which is introducing a
new danger to the public. That is undoubtedly the case. The House will see
that it must be so. You are taking from
a man his right to this little piece of land
at the corner of the road. With that
ownership goes the right of prosecution
for trespass thereon. There is such a
thing as trespass on a county road. The
use of the road which goes beyond the
right to pass and repass is trespass, and
that can be restrained by the owner of
the soil. It has been restrained again
and again in certain circumstances. Ii
you carry out this matter. the right to
that little bit of land at the corner would
be in the local authority, and there will
be no authority to prevent the assembly
of people at the corners; and it is the
assembly of people that contributes
materially to accidents happening at
these corners. Incidentally, this method
would be a very great breach of faith,
because the condition on which the land
was originally given by the man's predecessor was that he should retain the
ownership.
I pass from that to say that I cannot
see any reason why this question, if it
is to be introduced at all, should not be
confined to property taken by agreement,
and why that property should not include easements and rights over land
which are in the nature of easements.
cannot see that the difficulties which ]

141 Roads Improvement Bill—

understand have been suggested as to the
Registration of Title Act, 1891, are by
any means insuperable. After all, we
have to remember that the Registration
of Title Act was passed in 1891, as I have
said. In 1898 you had the Local Government Act, and in 1924 you had, in Northern Ireland, the Constabulary (Acquisition of Land) Act. Both these last Acts
dealt with easements and rights in relation
to roads. I have said I think that there is
no cheapening or acceleration of procedure.
I pause to draw attention to the regrettable fact that the opportunity has not
been taken to give something in the
nature of statutory recognition to what
is called the white line, but I must not
dwell on that to-dav.
That
That
There is a far more serious shortcoming,
and it is this shortcoming that I think
has produced the most grievous disappointment on the part of those acting in
the interests of county councils.
is. that this Bill does not attempt any
modification of the Act of 1911.
is an Act which enables local authorities,
under certain conditions, to obtain compensation for unreasonable and excessive
damage to roads. It is a matter which
has been very much before the public of
late owing to the difficulty which the
County of Down was under, owing, if I
remember rightly, to the transport by
mechanically-propelled vehicles of large
quantities of stone for Stormont, for use
in connection with various public buildings which it is proposed to erect there.
The Down County Council found themselves saddled with an enormous expense
to restore a road, and there was considerable litigation, which was a further expense. Ultimately, I believe, the county
council recovered something like
tenth of the amount they had to spene,
and their position was so serious that
Parliament had to step in through the
appropriate Department of the Government, and a sum of, I think, £3,000 was
paid to the Down County Council by way
of compensation.
one-
The county councils have again and
again brought forward this matter, and
they did expect this Bill would deal with
it. I think there was something almost
in the nature of a promise that it should,
but the Bill does nothing. Although I
must admit that the problem is one of
great difficulty, I cannot but regret that
it has not been dealt with in the Bill,
more so perhaps as, in the event of the
Measure being read a Second time and
passing to the Committee stage, I am very
much inclined to wonder, Mr. Speaker,
whether you will allow a Motion in Committee to insert a Clause dealing with
what I have just indicated. I am much
afraid you would feel constrained to say
it did not come within the compass of the
Bill. I am not sure whether it will be
possible to bring up such a Clause, but
at all events I intend to try. What has
happened in Down may, and I am very
much afraid will, happen very shortly
in a more northern county. There are
difficulties, I know, and indeed the noble
Viscount mentioned them, and he did
draw our attention to a Clause in the
Bill which admittedly does go some way.
He reminded us of what was done in
1920, but I am afraid the suggestion will
not be accepted in this matter by the
county councils as the road authority responsible for the roads out of the pockets
of the ratepayers.
Passing on to another Clause, where
more protection is to be given in towns
against obstruction by projection, I must
regret that nothing has been done for our
hamlets. I understand it was thought
there were some difficulties in defining a
village, but there are places of some size,
which would normally be called villages
in England, such as Bushmills. That is
not a town for local government purposes,
it is not an urban district, and it is not
under the Towns Improvement Act, but
surely it would be possible to do something for a place like that.
Again, as another shortcoming, I think
County Councils have been occasionally
unduly restricted. I shall take only one
instance, the one in Clause 3. Clause 3
is the Clause which deals with the monstrosity of an overhanging hedge. Such
a thing ought not to exist, and unless the
owner of the hedge is a very unreasonable
person it generally does not exist. At
present, if any action is to be taken and
again the County Council has got to issue
its Order either itself or through one of
its committees it cannot do it as it has
hitherto done, namely, merely through the
county surveyor. That is surely an unnecessary restriction which will not make
for speed or economy.
Now I have done with the shortcomings
of the Bill, and I will go on to what I call
the undue pretensions of the Measure, that
Roads Improvement Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
is, the cases where I think the Bill goes
too far. I must first draw attention, not
for the first time, to what I would call the
magnification of the Ministry of Home
Affairs. I shall take only three instances.
The magnification I object to is the
attempt to substitute the Ministry for the
Courts of Law. That is a procedure of
comparatively modern creation. It grew
very much during the late war; it has
increased, it is increasing, and I think it
ought to be diminished. Every time that
a proposal to substitute a Ministry for a
Court of Law comes before Parliament I
think this House ought to object and consider very carefully whether the matter
at issue is so trivial as to make the principle of no great consequence.
I shall take only three instances of this.
The first I can deal with very briefly. It
is Clause 1. Under Clause 1 and the
Schedule the Ministry provides for an
appeal against itself to itself. I think
that is almost ludicrous. In Clause 2 of
the Bill is produced what I cannot describe as otherwise than an egregious
blunder. It is, of course, a blunder due
to the policy; it is not a matter of drafting. It has nothing to do with the
drafting of the Bill. It is a matter of
policy, and what has been done is this, as
the House will see if they will read the
three and a half pages of Clause 2. Section
4 of the English Roads Improvement Act,
1925, has been taken practically verbatim
and incorporated in this Bill; but the
blunder is this: As the Section appears
in the English Act it is left for interpretation, if the need should unfortunately
arise, to the County Court. This English
Section, which bristles with words and
phrases which have been before the Courts
and which may again at any time be before
them, this Section deliberately prepared in
England and committed in England to
the Courts for interpretation has been
taken with all those words and phrases
in it and put into this Bill, and its interpretation in the last resort is left to the
Ministry of Home Affairs. There is no
defence of that that I can see possible.
In another Clause that deals with building lines it is apparently possible for the
Ministry, by means of a kind of Housethat-Jack-built process, to get control over
the building lines on every rural road in
Northern Ireland. I do not think that that
is right or that it is desirable. I do not
think that the public as a whole desires
that the Ministry should control a building
line in any section of road against the
will of the County Council. They prefer
the County Council to the Ministry. They
do not desire that service should develop
into mastery. The public as a wholeand this applies to all instances I have
given prefers the authority elected, or,
if it is not a matter for election, they
prefer the Courts of their country to the
Ministry or Department, if only because
they know perfectly well that the expression Ministry does not mean the Minister.
We know that things are done by the
Ministry that could not possibly be done
by the Minister himself. The expression
،،
Ministry" usually means some particular man. After a time it gets known
who that man is. I do not think that
particular man's life is altogether pleasant
in that case, and I do not think he is to
be envied his task of dealing in the
Ministry with everything that comes
before him from local authorities, but the
public, to put it shortly, prefer the County
Council or the Court to the officials of the
Ministry.
a
Again, I think this Bill raises expectations which cannot be realized. We
have no money available for it.
That
really brings me back again, for
moment, to the building line, because I
do not think there is any demand at the
moment in Northern Ireland for building
lines. We have, as we know, under the
Summary Jurisdiction Act, a law that
a new house must not be built within
thirty feet of the centre of the county
road
Is that not enough for us? Have
we got any money to spare to buy up
more frontages, because if a building line
is to be constructed, compensation has
got to be paid and the cost, directly or
indirectly, will fall on the ratepayers.
That will not tend to cheapen building.
It is, of course, the English section
again. I do not think the ordinary citizen will care for the provision that if
a building line is of sufficient depththere is no indication in the Bill how deep
it has to be, but if a man wants to have
in his front garden a sunken croquet
court, it seems to me he will have to go to
the county council to get permission, and
also if he wants to put up a summer
house. I do not like that. I think,
perhaps, we could have done without a
building line.

Th
the


are
Again, we
incidentally taking
powers to prevent a farmer putting his
corn stack where he likes. Some farmers
sometimes stack their corn in the fields,
and they generally do so for convenience
in thrashing. That, apparently, is now
to be subject to the approval of the
county council. Fortunately, I think the
notice will take so long that the corn
will be thrashed before anything serious
happens, but a provision of that sort will
not help us until we can deal with the
obstruction caused by heavily laden lorries that stand about our roads. They
are much worse than any haystack, and
we ought to try to control the obstructions
on the road itself, rather than the obstructions immediately off it.
Have
I now pass to the Clause to which the
noble Viscount drew special attention,
the Clause enabling an experiment to be
made, with the consent of the county
council, on one of their roads.
we got any money available for a purpose of that kind? We are not Great
Britain. Great Britain has a Road Fund
which is, apparently, capable of withstanding raid after raid by the Chancellor of the Exchequer. We are not in
that position. All the money we can
get, and are likely to get for years to
come, is required for the reconstruction
on modern lines of our main roads. After
that we have many subsidiary roads which
require attention. I think we have no
money to spare for experiments. I think
we ought to be content to learn from experiments which are conducted elsewhere
I should imagine that experiments in road
construction in England would be good
enough for Northern Ireland, and perhaps experiments made in Scotland on
the bog foundations there, might be of
use to us. I do not think we should en.
courage the provision of funds which,
after all, will apparently be absolved from
control by Parliament.
I recognize that this is an enabling
Bill, and that the county councils are
there to protect us. They will protect
us, I think, financially; and I hope they
will pay regard to the sylvan beauties of
some of our roads. I think the county
councils have served us in the past, and
I think they will continue to do so satisfactorily, in spite of the inducements to
extravagance and folly which I am afraid
will be showered upon them by this Bill
when it becomes law. But in spite of
that protection, I still think it would be
better if we could have something-1
cannot see why Parliament cannot have
something presented to it which is less
ambitious, which is less repellent, and
more in accordance with constitutional
practice. If we could have that, and if
we could get it from a Joint Committee, it
would, I think, be a great gain. A
matter of this sort is one, I think,
eminently suited to the consideration of
a
Joint Committee, because when
differences of opinion arise, as they must
arise-and, after all, difference of opinion
is the lifeblood of discussion when these
differences of opinion arise in Committee,
they get dealt with by vote upon their
merits, quite irrespective of the lines of
party. It would be a great gain if a
Bill of this kind could be dealt with in
that way. I ought not, I think, to
dwell further on that. I think this Bill
is not a good one, and, therefore, subject
to the opinion of others, I doubt whether
this House should pass it. In that sense
I have already moved.
Mr. LONG: I am sure we are all
agreed in admiring the care with which
the hon. Member, who has just spoken,
has examined the Bill; and I believe the
industry that he exhibits in that regard,
with reference to all Bills that come before this Senate, has materially contrihuted to the position which we occupy at
present, and the reputation which we
have as an effective revising assembly. I
was disappointd in the speech of the hon.
Senator, because his criticizm was more
laboured and ineffectual than usual, and
it evidently required all his ingenuity
to detect a flaw in the Bill. I am pleased
to be able to state that the Bill supplies
a want which has been much felt by the
local authorities. It is supported by a
great number of corporations and associations. I need not read you the entire
list which was submitted to the Roads
Advisory Committee.
I might just point out, as Senator Leslie
has mentioned, that there was no demand
for building lines, that representations
were put before the Roads Advisory Committee on behalf of the General Council of
Municipal Authorities and Bangor Urban
District Council. Seventeen authorities
asked to have the erection of petrol pumps
legalized, and representations were made
with regard to other matters which the
hon. Senator mentioned. With regard
to carrying out experiments, the Ulster
Roads Improvement Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
Association of County Councils and the
General Council of Municipalities and the
Roads Advisory Committee have made requests to have these experiments carried
out.
The necessity for the Bill arises in the
main from the complete change which has
taken place during the past few years in
the nature and extent of the traffic using
the roads of the Province. In 1921 there
were less than 12,000 mechanically-propelled road vehicles of all classes in the
Six Counties. Now there are over 30,000.
This great increase in the use of motor
vehicles has necessitated much more lasting
and better surfaced roads than were suitable for horse-drawn transport, and in
addition has greatly increased the liability
to serious accident to road users. During
the year 1925 there were as many as 1,300
accidents on roads in which motor vehicles
were involved. As a result 59 persons
were killed and 759 seriously injured.
The appalling increase in the number
of serious accidents on roads, despite the
fact that last year's Motor Vehicles Act
imposed great restriction on the dangerous
driving and speeding of motor-cars, clearly
shows that much remains to be done to
make roads safe for the public. For this
reason portions of the Bill are intended
to facilitate road authorities in their endeavours to get rid of dangerous and blind
corners and of obstructions to the view of
drivers of vehicles. There are many instances in which the removal of a tree or
the erection of a warning sign would prevent accidents at corners. In other cases
it may be necessary to acquire lands.
Provision is made in the Bill for improvement in all these directions.
Apart from the question of the safety
of road users, modern traffic has enormously increased the difficulties and responsibilities of road authorities as regards the maintenance of roads. Roads
are no longer local. They are open for
all motorists, and are liable to be used
by any motorist for business or pleasure.
However, the position the road authorities are in at the present time is this.
On many of the roads heavy traffic has
been brought in, motor buses are brought
in, and heavy goods lorries are brought in
which are quite unsuitable for the roads.
I can recite numerous cases in which
motor omnibuses are operating on thirdclass roads of water-bound macadam,
with very serious damage to the road.
It is necessary, therefore, to make some
provision for experimenting, with a view
to determining the effect of different
classes of vehicles on roads, and also the
most suitable types of roads for different
circumstances. It is also desirable that
road authorities should be enabled to prevent the destruction of roads by particular classes of vehicles which they were
not constructed to carry
With regard to Clause 1, which Senator
Leslie has severely criticized, I think the
expense of taking a small piece of land to
improve a corner is often out of all proportion to the value.
Senator DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Mr. LONG: In my opinion the proposal
indicated in the Clause will go a long way
to cheapen, facilitate, and expedite the
obtaining of that parcel of land. In my
district we were asked to pay at the rate of
£860 per acre for widening an extremely
dangerous corner in a purely rural district.
The procedure is, I think, simple in spite of
what Senator Leslie has said, and I think
it will be further possible for the council
under the provision of this Clause to enter
on lands say within two months from the
time of making the application. And still
the interests of all parties will be safeguarded.
In all cases where the purchase price is
under £100, as the Leader of the House
has explained, the local authorities will
simply get a statutory declaration from the
owner that he has been in receipt of the
rents and profits, and that the land is not
subject to mortgage. A form of receipt
will be prescribed for the compensation,
and the title will then be complete.
Of
course, as the Leader. of the House has
pointed out, we are running the risk of
having a second application if any other
person proves he was entitled to compensation. But the procedure is similar
to that obtaining under the Labourers
Act, and I have no recollection of a claim
of that nature being made.
With regard to the point that Senator
Leslie has made as to easements or other
form of taking title, I might point out
that it is true that road authorities have
only got the right of passage over the
road; and in theory the soil still belongs
to the man who originally owned the land.
