C
d

In practice, however, and in fact the land
is taken from him absolutely, and for all
practical purposes he is quit of it and
deprived of the use of it. A man by no
stretch of imagination could grow even
a catch crop on the land over which an
easement has been obtained. There is
no difference whatever in price, I understand, from inquiries I have made, in
obtaining an easement or the right of
passage over the road in perpetuity.
Under the Local Government Act of 1898
power was given to either acquire or
obtain an easement over land, and under
the Roads Act of 1909 power was given
to acquire only and not acquire and
easement.
I understand also from inquiries I have
made that the only instance in Great
Britain where land for road purposes is
not acquired in perpetuity is where the
land is gifted to the road authority or
dedicated to them. I, therefore, would
strongly support the proposal in the
Clause to acquire the land in perpetuity.
No injustice can be done to any one. No
one can suffer in the slightest degree. With
regard to Clause 5 there is a growing disposition by owners to fit motor lorries
with pneumatic tyres. So far as firstclass and second-class roads are concerned
they will soon be free from iron-shod
tyres. As satisfactory pneumatic tyres
can now be obtained for lorries carrying
heavy loads, the operation of the Clause
will probably act as an inducement to the
equipment of even more vehicles with
such tyres. In effect the Clause will
operate practically entirely in connection
with Class III roads.
As the Leader of the House has indicated, the cost of the construction and
maintenance of roads has increased very
considerably. Prior to the war the traffic
on roads in Northern Ireland was almost
entirely horse-drawn. Some motor
lorries existed, but they were neither
numerous nor heavy. A few road locomotives were to be found, and as their use
was not general the standard of road
maintenance required was not very high.
During the war very little could be done
in the matter of properly maintaining
roads, consequently after the war a considerable arrear of road work had to be
overtaken. At the same time motor
vehicle traffic began to expand and has
continued to expand, so that, while endeavouring to repair the wastage of the
war years, local authorities had also to
| provide for the altered traffic circumstances and to reconstruct many of their
roads to fit them for modern vehicles, an
important feature of which is the remarkable growth of omnibus services.
a
With regard to the matter Senator
Leslie dealt with, the damage to the road
at Stormont, there is no doubt that
considerable damage was done there in
connection with the erection of the new
Parliament buildings. It was the most
outstanding case, and there is also the
case of the constructional works connected
with the water supply for Londonderry
city. In the former case certain roads over
which building materials were carted were
so damaged that their repair meant the
imposition of a special rate of 9d in the f
for Castlereagh Rural District for
period of five years, notwithstanding that
every effort had been made to place the
liability on the persons responsible for
the damage. I take quite a different
view of this illustration to Senator Leslie,
because in my opinion this Clause will
have the effect of preventing a recurrence
of a case of that nature. It will be impossible for damage of that nature to
continue for any length of time if the
House is good enough to pass this Bill.
No undue hardship should result from this
particular Clause, because an appeal lies
with the Ministry and an order of a Local
Authority only lasts for one year, and then
the power of appeal comes into operation
again.
The general standard of road maintenance in Northern Ireland is continually improving, and accordingly as
roads are brought into such a condition
as will enable them to carry modern road
vehicles the necessity for the Clause will
continue to decrease until ultimately it
will cease to exist. Power already exists
in Section 7 (4) of the Roads Act, 1920,
under which the Ministry of Home Affairs
may on application from a county council
prohibit or restrict traffic of any particular class where the road is unsuitable for
the traffic or the traffic is unsuited to the
road. This power has, so far, been used
very sparingly and there is no reason for
assuming that the power the Clause
confers on local authorities will not be
used with discretion.
With regard to the provision of parking
places, there have been quite a number of
applications from different authorities,
including the Belfast and Bangor Corpora-
Constabulary Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
tions, and the Armagh, Dungannon, and
other urban district councils for power
to provide parking places in their respective areas. In the past no local
authority had any power to provide
such places and these authorities have
approached the Ministry to ascertain how
they can overcome this difficulty. Perhaps I ought to say at this point that the
Roads Advisory Committee were not
unanimous regarding this particular
matter. Motor traders professed to fear
that the provision of municipal parking
places would injuriously affect their business, but as the Clause does not endow
the local authorities with trading powers
it is not clear how any competition can
arise. The other matter on which the
Roads Advisory Committee were not
quite unanimous was that the chairman,
Colonel the right hon. Sharman Crawford,
considered that the Bill did not go far
enough in giving powers to local authorities. He thought they should have
larger powers in several directions. The
other Clauses are not important.
In conclusion, I should like to appeal
to Senator Leslie not to persist in his
Amendment. I think he will have
attained all he desires and the matters he
has raised can be more fully dealt with
on the Committee stage of the Bill. I
therefore very earnestly appeal to him to
seriously consider the possibility of withdrawing his Amendment. For my part
1. support the Bill.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, that this debate be now adjourned until to-morrow. I will then
invite the Minister of Home Affairs to
into this House to explain the
various matters Senator Leslie has raised.
come
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Constabulary Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
FERRARD: The Constabulary Bill
which I have the honour to present to
the House to-day covers certain matters
in relation to the Constabulary Code
which are of a sufficiently important
character to justify me dealing with them
at some length. The most important provision in this Bill is that relating to the
proposed revision of the arrangements
under which local authorities pay for onehalf of the cost of the extra force
stationed in their areas in excess of the
free distribution of police. This matter
of the charge for the extra force, together
with the question of the payment for
Special Constabulary stationed in the City
of Belfast in or about 1921-22, has been
the subject of prolonged negotiations with
the representatives of the Belfast Corporation, and an amicable arrangement has
been arrived at with which the Corporation and the Government are completely
satisfied.
It has been agreed that the free quota
as such shall be abolished, and in lieu of
the existing arrangements provided for in
the Constabulary Act, 1922, the Corporation of Belfast will pay a sum of £25,000
a year for seven years commencing on
the 1st April, 1926, as a contribution towards the cost of the R.U.C. stationed in
the city. This agreement will relieve the
Corporation of all further responsibility
for Constabulary during the seven-year
period.
It is a reasonable and fair arrangement
that the ratepayers of the city should
shoulder this small share of the burden
of police charges. Undoubtedly, the
situation of Belfast as a capital city and
the factors which determine the number
of police which should be stationed there
justify the making of some contribution
at any rate towards police charges from
city funds, and the Government therefore
have no hesitation in recommending this
settlement for the approval of the House.
With reference to the question of the
claim for Special Constabulary, I would
point out that if the cost of this Force
had been calculated on the entire number
of men employed and on the same basis
as applied in the case of the R.U.C., the
Belfast Corporation would have been
liable for payment of no less than
£400,000. The Government have felt,
however, that settlement of this matter
should be approached on the lines of give
and take, and has agreed with the Corporation to accept a sum of £66,000 in
final discharge of this claim. This sum
has been calculated by reference to the
numbers of Special Constabulary that
would have been required to bring the
total R.U.C. force in Belfast at that
time up to established strength. I trust
that the settlement of these outstanding
153 Constabulary Bill-

25 APRIL 1928
Second Reading


differences with the Belfast Corporation
will commend themselves to hon. Members. It will be found on perusal of the
Bill that Clause 2 and Clause 3 make the
necessary provision for these arrangements.
The Bill also proposes certain other
amendments in the Constabulary Code,
which I will now summarize briefly. Under
the Weights and Measures Acts the fees
taken by the members of the R.U.C., who
act as inspectors of weights and measures,
are applied, after certain deductions, for
the benefit of the R.U.C., and the existing arrangement, under the Constabulary
Act of 1922, provides for the payment of
these fees, less the deductions, into the
Reward Fund. The deductions I have referred to cover, briefly, the subsistence
expense of the inspectors, the cost of
classes of instruction for members of the
Force coming forward for the position of
inspector, and an annual bonus to each
inspector. I
advised that about
£1,000 passes into the Reward Fund each
year under this arrangement, although
the Fund itself which has other sources
of income in addition to the weights and
measures fees is more than sufficient to
pay rewards to the Force for meritorious
duty on the usual scale.
am
The Government feel that practically
the entire cost of the service of inspecting
and verifying weights and measures is
borne on the Police Vote, there is little
justification for continuing the system
under which so large a proportion of the
fees is utilized for the reward and benefit
of the Force generally, and it is proposed, therefore, to apply the fees,
firstly, in aid of the Ministry of Commerce expenses in carrying out the duties
of that office under the Weights and
Measures Acts, and secondly, the balance
in relief of the R.U.C. Vote, which will
in future bear the charges which have
hitherto been payable from fees. Section
4 of the Bill gives effect to this arrangement.
It is also proposed to make a change in
relation to the Special Constabulary Reward Fund. This Fund was set up under
the 1922 Act, and the distribution of its
moneys is provided for in Statutory regulations which, briefly, provide for the payment or rewards and small grants to serving members. On the disbandment of the
Special Constabulary, however, it was
found that
substantial balance had
a
a
accumulated in the Fund, which, in the
absence of legislation, would remain to a
large extent dormant. I should explain
that on the disbandment of the Special
Constabulary it was found that in
number of cases barrack damages had
taken place, for which in the ordinary
course the men themselves would have
been liable. It was not practicable to recover the amount of such barrack damages
from the personnel on the evacuation of
the premises, since it would have been imposible definitely to fix responsibility, and
the recovery of the amounts involved
might have imposed hardship on individuals not directly to blame. However,
as it was of the first importance that this
disbandment of the Force should be proceeded with without delay, it was not
possible to wait until the damages had
been assessed. In these circumstances it
was necessary to make good the damage
from voted moneys.
Members of the Special Constabulary
have accordingly been relieved in this way
of charges which in the ordinary course
should have fallen to be paid by them,
and it is hardly reasonable that public
tunds should bear the burden. It is proposed, therefore to provide that the part
of the balance of the Fund which is in
excess of the amount required to meet
its liabilities under the existing regulations for the portion of the Force which
is still in being should be paid into the
Exchequer. I think this arrangement
will commend itself generally to the
House.
The Bill is also designed to meet a
technical difficulty which has arisen in
regard to the receivership of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary. Under the existing
law all police property is vested in the
receiver an officer appointed by the
Ministry of Finance and this same
officer has also imposed upon him by
statute certain financial duties. Legal
difficulties arose as to whether Royal
Ulster Constabulary property was vested
in the personal name of the receiver, and
on investigating the matter we were faced
by the fact that the transfer of this
property on the death of an officer holding
this position involved difficult questions
of conveyance. Hon. Members will not,
I think, look for a detailed statement on
the technical question of the conveyance
of property, and briefly, therefore, it is
proposed that all Royal Ulster Constabulary property shall vest in the
Constabulary Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Ministry of Home Affairs, which, being
a corporation, will keep the position free
from doubt in the future.
There is one further point. Under the
Constabulary (Acquisition of Land) Act,
1924, the Ministry of Home Affairs have
power to acquire, compulsorily if necessary, land for the use or accommodation
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary. Hon.
Members will appreciate the necessity for
this power in view of the large programme
of barrack reconstruction which is now
under way, and although it has not, as
yet, been necessary to exercise this right,
it is considered essential that the powers
of the 1924 Act, which expire next year,
should be extended for a further period of
five years. Hon. Members may
fidently expect that the right to acquire
land compulsorily will only be exercised
in cases where sites or buildings cannot
otherwise be obtained at a reasonable
figure. I beg to move.
con-
Mr. LESLIE: I think there is nothing to be done as regards the Bill generally except to congratulate the Government on having brought to a conclusion
the long-drawn-out discussion with the
Belfast Corporation as regards the expenses of the police force. Other details
of the Bill were clearly explained by the
noble Viscount. I do not think they call
for any comment. But as far as the last
Clause is concerned, the one which he has
just mentioned for the acquisition of
land, I think that the House ought to
consider whether it is necessary to continue these powers in quite so strong a
form as they were originally given. I
did not myself object in 1924 to the giving
of these powers. I should not object to
them now, but I gathered from what the
noble Viscount said that it was intended
to confine them strictly to barracks. If
that is so, there is no great objection possibly to a further period of five years.
I am afraid the section in the 1924 Act
goes rather further than the simple barracks, and if that is so, I think, perhaps,
the House should consider whether it
should not curtail these powers. I have,
as I say, a sort of horror of giving compulsory powers without any form of
limitation. It is very often necessary
that they should be given, but there are
other methods. There is, for instance,
the method of a provisional order subject
to the control of Parliament. There is
the method just spoken of, of the county
council subject to appeal to the court and
others. I do not think that indefinite
power and too wide power should be given
for long. We can talk about that, perhaps, at a later date. The Bill as a
whole certainly meets a difficulty and will,
I believe, effect considerable economy.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House.
School Sites Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Moved: That this Report be now received.-(Viscount Charſemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now
adjourn until to-morrow.-(Viscount
Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five Minutes past Five
o'clock.
157 Roads Improvement Bill-

26 APRIL 1928
Second Reading

+
J
THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Thursday, 26th April, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter past Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Finance-Census of Northern Ireland, 1926, County of Antrim.
Presented pursuant to Act, and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Roads Improvement Bill.
Order read for resuming debate on
Amendment to Motion that the Bill be
now read a Second time, viz., to leave
out "now" and add at the end of Motion
"this day six months.''-(Mr. Leslie).
Question again proposed.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: The Bill was debated at considerable length yesterday
afternoon, though not at too great length,
having regard to the importance of it and
the complication of it. I was not able to
go the whole way with my hon. Friend
opposite (Mr. Leslie) in moving that
the Second Reading of the Bill be
taken six months hence. That would
not kill the Bill, because the object
of that Motion at Westminster, of course,
is to postpone the Second Reading till
Parliament would no longer be sitting.
But I apprehend we should be sitting at
the end of six months here. On the other
hand I was not quite able to agree with
my hon. Friend on my right (Mr. Long)
in the view he took on some questions
which I will allude to later on.
There can be no doubt there is a general
feeling of welcome to the Bill, because it
would tend, as it is intended, to diminish
the horrible accidents that are occurring
day after day in such numbers. Of course,
they cannot be altogether done away with.
That is an impossibility so long as this
worship of speed in itself is in fashion.
But I think, perhaps, worse than even the
Joss of life and the injury to people that
result from these accidents, is the fact that
the hospitals which are intended for those
who suffer from disease are occupied, and
these people are excluded in great numbers, to make room for persons who are
either suffering from their own recklessness or the recklessness of somebody else.
The taking of land to increase the
width of roads and to cut off corners is,
of course, a thing that has been going on
for some time under a voluntary system,
and though Senator Long described the
extortionate sums that have been demanded and perhaps paid for those pieces,
that has not been so much my experience.
It depends on the quantity of land that is
taken. Although £400 an acre may
sound an enormous sum, the figure, when
it is only a perch, is really small and
really only compensates the man, not only
for the loss of ground but for the inferior
fence, because he only gets a paling instead of a fence that would last practically
for ever. Where I differed more particularly from Senator Long was in his
view that the soil under the road, if
it did belong to the adjoining owner or
owners, was not of great consequence, and
it was rather a piece of legal pedantry to
suggest that it was of any practical value.
I cannot agree with that, because in my
experience a bog road for some mile and
a half was cut down in my county and
valuable turf was found under it. That
went to the owner. There was a similar
case in another part of the country.
I think the question will also arise with
regard to the quarry at Goraghwood. The
Great Northern Railway has reached the
road on top of the quarry. I think it
will be their duty to their shareholders to
insist on their rights, so long as they preserve the right of passing to the public
which was dedicated in time past. But
then we do not think that perhaps the
soil in some roads would be of any great
value, but the time may come when,
through discoveries we cannot foresee,
some of the soil under a road may have a
value we do not appreciate at the present
time. We must always remember that,
basic slag, for so many years thought of
as a waste product, has been sold at £5
a ton since. The question of the property
under the road is really, I think, beyond
dispute. I do not know whether it is
159 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Mr. Armstrong]
questioned that the land is valued to the
owner and he pays rates on it, and if the
road is no longer used it reverts to him
without payment. Therefore, I do not
see how it can be contended that the soil
does not belong to him. Senator Long
said it could not be of any use, because the
owner could not grow even a catch crop
on it. That is no doubt true, because
catch crops are grown on the surface, and
science has not advanced to the point yet
where crops can be grown under the surface. The surface, no doubt, has been
dedicated to the public, and the public
continue to use it.
a
Then there is the further point with regard to the right of the public. I have
known several cases where
definite
decision has been given that there is only
a right of the public to pass over a road,
and in one case where an enterprising
member of the Press took up a position
to watch the training of racehorses it was
held that it was not using the road pursuant to dedication. Another case was
where a man deliberately used to ride
backward and forward to prevent the
Duke of Rutland shooting on his moors.
Again it was held that that was not a
bona-fide exercise of the rights given to
the public.
A further question that I am interested
in rather is in the principle of ousting the
law courts and making the Home Office
the final place of appeal. Of course, I
do not mean to suggest that the Home
Office is a body which is less worthy than
any other office, but I consider there is a
constitutional right in individuals of the
public to resort to the law courts that have
been established, and there is a tendency
of late to substitute for the decision of
the judges the decision of some official.
I cannot think that is a good thing. It
was introduced by Mr. Lloyd George, a
lawyer though he never particularly distinguished himself in his profession. He
brought it in as a politician, the politician
and the good lawyer being rarely
united in the same person. I must
say the Home Secretary here must
have had some qualms when he
sanctioned the introduction of this Clause,
because it is very discouraging to the profession of which he is a member. Or
course, it will be defended on the ground
of expense and probably of time-I do
not know about that. But as to expense
I think that the county court is a comparatively cheap court for dealing with

matters of this kind, and I see no reason
for this advancing movement, because it
appears in many statutes, of substituting
some official's decision for that of the law
courts. There are one or two other little
matters that might be more fitly raised in
Committee and, therefore, at this moment
I will not trouble the Senate with any
more remarks.
Mr. DORMAN: In the first place I
wish to express my appreciation of the
very clear and concise exposition the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House, has
given of the various Clauses of this
Measure during the sitting yesterday
afternoon, and also to express my appreciation of the criticizm offered —
Mr. SPEAKER: Would the hon.
Member speak up?
Mr. DORMAN: Also to express my
appreciation of the criticizm offered by the
hon. and learned Senator (Senator Leslie)
on the various Clauses of the Bill. I think
the Bill is one that has been overdue, and
that it is absolutely necessary it should be
made law here as soon as possible. Therefore, I hope that the hon. and learned
Senator will not press his Amendment to
have the consideration of this question
deferred to a later date, as it would play
into the hands of those who may thereby
be offered an opportunity of profiteering
at the expense of the public. I also welcome the Bill because it contains
principle that I have been extremely interested in for many years. I represent
in this House a section of the community that is frequently condemned by
Members of the Government, and whose
philosophy is held up to the electors as a
philosophy that is calculated to produce
disastrous results in the community if the
electors should ever embrace that philosophy, and try to put into force by legal
and constitutional means in the representative body of the country.
a
We are considering a Bill to safeguard
the lives and well-being of the citizens in
connection with the use of communal
property that is at present vested in the
local authorities of the country, and
which some time ago was vested in individuals and bodies of individuals. Any
hon. Member who is accustomed to refer
back to old literature, and who reads the
speeches delivered by interested parties
at that time, will see that the State found
it necessary to deprive these individuals
161 Roads Improvement Bill-

26 APRIL 1928
Second Reading
고
}


and bodies of individuals of this right
which was vested in them of controlling
the roads, and levying toll of the roads
not merely for the specific purpose of the
upkeep of the roads, but for the purpose
of profit either as individuals or Corporations. Therefore, I am very pleased to see
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Home Affairs, here to-day, because in this
Bill which has emanated from his Department he has accepted this particularly
obnoxious philosophy of Socialism or
collectivism or the right of the community to interfere with the rights of individual property in the interests of the
users of communal property. I therefore
suggest to the right hon. Gentleman that
perhaps in the future he should give a
little more consideration to the philosophy
which is embodied in this Measure.
I quite agree with the hon. and learned
Senator (Senator Leslie) who objected to
a question in dispute between a citizen or
local authority and a Department of the
State being referred to any Ministry or
Department of the State as the final
arbitrator in the settlement of that
dispute. As I look upon our constitution
I regard the courts as having been set up
for the specific purpose of protecting the
citizen against the Crown or the various
Departments of the Crown, and I hope
that this growing tendency of referring
to Ministries questions in dispute between local authorities
individual
citizens and the State will not be continued, because in my opinion it is
unconsciously introducing what is commonly called
servile state. It may
ultimately happen that it will be impossible for a citizen to appeal to a Court
of Justice to settle a dispute between him
or her and a Department of the State.
a
or
There is another reason why I would
appeal to Senator Leslie not to persist in
his Amendment, and it is this. Most of
the people who deal with a community
through its Corporation or local authority
regarding land more or less regard the
community as having neither body to
keep or soul to save. They do not
recognize that they are either a physical
or spiritual entity, but they do keep their
eyes continuously upon the physical property of that entity which they ignore,
namely, the public purse. It is quite
possible for extreme profiteering to take
place unless the Department which has
the control and administration of this
Measure when it becomes an Act, as I hope
it will, exercise the greatest care.
Senator Long yesterday mentioned a
case in this county where land was required for the purpose of broadening a
road and where the owner of that demanded compensation at the rate of
£800 an acre. Probably the present owners of that land are persons
who have purchased under the Wyndham Act at anything from £30 to
£50 an acre and now, when the community requires land for public purposes,
they ask a price which amounts to £800
an acre. It is quite possible that in a
rural area a fortieth part of an acre may
be required by the local authority in the
interests of the users of the road, and
when the farmer who may have purchased
that land in the past and may have cried
out about the injustice and oppression of
the landlord, is afforded an opportunity
of asking his price he may demand the
price of the farm for a small moiety of
land that is necessary for the safety of
the community. I therefore have great
pleasure in whole-heartedly supporting
this Bill, containing as it does a principle
that, whether the Government like it or
not, will compel bodies to recognize the
right of the community, and that the
principle of the greatest good of the
greatest number must overrule the right
of any individual when injury is likely to
be inflicted upon the community.
a
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir DAWSON BATES): May I first of
all thank this honourable House for the
courtesy they have extended to me in al
lowing me to come here this afternoon in
defence of what my hon. Friend opposite
(Mr. Dorman) rather described as
Socialist Measure. I am sorry I was not
able to be present yesterday to hear the
debate and to personally take a note of the
various points raised by the hon. and
learned Senator opposite (Senator Leslie).
I have some notes of his remarks, ard I
am sure he will forgive me if I am guilty
of any error in quoting what he said. I
understand the hon. Senator commenced
more or less with the statement and this
is borne out by the fact that he moved
his Amendment that there is no demand
for this Bill. This Bill has occupied the
attention of my department and also the
attention of various local authorities, particularly the Ulster Association of Courty
Councils, the Ulster Tourist Development
Association, the General Council of Municipal Authorities, and the Roads Advisory
Committee-a statutory body set up for
the purpose of advising the Government
Roads Improvement Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
in regard to road matters and I wonder
if the hon. and learned Senator suggests
that all these gentlemen, almost all of
whom are practical men who have had
wide experience of road administration,
have devoted so much of their time during
the past eighteen months in considering
this Measure merely for amusement.
If I wanted to justify the action of the
Government in promoting this Bill I would
refer to the appalling number of accidents
that have taken place during the past
year. In that period something like 1,500
persons have been injured and 100 persons
have been killed in this comparatively
small community of ours. I think that is
an appalling condition of affairs. I do
not suggest that this is a perfect Bill, but
at all events I believe it will do much to
lessen that loss of life which I am sorry to
say shows a tendency to increase rather
than to decrease. The sole object of this
Measure is to have a cheap and rapid way
of acquiring land. I will try to put in
a few words the practical working, particularly of the first Clause of the Bill
so that hon. Members may see the real
weight of it. I am sorry Senator Leslie
is not a member of a local authority,
otherwise he would appreciate, more than
anyone outside a local authority can appreciate, the extraordinary difficulties that
have been placed in the way of these local
authorities by a certain type of road frontage. In this connection I must pay a
tribute to the large number of landowners
along our roads who have in many cases
given land practically free of cost to local
authorities
I
What happens under this Clause? A
scheme is prepared by a local authority,
that is a county council, and that scheme
may have to be prepared prior to the
preparation of the Estimates for the year.
The local authority take each road and
they decide that that corner and that
corner and so on, should be widened.
want to dwell upon this to show that from
the practical point of view this is the
cheapest procedure that can be divised.
That scheme comes to the Home Office and
the technical experts there will at once go
and inspect the roads involved in that
scheme. Under this Bill it is proposed to
acquire land compulsorily, and I think
that even Senator Dorman will agree that
if land is acquired compulsorily for the
good of the community some compensation
should be paid. On the other hand, the
ratepayer has to be considered, and I am
sure it will be perfectly obvious to hon.
Members that when the roads involved in
a scheme of this nature are inspected it
can be readily seen whether the land concerned is valuable land or not.
May I take a case in point? Supposing
a local authority, in a spirit of caprice,
instead of deciding to widen a side of the
road which is entirely bare and can be
dealt with cheaply, go to the other side
of the road where there are houses.
That
is a case where I suggest the scheme should
not be approved in the interests of either
the ratepayers or the owner of the land.
If no principle is involved, if it is simply
an ordinary corner on bare land on which
anyone can put a value and which has no
potential value for building purposes or
anything of that sort in those circumstances an Order will be made without any
delay. On the other hand, if it is a case
involving a large sum of money to the
ratepayers and unnecessary damage to the
property of a landowner, a public inquiry
will then be held to decide whether or not
a scheme should be prepared. My hon.
and learned Friend referred to this as an
appeal by the Ministry of Home Affairs to
the Ministry of Home Affairs. I think
he has entirely misunderstood the effect of
this Clause. This is an appeal by the
landowner whose land it is proposed to
take
Mr. LESLIE: Against an Order of
the Ministrv
Sir DAWSON BATES: I beg your
pardon, it is not. I will try to again explain the point to my hon. and learned
Friend. A scheme comes to the Ministry
from the local authority and the merits
of that scheme have to be considered by
the Ministry. If a principle is involved
the Ministry may order a public inquiry
to be held, but if it is an ordinary simple
case the Ministry will decide it and will
issue a Vesting Order which will have the
effect of immediately vesting the land in
the local authority. After that, resort
is had to ordinary arbitration.
It has
been said that there is something new in
this principle. Nothing of the kind. If
my hon. Friend will refer to the Development and Roads Improvement Act of
1909 he will find there very similar procedure, only in that case instead of the
Home Office it is the Development Commissioners. I do not know whether or
not the Development Commissioners are

to

ande
cal
fd

more likely to meet the views of my hon.
Friend than the Ministry of Home
Affairs. At all events the principle is
not new.
I do not think anybody has made any
suggestion that the method of arriving at
the value is a wrong one. It is one that
ed simply carries out the Acquisition of
Lands Acts, which have worked very satis-
The factorily in regard to the various powers
I given from time to time to the local
authorities. Looking back on all these
cases I find that the same principle is inevolved in assessing compensation. The
only slight difference in this, and it is
embodied in the 1909 Act. The procedure is similar to that we propose to
adopt that is make the Vesting Order
straight away, and if the people cannot
settle their differences with the landthen they can resort to the
ordinary law to get compensation. And
to my hon. Friend suggested that we have

nothing of the kind, because under this
Act we give power to any owner to appeal
to the county court judge in the event of
the damage being £100 or over.
not propose to give appeal to the county
court in amounts smaller, because we feel
that we are living now in 1928, and we
really must have a more up-to-date system, and a cheaper system of acquiring
lands.
out

th
fe
owners
We do
a
I know there used to be a very old and
cumbersome system, which from practical
working has proved a complete failure.
It is the present system under which
county councils, when they made
scheme, had to proceed to the Judge of
Assize, with enormous expense to everybody, for a little strip of land worth very
little, and the result has been that the
county councils refused to adopt existing
methods, and have not acquired land compulsorily. Therefore, unless we have
some form such as we propose to adopt,
we might as well abandon developing
roads in Northern Ireland, and this vast
expense that we have gone to by means
of the Road Fund, which has come from
the motorists, will simply go by the board.
and this appalling loss of life will still
continue.
Another point that stress has been laid
upon is in regard to these appeals. It is
not a new idea at all. It is quite a usual
method, and it even goes back to the

Labourers' Cottages Acts. There the
owner was left alternative appeals. He
could go to the Minister of Home Affairs,
or to the County Court Judge. But from
the returns I have, out of twenty-seven
appeals that have taken place since the
Ministry of Home Affairs came into being,
the owner elected to go before the County
Court in six cases. In the remaining
cases they elected to go before the Ministry of Home Affairs, first of all because
they do not believe the Ministry of Home
Affairs is such a corrupt body as my hon.
Friend suggested, and secondly, because
in appeals to the Court they must pay
the lawyer's fee. While I sympathize as a
lawyer, at the same time I am here as
Minister of Home Affairs, and not as a
lawyer.
Then, again,
ment auditors.
take the Local Govern-
It is quite a common
thing, as hon. Members know, if the
Local Government auditor, who occupies
a quasi-judicial position, surcharges a
member of the local authority, the person
surcharged has two alternatives. He can
appeal to the Ministry of Home Affairs
or to the High Court. May I point out
that since the Home Office was established
no appeal has been made against the decision of the auditor to the High Court.
In every case they have come to the Ministry of Home Affairs. Take the case of
fixing fair rents. In the old days, now
passed away, litigants there had the
opportunity of going to two tribunals,
either the Fair Rent Commission, similar
to arbitration; or to the County Court
Judge. My hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) will
agree with me and I think he was involved in a great many cases of that
character---I do not think that he, or any
of his tenants, went to the County Court
Judge in regard to fair-rent cases. I only
dwell upon this to show that the principle
is not new. The other objection to
bringing in the Courts further than we
have already done is that it increases
delay.
In dealing with Clause 2, which has for
its object the removal of fences and other
obstructions, my hon. Friend suggests
that there again this would be a splendid
way of reducing the motorists who drive
too quickly along the road. Well, I am
afraid this is not the case, and he points
out that 4.6 per cent. - I do not know
how he gets those figures-4.6 per cent. of
the accidents that took place occurred at
167 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Sir Dawson Bates]
cross roads, and not at corners.
If he is
right in suggesting that 4.6 per cent. of
the accidents took place at cross roads, I
suggest that I am equally right in suggesting that the same figures apply to
accidents at corners. Worked out, in
means something like 70 accidents caused
by these obstructions.
But surely the
owner of property has a duty to the
public
Mr. DORMAN: Hear, hear.
Sir DAWSON BATES: Surely the
owner has a right to use his property in
such a way that it will not do harm to
the general public. I do not think that
is a very Socialistic principle. It is the
general law of the land that a person must
use his property in such a way that it will
not do harm to his neighbour. What we
ask here is simply that if there is an obstruction the local authority can serve
notice on the owner, telling him he must
remove that obstruction. What happens
next? I must say I was rather surprised
my hon. Friend omitted-I may be wrong
---but he did not suggest for one moment
that there was an appeal in this case also.
When the notice is served, what happens
is this. The local authority and the
owner in respect of whose fences this
notice has been served can jointly appoint
an arbitrator entirely free from the
Ministry of Home Affairs. I do not think
my hon. Friend explained that to the
House. That is to say, the local authority and the owner whose land may be
taken can put their own heads together
and say we agree to Mr. So-and-so. And
this gentleman's decision is final as to
whether or not the notice to remove the
obstruction is a proper notice. It is only
when the local authority and the owner
cannot agree as to the individual who is
to act as arbitrator that the matter comes
before the Ministry of Home Affairs to
determine
All I can say is this, that if the Minustry of Home Affairs is such an unsatisfactory body as my hon. Friend indicates,
in the eves of both the owner and the
local authority, I think there is no question about it that both these people will
put their heads together and appoint an
arbitrator, who shall determine whether
or not the obstruction is to be removed.
What follows after that? Any loss that
that owner sustains can be recovered in
due time before the County Court Judge.
Another thing that my hon. Friend
dwelt upon was that the Ministry of Home
Affairs wanted to control the entire
frontage line. They do not intend to do
anything of the kind. My hon. Friend
really cannot have read this Clause, because if he looks at Clause 4 he will find
that the local authority is only bound to
inform the Ministry as to their intentions
and as to the line they propose to fix.
It is only for keeping us informed and
keeping us in touch with the local authorities. The Ministry have no power to
interfere with the discretion of the local
authority in fixing that line. I hope my
hon. Friend will accept this assurance
that he was a little bit inaccurate.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid the right
hon. Gentleman has not quite appreciated
what I did say about the line and the
Ministry of Home Affairs. The part I
was talking about was not the fact that
the Ministry had to be informed. It was
Sub-clause (8).
Sir DAWSON BATES: I will deal
with that now. Sub-clause (8) deals with
the exceptional case. I was coming to
that. It is where the frontage line is
made on a road running into an adjoining
county, and where, it must be obvious to
my hon. Friend, it would be undesirable
that one county should have a frontage
line say 20 feet from the centre of the
road, and further on along the road the
other county council should have say 30
feet from the centre of the road. That
would obviously be undesirable.
effect of this Clause is where two counties
cannot agree, then we are to decide. That
is an entirely different matter.
I cannot
imagine two local authorities differing like
that. Yet my hon. Friend suggests that
two local authorities having the care of
the public purse should go into Court to
fight a case of that sort.
The
After all, where the Ministry of Home
Affairs have the distribution of large sums
in regard to roads, I do not think it is an
unusual principle for them to have some
say. There is an old saying that the person who pays the piper has to right to
call the tune. In this particular case I
think my hon. Friend has really overstressed the point. Now another point.
My hon. Friend suggested there should
be
modfication in regard to the

that where damage is done to certain
roads the local authority can recover the

cost or expense from the individual who

That was not the fault of the Act.
was one of those cases which it is very
It
Ok hard to prove. Senator Leslie suggested
that we should amend the Act, but he has
not told us what practical way we have of
doing it. The British Government have
under consideration a new Roads Bill, and
they intend to embody in it the very
Section of the Act under which that parparticular action was brought. The result
of that action was not the fault of the
Act, but I take it the evidence was not
strong enough to support it.

We have, however, taken the only step
if it was feasible for us to take. We have
given a local authority power at once to
stop any traffic of an undesirable character using third-class roads, and a case
such as I have referred to cannot possibly happen again. If it does, it will be
entirely the fault of the local authority.
Here again, instead of taking further
power to the Ministry, we are giving away
power to the local authority, because
under the existing law the only authority
that can close roads against
certain class
of traffic is the Ministry of Home Affairs.
Under this Bill, as regards third-class
roads which are not fit for heavy traffic,
we give immediate power to
authority, and the moment the county
surveyor sees that certain traffic is doing
damage to a road, the local authority can
issue an Order to stop that traffic.
think that is the most practical way of
- dealing with that matter.

that is made over a bog without any
cutting at all, a reinforced concrete road
about nine inches in depth. I hope it
will last, but I quote it as one example
to show that we should try to encourage
inventors to experiment, in order to construct a road that will meet the requirements of a particular locality. I think
Senator Leslie said this power would lead
to folly and extravagance.
I think he
used those words.
Mr. LESLIE: It was to something
further on.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think that
was what was in my hon. and learned
Friend's mind.

The
Sir DAWSON BATES : Here again the
Ministry has not allocated to itself any
power, because it is laid down that these
experiments must be conducted with the
permission of the local authority.
question of easements was referred to. I
know that that question was raised in an
acute form by one or two hon. Members in
another place, and there seems to be a
general misunderstanding as to what an
easement is. The public have no easement in regard to roads. I cannot do
better than quote from the case of Rangely
and the Midland Railway.
Mr. LESLIE: In 1868?
Sir DAWSON BATES: Yes. At the
present time the public have only the
right to pass and repass over a road. The
land is dedicated to the use of the public,
and once the land is dedicated that right
cannot be taken away. It must last for
all time. Therefore, it is essential that
the person who dedicates that land should
be the owner in fee simple, not a leaseholder, and not subject to any charges.
In this Court of Appeal case it is laid
down:
In truth, a public road or highway
is not an easement, it is a dedication to
the public of the occupation of the surface of the land for the purpose of
passing and repassing, the public
generally taking upon themselves
(through the parochial authorities or
otherwise) the obligation of repairing
it
Then Senator Leslie suggests that we
should not have power to conduct experiments on roads. He suggests we should
live on the experiments made in Great
Britain. There is a certain type of road
in Northern Ireland which does not exist
in England. I refer to a bog road, with
which Senator Long is so familiar. One
of the great disadvantages local authorities are labouring under at the present
time is the enormous cost of the modern
You can agree with a man that he will
road. I have in mind one particular road | dedicate land for the use of the public,
171 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Sir Dawson Bates]
The
but you cannot compel him to dedicate
land for the use of the public. I have
searched through various Acts of Parliament, and I cannot find a case where this
extraordinary act of compelling a man to
given an easement has been done.
whole thing is fantastical, and I am glad
that no hon. Member suggested that an
easement should be given, because assume
that we could acquire an easement by compulsion, the county council could not dedicate it to the public. It must be borne
in mind that you must acquire a good
title and the fee simple of that land, and
the local authority can then dedicate it
to the public. I am not aware that even
county councils have the right, once that
land is dedicated, to take it back again.
If I have a frontage, I can cut off a corner
and say that I dedicate it to the public,
but once I do that I cannot take it back.
There has been an extraordinary amount
of misunderstanding in regard to this
matter. I know that in the old days,
when tracks were made over land, there
was a common law that the public had the
right to pass and repass over that land.
The bed and soil may remain the property of the adjoining owners, but that
is entirely a theoretical matter. It is of
no practical value, because the public
had that right, and that right remains
for all time.
I am not aware of any section under
which there is any power given to close
such a road against the public. In any
event assume for a moment that this was
a practical method, and that a local authority acquired an easement instead of acquiring the fee simple, which is the
principle in all the English Acts.
In
fact, every Act that has ever been passed,
whether it is a public Act for the benefit
of local authorities, or a private Act promoted by a local body, provides for the
acquisition of the fee simple of the land,
and afterwards the land is dedicated to
the public. The cost of acquiring an
easement although I do not know how
you would do it would be exactly the
same, but the result would be very
different. I believe that the Registration
of Titles Act, with which Senator Leslie
is familiar, governs the holding of lands
which have been purchased under the
Land Purchase Act. I understand that
it is not necessary to register an easement, and I can imagine all sorts of complications. If a local authority wanted
to acquire a large number of these easements, and they were not registered, the
maps of the various farms and lands on
which they were situated would be entirely
inadequate. I do not think any practical
reason has been advanced why this proposal should be adopted.
Senator Armstrong mentioned the question of hospitals accommodating injured
motorists and persons injured by motors.
I am entirely in sympathy with him, and
I agree that it would be a very desirable
thing if some scheme could be devised
whereby motorists could be induced to
contribute to hospitals. However, that
is not a matter that can be dealt with in
this Bill. If the hon. Senator can devise a method whereby motorists could
be enabled to contribute to hospitals,
either themselves or through Insurance
Companies, I shall be only too glad to
consider it, because I think it is unfair
that the general public should have to
subscribe for the upkeep of voluntary
hospitals, and the beds should be filled
with people who are injured on the roads,
when other people cannot get into the
hospitals for treatment. I must apologize for having taken up so much of the
time of the House. I have tried to deal
with the various points that were raised
by hon. Members, but if there is any
matter I have not touched, I shall be only
too glad to deal with it by the permission
of the House.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM :
Unfortunately I was not present yesterday to hear the debate, but it appears
to have been very interesting, and it has
drawn from the Minister of Home Affairs
one of those speeches which cover a
great deal of ground, and clear up a
lot of difficulties. I hope this Bill will
receive its Second Reading and will become an Act. I know that when it was
postponed last Session it caused a good
deal of apprehension on the part of many
people who use the roads, and who realize
the difficulties and dangers that exist at
the present time. I think it would be a
disaster if we did not pass the Bill now.
Only yesterday I was speaking to a lady
on a matter which had no reference to this
Bill, and she told me that a motor car
dashed out of a side road where there
were high hedges, and that they just
missed it by the skin of their teeth.
Those are risks which people should not
have to take. I do not think there is any
new principle introduced in this Measure.
If I contract smallpox or any other infec-

If it is ncessary, in the interests of the
public safety, to cut down a hedge or to
clear away any other obstruction on or
off the roads, we should have the power
to do it. If the county council has to go
to the Court in order to acquire the land
great delay may ensue. I happen to have
obtained a penny compensation for some
land that was taken in Belfast, and I could
probably have delayed the matter for a
year, or at least for six months, if I had
gone to the Court, but where human life is
at stake that is absurd. I will not detain
the House further than to say that I give
the Bill my full support, and if there are
any outstanding difficulties that have not
been satisfactorily explained by the Minister of Home Affairs they can be dealt
with on the Committee stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I am really extremely
gratified that I took this step and suggested an Amendment to the Motion of
the noble Viscount, because I think the
fact that I took that step has resulted
perhaps in a rather more extended debate
on this important Bill that we otherwise
might have had. In the first place, we
drew from the Chairman of Londonderry
County Council an extremely able exposition of the views of the Roads Advisory
Committee. Incidentally, I was so fortunate as to receive the very much appreciated congratulations from my hon.
Friend. And, again, this afternoon we
have had the advantage of hearing the
hon. Senator, the Lieutenant for Armagh
(Mr. Armstrong), and also the Leader of
the Opposition in this House. And,
finally, we have drawn the Minister of
Home Affairs himself to come down and
explain to us his views on some of the
points in the Bill.
I
Now, like the right hon. Gentleman,
I very much regret that he was not able
to be with us yesterday afternoon. If he
had been I think perhaps there are one or
two things in which he would have expressed himself differently. I entirely
accept what he said about his intention
not to misrepresent anything I said.
hope he will accept my assurance that I
do not intend to misrepresent anything
he said. I am sure he and I both approach the Bill from the same desire to
serve the public interest. Now, the right
hon. Gentleman has given, and I am glad
to have it, his illustration of the working
of Clause 1.
The right hon. Gentleman
suggested that I said there was no demand
for the Bill. That was not what I meant.
I said, I am quite sure the Bill is not
what the public interest at this time in
reality demanded.
The right hon.
Gentleman spoke about the very regrettable accidents. The figure I quoted was
taken from a London newspaper, which
had been examining the causes of accidents, and which gave, for what it was
worth, the statement that 4.3 per cent. of
the fatalities were due to carlessness at
road junctions, whether they meant
road junctions at cross roads, or other
form of road junction. I do not know.
I did not accept any responsibility for the
figure myself.
The right hon. Gentleman also said that
perhaps if I had been a member of a local
authority I might have known better
what I was talking about. It is quite true
I am not now a member of a local authority, but I have served on the county
council, and on the finance and law committee of the county council. I have
met cases of difficulty about taking land.
I have heard of cases where an undue
price was asked, and I have heard of other
cases where there really was difficulty involved. I am afraid I thought the right
hon. Gentleman took a little from the
claim that the new procedure would be
so easy. I hope it will. At the same
time I do believe the saving will be slight.
The right hon. Gentleman objected to
my statement that the Clause was an
appeal from the Ministry to the Ministry,
or something of that nature. He appears
to suggest that it is a Clause for the compulsory acquisition of land without any
appeal at all. I am sure that must be
due to a mixing of ideas. The procedure
is in the Schedule, and one can see
exactly how it works. It is not of great
importance. The right hon. Gentleman
drew rather special attention to Clause
2, and he pointed out that I did not tell
the House yesterday---that is quite right
--that a dispute under Clause 2 could be
referred to an arbitrator. Well, the
reason I did not tell the House anything
about the arbitrator is this, that it is so
plainly mentioned in the Bill that it was
unnecessary. I did not want to lengthen
unduly a speech which was perhaps long
enough as it was. But the right hon.
Gentleman has not told us to-day why
The inserts in this Bill, as the alternative
to the arbitrator, the Ministry of Home
Affairs, instead of following the English
175 Roads Improvement Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
practice of inserting in that place and in
that Clause the County Court Judge. The
English Section, from which this is taken,
gives appeal in default of arrangement of
an arbitrator to the County Court Judge,
not the Ministry. That is distinct.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry to
interrupt, but I think the hon. Senator
asked the reason why I did not give that
appeal. It is perfectly simple. Hon.
Members will appreciate that if there is
an obstruction on the road, it is absolutely
necessary that that obstruction should be
removed at the earliest possible moment.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) My hon.
Friend knows the County Court Judge
only goes round four times a year. They
might just miss one court, with the result
that a delay of six months might take
place, and loss of life might intervene in
the meantime. Under this arrangement,
if the local authority and the owner agree
with each other they appoint an arbitrator. If they are not sensible enough to
appoint an arbitrator, then it comes to
the Home Office. The hearing will be
finished in a comparatively short time,
instead of having to wait six months,
with perhaps the loss of lives.
Mr. LESLIE: After all it is a matter
of opinion, and perhaps we has better
leave that for the moment. Then the
right hon. Gentleman gave us figures of
appeals to the County Court, under the
Labourers' Cottages Acts. Out of twentyseven, only six have gone to the Home
Office.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The other way
about. Only six out of twenty-seven
took advantage of going to the County
Court. In twenty-one cases they preferred to come to the Home Office.
It
Mr. LESLIE: I beg pardon. I said
the wrong thing. Only six out of twentyseven went to the County Court.
seems to me that six out of twenty-seven
is a fairly large percentage. It is rather
more that 20 per cent. Again, the right
hon. Gentleman referred to the Land
Commission, and suggested that I had
never taken an appeal against a fair-rent
decision to the County Court. In that he
is perfectly correct, as I sold my land
more than twenty years ago. He then
spoke about building lines, and evidently

point out. What I said was, if I remember, that it was possible under that Clause

-I think the words I used were, by the

did not agree with another as to the building line, the Ministry decides. If the
building line extends to the border of a
county, call it county A, which has a
30-feet building line-at all events it
extends to the dividing line between

one building line and county B does not
want the same building line or wants none
at all. The suggestion in the Bill seems
to be that the Ministry may then come
in and make such order as they think fit.
That is to say, surely the Ministry can
impose on one or the other county a thing
that it does not want.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am afraid
my hon. Friend has exaggerated the
point. This only applies where the roads
in the respective counties run into each
other. It does not in any sense give
power to the Ministry of Home Affairs to
fix a general building line. It is only
where the roads run in, and where two
counties cannot agree. Then somebody
must settle, and it is suggested that the
Home Office will. It is not a new
principle, in regard to Local Government.
that the Home Office or the Local Government Board have to decide certain points

ciative of what has been done in this Bill.

1 difficult one of easements. I do not think

was glad when the right hon. Gentleman

was the year in which certain decisions,
more or less important, took place. Apart
from these, the hon. Senator from Armagh
(Mr. Armstrong), I think, was called to
the English Bar, a matter which I am sure
is one for congratulation. Another far
less important, and perhaps not a matter
for congratulation is that I was born in
that year. At all events, the particular decision was one of great importance. Since
that decision was given, nobody has definitely gone back on the old statement
that the right of the public was an easement. They always confine themselves to
saying it was in the nature of an ease.

Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I regret to say that my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, who took the Second Reading of
this Bill, has been called to London on
important business. I received the mes-
181 Roads Improvement Bill

3 MAY 1928
Constabulary Bill-

sage from him only last night, so that I
was unable to make any alteration in the
Orders of the Day. This is a very highly
technical Bill, and as this is a revising
Chamber I think it would be very advisable to have the technical amendments
which are down on the Order Paper dealt
with by a person who is somewhat more
competent to deal with them than I am.
At the same time, I am prepared to take
the Bill, although I think it would be
better if it was postponed to a date that
would suit the convenience of the hon.
and learned Senator who has put down
the Amendments (Mr. Leslie) and the
Minister of Home Affairs, whose Bill it
is. I would, therefore, suggest that the
Committee stage be postponed sine die.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that I should
myself have no objection whatever to the
postponement of the Committee stage of
this Bill. I agree with the noble Viscount
that it would be desirable that the
Minister in charge of the Bill should be
here when we are discussing it, but I
cannot agree with him in his suggestion
that he himself is not perfectly competent
to take charge of this or any other Bill.
The question of postponement unfortunately affects me personally, because owing
to circumstances that I need not mention
I shall not be able to be in this House
during next week.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Then the
week after.
Mr. LESLIE: To the week after I can
have no possible objection.

CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of principal
Act as to distribution of Royal Ulster
Constabulary), 2 (Amendment of principal Act as to free quota and additional
constabulary), 3 (Contribution by Belfast
Corporation in respect of Special Constabulary), 4 (Amendment of s. 19 of 52
and 53 Vict., c. 21 and s. 4 of principal
Act); 5 (Provisions for payment to
Exchequer of surplus accruing to the
Special Constabulary Reward Fund), and
6 (Provisions as to constabulary property
and office of receiver), ordered to stand
part of the Bill
CLAUSE 7. (Extension of period for
compulsory acquisition of land).
The power of the Ministry of Home
Affairs to acquire land compulsorily for
the purposes mentioned in sub-section
(1) of section one of the Constabulary
(Acquisition of Land) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1924, may be exercised at any
time not later than the twenty-eighth
day of May, nineteen hundred and
thirty-four, and the proviso to the said
sub-section shall be amended accordingly.
:-
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie : -
Clause 7, page 4, leave out the Clause.
It
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment which
I have put on the Order Paper is a short
and simple one. It is simply a suggestion to
leave out the Clause, and I have not suggested the insertion of anything in substitution. The difficulty, such as it is, can
perhaps be met in some manner less drastic than the excision of the Clause.
arises in this way. The provisions of the
Act of 1924 were specifically cut down,
so far as the compulsory power for the
acquisition of land is concerned, to a
period of five years from the passing of
the Act, and that period is about to expire. It is obviously very desirable that
no difficulty should be placed in the way
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary authorities acquiring land for the erection of
constabulary barracks, and no doubt that
was in the mind of Parliament when they
gave the powers in question in 1924. The
point is that the real powers then given
were of a very wide character
The important part of the Section reads:-
When any land is acquired for the
purposes of being occupied, used, or
built upon for the use or accommodation
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary Force,
or for any purpose of the Constabulary
Act (Northern Ireland), 1922.
Constabulary Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leeke].
That is a very wide power indeed, and
I am naturally chary myself, and I think
Parliament should be chary, of extending a power definitely given for a temporary period, so that that which was
intended to be temporary may not, in
fact, become permanent. I think Parliament should keep a very close watch
on matters of that character, if we are
ever to admit the principle of the
compulsory acquisition of land for
constabulary barracks. If you cannot
get it on reasonable terms in any other
way, it is perfectly justified, and there
would be no objection to making that
simple power permanent. But when you
give unrestricted power, as regards area
and price, to acquire land compulsorily
for the purpose of being used, or for
accommodation--it might be ground, not
only for barracks, but for recreation
fields or rifle ranges, or even for
manœuvres on an extended scale-that is
a power which I do not think exists in
connection with any of His Majesty's
Forces, and I think that something less
than that might surely serve the purposes
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary.
If the Committee decides to take the
Clause out altogether, the way would be
clear for the insertion of some other
Clause, or even for the repeal of the entire Act of 1924, a great deal of which
has already gone by virtue of the Bill
now before the Committee. If, on the
other hand, the Government does not
think it worth while to take that course,
and if they can assure us that the intention is strictly to acquire barracks only,
if they cannot get them in any other way,
and that although they have had this
power for four years they have never
used it, the Committee might consider
the point would be met by calling attention to the matter, and the Clause might
be allowed to stand as it is. However,
for the purpose of getting information on
the subject, I beg to move that Clause 7
be left out..
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. and learned
Senator, I would like to remind the Committee that in 1924 a sum of money,
amounting to £275,000, was voted for
the (purpose of building and restoring
constabulary barracks in the Northern
Ireland area. As we all know, many
barracks at that time were in a deplorable
state. This sum was found not to he
sufficient, and in 1926 it was increased
to £800,000, which sum is still being
utilized for the purpose of providing
barracks for the Royal Ulster Constabulary. It is quite true, as the hon. and
learned Senator has said, that the power
conferred in this Constabulary Act is of
a very wide nature, but I can assure him
that the intention of the Ministry is
only to utilize this power of compulsory
acquisition when it is not possible to
secure sites for barracks at a reasonable
price. Up to the present, although a
large number of sites and buildings have
been purchased, it has never been found
necessary to invoke compulsory powers.
I should like to emphasize the fact that
although these powers are of a general
character, it is intended that recourse
should only be had to provisions of this
Bill for the purpose of barrack schemes,
and I can give the hon. and learned Member, and the Senate, the assurance that
it is not intended to apply this Act for
the purpose of obtaining large training
grounds or such things as the hon. and
learned Senator suggested. It is only
to be put in force if the Government are
unable to obtain sites for barracks at a
reasonable price. Therefore, I trust the
hon. and learned Senator will see his way
to withdraw his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the Committee will accept the assurance of the
noble Viscount, and will allow me to
withdraw the Amendment. It has fully
answered the purpose for which it was put
down.
Ainendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Short title, construction,
citation, and repeal) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE agreed to.
SECOND SCHEDULE agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.

Superannuation and other Trust Funds
(Validation) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill which I submit to the
House to-day deals with a matter of some
importance to bodies such as banks, insurance companies, and other concerns in
Northern Ireland, who may have superannuation arrangements for their employees. As hon. Members are, of course,
aware, many industrial concerns have
established superannuation funds for the
purpose of providing pensions for their
servants on retirement, and sometimes
also for providing for their widows and
children. This is a very laudable proceeding, but there are legal objections
against these trusts on the ground that
they infringe what is known as the Rule
of Law against Perpetuities.
The public policy on which this rule of
law is based was stated by the Master of
the Rolls in England as far back as 1732,
and is to the effect that property or
money should not be tied up indefinitely;
but hon. Members will, I think, agree
that this question of the provision of
superannuation arrangements is clearly in
accordance with the public interest; and
that for the success of such schemes an
element of permanence is essential. It
is accordingly considered that there
should be some means of escape for them
from the Rule.
A Departmental Committee was set up
by the Treasury to examine into this difficulty, and, as a result of its recommendations, an Act was passed in the Imperial Parliament last year to secure that
the Rule against Perpetuities should not
apply to such funds. As a result of inquiries made by my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Commerce, it would appear that there are good and sufficient
grounds for similar legislation in our
area.
Our present Bill follows closely the
Imperial Act. It is enacted in Clause
1 that the trusts of any fund which may
be registered thereunder are not subject
to the Rule of Law relating to Perpetuities. Other provisions in the further
clauses lay down the conditions which
have to be complied with, the main condition being the registration of the fund
189 Education Associations Bill-

8 MAY 1928
Second Reading
DE
and its rules with the Registrar of
Friendly Societies, the qualifications for
registration being given in Clause 2.
As I have said, the Government is satisfied that there are good grounds for exdtending the principle of this legislation
to Northern Ireland. I cannot give hon.
Members the details of the various funds
which are affected, but there is a general
wish on the part of leading business concerns and respective associations that
this course is desirable. I beg to move.

Education Associations Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This Bill to which I now ask
the House to give a Second Reading is a
simple Measure. It is proposed to
regularize the question of the payments
of expenses incurred by education
authorities and committees in connection
with their membership of Education
Associations.
com-
The principle is not a new one; in fact,
all local authorities except local education
authorities and their education
mittees have statutory powers enabling
them to become members of kindred
Associations and to pay, as part of their
expenditure, the reasonable expenses of
their representatives and officers who attend their meetings. In regard to this
existing legislation it may be sufficient to
say that the Local Government (Ireland)
Act of 1902 gave power to county councils in Ireland to pay an annual subscription and the expenses of representatives
in connection with the Association of
County Councils. A further Act of our
own Parliament in 1923 gave similar
powers to rural district councils.
Shortly after the education committees
throughout the Province were set up in
accordance with the terms of the Education Act of 1923, it was proposed to
establish an Association of these bodies,
and this proposal received my cordial
approval. After this Association had
been already in being and had held some
meetings the Local Government Auditor
raised the question whether there was
statutory power to form such a body and
to incur the necessary expenses. The matter
was examined, and we were advised that
the appropriate course was to seek powers
giving these education committees statutory authority for these purposes in the
other bodies. This,
therefore, is the object of the present
Bill. I beg to move.
same manner as
Mr. LONG: During the many years I
have been a member of local authorities
I have taken part in the appointment of
numerous delegates to attend conferences on almost every conceivable subject
in almost every town in the British Isles.
It is no doubt a very pleasant way for a
married man to obtain a decent excuse
to get away from home and to enjoy a wellearned holiday at the public expense. As
a rule they come back better for their experience. In the case of a lady delegate,
she also enjoys a well-deserved break in
the monotony of her daily life and common task, and as a rule she comes back
with broader views and a better temper.
Altogether the members of such a delegation generally return better fitted to discharge their public duties and with their
outlook broadened as citizens of this great
Empire. With the possible exception of
the meetings of the General Council of
County Councils, I cannot recall any occasion on which any member of any of these
delegations served any very useful purpose.
However, I think the particular case
which this Bill is intended to cover is an
exception. The education authorities are
quite new to their work and they may by
means of conferences achieve some very
useful work by the discussion of various
problems that are more or less common to
all education authorities in the Six Counties. The fact that they are undertaking
fresh duties and are establishing practically a new system of education for Northern Ireland gives them an even stronger
claim than other local authorities have to
take part in these conferences and to have
their expenses paid. Therefore I support
the proposal contained in this Bill that
they should be placed on the same footing
as other local authorities in this respect.
Mr. ARMSTRONG : I should like to say
a word or two of thanks to the noble
191 Education Associations Bill
Constabulary Bill 192
THE SENATE
[Mr. Armstrong]
Leader of the House for having introduced
this Measure. I think it is a useful one
and one to which the members of the Association of Education Committees are entitled. Many of them are busy people
who travel great distances to attend the
meetings of the Association, and they devote a good deal of time to their duties.
Therefore I think that any money expended in recouping them for their expenses in attending such meetings is money
that will be repaid. From my own experience I can say they take a great interest in the work they have to do, and I
think this Bill is evidence that the Ministry of Education consider that their services are useful and that they deserve the
recognition that is given to them by this
Measure.
The MARQUESS OF LONDON-
DERRY: I rise merely to ask the Leader
of the House two questions, namely,
whether he has any idea of the extent of
the expenditure that may be incurred under this Bill, and whether this expenditure will fall upon the county authority or
will come into the general Estimates of the
Ministry of Education.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The expenditure will be incurred by the Regional
Committees as approved expenditure, but
I am afraid I cannot give any details as to
the amount of the expenditure, although
I do not think it will be very large.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
approval of this Bill. It is to be hoped
that one result of our new system of education will be that a larger number of the
working classes will be enabled to become
sufficiently advanced in education to offer
their services in furthering the dissemination of higher education amongst the
masses of the people. At present such
men are unable to render the service that
they would be able to render if their economic circumstances permitted their undertaking the expense of attending conferences such as are sanctioned in this particular Bill. I therefore express my wholehearted approval of the Measure, and I
thank the Government for having introduced it.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
While supporting this Bill, I am afraid
that it is opening the door to a practice
which may lead to an extension of the
policy of paying the expenses of persons
engaged on public duties. I think we
have got on very well in the past by
people giving their services voluntarily,
but if the principle contained in this
Measure is adopted, as it apparently will
be, I do not see how the Government can
in the future prevent its application to
other public bodies. In the case of the
county councils, you have representative men discharging the duties without
any payment, but when this Bill is
passed, if the members of my own county
council, for instance, apply for their expenses to be paid I do not see how it
can be refused. The danger is that in the
future people will join various Associations
because they will get their expenses paid.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: By leave
of the House I should like to say a word
in reply to the right hon. and gallant
Senator who spoke last. The Bill before the House deals only with education
authorities, and, therefore, I cannot very
well go into the question of what might
possibly be brought forward by other
local authorities. In connection with the
Bill before the House, the Association of
Education Authorities, to which Senator
Armstrong referred, meets the Minister
of Education pretty frequently in Belfast, and it is rather hard that one of its
members who comes from, say, Fermanagh. should be asked to pay his own
expenses.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
The only point is that you are creating a
precedent.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill
be now read a Second time) agreed to
