نہیں
E
Decontrol of Houses
Adjournment-

4 JUNE 1928
Mr. DORMAN: I think this occasion
of the adjournment of the House is one
that I ought to embrace for the purpose
of drawing the Government's attention to
the consequences of the decontrol of certain
types of houses in Belfast. Since the last
sitting of this House I had occasion to
make a business call at a place in Victoria
Street, where two or three sisters have
been carrying on business for practically
a generation, and they have put their
lives' work into that business. That
house has hitherto been subject to a rent
of £52 a year, and in consequence of the
premises now being decontrolled the
tenants have got notice to quit, and the
landlord has generously offered to renew
the tenancy on consideration of their paying a rent of £106 a year. I could quote
numerous other instances of a similar
character that have come under my personal observation, and it will be remembered that I drew the attention of the
Government to the matter when the last
Rent Restrictions Act was passing through
this House. If some steps are not taken
to protect the public from this scandalous
profiteering in houses, enormous suffering
will be inflicted, especially when the working-class houses come to be decontrolled.
I hope the Government will take the matter into consideration with a view to protecting tenants from the rapacious greed
of landlords of the type I have mentioned.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I feel
compelled to rise to support the complaint
made by Senator Dorman regarding the
imposition of extravagant rents on tenants
whose houses have become decontrolled. I
have in mind the case of a small shopkeeper who lives convenient to my own
residence. His rent was £33 a year, and
on top of that the landlord put another
£45, an increase of over 100 per cent.
Such a thing is scandalous, and while I
supported the Rent Restrictions Bill in
this House I had no idea that there was

anyone in the North of Ireland who would
be prepared to take such advantage of it
and to impose on his neighbour to the
extent that is being done at the present
time. I do sincerely trust that the
Government will take some steps, either
to expose those landlords who are guilty
of such a practice, or to put a stop to this
victimization of tenants, and especially
small shopkeepers, who can ill afford to
pay the tremendously increased rents that
are being charged.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I can assure the House that the Government have
had under consideration the matter to
which Senators Dorman and Joseph Cunningham have drawn attention, and that
it will continue to receive their consideration, especially after the two speeches
that have been made to-day on the subject. At the same time one must remember that decontrol must come sooner or
later, and that whether it comes now or
ten years hence certain rapacious landlords will seize the opportunity to do
unjust things. However, there is no
reason why attention should not be
drawn to the matter, and I am sure that
anything the Government can do they will
do. With regard to the two points raised
by the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie), I will bring them to the notice of
the Government. I quite agree with him
that it is advisable that Members should
have in their possession the printed debate
on a Second Reading before the Committee stage of a Bill is taken, otherwise
Members are at a great disadvantage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
half after Twelve o'clock
until such date as Mr.
Speaker may appoint.

Slaughtered Animals (Compensation)
Bill.
A Bill to provide for the establishment,
maintenance, and management of a fund
for the payment of compensation to the
owners of certain live stock, which are
exported from Northern Ireland and
slaughtered under the authority of law at
a port in Great Britain in consequence of
an outbreak, or suspected outbreak or apprehended danger, of foot and mouth
disease; and to make provision for the
method of investigating and determining
claims for such compensation, was presented by Viscount Charlemont, read a
First time, and ordered to be read a
Second time on the next sitting day, and
to be printed. (Bill No. 5.)

Borough Funds Bill (S.) A L L Y
Order for Second Reading read. LINCIS
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
of the House): At the outset I may say
that this Measure which, I am confident,
will not provoke any controversy, introduces no new question of principle except
in one minor detail to which I shall refer
later, but is simply intended to amend a
procedure which has long been in existence so as to bring it more into harmony
with present-day conditions.
As hon. Members are no doubt aware,
certain local authorities have power,
under the Borough Funds (Ireland) Act,
1888, to promote Bills in Parliament in
the interests of the inhabitants of their
district, but this power can only be exercised in accordance with and subject to,
certain restrictions set forth in that
Statute. These restrictions, which are
designed to prevent local authorities embarking on hasty or ill-conceived legislation, may be said to be of a three-foid
character.
Firstly, precautions are taken to en.
sure that any proposal to promote a Bill
will receive due and careful consideration
from the members of the local authority
themselves. It is, therefore, provided
that any proposal to promote a Bill can
only be dealt with at a meeting specially
convened for the purpose, of which
special notice must have been published
in the local Press, and any resolution
thereon must be passed by a majority,
not only of the members present but of
all the members of the authority. In
addition, any such resolution must be
confirmed at a further special meeting of
the local authority held at a later date.
a
Secondly, the local authority must
satisfy a Government Department that
they have
prima facie case for
bringing the Bill before Parliament for
consideration, as the approval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs to the promotion of the Bill must also be obtained.
And thirdly, safeguards exist to ensure
that the action of the local authority is
in accord with the wishes of the local
government electors of the district.
I am not here concerned with either
the first or second of these safeguards, as
experience has shown that the machinery
391 Borough Funds Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE

[Viscount Charlemont]
for giving effect to them has worked
satisfactorily, and this Bill, therefore,
does not touch either of them.
It is with
I
the procedure whereby effect may be
given to the third of these safeguards
that this Bill deals, that is, the procedure
for obtaining the opinion of the electors
as to whether they are in favour of the
action of the local authority in proceeding with the promotion of any Bill.
want hon. Members clearly to understand
that, we do not propose to interfere in
any way with the right of the electors to
say whether or not a local authority
should proceed with any local legislation,
and that it is only the procedure for obtaining the opinion of the electors which
we propose to alter. The principle itself
whereby local authorities must, act in
accordance with the wishes of their
electors is a proper one, and one which
certainly should be preserved.
Under Section 7 of the Borough Funds
(Ireland) Act, 1888, when a local authority has decided to promote a Bill in
Parliament and has passed the necessary
resolutions, a meeting of the local government electors must be held for the
purpose of obtaining the approval of the
electors to the promotion of the Bill. At
this meeting a resolution is put in favour
of the promotion of the Bill as a whole,
and if this resolution is defeated, no further steps in connection with the promotion of the Bill can be taken.
If the
resolution is passed, any five electors
present can demand that a plebiscite
of the electors of the district should be
held. A plebiscite must, then be held and
the electors must vote for the promotion
or rejection of the Bill as a whole.
The disadvantages of this Bill will at
once be apparent to hon. Members. At.
present a Bill must stand or fall as a
whole, and its component parts cannot
be segregated. A local Bill, which
usually deals with a number of diverse
matters, may be good in some parts and
bad in others, and it is ridiculous that
the electors should be compelled to decide on its merits as a whole, instead of
having an opportunity of rejecting the
portions to which they take exception,
and recording their approval of the portions of which they are in favour. The
Bill which is now before you, proposes to
amend the existing procedure so as to
enable this to be done. It is intended
that at the meeting of electors the various
Clauses or Parts of a Bill will be put o
the vote of electors separately, and a poll
may be demanded on any Clause or Part
that, is passed at the meeting.
A striking example of the shortcomings
of the existing procedure is afforded by
what happened in connection with the
Bill which was promoted by Belfast Corporation last year. It will be remembered
that this Bill contained provisions to
enable the Belfast Corporation to run
omnibuses outside the city in addition to
a number of desirable and non-controversial provisions, including a Clause to
enable the Corporation to grant land to
the Children's Hospital. The question
of running omnibuses outside the city was
a matter of acute controversy, and the
acceptance or rejection of the Measure by
the Local Government electors turned
entirely on this provision. A plebiscite
was demanded, and on this the Bill was
rejected, solely on account of this controversial provision. The result was
that, after considerable expense had been
incurred, the whole Bill had to be
abandoned, including all the provisions
which were clearly desirable and to which
no exception was taken. It was then too
late, owing to Standing Orders of Parliament, to proceed with a new Bill containing the non-controversial provisions,
but as it was a matter of extreme urgency
that the land should be granted to the
Children's Hospital, the Corporation
were able to get Standing Orders suspended to enable a Bill to be introduced
for this purpose.
The law was the same in this respect
in England until 1903, but, in that year
a Bill was passed entitled the Borough
Funds Act, 1903, which amended the
procedure on the lines we now propose
to amend it here.
Why the Irish Act
was not amended at the same time I cannot conceive, and I think hon. Members
will have no hesitation in agreeing that
the amendments now suggested are long
overdue.
Further, under the existing law, it is
not necessary that the Bill should be
printed or lodged in Parliament before
the meeting of electors, and they may
have nothing beyond a verbal statement
as to the effect of the Bill on which to
vase their opinion when called upon to
vote. We propose to remedy this by providing that the Bill should be printed
and deposited in Parliament before the
meeting of electors is convened, and by
requiring a copy of the Bill to be avail-
9:

Borough Funds Bill-
Second Reading
16 OCTOBER 1928
able for any elector who may wish to purchase it. At present one month's public
notice of the meeting must be given, but
in view of the proposal that the meeting
shall not be called until after the deposit
of the Bill in Parliament, it is felt that
this period would, in future,. be too long,
and it is proposed to require only 21
=days notice to be given.
a
Under the existing law, as already
stated, five electors present at the meeting can demand a plebiscite, and
Whatplebiscite must then be taken.
ever could have been said as to the suitability of this number at the time the
Borough Funds Act came into force in
1888, when the franchise was much more
restricted than now, it is clearly unfair that
such a small number should be able, perhaps frivolously or vexatiously, to put the
local authority to the trouble and expense, which may be by no means inconsiderable, of taking a poll when in
reality there is an overwhelming majority
in favour of the Measure and only a small
number of disgruntled electors against
it. In England a poll must be demanded
by not less than 100 electors or onetwentieth of the total number of electors,
whichever may be the less. This, I think,
is a number which is more suitable to
present-day conditions and we propose to
adopt the same rule here. Further, if
the vote at the meeting of electors goes
against any resolution, the local authority cannot at present require a poll.
There is no reason why a local authority should not have power to demand
a poll as well as the electors, and we propose to give them this power, which is
a power they have had in England since
1903.
quite clear under the Act of 1903 that
local authorities in England have this
power and sub-clause 4 of Clause 1 of
this Bill is intended to make the position equally clear here.
As I have already indicated, the sanction of the Ministry of Home Affairs must
be obtained so as to enable a local authority to charge the expenses of the promotion of a Bill on their funds, but as
the law stands at present there is some
obscurity as to whether the sanction of
the Ministry operates to authorise payment of expenses of promotion out of the
funds of the local authority, when the
Bill is rejected by the electors. It is
only right that when the Ministry has
sanctioned the preliminary steps for the
promotion of the Bill, the local authority
should be entitled to pay their expenses
out of their funds, whether or not the
Bill is rejected by the electors.
It is
electors was
CELLLOTS
びじんん、
As I indicated at the commencement of
my speech, the Bill makes one slight
alteration in a matter of principle. Under Section 7 of the Borough Funds
(Ireland) Act, 1888, the approval of
also necessary where any
local authority wished to oppose a Bill
in Parliament, but more recent legislation has considerably restricted the general application of this provision. The
Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898,
which created County Councils, gave
these bodies power to oppose, but not to
promote, Bills in Prliament under the
Borough Funds Act, but specifically provided that the approval of the electors
should not be required for this purpose.
The Local Government Act also provided
that the approval of electors under the
Borough Funds Act should not be
necessary where the Council of a County
Borough proposed to oppose a Bill in
Parliament.
The position, therefore, at present is
that, so far as the opposition to Bills in
Parliament is concerned, the approval of
the electors is still necessary in the case
of an urban district council, but not
in the case of the council of either a
county or county borough. This is an
anomaly, and it is considered that
the introduction of this present Bill
affords a convenient opportunity for its
removal. Prior to the Borough Funds
Act, 1903, approval to opposition was
also necessary in England, but that Act
abolished the necessity for such approval
in the case of all local authorities. It
will, therefore, be seen that the trend
of more modern legislation has been to
abolish the necessity for the sanction of
electors to opposition to a Bill in Parliament, as it has been felt that the requirement of the approval of the Ministry under the Borough Funds Act is a
sufficient safeguard against reckless or
hasty action in this respect on the part
of the local authority. It is, accordingly, proposed to bring the law here
into line with the law in England and to
place urban district councils in the same
position as county borough and county
councils as regards opposition to Bills in
Parliament.
395 Borough Funds Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading

[Viscount Charlemont]
I have now outlined the changes in the
law which will be effected by this Bill,
and I do not propose to deal in any detail with its actual Clauses, as the Bill
is simple in form and practically selfexplanatory. It substitutes a new Section for Section 7 of the Borough Funds
(Ireland) Act, 1888, and sets forth at
length in the Schedule the procedure
which will require to be followed in connection with the meeting of electors and
the taking of a poll if such is made necessary. Part I of the Schedule contains
the Regulations for the meeting and is
based on the Schedule to the English Act
of 1903. Parts II and III contain the
rules for taking a poll and are simply a
re-enactment of the Schedules to the Borough Funds (Ireland) Act, 1888, with
such consequential amendments as are
rendered necessary by the changes proposed.
Hon. Members will appreciate that this
Measure is intended simply to provide a
more equitable and convenient method
for ascertaining the wishes of the electors
in regard to any Bill which a local authority proposes to promote in Parliament,
and to remove some of the inconveniences
which may arise from the present position of the law. It does not in any way
weaken the reasonable safeguards which
at present exist to prevent a local authority acting in this connection in opposition to the majority of the electors.
beg to move.
I
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
approval of this Measure, which is long
overdue. It is scarcely possible for a
local authority in any part of the country
to promote legislation except in the interests of the inhabitants, and under the
law as it at present exists any legislation
which the people's representatives on local
boards think necessary for the common
well-being is very often destroyed by interested individuals who do what has
recently been done in Belfast, namely,
utilise the long period which must necessarily elapse between the notification of
the statutory public meeting and the holding of that meeting for the specific purpose of, in my opinion, misleading the
electors who have not had sufficient time
to consider that particular Measure in all
its aspects. They go to the expense of
spending money and canvassing, and after
the local authority has gone to the expense of promoting these Measures they
can be nullified by a very small section of
the community who have a personal interest in preventing the passing of legislation in the interests of the people as a
whole. In addition to that, few of our
public representatives have sufficient time
to take the necessary steps to inform the
public as to the real merits of a Bill before
the statutory public meeting is held.
They have their business to attend to and
they give their services voluntarily and
are not to be expected to utilise their
private income in trying to educate the
public as to the meaning and effect of the
various Bills that may be promoted.
Therefore I am very pleased that legislation of this kind has been introduced,
especially in view of the fact that the
Belfast Corporation will very soon again
be promoting a Bill in what it conceives to
be the interests of the city as a whole.
an
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that everybody who has thought about the subject
at all will agree not only with the noble
Viscount who has moved the Second Reading, but with the hon. Senator who has
just sat down. The noble Viscount illustrated the necessity for the Bill by reference to an occurrence which is fresh in
the remembrance of us all, and
occurrence which for a time seemed likely
to give this Parliament some little
difficulty, it being felt that, quite irrespective of the feelings of Members of
Parliament themselves, public opinion
would not have tolerated any delay
in the giving of that piece of ground
to the particular hospital concerned.
As far as the Bill goes it
to be most admirably suited to its purpose, and I quite endorse the recommendation to accept the new proposal that
the right of appeal should be given to
the governing body as well as to the
opposition.
seems
The only thing that seems to me likely
to call for our attention at a later stage
is the question of machinery, particularly
as to who is to settle whether any misfeasance, however slight, in the method
of conducting an appeal is sufficient to
invalidate what has been done. We must
also discuss whether all the wording of the
provisions of the Schedule, particularly
the part based on the Act of 1888, without the assistance of the Act of 1903, is
quite in accordance with our intention.
Those are not, matters that should
occupy us for any length of time, nor

MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg

a
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to ask the noble
Leader of the House if, when considering
the extension of the Rent Restriction Act,
the Government will take into consideration the advisability of re-controlling
houses of
combined residential and
business character with a valuation of
between £26 and £40. I have been making some inquiries as to the position of
many of the business men in the city who
occupy premises such as I have described,
namely, premises in which they carry on
their business and also reside with their
families. I find that landlords are taking
advantage of the decontrol of these houses
by raising the rent 100 per cent., 200 per
cent., and in many cases up to 220 per
cent. A great number of these houses
are doing an average turn-over of from
about £600 to about £2,000 per annum.
In one particular case where the rent was
only £53 until it became decontrolled the
landlord demanded an increased rent of
from 100 per cent. to 120 per cent. From
inquiry I am perfectly satisfied that the
average turn-over of that person——
Mr. SPEAKER: I am afraid I must
interrupt the hon. Senator. He would
not be in order in discussing a Bill which
is under consideration in another place.
Mr. DORMAN: Then I will merely
ask the noble Leader of the House
whether the Government will take this
question into consideration in connection
with the extension of the Rent Restriction
Act. The reason I have taken this
opportunity of raising the matter was
because a Bill is being promoted by the
Government for extending the control of
houses of a valuation of £26 and under,
and I thought that when they were dealing with the subject they might also give
consideration to the class of premises I
have mentioned.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I can
only say that I will put the matter before
the proper authority.
On Question, Motion (That this House
do now adjourn until Tuesday next)
agreed to.

fifteen minutes before
Four o'clock until Tuesday next.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 23rd October, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:

Ministry of Labour:

Presented (pursuant to Act) and
ordered to lie on the Table.
Standing Orders Relative to Local Bills.
Order No. 108 (Hour of meeting, etc.,
of Committees on opposed Bills).
Every Joint Committee on opposed
Bills shall meet not later than eleven
o'clock every morning and shall sit till
four, and shall not meet at a later hour
nor adjourn at an earlier hour without
leave of both Houses, or without reporting to both Houses the cause of such
later meeting or earlier adjournment.
No Committee shall adjourn over any
day except Saturday, Sunday, Christmas Day, and Good Friday, without
leave of both Houses, or without reporting to both Houses the cause of such
adjournment, but should a Committee
meet on a Saturday, the sitting is to be
in conformity with this Order.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, that Standing
Order No. 108 relative to Local Bills be
amended as follows: - In line 6, after the
word "except" (except Saturday) insert
"Friday," and after the word "Sunday"
insert "Monday." The object of this
Amendment is to allow the Joint Committee to adjourn from Thursday until the
following Tuesday and thus confine the
sittings of these Committees to the days on
which the House usually sits. I need
hardly say that those Members of this
House who have given up their time by
sitting on these Committees will no doubt
very much appreciate the Amendment that
it is proposed to make. I beg to move.
Amendment made.
Order No. 108, line 6, after "except"
insert "Friday," and after "Sunday"
insert "Monday."
Order No. 180.
No such Bill shall be read a Second
Time in the House of Commons earlier
than the third, or later than the fifth,
day on which the House shall sit after
the day upon which such Bill has been
read a First Time.
Order No. 182.
No such Bill shall be read a Second
Time in the Senate earlier than the
third, or later than the fifth, day on
which the House shall sit after the first
reading thereof.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move to leave out
Order No. 180 and Order No. 182.
These two Amendments refer to Divorce
Bills. Originally the Standing Orders
were framed to bring Divorce Bills into
line with other Bills, but as there is no
necessity for an interval between the First
and Second Reading in the case of a
Divorce Bill, although there may be in
the case of other Bills, it is felt that it
is just as well to remove this unnecessary
cause of delay. I beg to move.
Amendments made.
Leave out Order No. 180.
Leave out Order No. 182.
Ordered: That the Standing Orders Relative to Local Bills be printed as
amended.

FAIR RENT COURTS.
Mr. BARCLAY: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is the
intention of the Government to establish
Fair Rent Courts in respect of dwellinghouses and shops up to an annual rental
of one hundred and fifty pounds, and in
respect of the capital value of land,
dwelling-houses and shops.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): As the hon. Member is no
doubt aware, my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Home Affairs, appointed a
Committee to consider the question of the
relationship between landlord and tenant
in urban centres in Northern Ireland. He
is awaiting the report of that Committee,
and when it is received any recommendations that it may make will receive the
very careful consideration of the Minister
and the Government.
Pending the report of the Committee,
the Government do not think that any
useful purpose would be served by discussing this question, and it is not in a
position at the moment to make any pronouncement upon it.

10. A requisition for a poll by the
governing body must be authorised by
a resolution of the governing body, and
a copy of the resolution must be delivered to the mayor or chairman
within seven days after the date of the
meeting or any adjournment thereof:
Provided that, if the regulations
governing the meetings of the governing body do not permit of an ordinary
meeting of the governing body being
held within the said seven days, the
time for the delivery of a copy of the
resolution shall be within three days
after the date on which an ordinary
meeting of the governing body can
first be held subsequently to the date
of the meeting of the electors or any
adjournment thereof.

Mr. LESLIE : In Regulation 1 of
Part 2, lines 2 and 3, I beg to move to
leave out the words "in case of his
absence," and to insert instead thereof
the words, in the event of the Mayor
or Chairman being unable or unwilling
to act as presiding officer." I think the
expression, “in case of his absence"
occurs in the Borough Funds Act, 1888,
but I do not think it was ever very happy
because it did not agree very well with
the Second Regulation. Now that we
have followed 'England in introducing
Regulation 3 in Part 1, I think it would
be desirable that we should make ourselves perfectly plain and leave out the
words "in case of the absence" and insert the words on the Paper, which, are
as near as may be the words of the new
Regulation 3 in Part 1. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Regulation 11—
*
*

(b) The decision of the presiding
officer on any question arising in respect of any voting paper shall be final.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in
Regulation 11 to leave out paragraph
(b). This Amendment involves, I think,
some little difficulty. It is obvious that
somebody must decide as to whether any
particular voting paper is or is not valid,
but the original Act of 1888 did not
contain words similar to those to which I
am calling attention. On the other
hand, the English Act of 1903, to which
the Noble Viscount repeatedly drew our
attention, on the Second reading of this
Bill, contained such provisions, and the
difficulty is whether you are to do anything, and, if so, what. It will, I think,
be obvious to the Committee, that it will
be possible on the face of things for the
decision of the presiding officer on a
small number of voting papers to have the
effect of altering the result of a referendum, and that would not seem to be
desirable.
There is no appeal of any
sort given here. The only parallel case
I can find is in the Ballot Act dealing
with Parliamentary elections. There
the same expression is used, but in that
case the Act continues, "subject to reversal on petition." It would be very
difficult, and not very desirable, I think,
to set up here the same machinery as we
have for hearing a petition relating to a
Parliamentary election. At the same
time the position does not seem quite
clear, and it occurred to me that the best
way to direct attention to the matter was
simply to move the omission of the entire
paragraph so that we might be entirely
clear for any alteration the noble Viscount, under advice taken, might suggest
to the Committee. Therefore, for the
moment, at all events, I beg to move the
omission of the whole paragraph.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I cannot accept this Amendment.
It is very necessary that someone should
have authority to decide the validity of
votes and I think it is obvious that the
presiding officer is the proper person to
do that. Of course, in all cases he would
have the advantage of having the advice
of the assessor on technical points, so
that I do not think the circumstances
Senator Leslie has drawn attention to are
likely to arise. Therefore I think I
should prefer to leave the paragraph as
it is.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.

Regulation 14.
Any person who--
(a) fabricates in whole or in part, or
alters, defaces, destroys, abstracts or
purloins any requisition of electors,
voting paper or other document to
which this Schedule applies; or
(b) personates any 'person entitled
to vote at any poll under this Schedule
or
(c) falsely assumes to act in the
name or on behalf of any person
so entitled to vote; or
(d) interferes with the delivery or
collection of any such voting papers, or
(e) delivers any such voting paper
under a false pretence of being lawfully authorised so to do,
to
shall be liable on summary conviction
a penalty not exceeding twenty
pounds, or, in the discretion of the
court, to imprisonment for any period
not exceeding three months.
An attempt to commit any offence
specified in this regulation shall be
punishable in the manner in which the
offence is punishable.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in paragraph (a), of Regulation 14, after the
second word "or" (or alters) to insert the
word "unlawfully." The difficulty here is
that we have nothing to help us in this
Regulation as to the illegality of those
various words "alters," "defaces," and
so on. We have nothing but the word
"fabricates" and I think we require to
introduce the word "unlawfully."
are still dealing with a Regulation that
is forty years old.
We
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
that Amendment with thanks.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: Again in paragraph (a)
of Regulation 14 I beg to move to leave
out the words "abstracts or purloins," and
to insert instead thereof the words "takes
possession of." Both the words, "abstracts" and purloins" are rather antedeluvian and I think that forty years
after their original production we might
get rid of both of them and insert the
words 'or takes possession of." I think
that perhaps the original intention was
to cover a case of actually stealing a document or putting it somewhere where it
could not be found for the time being. If
that was the idea of the draughtsman I
do not think he gave effect to it. I think
the words are somewhat out of date and
ought to be omitted.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
think it is probably a misprint, but the
word "portions" appears on the Order
Paper and not the word "purloins."
The CHAIRMAN: That is a misprint.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: An ordinary person like myself sees very little
difference between 'abstracts or purloins"
and "takes possession of." I do not see
how anyone could abstract or take possession of and not purloin. At the same
time, as the hon Member says, the words
are antedeluvian, and as we do not wish
to be antedeluvian in the year 1928, I am
willing to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in paragraph (e) of Regulation 14 after the word
"do" (so to do) to insert "or (f) attempts
to commit any offence specified in this
regulation." This Amendment is, of
course, merely a matter of arrangement.
It incidentally shortens the Bill. It
occurred to me that as these Regulations
are likely to be reprinted it would be
distinctly desirable to collect all the
offences together and put the penalty for
those offences under them; that is, to
follow the old plan. Therefore, if we introduce the new paragraph (f) dealing
with an attempt to commit any offence
specified in this Regulation and remove
the last two lines of the page we shall
effect that. I move in the first instance
to insert the new paragraph (f).
Viscount CHARLEMONT : This
Amendment and the last one on the Order
Paper can be taken together. They really
purport to redraft the paragraph to secure
the same object as is attained in the Bill
before the Committee. After submitting
the point to the parliamentary draughtsman the Government are of the opinion
that the paragraph is best in its present
form and therefore I cannot accept the
Amendment

THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: Then I ask leave to withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie: -
:-
Regulation 14, paragraph (e) leave out
"three" and insert "four."
Mr. LESLIE: The next Amendment,
if I may say so, deals with almost a comicality. The object of leaving out three
months and inserting four months is not
to increase the maximum amount of imprisonment but to avoid an absurdity
which is due to the fact that the English
draughtsman in 1888 had his mind fixed
on his own Summary Jurisdiction Act in
which three months was the proper alternative to a fine of £20, and he forgot
that in Ireland the proper amount of
imprisonment in default of paying a fine
of £20 in not three months but four
months. The point is that the English
Summary Jurisdiction Act of 1879 and the
Irish Petty Sessions Act of 1851 did not
agree as regards their scales of imprisonment. It therefore seems that if we
leave the penalty three months we lay
ourselves open to the charge of disfavouring the poor at the expense of the rich,
because
of £20 which he cannot pay will have
to go to prison for four months in Ireland
instead of for three months in England.
It is very unlikely the maximum term will
ever be imposed but it is only logical that
we should keep our scale in proportion to
what it has been in the past in comparison with the scale in England. It might
be better to make it six months so as to
provide a margin, but as that is not proposed in the Bill I thought it better to
suggest four months. I accordingly beg
to move.
a
man sentenced to
a
fine
I should
ex-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to point out that a period not
ceeding four months imprisonment is the
penalty not only in default of the payment of a fine of £20 but it is the penalty
in default of the payment of a fine up to
£30. The period of imprisonment in default is not a fixed period but a period
not exceeding three months and there is
no obligation on the magistrates to fix the
maximum period. In these circumstances
I do not think there is any inequality involved and therefore I cannot accept the
Amendment. The person the hon.
Senator has in mind had better commit
his crime in England where he will receive only three months imprisonment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment, for to-day at
all events.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Bill reported with Amendments
Slaughtered Animals (Compensation
Bill [S.].
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
to which I would ask the House to give a
Second Reading to-day is a simple Measure and I think the various clauses are
self-explanatory. I should like, however,
to say a few words in regard to the need
for it. The question dealt with is, of
course, an important one from the point
of view of our live-stock trade.
account
of
Prior to the passing of the Importation
of Animals Act, 1922, the British authorities were, under the Diseases of Animals
Acts, liable to pay compensation for all
animals slaughtered in Great Britain on
foot-and-mouth disease,
whether such slaughter took place in
landing places or elsewhere. By the Act
of 1922, however, the British Ministry relieved itself from the obligation of paying
compensation
imported animals
slaughtered while in landing places. The
result is that at present Irish exporters
for
409 Slaughtered Animals Bill-

23 OCTOBER 1928
Second Reading
are liable to have animals slaughtered at
British ports without compensation.
Strong official representations on the
matter have from time to time been made
to the British authorities, but there is no
hope that the policy embodied in their
Act of 1922 will be reversed.
The serious problem for the Irish export trade which has consequently arisen
has been the subject of an exchange of
views between representatives of the
Ministries of Agriculture of Northern
Ireland and of the Irish Free State and
the conclusion has been arrived at that
the only practicable solution is to establish an insurance scheme whereby exporters would pay a small fee per head
for each animal exported so as to constitute a fund out of which compensation
would be paid as necessity may demand.
The Parliament of the Free State has already passed an Act giving the necessary
authority to set up such an insurance
scheme and it is understood that arrangements are now being made to bring it into
operation at an early date. This Measure
which is before us to-day is on similar
lines and deals with the establishment of
an insurance fund for Northern Ireland
and with the procedure of settling compensation claims which may arise. The
intention of the Bill is that the administration of the fund should be in the hands
of trustees nominated by trade interests
concerned. The scheme will be selfsupporting and no expenditure from
public funds will be involved.
It may further be observed that owing
to difficulties arising out of Section 4 of
the Government of Ireland Act of 1920
it has not been possible heretofore to deal
with this question in a satisfactory way
in this Parliament. Any doubts as to our
powers have, however, now been removed
by the Northern Ireland Miscellaneous
Provisions Act recently passed by the
Imperial Parliament, so that the way is
now clear to deal with the matter. I beg
to move.
Mr. LONG: I should like to support what I regard as a very important
Bill. It is, I think, a very necessary
Measure. No doubt it contains some
things that might be objectionable, but
I think they will be removed in administration. There is no doubt that in the
course of time the trustees will amass a
very considerable amount of money by
a
sum
charging 2d per head on cattle, 11d on
sheep, and d on pigs, and I should like
to be informed how the trustees would
deal with a case of some magnitude before they had accumulated
sufficient to meet the bill in that case.
I see that by Clause 14 they are enabled
to borrow, but I suggest that it might
simplify matters if the Government
would start them in housekeeping with
a
sum of money which would afterwards be repaid. I should also like to
be informed whether any appeal lies
from these trustees in the event of their
deciding that no compensation was payable, and to what body the appeal would
lie.
The custom in a good many districts
in the country is for the exporter of
cattle to buy a very considerable number
of cattle for which he does not pay cash.
Most of these exporters pay in a week
from the time of taking delivery. I can
recall the case of an exporter with whom
I was slightly acquainted. He made a
very large purchase of cattle, the greater
portion of which was not paid for. But
he was a solvent man and the farmers
from whom he bought cattle were quite
satisfied to allow him to take delivery of
the cattle without payment. It turned
out, however, that on landing at Birkenhead his cattle were found to be infected
with foot and mouth disease. The result
was that this perfectly solvent decent
man was rendered practically penniless
through no fault of his own. I am sure
hon. Senators can realise the dismay and
pain that this man and his wife and
children experienced.
I think this Bill
will prevent such a thing happening in
the future and I heartily commend it to
the support of the House.
Mr. LESLIE: Like the last speaker I
desire to support the Measure now before
the House. A Bill of this sort has been
talked about for some time past and representations I doubt not have been made
to other hon. Members on the subject as
well as to myself. One of the points
which was brought before me was the loss
which dealers suffer owing, not to the
slaughter of their cattle, but to the detention of their cattle for lengthy periods
before they were allowed to let them
loose over Great Britain. That question
of detention is not dealt with in the Bill
but I imagine it has been before the
Government and they found it difficult
to provide for that. No doubt the noble
411 Slaughtered Animals Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
Viscount will inform us as to that at an
early opportunity.
Another point is that under the Bill
compensation is restricted to the slaughter
of cattle on account of real or suspected
foot and mouth disease. The Bill stops
there. I should like to know what is
proposed, if anything, as regards cattle
slaughtered in respect of pleuro-pneumonia, cattle plague, swine with swine
fever, or sheep with sheep scab, or any
disease which is a disease for the purposes of the Diseases of Animals Act. I
believe there is a provision in existence
for securing compensation in those matters if the disease arises in Northern Ireland, but I do not see anything in the
Bill before the House which deals with
those possibilities arising on the other
side. Of course, up to now the chief difficulty has been foot and mouth disease,
but we cannot shut our eyes to such
things as cattle plague, pleuro-pneumonia
and other diseases of animals.
Then I see there is very generous provision as regards cattle to which the provisions of this Bill are to relate. Apparently
any beast which has been exported from
Northern Ireland, whether before the Act
passed into law or not, is to be subject to
compensation. There is no time limit
fixed at all. The only approach to a
time limit seems to be the large power
given to the trustees to give only such
compensation as in the circumstances they
think just. Nor is anything said as to
whether, in the case of cattle exported
from Northern Ireland which have become the subject of compensation, any
payment for insurance is to be collected
in respect of them. That is a point on
which we ought to receive information
either now or a little later on.
I think, too, we should be satisfied as to
what is to happen to the accumulated
funds in the event of the association referred to in the Bill coming to an end.
We are told that the Minister of Agriculture may take steps for the performance of the duties of the trustees, but we
are not told that the Minister of Agriculture is to have power over the accumulated funds. I hope that information on
the points to which I have drawn attention will be given to us in due course, and
I conclude, as I began, by supporting the
Bill, which is much desired by those engaged in the export of cattle from
Northern Ireland.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to support this Bill because I think it is a
very good example of the very beneficient
legislation which the Government has introduced for the benefit of the producer.
One might have thought that the fact
that the necessity for this Bill was made so
apparent in 1922 would have induced the
exporters of cattle to put their heads together with the object of starting some
voluntary insurance fund. But that was
not done and the only thing left to be
done was for a beneficent Government to
step into the breach. I am sure the exporter who realises that he is liable to
sustain a very great loss will not grudge
paying 2ld or lad or ld for his animals
to be insured. I notice that Senator
Long's question as to how the initial
money is to be raised is answered in Clause
14, which gives power to the trustees to
borrow, and I presume they will borrow
under Government guarantee. I heartily
support the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
prefer to give a more detailed answer to
the various points that have been raised
at a later stage. I might say that the
Bill is to a certain extent retrospective
in that it goes back to 1923, but I am informed that though one or two men have
been hit very severely by the slaughter
of animals the total sum involved is not
very great. With regard to the other
diseases, including pleuro-pneumonia and
cattle plague, they have been non-existent
in these countries for many years and it
is devoutly to be hoped they will not become common again. This Bill refers
only to a disease that is likely to occur
and does not deal with those diseases
which, though possible, are much more
unlikely to break out. The question of
detention is a very difficult one. It is
very hard to define what detention is,
whether it is a day or two days or longer,
and there is no question of including anything of that nature in this Bill because
it has been found impossible to do so.
There is no appeal from the trustees.
Under the provision of the Bill, if the
trustees are not carrying out their duties
properly, they can be removed, and of
course there would be an appeal to the
Ministry of Agriculture in that sense,
but it would be an unofficial appeal. Any
other points to which I have not replied
I will deal with at a later stage.
On Question, Motion (that the Bill be
now read a Second Time) agreed to.
Sittings of the Senate. 414
Adjournment-
23 OCTOBER 1928
E

Sittings of the Senate.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, that this House do now adjourn
until Thursday.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
I do not know whether I am in order or
not, but I desire to raise a point about
the sittings of the Senate. Some of the
Senators are brought here from a considerable distance, and I think the
sittings of the House might be more continuous than they are. The last adjournment of the House was for a week
and now, after mecting for one day, we
are adjourning until Thursday. I would
like to ask if some arrangement could be
made with the other House whereby the
Senate would be enabled to sit more continuously than it does at present.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The question the hon. Senator has raised is one
that affects all revising Chambers so far
as I can learn. There is always a dearth
of Bills at the beginning of a Session and
a plethora of Bills at the end of the
Session, and owing to the fact that an interval must elapse between the various
stages of Bills I do not see how these
short sittings can be avoided, although I
admit they are inconvenient. I would
much prefer to sit from 3 o'clock until
the middle of the night and then have
no more sittings for six months. That
would suit me extremely well, but the
difficulty is that unless some Senator made
a speech of three or four hours duration
it would be impossible to give practical
effect to that plan. I will do my best,
but I do not see any arrangement other
than the present one can be devised.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
agree with the noble Leader of the House,
but the same difficulty arises in the revising Chamber across the water, and the
feeling of Members of the Upper House
there is shared by Senators here, namely,
that the convenience of the Lower House
is consulted by the Government more than
the convenience of the Upper House.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope that whatever
arrangement is made the noble Leader of
the House will not shut his eyes to the
fact that the Senate really does require
a certain interval between the various
stages of a Bill in order to study its provisions. It is impossible to take the
Second Reading of a Bill to-day, the Committee stage to-morrow, and the Third
Reading the day after that. I am sure
the noble Viscount will not lose sight of
that.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is just
because I do not lose sight of that that
I find it impossible to alter the present
arrangement. We must have an interval
between the different stages of the Bills,
and I cannot produce more Bills than are
sent down from another place.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Could not some of the Bills be delayed
until others had been sent up?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That is
possible, but the assumption is that all
Bills sent up to us are urgent and that
the country at large is eagerly awaiting
their passage into law.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four
o'clock until Thursday next.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 25th October, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Egan's Divorce Bill
Resolved: That this Bill be now read
a Second time.
moment legislation on the lines of the
Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1928,
recently passed by the Imperial Parliament. As to when this Bill will be introduced I am afraid I cannot say, but I
can give the hon. Senator the assurance
that it will in any event be introduced as
one of the first Measures of the forthcoming Spring Session.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read a Second time
McMullan's Divorce Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
They have passed the Marketing of
Potatoes Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
CRIMINAL LAW AMENDMENT
ACT.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
To ask the Leader of the House
whether it is the intention of the Governmen during the present Session to introduce a Bill to amend, on the lines of the
Act passed by the Imperial Parliament
in August last, the law in Northern Ireland relating to the time limit for prosecuting certain offences against females;
and, if not, whether facilities would be
given to such a Bill if introduced by a
Private Member.
Mr. LESLIE: In asking the unstarred
question which stands in my name I
think it is only necessary to explain that
the Imperial Act to which I refer will
come into operation on 1st January next
year, and that the time limit to which it
relates is in respect of the offence of unlawful carnal knowledge of a girl under
sixteen. The period for prosecuting such
an offence at the moment in Great Britain
and Northern Ireland is nine months.
After 1st January next year it will be
twelve months in Great Britain. I need
not, I think, say more than that.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the question of the
hon. Senator, I should like to say that
this question has been receiving the attention of the Ministry of Home Affairs
for some time past. It has been decided
to introduce at the earliest possible
McMullan's Divorce Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Marketing of Potatoes Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time. (Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

there is no change from those which were

quoted in the Schedule, and I rise only

Sir WILLIAM COATES: I have a
number of Amendments on the Order
Paper to-day in reference to this Bill.
The first refers to the Bill itself, and the
others refer to the Schedule to the Bill.
The Amendments are largely matters of
detail which the experience of the Belfast
Corporation has shown to be necessary in
view of the antiquated working of the
old Act of 1888. I may say that the
Bill as a whole is warmly welcomed and
approved by the Corporation of Belfast
and the new system of putting a proposed
Bill to the electors is very desirable. The
first Amendment which I have to propose is one in connection with the text
of the Bill. It is in Clause 1, page 2,
line 25, to leave out from "when” to the
end of sub-section (4), and insert:
"allowed under the principal Act, be
chargeable upon the funds or rates upon
which the expenses of promoting such
Bills are made chargeable under the
principal Act."
This Amendment proposes to do away
with the necessity of having the expenses
of an abortive Bill taxed by the taxing
officer of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland. I would point out that such
a provision is not necessary and is not in
conformity with the old Borough Funds
Act of 1888, under which the sole authority appointed to tax the expenses of the
Bill was the auditor of the Local Government Board. All authorities who will be
affected by the Bill are subject to this
audit, and as a matter of fact it is the
custom in connection with Bills promoted
by the Belfast Corporation not to provide for the taxation of the costs of the
Bill by the taxing officer of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, but to allow
the matter to be dealt with in the ordinary way by the public auditor. With
this explanation I beg to move the
Amendment which stands in my name.
Mr. LESLIE: Before we conclude the
discussion on that Amendment I should
Borough Funds Bill-
Report Stage.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
like to ask whether authorities other than
the Belfast Corporation have been consulted in this matter.
The Bill will
apply not solely to the Belfast Corporation but to other public bodies throughout the Six Counties, and one would like
to be assured that this and the various
other Amendments have received their
approval. I welcome the presentation
to this House of a series of Amendments
by any hon. Member who has gone into a
particular Bill, but the time we have had
for considering these Amendments has been
very short indeed. I think none of us
saw them until we arrived in the House
this afternoon, and it does seem necessary that we should lay stress on the
point that other bodies besides the Belfast Corporation are entitled to be heard
on them.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I would
like to answer Senator Leslie by saying
that of course every authority in Northern Ireland has its accounts audited,
and from that point of view the procedure here is rather different to the procedure in England. There they have no
public auditor as we have here, and the
question of the costs of a Bill would in
Northern Ireland be entirely subject to
the public auditor's purview.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I understand that no other
authorities have been consulted with
regard to the costs of a local Bill being
taxed, but I have no reason to anticipate
that they would not welcome the Amendment. Therefore the Government do not
propose to offer any objection to it.
Amendment agreed to.

Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move in the Schedule, Part I, page 4,
Regulation 1 (d) to leave out 'five" (five
in the afternoon) and insert "half-past
four." It is desired to limit the time
for the purchase and inspection of a Bill.
Instead of it being, as in the Bill at present, from ten to five on weekdays and
on Saturdays, we desire to limit it to from
ten to four-thirty on weekdays and from
ten to 12-30 on Saturdays. It is a small
matter, but I trust the Government will
see their way to agree to it.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared to
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: There is a
further Amendment in the same paragraph (d), after the word "day" (every
weekday) to insert "except Saturday, and
between the hours of ten in the forenoon
and half-past twelve in the afternoon on
every Saturday." I beg to move.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government have no objection to this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
9. A requisition for a poll by electors
must be in writing signed by the persons
making it, and must be delivered to the
mayor or chairman within seven days
after the date of the meeting or any
adjournment thereof.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment I have to move is in Regulation 9. It is to leave out the words "in
writing," and insert in the form set out
in Part III of this Schedule, and be." It
is proposed in the Bill that a requisition
for a poll must be in writing, but it is
desired to have notice of a poll given in
the form set out in Part III of the Schedule. The reason for this is that it is
considered necessary for the purpose of
checking signatures with the register of
electors that the necessary information
should be given by those who demand a
poll. I think the Amendment is very
desirable, and I trust it will be accepted.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not like to keep
rising to bring up what is the same point,
but I do think this is a matter for which
there should be some little time given for
consideration. To present on the Report
stage a very important proposal of this
kind requiring that notice shall be given
Borough Funds Bill-
Report Stage.
25 OCTOBER 1928
B
in the form set out in the Schedule when
both the notice itself and the form appear
for the first time is really, I think, somewhat unfair to the House. There would
have been an opportunity of bringing
these matters forward both on the Second
Reading and on the Committee stage, and
those opportunities were not availed of. I
do not think the House ought to be asked
to accept this proposal this afternoon.
The Amendment might perhaps be moved
A again after the Third Reading.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: So far as the Government are
concerned they are prepared to accept this
Amendment, but the hon. Member who
has just spoken desires some further time
to consider the proposal. Therefore, I
suggest it should be brought up again on
the Third Reading.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Icertainly feel very much inclined to support
the view expressed by Senator Leslie.
Other public bodies may have reason to
complain of these Amendments if they are
rushed through without any notice. They
may injuriously affect other bodies although they are satisfactory to the Belfast
Corporation, and the statement may be
made that this Parliament is only out to
satisfy one particular body and is unconcerned about others.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I am quite
agreeable to the suggestion of the noble
Viscount that this Amendment should be
left over for the Third Reading. I agree
that the time for its consideration has been
short. As a matter of fact I was only put
in possession of these facts and figures
yesterday, and if it is the desire of the
House that this and other Amendments
should be left over to the Third Reading
I will agree to that course.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Part II (Regulations for taking a poll.)
4. The presiding officer shall, within
three days after the expiration of the
time within which a poll may be required under Part I of this Schedule,
but not less than seven days before the
day appointed for the collection of the
voting: papers, publish a notice signed
by hun and specifying the date and
place of the said meeting, the resolution or resolutions proposed thereat,
the facts that a poll was required and
will be taken upon any such resolution,
the day or days on which the voting
papers will be delivered, and the day
on which they will be collected, and
the place for the examination and
casting up of the votes, and shall also
cause copies of such notice to be affixed
on the usual place or places for affixing
notices in the district.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I beg to
move, in Part II, Regulation 4, after
"the" (before the day) to insert "first."
In this instance the point is that three
days are given for the distribution of
voting papers in connection with any poll
it may be necessary to take on a Bill promoted by any local authority. On the
other hand, the papers have to be collected on one day. In the first place that
is almost a physical impossibility, and in
the second place in the case of houses
that are closed the papers are not collected at all in many cases. It is therefore suggested that it would be well to
allow three days for collection as well as
three days for distribution, and that is
the object of this Amendment.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared
to accept this Amendment, and I do not
think any local authority could possibly
object to it.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment I have to move is in line 6
of Regulation 4. It is to leave out the
words "proposed thereat" and to insert
"with respect to which a poll has been
required." Under the new arrangements
some parts of a Bill might be voted on
favourably and some might be defeated.
It is not desired therefore to put every
one to the electors, but only those in respect of which a poll has been required.
I think that will make the Amendment
clear, and I beg to move.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are prepared to
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-

Sir WILLIAM COATES: The next
Amendment standing in my name is, at
the end of Regulation 5 (b) to insert
"The presiding officer shall also insert
on each voting paper the name and number on the register and polling district
letter of the elector, and the address at
which the voting paper of such elector is
to be delivered."
This Amendment deals with a case
where there are four voters in one house.
When four voting papers are left at that
house it is almost impossible for the occupiers to say which paper belongs to
each person. Consequently it is thought
desirable that the name of the elector,
his number, his polling district letter,
and his address should form part of the
voting paper. I think this is a very wise
thing, and I hope it will be accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: I would ask that this
proposal also should stand over for Third
Reading. It necessitates reference to the
principal Act.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: So far as I
am concerned I am agreeable that it
should stand over.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

II.-(a) The presiding officer shall,
on the day immediately following the
day of the collection of the voting
papers, and on as many days immediately succeeding as may be necessary,
attend at the place appointed for the
examination and casting up of the
votes, and ascertain the validity of the
votes by an examination of the Register of Electors for the time being, and
by examining such persons as he may
see fit. He shall cast up such of the
votes as he finds to be valid and to have
been duly given, collected or received,
and shall ascertain the number of such
votes respectively in favour of and
against the adoption of a resolution.

required a poll may themselves attend
or appoint an agent to attend the examination and casting up of the votes
given thereat; and any person so attending who obstructs or in any way
interferes with the examination and
casting up of the votes may, by order
of the presiding officer, be forthwith
removed from the place appointed for
that purpose, and if so removed shall
not be permitted to return.

The word "day" should read "last."
Sir WILLIAM COATES: My next

accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

14. shall be liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding

The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:-
Page 8, Regulation 14, after 'twenty
pounds or' insert "in default of payment of such penalty, or, alternatively."
for
a
or to
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment relates to a matter to which I referred when
the Bill was in Committee.
I pointed
out then that it was possible that
although Parliament was providing a
maximum period of imprisonment for
a possible offence yet it was possible
to be sentenced man
have to serve a longer term of imprisonment because he could not pay the
fine which is provided by Parliament as an
alternative, and presumably a preferable
alternative, to imprisonment. I suggested that in order to bring the two
points into line we should raise the period
of imprisonment from three to four
months, but that suggestion did not find
favour, and I must say I am glad it did
not. Another alternative would be to reduce the amount of the fine so as to
make that correspond, but I think
better way still is to use the words I have
on the Paper, or some other words if a
better form of words can be found. I
think that if time were given to consult
the older Statutes a better form of words
might be found.
a
I suggest that imprisonment in default
of payment should be limited by Statute
to three months. It is, as was pointed
out to me, quite possible under the Petty
Sessions Act for magistrates who issue
warrants of distress to limit the period of
imprisonment to some period less than the
maximum, but the fact remains that it is
possible for a man to serve more imprisonment because he cannot pay a fine than he
would have to serve if he had been
sentenced to imprisonment in the first instance. That cannot possibly be the intention of Parliament, nor do I think it is
in itself desirable. Accordingly I have put
down this form of words, but if a more
satisfactory form of words can be suggested I shall be only too happy to agree
to it.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The object of this Amendment is,
as the hon. Senator has said, similar to
the Amendment he proposed on the Committee stage of this Bill, increasing the
period of imprisonment from three to
four months. That is to say, the hon.
Member wishes to secure uniformity between the penalty for default in payment of a fine of £20 and the penalty of
imprisonment without the option of a
fine. The provision for penalties contained in the Bill follows that contained
in the Borough Funds (Ireland) Act,
1888. This provision has therefore been
in force for forty years and there is
nothing to show that any difficulty has
ever resulted from it. . In fact, I may
say that no difficulty can possibly arise if
it is administered with common sense, as
the period of imprisonment under the
Petty Sessions Act for default of payment of a fine of £20 is a term not exceeding four months. In other words,
the term is not a fixed term and allows
discretion as to the length of imprisonment up to four months. No hard and
fast rule has been followed in legislation
so as to make the period of imprisonment
the same as might be imposed in default
of payment of a fine. For example, the
penalty for a third offence under Section
1 of the Motor Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act, 1926, is a fine not exceeding £50 or imprisonment not exceeding
three months.
Under these circumstances the Government do not consider
the Amendment necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I could have wished the
noble Viscount had told us how many
prosecutions there had been under the Act
of 1888. I do not think there have been
any at all. It is an academic subject,
and it seemed to me that an academic
Bill provided a very good opportunity
to deal with the point. It must receive
attention when we consider our Summary Jurisdiction Act. However, I do
not think it is worth while putting the
House to a division, and I will withdraw
the Amendment.

Colonel PAKENHAM: In rising to

stands in my name on the Order Paper I

general and that any alleviation of these

by the subscribers to the service.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): My right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Commerce, has made enquiries and learns that additional telephone circuits have recently been provided between Crumlin and Antrim and
between Antrim and Belfast, the installation of the latter having been 1. Regulations governing the Certification, Recognition, Payment of Salaries, and Conditions of Employment of

McMULLAN'S DIVORCE BILL.
