[Mr. Leslie]
ties, perhaps, of administration. As regards the difficulties of apportionment one
may, perhaps, say that in this Bill
industrial and freight-transport hereditaments are not entirely mutually exclusive.
It does not seem to me to matter very
much whether they are or are not from
one point of view, because the percentage
of relief is to be the same although the
ultimate destination of that relief is different. The question seems to come up
rather in connection with a matter which
affects us, although I do not think it
affects our friends in Great Britain, and
that is in the case of those motor services
which we have here as ancillary to our
railway companies, the motor services
which are used by the railway companies
under our Act of 1927, and will be used
more perhaps in the future for the conveyance of passengers with or without
their luggage either directly to their
destinations or partly as feeders for the
railway systems.
a
matter
Now railway motor services of that sort
are distinctly different from the ordinary
omnibus motor service. The ordinary
omnibus motor service is not rated in any
way in respect of the roads over which it
runs and, therefore, such omnibus service
would not have any claim that I can see
to share in the relief which it is proposed
to give to railways, which do run along
the railways they have made and in
respect of which they are rated directly.
Whether it is intended or not that such
hereditaments as are directly attributable
to what I call auxiliary railway motor
services-whether it is intended that they
should share in this relief is
which, perhaps, the Minister of Finance
(Mr. Pollock) will be able to tell us, if
not to-day at all events on some future
date. I must confess that I myself am
not particularly hopeful that the scope of
the Measure does extend to such hereditaments as I have mentioned, but I
would, however, like to know, because if
it is intended that the Measure should so
extend it would be necessary to make
some very trivial alterations in the text of
the Bill. I believe it would be necessary
to move three Amendments involving, I
think, the omission of three words and
the substitution of four others. That
would be the aggregate verbal alteration
that would be necessary, but the material
difference would be very great. I hope
we will be told that to-day one way or the
other.
I
Passing to the question of administration, most of it will be done by the county
councils, and they will work principally
on the directions given in the Schedule.
The only point I should like to make
about that is that it seems to me that
the time allowed to the county councils is insufficient for the purpose.
rather doubt whether all our county
councils, having regard to the slowness of
the rural posts, can perfect their final
lists within fourteen days, but I am assured by a really competent authority
that if fourteen days is too little it is not
much too little. The question of Belfast,
where they have no county council but
where they have a substitute for a county
council which is called a Corporation, and
its very large number of industrial hereditaments will, I have no doubt, be dealt
with by someone having more knowledge
of the city of Belfast than I have.
all events, I think we may take it that
this period of fourteen days and the other
periods mentioned in the Bill will be understood as being directory and not mandatory, and, therefore, it is not necessary
for us to make any suggestion for extension here. If, in fact, any alteration of
that sort is necessary I think it could best
be achieved by allowing a little time to
elapse. There must necessarily be some
time between this Bill and its successor,
and during that time I imagine full information can be obtained on a matter of
that sort, and if it should be necessary
to make any definite alteration it could
I suggest, be very easily done.
At
Now, I have spoken for a moment of
the future. There is one word I want
to say about the Bill which is to follow
this one, more or less a technical point
perhaps. I do hope that when we get
the final Bill, that is, the second chapter,
the old expression "Irish Valuation Act"
will disappear. It is quite time we had
a little valuation code of our own, and
I should like the new Bill to be so worded
that it will end with the words "This
Act shall be construed and may be cited"
-if that is the appropriate expression-
"with the Northern Ireland Valuation
Acts, 1852 to 1929." I shall hope to see,
at the same time, Section 24 of the Intrepretation Act repealed, and then we
shall get rid of the expression "Irish
Valuation" and start right afresh in Northern Ireland.
Taking the matter as a whole I am
certain, as my hon. Friend (Mr. Long)
645 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading
=
opposite said, that this Bill will be
accepted in the Province as an instalment,
perhaps, of an old promise, not made by
our own Government, that when opportunity served something should be done
for realty at the expense, perhaps, of
personalty, but even more that it will be
accepted as a necessary instalment of the
really great thing that is going to be done
for the Province by one of the Parliaments of the United Kingdom under the
leadership of a Government which has
done so much to keep the Province which
is to benefit within the United Kingdom.
It is a benefit that Northern Ireland
would not have got, as I think my hon.
Friend (Mr. Long) said, had she joined up
with the Free State. It is a benefit that
the farmers and industrialists of Northern Ireland will get by virtue of their
continued connection with the United
Kingdom. For that reason alone the
Bill will be received with gratification,
and I hope it will also be received with
some gratitude to those who are more immediately responsible for bringing the
matter before this House. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
as
a
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not wish
to detain the House, for nearly all that
has been said is what I should have liked
to have said myself in favour of the Bill.
But I would not like the Bill to go further without,
a representative of
agriculture in this community, thanking
the Government for introducing
Measure that will be one of the greatest
boons to the farmers of Northern Ireland that they have ever received. I do
not know that the reduction in freighttransport charges will benefit the smaller
farmers to any great extent. It will, of
course, benefit the larger dealers and the
bigger men, but at my station to-day
there was a small farmer who had got
delivery of 6 cwt. of Indian meal, and
when the stationmaster got him to sign
the form the ten per cent. reduction on
the carriage of that amounted to 2d. He
asked where this came from; the stationmaster said it was a refund from the
Government, and his reply was that he
thought it would not go to his head.
| mentioned, is to know where the money
is going to come from for schemes connected with the county. I am very much
afraid that the borrowing powers of the
councils for the building of new schools,
hospitals, or asylums, as my hon. Friend
(Mr. Long) has said, will be considerably
curtailed. We will be very much obliged
if the Minister of Finance, who is so welcome here to-day, will let us know if the
borrowing powers of the councils will not
be curtailed when the rateable value of
all land in the county is taken out of their
hands.
Mr. DORMAN: I find it difficult to
speak on a Measure such as this. As a
Socialist I look upon this as an acknowledgment on the part of the Government
of the failure of our present method of
producing and distributing
wealth.
Practically every stage of the production
of wealth consumed in the community
will be covered by this Derating Measure.
I would very much like to see the duty of
passing on the advantage to the community imposed not merely upon those
who are engaged in the freight end of the
industries dealt with in this particular
Bill. We have no guarantee that the
freedom from rates given to the agriculturists will be passed on to the vast masses
of the consumers of the agricultural proour area, nor have we any
guarantee that it will be passed on from
the productive end of industry to the conduce of
sumers.
What I understand will take place will
be this: Immediately the saleable value
of farms will go up, the market price of
productive shares will go up, and the advantage of this Derating Bill will not be
enjoyed by the masses of the consumers.
It is not the first time that a scheme of
derating has been found necessary. After
the long Napoleonic wars a derating
scheme was tried in Great Britain at the
lower end of the social scale, and it was
found that derating did not ultimately
effect the object the Government had in
view. At that particular period hand
labour was largely being displaced by
machine production; great misery was
prevalent amongst the community, and it
was found necessary because of the shortage of house accommodation and the
poverty of the lower classes of the community to derate working-class houses.
As an agriculturist I certainly welcome
the Bill and thank the Government for
its introduction. As a member of the
county council and other boards, our
difficulty, as the right hon. and gallant
Now the working classes did not obtain
Gentleman (Colonel Sharman Crawford) | the advantage of that derating scheme,
647 Rating and Valuation Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
and, if my memory serves me right, a
Parliamentary Committee set up across
the water investigated the results and
found that practically in all cases the derating did not result in any advantage
to the people it was intended to benefit.
If my memory is correct, people launched
into the excessive erection of dwellinghouses with a view to obtaining higher
rentals than they otherwise would have
had if the de-rating scheme had not been
in existence. I do not know how the
Government will arrange that the advantages of this de-rating scheme will be
obtained by the masses of the community.
My own opinion is that it is a proof of
the failure of private enterprise and of
competition to supply the necessary needs
of the community. I believe that this
de-rating scheme has originated in similar
causes to those that led to the de-rating
scheme in the early part of last century,
and that ultimately it will prove as ineffectual to remedy the evils that at
present exist in society.
I am sure the agriculturists will all be
thankful for this Measure. Roughly
speaking, if the burden had to be borne
locally, it would amount to 15s. a head
of the population. If the Imperial Parliament is giving to Northern Ireland a
grant of 15s. a head per annum for derating, when we ought to be thankful to
the Imperial Government.
Mr. BARCLAY: I might say, with
others, that I welcome this Bill which is
before the House to-day, and which has
as its object the assistance of our industries, especially our competitive industries;
but at the same time I am not satisfied
that the relief that is going to be given
to the rates will lower the cost of production to such an extent that it will
improve trade. We have heard a great
deal this afternoon of the benefits that
are to be derived from this de-rating
scheme, so far as the agricultural community are concerned, but I did not hear
very much said about the benefits to be
derived from it by other industries, such
as the linen industry, the shipbuilding
industry, and all the other industries that
we have in the city of Belfast, upon which
hundreds and thousands of people depend
for their daily bread.
The reason I make the statement that I
am afraid it will not improve trade to
such an extent is that the Government
have not half enough money at their disposal for the purpose of financing all the
industries to such an extent that they will
be able to reduce the cost of the products
which they put upon the competitive
market. I do not know the amount that
the Chancellor of the Exchequer has past
him for this purpose, but from what I
saw in the paper the other evening it
would not go very far. A million of
money has been mentioned to-day. What
would a million of money do in the North
of Ireland to assist all our competitive
industries? It would not go very far.
With your permission, Mr. Speaker,
and with the permission of the Houseif I am in order-I would like to say that
personally I believe an extension of the
Safeguarding of Industries Act would
have served the purpose in view much
better than the relief proposed to be given
to the rates under this Bill. I do not
know whether I am in order in making
that statement at the present time, but
I am fully convinced in my own mind
that it would do so, because our industries
are calling for safeguarding, and if we
had a safeguarding scheme we would not
be taking the money out of Peter's pocket
and putting it into Paul's. We would be
taking it out of the foreigner's pocket,
and putting it into the pockets of our own
people in this country. If we look back
over the industries that have been safeguarded the results go to prove that we
are quite able to hold our own in the
competitive markets, provided that we
get a fair chance. I am sorry to say it
is not within the jurisdiction of our
Government to safeguard our industries.
We depend upon the Imperial Government in that connection, and I would
have been very pleased if the Imperial
Government, instead of bringing in a derating scheme such as this, had brought
in an extension of the Safeguarding of
Industries Act instead.
We have heard a great deal this afternoon as to the great benefit that is going
to accrue to the agricultural community
from this Measure, and I agree that it
is going to be of great benefit to the
agricultural industry. I was brought up
on a farm myself. I know all the sorrows
and sufferings, the weeping, the wailing
and gnashing of teeth, of the farmers
especially in a bad year, and I know from
personal experience the difficulties that
649 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading

they have to face. Of course my experience was gained a good number of years
ago. I agree, however, with Senator
Long, that sometimes a farmer works the
whole year round, and, when he pays all
his just debts-that is, if he is a just man
-he has scarcely as much left as buys
himself a pair of boots or a pair of
corduroy trousers.
I remember the time when we used to
sell flax in the market for something like
5/6 to 7/6. We used to get 30s to 40s per
cwt. for our pork, and I have taken two
loads of potatoes to market with my
father, and got 13d a cwt. Things are
not as bad now as they were then, but so
far as I understand things are very bad
at the present time. The price of cattle
is coming down; the price of pork is
coming down; the prices of other farm
produce are coming down, and so farmers
are very badly hit at the present time. I
was very pleased to hear it said to-day
that they are going to benefit by this
relief to the rates, but what strikes me is
this is this relief to the rates going to
make the farmer produce anything more
than he is producing at the present time?
I dare say there are a number of hardworking farmers, men of business
capacity, who will take advantage of this
relief, and who will use this money for
the purpose of buying seed, buying extra
manure or paying wages for the breaking
up of land that is lying at the present
time under grass. I agree that a certain
number of farmers will do that, but, on
the other hand, if I know anything about
some of them, they will take a deep sigh
of relief, and perhaps they will say,
"Thank God for that; that is a tremendous burden removed from my shoulders."
They may be tempted to say, in the words
of the Old Book:
Take thine ease, eat, drink and be
merry--
for the great burden of rates that was
upon you at one time is now removed,
and hangs about the neck of the Chancellor of the Exchequer.
I would like to know how pressure could
be brought to bear on those men to cause
them to produce something extra for this
relief. But from what I know of the
agricultural community, especially of those
who want to do well, they really do not
want relief of rates or subsidies, or any
a
kind of charitable gift, so much as they
want a market and prices for their products. If the farmers have a market and
prices for their products, they will produce the goods; the only way we can get
the farmer to produce the goods is to give
him a price for them. We have one of
the best markets within our reach, within
a few hours' journey of our own shores.
I refer to the British market. We have
in Great Britain
great industrial
population that needs to be fed, and that
requires the products that Northern Ire-'
land is quite capable of producing; and
if we could obtain better prices I have
not the least doubt Great Britain would
take from us all that we could possibly
give them. There is put upon the British
market annually about twenty-two million
pounds' worth of beef. That is one thing
we could produce a great deal more of in
Northern Ireland than we are doing at
the present time, and I understand that
cattle are coming down in price. There
is also put upon the market in Great
Britain about ninety million pounds
worth of mutton. We could produce a
great deal more mutton than we are
doing. There is also put upon that market a million pounds worth of poultry.
There are three articles of food, three
commodities, that we in Northern Ireland
are quite capable of putting upon the
British market.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon.
Member will not develop that argument.
I have allowed him to go on for some
time, but I hope he will get back to the
question before the House. His remarks
are not in order.
Mr. ARMSTRONG rose.
Mr. SPEAKER: Mr. Barclay has
not finished, I think.
Mr. BARCLAY: I am sorry, but I
think I have wandered a little too far.
However, I was just going to say that we
have a market in Great Britain for our
products, and what was troubling me
most was this-whether the Ulster farmer
is going to take advantage of this market
now that he is going to receive this relief
in rates by putting more goods upon the
market. We have the people in Great
Britain who will buy the goods.
doubt this Measure will be of great benefit
to Northern Ireland. I have great pleasure in supporting the Motion for the
Second Reading of this Bill.
No
631 Rating and Valuation Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
a
Mr. ARMSTRONG: At this stage of
the debate, I should be very sorry to detain the Senate for more than a few
moments, but reference has been made in
the course of the proceedings to the relief
of rating on farmers' houses. I think it
is well for us to remember that, in
small farming community, that is almost
a negligible quantity. On looking over
the rate books, I find that on the houses
in my county-which I think is a fair
example of them all the rates are from
35s up to £4 or £5.
The relief to the
farmer in the case of a house of £2 a year
would only be 4s, and so on in proportion, so that whether he gets one-third or
one-half is perfectly immaterial. I
think that the fears of my hon. Friend
opposite-perhaps not my political
Friend (Senator Dorman)-are groundless in regard to farmers putting up the
prices to the consumers. Foreign competition will be quite sufficient to prevent
that. My hon. Friend, Senator Barclay,
is evidently not an ardent Free Trader,
and I do not suppose there is anybody in
the Senate who is, but at the same time
that is not a subject that we can discuss
on this Bill. I am sure the farmers and
the owners of every sort of hereditament
ought to be extremely grateful to the
Government for this bountiful Measure.
I believe they are so. There is
criticism among the people about it. Any
criticism we hear is engineered.
no
Mr. POLLOCK: I appreciate the
privilege that is offered to me of joining
in the discussion in regard to this
complicated Measure, and I shall
endeavour to abbreviate as much as
possible in touching upon the various
points that have been raised by hon.
Senators. In order not to lose any time
I will get right into the heart of things if I
can. The right hon. Senator (Colonel Sharman Crawford) evidently labours under
the fear that both the difficulties of the
finance and the amounts involved would
present some trouble and, therefore, that
some interests might suffer. I think the
right hon. Senator misapprehended a
little the figures given by the noble
Viscount in respect of the cost of the
operation. Perhaps I had better state
them with such definiteness that there
can be no doubt about them
Already there is in existence relief
given to agricultural rates amounting to
£256,000. That relief has been given for
years, and consequently that amount does
not enter into our calculations for the
future. I think I have made that point
clear. Under the present scheme it is
estimated that the relief on agricultural
lands and buildings will amount to
£512,000, and the relief given to industry will amount to £120,000. Then
again, and this is a matter that perhaps
is not clearly understood up to the present, there must necessarily be some
money provided in order to relieve those
interests that may be affected prejudicially by the reduction in the area of rating,
that is to say, that the county councils
would be prejudiced because they would
not have the same area to draw from in
respect of rating, and for that purpose
we have provisionally estimated that a
sum of £75,000 a year would be necessary.
a
to
Indeed, in the Schedule to the Report
which I see lying on the Table, there is
further estimated maximum
indemnify the smaller urban areas
against any immediate increase for rating
purposes. So you see that as far as it
was possible to estimate the difficulties
that would naturally arise, the Government, in their estimate at any rate, have
realized how much money will have to be
provided. At this point I want to make
quite clear to the House something which
also has been referred to and which it is
wise should be emphasized now, namely,
that this Measure has nothing whatever
to do with either the finance or the details that will ultimately arise in connection with the Government's relation to
local authorities, or the relation of local
authorities to those who benefit.
This is only, as has been stated by the
noble Viscount, a preliminary Measure
paving the way and clearing away whatever difficulties arise in regard to valuation. Apportionments have got to be
made in regard to industry, and also in
regard to agriculture to some extent, and
this Measure is really dealing with the
process of apportionment. Therefore, in
discussing the subject in my own House
I thought it was only courteous and right
that all the information which the
Government possessed in regard to the
ultimate working out of the Measure
should be presented, and I gave it at very
great length. I dealt very exhaustively
with the matter.
Having made these preliminary remarks
I will now touch in their order upon one
653 Rating and Valuation Bill-

5 DECEMBER 1928
Second Reading
or two of the points raised. The right hon.
Senator (Colonel Sharman Crawford)
opposite was concerned as to how this
would be financed, in other words. Where
is the money to come from? My reply
is very simple: It comes from the taxpayers of Northern Ireland. In the adjustment of our accounts with the Imperial Treasury it will be our business to
see that a charge against our revenue will
be made sufficient to cover the liability
that would be incurred in connection with
this scheme. The whole principle of the
formula which is brought into operation
in determining our financial relations is
that social service of a corresponding
character in Great Britain and Ireland
should be admitted, that is to say, in
proportion as the local expenditure in
Great Britain rises from any cause we
here should be entitled to a corresponding
rise and charge against our revenue for
this similar service.
Therefore, as this Measure has been
initiated in Great Britain and will presumably be brought into operation if the
Measure at present before the Imperial
Parliament goes through, of which we
have no doubt, it then follows, as we contend, as a natural corollary, that we are
entitled to follow the same course and
spend a relative amount of money.
carrying out that relationship the figures
which I have now given may be taken as
appertaining. I hope this point is now
made quite clear.
In
My right hon. Friend opposite was also
rather concerned as to whether farmers
would be relieved of all rates. The
farmers will be relieved of all rates-that
word "all" is a very comprehensive wordwith the exception of drainage rates in
respect of those farms that are relieved
by drainage, and, of course, I need not
tell you that is not the whole of the
farmers of Northern Ireland. If there
is any special advantage or any special
benefit given to farmers by reason of
special drainage rates that is a matter for
the county councils. Therefore, those
who benefit would not be relieved from
whatever small charge would be made in
respect of these drainage rates, but in regard to such matters as education and
roads, and other matters of that kind,
all these things will be removed.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD.
Education?
Mr. POLLOCK: Yes, education.
The word "all" comprises everything
except what I have mentioned. Another
point made by the right hon. Senator was
the possibility of a tendency to extravagance. On the contrary, I hope this will
have a tendency to economy, and I think,
although this matter is too involved for
me to go into all the features of the case,
it will be found when the arrangements
are completed as between the Government
and the county councils there will be an
incentive to economy on the part of the
county council. I think, therefore, that
those troubles which the right hon.
Senator fears will not arise.
Senator Long was concerned that the
measure of relief in respect of buildings
only extended to one-third. Although
my hon. Friend, Senator Armstrong, behind me disposed of that by the examples
he gave, still perhaps it might be interesting to the Senator who raised the
question to say that in order that the
Government might be sure that no injustice was done in respect of valuation
being made in that form, I had our
Valuation Department go over a large
area of the country and take at random
numbers of examples in order that we
might test as far as possible whether we
were doing justly or unjustly in adopting
33 per cent. as the valuation of the farm
buildings.
Taking areas such as Downpatrick,
Lisnaskea, Hillsborough, Lurgan, Dungannon, Newry No. 1, and Ballymena,
and taking 300 farms, we found that the
average by a curious coincidence worked
out at 33.3 per cent. Therefore, I think
any doubt hon. Senators may have in
their minds in regard to this may be removed, but I should add in respect of
that question that if any individual
farmer feels he is prejudiced by that
method of calculation it is open to him
to appeal to a fair, and just, and impartial tribunal for the purpose of having
his valuation revised, and in that way
separation will be made as between the
house and buildings as exists at the present time in England. Therefore, from
that point of view I think the House will
be assured that there is no injustice done.
The question of expansions referred to
by Senator Long I have already referred
to. We have estimated that a sum of
£75,000 should be sufficient to meet those
expenses of county authorities that will
in the ordinary course of their business
probably arise.
655 Rating and Valuation Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE

Mr. LONG: Per annum?
Mr. POLLOCK: A sum of £75,000
per annum. There were various other
matters of detail mentioned, but I do not
know that they were really of any great
importance. Senator Leslie did raise some
small points. He asked an assurance
that we do not give with one hand and
take away with the other. I think from
what I have said up to the present he will
be assured that nothing of that kind is
likely to take place.
Mr. LESLIE : I made no point of
that.
Mr. POLLOCK: Some hon. Senator
did. We have the Valuation Act of
1925, and that is the ark of safety of all
property owners in Northern Ireland
when difficulties arise in connection with
the old Valuation Acts the hon. Member
referred to. Perhaps he will recall that
we passed an Act limiting the powers of
valuers to a certain percentage over the
valuations of 1906; that was practically
the Griffiths valuation of 80 or 90 years
ago. That is a great safeguard which
the farmers and property owners possess
in regard to these things.
As to freight arrangements and the
point made by Senator Hill Dickson touching a small and unimportant amount, no
doubt that was a short distance and a
small freight, but let me give an instance of what relief in freight means to
industry and also to agriculture, but to
industry particularly. This particular
matter was referred to, I think, by
Senator Dorman. Now, we have a very
big industry in Belfast, the shipbuilding
industry, on which so much of the prosperity of Belfast depends. Let me begin with the cumulative effect of freights
and rebates upon this question of
the value of steel plates which enter
very largely into industry in Belfast. There is first ore. At the mine
there is a rebate of rates. Then you get
the pig iron manufacturer and there is a
rebate and so on. You get the different
processes right up, and all receive different
rebates. You come to the rolling mill
where the plates are made and you get
rebate. These plates will get the advantage from the original point right to the
seaboard. At the seaboard, if it is Liverpool, they get a reduction of the rates
upon the article, and when they arrive in
Belfast they will get a reduction in the
rates at the harbour here. So that if
you add all together you will find that the
cumulative effect of the reductions will
amount to 10s or 12s a ton on the plates.
I need not impress upon the House the
tremendous advantage 10s a ton on steel
plates will be. As to whether the builders
will get the full advantage of that from
the manufacturers is not for me to say.
At any rate the picture I have drawn is
exactly a picture of a practical character.
Let me take another instance, and it is
well to know this because it shows the
value of this Measure in all its various
details. Take the linen trade and the
different processes that the material must
go through before the ultimate condition
of linen is reached. You have first the
farmer who gets de-rating. Then you
have the scutcher de-rated; you have the
spinner de-rated; you have the weaver
de-rated; the bleacher de-rated, and so
on. You will find that the cumulative
effect of that will be at any
rate something substantial. I think
that these examples I have given will
bring to the hon. Senator's mind very
forcibly that although his particular instance was not of any account and did
not satisfy or did not impress the farmer,
still the general effect on the different
industries, particularly in the country,
must be very considerable as a result of
this de-rating scheme.
And there is this to be borne in mind,
that the basic industries of the country
upon which the existence of the country
depends are reduced to such a condition of
despair that in default of something in
the nature of safeguarding, or at any rate
some radical method of dealing with the
problem, they must cease. And this
rating reform is probably the best, perhaps the only, Measure that can be adopted
in order to save many of these great basic
industries from extinction. That is the
cumulative effect of the whole Measure.
I do not know that there was any
feature of the Bill raised that I have not
touched upon. It is not plain of course
that we will reach the millennium as a
result of this, nor yet that we will get
within sight of the Promised Land,
although I think we are on the Pisgah
height where we can see something ahead
of us that will accrue to the advantage
of the community and of the people. I
657 Rating and Valuation Bill

5 DECEMBER 1928
Adjournment.
t


W
-1
র
hope also that while the farmer, in this
case the largest and greatest industry in
the country, will be benefited by the rates,
he will receive it as a net result to himself,
and therefore the price of his produce will
not be affected by it. In other words
the farmer himself will be benefited and
also the consumer will, presumablyalthough it is not intended that this
should necessarily benefit the consumer.
The consumer will have some regard to
the miserable prices that farmers are
receiving for their produce, and the
dealers who buy from the farmers and
distribute the goods will understand that
two shillings to-day for potatoes will not
keep the farmer living. It is in an
endeavour to remedy and to help all these
things that in part this Measure is being
submitted.
A question a Senator did ask was with
regard to the borrowing powers of local
authorities. The position of local authorities will not be affected in the slightest
in regard to this. I am sure hon. Senators will recall that this is a matter on
which the British conditions differ radically from ours. The British Measure is
as much a reform of local government as
it is a De-rating Bill. We do not touch
the question of local government.
Our
county councils will be the authorities
that deal with these matters we are now
discussing. They are in existence and
their powers will not in the slightest
degree be affected by anything we are
undertaking in this Bill. I think I have
touched upon all the matters that have
been raised in the House to-day. I hope
when the Committee Stage is reached if
anything in the meantime should strike
hon. Senators there will be no difficulty
in reassuring them that the Government
whole-heartedly endeavouring to
place our agriculture and, so far as it can
be done, our industries in a better position
than they have been in the past, by this
Measure that is before the House.

Mr. LESLIE: About taking the Committee Stage to-morrow, so far as I am
concerned I do not think I need put

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Has any
other Member any objection?

Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD :
I. should like to raise a question
matter that happened to me
yesterday. When this Senate adjourned during pleasure yesterday
afternoon the Prime Minister sent for me
on a rather important matter and I was
with him. As far as I could hear, no
notice was sent that the House was resuming, and when I came down after the
interview I found the Senate closed and
everybody gone. I think Senators when
they are in the building should get
notice.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am very sorry that
should have happened. I will see that
in future notice is given to Members when
the House adjourns during pleasure and
resumes.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly

Thursday, 6th December, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move,
'That
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting, for the purpose of
taking the remaining stages of the Appropriation (No. 2) Bill and the Rating and
Valuation (Apportionment) Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount MASSEREENE and FER-
RARD: I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time." This is a
Money Bill and is, I venture to say, selfexplanatory. Therefore I do not think it
is necessary for me to enter into the details of the Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: I think I am correct in
assuming that this Bill contains a provision for a contribution, at all events,
towards the cost of maintaining the Ulster
War Memorial in France. If so, I rise
to say that that is a purpose which will be
accepted with the greatest gratification
and will give the utmost satisfaction to
that part of Northern Ireland from which
I come, and will, I am sure, be received
with equal acclamation in other parts of
Northern Ireland.

661 Rating and Valuation Bill-

6 DECEMBER 1928
Committee.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: There is a question I should like to ask the Minister of
Finance regarding Sub-Clause 2, paragraph (b). Agricultural land in an urban area is derated if it is used as farm
land, but there is a provision that it must
not be land which is part of an ornamental park, garden, or pleasure ground.
It seems to me that there might be farm
land which is part of an ornamental park
and which might be producing food.
happen to know a case of that kind.
There might be a difficulty in giving a
separate valuation to the part that was
used as farm land, but it might be done
as in the case of farm buildings. If the
farmer is dissatisfied with the one-third
valuation put upon his buildings he has
a right to have a revaluation.
I
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): I have no doubt that if any
material part of land included in an ornamental park was devoted to productive
agricultural purposes a separate valuation could be arrived at and it would
come within the ambit of this Measure.
It does not appear to me to be insuperable to have the part producing food
separately valued.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: May we take
it that the Bill also includes any part
of an ornamental park that is used for
producing food for man by grazing cattle
and sheep?
Mr. POLLOCK: Not necessarily.

Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Definition of industrial
hereditaments) and 5 (Entries in valuation lists as to industrial hereditaments),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Definition of freighttransport hereditaments).
Mr. LESLIE: On Clause 6 I have two
questions to ask, and I think, perhaps, it
would be convenient if I asked them together as they both relate to this Clause.
The first of them is in regard to the apparent difficulty to which I alluded yesterday as regards the motor services which
may come into existence as a consequence
of our Railway (Motor Vehicles) Bill of
last year. The point I want to raise is
whether any hereditament which can be
directly attributed to a motor transport
service run by a railway company, whether
for the conveyance of passengers or for
the transport of goods, is or is not to be a
hereditament for freight-transport purposes. If the Committee will look at the
Bill itself they will see that in this Clause
we are dealing with, in Sub-Clause 1,
paragraph (a), we speak of
"A hereditament occupied and used
wholly or partly for railway transport
purposes as part of a railway undertaking carried on by a railway company."
On that there would seem to be no difficulty in answering my question, but if we
go on to to Sub-Clause 2, of course of the
same Clause, we say round about line 14,
"Transport purposes' means all or any
of the following purposes: –
seem
(a) 'railway transport purposes,"
that is to say, all purposes connected
with the conveyance or transport by
railway of passengers and their
luggage.
a
The use of motor omnibuses supplied
and run by a railway company to bring in
passengers from outlying districts to
railway station in order that they may
then travel by rail seems to me to be a
purpose connected with transport by railway of passengers, and it therefore would
appear that the Bill, perhaps, is not quite
clear. On the other hand, it would not
necessary to suggest an actual
Amendment at this stage, because it must,
I think, follow that when the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, is
making the inquiries of which he spoke
yesterday as to the various details of working out, not so much this Bill, but the
Bill that is to come, he will necessarily
be brought into contact with little difficulties of this description, and it will be easy
in the future to clear up any obscurities
that more practical investigation may discover to exist. At the moment there does
seem to be a point as to whether any
hereditament, whether in the shape of a
garage for the housing of vehicles used for
this purpose or whether it be the shop or
repairing establishment necessarily used
for such vehicles, comes in and can be
separately assessed. That does seem to
present some little difficulty.
663 Rating and Valuation Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
Passing from that to my second point,
which again is not of very great importance, we see on page 10 a definition of
vessels. Vessels, of course, are referred
to in so far as they relate to docks, and
we observe in Sub-Clause 2, paragraph
(c) a reference to purposes connected with
the provision of accommodation for vessels
and their stores, including fishing tackle,
which brings in fishing vessels. We see
also in Sub-Clause 3, again round about
line 14, that "dock" includes any harbours, and so on, at which vessels can
ship or unship merchandise or passengers,
and then, finally, at the end of the Sub-
Clause, we get a definition of “vessel.”
We are told that "vessel" has the same
meaning as that assigned to it by Section
seven hundred and forty-two of the Merchant Shipping Act, 1894. That Section
of a somewhat long Act tells us that
"vessel' includes any ship or boat or any
other description of vessel used in navigation. The English Act, which
follow as closely as we can, defines
"vessel" not in the words used there but
in exactly the same words as are used in
the Act of 1894. They set them out at
length.
we
a
It is perfectly obvious that in the
departure from the English Act the
draughtsman had a purpose. I was certain there was an explanation. The
explanation, it occurred to me, might be
that it was intended to apply the English
Act of 1921, which meant that the term
"vessel" in the 1894 Act, included in
Great Britain for the purposes of Parts
one and eight of the Merchant Shipping
Act of 1894, the parts relating to the
registry of ships and the liability of
owners relating thereto extended it to any
lighter or barge however propelled, provided it is used in exclusively tidal waters
or harbours. And it is obvious, I think,
that the question of any hereditament
connected with a lighter might be of some
but not of very great importance, and
that any benefit such hereditament would
get in freight-transport would have to be
passed on in accordance with the suggestions in the last page of the White Paper.
The matter is not of first-class importance. But as we are dealing with a definition Bill we ought to understand what
we are doing, and if there is anything required to be done it could very easily be
done at a later date. I therefore ask the
right hon. Gentleman to be so kind as to
tell us what the intention is as regards the
two points of the railway motor service
and vessels with special reference to barges
and lighters.
Mr. POLLOCK: So far as the motor
services are concerned they are excluded
from the purview of this Measure, and
obviously so, I think, because even if the
hon. Senator will carefully read the line
to which he referred in page 8 I think it
is he will see that "transport purposes'
means all or any of the following purposes: "Railway transport purposes.
that is to say, all purposes connected with
the conveyance or transport by railway of
passengers. I am quite sure the hon.
Senator sees the difficulty of introducing
motors in this connection, for the reason
that there is no tangible hereditament to
which a valuation could be attached.
Therefore, that in itself would rule motors
outside the ambit of this Measure.
think that in itself is quite sufficient evidence that it is not contemplated at all
that motors would come under the umbrella, if I may call it so, of the Bill.
I
In regard to the definition of the word
"dock," I think we will have to be satisfied with the ordinary interpretation and
meaning of the word. It applies to any
harbour, whether inland or on the coast,
where goods are landed from ve..els
carrying them either to or from. That,
I think, would be regarded as sufficient
definition.
But when we come to the word “vessel
which seems to have concerned the hon.
Senator, I do not think the class of craft
that would come under the designation
"vessel' is of first-class importance. Stili
it must be sufficiently clear if we accept
the definition which is given in that Act
to which the hon. Senator referred, that
is, the Merchant Shipping Act of 1894,
where the description was read "any snip
or boat or any other description of vessel
used in navigation," that is to say, any
class of craft that is used in the transport
of goods or carrying passengers will come
under the description of the word
"vessel."
As to the liability that might be attached to it, that has no reference and
no connection whatever with this Measure
which deals entirely with the matter of
dock arrangements and rebates concerning
docks. I do not think either of the

Mr. LESLIE: I want to say a word on
Clause 8, if I may refer again for a minute
to something I said yesterday about the
Irish Valuation Acts. I am afraid I did
not make myself entirely clear. What I
wanted to get at was the abolition of the
term. I had thought we might have
done it in this Bill by adding a few
words to the final Clause and setting out
the enactments which, it was considered,
formed the Irish Valuation Acts in the
Schedule. But there was an insuperable
obstacle, and that was the title of the
Bill. I hope we may do it in the future.
There is a definition of the Irish Valuation Acts, but it reminds one of the celebrated definition of an archdeacon. The
definition of the Irish Land Acts was
"the Acts relating to the valuation of
land in Ireland." It is not very helpful.
I do hope that in future we may be able
to set out what Acts or portions of Acts
we really mean, and then bring them into
the new Act as the Valuation Acts (Northern Ireland), 1852 to 1929. There is
no question arising on the details.
Mr. POLLOCK: That could only be
possible at some future time when legislation would be contemplated in relation
to valuation or revaluation. I do not
think there is anyone in Northern Ireland
at the present time very anxious to raise
the question of revaluation. I rather
suspect they wish to keep it underground
indefinitely. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). It will meet, I gather, with the
satisfaction of everyone here.

Mr. LESLIE: There is another matter. I want to be assured that I have

of the Schedule may be treated as a

is in the event of any secretary of

purpose Belfast Corporation, finding it

world will not come to an end. I take it

a

and the county borough authorities, that,

Bill reported, without amendment, to

Mr. LESLIE: We cannot pass from
this Bill without first of all, I think, expressing-I am sure I am speaking now
for everybody in the House-our sense of
gratification that the Minister of Finance
himself has been so kind as to come here
to be with us during the Second Reading
That
and during the Committee Stage.
has been, I am sure, the greatest possible
help to us in solving small difficulties and
greater difficulties, and it will help us
very much when we have to go and do
as most of us will attempt to explain
this Measure to our friends outside.
Now, on the question of the Third
Reading, I have to put up another question and I am doing this more on behalf
of the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
who addressed us yesterday and who was
unable to be here to-day. My right hon.
and gallant Friend is a little in doubt
still over what the £75,000, which was
mentioned yesterday, is intended for.
Something was said about its being a
measure of compensation for the reduction of contribution. My right hon. and
gallant Friend was looking at it purely
from the point of view of the rating
authority. He seemed to be afraid that
there was some reduction in the rateable
area. If so, an explanation was required
He did not seem really to be
on that.
clear whether it was not a provision for
the first six months of the new scheme.
That was the first point.
The second point was what protection
would the ordinary villa residence, as I
may call it, have in the event, let us say.
of a sudden material rise in the education
I rather gathered myself from
rate.
page 31 of the blue book that the annual
Government grant to local authorities
will be calculated on a basis of three
years and will endure for five years.
Mr. POLLOCK: That is right.
Mr. LESLIE: That is information
that is somewhat public property.
At
all events, we have had time to read it.
The question rather was as to the future.
I want, if the right hon. Gentleman will
tell me, to know that the position of our
friend, the villa resident, in the event
of a sudden rise in any rate will be
neither better nor worse by reason of the
passage of this Bill than it otherwise would have been. I hope for and expect a satisfactory answer to that point.
As regards the rest, needless to say, I do
not oppose the Third Reading of the Bill.
Quite the contrary.
Mr. POLLOCK: With regard to the
question of the disposal of the £75,000 to
which reference has been made by the
hon. Senator, if he will refer to the report of the Committee on the recommendations of which the Bill largely is
founded he will see it explained there,
on page 27, in the sixth paragraph.
"We recommend, therefore, that for
the first quinquennial period the State
should contribute towards increases in
expenditure by each local authority in
the proportion which the 'deficiency'
grant bears to the total expenditure on
which such grant was calculated, subject, however, to a maximum increase
in expenditure, towards which the supplementary grants will be made,
equivalent to the produce of a rate of
8d in the £ on the valuation of all
hereditaments, including those derated,
in the area. The maximum sum which
could be required for this purpose
is approximately £75,000 per annum,
but we do not anticipate that anything
like this maximum will be reached."
Clearly this £75,000 during the quinquennial period will be directed to indemnifying the villa resident to which
the Senator has referred against any increase that would be imposed upon him as
a result of what I have described-perhaps
not quite clearly as a restriction of the
area of rating, that is to say, the loss the
local authority sustained by the fact that
they are not empowered to ask any rate
from farmers and will only get 25 per cent.
from industralists. Under these circumstances the reduction thus caused or the
deficiency thus created-a villa resident
is protected against any increase in
his rating by the provision which I have
just read. I think that makes the position perfectly clear, unless my language
is obscure, as to the protection that will
be afforded to those who are outside the
669 Rating and Valuation Bill- 6 DECEMBER 1928

Committee.
ambit of the Measure in the way of relief.
What I did say yesterday, if I may recall
it, was that it is the intention of the
Government that no one should suffer by
the Measure, that is to say, be prejudiced
by the Measure. I should think that
practically covers the whole question
which the Senator has in his mind.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, that clears the
point.
Mr. DORMAN: Since speaking on this
Measure yesterday I have given a very
considerable amount of thought to the
possible results. I would very much like
to believe that the object that the Government has in view will be carried out, but
it appears to me after a wide field of observation of the agriculturists of Ireland
that unless the agriculturists accompany this Measure with a change of outlook, co-ordinate their efforts and refer
more to co-operation in the area, the results of this Measure will not be as ripe
as the Government anticipate as to the
relief that is given to the agriculturists of
the country. I had very great experience
in my young life of the adverse consequences to the farming interests through
want of co-operation in supplying their
products to the market. The absence of
co-operation and collective effort on the
part of farmers to get their products into
the market according to the demand has
been disastrous.
I happened some 48 years ago to have
been widely dealing with a product the
quality of which Ireland exceeded the
world in, that is barley, and I then found
that in the early stages of the malting
season the farmers, irrespective of the consequences, glutted the market and depressed the price of their product. Not
merely did they glut the market and
depress the price of the produce but they
also exposed that product to rapid deterioration and excessive charges for demurrage and storage during the period of
transit from the farm to the source of
consumption. The result was that in the
space of two or three years the agriculturists' products, particularly barley, were
reduced to an average of about £750 per
day. In one part of the country the
farmers had entered into a co-operative
effort for the purpose of marketing, and
that resulted in this organisation keeping
up the price of their product and placing
it on the market as the demand arose for
it. Unless those who are engaged in
agriculture avail themselves of the advantages of co-operation and control in
marketing their products to a greater
extent than they do at present I fear
this Measure will not have the beneficial
consequences that the Government expect.
Mr. POLLOCK: May I, with the permission of the House, touch upon the
subject raised by the Senator who has
just spoken. He has outlined exactly
the reasons, the underlying causes, for
this legislation; that is to say, that the
condition of the farmers for the past 40
or 50 years has been going from bad to
worse, and that the burden of rates has
therefore fallen upon them much more
heavily than during periods of progress
and prosperity. As I say, it is very difficult it would be impossible for us here
-to help the farmers by any fiscal
measures such as the hon. Senator referred to, I think, in his former speech
on the subject, and, therefore, the only
method by which we could enable the
farmer to take a brighter outlook and
take a brighter view of the future was
to encourage him by removing from him
the rates that burden his land.
It is a great misfortune that our North
of Ireland farmers have only one market,
the British market, which is open to the
world. As a Government we cannot do
anything except through our Ministry of
Agriculture. It is only by the encouragement of co-operative efforts and by a great
many other Measures which the Department of Agriculture has undertaken, and
I think very successfully undertaken-
(hon. Members: Hear, hear)-by the
adoption of economic Measures which the
House is quite familiar with, I am sure,
because these Measures have come before
it from time to time it is only by these
Measures that we enable the farmer to
take a somewhat brighter outlook of his
condition. We are particularly unfortunate in the North of Ireland that our
farms are largely so small. The fact
that 80,000 out of 120,000 farmers own
under 20 acres of land of £20 valuation
is sufficiently significant to enable a House
such as this dealing with matters of this
kind to realise the difficulties of helping
men of this class.
In the first place they want education.
They want to be assured that these Meas-
671 Rating and Valuation Bill.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
[Mr. Pollock]
ures that the Government are undertaking are good for them as a whole. Many
of them resisted these Measures because
they thought they interfered with their
personal liberty and with carrying on
their business in their own way. I am
bound to say from information we have
received that many of them who laboured
under impressions of that kind have now
begun to realise that what the Government has done for them has been actually
to their advantage. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). I think the House will
admit that this Measure, which has
reached a finality to-day so far as this
House is concerned, is almost the coping
stone of the various undertakings the
Government have made in order to assist
the agriculturists of the Province. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be

Mr. LESLIE: Might I ask when it is
proposed to adjourn to?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Until the
18th December, when Parliament will be

House will meet perhaps an hour or so
earlier in order that any questions put
may be answered. We will leave the hour
until such time as Mr. Speaker directs.

i
THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Tuesday, 18th December, 1928.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Eleven of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Returns of Local Taxation in Northern
Ireland for the year 1924-25.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister on his behalf to acquaint
the House that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on be half of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the following Bills: -
Expiring Laws Continuance,
Marketing of Potatoes,
Poor Relief (Exceptional Distress),
Rent and Mortgages Interest (Restrictions),
Loans Guarantee (No. 2),
Queen's University of Belfast,
Borough Funds,
Housing,
Game Preservation,
Slaughtered Animals (Compensation),
Marketing of Eggs,
Appropriation (No. 2),
Finance (No. 2),
Rating and Valuation (Apportionment),
McMullan's Divorce,
Egan's Divorce,
Antrim Electricity Supply.
PERSONAL EXPLANATION.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to give a word
of explanation as to my conduct in this
House on a recent occasion. Since our
last sitting I have been assailed in public on account of the attitude I assumed
when the Representation of the People
Bill was before this House. I have been
accused of unworthy conduct, conduct to
which no hon. Member of this House
would, I am sure, descend. I have been
accused of attending in this House for the
mere purpose of obtaining the fees connected with the duties of the Senate. My
Lord, I wish to repudiate that. I have
never come here for the purpose of merely
obtaining the fees in connection with
Senatorial office, and I believe that no
other hon. Member of this House has
ever been guilty of conduct of that kind.
I believe that were I guilty of
conduct of that character I would be unworthy to associate with Members of this
House because I recognize that the
honour of this House is embodied in the
honour of every individual Member, and
the conduct of every Member, both inside and outside the House, should be
becoming the honourable position of a
Senator of Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sure
I am voicing the feelings of this House
when I say that anybody who knows
Senator Dorman would regard such an
accusation as perfectly unfounded. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I suggest
that the House should now adjourn during
pleasure.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Prorogation of Parliament.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know, "It is his Grace's pleasure that they attend him in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, his Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:
675 Prorogation of Parliament.

THE SENATE
King's Speech.
KING'S SPEECH.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.

I congratulate you upon the result
of your labours during the session now
concluded.
I feel that widespread satisfaction will
be occasioned by the extension of the
Franchise to women of 21 years of age.
I am glad that the powers of Local
Authorities in connection with the widening and improving of our road systems
have been extended. Every step in this
direction safeguards users of our highways
by minimising the risk of accident.
a
The decision of my Government to
postpone till 1933 decontrol of rents of
the smaller type of dwellings and to
continue the housing subsidy for
further year causes me profound gratification, as I feel that if the present rate
of building is maintained one at least of
the many difficulties facing the working
classes will be rapidly overcome.
The necessary preliminaries in connection with the drainage of Lough Neagh
and the River Bann have been initiated.
I earnestly hope that when the scheme
is completed relief from flooding so long
looked forward to and so urgently required will be amply afforded.
It is satisfactory to note the passage of
further legislation remedying difficulties
and removing anomalies in the laws
dealing with the sale and consumption
of intoxicating liquors; and that in
particular steps have been taken, with
beneficial results, enabling
stricter
control to be exercised over the sale and
a
consumption of noxious beverages.
A National Health Insurance Measure
has passed into law which will extend
the usefulness and simplify the machinery of that scheme, and an Unemployment Insurance Act has been passed
placing on a simpler and more equitable
basis the conditions governing the distribution of benefit to unemployed workers.
The administrative changes in connection
with the Non-Contributory Old Age
Pensions were smoothly and satisfactorily
effected.
I am glad to observe that during the
present year there has been a welcome
increase of activity in the shipbuilding
industry with consequent additional employment. The operation of the Loans
Guarantee Acts has been a very important
contributory factor in this improvement.
The limit within which guarantees may
be issued has been extended from ten and
a half million pounds to eleven and a half
million pounds.
I note that additional support to the
Linen Publicity Campaign has been
afforded, but I regret that owing mainly
to international causes, this trade is still
in a depressed condition.
My Government has continued its policy
of subsidising schemes beneficial to the
community which are promoted by public
bodies for the purpose of giving employment.
During the present year grants
amounting to two hundred and fifty-four
thousand pounds have facilitated the initiation of one hundred such schemes on
which the expenditure on wages alone
will, it is estimated, amount to three
hundred and forty-one thousand pounds.
The placing upon the Statute Book of
the first of the measures designed to give
effect to the Government's proposals for
de-rating of Agriculture and productive
industry is a very satisfactory feature of
your labours. The operation of the provisions relating to freight and other transport charges will provide a measure of
immediate relief.
comes
Legislation has been passed to regulate
the sale of fish and to secure the better
preservation of the inland fisheries. This
Act
into full operation at the
beginning of next year, and it is hoped
that the benefits which are expected to
accrue therefrom will manifest themselves
within the coming season.
It is gratifying to observe the success
that has attended legislation already
passed in the interests of the egg industry.
This augurs well for the third Marketing
of Eggs Act that has so recently been
added to the Statute Book to advance
yet further the interests of that most
important branch of agricultural activity
Regard has also been paid to the potato
trade, and you have satisfied progressive
agriculturists by passing the Marketing
of Potatoes Act, kindred in principle and
purpose to the enactments relating to the
marketing of eggs.
Proclamation.
18 DECEMBER 1928
I am glad that it has been found
possible to make provision for additional
financial assistance to meet the needs of
Queen's University. I trust this will be
of value in furthering the educational
requirements of the community.
Members of the House of Commons.
I thank you for the provision of
supplies for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
I note with great satisfaction that
good order generally prevails throughout
Northern Ireland and that there has been
a diminution of serious crime.
A gratifying feature of the industriai
life of Northern Ireland is its continued
immunity, save in very rare instances,
from trade disputes, and the maintenance
of harmonious relations between
ployers and employed.
em-
It is my earnest prayer that the blessing
of Almighty God may rest upon all your
past and coming labours.
Then the Proclamation Proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
was read by the Speaker as followeth:
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amendin the said
Act, that the Governor of Northern
Ireland shall in His Majesty's name
summon, prorogue and dissolve the
Parliament of Northern Ireland:
End of Session.

AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern Ireland should be prorogued to the twentysixth day of February, One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Twenty-nine :

JOJS
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT ED-
WARD, DUKE OF ABERCORN,
KNIGHT OF THE MOST NOBLE
ORDER OF THE GARTER, KNIGHT
OF THE MOST ILLUSTRIOUS OR-
DER OF ST. PATRICK, Governor of
Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation
do publish and declare His Majesty's
Pleasure that the said Parliament be, as
from the reading of this Proclamation in
the Parliament of Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday, the twenty-sixth day
of February, One Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty-nine.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
twenty-sixth day of February, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Twentynine, assemble and be holden for the despatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of
the House of Commons of Northern
Ireland are hereby required to give their
attendance accordingly.
Given at the Council Chamber, Belfast,
the seventeenth day of December, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Twentyeight.
Signed by his own hand.
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
His Grace, the Governor, then retired.
End of the Fourth Session (opened 6th
March, 1928), of the Second Parliament
of Northern Ireland, in the Nineteenth
year of the Reign of His Majesty King
George the Fifth.

INDEX.
SECOND PARLIAMENT.
VOLUME OF FOURTH SESSION, 1928.
6th MARCH to 18th DECEMBER.
